One-line: An algorithm that groups nearby genes into “LD loci” whenever the LD-induced correlation of their gene-based test statistics exceeds a threshold, restoring valid multiple-testing guarantees; paired with an AdaPT/XGBoost metadata-guided FDR workflow and an interactive LD locus zoom visualization tool.
What it is

A statistical workflow for genome-wide-association-study summary statistics that extends gene-based testing (e.g. VEGAS/MAGMA-style quadratic statistics Tg = zg^T zg) to account for LD between SNPs in different nearby genes.
Core algorithm: agglomerative hierarchical clustering where linkage = LD-based correlation Cor(Tg, Tg’) computed from a reference panel; merge the most-correlated gene/locus pair until all remaining pairwise squared correlations fall below r^2, within a window w Mb.
P-values for merged loci are computed by Monte-Carlo simulation of null multivariate-normal z-vectors (Cholesky decomposition of the LD covariance), since the merged statistic has no closed form.
Downstream: adaptive P-value thresholding (AdaPT) with gradient boosted trees (XGBoost) over gene/locus metadata for metadata-guided FDR control; Nadaraya–Watson kernel smoothing of squared z statistics to localize signal.

Key facts

Reference LD: 503 individuals from the 1000 Genomes EUR sample.
Recommended settings in the paper: stringent r^2 = 0.25, window w = 6 Mb.
Distributed as R code (adaptMT package, github.com/ryurko/adaptMT) plus an R Shiny + plotly interactive “LD locus zoom” app (ron-yurko.shinyapps.io/ld_locus_zoom/); data/code at github.com/ryurko/Agglomerative-LD-loci-testing.
Applicable to any gene-based testing framework and, per authors, a wide range of omics problems.

Provenance

Introduced in yurko-2021-approach-to-gene-based-testing.

wiki/entities/agre-cohort.title: agre-cohort
wiki/entities/agre-cohort.content:
AGRE (Autism Genetic Resource Exchange)
One-line: A repository of clinically well-characterized multiplex autism families (two or more autistic children) used to amplify rare inherited ASD risk signal that is depleted in simplex-family designs.
What it is

The Autism Genetic Resource Exchange (AGRE) is a family-based ASD collection; Cirnigliaro et al. performed >30x whole-genome sequencing on 1,004 AGRE multiplex families (4,551 individuals), the largest such multiplex WGS cohort to date.
Multiplex ascertainment (recurrent ASD within a family) enriches for transmitted/inherited risk, giving a genetic architecture distinct from simplex cohorts such as SSC and ASC, where rare de novo variation dominates.
Autistic individuals diagnosed via validated ADI-R / ADOS protocols; deep phenotyping (verbal/nonverbal IQ, ADI-R developmental milestones, SRS) supports genotype-phenotype analyses.

wiki/entities/aida.title: aida
wiki/entities/aida.content:
AIDA — Asian Immune Diversity Atlas
Introduced in liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral (Nat Genet 2024) as the cohort and resource underlying a population-scale single-cell splicing and sQTL study of human peripheral blood.
What it is
AIDA (Asian Immune Diversity Atlas), Data Freeze v.1, is a genetics-coupled single-cell RNA-seq resource of ~1 million PBMCs from 474 healthy donors of Eastern, Southeastern and South Asian ancestry (Japan, Korea, Singapore — including Singaporean Chinese, Malay and Indian individuals). Profiled with 10x Genomics 5′ scRNA-seq (leveraging endogenous “exon painting” to extend exon coverage), it spans 21 PBMC subtypes and supports two-level alternative-splicing quantification (SpliZ single-cell, leafcutter pseudobulk). It provides a multi-ancestry catalogue of cell-type-specific, sex-biased and ancestry-biased differential splicing, 11,577 cis-sQTLs, 607 trans-sGenes and 107 dynamic sQTLs, framed as a roadmap to dissect splicing’s role in complex disease at single-cell resolution.
In this wiki

A reusable multi-ancestry immune single-cell + splicing-qtl resource; one of the few cell-type-resolved sQTL maps centered on Asian genetic ancestry, and a substrate for splicing-and-disease colocalization (autoimmune/inflammatory traits).

Sources

liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral

wiki/entities/akita.title: akita
wiki/entities/akita.content:
Akita
What: A convolutional neural network that predicts locus-specific 3D genome-architecture (Hi-C / Micro-C contact maps) directly from ~1 Mb of DNA sequence alone.
Akita takes 2^20 bp (~1 Mb) of one-hot-encoded DNA and outputs log(observed/expected) contact-frequency maps for all pairs of 2,048-bp bins in a cropped ~917 kb window, with one output head per training dataset fudenberg-2020-predicting-3d-genome-folding. It was introduced by Fudenberg, Kelley & Pollard (Nature Methods, 2020) and released with the Basenji codebase (github.com/calico/basenji).
Architecture
A “trunk” (based on the Basenji CNN) applies 1D convolutions with max-pooling to reduce the sequence to 512 vectors at 2,048-bp resolution, then dilated residual convolutions to propagate information to megabase scale. A “head” averages each pair of bin representations (i, j), appends a positional encoding of genomic distance, and applies symmetric dilated 2D convolutions to produce the contact map. It was trained on five high-quality human Hi-C/Micro-C datasets (HFF, H1ESC, GM12878, IMR90, HCT116) in a multi-task framework, minimizing MSE.
Capabilities
Once trained, Akita makes in-silico predictions in seconds, enabling: saturation mutagenesis at motif and single-nucleotide resolution; interpretation of GTEx eQTLs; prediction of engineered structural variants (deletions, inversions); and cross-species prediction of genome folding. Its learned representations recover an orientation-specific grammar for CTCF binding sites and reveal predictive nucleotides beyond the core CTCF motif.
Limits
Predictions are confined to ~1-Mb windows and are not influenced by information beyond that window (no A/B compartments or inter-chromosomal features), and cell-type specificity of predictions is limited. Akita requires Hi-C/Micro-C training data for any cell type of interest.
Sources

fudenberg-2020-predicting-3d-genome-folding — introduces Akita, its architecture, training, and downstream applications.

wiki/entities/alphagenome.title: alphagenome
wiki/entities/alphagenome.content:
AlphaGenome
One-line: Google DeepMind’s unified DNA sequence-to-function model that takes 1 Mb of sequence and predicts thousands of functional genomic tracks — including splice sites, splice-site usage and splice junctions — at up to single-base-pair resolution, achieving SOTA variant-effect prediction.
A u-net-inspired backbone plus transformers processes 1 Mb of input (capturing distal enhancer–gene regulation) while emitting base-resolution tracks; trained on human and mouse genomes with two-stage fold-specific pretraining then teacher–student distillation into one all-fold student model (<1 s per variant on an H100). It is a new SOTA on splicing variant effects, outperforming dedicated models (spliceai, pangolin, deltasplice, borzoi); its explicit splice-junction read-count prediction is a mechanism the bin-based Enformer/Borzoi outputs lack. Reported benchmarks: splice-site auPRC 0.79, splice-site-usage Pearson r 0.86, and +14.7% relative improvement over Borzoi on cell-type-specific expression. Extends the sequence-to-function lineage of enformer and Borzoi (Avsec et al. 2026, Nature).
Referenced by

avsec-2026-advancing-regulatory-variant-effect-prediction

wiki/entities/banksy.title: banksy
wiki/entities/banksy.content:
BANKSY
Building Aggregates with a Neighborhood Kernel and Spatial Yardstick — a scalable spatial-clustering algorithm that unifies cell typing and tissue-domain segmentation for spatial omics data.

Augments each cell’s own transcriptome with two neighborhood descriptors: the weighted mean neighborhood expression and an azimuthal Gabor filter (AGF) capturing the local expression gradient (rotation-invariant), embedding cells in a neighbor-augmented product space.
A single mixing parameter λ tunes the balance: low λ yields cell typing (λ = 0 = conventional nonspatial clustering), high λ yields tissue-domain segmentation.
Uses scalable graph partitioning (Leiden by default) with effectively O(n) neighbor-computation complexity; scales to 2 million cells and is 10–1,000× faster than competing spatial methods.
Works across RNA imaging (MERFISH, MERSCOPE, VeraFISH, STARmap), RNA sequencing (Slide-seq, Visium, CosMx) and protein imaging (CODEX); also supports spatial QC and spatially aware batch correction. Interoperable with Seurat, SingleCellExperiment and SCANPY.

Introduced in singhal-2024-banksy-unifies-cell-typing (Nature Genetics, 2024).

wiki/entities/benspm.title: benspm
wiki/entities/benspm.content:
BENSpm
One-line: N1,N11-bis(ethyl)norspermine, a polyamine analogue that inhibits de novo polyamine synthesis while staying polycationic, used to separate “conventional” polyamine functions from polyamine-mediated metabolic-shielding of splicing factors.
Because BENSpm remains a polycation, it still binds the acidic 350–380 region of sf3a3 (Kd ≈ 0.05 mM), blocks CK1 phosphorylation of SF3A3, and prevents the alternative-splicing changes caused by combined polyamine-synthesis inhibition (SAM486A + DFMO) — despite depleting bulk polyamines. This dissociates the shielding function (physical protection of phosphorylatable acidic motifs in U2 snRNP SF3 splicing-factors) from polyamines’ other cellular roles, and is proposed as a candidate for therapeutically normalizing splicing in cancers with dysregulated polyamine metabolism.
Referenced by

zabala-letona-2026-polyamine-dependent-metabolic-shielding-regulates-alternative

wiki/entities/bigwig-bigbed.title: bigwig-bigbed
wiki/entities/bigwig-bigbed.content:
BigWig and BigBed (BBI)
Compressed, binary, indexed genome-annotation file formats developed at UCSC, collectively called Big Binary Indexed (BBI) files. They store data at several resolutions and support random access so that a genome browser transmits only the data needed for the current view rather than the whole file, enabling fast remote browsing of large distributed datasets.

BigBed — binary indexed version of BED (Browser Extensible Data) tables; can carry custom fields with autoSql (.as) declarations and can hold overlapping intervals.
BigWig — binary indexed version of wiggle (fixedStep/variableStep) or bedGraph text; associates a floating-point value with genomic positions for graph tracks.
Creation tools: bedToBigBed, wigToBigWig, bedGraphToBigWig (plus fetchChromSizes for chrom.sizes).
Parsing tools: bigWigSummary/bigBedSummary, bigWigInfo/bigBedInfo, bigBedToBed, bigWigToWig, bigWigToBedGraph.
Internals: cirTree index (B+ tree for chromosome names/IDs + single-dimensional R tree), zlib deflate compression of data blocks, HTTP/HTTPS byte-range and FTP-resume remote access, and a local block cache.

Introduced in kent-2010-bigwig-bigbed-enabling-browsing.

wiki/entities/borzoi.title: borzoi
wiki/entities/borzoi.content:
Borzoi
One-line: A deep-learning sequence-to-function model that predicts RNA-seq coverage in 32-bp bins from genomic sequence over a long input context, used as a main track/expression/QTL baseline (Linder et al. 2025, Nat Genet).
Borzoi extends the enformer sequence-to-function lineage to RNA-seq coverage prediction and serves as the principal baseline against which alphagenome is benchmarked. AlphaGenome reports a +14.7% relative improvement over Borzoi on cell-type-specific gene-expression log-fold-change and also beats it on splicing variant effects — where AlphaGenome’s explicit splice-junction read-count prediction is a mechanism separate from Borzoi’s bin-based outputs. It is used as one of the splicing/expression baselines alongside spliceai, pangolin and deltasplice.
Referenced by

avsec-2026-advancing-regulatory-variant-effect-prediction

wiki/entities/brain-expressed-enhancers-.title: brain-expressed-enhancers-browser
wiki/entities/brain-expressed-enhancers-.content:
Brain-Expressed Enhancers Resource (Voineagu lab)
What: A human-brain enhancer RNA (eRNA) resource introduced by Yao et al. (2015), comprising the catalog of brain-expressed enhancers (BEEs / robust BEEs = rBEEs), their eRNA expression and chromatin-mark annotations, eRNA–gene coexpression network data, and an interactive genome browser track.
Key facts

Contains 908 brain-expressed enhancers (BEEs), with a stringently filtered robust subset of 103 rBEEs that show replicable, brain-specific eRNA expression and overlap enhancer chromatin marks.
Genome browser track visualizes rBEE expression alongside FANTOM5 CAGE data and multiple external enhancer data sets.
Presented as the first resource of human brain eRNA expression data and the first to provide eRNA coexpression network information for candidate enhancer–target-gene links.
Intended as a resource for targeted sequencing of noncoding regions in neurodevelopmental disorders and for interpreting whole-genome sequencing data.
Companion website: voineagulab.unsw.edu.au/brain-expressed-enhancers/. Raw CAGE and RNA-seq deposited under BioProject PRJNA273171.

Used in

yao-2015-coexpression-networks-identify-brain-region

wiki/entities/brain-rna-isoform-browser-.title: brain-rna-isoform-browser-tool
wiki/entities/brain-rna-isoform-browser-.content:
Brain RNA Isoform Browser
Brain RNA Isoform Browser is a public web application for visualizing individual RNA isoform expression in aged human frontal cortex tissue. Introduced in heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform (Heberle et al., Nature Biotechnology 2024) and hosted at ebbertlab.com/brain_rna_isoform_seq.html.
What it is

A Shiny web app (built with R v.4.2.1 and Rshiny v.1.7.4) that exposes the study’s summary transcriptomic data for browsing.
Lets users explore per-isoform expression — including newly discovered isoforms in medically relevant genes — across the 12 deep Oxford Nanopore long-read-rna-seq frontal-cortex samples (6 Alzheimer disease, 6 control).
Backed by bambu-quantified isoform counts against Ensembl v.107 plus the study’s newly discovered transcripts and new gene bodies.

In this wiki

A browsable companion to a human-brain long-read-rna-seq isoform atlas, supporting isoform-resolution interpretation of brain disease genes (splicing-and-disease).
Conceptually parallel to isomiga, the long-read microglia isoform atlas/browser from humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas.

Sources

heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform — the source paper (deep long-read frontal-cortex isoform discovery, quantification, and AD-vs-control differential isoform analysis).

wiki/entities/braintf.title: braintf
wiki/entities/braintf.content:
BrainTF
Introduced in loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor. A multiomic resource of transcription-factor binding and function in human postmortem brain — to date the largest direct measurement of TF occupancy in human brain tissue.
What it is

ChIP–seq occupancy maps for 106 total TFs (96 in bulk homogenate, 76 in FANS-sorted nuclei) plus histone marks (H3K4me1/me3, H3K27ac, H3K27me3, H3K9ac), generated across nine brain regions (four large: DLPFC, frontal pole, occipital lobe, cerebellum) from four neurotypical donors.
Paired multiomic layers on the same tissue/nuclei: ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, phased whole-genome sequencing and DNA methylation (WGBS); sorted populations are NeuN+ (neurons), Olig2+ (oligodendrocytes) and NeuN−/Olig2− (microglia + astrocytes).
Yields a union of ~239,361 TF-bound regions (6.94% of hg38), high-occupancy target (HOT) site definitions, per-TF motif/centrality and gene-expression-effect models, and stratified-LDSC links between TF binding and neuropsychiatric disease heritability.

In this wiki

The primary reusable deliverable of loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor and a human-brain cis-regulatory-elements / TF-occupancy resource within functional-single-cell-genomics.
Characterizes thousands of brain cCREs absent from the ENCODE registry, including neuron-specific “none” elements relevant to brain-development-neurogenomics and psychiatric risk.

Sources

loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor

wiki/entities/branaplam.title: branaplam
wiki/entities/branaplam.content:
Branaplam (NVS-SM1)
One-line: A small-molecule splicing modifier (Novartis) that promotes inclusion of exons with weak 5′ splice sites — developed for SMN2 exon-7 inclusion in SMA and also able to induce an HTT frame-shifting pseudoexon for Huntington’s-lowering.
Branaplam prefers exons with an A₋₃G₋₂A₋₁ (“AGA”) 3′-exon-end motif and acts via the spliceosome U1 snRNP at the 5′ splice site, stabilizing a bulged-out adenosine at the exon −1 position (splice-sites, splicing-therapeutics). Its specificity is not explained by the exon-end motif alone: baseline U1 snRNA–5′SS complementarity and the required A₋₁-bulge secondary structure govern which of the thousands of matching exons are actually druggable, and mutating U1 snRNA can reprogram its specificity. It is a member of the same splice-switching class as risdiplam; genome-wide candidate 5′SS were enumerated from GTEx RNA-seq via snaptron.
Referenced by

lenkeit-2026-specificity-and-exon-target-space

wiki/entities/brd9.title: brd9
wiki/entities/brd9.content:
BRD9
One-line: Bromodomain-containing protein 9, the defining subunit of the non-canonical BAF (ncBAF / GBAF) chromatin-remodelling complex, mis-spliced and degraded in SF3B1-mutant cancers.
BRD9 is a subunit unique to the ncbaf SWI/SNF chromatin-remodelling complex, where it associates with the ncBAF-specific scaffold gltscr1 (via BRD9’s DUF3512 domain). In cancers carrying mutant sf3b1, aberrant splicing introduces a poison exon into the BRD9 transcript, targeting it for nonsense-mediated-decay and depleting BRD9 protein; because BRD9/ncBAF acts as a tumour suppressor in this context, its loss contributes to transformation (see inoue-2019-spliceosomal-disruption-of-the-non-canonical). Restoring BRD9 splicing (e.g. with antisense oligonucleotides) reverses the phenotype, making this a therapeutic node in splicing-factor-mutant malignancies.
Referenced by

inoue-2019-spliceosomal-disruption-of-the-non-canonical

wiki/entities/cadd-score.title: cadd-score
wiki/entities/cadd-score.content:
CADD (Combined Annotation-Dependent Depletion)
Integrative deleteriousness score for ranking the pathogenicity of variants throughout the human genome, coding and noncoding (Rentzsch et al., Nucleic Acids Res. 2019).

Recommended (with ensembl-vep) in the paper’s functional-risk assessment to prioritize which CRISPR off-target edits are likely to have functional impact (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: mutation-effect-prediction, noncoding-variants.

wiki/entities/calvados-model.title: calvados-model
wiki/entities/calvados-model.content:
CALVADOS Model
One-line: A residue-level coarse-grained molecular model for simulating the phase separation of intrinsically disordered proteins.
CALVADOS represents each amino acid as a single bead with tuned hydrophobicity/charge parameters, allowing efficient simulation of intrinsically disordered region (IDR) behaviour and biomolecular condensate formation. In the study of cpeb4, it was used to model how reduced inclusion of the neuronal microexon me4 shifts the protein’s IDR toward a condensate-to-aggregate transition implicated in autism spectrum disorder (see garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon).
Referenced by

garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon

wiki/entities/campra.title: campra
wiki/entities/campra.content:
caMPRA (capture-based Massively Parallel Reporter Assay)
One-line: A capture-based variant of the massively-parallel-reporter-assay that uses molecular inversion probes (MIPs) to barcode and clone thousands of long (~500 bp) noncoding genomic regions for parallel enhancer testing.
What it is

Unlike conventional oligo-synthesis MPRA (limited to ~150–200 bp sequences), caMPRA captures native genomic sequences up to ~500 bp via molecular inversion probes, enabling assay of long elements such as HARs, Vista Enhancers, and conserved neural enhancers — critical because a large fraction of these elements exceed 200 bp.
Workflow: MIP-capture and barcode genomic regions → clone upstream of a minimal promoter driving a reporter (luciferase) → transfect into N2A (Neuro2A) cells → harvest at 1 or 3 days → sequence transcribed barcodes vs original plasmid barcodes to quantify enhancer activity.
A mutagenesis extension sparsely introduces random variants (error-prone PCR) into captured sequences, allowing high-throughput measurement of how single-nucleotide variants change regulatory activity.
Originally introduced by Girskis et al. (2021); applied in shin-2023-rare-variation-in-noncoding-regions to test HARs/VEs/CNEs and patient variants for ASD-relevant enhancer function.

wiki/entities/cargo-bioid.title: cargo-bioid
wiki/entities/cargo-bioid.content:
CARGO-BioID
One-line: A CRISPR-based, transposable-element-centric proximity-labeling proteomics method that captures proteins associated with the genomic loci of a repetitive TE family in living cells.
What it is
CARGO-BioID combines multiplexed CRISPR targeting (“chimeric array of gRNA oligos”, CARGO; up to 15 sgRNAs) with proximity-dependent biotin identification (BioID). Catalytically dead Cas9 (dCas9) is fused to the biotin ligase TurboID; the fusion is directed to hundreds-to-thousands of copies of a target TE (here LTR7/HERV-H), biotinylating nearby proteins for streptavidin purification and LC–MS/MS.
Notes

Applied in H1 human embryonic stem cells to target 1,815 LTR7 copies (73.5% of total); identified 144 LTR7-enriched candidate proteins.
Designed to overcome the difficulty of resolving regulatory complexes on highly repetitive, high-copy TE sequences.
sgRNAs were placed to avoid predicted TF binding motifs so the native LTR7/HERV-H interactome and epigenetic state were not disrupted.

wiki/entities/cas-offinder.title: cas-offinder
wiki/entities/cas-offinder.content:
Cas-OFFinder
Alignment-based in silico algorithm that searches a reference genome for potential Cas9 off-target sites tolerating guide RNA–target mismatches (Bae, Park & Kim, Bioinformatics 2014).

One of the earliest and most widely used sequence-similarity nomination tools; reference-genome based, so it can miss variant-dependent off-targets (contrast crisprme).
Listed in the in silico nomination column of the off-target detection catalog (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, crispor, ai-in-genomics.

wiki/entities/cas13d.title: cas13d
wiki/entities/cas13d.content:
Cas13d (CasRx)
One-line: A compact type VI-D CRISPR effector that binds and cleaves single-stranded RNA (not DNA), widely used for programmable, isoform-level RNA knockdown.
Cas13d (the RfxCas13d ortholog is marketed as CasRx) is guided by a CRISPR RNA to complementary target transcripts, where its HEPN domains cleave RNA. Because it acts on RNA rather than the genome, it enables reversible knockdown and — when guides are tiled across exon–exon junctions — isoform-specific depletion that distinguishes alternatively spliced products of the same gene (schertzer-2025-cas13d-mediated-isoform-specific-rna-knockdown-with). Guide efficiency can be predicted computationally (see tiger-model) and isoform-resolved effects visualized with dedicated tooling (isoviz). A catalytically dead variant (dCas13d) also serves as an RNA-tethering scaffold for artificial-splicing-factors.
Referenced by

isoviz
tiger-model
schertzer-2025-cas13d-mediated-isoform-specific-rna-knockdown-with

wiki/entities/cas3.title: cas3
wiki/entities/cas3.content:
Cas3
One-line: The nuclease/helicase effector recruited by the Cascade complex in type I CRISPR-Cas systems to degrade invading DNA during interference.

In type I interference, Cascade localizes invading DNA in a crRNA-dependent manner and recruits Cas3, which nicks the foreign DNA and then degrades it via 3’→5’ exonucleolytic activity (see hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance).
Its nuclease activity also contributes to generating DNA fragments captured during primed spacer acquisition.
Targeted by the phage anti-CRISPR protein AcrF3, which binds Cas3 to inhibit target degradation.

wiki/entities/cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex.title: cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex
wiki/entities/cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex.content:
Cas9–R7L10 nanocomplex
One-line: A non-viral, amphiphilic peptide–based delivery vehicle that self-assembles the CRISPR–Cas9 protein–sgRNA ribonucleoprotein into ~125 nm nanocomplexes for gene editing in post-mitotic neurons in vivo.
What it is

R7L10 is a chemically synthesized amphiphilic peptide (sequence NH2-RRRRRRRLLLLLLLLLL-COOH): a cationic poly-arginine (R7) head plus a hydrophobic poly-leucine (L10) tail, purified by C18 reverse-phase HPLC.
Mixed with Cas9 protein + sgRNA (Cas9-sgRNA RNP) at defined weight ratios (e.g. 1:1, 1:3, 1:4, 1:5 Cas9-sgRNA:R7L10), it forms stable, positively charged, ~125 nm spherical nanocomplexes (DLS/AFM/SEM; STEM-EDS shows colocalized nitrogen from peptide and phosphorus from sgRNA).
The cationic peptide condenses the RNP and promotes cellular uptake without a viral vector, avoiding genomic integration and prolonged Cas9 expression.

Key properties / results (Park et al. 2019)

Delivers functional Cas9 that persists ~1 week and is undetectable by ~3 weeks post-injection in brain — transient exposure.
Achieves efficient on-target indels (NHEJ) at Bace1 and Th in primary neurons and in vivo (WT hippocampus/midbrain), and supports HDR knock-in in human NSC-derived neurons.
Low toxicity (up to 10 µM in fibroblasts); no elevated inflammation, blood urea nitrogen toxicity, or apoptosis in treated brains.
Used therapeutically to edit Bace1 and reduce amyloid pathology in the 5xfad-mouse-model and App knock-in mice.

Related

Enables neuronal genome-editing without viruses.
Source: park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing
Off-target safety assessed with digenome-seq.

wiki/entities/cas9.title: cas9
wiki/entities/cas9.content:
Cas9
One-line: The single effector protein of type II CRISPR-Cas systems — an RNA-guided DNA endonuclease that, guided by a tracrRNA:crRNA duplex (or an engineered single-guide RNA), makes a sequence-specific double-strand break; the workhorse of CRISPR genome-editing.

In native type II immunity, Cas9’s PAM-recognizing domain selects protospacers during adaptation and recruits Cas1/Cas2/Csn2 for spacer integration; in interference the tracrRNA:crRNA duplex directs Cas9 to introduce a double-strand break in target DNA (see hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance).
Simplification into a single-guide RNA (sgRNA) fusion of tracrRNA and crRNA turned Cas9 into a programmable editing tool: DSBs are repaired by error-prone NHEJ (knockouts) or HDR (precise insertion).
Catalytically inactive variant → dcas9 for CRISPRi/CRISPRa and epigenome editing.
Off-target cleavage is the principal safety concern for therapeutic use.
Basis for pooled-crispr-screen and single-cell-crispr-screen technologies.
Mechanistic detail: two nuclease domains — HNH (cleaves the target strand) and RuvC (cleaves the nontarget strand) — give a blunt DSB ~3 nt from the 3’ G-rich PAM, gated by a 12-nt seed and complete R-loop formation (see mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic).

wiki/entities/casc3-ejc.title: casc3-ejc
wiki/entities/casc3-ejc.content:
CASC3-EJC
One-line: An alternative exon junction complex composition — the EJC core plus AKT and CASC3 — enriched at detained introns and recursively spliced exons.
The CASC3-EJC is one of the compositionally distinct “heterogeneous” EJCs central to ejc-modularity: the common core (exon-junction-complex) acquires CASC3 and AKT as peripheral factors. It promotes translation through a SELOR–EIF3 contact and drives AKT-mediated, UPF2-independent nonsense-mediated-decay, contrasting with the UPF2-dependent rnps1-ejc branch. CASC3-EJCs are enriched at detained-introns and recursively spliced exons, where they can repress RS exon inclusion (see abshire-2025-gene-regulation-through-exon-junction).
Referenced by

abshire-2025-gene-regulation-through-exon-junction

wiki/entities/casein-kinase-1.title: casein-kinase-1
wiki/entities/casein-kinase-1.content:
Casein Kinase 1 (CK1)
One-line: CK1 (and CK2) kinases that phosphorylate SF3-complex splicing factors; their action is blocked by polyamine “metabolic shielding.”
Casein kinase 1 phosphorylates an acidic phosphorylatable motif (S365/S367/S369) on the U2 snRNP splicing factor sf3a3. In the polyamine-shielding model, polyamines (spermidine/spermine) bind this same acidic motif and sterically shield it, preventing CK1-mediated phosphorylation and thereby tuning alternative-splicing (see zabala-letona-2026-polyamine-dependent-metabolic-shielding-regulates-alternative). Perturbing polyamine biosynthesis (e.g. via AMD1 inhibition) removes this shield and alters CK1 access to SF3 substrates.
Referenced by

zabala-letona-2026-polyamine-dependent-metabolic-shielding-regulates-alternative

wiki/entities/casgevy.title: casgevy
wiki/entities/casgevy.content:
Casgevy
FDA-approved CRISPR–Cas9 ex vivo gene therapy for sickle cell disease (2023) and β-thalassemia (2024) — the first landmark FDA approval of a CRISPR therapy (based on Frangoul et al., N. Engl. J. Med. 2021).


Used in the paper as the exemplar of variant-aware off-target discovery: reference-genome in silico predictions were not observed in cell assays, but additional off-targets tied to a non-reference single-nucleotide variant were found via crisprme and validated in variant-carrying cell lines — motivating patient-population variant-aware nomination (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).


Its origin story — sickle cell disease → fetal-haemoglobin protection → BCL11A erythroid-enhancer disruption in CD34⁺ HSCs → first approved CRISPR medicine — is narrated in the book chapter pao-2025-dawn-of-gene-editing.


Related: genome-editing, crisprme, pao-2025-dawn-of-gene-editing.

wiki/entities/cast-seq.title: cast-seq
wiki/entities/cast-seq.content:
CAST-Seq
Chromosomal Aberration analysis by Single Targeted linker-mediated PCR Sequencing — a method for quantitative evaluation of chromosomal rearrangements (translocations, large structural variants) in gene-edited human stem cells (Turchiano et al., Cell Stem Cell 2021).

Placed in the paper’s structural-variant verification column alongside WGS, LR-PCR, and optical genome mapping — targeting the large, on-target-originating rearrangements that sequence-similarity off-target assays miss (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, 3d-genome-and-disease.

wiki/entities/castle.title: castle
wiki/entities/castle.content:
casTLE
One-line: A maximum-likelihood statistical framework used to estimate the maximum gene-perturbation effect size and an associated significance score from pooled CRISPR screen sgRNA-enrichment data.
What it is
casTLE (Cas9 high-Throughput maximum Likelihood Estimator) models the enrichment/depletion of multiple sgRNAs per gene against negative-control sgRNAs to call gene-level hits in pooled CRISPR screens. Each gene receives a casTLE effect size (magnitude/direction of the perturbation) and a casTLE score (significance).
Usage in the wiki

Used in meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact to score both the interneuron-generation and interneuron-migration screens over 425 NDD genes. Candidate hits required at least two sgRNAs with effects beyond roughly twice the standard deviation of negative controls (generation screen: casTLE effect ≤ −1.57 or ≥ +1.57; migration screen: ≤ −2.5 or ≥ +2.5). Volcano plots of casTLE effect vs score identified hits such as CSDE1, SMAD4 (generation) and LNPK, TERF2, NCKAP1 (migration).

Connections

A hit-calling method for functional-single-cell-genomics / CRISPR screening analyses.

wiki/entities/change-seq.title: change-seq
wiki/entities/change-seq.content:
CHANGE-seq
Biochemical circularized-DNA off-target assay revealing genetic and epigenetic effects on genome-wide CRISPR–Cas9 activity (Lazzarotto et al., Nat. Biotechnol. 2020); a population-scale CHANGE-seq-R variant reports human genetic variation frequently affects Cas9 off-target activity (2025).

Cell-free nomination method; cataloged alongside circle-seq in the circularized-DNA family (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).
Co-author S.Q. Tsai holds the CHANGE-seq patent (US 10,920,272 B2) and has filed on CHANGE-seq-R.

Related: genome-editing, circle-seq, guide-seq.

wiki/entities/charm-epigenetic-editor.title: charm-epigenetic-editor
wiki/entities/charm-epigenetic-editor.content:
CHARM (Coupled Histone tail for Autoinhibition Release of Methyltransferase)
One-line: A compact, enzyme-free epigenetic editor that silences target genes through programmable DNA methylation by recruiting and activating the cell’s own DNMT3A.
CHARM is a fusion of a histone H3 tail and the noncatalytic Dnmt3l C-terminal domain (D3L), joined to a programmable DNA-binding domain (dCas9, zinc-finger protein/ZFP, or TALE). Rather than overexpressing the potentially cytotoxic DNMT3A catalytic domain (D3A) as in CRISPRoff, CHARM recruits the endogenous full-length DNMT3A via D3L and releases its autoinhibition using the unmethylated H3 tail (which engages the DNMT3A ADD domain that reads H3K4me0). This yields durable 5-methyl-CpG deposition and heritable transcriptional silencing.
Key properties:

Enzyme-free / small: dropping the bacterial dCas9 and D3A domain lets ZFP-based CHARMs (ZFcharm) fit within the ~5 kb AAV packaging limit, enabling single-vector and multiplexed delivery.
DNA-binding-agnostic: works with CRISPR-dCas9, ZFPs, and TALEs.
Self-silencing: installing the ZFP binding motif around the CHARM promoter lets the editor deactivate itself after silencing its target, temporally limiting expression to reduce antigenicity and off-target risk.
Independent of KRAB: DNA methylation alone suffices for silencing, though KRAB variants (Kv1/Kv2) were also built.

Introduced in Neumann et al., Science 2024, where AAV-delivered ZFcharm targeting Prnp achieved up to ~80% brainwide reduction of prion protein in mice.

wiki/entities/chd8.title: chd8
wiki/entities/chd8.content:
CHD8 (chromodomain helicase DNA-binding protein 8)
A major ATP-dependent chromatin remodeler and one of the most frequently mutated, highest-confidence autism risk genes. Recurrent de novo loss-of-function CHD8 mutations were identified by large-scale exome sequencing of individuals with ASD; the human phenotype includes autism, macrocephaly, and gastrointestinal disturbances.
What it is

Function. CHD8 is a chromatin remodeler that regulates gene expression across many neural and nonneural cell types, controlling proliferation and differentiation of progenitors for excitatory (pyramidal) neurons, GABAergic interneurons, oligodendrocytes, and astrocytes.
Dose sensitivity. Homozygous Chd8 deletion in mice is embryonic lethal; heterozygous (haploinsufficient) mutants are viable and recapitulate key human features — behavioral abnormalities, macrocephaly, and gastrointestinal disturbances. Outside the CNS, CHD8 deficiency is linked to slenderness and infertility.

Mouse alleles used in nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8

Chd8+/F — conditional loss-of-function allele with exons 11–13 flanked by loxP; Cre converts it to the null Chd8+/–. Crossed to Nestin-Cre, Nestin-CreERT2, CAG-CreER, or Olig1-Cre to control where/when heterozygosity is induced.
Chd8+/LSL — a conditional knock-in “rescue” allele made by CRISPR/Cas9: a splice-acceptor + loxP-flanked stop (LSL) cassette inserted between exons 8 and 9 silences one allele by default (mimicking haploinsufficiency), and Cre restores near–wild-type CHD8 expression. Lets the authors turn CHD8 back on at a chosen time/cell type.

Role in this wiki

The paper uses these alleles to pin the autism-relevant action of CHD8 to a specific window (midfetal, ~E14.5) and cell population (ventral / ganglionic-eminence progenitors of interneurons and oligodendrocytes), where haploinsufficiency accelerates cell-cycle exit and differentiation.
A canonical example of a chromatin-remodeler ASD gene, complementary to synaptic (scn2a-conditional-ki-mouse) and broader transcriptomic (gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across) accounts of autism.

Sources

nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8

wiki/entities/chm1-centromere-assemblies.title: chm1-centromere-assemblies
wiki/entities/chm1-centromere-assemblies.content:
CHM1 complete centromere assemblies
What: The first complete, telomere-to-telomere assembly of all centromeres from a second human genome, the complete hydatidiform mole cell line CHM1, produced by Logsdon et al. (Nature 2024).
Summary

Built from ~66-fold PacBio HiFi and ~98-fold Oxford Nanopore ultra-long data using a hifiasm backbone, singly unique nucleotide k-mer (SUNK) barcoding to bridge contigs with ONT reads, and HiFi polishing, yielding an estimated base accuracy >99.9999% (QV > 60).
Validated independently with VerityMap, GAVISUNK and a Verkko assembly (>99.99% pairwise identity).
Provides an allelically distinct counterpart to the T2T-CHM13 centromeres, enabling the first two-genome baseline of human centromeric single-nucleotide, structural and epigenetic (DNA methylation / CENP-A kinetochore) variation.
Accompanied by 31 orthologous complete centromere assemblies for six chromosomes from chimpanzee, orangutan and macaque for comparative/evolutionary analysis.

Notes

Both CHM1 and CHM13 derive from hydatidiform moles (single paternal haplotype), which simplifies assembly but represents a developmental aberration; neither genome alone captures the full extent of human centromeric diversity across HPRC/HGSVC genomes.

wiki/entities/chrombpnet.title: chrombpnet
wiki/entities/chrombpnet.content:
ChromBPNet
ChromBPNet is a lightweight, base-resolution deep-learning DNA sequence model of chromatin accessibility (DNase-seq and ATAC-seq) that detects and deconvolves assay-specific enzyme cleavage bias (DNase-I nuclease, Tn5 transposase) from the underlying regulatory sequence determinants of accessibility. By “bias factorizing” the signal, it recovers compact transcription-factor motif lexicons, cooperative motif syntax, and precision TF footprints, and predicts the quantitative effects of noncoding genetic variants on accessibility, pioneer TF binding, and reporter activity. It is competitive with much larger contemporary sequence models (e.g. Enformer) at variant-effect prediction while remaining interpretable via per-base contribution scores. Introduced by Pampari, Shcherbina, Kundaje et al. (Stanford), bioRxiv 2024.

wiki/entities/circle-seq.title: circle-seq
wiki/entities/circle-seq.content:
CIRCLE-seq
Biochemical, circularized-DNA off-target nomination assay — a highly sensitive in vitro screen for genome-wide CRISPR–Cas9 nuclease off-targets using circularized genomic DNA (Tsai et al., Nat. Methods 2017).

A naked/cell-free biochemical method: highly sensitive for nomination but, like other naked-DNA assays, prone to overestimating true off-targets, so it requires cellular verification (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).
Co-author S.Q. Tsai holds the CIRCLE-seq patent (US 9,850,484 B2).

Related: genome-editing, change-seq, guide-seq.

wiki/entities/clk-kinase.title: clk-kinase
wiki/entities/clk-kinase.content:
Clk Kinase
One-line: CDC-like kinases (Clk1/Clk4) that phosphorylate SR proteins and regulate the splicing of a specific set of detained introns.
Clk kinases control detained-intron (DI) splicing by phosphorylating SR proteins such as srsf4. Pharmacological inhibition with the Clk1/4 inhibitor CB19 significantly changed >300 DIs within 2 hours — roughly half showing increased splicing and half increased detention — especially in genes encoding splicing-factors and RNA-processing proteins, coupling splicing rate to gene expression (see boutz-2015-detained-introns-are-a-novel). This positions Clk activity as a rapid, regulated switch over the detained-intron pool.
Referenced by

boutz-2015-detained-introns-are-a-novel

wiki/entities/cmonge.title: cmonge
wiki/entities/cmonge.content:
CMonge
CMonge (Conditional Monge Gap) is a conditional neural optimal-transport
library for single-cell-perturbation-modeling, introduced in
driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable.
What it is

Learns a single global transport map conditioned on arbitrary covariates
(drug, dose, combinations) via the Monge Gap regularizer, instead of training one
model per condition.
Encodes conditions by RDKit structure fingerprints or data-driven mode-of-action
embeddings; combinations handled with permutation-invariant DeepSets pooling.
Open-source (PyPI cmonge); very lightweight (~23K–580K params vs chemCPA’s
~1.37M).

Standing

Matches condition-specific models in-sample and generalizes to unseen drugs
from structure (SciPlex OOS R² up to 0.900), beating chemCPA especially on
distributional metrics.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence method; sibling to txpert and
signature-tool in the single-cell cluster; contrasts with heavyweight
single-cell foundation-models on parameter efficiency.

Sources

driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable — the method paper

wiki/entities/cosmic-database.title: cosmic-database
wiki/entities/cosmic-database.content:
COSMIC (Catalogue of Somatic Mutations in Cancer)
Curated database of somatic cancer mutations (Tate et al., Nucleic Acids Res. 2019), used as the reference for identifying whether a CRISPR off-target falls in or near a known cancer-causing gene.

In the paper’s evaluation framework, COSMIC anchors the cancer-risk assessment: cross-reference candidate off-targets against known oncogenes/tumor-suppressors and apply zero tolerance for such edits, with deep sequencing to lower detection thresholds (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, ensembl-vep, cadd-score.

wiki/entities/cpeb4.title: cpeb4
wiki/entities/cpeb4.content:
CPEB4
One-line: Cytoplasmic polyadenylation element-binding protein 4, an RNA-binding translational regulator whose neuronal microexon mis-splicing is implicated in autism.
CPEB4 controls cytoplasmic polyadenylation and translation of target mRNAs. Reduced inclusion of its 24-nucleotide neuronal microexon (me4) — part of the neuron-specific splicing program driven by srrm4 — alters the balance of its intrinsically disordered region, driving a condensate-to-aggregate conversion in autism spectrum disorder. This biophysical transition was characterized with the calvados-model coarse-grained simulation (see garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon).
Referenced by

garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon

wiki/entities/cpf1.title: cpf1
wiki/entities/cpf1.content:
Cpf1 (Cas12a)
One-line: The single crRNA-guided effector protein of type V-A (class 2) CRISPR-Cas systems — a Cas12-family endonuclease with dual function in both crRNA maturation and target DNA cleavage.

Unlike type II Cas9, Cpf1 needs no tracrRNA: it processes its own premature crRNAs and then uses the mature crRNA to locate and cleave target DNA (see hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance).
Type V systems recognize the PAM upstream of the protospacer (as in type I), distinct from Cas9’s downstream PAM.
An alternative RNA-guided nuclease for genome-editing applications.
Uses a single RuvC-like domain plus a novel second nuclease domain, recognizes a 5’ T-rich PAM, and generates staggered cuts with 4-5-nt 5’ overhangs (sticky ends) — contrasting Cas9’s blunt cut (see mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic).

wiki/entities/crispey.title: crispey
wiki/entities/crispey.content:
CRISPEY (Cas9 Retron precISE Parallel Editing via homologY)
Introduced in sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel. A pooled CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing platform that couples a bacterial retron to an sgRNA so each cell generates its own in-vivo single-stranded repair donor, enabling highly efficient, single-base-resolution precise editing of thousands of variants in parallel.
What it is

Chimeric retron-guide RNA: an E. coli Ec86 retron scaffold joined at the 3’ end to an SpCas9 sgRNA, with part of the retron replaced by a ~100-nt donor sequence carrying the desired mutation flanked by 50-bp homology arms.
The retron reverse transcriptase (Ec86-RT) reverse-transcribes the donor segment into multi-copy single-stranded DNA (msDNA) that stays covalently tethered to the guide RNA, concentrating an HDR-preferred ssDNA donor at the Cas9 cut site.
Components: genomically integrated SpCas9 and Ec86-RT under galactose induction (pGAL7, Pol II), with the retron-guide on a low-copy plasmid flanked by self-cleaving ribozymes to strip cap and poly(A).
Performance: up to 100% editing at ADE2 (0/95 undesired mutations), 765-nt insertion at 92% efficiency (<0.01% error), ~96% efficiency / 0% NHEJ at validation loci.
As a screen: array-synthesized guide/donor oligo libraries are cloned to low-copy plasmid, transformed and edited as a pool, then competed in growth so each edited strain’s relative fitness is read from guide/donor sequencing over time.
Advantages over prior high-throughput yeast editors: no reliance on high-copy plasmid dsDNA donors, efficient ssDNA HDR, donor-to-guide tethering, and no need for NEJ1 knockout or linearized vectors; limited mainly by the requirement of a nearby NGG PAM (~70% of BY/RM SNPs targetable).

In this wiki

A reusable precise-editing resource within the functional-single-cell-genomics topic; applied here to map fitness effects of noncoding-variants and cis-regulatory-elements in yeast.
Complements reporter-based functional-variant screens such as deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory by editing variants in their native chromosomal context.

Sources

sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel

wiki/entities/crispor.title: crispor
wiki/entities/crispor.content:
CRISPOR
Scoring-based in silico guide RNA selection tool that evaluates and integrates on-target and off-target scoring algorithms to rank candidate guides and predicted off-target sites (Haeussler et al., Genome Biol. 2016).

Represents the “scoring” family of nomination methods, between raw alignment (cas-offinder) and machine/deep learning (elevation-crispr, deepcrispr).
Cataloged among in silico off-target nomination tools (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, ai-in-genomics.

wiki/entities/crisprbrain.title: crisprbrain
wiki/entities/crisprbrain.content:
CRISPRbrain
One-line: A data commons, established by the Kampmann laboratory, for sharing and comparing CRISPR-based functional-genomics screens performed in differentiated human cell types (including iPSC-derived neurons).
What it is

A public resource (crisprbrain.org) intended to enable comparison of functional-genomics screen results across different differentiated human cell types, so that cell-type-specific roles of genes in health and disease can be elucidated (see kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for).
Motivated by the finding that gene essentiality and knockdown phenotypes differ markedly between cell types (e.g. neuronal essential genes differ from those of iPSCs and cancer lines), making cross-cell-type comparison scientifically valuable.

Connections

Aggregates outputs of pooled-crispr-screen and single-cell-crispr-screen approaches applied in iPSC-derived cells.
Supports the review’s thesis on CRISPRa screens in neurological disease models.

wiki/entities/crisprme.title: crisprme
wiki/entities/crisprme.content:
CRISPRme
Variant-aware in silico CRISPR off-target prediction tool that accounts for human population polymorphisms rather than relying solely on a single reference genome (Cancellieri et al., Nat. Genet. 2022).

Motivated by the observation that reference-genome-only predictions miss off-targets created by individual/population sequence variation.
In the Casgevy development case, CRISPRme surfaced additional off-targets (associated with a single-nucleotide variant differing from the reference) that were then validated in variant-carrying cell lines — off-targets the original reference-based predictions had missed (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).
Positioned in the paper’s Table 1 among in silico nomination tools alongside cas-offinder and crispor, and cited as the basis for “consider patient population genomic variants” in the evaluation framework.

Related: genome-editing, ai-in-genomics, noncoding-variants.

wiki/entities/crisprot-database.title: crisprot-database
wiki/entities/crisprot-database.content:
CRISPRofT
Comprehensive database of CRISPR/Cas off-targets (Wang et al., Nucleic Acids Res. 2025).

Cited as an example of a field-wide catalog of genomic changes with functional consequences — the kind of resource the authors argue should be built (and could fuel machine learning) to map off-target edits to avoid, while protecting patient genetic privacy (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, cosmic-database, ai-in-genomics.

wiki/entities/crossdna.title: crossdna
wiki/entities/crossdna.content:
CrossDNA
CrossDNA is a parameter-efficient DNA language model
that explicitly and dynamically models cross-strand (forward/reverse-complement)
interactions, introduced in
yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions.
What it is

Dual-branch model with a rotating forward/RC “cross-view” schedule, a Comba +
sliding-window-attention backbone, a TokenBridge module exchanging
cross-strand context, gated fusion, and EMA self-distillation.
Single-nucleotide tokenization; pretrained on GRCh38 with a masked-LM objective;
variants from 408K to 519M parameters.

Standing

Orientation-robust (forward vs RC scores tightly aligned) without augmentation or
hard equivariance; a 408K variant tops the Genomic Benchmark (88.2%) against
models 2–4 orders of magnitude larger (Evo2 7B, AlphaGenome, NTv2).
Weak at individual-level/eQTL effect sizes (single-reference-genome pretraining).

In this wiki

A genomics foundation-models instance (ai-in-genomics); connects to the
splicing-code / spliceai sequence-to-function lineage (reports splice-site
prediction, with large gains in antisense regions).

Sources

yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions — the method paper

wiki/entities/crosssplice.title: crosssplice
wiki/entities/crosssplice.content:
CrosSplice
CrosSplice is a pipeline for identifying rare splice-site-creating variants (SSCVs)
from cross-tissue transcriptome data, introduced in
yano-2025-crosssplice-a-pipeline-for (Shiraishi lab).
What it is

Combines SpliceAI prediction + allele-frequency filtering with statistical
association testing of novel-junction usage, aggregated across many tissues
(GTEx), to detect rare and tissue-specific SSCVs that single-tissue sQTL or
prediction-only methods miss.
Output: an SSCV catalogue (1,743 from GTEx; 65% deep-intronic) framed as a platform
for antisense-oligonucleotide (ASO) target discovery in rare disease.

In this wiki

A rare/deep-intronic splice-variant discovery tool in the splicing-methods /
splicing-therapeutics space; shares the prediction core with spliceai and the
variant→splicing goal with absplice2, adding cross-tissue confirmation + ASO framing.
Disease/cross-tissue oriented (no developmental axis).

Sources

yano-2025-crosssplice-a-pipeline-for

wiki/entities/ct-sleb.title: ct-sleb
wiki/entities/ct-sleb.content:
CT-SLEB
One-line: A scalable, model-free method for building multiancestry polygenic risk scores that combines two-dimensional clumping-and-thresholding (CT), empirical Bayes (EB) effect-size estimation, and superlearning (SL).
What it is
CT-SLEB generates ancestry-specific PRSs from multiancestry GWAS summary statistics via three steps: (1) 2D CT to select SNPs with either EUR-shared or target-specific effects, clumped using population-matched LD; (2) EB posterior estimation of effect sizes that borrows information across populations via a bias-variance trade-off; and (3) an SL model (Lasso, ridge, neural network) combining PRSs across P-value thresholds. Requires separate training, tuning, and validation datasets.
Key facts

Introduced by Haoyu Zhang et al., Nature Genetics 2023 (doi:10.1038/s41588-023-01501-z).
~25-91x faster than PRS-CSx; can scale to ~19 million SNPs vs HM3-restricted Bayesian methods.
Especially strong for AFR-origin populations (e.g. +140% and +66.4% adjusted R2 vs PRS-CSx for log(TGs) and LDL in GLGC AFR).
R implementation uses SuperLearner v.2.0-26.

wiki/entities/ctcf.title: ctcf
wiki/entities/ctcf.content:
CTCF
One-line: CCCTC-binding factor, a DNA-binding protein best known as a chromatin insulator that also directly regulates alternative splicing by promoting local RNA Pol II pausing.
Beyond its insulator role, CTCF has an intragenic “non-insulator” function: bound within a gene body it acts as a roadblock that slows elongating RNA Pol II, giving the co-transcriptional spliceosome more time to recognize a weak upstream splice site and thereby promoting exon inclusion — a concrete instance of the kinetic-coupling model of cotranscriptional-splicing. This was demonstrated at the CD45 (PTPRC) exon 5 locus, where CTCF binding promotes exon 5 inclusion, and where DNA methylation blocks CTCF binding to reciprocally reduce inclusion, linking heritable epigenetic marks to alternative-splicing (see shukla-2011-ctcf-promoted-rna-polymerase-ii-pausing).
Referenced by

shukla-2011-ctcf-promoted-rna-polymerase-ii-pausing

wiki/entities/ctwas.title: ctwas
wiki/entities/ctwas.content:
cTWAS (causal-TWAS)
A statistical method that sharpens TWAS by
fine-mapping genes and variants jointly: it takes GWAS summary statistics plus
QTL-derived prediction weights and an LD reference, then computes a posterior
inclusion probability (PIP) that each gene (or variant) is causal for a trait —
controlling the confounding (LD and pleiotropy) that makes standard TWAS over-call
genes. A gene’s PIP can be aggregated across multiple conditions.
In this wiki

Used as the disease-mapping engine on activity-dependent neuron QTLs
(liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron): feeding stimulation-specific
eQTLs/caQTLs/ASoC into cTWAS prioritized NPD
risk genes — 48 SCZ candidates (PIP>0.5), most of them “dynamic” (higher PIP
in stimulated than resting states). Surfaced CPT1C
(cholesterol-and-brain-disorders) and recovered credible SCZ genes
(FOXN2, ZNF823) that GTEx-brain-derived TWAS missed.

Connections

A causal-inference refinement of TWAS
(cf. isoform-level isotwas); its power here comes from the context-specific
QTLs of neuronal-activation, and it operationalizes the
psychiatric-genetics hunt for causal genes that direct functional assays
(pooled-crispr-screen) then validate.

Sources

liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron

wiki/entities/cufflinks.title: cufflinks
wiki/entities/cufflinks.content:
Cufflinks
Cufflinks is an open-source C++ program that assembles full-length transcripts from paired-end RNA-Seq alignments without relying on prior gene annotations and probabilistically estimates isoform abundances in FPKM. Introduced in trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification.
What it is

Annotation-free comparative transcriptome assembler: reduces assembly to a minimum path cover of an “overlap graph” of compatible fragments, via a constructive proof of Dilworth’s Theorem (solved as a maximum matching in a weighted bipartite reachability graph). Does not require an ORF, so it can recover noncoding isoforms.
Abundance estimation: a generative statistical model in which each fragment’s observation probability is a linear function of transcript abundances; uses the fragment-length distribution to assign paired-end fragments to isoforms. Reports FPKM (extends the unpaired Jiang–Wong model to paired-end). MAP estimates and 95% confidence intervals obtained by Bayesian importance sampling.
Takes spliced alignments (e.g., from TopHat) as input. Ships with companion utilities Cuffcompare (structural comparison of assemblies against annotations) and Cuffdiff (differential expression testing). Distributed under the Boost License; runs on Linux and Mac OS X (version 0.8.0 at publication).
Edges in the reachability graph are weighted by the percent-spliced-in metric to phase distant exons; arbitrates between equally parsimonious assemblies via minimum-cost maximum matching.

In this wiki

A foundational splicing-methods tool for isoform-level RNA-Seq quantification and novel-transcript discovery; conceptual predecessor to later differential-splicing tools like rmats.
Uses the percent-spliced-in metric to weight its assembly graph.

Sources

trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification

wiki/entities/cvcl.title: cvcl
wiki/entities/cvcl.content:
CVCL (Child’s View for Contrastive Learning)
One-line: A self-supervised multimodal neural network that learns grounded word-referent mappings from one child’s egocentric head-camera recordings by contrastively aligning a vision encoder and a language encoder.
What it is

A model instantiating cross-situational associative learning through modern multimodal-learning: two modality-specific neural networks (a vision encoder and a language encoder) trained with a contrastive objective that pulls together embeddings of temporally co-occurring video frames and utterances while pushing apart non-co-occurring pairs.
Trained self-supervised on the SAYCam-S dataset (one child, 6 to 25 months; ~600,000 frames paired with 37,500 transcribed utterances from 61 hours) with no outside labels or predefined referents.

Key properties

Acquires many word-referent mappings from developmentally realistic, individual (not aggregate) data; 61.6% accuracy on the 22-concept Labeled-S evaluation, comparable within-distribution to a much larger web-scale CLIP model.
Enables zero-shot generalization to novel visual exemplars and out-of-distribution stimuli (Konkle Objects, 64 categories).
Aligns visual and linguistic conceptual systems and forms emergent multicluster word representations despite one vector per word; partial referent localization via Grad-CAM.

Availability

Pretrained models and code released at github.com/wkvong/multimodalbaby; SAYCam dataset accessible to authorized users via Databrary.

Appears in

vong-2024-grounded-language-acquisition-through

wiki/entities/d-and-d-seq.title: d-and-d-seq
wiki/entities/d-and-d-seq.content:
D&D-seq
D&D-seq (docking and deamination followed by sequencing) is a single-cell immuno-tethering technology for profiling DNA-protein interactions, introduced in chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory.
What it is

Couples a secondary, antibody-binding nanobody to the double-stranded DNA cytosine deaminase DddA (engineered split DddA11; nb-DddA). The fusion docks onto a primary antibody bound to a target TF or chromatin remodeler and deaminates nearby cytosines (C→U), recorded as C-to-T edits on sequencing.
Split-enzyme switch: nb-DddA_NT is inactive until the DddA_CT peptide (+ Zn2+) reconstitutes catalysis, preventing nonspecific editing — enabling capture of weak or transient binding that high-salt tagmentation (CUT&Tag) misses.
Readout is integrable with standard single-cell multi-omics: scATAC-seq (10x), WGS (PTA/DLP+), Paired-seq/SHARE-seq, 10x Multiome, and single-cell genotyping (GoT-ChA → D&D-GoT-ChA).
Produces a bimodal “molecular footprint” of edits centered on motif sites; an on-target peak classifier (lasso, test AUC 0.84) scores real signal.

In this wiki

A functional-single-cell-genomics method extending single-cell mapping of cis-regulatory-elements to weak/transient factors and to inaccessible chromatin; its CTCF profiles feed C.Origami for 3d-genome (Hi-C) prediction.
Outperforms uliCUT&RUN / scCUT&Tag on factor specificity (FRiP ~0.57 CTCF vs ~0.13–0.22).

Sources

chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory — the method paper

wiki/entities/darts.title: darts
wiki/entities/darts.content:
DARTS
DARTS (Deep-learning Augmented RNA-seq analysis of Transcript Splicing) is a computational framework that integrates a deep-learning prediction of differential alternative splicing with empirical RNA-seq evidence via Bayesian inference, enabling reliable splicing analysis even at modest sequencing coverage. Introduced in zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis.
What it is

Two components: the DARTS DNN, a deep neural network predicting differential splicing between two conditions from exon-specific cis sequence features plus trans RBP mRNA-expression levels; and the DARTS BHT, a Bayesian hypothesis test with a spike-and-slab prior.
BHT(flat): uninformative prior, RNA-seq data only (equivalent to a likelihood-ratio test); used to generate high-confidence training labels.
BHT(info): folds the DNN prediction in as an informative prior, with the largest accuracy gains on low-coverage / low-expression events.
Trained on ENCODE RBP-knockdown RNA-seq and Roadmap Epigenomics data; extended to exon skipping, alternative 5’/3’ splice sites, and retained introns.
Open source (Python/R, Keras DNN) at github.com/Xinglab/DARTS, with trained model parameters and predictive features provided.

In this wiki

Built on top of rmats for splicing event detection and read counting, and benchmarked against rMATS, MISO, and SUPPA2.
Quantifies and tests changes in percent-spliced-in (psi / exon-inclusion level).
A learned, data-driven splicing-regulation model contributing to the splicing-code effort; complements sequence-only predictors like spliceai by additionally conditioning on RBP expression.

Sources

zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis

wiki/entities/dcas9.title: dcas9
wiki/entities/dcas9.content:
dCas9 (dead Cas9)
One-line: A catalytically inactive (“dead”) cas9 variant that binds a target DNA sequence without cleaving it, used as a programmable DNA-targeting scaffold for transcriptional and epigenome control.

Mutations in Cas9’s nuclease motifs yield dCas9, which binds but does not cut (see hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance).
CRISPRi: dCas9 bound to a promoter/ORF physically blocks RNA polymerase and represses transcription; can be fused to a transcriptional repressor (e.g. KRAB) for stronger silencing.
CRISPRa: dCas9 fused to a transcriptional activator recruits RNA polymerase to induce gene expression.
Epigenome editing: dCas9 fused to effectors such as the acetyltransferase p300 (activation) or methylation machinery (silencing) enables site-specific chromatin modification.
Enables reversible gene knock-downs preferred over knockouts (e.g. for essential genes).

wiki/entities/deepc.title: deepc
wiki/entities/deepc.content:
DeepC
What: A transfer-learning deep neural network that predicts 3D genome-architecture (Hi-C-style chromatin interactions) directly from ~1-Mb windows of DNA sequence schwessinger-2020-deepc-predicting-3d-genome-folding.
DeepC bridges base-pair resolution to megabase-scale structure, letting a single sequence-based model call topologically-associating-domains boundaries at high resolution, dissect the sequence determinants of folding, and predict the impact of variation from large structural rearrangements down to single SNPs.
How it works
Two-phase training with transfer learning. Phase I: a convolutional module (5 conv layers, architecture adapted from DeepSEA) is pretrained on a compendium of 936 chromatin-feature datasets (ENCODE/Roadmap peaks — open chromatin, CTCF, histone marks) to learn regulatory sequence patterns from 1-kb windows. Phase II: those learned filters are transferred and frozen-then-refined, and a new module — ten dilated, gated 1D convolutional layers with residuals plus a fully connected layer — is trained ab initio to predict Hi-C interactions from a 1-Mb + 1-bin sequence input. The Hi-C target is percentile-normalized by genomic distance (the “skeleton”) in a pyramid scheme and predicted as a regression over interaction bins up to ~1 Mb. Final models have ~60M parameters; the same pretrained weights seed every cell-type model.
Significance & limits
DeepC learns a grammar of genome folding beyond CTCF orientation — saliency scores mark CTCF sites and active promoters, and an in silico deletion screen shows promoters and enhancers (not just CTCF) shape folding. It recapitulates a known ~30-kb CTCF-deletion domain fusion (validated by 5C) and prioritizes causal asthma SNPs at ORMDL3. Limits: de-emphasizes the punctate apex of CTCF loops (loop-extrusion mechanics like cohesin processivity are not sequence-encoded), overpredicts interdomain stripes, and predicts lamina-associated repressive domains poorly. A parallel model, Akita (Fudenberg et al.), shares the convolutional base but differs in encoding and architecture.
Sources

schwessinger-2020-deepc-predicting-3d-genome-folding — introduces deepC, its transfer-learning architecture, validation, and variant-effect predictions.

wiki/entities/deepcrispr.title: deepcrispr
wiki/entities/deepcrispr.content:
DeepCRISPR
Deep-learning method for CRISPR guide RNA design and off-target prediction (Chuai et al., Genome Biol. 2018), cited as the deep-learning exemplar in the in silico off-target nomination toolkit.

Sits at the machine-learning end of the nomination spectrum with elevation-crispr, contrasted against alignment (cas-offinder) and scoring (crispor) approaches (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, ai-in-genomics, foundation-models.

wiki/entities/deltasplice.title: deltasplice
wiki/entities/deltasplice.content:
DeltaSplice
One-line: A deep-learning splicing variant-effect model, used as a splicing baseline for benchmarking sequence-to-function predictors.
DeltaSplice predicts the effect of genetic variants on splicing. In the AlphaGenome evaluation it served as one of the splicing baselines against which the unified alphagenome model’s splice-site/usage/junction predictions were compared, alongside pangolin and spliceai (see avsec-2026-advancing-regulatory-variant-effect-prediction).
Referenced by

avsec-2026-advancing-regulatory-variant-effect-prediction

wiki/entities/depict-viewer.title: depict-viewer
wiki/entities/depict-viewer.content:
DEPICT viewer (DEveloPIng Cortex Transcriptome viewer)
Introduced in walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and. A web portal for browsing the prenatal (mid-gestational) human-brain eQTL and sQTL atlas, by gene or SNP at the summary level.
What it is

A public web browser built to give easier access to the developmental-cortex eQTL and sQTL maps generated from 201 mid-gestational human brains.
Serves summary-level data: users can query by gene or by SNP to retrieve expression and splice QTL associations in developing human cortex.
Backs a splicing-qtl/eQTL resource whose full results are also released as supplemental tables; individual-level raw data were deposited in dbGaP.
Hosted at labs.dgsom.ucla.edu/geschwind/pages/eqtl-browser (Geschwind lab, UCLA); the browser was built by Sandeep Deverasetty.

In this wiki

It is the browsable deliverable of walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, the first prenatal-brain splicing-qtl atlas, supporting variant interpretation for splicing-and-disease in neurodevelopment.

Sources

walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and

wiki/entities/desmodus-rotundus.title: desmodus-rotundus
wiki/entities/desmodus-rotundus.content:
Desmodus rotundus (Vampire Bat)
One-line: The common vampire bat, which achieves infrared sensation through tissue-specific alternative splicing of the heat-sensing channel TRPV1.
Desmodus rotundus detects infrared radiation from warm-blooded prey not via a new receptor but through trigeminal-ganglion-specific alternative splicing of trpv1. A truncated short isoform (TRPV1-S), produced by inclusion of a stop-codon-bearing exon 14a, lowers the heat-activation threshold to ~30 °C and makes up 35–46% of TRPV1 transcripts in the trigeminal ganglion but <3% in dorsal root ganglia. This is a striking case of regulated alternative-splicing — rather than gene duplication — driving sensory/evolutionary adaptation, with the Trpv1 splice architecture conserved across Laurasiatheria (see gracheva-2011-ganglion-specific-splicing-of-trpv1-underlies).
Referenced by

gracheva-2011-ganglion-specific-splicing-of-trpv1-underlies

wiki/entities/devbrainhub.title: devbrainhub
wiki/entities/devbrainhub.content:
DevBrainHub
Introduced in wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform. A web portal (devbrainhub.gandallab.org) for browsing the cross-ancestry atlas of gene, isoform, and local-splicing regulation in the developing human brain.
What it is

The public-facing resource for the PsychENCODE2 developing-brain xQTL atlas: uniformly processed gene-expression (eQTL), isoform (isoQTL), and leafcutter local-splicing (sQTL) quantitative trait loci from 672 distinct developing human neocortex donors (4–39 postconception weeks) across European, Latino/admixed American, African-American, and East/Southeast Asian ancestries.
Exposes 15,752 xQTL genes, trimester-stratified (Tri1/Tri2) and ancestry-stratified QTL maps, cross-ancestry fine-mapping, neuropsychiatric GWAS colocalizations/isoTWAS results, and developmentally regulated gene/isoform coexpression networks with module-interaction QTLs.
Extended/underlying data are also deposited on Synapse (syn50897018).

In this wiki

The primary reusable deliverable of wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform and a human developing-brain splicing-qtl resource for splicing-and-disease.
A developmental, cross-ancestry counterpart to other brain sQTL resources here such as zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls and humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas.

Sources

wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform

wiki/entities/dexseq.title: dexseq
wiki/entities/dexseq.content:
DEXSeq
DEXSeq is a statistical method and R/Bioconductor package for detecting differential exon usage between experimental conditions in RNA-Seq data, using negative-binomial generalized linear models that account for biological variability. Introduced in anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons.
What it is

Tests, per “counting bin” (exon or part of an exon, obtained by flattening overlapping transcript annotations), whether the fraction of a gene’s reads falling into that bin changes between conditions.
Fits per-gene NB GLMs and detects differential exon usage via a counting-bin x condition interaction term, tested by analysis of deviance (chi-squared likelihood-ratio test of full vs reduced model).
Estimates per-bin dispersion with the Cox-Reid adjusted profile likelihood (as in edgeR / McCarthy et al. 2012) and shares information across genes through a fitted mean-dispersion trend; uses the maximum of per-bin and fitted dispersion for conservative FDR control (Benjamini-Hochberg).
Distributed as an open-source Bioconductor R package plus HTSeq-based Python scripts for annotation flattening and read counting; produces HTML reports, GLM-fit and normalized-count plots, and a variance-stabilizing axis transform.
Methodologically derived from DESeq and edgeR; designed to use biological replicates, unlike contemporaneous Fisher’s-exact / single-sample approaches.

In this wiki

An exon-centric tool under splicing-methods; contrasts with isoform/event-centric tools such as miso and cufflinks, and complements read-counting event detectors like rmats.
Quantifies relative exon usage, conceptually related to percent-spliced-in.

Sources

anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons

wiki/entities/dhs-index.title: dhs-index
wiki/entities/dhs-index.content:
DHS Index (Index of human DNase I hypersensitive sites)
One-line: A consensus reference atlas of ~3.59 million archetypal human DNase I hypersensitive sites (DHSs), providing a universal, extensible numerical coordinate system and regulatory vocabulary for regulatory DNA.
What it is

Built by aggregating 76,549,656 DHSs called across 733 high-quality DNase-seq biosamples (438 cell/tissue types and states) from newly generated data plus prior ENCODE and Roadmap Epigenomics data.
Individual biosample DHSs (0.1% FDR) are integrated into 3,591,898 spatially distinct archetypal DHS-encoding elements, each with a consensus centroid (single-base summit), start/end coordinates, a core region (confidence bounds on the centroid), a confidence score, and a unique numerical identifier.
The identifier schema encodes genomic localization, allows unlimited extension to newly discovered elements, is portable to future genome builds and personal genomes, and integrates with DNase I footprints.
Each archetypal DHS derives from an average of 21 biosamples; provenance of each contributing DHS is traceable back to its biosample.

Regulatory vocabulary

Non-negative matrix factorization (k=16 components) of the 3.59M DHS × 733 biosample actuation matrix yields interpretable biological components (e.g. lymphoid, myeloid/erythroid, cardiac, neural, placental, tissue-invariant).
Each DHS gets a regulatory barcode (mixture of components) summarizing its tissue manifestations; a single dominant component gives a compact summary annotation.

Scale / key numbers

~3.6M consensus DHSs, average width 204 bp, spanning 665.57 Mb (21.55%) of the genome; cores span 197.74 Mb (6.4%).
~2.5-fold more DHSs than earlier ENCODE phases; ~5.5-fold expansion of sampled cell/tissue types/states.

wiki/entities/digenome-seq.title: digenome-seq
wiki/entities/digenome-seq.content:
Digenome-seq
In vitro digested-DNA off-target profiling method: genome-wide profiling of CRISPR–Cas9 off-target effects by whole-genome sequencing of Cas9-digested genomic DNA (Kim et al., Nat. Methods 2015). A chromatin-based extension, DIG-seq, profiles off-targets on chromatin DNA (Kim & Kim, Genome Res. 2018).

A naked/cell-free (digested-DNA) nomination method requiring cellular verification (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).
Applied to Cas9-Bace1 nanocomplex–treated 5XFAD mouse brains to screen genome-wide for off-target editing; captured the on-target double-peak with no validated off-target indels (see park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing).

Related: genome-editing, circle-seq, chromatin-accessibility.

wiki/entities/discover-seq.title: discover-seq
wiki/entities/discover-seq.content:
DISCOVER-Seq
Cell-based, double-strand-break-based assay for unbiased detection of CRISPR off-targets in vivo, leveraging recruitment of DNA-repair factor MRE11 to cut sites (Wienert et al., Science 2019).

A cellular nomination method able to work in vivo; grouped with induce-seq in the double-strand-break-based family (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, guide-seq.

wiki/entities/dscam.title: dscam
wiki/entities/dscam.content:
Dscam
What: The Drosophila Down syndrome cell adhesion molecule gene, the canonical extreme exemplar of alternative-splicing, whose mutually exclusive exon clusters can combinatorially generate tens of thousands of isoforms.
Dscam arranges alternative exons into four clusters used in a mutually exclusive manner. lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative reports this can combinatorially generate >36,000 distinct spliced mRNA isoforms — roughly three times the fly’s gene count — with the exon-6 cluster alone containing 48 mutually exclusive exons. marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative states the gene can give rise to up to 38,000 mRNA variants, half (~19,000) encoding distinct extracellular domains. Using long-read RNA sequencing, wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source identified 7,874 complete transcript isoforms of Dscam1, thousands essential for normal neural circuit development.
Selection within a cluster is dictated by RNA–RNA base pairing: a conserved “docking” site base-pairs with a complementary “selector” site upstream of each exon-6 variant, ensuring exactly one is included (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative). The hnRNP protein hrp36 enforces mutual exclusivity by repressing the other exons. This makes Dscam the best example of cis-acting RNA secondary structure controlling splice-sites choice, a mechanism beyond protein splicing-factors alone.
Why it matters
Each neuron stochastically expresses a unique subset of DSCAM isoforms; identical extracellular domains bind homophilically and trigger repulsion, enabling neuronal self-avoidance (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). It is the showcase for how alternative-splicing expands proteomic and functional diversity far beyond gene number (splicing-and-evolution).
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — >36,000 isoforms, 48 exon-6 exons, docking/selector base pairing, hrp36 repressor.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — up to 38,000 variants (~19,000 extracellular domains); self-avoidance via homophilic repulsion.
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — long-read RNA-seq found 7,874 complete Dscam1 isoforms.

wiki/entities/dt-lung.title: dt-lung
wiki/entities/dt-lung.content:
DT-Lung
DT-Lung is an ex vivo human lung digital-twin platform
(Sage Lab / University Health Network) introduced in
zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex.
What it is

Hybrid physics-informed + machine-learning models (XGBoost + GRU) trained on the
largest clinical ex vivo lung perfusion (EVLP) dataset (951 human lungs) to
forecast >75 multimodal parameters of an individual lung.
Runs “static” (baseline-only) and “dynamic” (continuously recalibrated) twins;
serves as a per-organ untreated counterfactual control.
Deliverables: code (github.com/Sage-Lab-ai/DT_Lung), models + demo data
(huggingface.co/SageLabUHN), web app (dt-lung.streamlit.app).

Standing

Detected alteplase efficacy (lower PAP, P=0.031) that a conventional control
cohort missed, with ~3× sample-size efficiency.
Single center, disease-free donors, first-3-h forecasts; data not publicly
shareable.

In this wiki

The translational/biomedical digital-twins entry of
functional-single-cell-genomics; a multimodal human-tissue platform.

Sources

zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex — the method paper

wiki/entities/easysci.title: easysci
wiki/entities/easysci.content:
EasySci
One-line: A cost-effective single-cell combinatorial-indexing platform (EasySci-RNA and EasySci-ATAC) for high-throughput, full-gene-body transcriptome and chromatin-accessibility profiling of millions of nuclei.
What it is

Optimized single-cell combinatorial indexing built on sci-RNA-seq3 and sci-ATAC-seq3, introduced by Sziraki et al. 2023 (Nature Genetics).
EasySci-RNA uses indexed oligo-dT and random-hexamer RT primers to recover full gene-body coverage, enabling combined gene-level and exon-level quantification (isoform-aware subclustering).
Prepares ~1 million single-nucleus transcriptomes for ~US $700 in library-prep cost.
EasySci-ATAC uses indexed Tn5 tagmentation compatible with the RNA ligation primers, allowing paired RNA/ATAC profiling from the same protocol.

Use in Sziraki et al. 2023

Profiled ~1,469,111 mouse single-cell transcriptomes and 376,309 chromatin-accessibility profiles across ages, sexes and genotypes, plus 118,240 human brain transcriptomes.
Yielded a mouse brain atlas of 31 main cell types and 359 subclusters, resolving rare aging- and AD-associated populations.

Related

Applied to the 5xfad-mouse-model (EOAD) and an APOE4/Trem2R47H (LOAD) model.
