Profiles chromatin-accessibility and rna-seq at single-cell resolution.
Source: sziraki-2023-a-global-view-of-aging

wiki/entities/eccite-seq.title: eccite-seq
wiki/entities/eccite-seq.content:
ECCITE-Seq
One-line: A pooled single-cell CRISPR screening readout that couples measurement of the single-cell transcriptome with protein levels, by sequencing oligonucleotide barcodes conjugated to antibodies alongside the transcriptome.
What it is

Cited in kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for as an example of adding “multiomic” phenotypes to single-cell CRISPR screens: simultaneous readout of single-cell transcriptomes and quantified protein abundances in the same cell.
Extends single-cell RNA-seq CRISPR screen readouts (e.g. perturb-seq) beyond transcriptome-only measurements.

Connections

A variant readout for single-cell-crispr-screen / pooled-crispr-screen designs.
Related methods in the wiki: perturb-seq, dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening.

wiki/entities/eclip.title: eclip
wiki/entities/eclip.content:
eCLIP (enhanced crosslinking immunoprecipitation)
One-line: An enhanced CLIP method for mapping the transcriptome-wide binding sites of RNA-binding proteins at high resolution and efficiency.
eCLIP crosslinks rna-binding-proteins to their bound RNA, immunoprecipitates the protein, and sequences the associated fragments to produce genome-wide RBP occupancy maps. It is used to validate and characterize candidate splicing regulators — for example, to confirm binding profiles of RBPs identified in tethered-function screens of alternative splicing.

Related single-nucleotide-resolution variant: iclip.

Referenced by

schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability

wiki/entities/elevation-crispr.title: elevation-crispr
wiki/entities/elevation-crispr.content:
Elevation
Machine-learning model for predicting CRISPR off-target activities as part of end-to-end guide RNA design (Listgarten et al., Nat. Biomed. Eng. 2018).

Cited as the machine-learning exemplar (vs deep-learning deepcrispr) among in silico off-target nomination methods (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, ai-in-genomics.

wiki/entities/elongate-model.title: elongate-model
wiki/entities/elongate-model.content:
ELongATE (Extra-Long repeats, Acquired Toxic Effect)
One-line: A single-neuron pathogenesis model for Huntington disease in which the inherited HTT CAG repeat is biologically innocuous and toxicity is acquired only after somatic expansion crosses a high (~150 CAG) threshold, introduced by handsaker-2025-long-somatic-dna-repeat-expansion.
What it is
ELongATE proposes that each striatal projection neuron (SPN) passes asynchronously through five phases driven cell-autonomously by its own expanding HTT allele:

Phase A (36-80 CAGs): decades of slow-but-accelerating repeat expansion; no expression effect (a “ticking DNA clock”).
Phase B (80-150 CAGs): brisk expansion (years); still no expression effect.
Phase C (>150 CAGs): continuous, escalating gene-expression change eroding SPN-identity genes (months).
Phase D (>>150 CAGs): a “de-repression crisis” — normally-silent genes (HOX clusters, ectopic TFs, senescence/apoptosis genes CDKN2A/CDKN2B) become de-repressed.
Phase E: neuron elimination.

Why it matters

Reframes HD’s three classic mysteries (cell-type specificity, long latency, mode of toxicity) as consequences of cell-type-specific somatic DNA-repeat instability plus a high toxicity threshold, rather than graded lifelong mutant-HTT protein toxicity.
Because neurons spend >95% of life below the threshold and decline over months once past it, the model argues for therapies that slow somatic expansion over HTT-lowering, with a potentially wide therapeutic window even in symptomatic patients.
Supported by HD modifier genetics (MSH3, FAN1, MLH1, LIG1, PMS1/2 — DNA-repeat-stability genes) and by an armadillo-shaped SPN CAG-length distribution reproduced by two-phase stochastic simulation.

wiki/entities/encode-rbp-map.title: encode-rbp-map
wiki/entities/encode-rbp-map.content:
ENCODE RBP map
ENCODE RBP map is the ENCODE Project Phase III integrative binding-and-functional resource for human RNA-binding proteins — 1,223 replicated datasets spanning 356 RBPs in K562 and HepG2 cells. Introduced in van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional.
What it is

Five orthogonal assay types: eCLIP (in vivo binding, 150 RBPs / 223 datasets), RNA Bind-N-Seq (in vitro specificity, 78 RBPs), knockdown RNA-seq (function, 263 RBPs / 472 profiles), immunofluorescence (localization, 274 RBPs), and ChIP–seq (chromatin association, 37+ RBPs).
844,854 eCLIP peaks; 38,555 knockdown-responsive alternative splicing events; per-RBP “RNA splicing maps.”
Data released at the ENCODE Data Coordination Center (encodeproject.org), GEO (GSE107768), and the RBP Image Database (rnabiology.ircm.qc.ca/RBPImage/).

In this wiki

A resource catalogue for rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors, supporting splicing-methods and splicing-map analyses.
Contains data for factor entities including ptbp1, srsf1, hnrnpa1, u2af1.

Sources

van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional

wiki/entities/enformer.title: enformer
wiki/entities/enformer.content:
Enformer
What: A transformer-based deep-learning model (Avsec et al., 2021) that predicts gene expression and epigenetic profiles directly from DNA sequence, integrating long-range regulatory context up to 100 kb on either side of a target locus lu-2021-wider-field-of-view.
Enformer applies the self-attention architecture from natural language processing to genomics. Its building blocks are self-attention layers that turn each sequence position into a latent representation via a weighted sum over all other positions, where attention weights reflect similarity between latent representations. Because every position is directly associated with all others, information flows freely between distal elements, giving a ~200 kb receptive field and letting the model identify sparsely distributed distal regulatory elements more easily than convolutional models with local receptive fields lu-2021-wider-field-of-view.
Significance & limits
Trained on hundreds of human and mouse cell types, Enformer outputs thousands of predicted tracks (transcription-factor and histone ChIP-seq, DNase-seq, ATAC-seq, and CAGE). It substantially outperforms prior models (Basenji2, ExPecto) at predicting CAGE-based tissue-specific expression, focuses attention on cell-type-specific promoters, enhancers, and insulators (cis-regulatory-elements), can identify enhancer-promoter interactions, and predicts the effects of natural or CRISPR-perturbed variants on expression (noncoding-variants). A core limit shared by such dna-language-models is that it cannot predict for cell types outside its training set.
Sources

lu-2021-wider-field-of-view — News & Views describing Enformer’s transformer architecture, long-range receptive field, CAGE performance, and variant-effect prediction.

wiki/entities/ensembl-vep.title: ensembl-vep
wiki/entities/ensembl-vep.content:
Ensembl Variant Effect Predictor (VEP)
Variant annotation tool that predicts the functional consequences of genomic variants on genes, transcripts, and regulatory regions (McLaren et al., Genome Biol. 2016).

In the paper’s functional-risk assessment, VEP is recommended (with cadd-score) to judge whether an off-target edit hits a genomic site likely to have functional impact — coding vs noncoding (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: mutation-effect-prediction, noncoding-variants, cis-regulatory-elements.

wiki/entities/erange.title: erange
wiki/entities/erange.content:
ERANGE
ERANGE (Enhanced Read Analysis of Gene Expression) is a computational pipeline for analyzing RNA-Seq data that assigns reads to their gene of origin, allocates ambiguous “multireads”, detects splice-crossing reads, and quantifies transcript prevalence in RPKM. Introduced in mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian.
What it is

Assigns uniquely mapped reads to their site of origin and probabilistically allocates multireads (reads matching 2–10 genomic loci) in proportion to unique + splice reads at candidate loci.
Detects splice-crossing reads against a library of known gene-model splices and assigns them to their gene of origin.
Clusters reads that fall outside known gene models into “RNAFAR” candidate exons, which can be appended to neighboring gene models (within a tunable radius parameter) or flagged as candidate novel transcripts.
Calculates normalized transcript prevalence (RPKM) from unique reads, spliced reads, and multireads.
Distributed as Supplementary Software and from the Wold lab (woldlab.caltech.edu/RNA-Seq).

In this wiki

One of the splicing-methods; an early RNA-seq quantification pipeline conceptually upstream of dedicated alternative-splicing tools such as rmats.
Produces RPKM values; related to isoform-level metrics like percent-spliced-in used by later splicing analyses.

Sources

mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian

wiki/entities/eugene.title: eugene
wiki/entities/eugene.content:
EUGENe
What: EUGENe (elucidating the utility of genomic elements with neural nets) is a FAIR, PyTorch-based Python toolkit for end-to-end deep-learning analysis of regulatory DNA/RNA sequences klie-2023-predictive-analyses-of-regulatory-sequences.
EUGENe organizes a genomics deep-learning workflow into three stages: (1) ETL — extract/transform/load sequence data from many file formats; (2) IIT — instantiate, initialize and train diverse architectures; (3) EI — evaluate and interpret model behavior. It aims to be simpler and more end-to-end than prior packages (Kipoi, Selene, Janggu) while remaining extensible.
Architecture (subpackages)

SeqData — reads CSV/TSV, FASTA, BED, BAM, BigWig into XArray datasets backed by Zarr stores; supports out-of-core loading via Dask for large datasets (e.g. Enformer/Basenji-scale). SeqDatasets supplies hand-curated downloadable datasets.
SeqPro — Numba-accelerated one-hot encoding, padding, jittering, k-mer-preserving shuffling.
Model layer builds on PyTorch + PyTorch Lightning; supports FCN, CNN, RNN and Hybrid architectures plus published models (DeepSTARR, DeepBind, Jores21CNN, Kopp21CNN), instantiable from API calls or YAML configs, importable from Kipoi, with convolutional filters initializable from known motifs. RayTune for hyperparameter search.
SeqExplainer (backed by Captum) — filter interpretation (PFM/PWM + TomTom annotation), attribution analysis (saturation mutagenesis, InputXGradient, DeepLIFT, GradientSHAP), global importance analyses (GIA), TF-MoDISco wrappers, and in silico sequence evolution.

Validated use cases
Demonstrated on three tasks: plant-promoter STARR-seq activity regression (79,838 promoters), RNA-binding-protein specificity (244 DeepBind-style models + a 233-task multitask model), and JunD ChIP-seq binding classification. In each, EUGENe reproduced published performance and recovered canonical motifs (TATA box, RBP motifs, JunD/CTCF).
Sources

klie-2023-predictive-analyses-of-regulatory-sequences — introduces EUGENe, its subpackages, and the three benchmark applications.

wiki/entities/evoetr.title: evoetr
wiki/entities/evoetr.content:
EvoETR (evolved engineered transcriptional repressor)
One-line: An all-in-one epigenetic silencing editor fusing a zinc-finger DNA-binding domain to KRAB plus an obligate cdDNMT3A–DNMT3L heterodimer, installing durable DNA-methylation-based gene silencing without DNA breaks.
What it is

A single-molecule (“all-in-one”) evolution of the earlier three-component engineered transcriptional repressor (ETR) combination, which required three separate mRNAs (KRAB, cdDNMT3A, DNMT3L each on a separate ZFP array) to be co-delivered.
Architecture: an obligate heterodimer of the de novo methyltransferase catalytic domain cdDNMT3A and its inactive cofactor DNMT3L appended at the N terminus of a zinc-finger protein (ZFP) DNA-binding domain, with the KRAB repressor domain at the C terminus (joined via XTEN linkers).
Best-performing variant is EvoETR-8 (using the ZFP-8 array targeting the Pcsk9 CpG-island promoter), selected for its highest specificity among ZFP-3/6/8 configurations.
Belongs to the epigenome-editing class of programmable gene-regulation tools; unlike genome-editing it leaves primary DNA sequence intact, relying instead on inherited dna-methylation.

Key properties

Delivered transiently as LNP-packaged mRNA; a single dose reduced circulating PCSK9 by ~75% in mice (vs ~50% for the triple-ETR combination), comparable to CRISPR–Cas9 gene disruption but without DNA breaks.
~1.9-fold higher mean Pcsk9-promoter methylation than the triple-ETR combination, with a cleaner off-target profile (Pcsk9 the only significantly downregulated gene in vitro).
Mechanism relies on endogenous DNMT1 to heritably propagate the installed CpG methylation through cell division (“hit-and-run”: editor expression needed only initially).

Provenance

Introduced in Cappelluti et al., Nature 2024 (cappelluti-2024-durable-efficient-gene-silencing).

wiki/entities/evonmd.title: evonmd
wiki/entities/evonmd.content:
EvoNMD
Introduced in pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in.
What it is
EvoNMD is a Python-based pipeline for systematically predicting nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) isoforms and poison exons (PEs) directly from genome annotations (GTF). For each transcript it identifies the annotated stop codon and flags the isoform as NMD-targeted when the stop sits more than 50 nt upstream of the last exon-exon junction (the canonical 50-nt rule), then collects per-genome statistics (coding genes, isoforms per gene, exons per gene, transcript lengths). It was applied across 957 eukaryotic genomes from Ensembl (Releases 60 and 113) to show that Homo sapiens has the highest enrichment of NMD isoforms, and it underpins the companion PE-annotation strategy that catalogs 9,814 putative human poison exons. It can re-annotate genomes lacking an explicit nonsense_mediated_decay biotype (e.g. Drosophila, Arabidopsis). Code: github.com/ppigini/EvoNMD.
In this wiki
EvoNMD is the evolutionary/annotation engine behind the human poison-exon catalog in pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in. It is upstream of, and complementary to, sequence-based variant predictors like spliceai (used in the same paper to score pathogenic variants disrupting PE splicing): EvoNMD works from annotation structure to enumerate NMD/PE events, whereas SpliceAI scores variant effects on splice-site usage.
Sources

pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in — introduces EvoNMD; applies it to 957 eukaryotic transcriptomes and to building the 9,814-PE human catalog.

wiki/entities/exonscan.title: exonscan
wiki/entities/exonscan.content:
ExonScan
ExonScan is a general splicing-simulation algorithm that predicts the splicing pattern of a pre-mRNA by scanning for candidate exons and scoring their splice sites together with nearby exonic and intronic splicing regulatory elements. Introduced in wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts (originally from Wang et al. 2004).
What it is

A three-step simulator: (1) identify exon candidates as pairs of nearby potential 3’ss and 5’ss ~50-250 bp apart; (2) score the splice sites and the splicing enhancers/silencers (ESEs/ESSs etc.) within the candidate and sum them; (3) apply a threshold to make the final inclusion prediction.
Uses log-odds scoring that rewards elements by their statistical enrichment in the relevant location relative to genomic background, naturally giving enhancers positive and silencers negative scores.
Designed for “faithfulness to the in vivo mechanism”: it uses only information accessible to the spliceosome (sequence/SRE content), deliberately excluding reading-frame consistency and cross-species conservation that gene-finders rely on.
Helped clarify the in vivo function of ESSs and serves as an early integration of a “parts list” of cis-elements toward a predictive splicing code.

In this wiki

An early instance of splicing-methods for sequence-to-splicing prediction; conceptual ancestor of modern deep-learning predictors like spliceai.
Operates on the splicing-regulatory-elements / cis-regulatory-elements catalogued toward the splicing-code.

Sources

wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts

wiki/entities/exp-starr-seq.title: exp-starr-seq
wiki/entities/exp-starr-seq.content:
ExP STARR-seq
One-line: Enhancer × promoter self-transcribing active regulatory region sequencing — a high-throughput episomal reporter assay that measures RNA output for all pairwise combinations of many enhancer and promoter sequences at once.
What it is
A modified plasmid-based STARR-seq vector in which candidate enhancer (264 bp) and promoter (264 bp) sequences are cloned ~340 bp apart in all pairwise combinations, with a 16-bp plasmid barcode adjacent to the enhancer. The promoter initiates transcription of a polyadenylated RNA containing the barcode and enhancer, so targeted RNA-seq (normalized to input DNA) quantifies expression per enhancer–promoter pair. Introduced by Bergman et al. (2022) and applied to 1,000 enhancers × 1,000 promoters in human K562 cells (604,268 well-covered pairs).
Why it matters
Enables systematic, quantitative measurement of enhancer–promoter compatibility over ~6 orders of magnitude of expression, disentangling intrinsic enhancer activity, intrinsic promoter activity, and pair-specific specificity.

wiki/entities/flare.title: flare
wiki/entities/flare.content:
FLARE
FLARE (Functional Lasso Analysis of Regulatory Evolution) is a context-specific lasso-regression model, introduced in marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding, that integrates evolutionary constraint with deep-learning regulatory predictions to prioritize noncoding variants with extreme, likely-deleterious regulatory effects.
What it does

Learns a quantitative mapping from context-specific molecular variant effects (ChromBPNet chromatin-accessibility predictions), chromatin accessibility, and genomic-context features to evolutionary conservation (PhyloP).
Trained per developmental/tissue context (e.g. FLARE-fb = fetal brain, FLARE-h = heart, plus adult brain), using leave-one-chromosome-out training on ultra-rare variants. Highlighted fetal neuronal contexts and sequence-based predictions as most informative for explaining conservation.
Captures a variant’s regulatory potential across multiple cell types, going beyond any single-cell-type score or region-based annotation.

Inputs / outputs

Inputs: ChromBPNet context-specific variant-effect scores, ATAC-based accessibility features, and genomic-context features (e.g. TSS distance, nearest-gene constraint).
Output: a per-variant FLARE score predicting evolutionary conservation (PhyloP); high-scoring outliers flag constrained, potentially deleterious noncoding variants.
Training data: 8,757,029 ultra-rare (MAF < 0.1%) variants from 1000 Genomes.

Key results

Autism (FLARE-fb): 14 of 16 highest-scoring de novo noncoding mutations near syndromic ASD genes were in cases; significant case/control mean-score difference (t-test P = 6.8e-3); estimated excess ~34 case vs 10.8 control de novo mutations (~1.2% of probands, CI 0.5–1.9%). Examples: a variant 878 kb from FOXG1 disrupting an RFX motif, and a CNTNAP2-intron variant disrupting NFI. CADD, PhyloP, TSS distance and single-cell-type ChromBPNet scores failed to enrich.
Congenital heart disease (FLARE-h): 7 of top 10 variants near high-confidence CHD genes were in cases (P = 0.016); top hits near ARID1B with endocardial-specific effects.
Adult-brain expression outliers (ROSMAP): top 0.1% FLARE near genes with z < −3 gave OR = 15.4 (CI 7.0–30.3, P < 4.4e-9), driven by TSS-proximal variants.
Schizophrenia heritability (S-LDSC): at top 1% threshold, FLARE-fb showed 19.1-fold enrichment (τ = 4.4e-7, P = 2.7e-18) and FLARE-h 4.7-fold (P = 0.026), outperforming the best fetal-brain peak annotation — extending FLARE from rare-variant prioritization to common-variant psychiatric-genetics / heritability annotation.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence / ai-in-genomics method for mutation-effect-prediction over noncoding-variants, built on context-specific regulatory sequence models and population-genetic constraint.
Conceptually adjacent to S-LDSC-based single-cell annotation tools such as sc-linker.

Sources

marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding

wiki/entities/flrt.title: flrt
wiki/entities/flrt.content:
FLRT (fibronectin leucine-rich transmembrane protein)
One-line: A family of transmembrane cell-adhesion proteins that act as latrophilin ligands, cooperating with teneurin in trans-synaptic adhesion.
FLRT proteins bind the postsynaptic adhesion GPCR latrophilin (lphn3) at the synapse and work together with teneurins to organize trans-synaptic contacts. In the latrophilin-3 splicing work, FLRT is one of the presynaptic ligands whose engagement is read out through LPHN3, whose C-terminal alternative splicing (exon 31 vs 32) switches downstream G-protein coupling and synaptogenic activity.
Referenced by

wang-2024-alternative-splicing-of-latrophilin-3-controls

wiki/entities/forebrain-assembloid.title: forebrain-assembloid
wiki/entities/forebrain-assembloid.content:
Forebrain assembloid (hFA)
One-line: A human 3D model of cortical circuit assembly made by fusing a ventral-forebrain subpallial organoid (hSO) with a dorsal cortical organoid (hCO), enabling study of interneuron generation and long-range migration.
What it is
Human forebrain assembloids (hFA) are generated by integrating hiPS cell-derived subpallial (ventral) organoids (hSO) with cortical (dorsal) organoids (hCO). GABAergic interneurons are generated in the hSO and migrate into the hCO, recapitulating the ventral-to-dorsal tangential migration of human cortical interneurons. A Dlxi1/2b::eGFP reporter labels MGE-derived interneurons so migration between the two parts can be tracked and FACS-sorted. The platform was previously developed by the Pașca lab and here coupled with CRISPR screening.
Usage in the wiki

Core model system of meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact: the interneuron-generation screen was run in hSO and the interneuron-migration screen in hFA (>1,000 assembloids for ~500× coverage). Used with a SEC61B-mEGFP ER reporter to show that the endoplasmic reticulum is displaced forward along the leading branch before nucleokinesis, and that LNPK loss disrupts this.

Connections

A brain-organoid-based system for modeling human neurodevelopment and cortical circuit assembly.

wiki/entities/g-quadruplex.title: g-quadruplex
wiki/entities/g-quadruplex.content:
G-quadruplex
What: A noncanonical four-stranded nucleic-acid secondary structure formed in
guanine-rich regions, where four guanines Hoogsteen-bond into a G-quartet and
stacked quartets are stabilised by K⁺; it forms in both DNA (dG4) and RNA (rG4)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Role in splicing
rG4s are an emerging structural layer of alternative-splicing regulation
(rna-structure-and-splicing). Positioned near 5′/3′ splice-sites, they act
position-dependently as enhancers or silencers across all event types — by
recruiting or blocking splicing-factors and by remodelling the pre-mRNA to
mask or expose splice sites. dG4s additionally influence splicing indirectly
via RNA Pol II elongation and R-loops (cotranscriptional-splicing)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Reader proteins named in the source include the hnRNP family (F, H1/H, L),
FMRP (RGG motif), the AFF family, SRSF1, and RBM25
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Significance

Prevalence: ~60% of transcripts carry ≥1 putative quadruplex sequence (~1.2M
PQSs); splice junctions are ~3-fold enriched for G4s on the coding strand.
Disease: linked to cancer (TP53, BCL-x, hTERT, EWS-FLI)
and neurological disease (FMR1/Fragile X, BACE1/Alzheimer).
Therapeutics: G4-stabilising ligands (e.g.
pyridostatin, PhenDC3) can modulate splicing, though effects are sometimes
contradictory and ligand selectivity remains unsolved.
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes

Sources

peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — definition, mechanism, readers, prevalence, disease, ligands

wiki/entities/gears.title: gears
wiki/entities/gears.content:
GEARS (graph-enhanced gene activation and repression simulator)
Deep-learning model that predicts single-cell transcriptional responses to single- and multi-gene perturbations. Represents each gene with two learnable embeddings passed through graph neural networks over a gene coexpression graph (gene embedding) and a Gene Ontology-derived gene-similarity graph (perturbation embedding), then composes perturbation effects with a ‘sum’ operator plus cross-gene and gene-specific decoders. The GO/coexpression prior lets GEARS predict outcomes for genes never experimentally perturbed, including non-additive genetic interactions (synergy, suppression, neomorphism, redundancy, epistasis). Trained on Perturb-seq data; includes a Bayesian uncertainty score. Code at github.com/snap-stanford/GEARS.

wiki/entities/geneformer.title: geneformer
wiki/entities/geneformer.content:
Geneformer
Context-aware, attention-based deep learning model (transformer) for single-cell transcriptomics, pretrained via a self-supervised masked learning objective on Genecorpus-30M (~29.9M human single-cell transcriptomes). Each cell is presented as a rank value encoding of genes; the model uses 6 transformer encoder layers (256 embedding dims, 4 attention heads/layer, input size 2,048).
Pretrained knowledge of gene network dynamics is transferred by fine-tuning toward diverse downstream tasks (dosage sensitivity, chromatin bivalency, TF regulatory range, network centrality) using limited task-specific data. Supports in silico deletion and in silico treatment for perturbation modeling and candidate therapeutic target discovery, validated experimentally in cardiomyopathy (TEAD4, GSN, PLN).
Introduced in theodoris-2023-transfer-learning-enables-predictions.

wiki/entities/gllamm.title: gllamm
wiki/entities/gllamm.content:
gllamm
gllamm (“Generalized Linear Latent And Mixed Models”) is a user-written
Stata program (Rabe-Hesketh, Skrondal & Pickles) for fitting multilevel and
mixed models — including generalized-linear-mixed-models — by maximum
likelihood. It is a general-purpose, widely available tool rather than a
genetics-specific one.
Role in this wiki
rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and uses gllamm as the worked
example of its thesis: that biometric variance-components-models for
twin-and-family-study-design data — for both continuous and dichotomous
phenotypes — can be estimated with standard mixed-model software, sidestepping
specialized SEM programs such as mx-software. Example datasets and gllamm
commands accompany that paper.
Contrast with sas-proc-mixed, the SAS counterpart used for the continuous-trait
VCM in mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models.

wiki/entities/gltscr1.title: gltscr1
wiki/entities/gltscr1.content:
GLTSCR1
One-line: An ncBAF-specific scaffold subunit (with paralog GLTSCR1L) that binds the DUF3512 domain of brd9 to define the non-canonical BAF chromatin-remodelling complex.
GLTSCR1/GLTSCR1L are defining subunits of the non-canonical BAF (ncbaf, also GBAF) SWI/SNF complex, which targets CTCF-associated loci. Loss of BRD9 — as caused by mutant sf3b1 mis-splicing a poison exon into BRD9 — abolishes the BRG1–GLTSCR1 interaction and collapses ncBAF specifically, redistributing the complex away from CTCF sites (splicing-and-chromatin).
Referenced by

inoue-2019-spliceosomal-disruption-of-the-non-canonical

wiki/entities/goflof-database.title: goflof-database
wiki/entities/goflof-database.content:
GOF/LOF database (goflof)
Public germline gain-of-function / loss-of-function variant database with an R-Shiny web interface (itanlab.shinyapps.io/goflof), built by Sevim Bayrak et al. (AJHG 2021).

Contains 9,541 pathogenic GOF/LOF variants extracted from HGMD (Dec 2019 release) via NLP text-mining of paper abstracts, plus a 4,957-variant ClinVar-derived set (see sevim-bayrak-2021-identification-discriminative-gene-level).
Each variant is annotated with 10 variant-level, 11 gene-level, and 12 protein-level features (1,725 variables), allowing users to extract, filter, and download annotated subsets.
Variant-level features annotated with ensembl-vep and pathogenicity scores including cadd-score.

Related: variant-effect-prediction, mutation-effect-prediction.

wiki/entities/guide-seq.title: guide-seq
wiki/entities/guide-seq.content:
GUIDE-seq
Cell-based, repair-product-based assay for genome-wide profiling of off-target cleavage by CRISPR–Cas nucleases, based on tagging double-strand-break sites with a short dsODN that is captured and sequenced (Tsai et al., Nat. Biotechnol. 2015). An updated GUIDE-seq-2 exists (Lazzarotto et al., 2025).

A cellular nomination method (false-positive rates generally <20%, unlike naked-DNA assays); nominated sites are followed up with targeted sequencing for verification (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).
Invented by S.Q. Tsai (a co-author), who holds the GUIDE-seq patent (US 9,822,407 B2).

Related: genome-editing, circle-seq, change-seq.

wiki/entities/hind.title: hind
wiki/entities/hind.content:
HIND (Hub1-interaction domain)
One-line: A conserved sequence element through which the ubiquitin-like protein hub1 binds the spliceosome, present in the tri-snRNP component snu66 (yeast/mammalian SART1) and in the plant splicing factor Prp38 (mishra-2011-role-of-the-ubiquitin-like-protein).
HIND lies in the intrinsically disordered N-terminal region of Snu66 and forms a short (~11-residue) helix that docks non-covalently onto the ubiquitin-like fold of Hub1. Crystal structures (1.4 Å) show a small (~500 Å²) interface built from a salt bridge (Snu66 Arg16/47–Hub1 Asp22) plus hydrophobic contacts, giving high affinity (Kd ≈ 0.6–1.7 µM); mutating Hub1 Asp22 or the Snu66 arginines abolishes binding. This Hub1–HIND interaction recruits Hub1 to the spliceosome and is required for use of certain non-canonical 5′ splice sites and for alternative-splicing of the yeast SRC1 pre-mRNA.
Referenced by

mishra-2011-role-of-the-ubiquitin-like-protein

wiki/entities/hnrnp-c.title: hnrnp-c
wiki/entities/hnrnp-c.content:
hnRNP C
One-line: Heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein C1/C2, an abundant nuclear tetramer that nucleates hnRNP particles, binds uridine/polypyrimidine tracts, and position-dependently regulates alternative exons (konig-2010-iclip-reveals-the-function-of).
hnRNP C1/C2 assembles into tetramers that package nascent pre-mRNA and read out U-rich sequences. Its effect on alternative-splicing is position-dependent: binding can either silence or enhance an alternative exon depending on where the contact sits relative to the regulated exon. Mapping these contacts at single-nucleotide resolution with iclip revealed how hnRNP C occupancy shapes exon inclusion transcriptome-wide (rna-binding-proteins).
Referenced by

konig-2010-iclip-reveals-the-function-of

wiki/entities/hnrnp-f.title: hnrnp-f
wiki/entities/hnrnp-f.content:
hnRNP F
One-line: Heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoprotein F, a paralog of hnrnp-h that recognizes G-rich intronic splicing enhancers via quasi-RRM domains.
hnRNP F is a G-run / G-rich-motif-binding splicing factor closely related to hnrnp-h (hnRNP H1). In the functional ISE screen of wang-2012, hnRNP H1, hnRNP F and GRSF1 together were identified as the cognate trans-factors for five of six ISE motif groups (A–E), despite the diversity of the motifs. hnRNP H1/F overexpression synergistically promoted splicing of a group-B-ISE intron, and RNAi knockdown of either factor reduced it, demonstrating specificity. Like hnRNP H1, it acts as a context-dependent, modular splicing regulator — enhancing splicing from introns and silencing from exons.
Referenced by

wang-2012-intronic-splicing-enhancers-cognate-splicing

wiki/entities/hnrnp-h.title: hnrnp-h
wiki/entities/hnrnp-h.content:
hnRNP H
One-line: hnRNP H1, a G-run / G-rich-motif-binding splicing factor whose glycine/tyrosine-rich C-terminal domains modularly enhance splicing from introns and silence it from exons.
hnRNP H1 is the principal cognate trans-factor for the general intronic splicing enhancers (ISEs) defined by wang-2012, recognizing five of six ISE motif groups (A–E) together with its paralog hnrnp-f and GRSF1. Its Gly/Tyr-rich C-terminal domain is sufficient to confer context-dependent activity: tethered to an intron it enhances splicing, tethered to an exon it silences it — a modular activity opposite in sign to the RS domains of SR proteins. Even the last 85 amino acids of the Gly/Tyr-rich region retained this activity when fused to a programmable PUF RNA-binding domain, marking hnRNP H/F as programmable, modular splicing-factors. Its position-dependent logic parallels that mapped transcriptome-wide by iclip for hnrnp-c.
Referenced by

wang-2012-intronic-splicing-enhancers-cognate-splicing

wiki/entities/hnrnpa1.title: hnrnpa1
wiki/entities/hnrnpa1.content:
hnRNPA1
What: The prototypical hnRNP, a well-studied splicing factor that represses splicing by binding silencer elements.
Human hnRNPA1 is the archetypal repressor among the hnRNP family of rna-binding-proteins, the silencing arm of the splicing-code. It binds exonic and intronic splicing silencers (a class of splicing-regulatory-elements) to fine-tune alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This contrasts with SR activators like srsf1; consistent with the position-dependent rule, hnRNPA1 can in some cases act as an activator, and can even promote U1 snRNP binding during splicing silencing.
Role in splicing
hnRNPA1 has several mechanisms: (a) binding a silencer to block recognition of nearby splice-sites; (b) binding a high-affinity site, then cooperatively spreading to adjacent low-affinity sites; and (c) looping of pre-mRNA via interactions between hnRNPA1 proteins bound at flanking intronic sites, promoting exon exclusion lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. It regulates smn2 and HIV pre-mRNA splicing, and (with hnRNPA2/PTBP1) drives the PKM Warburg switch marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Disease / significance
Cis-acting mutations in the prion-like, glycine-rich C-terminal domain of hnRNPA1 cause amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Such prion-like low-complexity domains are shared with hnRNPA2B1, TDP-43, FUS, EWSR1 and TAF15; TDP-43 and FUS prion-like-domain mutations are likewise linked to ALS (splicing-and-disease) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — repressor mechanisms (blocking, spreading, looping), SMN2/HIV, ALS prion-like domain
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — PKM/Warburg switch; SMN2 intron-7 ISS

wiki/entities/hprc-pangenome.title: hprc-pangenome
wiki/entities/hprc-pangenome.content:
HPRC draft human pangenome reference
The first-draft human pangenome reference released by the Human Pangenome Reference Consortium (HPRC). Rather than a single mosaic scaffold like GRCh38, it is a graph-based reference built from 47 phased, diploid assemblies of genetically diverse individuals (90 haplotypes including GRCh38 and T2T-CHM13, with three samples held out for benchmarking).
What it is

A collection of high-quality assemblies (>99% complete, >99% accurate) plus pangenome graphs constructed by three methods: Minigraph (SV-only), Minigraph-Cactus (MC, base-level), and PanGenome Graph Builder (PGGB, reference-free).
Adds 119 Mb of euchromatic polymorphic sequence and 1,115 gene duplications relative to GRCh38, of which ~90 Mb derives from structural variation.
A subset of the planned HPRC panel targeting 700 haplotypes across 350 individuals to capture global genomic diversity.

Why it matters

Reduces reference bias (the “streetlamp effect”): short-read analysis against the pangenome cut small-variant errors by 34% and increased SVs detected per haplotype by 104% versus GRCh38 workflows.
Serves as a community resource: applied via Giraffe + DeepVariant to 3,202 1000 Genomes samples and genotyped with PanGenie, providing allele frequencies at complex, medically relevant loci previously inaccessible with a linear reference.

Introduced in liao-2023-draft-human-pangenome-reference.

wiki/entities/hub1.title: hub1
wiki/entities/hub1.content:
Hub1 (UBL5)
One-line: An evolutionarily conserved ubiquitin-like protein (UBL5/beacon) that non-covalently modifies the spliceosome to enable use of certain non-canonical 5′ splice sites and thereby alternative splicing.
Unlike canonical ubiquitin-family modifiers, Hub1 lacks a C-terminal di-glycine motif and does not form covalent conjugates — it acts by binding proteins non-covalently (mishra-2011). It binds the tri-snRNP component snu66 (mammalian SART1) through a conserved sequence element named hind (Hub1-interaction domain), also present in the plant splicing factor Prp38. Hub1 modifies the spliceosome as a whole rather than any single partner, only slightly altering composition while preserving core snRNP architecture. Spliceosomes lacking Hub1 (or defective in the Hub1–HIND interaction) cannot use certain non-canonical 5′ splice-sites and are defective in alternative splicing of the yeast SRC1 pre-mRNA, establishing a mechanism for tolerating and selecting non-consensus splice sites. Crystal structures show a ubiquitin-like ββαβαβ fold with a novel HIND-binding interface (Kd ≈ 0.6–1.7 µM).
Referenced by

mishra-2011-role-of-the-ubiquitin-like-protein

wiki/entities/human-brain-transcriptome-.title: human-brain-transcriptome-atlas
wiki/entities/human-brain-transcriptome-.content:
Human Brain Transcriptome (HBT) atlas
One-line: Spatio-temporal exon-level microarray atlas of the developing and adult human brain (Kang et al. 2011), spanning 16 regions across 15 developmental periods.
What it is

Exon-level transcriptome plus genotyping data generated from 1,340 tissue samples from 57 clinically unremarkable post-mortem brains (ages 5.7 post-conceptional weeks to 82 years; 31 males, 26 females; multiple ethnicities).
Profiled on the Affymetrix GeneChip Human Exon 1.0 ST Array (1.4 million probe sets) with an Illumina 2.5-million SNP genotyping chip on donor DNA.
Covers 16 brain regions including 11 neocortex areas (collectively NCX), hippocampus, amygdala, striatum, mediodorsal thalamus and cerebellar cortex, organized into a 15-period developmental system.
Enables gene- and exon-level differential expression, differential exon usage, co-expression network, sex-bias and cis-eQTL analyses across space and time.

Access

Public browser at humanbraintranscriptome.org.
Exon array deposited in NCBI GEO under accession GSE25219; genotyping data in dbGaP under phs000406.v1.p1.

Source

Introduced in kang-2011-spatio-temporal-transcriptome-human-brain.

wiki/entities/human-cortical-development.title: human-cortical-development-cell-browser
wiki/entities/human-cortical-development.content:
Human Cortical Development atlas (UCSC Cell Browser collection)
Introduced in velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal. An interactive single-cell atlas of prenatal-to-adult human cortical development, hosted as the “Human Cortical Development” collection on the UCSC Cell Browser (pre-postnatal-cortex.cells.ucsc.edu).
What it is

A browsable atlas of the 709,372-nucleus snRNA-seq dataset spanning second-trimester gestation through adulthood (169 tissue samples, 106 donors), integrated with single-nucleus chromatin accessibility (snATAC-seq) data.
Provides cell-type and lineage annotations (excitatory neuron subtypes, interneuron classes, astrocytes, OPCs/oligodendrocytes, microglia, vascular cells), developmental-age labels, lineage trajectories/pseudotime, and lineage-specific gene programs.
Exposes the study’s enhancer gene regulatory networks (eGRNs) and sex- and region-specific developmental gene programs underlying human cortical development.
Distribution: analyzed cell-count matrix and metadata via the UCSC Cell Browser collection “Human Cortical Development”; also deposited at CELLxGENE; raw data at the Neuroscience Multi-omic (NeMo) Archive (accession nemo:dat-3ah9h9x); analysis code at github.com/velmeshevlab/dev_hum_cortex.

In this wiki

The primary reusable deliverable of velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal and a functional-single-cell-genomics human brain-development resource.
Complements the proteomic developing-brain resource described in wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of.

Sources

velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal

wiki/entities/human-reference-genome.title: human-reference-genome
wiki/entities/human-reference-genome.content:
Human Reference Genome (draft)
What: The draft sequence of the human genome produced by the public International Human Genome Sequencing Consortium and reported in 2001. It was assembled by hierarchical shotgun sequencing (a genome-wide physical map of overlapping large-insert BAC clones, each clone shotgun-sequenced and assembled), covering ~94% of the genome (>96% of the euchromatic portion) at an estimated total size of ~3,200 Mb.
Why it matters: Released freely and without restriction, it became the foundational coordinate system and reference substrate for human genetics and genomics — the basis for variant calling, annotation, transcriptome mapping and comparative analysis. Its initial analysis established key facts: ~30,000-40,000 protein-coding genes, >50% of the genome from transposable-element repeats, >1.4 million SNPs, and higher male-vs-female mutation rates.
Introduced in: ihgsc-2001-initial-sequencing-analysis-human

wiki/entities/iclip.title: iclip
wiki/entities/iclip.content:
iCLIP
One-line: Individual-nucleotide-resolution UV cross-linking and immunoprecipitation, mapping RNA-binding-protein contacts on RNA in vivo at single-nucleotide resolution (König & Ule 2010).
iCLIP captures the cDNAs that truncate at the protein–RNA cross-link site — normally lost in conventional CLIP — by circularizing them after reverse transcription, yielding single-nucleotide-resolution maps of rna-binding-proteins on RNA (konig-2010). It adds a random barcode to the RT adaptor so PCR duplicates can be removed and each cross-link event counted quantitatively (a “cDNA count”), a conceptual precursor to UMIs. Applied to hnrnp-c, iCLIP revealed uridine-tract binding with a defined ~165-nt spacing and, integrated with splicing microarrays into an rna-splicing-map, showed strictly position-dependent regulation of alternative exon inclusion. It is a landmark advance among splicing-methods.
Referenced by

konig-2010-iclip-reveals-the-function-of

wiki/entities/ihart-cohort.title: ihart-cohort
wiki/entities/ihart-cohort.content:
iHART cohort (Hartwell Autism Research and Technology Initiative)
One-line: The largest whole-genome-sequenced cohort of multiplex autism families — 493 AGRE families / 2,308 individuals — assembled specifically to detect rare inherited ASD risk.
What it is

~30x whole-genome sequencing (hg19) of 493 families from the Autism Genetic Resource Exchange (AGRE), each with two or more children affected by ASD; 960 affected and 217 unaffected children with both biological parents sequenced.
Multiplex ascertainment enriches for transmitted inherited risk, contrasting with simplex collections (e.g. the Simons Simplex Collection) that are dominated by de novo variation.
Includes 76 monozygotic-twin pairs used to train the ARC (Artifact Removal by Classifier) machine-learning filter for lymphoblastoid-cell-line and sequencing artifacts.
Data (genotype/phenotype search, variant calls) released via the iHART portal (ihart.org) for reuse and integration with future cohorts.

Why it matters

Enabled discovery of 16 novel ASD risk genes (69 total at FDR < 0.1; 24 genome-wide significant) and the first inherited-variant evidence for NR3C2 and DLG2 regulatory deletions.
A reusable reference for studying inherited-vs-de-novo genetic architecture in autism and psychiatric-genetics.

See source: ruzzo-2019-inherited-and-de-novo-genetic.

wiki/entities/induce-seq.title: induce-seq
wiki/entities/induce-seq.content:
INDUCE-seq
Cell-based, double-strand-break-based assay for precision digital mapping of endogenous and induced genomic DNA breaks (Dobbs et al., Nat. Commun. 2022).

A cellular off-target nomination method grouped with discover-seq in the double-strand-break-based family (see stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Related: genome-editing, guide-seq.

wiki/entities/ingene-module.title: ingene-module
wiki/entities/ingene-module.content:
INGENE & MODULE
Introduced in rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl. Two co-expression-network elastic-net models that capture distal (trans-acting) regulatory effects on gene expression and combine with conventional cis-eQTL predictors to improve transcriptome-wide association studies.
What it is

INGENE (imputed network gene-expression trans-eQTL): models the trans effects of a target gene’s cis-regulated coexpression partners — its prediction is built from propagated partner-gene cis effects rather than direct SNP-to-target associations.
MODULE (module quantitative trait loci eigengene): identifies SNPs (termed co-eQTLs) associated with the eigengene (first principal component) of a gene’s coexpression module.
Both are trans-only elastic-net models trained on LIBD postmortem brain RNA-seq across six regions (caudate, dACC, sACC, amygdala, DLPFC, hippocampus), with cross-dataset filtering/replication in GTEx and CommonMind (CMC); only genes replicated across datasets are retained.
Benchmarked against two cis-based models in the same framework: CIS (standard PrediXcan-style elastic-net) and EpiXcan (adds Roadmap Epigenomics chromatin priors). Cis-SNPs are excluded when building trans predictors, so cis and trans components are statistically independent and can be combined linearly.
Performance highlights: MODULE achieved the highest cross-validated adjusted R² of the four models (e.g. ΔR² = 0.0464 vs INGENE); trans models predicted up to ~1.8× more genes than cis-only in GTEx; the combined cis+trans model significantly improved imputation for 18,744 pooled genes. In a PGC3 coTWAS the framework found 766 schizophrenia genes, 641 novel, with novel hits enriched among trans-only (INGENE-dominated) predictions.

In this wiki

Topic: artificial-intelligence (a computational modeling method) bridging to psychiatric-genetics and heritability.
Conceptual sibling to isoform-level TWAS extension isotwas — both extend gene-level cis TWAS, INGENE/MODULE along a trans/co-expression axis.
Aims at the missing-heritability gap left by cis-only eQTL prediction in GWAS follow-up.

Sources

rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl

wiki/entities/intervalrna-portal.title: intervalrna-portal
wiki/entities/intervalrna-portal.content:
INTERVAL RNA-seq QTL portal (IntervalRNA.org.uk)
Introduced in tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants. An open-access web resource of blood gene-expression and splicing QTLs from the INTERVAL cohort, with their colocalizations and mediation links to plasma proteins, metabolites and disease.
What it is

A compendium of molecular QTLs derived from peripheral-blood RNA-seq of 4,732 INTERVAL participants: cis-eQTLs for 17,233 genes and cis-sQTLs for 29,514 de novo splicing events in 6,853 genes, plus full trans-QTL summary statistics.
Integrates these transcriptional QTLs with same-cohort plasma protein QTLs (Olink, SomaScan), metabolite QTLs (Metabolon, Nightingale) and external pQTLs (deCODE, UKB-PPP), exposing statistical colocalization results across 3,430 molecular traits and mediation models (222 molecular phenotypes mediated by expression or splicing).
Also surfaces colocalizations with disease GWAS (FinnGen, COVID-19 HGI) and Open Targets Genetics look-ups, supporting variant-to-function interpretation (e.g. WARS1/hypertension, IL7R/dermatitis, OAS1 & IFNAR2/COVID-19).
Built on an annotation-free leafcutter splicing definition; QTLs called with tensorQTL. Cohort is European-ancestry blood donors. Hosted at https://IntervalRNA.org.uk.

In this wiki

It is the primary deliverable of tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants and a population-scale splicing-qtl resource focused on linking splicing to circulating proteins and splicing-and-disease.

Sources

tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants

wiki/entities/isolaser.title: isolaser
wiki/entities/isolaser.content:
isoLASER
isoLASER (isoform-Level analysis of Allele-Specific processing of Exonic Regions) is a computational method/software for long-read RNA-seq that detects allele-specific (cis-directed) alternative splicing and distinguishes it from trans-directed splicing — from a single sample, without a pre-phased genome. Introduced in quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis.
What it is

A one-stop pipeline performing three tasks: (1) de novo variant calling (de Bruijn graph local reassembly of reads, as in the lab’s earlier scAllele, with three multi-layer-perceptron classifiers — SNPs/insertions/deletions — filtering false positives); (2) gene-level haplotype phasing by weighted k-means clustering of reads (haplotagging, K=2 for diploid, phasing done in connected blocks each requiring ≥1 het SNP); (3) linkage testing between phased haplotypes and exonic parts via adjusted mutual information (AMI).
Classifies each exonic part (non-overlapping exonic segment, ~100–200 nt, derived by collapsing TALON-inferred isoforms) as cis-directed (AMI > 99th percentile of a simulated unlinked background AND |delta PSI| > 5%), trans-directed (< 95th percentile), or ambiguous. Quantifies effect size as delta PSI between haplotypes.
isoLASER-joint merges reads of the same phasing group across samples for a new AMI, recovering events untestable in single samples (incl. homozygous-variant-driven and rare variants) and prioritizing Splicing-Associated Variants (SAVs); approximates an splicing-qtl analysis with small cohorts.
Runs on both PacBio HiFi and Oxford Nanopore reads (Nanopore recommended SNP-only with external validation). Inputs: aligned long reads (minimap2); no pre-genotyped/pre-phased genome required — a key contrast with LORALS. Source code at github.com/gxiaolab/isoLASER; analysis Snakemake at github.com/gxiaolab/isoLASER_paper.

In this wiki

A long-read, single-sample allele-specific-splicing method; complements short-read population tools such as pairadise and is a small-cohort alternative to splicing-qtl mapping.
Operates on exonic parts and reports percent-spliced-in (delta PSI); analyzes intron-retention events (e.g. UBC in Alzheimer’s).
Benchmarked vs HapCUT2 (phasing), GATK/DeepVariant/Clair3 (variant calling), and LORALS (isoform-level allelic linkage); links SAVs to rna-binding-proteins via DeepRiPe/RBNS motif analysis.
Enables HLA typing from RNA and uncovers HLA / Alzheimer’s-disease cis-directed splicing.

Sources

quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis

wiki/entities/isomiga.title: isomiga
wiki/entities/isomiga.content:
isoMiGA
isoMiGA (isoform-centric microglia genomic atlas) is a long-read RNA-seq isoform reference and paired genetic/transcriptomic QTL catalog for human microglia. Introduced in humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (Humphrey et al., Nature Genetics 2025).
What it is

Isoform atlas: built from 89 million PacBio IsoSeq full-length long reads across 30 postmortem human microglia samples (hybrid-assembled with matched Illumina short reads), cataloging 128,436 isoforms in 25,956 genes — including 35,879 novel isoforms and 2,238 novel genes absent from GENCODE v.38.
Augmented reference: the novel isoforms are merged with GENCODE v.38 (“GENCODE+Novel”, 235,559 annotated + 35,879 novel) to re-quantify isoform/gene expression in short-read cohorts.
QTL catalog: a multi-ancestry meta-analysis of 555 short-read microglia samples from 391 donors maps eQTLs plus multiple sQTL types — junction usage (leafcutter), transcript usage, and SUPPA splicing events — and colocalizes them with Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s GWAS (splicing-and-disease).
Supporting data: iPSC-derived microglia stimulation (IFNγ/LPS), four-brain-region comparison, and mass-spec proteomics (68 samples) for novel-isoform validation.

In this wiki

A human-microglia splicing-qtl resource that improves disease-locus interpretation in splicing-and-disease (nominates PLCG2 in AD, SIPA1L2 in PD as splicing-mediated).
Demonstrates that a long-read-augmented reference boosts transcript-usage and splicing-event QTL discovery over leafcutter-only junction mapping, and uses spliceai for variant splice-effect prediction.

Sources

humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas — the atlas/QTL paper (long-read isoform discovery, augmented reference, multi-ancestry sQTL meta-analysis, AD/PD colocalization)

wiki/entities/isotwas.title: isotwas
wiki/entities/isotwas.content:
isoTWAS
isoTWAS (isoform-level transcriptome-wide association study) is a multivariate, stepwise statistical framework that integrates genetics, isoform-level expression and GWAS to prioritize disease risk genes and the specific transcript isoforms driving an association. Introduced in bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic (Bhattacharya, Vo, Jops … Pasaniuc & Gandal, Nature Genetics 2023); released as an R package.
What it is

Multivariate isoform prediction: jointly models the correlated isoforms of a gene as a matrix and predicts each isoform’s expression from all cis-SNPs within 1 Mb, leveraging inter-isoform correlation. It picks the best of four multivariate penalized methods (multivariate elastic net was usually best) vs a univariate baseline, selected by 5-fold CV (R² > 0.01).
Trait mapping: imputes isoform expression into a GWAS cohort, or runs a weighted burden test on GWAS summary statistics.
Stepwise hypothesis testing: isoform P values are aggregated to the gene level with the ACAT test (gene-level FDR control), then prioritized genes’ isoforms get family-wise error control; a locus-level permutation test controls for GWAS/LD architecture, with optional Bayesian fine-mapping of the credible set of causal isoforms.
Contrast with TWAS: standard TWAS models only total gene expression univariately; isoTWAS recovers isoform-resolved signal — including associations where the gene shows none — and outperforms both gene-level TWAS and leafcutter-based local-splicing-event mapping for neuropsychiatric trait discovery.
Quantification-agnostic (Salmon/kallisto pseudoalignment in the paper); ships precomputed models for 48 GTEx tissues, adult brain cortex, and developmental brain cortex.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods / splicing-qtl tool turning isoform-level genetic regulation into prioritized splicing-and-disease mechanisms for the human brain.
Trained on the developing-brain QTL panel from walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and used as the isoform-level engine in the cross-ancestry atlas wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform.
Benchmarked against local-splicing mapping with leafcutter; conceptually downstream of the splicing-and-disease framing in li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis.

Sources

bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic — the methods paper (multivariate isoform models, ACAT-based stepwise testing, 15 neuropsychiatric GWAS, AKT3/CUL3/HSPD1/PCLO examples)

wiki/entities/isoviz.title: isoviz
wiki/entities/isoviz.content:
Isoviz
One-line: An R package that integrates transcript annotations, exon-exon-junction read counts and gRNA efficiency predictions to design and visualize isoform-specific Cas13d knockdown experiments (Schertzer et al. 2025).
Isoviz combines GENCODE / long-read transcript annotations, exon-exon-junction (EEJ) read counts, and TIGER gRNA efficiency predictions to visualize which isoforms are expressed and to select isoform-specific guide RNAs (schertzer-2025). It is part of a unified toolbox for alternative-splicing research that targets RNA-guided cas13d to EEJs in mature RNA — enabling isoform-specific knockdown across diverse splicing, alternative-transcription and polyadenylation events rather than only cassette exons. It supported experimental validation of EEJ-specific knockdown for genes including COP1, SPAG9, MKNK2 and rbfox2.
Referenced by

schertzer-2025-cas13d-mediated-isoform-specific-rna-knockdown-with

wiki/entities/ivns1abp.title: ivns1abp
wiki/entities/ivns1abp.content:
IVNS1ABP
One-line: An RNA-binding splicing regulatory cofactor (not a core splicing factor) that partners with HNRNPK and other RBPs to modulate alternative splicing.
In the single-cell splicing-QTL study of liang-2026, IVNS1ABP is the mechanistic link in a worked trans-sQTL example: genetic variants exert distal effects on RPS24 splicing by cis-regulating IVNS1ABP expression, which in turn interacts with the splicing factor HNRNPK to regulate the RPS24 cancer-associated microexon (supported by eCLIP, motif scanning and mediation analysis). It is grouped with other rna-binding-proteins regulators (SR/hnRNP, HNRNPK, HNRNPL, ptbp1) and illustrates how proximal transcriptional control of a splicing cofactor can drive distal (trans) splicing changes.
Referenced by

liang-2026-single-cell-resolution-of-splicing-regulation

wiki/entities/jcast.title: jcast
wiki/entities/jcast.content:
JCAST
JCAST is an open-source software tool (github.com/ed-lau/jcast) that translates RNA-seq alternative splice-junction pairs into compact, tissue-specific protein sequence databases for mass-spectrometry-based discovery of protein splicing isoforms. Introduced in lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of.
What it is

Takes rMATS-derived AS events (A3SS, A5SS, MXE, SE, RI) plus an Ensembl/GTF annotation and SwissProt canonical sequences as input.
Filters junctions by skipped-junction read count (Gaussian mixture cutoff) and replicate consistency, then performs one-frame in-silico translation (annotated reading frame, or a frame avoiding premature termination codons) to limit database-size inflation and false positives.
Stitches translated junction slices back to full-length canonical sequences through a 10-aa overhang and writes a FASTA database for standard proteomics search engines.
Produces databases 6–17% the size of RefSeq while still recovering noncanonical and undocumented isoforms; applicable to non-human proteomes too.

In this wiki

Consumes output from rmats and operates within the splicing-methods toolset, extending percent-spliced-in / AS quantification from the RNA level to the protein level.
Resource counterpart to RNA-centric splicing quantification tools, focused on the alternative-splicing proteome.

Sources

lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of

wiki/entities/kallisto.title: kallisto
wiki/entities/kallisto.content:
kallisto
kallisto is an RNA-seq transcript quantification program that uses pseudoalignment of reads to a reference transcriptome to estimate transcript abundances about two orders of magnitude faster than alignment-based pipelines while retaining comparable accuracy. Introduced in bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification.
What it is

Pseudoalignment, not base alignment: assigns each read to the subset of transcripts it is compatible with (its equivalence class) without computing exact base-level coordinates.
Index = transcriptome de Bruijn graph (T-DBG): a colored de Bruijn graph of transcriptome k-mers plus a hash table mapping each k-mer to its contig; k-mers are grouped into k-compatibility classes that are intersected per read.
EM over equivalence classes: abundances are estimated by the expectation-maximization algorithm applied to equivalence-class counts (sufficient statistics), making each iteration very fast; output in transcripts per million (TPM).
Fast and light: ~30M paired-end reads in <10 min on a laptop, max ~3.2 Gb RAM; few parameters (k-mer length, mean fragment length).
Bootstrap uncertainty: rapidly resamples equivalence-class counts to quantify inferential variance in abundance estimates (output compressed in HDF5).
Includes sequence-specific (6-mer priming) bias correction; supports allele-specific expression; available at pachterlab.github.io/kallisto/.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods tool: provides upstream isoform-level abundance estimates that feed alternative-splicing and differential-isoform analyses.
Complements rmats (differential alternative-splicing event detection/quantification) by supplying transcript-level quantification.
Benchmarked against Cufflinks, Sailfish, RSEM, eXpress and EMSAR in its introducing paper, outperforming Cufflinks and Sailfish in accuracy and all methods in speed.

Sources

bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification

wiki/entities/leafcutter.title: leafcutter
wiki/entities/leafcutter.content:
LeafCutter
LeafCutter is an annotation-free suite of methods that quantifies RNA splicing by measuring intron excision from split (junction) reads in short-read RNA-seq, then detects differential splicing and maps splicing QTLs. Introduced in li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna (Li, Knowles & Pritchard, Nature Genetics 2018).
What it is

Intron-centric quantification: pools split reads, groups overlapping introns that share a donor/acceptor splice site into a graph, and takes its connected components as intron clusters of alternatively excised introns; summarizes each intron as a count or within-cluster proportion. No transcript annotation required.
Differential splicing: a Dirichlet-multinomial generalized linear model tests whole-cluster changes in intron usage between two groups via a likelihood-ratio test; the multinomial likelihood normalizes implicitly (no library-size parameters, unlike dexseq).
sQTL mapping: outputs intron-excision proportions consumed by standard QTL mappers (fastQTL, MatrixEQTL); WASP filtering of allele-specific reads is required first.
LeafViz: an interactive R Shiny / ggplot2 browser app for visualizing differential-splicing results, labeling clusters “annotated” vs “cryptic”.
Fast and scalable: an order of magnitude or more faster/leaner than MAJIQ; open-source at github.com/davidaknowles/leafcutter.

In this wiki

A core splicing-methods tool in the percent-spliced-in / differential-splicing-caller family, alongside rmats (benchmarked against; comparable P-value calibration) and the isoform-assembly tool cufflinks (Cufflinks2, outperformed on sQTL discovery).
Distinct from event-based ΔPSI callers (rmats) and exon-count models (dexseq) by working on intron clusters rather than predefined events.

Sources

li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna — the methods paper (intron clustering, Dirichlet-multinomial model, GTEx/GEUVADIS benchmarks, sQTL/GWAS applications, LeafViz)

wiki/entities/lochness-score.title: lochness-score
wiki/entities/lochness-score.content:
lochNESS (local cellular heuristic neighbourhood enrichment specificity score)
lochNESS is a per-cell neighbourhood score for detecting subtle, label-agnostic shifts in cell-state distribution between a mutant (perturbed) and a pooled wild-type reference within a single-cell trajectory. Introduced in huang-2023-single-cell-whole-embryo-phenotyping (Huang et al., Nature 2023).
What it is

Co-embeds all replicates of a given mutant with all pooled wild-type replicates for a sub-trajectory, takes the aligned principal-component features, and finds each cell’s k nearest neighbours from other embryos.
For each mutant cell it computes the fold change of the observed vs expected number of mutant cells in that neighbourhood; the score is centred so that mixed regions ≈ 0, mutant-enriched regions > 0, mutant-depleted regions < 0.
Highlights enrichment/depletion in embedded space without needing predefined cluster labels — an effect can be both detected and localized to a subset of cells within a single annotated trajectory.
lochNESS distributions can be screened systematically per sub-trajectory to surface mutant-specific shifts even when global distributions look unremarkable (e.g. TAD-boundary KOs).
Genes whose expression is predicted by lochNESS can be used to annotate the enriched/depleted subpopulations.

In this wiki

A single-cell-perturbation-modeling readout method: it recovered effects invisible to gross cell-type composition analysis — e.g. the ZRS-enhancer-KO loss of Hoxa13/Hoxd13+ distal limb mesenchyme, and the Gli2-KO increase in choroid-plexus cells.
The paper notes a similar neighbourhood-based approach (Milo-style differential abundance) was developed independently; lochNESS is applied here at whole-embryo, multi-mutant scale over the mouse mutant cell atlas (MMCA).

Sources

huang-2023-single-cell-whole-embryo-phenotyping — introduces lochNESS and applies it across 22 mouse mutants at E13.5.

wiki/entities/longcell.title: longcell
wiki/entities/longcell.content:
Longcell
Longcell (Long-read single-cell transcriptomics) is a statistical and computational pipeline for isoform quantification from single-cell and spatially barcoded Nanopore long reads. Introduced in fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative (Fu et al., Nature Communications 2025), it recovers cell barcodes/UMIs, corrects sequencing/truncation/mapping errors, and models splicing heterogeneity within and between cells or spatial spots.
What it is

UMI-aware preprocessing (4 steps): (1) extract cell barcode + putative UMI; (2) cluster UMIs iteratively within meta-isoform groups, tolerating Nanopore errors; (3) correct misaligned short internal exons (microexons) and 5’/3’ truncations using consensus across the UMI cluster (PCR duplicates of one molecule); (4) adaptively prune small/singleton UMI clusters to counter UMI scattering (error-driven inflation of UMI counts).
Meta-splice-site quantification: groups co-varying putative splice sites into “meta splice sites” to reduce dimensionality and avoid biases from incomplete isoform annotations; can run annotation-free.
Heterogeneity modeling: a Beta-Binomial model of per-cell percent-spliced-in (ψ) decomposes exon-usage variation into intra-cell vs inter-cell components via a mean-invariant dispersion φ; a generalized likelihood-ratio test detects differential splicing between two cell populations (separating mean vs variance changes).
Composable: UMI-corrected reads can feed downstream tools (e.g. Longcell+IsoQuant) for transcript discovery/quantification.
Validated on simulated data, library-matched PacBio–Nanopore and Illumina-guided Nanopore datasets, and a single-cell CRISPR splicing-factor screen (targets validated by targeted sequencing). Source data: Zenodo 10.5281/zenodo.15320816.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods tool specialized for single-cell/spatial long-read data, complementary to short-read/bulk callers rmats, leafcutter, and psi-sigma.
Models alternative-splicing heterogeneity and identifies splicing-factors regulatory targets at single-cell resolution.

Sources

fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative — the methods paper (UMI scattering, consensus error correction, Beta-Binomial intra/inter-cell model, meta-splice-sites, spatial isoform switching, CRISPR splicing-factor screen).

wiki/entities/lphn3.title: lphn3
wiki/entities/lphn3.content:
LPHN3 (latrophilin-3)
One-line: Latrophilin-3 (Adgrl3), a postsynaptic adhesion GPCR whose C-terminal alternative splicing switches G-protein coupling and synaptogenic activity.
LPHN3 organizes hippocampal Schaffer-collateral synapses and undergoes extensive alternative-splicing at five sites (wang-2024). C-terminal splicing between exon 31 and exon 32 determines its signalling mode: the exon-31 variant couples to Gαs (drives cAMP) and is the synaptogenic isoform, whereas the exon-32 variant couples to Gα12/13 and is not synaptogenic. Only the Gαs-coupled exon-31 variant, via its C-terminal PDZ-binding motif, recruits phase-transitioned postsynaptic scaffold condensates by binding shank and co-sedimenting with the GHPS complex. Presynaptic teneurin and flrt ligands cluster LPHN3-coated condensates, and neuronal activity promotes the switch toward the synaptogenic exon-31 variant — an example of activity-dependent splicing in neurodevelopment.
Referenced by

wang-2024-alternative-splicing-of-latrophilin-3-controls

wiki/entities/majiq-v2.title: majiq-v2
wiki/entities/majiq-v2.content:
MAJIQ v2
MAJIQ v2 is a software suite for detecting, quantifying, and visualizing local splicing variations (LSVs) from large, heterogeneous RNA-seq datasets, extending the original MAJIQ (2016) to scale to thousands of non-replicate samples. Introduced in vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous.
What it is

Quantifies splicing in units of LSVs (splits into/out of a reference exon) in terms of percent-spliced-in (Ψ) and dPSI (ΔΨ) using a Bayesian posterior model, capturing complex (>2 junction), de novo, and intron-retention events.
MAJIQ HET: non-parametric, per-sample differential-splicing test (TNOM, InfoScore, or Mann–Whitney U) for heterogeneous populations, instead of assuming a shared group PSI.
Incremental builder: reads BAM coverage once into reusable SJ coverage files, accelerating subsequent builds (relevant for GTEx/ENCODE-style incremental releases).
Simplifier: prunes lowly-used junctions/introns to reduce splicegraph/LSV complexity.
VOILA Modulizer: organizes overlapping LSVs into single-entry/single-exit alternative splicing modules (ASMs), pattern-matched to 14 basic AS event types (incl. non-classical subtypes).
VOILA v2: cross-platform (macOS/Windows/Linux), server-capable visualization app rendering thousands of samples as violin beeswarm plots with on-the-fly filtering.
The only benchmarked method (vs rMATS, LeafCutter, SUPPA2, Whippet) offering de novo intron-retention detection and quantification; ships with a synthetic benchmark dataset and the validationstools comparison package.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods tool centered on percent-spliced-in quantification.
Direct alternative/competitor to rmats and other local-event splicing tools.
Used in its source paper to characterize cerebellar splicing regulation by ptbp1 and other splicing factors.

Sources

vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous

wiki/entities/majiqtl.title: majiqtl
wiki/entities/majiqtl.content:
MAJIQTL
MAJIQTL is a statistical framework for splicing-QTL (sQTL) discovery and
analysis from the Barash lab (UPenn), introduced in
wang-2025-a-deep-dive-into-statistical. It re-engineers three weak links in
conventional sQTL pipelines that were inherited from eQTL mapping.
What it is

Splicing representation — uses MAJIQ (majiq-v2) local splice
variations, capturing de novo junctions, IR and alternative
starts/ends that rMATS / LeafCutter / annotation-bound
isoform tools miss.
Effect-size model — a beta-binomial composite test that models the
discrete, heteroskedastic junction read counts behind Ψ,
instead of linear regression on Gaussianized Ψ.
Multiple testing — a covariate-weighted FWER (Westfall–Young) correction
for sGene discovery that allocates testing power by SNP-to-splice-site proximity
and junction coverage.
Ships VIOLA-QTL, a companion sQTL visualization tool.

Standing

On GTEx, finds significantly more functionally relevant sQTLs, better enriched
for functional annotations and co-localizing with neurodegenerative-disease
GWAS signals than prior methods (GLiMMPS, DRIMSeq, sQTLSeeker, THISTLE, IHW).
Surfaced and experimentally validated rs528823 (Alzheimer disease): disrupts a
YBX3 RBP site in MS4A3 → exon-7 skipping.

In this wiki

The statistical-engine counterpart to the wiki’s sQTL /
population cluster (park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative,
zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls) and brain e/sQTL atlases
(walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and) — it improves how sQTLs are
called, building on majiq-v2 and validated against splicing codes
(spliceai). Reinforces the splicing-and-disease ⇄
GWAS thesis.

Sources

wang-2025-a-deep-dive-into-statistical — the method paper

wiki/entities/mapt.title: mapt
wiki/entities/mapt.content:
MAPT (microtubule-associated protein tau)
One-line: The gene encoding microtubule-associated protein tau; its exon 10 alternative splicing is a classic, disease-relevant model of regulated cassette-exon inclusion.
MAPT exon 10 inclusion determines the 3R vs 4R tau isoform ratio, and its dysregulation is linked to tauopathies. In the schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability study it serves as the substrate for a tethered-function-splicing-reporter: a dual-luciferase MAPT exon 10 minigene that recruits candidate rna-binding-proteins via an MS2–MCP tether up- or downstream of the alternative exon to quantify their effect on percent-spliced-in from luminescence.
Referenced by

schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability

wiki/entities/mbnl1.title: mbnl1
wiki/entities/mbnl1.content:
MBNL1 (muscleblind-like 1)
One-line: A muscleblind-like RNA-binding splicing regulator that reads CUG/YGCY motifs in a position-dependent manner and shapes cell-type-specific alternative splicing.
MBNL1 is a member of the muscleblind protein family. It is expressed at low levels in embryonic stem cells; in han-2013-mbnl-proteins-repress-es-cell-specific-alternative it (together with mbnl2) represses ES-cell-differential exons and controls the FOXP1 “switch” exon, thereby modulating the pluripotency program. MBNL1 is also central to myotonic dystrophy, where CUG-repeat sequestration depletes functional protein.
Referenced by

han-2013-mbnl-proteins-repress-es-cell-specific-alternative

wiki/entities/mbnl2.title: mbnl2
wiki/entities/mbnl2.content:
MBNL2 (muscleblind-like 2)
One-line: A paralogue of mbnl1 in the muscleblind family that is partially redundant with it and co-represses the embryonic-stem-cell splicing program.
In han-2013-mbnl-proteins-repress-es-cell-specific-alternative, MBNL2 acts together with MBNL1 to repress ES-cell-specific alternative exons; the two paralogues share position-dependent (upstream = repress, downstream = activate) regulatory activity and overlapping target sets.
Referenced by

han-2013-mbnl-proteins-repress-es-cell-specific-alternative

wiki/entities/mbq167.title: mbq167
wiki/entities/mbq167.content:
MBQ-167
One-line: A dual RAC/CDC42 small-GTPase inhibitor used to suppress migration and metastasis of RBFOX2-depleted pancreatic cancer cells.
In jbara-2023-rbfox2-modulates-a-metastatic-signature, loss of rbfox2 in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma drives a pro-metastatic splicing program (including the oncogenic exon-23-skipped isoform of mprip) that activates RAC/CDC42 signaling; MBQ-167 pharmacologically blocks this axis and reduces the migratory/metastatic phenotype.
Referenced by

jbara-2023-rbfox2-modulates-a-metastatic-signature

wiki/entities/miso.title: miso
wiki/entities/miso.content:
MISO
MISO (mixture-of-isoforms) is a Bayesian statistical model that estimates alternative-exon and isoform expression from single- or paired-end RNA-seq, provides confidence intervals on those estimates, and tests for differential regulation. Introduced in katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna.
What it is

A probabilistic generative model of RNA-seq: isoforms are chosen by length-rescaled abundance and reads sampled uniformly along the mRNA; the posterior over isoform abundances Ψ is recovered by Bayes’ rule.
Produces Ŷ_MISO, an unbiased Ψ estimator that uses splice-junction reads, flanking constitutive-exon reads, and paired-end insert-length distributions.
Exon-centric (two-isoform) inference has an analytic solution; isoform-centric inference uses a hybrid MCMC sampler (Metropolis-Hastings + Gibbs with a logistic-normal proposal on the simplex).
Reports Bayesian confidence intervals for Ψ and detects differential expression via a Bayes factor over ΔΨ (Savage-Dickey density ratio).
Encodes isoforms generically as lists of exon coordinates, so it handles skipped exons, alternative 5’/3’ splice sites, alternative first/last exons, mutually exclusive exons, retained introns, and tandem alternative polyadenylation.
Software released at genes.mit.edu/burgelab/miso.

In this wiki

A method for quantifying percent-spliced-in and isoform abundance; part of splicing-methods.
Conceptually related to rmats for differential alternative-splicing analysis from RNA-seq, with a Bayesian rather than likelihood-ratio framing.

Sources

katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna

wiki/entities/mlof-score.title: mlof-score
wiki/entities/mlof-score.content:
mLOF score (missense loss-of-function likelihood score)
One-line: A protein-structure-based score that estimates whether a set of pathogenic missense variants acts through loss-of-function versus non-LOF (gain-of-function / dominant-negative) molecular mechanisms.
What it is

Combines two structural metrics of pathogenic missense variants: the extent of disease clustering (EDC, spatial clustering in 3D) and ΔΔGrank (rank-normalised predicted folding-stability impact via FoldX).
Computes marginal probabilities that observed EDC and ΔΔGrank are drawn from LOF vs. non-LOF gene distributions, combined into a single likelihood that variants have a LOF effect.
Used within a Bayesian framework: updates gene-level mechanism priors (pDN/GOF/LOF) into mechanism-specific posteriors (postDN/GOF/LOF).
Operates at the level of a variant set (e.g., all variants of one phenotype), not a single variant.

Key parameters / performance

Optimal LOF vs. non-LOF threshold = 0.508 (sensitivity 0.721, specificity 0.702, accuracy 0.712, F1 0.719 on dominant single-phenotype genes).
AUROC for postLOF up to 0.889-0.937 as stringency on residue count increases.

Access

Available as a Google Colab notebook at github.com/badonyi/mechanism-prediction for human protein-coding genes.

Source

Introduced in badonyi-2025-prevalence-loss-gain-dominant-negative.

wiki/entities/molm13-drna-seq.title: molm13-drna-seq
wiki/entities/molm13-drna-seq.content:
MOLM13 dRNA-seq resource
A deep Oxford Nanopore long-read direct RNA sequencing (dRNA-seq) dataset of the human MOLM13 leukemia cell line, introduced in kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rna-sequencing-of (Kim & Saville et al., Cell Genomics 2025). It simultaneously captures m6A methylation, poly(A) tail length, isoform/splicing, abundance, and stability on the same native mRNA molecules.
What it is

Samples: 6 PromethION dRNA-seq libraries — 4 control (scramble shRNA) and 2 METTL3-knockdown biological replicates of MOLM13 cells; plus actinomycin-D time-course (7 points) for mRNA half-life and a low-input total-RNA library to control for poly(A) enrichment bias.
Scale / quality: ~34M reads total (3–7M/sample), most reads >800 bp, Phred mostly >8–10 — substantially deeper than typical MinION human dRNA-seq (~1M reads/sample), enabling genome-wide feature-correlation analyses.
Chemistry: SQK_RNA002 kit. Processing pipeline: Minimap2 alignment, f5c “eventalign” signal segmentation, m6Anet (m6A), Nanopolish + tailfindR (poly(A)), Isoquant (isoforms), FLAIR (differential alternative splicing).
Availability: raw dRNA-seq and half-life sequencing data deposited at NCBI-SRA, accession PRJNA1135158; raw fast5 files are retained so the data can be re-mined for additional modifications (pseudouridine, m5C, inosine). No new code was produced.

In this wiki

The reusable resource deliverable of kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rna-sequencing-of; the paper’s findings on m6A, poly(A), and alternative-splicing crosstalk all derive from this dataset.
