U6/Pol III cassette, so the guide is captured as a transcript — no separate
barcode library, lower recombination risk.
Works on poly(dT) platforms (Stereo-seq, via mRNA-embedded capture) and probe
platforms (Visium HD, via direct spacer+scaffold capture).
Perturbed cells are transduced, sorted (GFP), injected into syngeneic mice;
after in vivo selection (metastasis, T cell infiltration), tissue is sectioned
and spatially sequenced to map sgRNA + transcriptome to location.

Standing

mRNA-embedded sgRNA matches genomic DNA sequencing (ρ up to 0.9970), beating
CROP-seq (0.8913), with 9.08× higher guide expression; scales to 9,040 sgRNAs.
Single-cell resolution via StarDist (95.54% of sgRNA counts assigned to cells);
benchmarked against the CROP-seq embedding system.

In this wiki

A functional-single-cell-genomics platform; extends pooled CRISPR /
Perturb-seq / CROP-seq into space; paired with its analysis toolkit
tardis-tool.

Sources

zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics — the method paper

wiki/entities/spliceai.title: spliceai
wiki/entities/spliceai.content:
SpliceAI
What: A landmark end-to-end deep-learning model of the splicing-code that predicts splice-sites directly from genomic sequence.
SpliceAI is a 32-layer deep residual convolutional neural network trained on human GENCODE sequence, learning features de novo rather than from predefined motifs capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. It marks a shift in the model lineage from core-motif scorers (PWMs, MaxEntScan) and feature-engineered integrative models toward learned representations, and it sits alongside related deep models — Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice — and feeds into large omics-prediction models (Borzoi, BigRNA) and self-supervised sequence language models.
How it works
The model is tasked to call donor and acceptor sites from raw sequence. Context window size is decisive: extending input from 80 nt to 400 nt raised top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95%, and going to 10,000 nt added a further 3–5% capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. Accuracy on lncRNAs (lacking coding bias) was similar to mRNAs (top-k ~84%). In silico mutagenesis showed it indirectly predicts nucleosome positions, contributing to exon-boundary determination (splicing-and-chromatin).
Significance & limits
SpliceAI predicts non-coding cryptic-splicing variants — estimated ~10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations — and underpins clinical splicing-and-disease workflows: it reached AUC 0.959 on BRCA1 near-splice-site variants and informs ClinGen/ACMG variant classification. Limits remain: exonic and distal variants are predicted poorly, tissue-specific prediction is weak, and black-box interpretability is an open problem.
Sources

capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — describes SpliceAI’s architecture, context-window effect, clinical use, and place in the splicing-code model lineage.

wiki/entities/splicescope.title: splicescope
wiki/entities/splicescope.content:
Splicescope
Splicescope is a computational method and software tool that models the alternative-splicing profile of any neuronal sample and assigns it to one of six neuronal maturation stages, introduced in weyn-vanhentenryck-2018-precise-temporal-regulation-of-alternative (Weyn-Vanhentenryck et al., Nature Communications 2018).
What it is

A maturation-stage classifier built on a developing-mouse-cortex reference (RNA-seq across 9 time points). It defines six stages (1–6) corresponding to cortex at E14.5, E16.5, P0, P4, P7, and P15-or-older.
Uses PCA of cortex-reference splicing profiles plus a regression model to project a new sample’s percent-spliced-in profile into a 2D maturation trajectory and predict its stage; ~96% accuracy on 56 samples with known ages (80% exact, +16% neighboring stage).
Validated/applied on 92 distinct neuronal samples (merged from 277 replicates across 28 studies): CNS tissues, purified neuron subtypes, and stem-cell-differentiated neurons.
Ships an RBP-specific variant: restricts the classifier to exons directly regulated by one of the four RBP families (Nova, Rbfox, Mbnl, ptbp1/Ptbp), isolating each family’s contribution to maturation (e.g. Mbnl-target-only model shows a larger stage drop on Mbnl1/2 dKO than the full model).
Key use cases in the paper: detecting that in-vitro ESC-derived neurons plateau at stage 4–5, and that peripheral sensory neurons (OSNs, photoreceptors) are predicted as very immature (stage 1) on Nova/Rbfox targets despite being adult cells.

In this wiki

A neurodevelopment-specific splicing-methods tool: unlike differential-splicing callers (rmats, leafcutter, suppa) it does not detect events but classifies developmental state from a precomputed splicing profile.
Operationalizes the early/late switch modules and the four-splicing-factors-family code described in its source paper.

Sources

weyn-vanhentenryck-2018-precise-temporal-regulation-of-alternative — introduces Splicescope, its six-stage reference, the RBP-specific variant, and all applications.

wiki/entities/splicestr.title: splicestr
wiki/entities/splicestr.content:
spliceSTR
A spliceSTR is a short tandem repeat (STR) whose copy number / repeat length
is significantly associated with one or more nearby alternative splicing events,
i.e. an STR-based splicing QTL. The term and the accompanying human-cortex
catalog were introduced in li-2026-the-contribution-of-short-tandem.
What it is

A class of splicing-qtl in which the causal/associated variant is a 1–6 bp
short tandem repeat rather than a SNP or short indel. Repeat dosage (summed
imputed allele length over both chromosomes) is regressed against exon
percent-spliced-in (PSI).
The deliverable is a catalog of 146,969 spliceSTRs (51,343 unique STRs ×
25,230 splicing events) mapped in 336 human dorsolateral prefrontal cortex
(DLPFC) samples (HBCC), at 5% FDR, plus a prioritized subset of 1,313 strictly
fine-mapped (and 21,893 leniently fine-mapped) candidate-causal spliceSTRs.
STRs were imputed (445,720 autosomal STRs) from a published SNP-STR reference
panel into SNP-array genotypes; splicing was quantified with rmats; STRs
within 100 kb of a splice event’s constitutive exons were tested. Crucially the
catalog includes homopolymers (~50% of all STRs), which a contemporaneous
brain study (Cui et al.) excluded.
Mechanistic annotations: candidate-causal spliceSTRs are T-rich, long, and
enriched within 100 bp upstream of affected exons (near the polypyrimidine
tract), and a subset overlap / modulate rna-binding-proteins binding sites
(e.g. HNRNPL–AC, TARDBP–GT). Released as a resource for complex-trait /
splicing-variant studies.

In this wiki

An STR-centric extension of the splicing-qtl concept, sibling to the
SNP/indel-based brain sQTL atlas in
zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls and the population-blood
sQTL/pQTL resource in tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants.
Splicing quantified with rmats (PSI / event-based), contrasting with
intron-ratio tools used by the studies it compares against.
Mechanism connects to rna-binding-proteins and to G-quadruplex–forming
repeats (g-quadruplex) for the SEPTIN3 AGG example.

Sources

li-2026-the-contribution-of-short-tandem

wiki/entities/splicetrap.title: splicetrap
wiki/entities/splicetrap.content:
SpliceTrap
SpliceTrap is a Bayesian method for quantifying local exon-inclusion ratios from paired-end RNA-seq data, estimating each exon’s inclusion as an independent inference problem instead of reconstructing full-length transcripts. Introduced in wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify.
What it is

A computational tool that reports the inclusion ratio of every exon under a single cellular condition, without requiring a background/reference read set.
Uses TXdb, a precompiled human exon-trio/duo database (RefSeq hg18 + EST-based dbCASE) of 224,995 trios/duos over 182,560 exons, as a mapping reference.
Detects the major AS classes: cassette exons (CA), alternative 5’ (AD) and 3’ (AA) splice sites, and intron retention (IR).
Combines a fragment-size prior (FSM) and an inclusion-ratio prior (IRM, beta-smoothed) in a Bayesian posterior maximized per exon; reads are aligned with Bowtie and filtered with a size-dependent dynamic coverage cutoff.
Outperformed Cufflinks, Scripture, and naive RPKM in simulation (PCC up to 0.87) and showed higher replicate robustness while reporting far more exons. Available via CSH Galaxy and rulai.cshl.edu/splicetrap.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods tool for RNA-seq alternative-splicing quantification.
Computes exon inclusion ratios, conceptually equivalent to percent-spliced-in.
Related to rmats as an RNA-seq exon-inclusion quantifier (SpliceTrap predates it and targets single-condition scoring).

Sources

wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify

wiki/entities/splicevault.title: splicevault
wiki/entities/splicevault.content:
SpliceVault
SpliceVault is a web portal + empirical method (Cooper lab, Sydney) that
predicts which mis-splicing event a splice-site variant will cause, introduced
in dawes-2022-splicevault-predicts-the-precise.
What it is

Built on 300K-RNA: unannotated splicing events tallied across 335,663
reference RNA-seq samples. The 300K-RNA Top-4 rule ranks the four most
common events around a splice site to predict the variant’s outcome — exon
skipping, cryptic-splice-site activation, or [[intron-retention|intron
retention]] — with no trained model, just observed rna-seq junction
frequencies.
Aimed at clinical variant interpretation (ACMG–AMP PVS1): says what
mis-splicing happens, not just whether splicing is disrupted.

Standing

92% sensitivity for the nature of mis-splicing; on 140 clinical cases,
96% of exon-skipping and 86% of cryptic events called — higher sensitivity
and PPV than SpliceAI.

In this wiki

The outcome-prediction complement to disruption scorers (spliceai,
absplice2) in the clinical/variant-effect splicing-methods cluster; an
empirical, data-driven alternative tied to the splicing-and-disease diagnostic
theme and splice-sites biology.

Sources

dawes-2022-splicevault-predicts-the-precise — the method paper

wiki/entities/spliser.title: spliser
wiki/entities/spliser.content:
SpliSER
One-line: Method computing the Splice-site Strength Estimate (SSE), quantifying usage of individual splice sites from RNA-seq — used as a direct, splice-site-level phenotype for GWAS (SpliSER-GWAS).
SpliSER quantifies how frequently an individual donor or acceptor site is used, giving a per-splice-site usage metric (SSE) rather than a gene-level or event-level measure (see dent-2025-a-basic-framework-to-explain). Dent et al. use SSE as the phenotype in SpliSER-GWAS, running >130,000 GWAS across three species (Arabidopsis, Drosophila, human GTEx heart) and detecting associations at 21,988 splice-sites, mostly in cis (78–91%). Compared to a published sQTL analysis on the same data, SpliSER-GWAS recovered 82% of mappable splice-site mutations. Related predictive tools referenced alongside it include spliceai and pangolin.
Referenced by

dent-2025-a-basic-framework-to-explain

wiki/entities/sprite.title: sprite
wiki/entities/sprite.content:
SPRITE
One-line: Split-Pool Recognition of Interactions by Tag Extension — a ligation-free method mapping higher-order DNA/RNA/protein contacts and nuclear-body organization (RD-SPRITE variant for RNA–DNA).
SPRITE uses iterative split-pool barcoding, rather than proximity ligation, to read out which molecules co-occur in the same crosslinked complex, capturing multi-way contacts and spatial organization around nuclear bodies. In the nuclear-speckle study it is used (with RD-SPRITE) to map DNA–RNA/protein contacts and derive a “speckle proximity score” that inversely correlates with microscopy distance to speckle foci (r = -0.72), showing speckle-close loci splice more efficiently (41.0% vs 19.1%) (see bhat-2024-genome-organization-around-nuclear-speckles). The method shares a lineage with Guttman-lab chromatin/RNA organization work, connecting nuclear-speckles biology to genome-architecture.
Referenced by

bhat-2024-genome-organization-around-nuclear-speckles

wiki/entities/srrm4.title: srrm4
wiki/entities/srrm4.content:
SRRM4 / SRRM3 (nSR100)
One-line: Neural/endocrine serine-arginine repetitive matrix splicing factors (SRRM4 = nSR100, and its paralog SRRM3) that drive the neuronal microexons inclusion program, disrupted in autism.
SRRM4 (nSR100) is a neuron-specific splicing factor that, via its eMIC domain, promotes inclusion of very short (3–27 nt) microexons enriched in neurons; its close paralog SRRM3 provides overlapping activity in neuronal and pancreatic-endocrine cells, so the program is usually attributed to the SRRM3/4 pair. The microexon program regulated by SRRM4 is misregulated in autism — e.g. reduced inclusion of the CPEB4 neuronal microexon drives condensate-to-aggregate conversion (see garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon). A microexon’s sensitivity to SRRM3/4 is set mainly by its core splicing architecture (splice-site strength, branch point/pyrimidine tract, exon length) and its intrinsic efficiency at recruiting early spliceosome components, rather than by SRRM-specific motifs; the UGC 3′ss motif facilitates but is not strictly required, and strengthening splice sites can render microexons SRRM4-independent (see bonnal-2025-core-splicing-architecture-and-early). SRRM3/4 sensitivity is conserved across ~450 My of vertebrate evolution.
Referenced by

garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon
bonnal-2025-core-splicing-architecture-and-early

wiki/entities/srsf1.title: srsf1
wiki/entities/srsf1.content:
SRSF1
What: The prototypical SR protein, a sequence-specific splicing factor that activates splicing and behaves as a proto-oncogene when overexpressed.
SRSF1 is a member of the SR-protein family of rna-binding-proteins that constitute the activating arm of the splicing-code. It typically binds exonic splicing enhancers (a class of splicing-regulatory-elements) and promotes inclusion of nearby exons during alternative-splicing. This is the classic counterpoint to hnRNP repressors like hnrnpa1: SR activators and hnRNP silencers act in a position-dependent manner, so the same factor or motif can promote or suppress an exon depending on where it binds lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
SRSF1 (ASF/SF2) can promote use of a proximal 5’ splice site (see splice-sites) by increasing U1 snRNP–pre-mRNA binding lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. An SF2/ASF-dependent exonic splicing enhancer in smn2 exon 7 illustrates how its loss alters disease-relevant splicing.
Disease / significance
SRSF1 overexpression acts as an oncogene in mice and qualifies the SRSF1 gene as a proto-oncogene in many human tumours, with targets in the mTORC1 pathway lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Its oncogenic function depends on methylation by PRMT5; SRSF1 also shuttles between nucleus and cytoplasm, making PRMT5 inhibitors candidate therapeutics (splicing-and-disease, splicing-therapeutics) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SR activator mechanism, proximal 5’ss/U1, proto-oncogene
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — proto-oncogene; PRMT5 methylation
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — PRMT5 dependence, nucleocytoplasmic shuttling

wiki/entities/srsf4.title: srsf4
wiki/entities/srsf4.content:
SRSF4
One-line: SR (serine/arginine-rich) splicing factor and major Clk-kinase substrate whose binding is enriched at detained introns responsive to CLK inhibition.
SRSF4 (Srsf4) is an SR protein whose phosphorylation state shifts sharply on Clk inhibition (CB19 treatment), marking it as a major clk-kinase substrate. Srsf3/Srsf4 iCLIP clusters are highly enriched at CB19-responsive detained introns (DIs), especially in RNA-processing genes: 36% (115/325) of DIs whose splicing responded to CB19 were bound by Srsf3, Srsf4, or both, and 78% of RNA-processing-gene DIs were SR-protein-bound versus 29% of other DIs — indicating many detained introns are direct SR-protein/Clk targets (see boutz-2015-detained-introns-are-a-novel).
Referenced by

boutz-2015-detained-introns-are-a-novel

wiki/entities/stau2.title: stau2
wiki/entities/stau2.content:
STAU2 (Staufen-2)
One-line: Double-stranded-RNA-binding protein, better known in mRNA transport/localization, identified as an unexpected exon-inclusion splicing regulator in a tethered-function screen.
STAU2 (Staufen double-stranded RNA-binding protein 2) was one of four “unexpected” hits (with trnau1ap, scaf8, rtca) from a tethered-function-splicing-reporter screen of 718 human RNA-binding proteins that scored capacity to drive exon inclusion (see schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability). It was validated as an endogenous splicing regulator by combining eCLIP, shRNA knockdown + RNA-seq, and affinity purification–mass spectrometry; its eCLIP motif is CCAGG-like. Whether STAU2 (and the other novel factors) regulate splicing directly or via effects on transcription elongation / RNA metabolism networks remains open.
Referenced by

schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability

wiki/entities/suppa.title: suppa
wiki/entities/suppa.content:
SUPPA
SUPPA is a fast computational tool that calculates relative inclusion (PSI / Ψ) values of alternative splicing events directly from transcript-level abundances (TPMs), rather than from reads mapped to the genome or to exon–exon junctions. Introduced in alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast.
What it is

Two-step pipeline: generateEvents derives splicing events from a GTF annotation (or a de novo reconstruction), and psiPerEvent computes Ψ per sample from transcript quantifications.
Defines Ψ as the abundance of inclusion-form transcripts (F1) divided by the abundance of transcripts in either form (F1∪F2), assigning each event a unique identifier encoding gene, event type, coordinates, and strand.
Handles event types SE (exon skipping), A5/A3 (alternative 5′/3′ splice sites), MX (mutually exclusive exons), RI (intron retention), and AF/AL (alternative first/last exons).
Quantifier-agnostic: benchmarked with Sailfish and RSEM; >1000× faster than read-based methods (MATS, MISO) for the Ψ step.
Implemented in Python 2.7 with no Python-library dependencies, MIT-licensed (bitbucket.org/regulatorygenomicsupf/suppa).
Accuracy depends on annotation completeness (full-length transcripts as in RefSeq outperform incomplete Ensembl annotation); this version does not propagate quantification uncertainty into Ψ.

In this wiki

A transcript-quantification-based alternative within splicing-methods, contrasting with read/junction-based event detectors like rmats.
Outputs the percent-spliced-in metric for alternative-splicing events.

Sources

alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast

wiki/entities/t2t-chm13.title: t2t-chm13
wiki/entities/t2t-chm13.content:
T2T-CHM13
What: The first complete, gapless telomere-to-telomere assembly of a human genome, produced by the Telomere-to-Telomere (T2T) Consortium and introduced in nurk-2022-complete-sequence-of-a-human.
Details:

3,054,815,472 bp of nuclear DNA covering all 22 autosomes and chromosome X (no chromosome Y), plus a 16,569-bp mitochondrial genome; released as version T2T-CHM13v1.1.
Derived from the near-uniformly homozygous CHM13hTERT hydatidiform-mole cell line (46,XX), assembled from PacBio HiFi and Oxford Nanopore ultralong reads with Illumina, Hi-C, BioNano, and Strand-seq validation.
Adds or corrects 238 Mbp of sequence non-syntenic to GRCh38, including all centromeric satellite arrays, recent segmental duplications, the short arms of the five acrocentric chromosomes, and 219 complete rDNA copies (9.9 Mbp).
Estimated consensus accuracy ~1 error per 10 Mbp; only ~0.3% of bases flagged as potential issues (vs ~8% for GRCh38). Annotation: 63,494 genes / 233,615 transcripts (19,969 protein-coding).
Serves as a more complete and representative reference for short- and long-read variant calling across human samples of all ancestries.

wiki/entities/tada-a.title: tada-a
wiki/entities/tada-a.content:
TADA-A (TADA-Annotations)
What: A statistical framework for mapping risk genes from de novo mutations (DNMs) in whole-genome-sequencing (WGS) studies, extending the earlier tada-method from coding-only analysis to genome-wide, annotation-aware analysis.
Summary
TADA-A models de novo mutation counts per genomic position as a Poisson process whose rate is elevated inside risk genes, with the fold increase driven by functional annotations in a log-linear model. It works in two stages: (1) a mutation model that calibrates study-specific background mutation rates via Poisson GLM (adjusting for coding status, promoter status, GC content, and local human-macaque divergence); (2) a functional model that learns which annotations (conservation, enhancer marks, splicing predictions) are informative of pathogenic mutations and computes per-gene Bayes factors.
Key features

Accepts an arbitrary number of possibly overlapping annotations; learns their relative risks from data via feature selection (no fixed weighting scheme or variant filtering).
Combines coding and non-coding evidence by multiplying Bayes factors; coding information can come from an independent WES study.
Supports meta-analysis across multiple WGS studies by fitting a separate background mutation model per study while sharing annotation parameters.
Software freely available on GitHub for all research uses.

Origin
Introduced by Liu et al., The American Journal of Human Genetics, 2018 (liu-2018-statistical-framework-mapping-risk).

wiki/entities/tada-method.title: tada-method
wiki/entities/tada-method.content:
TADA (Transmission and De novo Association test)
One-line: A Bayesian statistical framework that integrates de novo and inherited (transmitted) rare variants across mutation classes into a single per-gene association score and FDR q value for risk-gene discovery.
What it is

Introduced by He et al. (2013) for autism gene discovery; combines evidence from de novo loss-of-function (dnLoF), de novo missense, and transmitted rare variants under a gene-specific relative-risk model.
Outputs a per-gene Bayes factor and FDR q value; genes at FDR ≤ 0.1 are called risk genes.
In Sanders et al. (2015), TADA was extended to incorporate small de novo deletions (dnCNVs affecting a single/few genes) alongside SNV and indel exome data, yielding 65 ASD risk genes when combined with ASC and AGP data.

wiki/entities/tango.title: tango
wiki/entities/tango.content:
TANGO (Targeted Augmentation of Nuclear Gene Output)
One-line: An antisense-oligonucleotide platform that upregulates a target gene by suppressing a naturally occurring, nonproductive alternative-splicing event (a poison exon) so more transcript escapes nonsense-mediated decay and yields productive mRNA and protein.
What it is

A splice-modulating ASO strategy: many genes harbor a conserved poison exon whose inclusion introduces a premature termination codon, routing the transcript to nonsense-mediated decay. TANGO ASOs block inclusion of that exon, shifting output toward the productive isoform.
Because it boosts the wild-type allele, TANGO is aimed at autosomal-dominant haploinsufficiency disorders, compensating for a nonfunctional mutant allele rather than editing DNA.
Contrasted with viral gene therapy: reversible, nonimmunogenic, and independent of gene size.

Origin / notes

Named and applied in han-2020-antisense-oligonucleotides-increase-scn1a-expression (Stoke Therapeutics; Isom lab), which used the lead TANGO ASO-22 against SCN1A exon 20N to rescue a Dravet-syndrome mouse model.
A general alternative-splicing-based approach applicable to other haploinsufficiency genes carrying targetable nonproductive splicing events.

wiki/entities/tardis-tool.title: tardis-tool
wiki/entities/tardis-tool.content:
TARDIS
TARDIS (target prioritization toolkit for perturbation data in spatial omics)
is a statistical toolkit for analyzing spatial transcriptomics with perturbations,
introduced in zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics as
the analysis companion to spac-seq.
What it is

Spatially aligns sgRNA reads with the transcriptome and preprocesses spatial
sgRNA libraries.
Statistically models both global perturbation effects and local enrichments
(e.g. immune-infiltrated vs immune-excluded tumor niches).
Performs downstream spatial pathway analysis to prioritize functional targets
from spatially resolved phenotypic readouts.

Standing

Used to prioritize perturbations across spatial niches: identified 138 / 48 of
221 clonal perturbations as enriched in immune-infiltrated / excluded niches,
flagging Icam1 loss as both expanded and immune-excluded.

In this wiki

The analysis half of the spac-seq platform; a functional-single-cell-genomics
toolkit with ai-in-genomics statistical/spatial-niche modeling.

Sources

zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics — the method paper

wiki/entities/tass-catalogue.title: tass-catalogue
wiki/entities/tass-catalogue.content:
TASS catalogue
The TASS catalogue is an extended, RNA-seq-based catalogue of tandem
alternative splice sites (closely spaced ≤30 nt alternative donor/acceptor
splice-sites) in healthy human tissues, built from 8,548 GTEx samples and
distributed as a UCSC Genome Browser track hub. Introduced in
mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem.
What it is

A list of ~151k analyzed splice sites in TASS clusters, integrating annotated
(GENCODE v19 + RefSeq), GTEx-expressed (de novo), and spliceai-scored
cryptic sites; 115,912 expressed TASS and 45,739 expressed minor splice
sites (miSS).
Per-cluster ranking into a major splice site (maSS) and minor splice
sites (miSS), quantified by the relative-usage metric φ (a single-ended
TASS analogue of percent-spliced-in).
Annotations layered on top: significance (zero-inflated Poisson model, 9,303
significant miSS), tissue-specificity (2,496), NAGNAG/GYNNGY membership,
RBP-regulation predictions (rmats-turbo knockdown + eCLIP), protein
structural impact (disordered regions, SLiMs, PTM sites), and evolutionary
selection / noise-fraction estimates.
Substantially extends the EST-based TASSDB2 database (+~51k TASS); delivered as
a Genome Browser track hub (TASS indels, structural annotation, tissue-specific
expression) plus a supplementary table.

In this wiki

A reusable splicing-methods resource; uses rmats (rMATS-turbo novelSS)
for the RBP-knockdown analysis and spliceai to define cryptic sites.
Surfaces concrete rna-binding-proteins regulation, notably a QKI miSS
repressed by ptbp1.
Its φ metric is a relative of percent-spliced-in.

Sources

mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem

wiki/entities/tdp-43.title: tdp-43
wiki/entities/tdp-43.content:
TDP-43 (TARDBP)
One-line: Nuclear RNA-binding protein and splicing regulator, aggregated in the cytoplasm in >95% of ALS and >50% of Alzheimer’s cases; represses cryptic exons (STMN2, UNC13A) and sustains long brain pre-mRNAs.
TDP-43 (encoded by TARDBP) is a predominantly nuclear RNA-binding protein whose disease pathology involves nuclear clearance plus cytoplasmic aggregation, implicating loss of function. CLIP-seq shows it binds UG-rich, mostly distal-intronic sites across thousands of genes and is required to sustain levels of very long, brain-enriched pre-mRNAs — many encoding synaptic proteins — with ASO knockdown altering ~965 splicing events and hundreds of mRNA levels; it also autoregulates its own transcript via NMD (see polymenidou-2011-long-pre-mrna-depletion-and-rna). In neuronal aging, TDP-43 mislocalizes from nucleus to cytoplasm, reducing its intronic binding and de-repressing ALS-linked cryptic exons in STMN2 and UNC13A while dysregulating ~500 transcripts — a mechanistic bridge from aging to neurodegeneration (see rhine-2025-neuronal-aging-causes-mislocalization-of).
Referenced by

polymenidou-2011-long-pre-mrna-depletion-and-rna
rhine-2025-neuronal-aging-causes-mislocalization-of

wiki/entities/teneurin.title: teneurin
wiki/entities/teneurin.content:
Teneurin (TENM)
One-line: Presynaptic transmembrane adhesion molecule and latrophilin ligand that clusters postsynaptic condensates during synapse formation.
Teneurins (TENM family) are presynaptic transmembrane cell-adhesion proteins that, together with flrt, act as ligands for the postsynaptic adhesion GPCR latrophilin-3 (lphn3). In the latrophilin-3 splicing study, presynaptic teneurin and FLRT cluster LPHN3-coated postsynaptic condensates into higher-order assemblies, linking trans-synaptic adhesion to postsynaptic specialization; extracellular Lphn3 exons E6/E9 mediate teneurin binding (see wang-2024-alternative-splicing-of-latrophilin-3-controls). Dimeric Teneurin-2 and FLRT3 synergistically cluster LPHN3-E31-coated condensates, an effect lost with the E32 isoform or PBM deletion.
Referenced by

wang-2024-alternative-splicing-of-latrophilin-3-controls

wiki/entities/tiger-model.title: tiger-model
wiki/entities/tiger-model.content:
TIGER model
One-line: Deep-learning model (Wessels et al. 2023) predicting RNA-targeting Cas13d guide-RNA efficiency from the 23 bp target sequence; adapted here for exon-exon-junction-targeting guides.
TIGER predicts cas13d gRNA efficiency almost entirely from the local 23 bp gRNA/target sequence. For isoform-specific knockdown by targeting exon-exon junctions (EEJs), the authors retrain adaptations TIGER_junction, TIGER_bass, and TIGER_site; SHAP analysis confirms predictive signal comes from local sequence with negligible contribution from broader context or RBP binding (see schertzer-2025-cas13d-mediated-isoform-specific-rna-knockdown-with). Genome-wide, TIGER predicts ~899,673 active EEJ gRNAs, implying up to ~89% of isoforms from multi-isoform genes are uniquely targetable. Its predictions feed the isoviz R package for designing and visualizing isoform-specific knockdown experiments.
Referenced by

schertzer-2025-cas13d-mediated-isoform-specific-rna-knockdown-with

wiki/entities/trade.title: trade
wiki/entities/trade.content:
TRADE
TRADE (TRanscriptome-wide Analysis of Differential Expression) is a statistical framework that estimates the transcriptome-wide distribution of true differential-expression effects from noisy gene-level measurements, rather than testing individual genes against a significance threshold.
What it does

Takes differential-expression point estimates and standard errors as input (computed via DESeq2 on pseudo-bulk count matrices), then fits a flexible mixture model (using ash) to the estimated effects, incorporating standard errors to account for sampling variation.
Recovers the distribution of true log2(Fold Change) effects, distinguishing properties of a cellular response from properties of an experiment (sample size, sequencing depth).

Key derived metrics

Transcriptome-wide impact (TI) — Var(β), the variance of the effect-size distribution; a depth-stable estimator of a perturbation’s overall transcriptional effect.
Effective number of differentially expressed genes (π_eff) — a kurtosis-based measure of how evenly differential expression is spread across the transcriptome, without an arbitrary zero/near-zero cutoff.
Gene-set enrichments — perturbation impact enrichment and perturbation response enrichment.
Transcriptome-wide impact correlation — a bivariate extension estimating the correlation of effects across perturbations, cell types, dosages, or case/control comparisons, corrected for sampling-variance-induced downward bias.

Origin
Introduced by Nadig, Replogle et al. (bioRxiv 2024) and applied to genome-scale Perturb-seq screens and neuropsychiatric case/control differential-expression data.

wiki/entities/trex-complex.title: trex-complex
wiki/entities/trex-complex.content:
TREX Complex
One-line: The transcription-export (TREX) complex — ALYREF, UAP56/DDX39B, and the THO subcomplex — that packages spliced mRNA for NXF1–NXT1-mediated nuclear export.
TREX is recruited to spliced messages via the cap-binding complex and the exon-junction-complex: CBC-bound ALYREF is deposited upstream of EJCs in an EIF4A3-dependent manner, recruits UAP56 (DDX39B) and THO, and after SARNP remodeling hands the mRNA off to TREX2 and the NXF1–NXT1 export receptor at the nuclear pore. This makes TREX a key effector by which the splicing-history mark of the EJC couples completed splicing to export (see abshire-2025-gene-regulation-through-exon-junction).
Referenced by

abshire-2025-gene-regulation-through-exon-junction

wiki/entities/trike.title: trike
wiki/entities/trike.content:
TriKE (Trispecific Killer Engager)
What it is: An engineered antibody-based therapeutic platform for natural killer (NK) cell immunotherapy. A TriKE is a single polypeptide combining three functional modules: an Fv domain targeting a tumour antigen, an Fv domain engaging the NK activating receptor CD16, and an intervening IL-15 moiety that stimulates NK cell activation, survival and proliferation. By omitting the antibody Fc and binding CD16 directly through an Fv, TriKEs bind both the 158V and 158F CD16 polymorphisms with strong, equal affinity and reduce Fc-mediated off-target effects.
Key facts

Related to the two-module bispecific killer engager (BiKE), which links a tumour-antigen Fv and a CD16 Fv without the IL-15 component.
The built-in IL-15 distinguishes TriKEs from BiKEs, enhancing NK activation, persistence and expansion.
Preclinical TriKEs target CD133 (cancer stem cells), CD19 (Burkitt lymphoma, CLL) and CLEC12A (AML), among others.
A CD33-targeting TriKE has entered phase I/II clinical trials for MDS, relapsed/refractory AML and advanced systemic mastocytosis (NCT03214666).
Part of a broader class of multispecific NK-cell engagers that also includes tumour-antigen/CD16/NKp46 or NKp30 trispecific constructs.

Appears in

maskalenko-2022-harnessing-natural-killer-cells

wiki/entities/trnau1ap.title: trnau1ap
wiki/entities/trnau1ap.content:
TRNAU1AP
One-line: A tRNA-selenocysteine-associated RNA-binding protein (also known as SECp43) identified as an unexpected endogenous regulator of alternative splicing that drives exon inclusion.
In a tethered-function screen of 718 human RBPs, TRNAU1AP scored as one of four “unexpected” hits and was validated endogenously by eCLIP, shRNA knockdown + RNA-seq, and AP-MS. Its knockdown alters >500 skipped-exon events, and it commonly binds the intron downstream of exons that are skipped upon depletion (consistent with promoting inclusion). It operates within splicing co-regulatory networks — e.g. binding the intron flanking a poison exon in the splicing factor PRPF39, where knockdown eliminates that poison exon and raises PRPF39 mRNA (46→117 TPM) and protein. Its exon-inclusion activity maps to a C-terminal effector domain (a separable RNA-binding + effector modular architecture), and a dCas13d fusion can drive inclusion independent of MS2–MCP tethering. eCLIP motifs resemble RBFOX2/hnRNPC-like intronic patterns (see schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability).
Referenced by

schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability

wiki/entities/trpv1.title: trpv1
wiki/entities/trpv1.content:
TRPV1
One-line: The heat- and capsaicin-activated transient receptor potential cation channel whose function is tuned by tissue-specific alternative splicing.
TRPV1 is the canonical noxious-heat sensor. In vampire bats (Desmodus rotundus), trigeminal-ganglion-specific alternative-splicing produces a truncated short isoform (TRPV1-S) lacking 62 C-terminal amino acids via inclusion of a stop-codon-bearing alternative exon (exon 14a). TRPV1-S has a heat-activation threshold lowered to ~30 °C (vs ~40 °C for the long form), enabling infrared detection of warm-blooded prey, and it constitutes 35–46% of TRPV1 transcripts in trigeminal ganglia but <3% in dorsal root ganglia — a striking example of alternative splicing tuning a channel for evolutionary adaptation (see gracheva-2011-ganglion-specific-splicing-of-trpv1-underlies). Short and long isoforms co-assemble into heteromultimeric channels with an intermediate threshold.
Referenced by

gracheva-2011-ganglion-specific-splicing-of-trpv1-underlies

wiki/entities/trrosettarna2.title: trrosettarna2
wiki/entities/trrosettarna2.content:
trRosettaRNA2
trRosettaRNA2 is an end-to-end deep learning method (~30M parameters) for
RNA 3D structure and conformer prediction from the
Yang lab, introduced in wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure.
What it is

Four-stage pipeline (SS prior → RNAformer → SS-aware invariant-point-attention
structure module → PyRosetta refinement) whose signature trick is
SS-weighted softmax: a pre-trained secondary-structure prior biases the 3D
attention maps, compensating for scarce RNA 3D training data.
Embeds trRNA2-SS, a standalone state-of-the-art RNA secondary-structure
predictor (bpRNA pre-train → PDB fine-tune).
Sampling diverse SS inputs lets it generate alternative RNA conformers
without experimental data.

Standing

Top automated server group (Yang-Server) at CASP16; competitive with AlphaFold 3
on TS28 (RMSD 8.66 Å vs 9.14 Å) with far fewer clashes and ~1/12 the parameters.
Limitations: two-/single-chain RNA only (no RNA–protein/ligand complexes), crop
size ≤384 nt.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence AI-for-structural-biology method; a sibling of
genomics sequence models like spliceai in baking biological priors into the
network.

Sources

wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure — the method paper

wiki/entities/txpert.title: txpert
wiki/entities/txpert.content:
TxPert
TxPert is a latent-transfer deep learning model (Valence Labs / Recursion) for
single-cell-perturbation-modeling that fuses multiple gene–gene
knowledge-graphs, introduced in
wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge.
What it is

A basal-state encoder (control profiles) + a GNN perturbation encoder over
interaction graphs, combined by additive latent shift and decoded to the
perturbed transcriptome.
Handles three OOD tasks: unseen single perturbations, novel doubles, and
perturbations in unseen cell lines.
Key finding: combining complementary graphs (STRING, GO + screen-derived
PxMap/TxMap) is what drives performance.

Standing

Approaches split-half experimental reproducibility for unseen singles in 3/4 cell
lines; +8–25% Pearson Δ over prior methods; beats GEARS (which falls below a
nonlearned baseline).
Documented failure mode: does not predict the target gene’s own knockdown; relies
partly on proprietary graphs.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence method; sibling to cmonge and
signature-tool in the single-cell cluster.

Sources

wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge — the method paper

wiki/entities/u-net.title: u-net
wiki/entities/u-net.content:
U-Net
One-line: A convolutional encoder–decoder neural-network architecture with skip connections, whose downsample-then-upsample design lets a model integrate long input context while emitting fine-resolution outputs.
Originally developed for biomedical image segmentation, the U-Net’s contracting/expanding path is well suited to genomic sequence-to-track models that must reconcile long-range context with base-level precision. In AlphaGenome, a U-Net-inspired backbone combined with transformers processes 1 Mb of sequence (capturing distal enhancer–gene regulation) while still emitting base-resolution regulatory tracks, resolving the usual trade-off between context length and output resolution (see avsec-2026-advancing-regulatory-variant-effect-prediction).
Referenced by

avsec-2026-advancing-regulatory-variant-effect-prediction

wiki/entities/u2af1.title: u2af1
wiki/entities/u2af1.content:
U2AF1
What: The small subunit of the U2AF heterodimer that recognizes the 3’ splice-site AG dinucleotide during intron 3’-end definition.
U2AF1 (also called U2AF35) is one of the splicing-factors that define the 3’ end of introns at splice-sites, acting alongside SF1, sf3b1 and U2 snRNP rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
The U2AF heterodimer (U2AF1–U2AF2/U2AF65) binds three adjacent 3’-end sequence motifs: U2AF1 contacts the 3’ss AG dinucleotide while U2AF2 binds the polypyrimidine tract rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. This makes U2AF1 a determinant of 3’ss selection. As a RNA-binding step in 3’-end recognition, its binding can be modulated — e.g. m6A at the 3’ss AG inhibits U2AF1 binding, leading to intron retention rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Disease & therapeutics
U2AF1 (U2AF35) is recurrently somatically mutated in myeloid cancers including myelodysplastic syndrome lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Mutations alter which flanking-nucleotide 3’ss are selected: S34F/Y mutants favour inclusion of exons with CAG 3’ss and disfavour UAG, whereas Q157P/R mutants favour AG/A 3’ss rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing (splicing-and-disease). U2AF1, SRSF2 and sf3b1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — 3’ss recognition, S34/Q157 mutant effects, m6A
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — U2AF35 recurrent mutations in MDS/CLL
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — convergence of myeloid splicing-factor mutations

wiki/entities/uniair.title: uniair
wiki/entities/uniair.content:
UniAIR
UniAIR is a modular, multimodal sequence–structure framework for
generalizable mutation-effect-prediction (binding ΔΔG) across adaptive
immune-recognition (antibody–antigen and TCR–pMHC), introduced in
han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across.
What it is

ESSM fusion backbone — an S-Former co-attention transformer coupling a
protein language model (ESM2) with geometric
structure.
MoFPE — a mixture of four frozen structure-pretrained experts combined via a
structure-aware gating network (multimodal-learning).
UniAIR-LT — a latent adapter aligning predicted-structure embeddings to
experimental ones, for structure-free use.

Standing

State of the art on SKEMPIv2 (PCC 0.684); generalizes to held-out antibody and
TCR test sets; used zero-shot for antibody/peptide optimization validated by FEP.
Limitation: protein–protein interfaces only (no small molecules/nucleic acids).

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence / ai-in-genomics method; safety-adjacent
(viral escape, immunogen design) — cf.
hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation.

Sources

han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across — the method paper

wiki/entities/washu-comparative-epigenom.title: washu-comparative-epigenome-browser
wiki/entities/washu-comparative-epigenom.content:
WashU Comparative Epigenome Browser (Brain M1 data hub)
Introduced in zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory. A public genome-browser data hub for viewing cross-species primary motor cortex (M1) epigenome tracks, hosted on the WashU Epigenome Browser (epigenome.wustl.edu/BrainComparativeEpigenome/).
What it is

Displays cell-type-resolved multiomic tracks (Hi-C, RNA, ATAC and CG methylation) for human, macaque, marmoset and mouse M1, with cross-species genome alignments (e.g. hg38 vs rheMac10).
Backs the study’s comparative analysis of candidate cis-regulatory elements (cis-regulatory-elements), differentially methylated regions and TAD boundaries across 21 shared brain cell types.

In this wiki

The public browsing resource accompanying zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory; a human-brain-evolution / chromatin-accessibility data hub.

Sources

zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory

wiki/entities/won-developing-brain-hic-d.title: won-developing-brain-hic-dataset
wiki/entities/won-developing-brain-hic-d.content:
Won developing-brain Hi-C dataset
High-resolution Hi-C chromatin-contact maps of mid-gestation human cerebral cortex, profiled separately for the cortical/subcortical plate (CP, post-mitotic neurons) and germinal zone (GZ, neural progenitors). Deposited in GEO/dbGaP. Widely reused as a reference for assigning noncoding regulatory variants and enhancers to distal target genes during human corticogenesis, and for tissue-specific interpretation of psychiatric GWAS loci.

wiki/entities/zscape.title: zscape
wiki/entities/zscape.content:
ZSCAPE (zebrafish single-cell atlas of perturbed embryos)
Introduced in saunders-2023-embryo-scale-reverse-genetics. ZSCAPE is an individual-resolved, whole-organism single-cell transcriptomic atlas of zebrafish development paired with a systematic F0 CRISPR perturbation dataset, designed to phenotype gene function as organism-wide changes in cell-type abundance.
What it is

Two components. (1) An annotated wild-type reference atlas: ~1,167 individuals, ~1.2M cells, 19 timepoints (18–96 hpf), hierarchically labeled into 33 tissues / 99 broad cell types / 156 subtypes. (2) A perturbation dataset: 23 genetic perturbations (crispants), ~645 perturbed individuals / ~2M cells. Combined ~1,812 embryos and ~3.2M cells.
Method stack. sci-RNA-seq3 combinatorial-indexing scRNA-seq + sci-Plex oligonucleotide hashing (recovers embryo-of-origin for ~70% of QC-passing nuclei) + F0 CRISPR–Cas9 RNP injection at the one-cell stage.
Analysis. Mutant cells are projected onto the reference and annotated by approximate nearest neighbor; cell-type-abundance variance is modeled with a gamma-response GLM; differentially abundant cell types (DACTs) are called with beta-binomial regression.
Purpose. Reusable resource for mapping genetic dependencies of zebrafish cell types—including rare populations (<1% of the embryo) such as cranial sensory ganglia—via replicated (≥8 embryos/condition) individual-embryo phenotyping.

In this wiki

A whole-organism instance of single-cell-perturbation-modeling and an arrayed cousin of the pooled single-cell-crispr-screen paradigm (dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening).
A functional-single-cell-genomics atlas with deep coverage of neurodevelopment and diverse neuronal-cell-types.

Sources

saunders-2023-embryo-scale-reverse-genetics

wiki/overview.title: overview
wiki/overview.content:
Overview
The evolving top-level thesis of this wiki. It now spans eleven topics:

splicing — pre-mRNA / alternative splicing (138 sources, 1968–2026)
artificial-intelligence — AI/ML in biology & genomics: foundation models,
sequence/structure, single-cell & perturbation methods + safety (23 sources, 2012–2026)
3d-genome — 3D organization of the genome, its sequence-based prediction, and its
evolution (7 sources, 2016–2026)
gene-regulation — cis-regulatory genomics: enhancers, promoters, TF-binding
grammar, enhancer–promoter logic, regulatory variation & reference maps
(27 sources, 2009–2024)
functional-single-cell-genomics — experimental/resource genomics: single-cell
omics, MPRA, digital twins, spatial methods, perturbation & regulatory-variant
function (22 sources, 2016–2026)
crispr — CRISPR-Cas biology, genome editing, functional screens & gene therapy;
mechanism → tool → screen → therapy → safety (19 sources, 2014–2026)
brain-development-neurogenomics — brain development biology, neuronal cell types,
brain evolution & neuropsychiatric disease genetics, incl. a deep autism/NDD
genetic-architecture strand (70 sources, 2002–2026)
biometrical-genetics — heritability estimation (decomposing twin/family trait
variance into genetic & environmental components via (generalized) linear mixed
models) + the missing-heritability debate + multiple-testing/FDR & association
statistics (13 sources, 2002–2023)
variant-effect — variant effect & interpretation: deleteriousness scoring, ACMG
clinical classification, gain/loss-of-function mechanism, VUS resolution
(5 sources, 2014–2025)
genome-sequencing-and-references — genome sequencing & reference resources:
draft → T2T → pangenome assemblies, variant calling, data formats, genomic medicine
(10 sources, 2001–2024)
cancer-immunology — anti-tumour immunity & immunotherapy, centred on natural
killer cells (4 sources, 2018–2022)

Grounded strictly in the locally ingested sources. See index for the full
per-topic catalog.

Cross-topic threads: (1) deep-learning models of biological sequence connect
splicing and AI — the splicing-code / spliceai work is one instance of
the foundation-models discussed under artificial-intelligence;
(2) chromatin links splicing and the 3D genome — splicing-and-chromatin
meets genome-architecture.

Current thesis — topic: splicing
RNA splicing is a central layer of gene regulation, and across these sources
the field’s center of gravity has shifted from how splicing works to how it
is controlled and read:

The machinery is essentially solved. The spliceosome is a large,
dynamic, RNA-catalysed machine (a ribozyme related to group II introns), and
cryo-EM has resolved most of its states
(lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative,
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). Remaining questions are
about regulation, not chemistry.
Outcomes are set by a position-dependent code. Weak metazoan
splice-sites + splicing-regulatory-elements read by splicing-factors
determine isoform choice. Modeling this splicing-code has progressed from
position weight matrices to deep-learning models that now interpret ~10% of
pathogenic rare-disease variants
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the), and has matured
into tissue-specific and genome-wide variant-effect predictors
(you-2024-splicetransformer-predicts-tissue-specific-splicing-linked,
AlphaGenome,
pangolin) whose remaining limits — deep-intronic effects, isoform-level
reconstruction, and trans-acting/RBP context — are now explicitly mapped
(shen-2026-advances-and-challenges-of-splicing).
Timing is itself regulatory. Splicing is largely co-transcriptional, but
a large fraction (~40%) is post-transcriptional-splicing, gating mRNA
export and translation (choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Function vs noise is unresolved — but the “noise” is being quantified.
alternative-splicing occurs in ~95–100% of human multiexon genes, but how
much is functional is debated (see Open questions). Much apparent noise is in
fact regulated turnover: unproductive-splicing coupled to
nonsense-mediated-decay is ~3× more pervasive than steady-state estimates
(~15% of transcript molecules; fair-2024-global-impact-of-unproductive-splicing),
and microexons — 3–27 nt neuronal exons read by srrm4 — are a small but
sharply functional class whose mis-splicing drives disease
(CPEB4 in autism).
Mechanism is becoming medicine. Splicing-factor mutations drive cancer and
genetic disease (splicing-and-disease) — now extended to germline
SF3B1 and de novo spliceosomal-snrna
(ReNU syndrome)
neurodevelopmental disorders; splicing-targeted drugs are approved and
expanding (splicing-therapeutics, antisense-oligonucleotides,
risdiplam).
A mature measurement-and-genetics stack underpins all of it. A decade-plus of
rna-seq tooling now quantifies splicing two ways — isoform-based
(cufflinks, kallisto, suppa) and event-based (rmats/rmats-turbo,
miso, leafcutter, majiq-v2, dexseq) — and population genetics maps
sQTLs linking variation to disease
(park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative). Tools disagree, so the
open methodological question is which readout to trust when.

Open questions

Functionality of AS — ✅ Mostly reconciled (see
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate): the apparent
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative (functional) vs
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source (noise) clash is largely a
difference in the definition of “functional”, the denominator counted, and the
evidence weighted — not a factual contradiction. Both agree on a conserved
functional core; residual uncertainty is the size of the low-abundance tail,
resolvable only with long-read/single-cell/CRISPR data.
Co- vs post-transcriptional timing trigger — mechanism unknown
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Interpretable, tissue-specific splicing-code models — still open
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).

Current thesis — topic: artificial-intelligence
Across ten sources, foundation-models have become general-purpose
biological tools spanning every modality: DNA (crossdna), protein
(uniair), RNA structure (trrosettarna2), single cells (the FMs
signature-tool interprets), and single-cell dynamics (regvelo couples
RNA velocity with a learnable gene regulatory network). Two patterns recur: (1) injecting domain priors
beats blind scale — secondary-structure priors, explicit cross-strand symmetry,
gene knowledge-graphs, and optimal-transport let small models beat ones
orders of magnitude larger; (2) generalization to the unseen (drugs, cell
lines, mutations, conformers) is the shared frontier, limited by
data-quality-and-bias, and often checked by direct measurement
(massively-parallel-reporter-assay, ΔPSI) where models
are weak. Safety remains the backdrop: generative capability keeps
dual-use-risk immediate and no single ai-safety safeguard suffices,
motivating layered measures plus ai-governance
(hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation). The newest entry
pushes deep learning into population genetics: flare scores billions of
noncoding-variant effects from chromatin-accessibility
models and folds in evolutionary constraint, linking the AI methods to
functional-single-cell-genomics atlases and the
biometrical-genetics missing-heritability debate
(marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding). A second statistical-genetics
entry pushes expression imputation beyond cis: co-expression-network models
(ingene-module) inject distal trans signal (gene-coexpression-network) into
TWAS, expanding the imputable transcriptome and
surfacing hundreds of new schizophrenia genes in human brain
(rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl).
Current thesis — topic: 3d-genome
One source so far (che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome): nuclear
genome-architecture (loops → TADs → compartments) is an evolvable trait.
It evolves fastest in cancer (3d-genome-and-disease), diverges between close
species mainly via cis-regulatory-elements within conserved frameworks, and
recurs across kingdoms through convergent evolution with distinct machinery
(3d-genome-evolution). Thin until more sources are ingested.
Current thesis — topic: functional-single-cell-genomics
These sources frame the measurement / resource counterpart to the AI methods:
a single-cell proteomic atlas of the developing human brain
(wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of, single-cell-proteomics), an
MPRA mapping >30,000 cis-regulatory mutations in sorghum
(groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at,
massively-parallel-reporter-assay), and digital twins of ex vivo human lungs
(zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex, digital-twins). The unifying thesis:
direct, high-throughput measurement still beats prediction where models are
weakest (mRNA→protein inference, activating regulatory variants), and these
resources are exactly the atlases, variant maps, and platforms the
artificial-intelligence topic’s models learn from and are tested against.
The endogenous-perturbation foundations that grew up here — the
pooled-crispr-screen family — have now been promoted to their own crispr topic.
What remains in this topic is the reporter-assay / atlas measurement stack, including a
human-neural massively-parallel-reporter-assay of eight psychiatric disorders
(lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric) that turns regulatory
correlation into causation and validates the variants the
artificial-intelligence models nominate.
Current thesis — topic: crispr
Twelve sources trace CRISPR-Cas across a single arc — mechanism → tool → screen →
therapy → safety. It began as the only known prokaryotic adaptive immune system
(hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance,
mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic), was repurposed into a
democratized genome-editing toolkit
(barrangou-2016-applications-of-crispr-technologies-in,
wang-2023-crispr-technology-a-decade-of, pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of),
and scaled into the pooled-crispr-screen family — genome-wide screens
(wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells), transcriptome-readout
single-cell screens (perturb-seq), tiling deletions
(scandel), and precise-variant editing (crispey). Its non-cutting branch
(crispri-crispra) drives dosage screens and therapy. The frontier is the clinic:
in vivo neuronal editing (park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing) and CRISPRa
dosage rescue (tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related) push toward
treatment, where measuring and bounding off-target risk becomes decisive
(stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of). Deep dive:
crispr-screens-and-genome-editing.
Current thesis — topic: brain-development-neurogenomics
Twenty-five sources frame the biology + disease context for the brain-RNA work: how
the human brain is built (neurodevelopment, neurogenesis,
neuronal-cell-types, brain-organoids), what makes it human
(human-brain-evolution), and how its genetics goes wrong — neuropsychiatric risk
is polygenic, pleiotropic, and acts early in development, converging on specific
cells and heavily on autism (grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across,
gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across, psychiatric-genetics).
A recurring lesson — transcriptome ≠ cell identity
(kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell) — is exactly the resolution bulk
genomics lacks, so this topic explains which cells and stages the developing-brain
splicing-qtl/isoform atlases and single-cell methods are mapping, and why
cell-type + developmental resolution is the open frontier. The newest ASD pair
sharpens this: 11 mouse models converge on a transient radial-glial
delay that fades with maturation
(schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism), while the human ASD
cortical methylome shows only modest cell-type-specific change,
uncorrelated with expression and dwarfed by aging
(eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation). The newest entry adds the
missing context itself: population-scale neuronal-activation (KCl-stimulated
iPSC Glut/GABA neurons, 100 donors) shows most NPD genetic effects are
activation-specific — stimulation-driven eQTLs, caQTLs and
ASoC (caQTLs explaining ~2.5× more SCZ heritability
than eQTLs) feed ctwas to prioritize risk genes the resting brain misses, and
implicate an activation-regulated cholesterol axis
(liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron). CRISPR now enters this
topic as a tool: pooled screens in forebrain assembloids and
differentiating neurons map disease genes onto interneuron-migration and
differentiation (meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact,
amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes), while CRISPRa dosage rescue and
in vivo editing move toward therapy for neurodegeneration and NDDs
(tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related,
park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing) — see the crispr topic. The
experimental toolkit for dissecting NDD complexity is reviewed in
sun-2024-strategies-for-dissecting-the-complexity.
Key concepts
Splicing — spliceosome · alternative-splicing · splicing-code ·
post-transcriptional-splicing · splicing-and-disease · splicing-therapeutics.
AI — foundation-models · dna-language-models · single-cell-perturbation-modeling ·
mutation-effect-prediction · model-interpretability · transcriptome-wide-association-study ·
dual-use-risk · ai-safety.

wiki/sources/abshire-2025-gene-regulatio.title: abshire-2025-gene-regulation-through-exon-junction
wiki/sources/abshire-2025-gene-regulatio.content:
Gene regulation through exon junction complex modularity
One-line: A 2025 NSMB review arguing that the exon-junction-complex is not a monolith but a modular “mRNA hub”: a common three-protein core acquires alternative peripheral modules that create heterogeneous EJCs, and this compositional variation dictates the EJC’s many roles across splicing, m6A methylation, nuclear export, translation and mRNA decay.
Key takeaways

The EJC assembles on the spliceosome and is deposited ~20–24 nt upstream of most exon–exon junctions (older estimates 60–80%), a marker of the splicing history of an mRNA that persists into the cytoplasm.
The EJC core is three proteins: EIF4A3 (the DEAD-box RNA-clamp), RBM8A (Y14) and MAGOH. Alternative EJCs add peripheral modules — the review focuses on the rnps1-ejc (core + UPF2, RNPS1, ACIN1 or PNN, SAP18) and the casc3-ejc (core + AKT + CASC3) — so a single junction can be occupied by compositionally distinct, functionally distinct EJCs (ejc-modularity).
EJCs regulate splicing both actively and passively: peripheral constituents (PNN, SAP18) modulate exon skipping; casc3-ejc-bound AKT phosphorylates SR proteins / SR protein kinases to tune inclusion; and the core physically masks RNA to block cryptic 5′/3′ splice sites (cryptic-splice-sites) and recursive-splicing (RS exons).
EJCs shape the m6A epitranscriptome: by occupying an ~100-nt window around junctions they block METTL3–METTL14 access to DRACH motifs, which is why depleting EIF4A3 or RBM8A causes m6A hypermethylation around short internal exons; hypermethylation shortens mRNA half-life, linking nuclear EJC deposition to cytoplasmic decay.
EJCs promote nuclear mRNA export by recruiting the TREX complex: CBC-bound ALYREF is deposited upstream of EJCs (EIF4A3-dependent), recruits UAP56 (DDX39B) and THO, then SARNP remodeling hands the mRNA to TREX2 and the NXF1–NXT1 export receptor at the nuclear pore.
EJCs enhance translation through peripheral-specific interactions: CASC3 (via its SELOR domain) contacts EIF3A/EIF3D; EIF4A3 contacts EIF3G to drive circRNA translation; PYM bridges the EJC to the 40S subunit; and insulin-stimulated S6K1–SKAR promotes translation of CBC-bound spliced mRNAs.
EJCs are the central platform for 3′UTR NMD: an EJC ≥50–55 nt downstream of a stop codon triggers decay. In rnps1-ejcs, UPF2 binds the UPF1 CH domain to activate its helicase; in casc3-ejcs, AKT phosphorylates the UPF1 CH domain — a UPF2-independent branch — showing the same regulatory outcome achieved by different modules.
PYM is the principal EJC disassembly factor in the cytoplasm, competing with EIF4A3 for RBM8A–MAGOH to strip EJCs off translated mRNAs; ~85% of EJC removal depends on translating ribosomes.

Notable claims / data

EJC footprint: core protein EIF4A3 centers the complex ~27 nt upstream of most exon–exon junctions; variability in deposition is attributed to RNA secondary structure influencing positioning/efficiency.
~60–80% of exon–exon junctions carry an EJC; junctions with fewer EJCs than expected are enriched in mRNAs encoding ribosomal and housekeeping proteins — evidence of some target specificity.
CASC3-EJCs are enriched at detained introns and recursively spliced exons, whereas RNPS1-EJCs are enriched at other detained introns — a rare example of preferential alternative-EJC deposition; no sequence motif fully explains preference, though ACIN1 footprints favor SR-rich / polypyrimidine-tract contexts.
m6A: EJCs protect an ~100-nt junction-proximal region; only ~45% of hypermethylation sites are shared between RNPS1 knockdown and EIF4A3/RBM8A knockdown, implying EJC heterogeneity (not every junction’s EJC contains RNPS1).
NMD branch specificity: downregulating either AKT or UPF2 inhibited 3′UTR NMD for all assayed mRNAs, indicating the same junction can be bound by either a CASC3-EJC or an RNPS1-EJC — possibly a precursor→product relationship (RNPS1-EJC later replaced by CASC3-EJC).
Disease links: elevated AKT signaling raises NMD efficiency in fragile X syndrome; AKT hyperactivation and high CASC3 promote hepatocellular carcinoma proliferation; EIF4A3/RBM8A mutations cause Richieri–Costa–Pereira and TAR syndromes; core-protein haploinsufficiency causes microcephaly and neurodevelopmental defects (Upf2/Rnps1/Casc3/Magoh mouse and human data).

Connections

Provides the mechanistic scaffold connecting many wiki splicing threads: it links alternative-splicing regulation, cotranscriptional-splicing (detained introns removed post-transcriptionally), splicing-and-disease, and splicing-factors (SR proteins, RNPS1, ACIN1, PNN, SAP18) to downstream mRNA fate.
Complements rna-binding-proteins pages: EIF4A3/RBM8A/MAGOH are sequence-independent RBPs whose deposition is a splicing-dependent mark rather than a motif-driven one.
Bridges splicing to m6a-methylation and nonsense-mediated-decay, showing the EJC as the node where nuclear pre-mRNA processing “writes” instructions read out cytoplasmically.
Extends the theme that a fixed molecular machine gains combinatorial function through swappable subunits — parallels condensate/module logic elsewhere in the wiki.

Open questions

How do cells commit to forming one EJC type over another, and how is alternative-EJC target specificity dictated at a given exon–exon junction (no motif fully explains it)?
Is there a genuine RNPS1-EJC → CASC3-EJC precursor–product conversion, and is it coupled to spliceosome assembly stage?
What are the functional differences between alternative EJCs in export and translation efficiency, which “have yet to be assessed”?
Whether peripheral EJC constituents (e.g., UPF3B, UPF3A, AKT) are recruited co-transcriptionally onto the spliceosome or only after EJC-core deposition remains unresolved by cryo-EM.
To what extent EJC constituents regulate transcription itself (e.g., MAGOH stabilizing RNA Pol II at promoter-proximal pause sites) is an emerging, undetailed area.

wiki/sources/adamson-2016-multiplexed-si.title: adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening
wiki/sources/adamson-2016-multiplexed-si.content:
A Multiplexed Single-Cell CRISPR Screening Platform Enables Systematic Dissection of the Unfolded Protein Response
One-line: Introduces Perturb-seq — a platform that couples droplet single-cell RNA-seq with expressed guide-barcode (GBC) tagging of CRISPRi perturbations so pooled perturbed cells can be screened with full transcriptomic readout — and applies it to dissect the three-arm mammalian unfolded protein response (UPR).
Key takeaways

Defines the perturb-seq platform: a lentiviral “Perturb-seq vector” carries a Pol II-driven guide-barcode (GBC) cassette plus a Pol III sgRNA cassette, so each cell’s CRISPR perturbation identity is encoded in an expressed, polyadenylated transcript captured alongside the transcriptome in droplet scRNA-seq — an instance of single-cell-perturbation-modeling.
A three-guide vector with three homology-minimized sgRNA constant regions (cr1/cr2/cr3) and three U6 promoters delivers up to three simultaneous CRISPRi perturbations per cell with near-uniform >90% knockdown, enabling combinatorial epistasis experiments.
Companion analytical pipeline (low-rank ICA, “LRICA”) decomposes noisy high-dimensional single-cell data into interpretable components, computationally decoupling perturbation responses from confounders like the cell cycle — a worked example of model-interpretability for scRNA-seq.
Combinatorial CRISPRi of IRE1a/PERK/ATF6 plus chemical UPR inducers delineates and decouples the three transcriptional arms of the UPR at both bulk and single-cell levels.
Two genome-scale CRISPRi screens against an IRE1a (UPRE) reporter identify hundreds of genes whose repression disrupts ER homeostasis, and Perturb-seq of ~100 hits yields high-precision functional clustering of genes.
Single-cell resolution reveals bifurcated UPR-branch activation within cells under the same perturbation and uncovers a dedicated feedback loop in which the IRE1a/XBP1 branch selectively monitors translocon (SEC61) availability.

Notable claims / data

GBC calling: in a pilot of ~6,000 K562 cells carrying 8 distinct GBCs, a single dominant GBC was uniquely inferred for 92.2% of cells (median 45 independent UMI observations per cell); ~6.2% multiplets, ~1.6% unidentifiable. High-confidence calling lets droplets with >1 cell be discarded.
sgRNA from the Perturb-seq vector gave 95.4%–96.2% reduction of integrated GFP; final three-guide vector gave 96%–97% depletion from any of the three cassettes.
UPR epistasis experiment: sgRNAs targeting the three UPR sensors in all single/double/triple combinations, transcriptomes of ~15,000 cells, >80% depletion of targeted genes across conditions; inducers thapsigargin (ER Ca2+ pump inhibitor) and tunicamycin (N-glycosylation inhibitor), DMSO control.
LRICA decomposes expression matrix X = L + S (low-rank L = population dynamics, sparse S = cell-variable noise/effects); ICA on L yields components whose bimodality defines subpopulations. Identified a PERK-dependent thapsigargin-induced G1 cell-cycle arrest interacting with UPR activation (e.g. MYC).
Regulon structure: PERK/ATF4 had the largest, most-uniquely-controlled regulon; ATF6 and IRE1a overlapped more, with IRE1a uniquely controlling translocon/translocon-auxiliary components.
Reporter screens: CRISPRi-v1 (15,977 genes / 20,899 TSSs, 10 sgRNA/TSS) and CRISPRi-v2 (18,905 genes / 20,526 TSSs, 5 sgRNA/TSS). CRISPRi-v2 found 397 high-mCherry/GFP hits; 103/141 CRISPRi-v1 hits reproduced (Fisher p = 8.97e-138); reporter signal weakly correlated with growth (Spearman R = 0.2). All three translocon genes (SEC61A1/B/G) were among the 7 strongest hits.
Follow-up Perturb-seq sub-library: 91 sgRNAs targeting 82 genes; translocon-subunit depletion selectively induced XBP1 splicing / IRE1a signaling, supporting a homeostatic feedback model where IRE1a monitors translocation state.

Connections

Companion paper to dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, the other founding Perturb-seq study published the same period; both define the perturb-seq entity.
Methodologically upstream of later single-cell functional-genomics work in the wiki such as zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics and chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory.
A canonical case of single-cell-perturbation-modeling: pooled perturbations read out as full transcriptomes rather than a bulk reporter. The platform is a pooled/single-cell CRISPR screen (no dedicated wiki concept page yet).

Open questions

How far does GBC multiplexing scale in perturbation order and library size before recombination or dCas9-KRAB titration limits uniform multi-gene knockdown?
Which “non-canonical” hits (mitochondrial, general transcription/translation factors) act through genuine ER-homeostasis disruption versus reporter-system artifacts?
How general is the bifurcated, cell-to-cell heterogeneous branch activation across other stress pathways and cell types beyond K562?

wiki/sources/alam-2026-a-neuron-type-spe.title: alam-2026-a-neuron-type-specific-microexon-in
wiki/sources/alam-2026-a-neuron-type-spe.content:
A neuron type-specific microexon in Ank3/ankyrin-G modulates calcium activity and neuronal excitability
One-line: Functional dissection of a deeply conserved 27-nt microexon (E35a) in Ank3/ankyrin-G that is included in GABAergic but skipped in glutamatergic cortical neurons, showing that this single alternative-splicing event tunes intracellular Ca²⁺ signaling and interneuron excitability independently of ankyrin-G’s canonical axon-initial-segment role.
Key takeaways

Provides a rare example of connecting a neuron-type-specific alternative exon to actual cellular and organismal phenotype (splicing-and-neurodevelopment) — most such exons are catalogued but not functionally validated.
E35a is a 27-nt microexons in Ank3 (ankyrin-G / AnkG), predominantly included in cortical GABAergic interneurons (PSI ~0.65) and cerebellar neurons but skipped in glutamatergic excitatory neurons (PSI ~0.14); inclusion is neuron-type-specific and skipped in non-neuronal cells (neuronal-cell-types).
Splicing of E35a is under combinatorial control by multiple splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins: Ptbp2 represses it in non-neuronal/young cells; Celf2 and nSR100/SRRM4 promote inclusion; Mbnl2 (higher in glutamatergic neurons) is the major repressor driving glutamatergic skipping; Elavl2 and Qk are enriched in GABAergic neurons. Inclusion is also neuronal-activity-dependent (reduced after KCl depolarization).
E35a and its flanking introns are conserved across >450 million years of vertebrate evolution (mouse to fish), implying strong purifying selection and functional significance; a paralogous exon exists in Ank2 but not Ank1.
Ank3 E35a⁻ᐟ⁻ mice: GABAergic interneurons (especially fast-spiking) show increased intrinsic excitability (higher input resistance, lower rheobase, higher firing) and elevated somatic Ca²⁺ activity — without disruption of the axon initial segment (AIS length/position and action-potential initiation preserved).
Mechanistically, the E35a-encoded 9-amino-acid peptide (in an intrinsically disordered region between the UPA and death domains of AnkG) facilitates AnkG binding to the NCX/NKA/InsP3R (IP3R1) complex at ER–plasma-membrane junctions, important for intracellular Ca²⁺ handling — a role parallel to AnkB’s in cardiac cells.

Notable claims / data

Systematic scRNA-seq analysis of adult mouse neocortex found 469 cassette exons differentially spliced between glutamatergic and GABAergic neurons; E35a (27 nt, between constitutive exons E35 and E36, upstream of the giant exon E37) is one of them.
The homologous AnkB peptide differs: the Ank2 exon has six extra nucleotides (encodes 11 aa) and is constitutively included; an E1425G missense mutation in the AnkB homologous region causes human type-4 long-QT syndrome by abrogating AnkB–InsP3R interaction — motivating the Ca²⁺ hypothesis.
Minigene reporter assays: nSR100 overexpression raised E35a inclusion to ~20%; co-expressed Mbnl2 dose-dependently repressed inclusion to ~12%, validating combinatorial regulation (confirmed in N2a and HEK293T cells). E35a has a weak 3′ splice site (3′SS score −0.98 vs 5′SS 7.96 in the human homolog), consistent with the upstream intron being the main regulatory region.
Two-photon in vivo Ca²⁺ imaging (GCaMP6s) in prelimbic mPFC: E35a⁻ᐟ⁻ interneurons showed ~2-fold higher Ca²⁺ activity (AUC 8.05 vs 4.75; higher amplitude, duration, frequency), while glutamatergic pyramidal neurons were largely normal — matching the neuron-type-specific inclusion pattern.
Co-IP from cerebellum: E35a deletion reduced AnkG–InsP3R1 interaction by 77% and AnkG–NKA (Na-K ATPase) interaction by 66%; a FLAG UPA-DD minigene lacking E35a pulled down ~30% less InsP3R1 than the E35a-containing construct, showing the microexon peptide directly mediates the protein-protein interaction.
E35a⁻ᐟ⁻ mice were born at Mendelian ratios with grossly normal development; behavioral phenotypes were mild (increased step length and locomotion/gait, hyperactive tendency) despite Ank3’s link to bipolar disorder and schizophrenia — suggesting compensatory adjustments in excitatory/inhibitory balance.

Connections

A functional-genomics case study extending the neuron-type-specific microexon theme from nikom-2023-alternative-splicing-in-neurodegenerative-disease and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (SRRM4/nSR100-regulated microexons, misregulated in autism); grounds splicing-and-neurodevelopment and neuronal-cell-types.
Deep on combinatorial splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins logic (Ptbp2, Celf2, nSR100/SRRM4, Mbnl2, Elavl2, Qk) overlapping regulators discussed across the wiki’s splicing pages; connects to splicing-code and percent-spliced-in (PSI quantification).
Activity-dependent inclusion links to neuronal-activation biology; the AnkG/AIS angle and bipolar/SCZ/ASD genetic associations connect to autism and psychiatric-genetics; the Ca²⁺/InsP3R mechanism introduces a new microexons concept page.
Uses CRISPR-Cas9 to delete a specific microexon in vivo — a splicing-perturbation method complementing the splice-switching/ASO approaches in desterro-2020-targeting-mrna-processing-as-an and nikom-2023-alternative-splicing-in-neurodegenerative-disease.

Open questions

Does the ER-storage / ER-PM-junction Ca²⁺ model hold up to direct observation of the NCX/NKA/InsP3R complex at junctions and its dependence on E35a inclusion in vivo?
Why are the behavioral phenotypes so mild given robust cellular changes — what compensatory circuits rebalance excitation/inhibition, and would inbred-background or cognitive tasks reveal deficits?
How generally do neuron-type-specific microexons in scaffolding/adaptor proteins reprogram protein-interaction networks to diversify neuronal physiology?
Is there a parallel AnkB/cardiac vs AnkG/neuronal splicing mechanism representing reused machinery, and does human ANK3 E35a mis-splicing contribute to bipolar disorder?

wiki/sources/alamancos-2015-leveraging-t.title: alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast
wiki/sources/alamancos-2015-leveraging-t.content:
Leveraging transcript quantification for fast computation of alternative splicing profiles
One-line: Introduces suppa, a computational tool that derives PSI (Ψ) inclusion values for alternative splicing events directly from fast transcript abundances (TPMs), achieving accuracy comparable to read-based methods while running more than 1000 times faster.
Key takeaways

SUPPA computes relative inclusion levels (percent-spliced-in) of local alternative splicing events from transcript-level abundances rather than from reads mapped to the genome or junctions, sidestepping the time/storage cost of mapping.
It works in two operations: generateEvents derives events from a GTF annotation (or a de novo reconstruction), and psiPerEvent computes Ψ per sample from transcript TPMs, defining Ψ as the abundance of the inclusion-form transcripts (F1) over the abundance of all transcripts in either form (F1∪F2).
SUPPA is agnostic to the quantification method; it was tested coupled to Sailfish and RSEM, and benchmarked against the read-based methods MATS and MISO.
On simulated data SUPPA was closer to ground-truth than MATS and MISO; on RT-PCR–validated events it was slightly superior or comparable; and it was >1000× faster than MATS/MISO for the Ψ-calculation step.
Annotation completeness (full-length transcripts, e.g. RefSeq) matters more than the number of transcripts per gene for accurate Ψ estimation; incomplete annotations (Ensembl) degrade accuracy.
Software is implemented in Python 2.7, has no Python-library dependencies, and is MIT-licensed (released at bitbucket.org/regulatorygenomicsupf/suppa).

Notable claims / data

Supported event types: exon skipping (SE), alternative 5′/3′ splice sites (A5/A3), mutually exclusive exons (MX), intron retention (RI), and alternative first/last exons (AF/AL).
Simulated benchmark (144 events, RefSeq, FluxSimulator reads): Sailfish+SUPPA Pearson R = 0.971, RSEM+SUPPA R = 0.987, vs MATS R = 0.833 and MISO R = 0.862 against ground truth. SUPPA kept R > 0.9 for all event types, including MX where MATS/MISO accuracy dropped sharply.
ΔΨ cumulative-distribution differences between SUPPA and MATS/MISO were statistically significant on simulated data (rank-sum P-values from 8.89×10⁻¹² down to 2.2×10⁻¹⁶).
Experimentally validated set: 163 SE events validated by RT-PCR in MDA-MB-231 cells under ESRP1 overexpression vs empty vector (Shen et al. 2012); on the 60 RefSeq-common events SUPPA was slightly better or comparable to MATS/MISO (ΔΨ distributions not significantly different, P > 0.1).
Annotation effect: RefSeq > Ensembl accuracy across all methods; restricting Ensembl to CDS recovered some SUPPA accuracy, but Ensembl-based SUPPA still trailed MATS/MISO — implicating transcript-quantification quality driven by annotation completeness.
