Genetic specificity in fly: external sense organs need ac/sc (redundant); internal chordotonal organs/proprioceptors and founder photoreceptors need ato; remaining olfactory sensilla and multidendritic neurons need amos; ase and cato act post-selection as differentiation genes. asc/ato account for PNS proneural activity but NOT all CNS neuroblasts (some need no known proneural gene).
Vertebrate genetics: Mash1-null mice have severe neurogenesis defects in ventral telencephalon and olfactory epithelium; Ngn1/Ngn2 single mutants lack complementary cranial sensory ganglia, double mutants additionally lose spinal sensory ganglia and many ventral spinal neurons. Math1-null mice lack cerebellar granule cells, inner-ear hair cells and dorsal D1 spinal interneurons; Math5/ath5 loss eliminates most retinal ganglion cells. Clear proneural activity is established for only Mash1, Ngn1, Ngn2 (possibly Math1/Math5).
Glial inhibition: Mash1;Math3 or Mash1;Ngn2 double mutants block neuronal differentiation and cause premature astrocyte generation. Ngn1 inhibits astrocytic differentiation by an unexpected mechanism — sequestering p300/CBP–Smad1 and blocking Stat1/3 phosphorylation, independent of its transcriptional-activation role — paralleling lineage commitment by alternative-fate suppression in haematopoiesis.
Mash1 vs Ngn divergence: ectopic Ngns drive rapid cell-cycle exit and efficient differentiation; asc-family genes are less efficient (interpreted as greater sensitivity to Notch-mediated inhibition). Crucially, ectopic Mash1 can override endogenous programmes and respecify identity, whereas Ngn2 cannot alone — Ngn2 needs Olig2 to induce motor neurons, and Mash1 cannot substitute for Ngn2 in that pairing.

Connections

Topic hub: anchors the developmental-transcription-factor / neuronal cell-type axis of brain-development-neurogenomics; the bHLH→neuronal-subtype logic is upstream of the mature cell-type and isoform diversity catalogued in developing-brain sibling sources.
Mechanistically complementary to sibling brain-development sources that profile the outputs of neurogenesis (gene/isoform expression across development): walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna — this review supplies the upstream regulatory program (proneural bHLH → Notch → differentiation cascade) that those expression atlases sample downstream of.
The recurring “splicing layer in neurodevelopment” theme elsewhere in the wiki (splicing-and-neurodevelopment) operates downstream of and in parallel to the transcriptional proneural cascade described here; this source is strictly about transcription-factor logic, not splicing.

Open questions

Whether Math1 and Math5 act as true proneural genes (selecting progenitors from neuroepithelial stem cells) or only as subtype-specification factors — direct loss-of-progenitor evidence was still lacking.
Identity of additional proneural genes: sympathetic ganglia (Mash1-independent) and ventral telencephalon (residual Mash1-independent progenitors) imply unidentified factors.
The structural/biochemical basis of E-box and cofactor specificity beyond the basic domain; which cofactors confer regional/subtype specificity to Mash1 and Ngns.
Whether proneural genes have distinct roles within restricted glial progenitors (Mash1 in oligodendrocytic, Ngn2 in astrocytic precursors) and any role in adult neural stem cells.

wiki/sources/bhat-2024-genome-organizati.title: bhat-2024-genome-organization-around-nuclear-speckles
wiki/sources/bhat-2024-genome-organizati.content:
Genome organization around nuclear speckles drives mRNA splicing efficiency
One-line: Demonstrates that the 3D position of a gene relative to nuclear-speckles causally controls its splicing efficiency — genes near speckles sit in higher spliceosome concentrations, bind more U1/U2 snRNA and are spliced more efficiently — integrating nuclear architecture with the biochemistry of splicing.
Key takeaways

nuclear-speckles are nuclear bodies enriched in spliceosome components and pre-mRNA splicing regulators; their functional role in splicing had been debated (storage vs active site). This work shows speckle proximity drives co-transcriptional splicing efficiency.
Genes closest to speckles display ~tenfold higher local snRNA (U1/U2/U4/U6) concentrations and >2-fold higher splicing efficiency than genes farthest from speckles (41.0% vs 19.1% spliced), with a strong genome-wide correlation (r ≈ 0.92).
U1 snRNA binding to 5’ splice sites is elevated at speckle-proximal genes, so more spliceosome engagement — not just higher pre-mRNA level — occurs near speckles.
Speckle-proximity–driven splicing is cell-type dynamic: genes that reposition toward speckles in a given cell type (e.g. titin/Ttn in myocytes) gain splicing efficiency there (Spearman ≈ 0.87 between proximity change and splicing change).
Directed recruitment of a pre-mRNA to speckles (via SRRM1 or srsf1 tethered through MS2/MCP) is sufficient to increase its splicing; non-speckle proteins (SRSF3 in nucleoplasm, LBR at nuclear periphery) are not, and a speckle-localization-deficient SRRM1 (ΔNS) loses the effect.
Proposed model: proximity to a speckle shrinks the diffusion volume, raising the effective concentration of splicing factors around the nascent pre-mRNA and boosting spliceosome binding and conversion to mature mRNA (splicing-methods).

Notable claims / data

Measured with a suite of genomics tools: sprite and RD-SPRITE (DNA–RNA/protein contact mapping), seqFISH+ imaging, TSA-seq, ChIP-seq of PolII-S2P, chromatin/nascent (5-EU) RNA-seq, and psoralen-crosslink RAP-RNA for U1–pre-mRNA hybrids. A “speckle proximity score” from SPRITE was inversely correlated with microscopy distance to SF3a66 speckle foci (r = -0.72).
snRNA enrichment near speckles held even when comparing neighbouring genes transcribed at comparable levels (~fourfold difference in snRNA by position on chr7), and after normalizing for splice-site number, gene length and gene density.
Speckle-close vs speckle-far splicing efficiency: 41.0% vs 19.1% overall; RD-SPRITE nascent-locus analysis gave 16.1% vs 5.5%. Effect reproduced with independent nascent-RNA and imaging methods (r up to 0.95).
A splicing GFP reporter (intron spliced → GFP; bidirectional BFP as splicing-independent control) integrated at a speckle-close locus (Tcf3) showed higher GFP than one integrated far (Grik2), controlling for transcription.
MS2/MCP tethering: SRRM1-MCP and SRSF1-MCP boosted reporter splicing; SRSF3-MCP and LBR-MCP did not; a truncated ΔNS-SRRM1 that cannot localize to speckles abolished the boost — pinpointing speckle localization (not the protein per se) as the causal factor.

Connections

Establishes nuclear-speckles and 3D genome position as an upstream determinant of cotranscriptional-splicing and spliceosome activity — a structural complement to sequence-based splicing-code models.
Connects genome-architecture / 3D genome biology to splicing (shares SPRITE methodology lineage with Guttman-lab chromatin work).
The titin (Ttn) cell-type example links to developmental splicing themes in baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Adds a spatial-concentration mechanism to splicing-methods and splicing-factors (SRRM1, srsf1) biology.

Open questions

Do actively transcribed loci reposition toward pre-existing speckles, or can transcription seed new speckles near a gene?
How much of cell-type-specific alternative splicing (not just efficiency) is explained by speckle repositioning versus sequence/RBP context?
Does speckle proximity affect other co-transcriptional processes (export, 3’ processing) beyond splicing?

wiki/sources/bhattacharya-2023-isoform-l.title: bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic
wiki/sources/bhattacharya-2023-isoform-l.content:
Isoform-level transcriptome-wide association uncovers genetic risk mechanisms for neuropsychiatric disorders in the human brain
One-line: Introduces isoTWAS (isotwas), a multivariate isoform-level transcriptome-wide association framework that jointly models the correlated isoforms of a gene to predict isoform expression from cis-SNPs, recovering ~60% more neuropsychiatric GWAS loci than gene-level TWAS and prioritizing isoform-resolved disease mechanisms (splicing-and-disease) invisible at the gene level.
Key takeaways

Gene-level integrative methods (TWAS) explain only a fraction of GWAS loci because they use total gene expression as the molecular outcome and ignore the distinct transcript isoforms produced by alternative splicing — a limitation especially acute in brain, where genes are longer, have more exons, and show the most complex splicing.
isoTWAS is a three-step framework: (1) train multivariate predictive models of isoform-level expression from all cis-SNPs within 1 Mb, exploiting the correlation structure across isoforms of the same gene; (2) impute isoform expression into a GWAS cohort (or run a weighted burden test on summary stats); (3) stepwise hypothesis testing — isoform P values aggregated to gene-level via ACAT with gene-level FDR control, then isoform-level FWER control, plus locus permutation and optional Bayesian fine-mapping.
Because isoforms are correlated, multivariate modeling improves prediction of >80% of individual isoforms (~1.8–2.4-fold median gain) and of total gene expression by 25–70%, roughly doubling the number of testable features in trait mapping.
Across 15 neuropsychiatric GWAS, isoTWAS uncovered associations at ~60% more GWAS loci than gene-level TWAS, while remaining well-calibrated to the null — gains reflect true signal, not test-statistic inflation.
A key advantage: isoTWAS finds trait associations for an isoform when the gene itself shows no association — illustrated by isoforms of AKT3, CUL3 and HSPD1 in schizophrenia and PCLO across disorders, each driven by a strong isoQTL but weak/absent eQTL.
Isoform-centric mapping beat local-splicing-event mapping (leafcutter intron usage) by ~40% more GWAS loci, and gene-isoform-level models leverage the developing-brain and adult-cortex QTL panels also exploited in the sibling resources walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform.

Notable claims / data

Reference panels: adult frontal cortex (PsychENCODE + AMP-AD, N = 2,115) and developmental prefrontal cortex (N = 205, from walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and); also 48 GTEx tissues (13 brain) with N > 100.
Prediction (GTEx): trained 2.3–2.5-fold more isoform models at CV R² > 0.01 than univariate; multivariate elastic net was the best-performing method across most architectures; brain tissues showed significantly larger multivariate-over-univariate gains (P = 0.011), suggesting more shared isoQTL architecture in brain. Unique genes with >1 testable model were 1.9–2.5× more than gene-level TWAS.
Out-of-sample replication: isoTWAS beat TWAS in total gene expression prediction with median +15.2% adjusted R² (train GTEx → test PsychENCODE/AMP-AD) and +23.9% (vice versa).
Trait mapping (FDR-adj P < 0.05 and locus permutation P_ACAT < 0.05): more trait-associated genes via isoTWAS in adult (2,595 vs 1,589) and developmental (4,062 vs 890) panels; 3,436 unique gene- and 5,377 unique isoform-trait associations. Of 1,335 genes with multiple isoform-trait associations, 661 had isoform associations in opposing directions.
Across 1,149 high-confidence GWAS loci, isoTWAS hit 323 vs TWAS 201 (~60% increase); for SCZ (287 GWAS loci) isoTWAS prioritized 70/86 loci (adult/developmental) vs 56/29 for TWAS. 96% (193/201) of TWAS associations were concordantly recovered; gene- and isoform-level effect sizes correlated r = 0.84.
Calibration: no significant difference in empirical-Bayes test-statistic inflation between TWAS and isoTWAS despite ~4× more tests; effective sample size up ~10–20%. isoTWAS prioritized more constrained (pLI ≥ 0.9) genes (adult 724 vs 106; developmental 385 vs 200).
vs local splicing (leafcutter intron usage → M-values, modeled in the isoTWAS multivariate framework): isoTWAS prioritized features at ~40% more independent GWAS loci (167 vs 119; 108 jointly).
Mechanistic examples (all adult cortex): ENST00000492957 of AKT3 (pLI = 1) in SCZ — strong isoQTL (P < 10⁻⁵⁰), only one weak eQTL; a different AKT3 isoform (ENST00000681794) linked to brain volume. ENST00000409096 of CUL3 (pLI = 0.99) in SCZ. ENST00000678969 of HSPD1 (pLI = 0.99) in SCZ. ENST00000423517 of PCLO (pLI = 1) in the cross-disorder GWAS — each with strong isoQTL but weak/absent eQTL.
isoTWAS is released as an R package (Zenodo v1.0.0) with precomputed models for 48 GTEx tissues, adult brain cortex, and developmental brain cortex.

Connections

Built on isoQTL signal — the same human-brain isoform/splicing-qtl regulation mapped by the developing-brain panel walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and (its N = 205 panel is isoTWAS’s developmental reference) and the cross-ancestry atlas wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform (which itself applies isoTWAS for isoform-level disease mediation).
Directly contrasts isoform-centric mapping with local-splicing-event mapping using leafcutter (li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis is the splicing-QTL-and-disease antecedent for the “RNA splicing as a primary link to disease” framing the paper cites).
A concrete demonstration of splicing-and-disease: isoform-level genetic regulation prioritizes neuropsychiatric risk mechanisms (AKT3, CUL3, HSPD1, PCLO) undetectable through total gene expression.
Shares authorship/data lineage with the Gandal-lab brain transcriptomics resources; sibling to wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform in the PsychENCODE isoform-QTL program.
New tool entity: isotwas.

Open questions

Isoform quantification from short-read RNA-seq is annotation-dependent and a maximum-likelihood estimate; long-read sequencing should improve tissue-specific reference annotations and thus isoTWAS — how much does annotation completeness currently limit discovery?
Fine-mapping the true “effect isoform” is undercalibrated under high isoQTL horizontal pleiotropy and shared isoQTLs — summary-statistic methods controlling pleiotropy across isoforms of the same locus are not yet effective.
Could inferential replicates (technical variation from pseudoalignment) be incorporated to give prediction intervals for imputed isoform expression?
Extension to genetically regulated transcript usage (compositional data) — does the compositional nature need explicit handling during prediction or trait mapping?

wiki/sources/bicks-2024-functional-neuro.title: bicks-2024-functional-neurogenomics-in-autism-spectrum
wiki/sources/bicks-2024-functional-neuro.content:
Functional neurogenomics in autism spectrum disorders: A decade of progress
One-line: A review arguing that a decade of functional genomics shows ASD genetic risk converging on early cortical neurogenesis, producing a shared molecular pathology of synaptic dysregulation and glial reactivity.
Key takeaways

autism is highly heritable (~60-80%); risk splits into common inherited SNPs (the majority of susceptibility, acting additively/polygenically), rare inherited, and rare de novo variants, with rare + common combining under a threshold susceptibility model (liability-threshold-model).
Rare, high-penetrance risk genes converge across levels: developmental timing (fetal brain), cell types (developing excitatory L2/3 and L5 projection neurons, some interneurons), coexpression networks (gene-coexpression-network), and protein interactomes — implicating chromatin remodeling and synapse formation/function.
Rare de novo variants track more with intellectual disability and motor impairment, while common polygenic risk aligns more with the core social/repetitive features of ASD — so understanding common-variant biology is essential.
Common-variant biology requires linking genome-wide-association-study loci to genes via eQTLs, splicing-qtl (sQTL), 3D chromatin looping (chromatin-conformation-capture, Hi-C), and massively-parallel-reporter-assay on noncoding-variants (cis-regulatory-elements).
Postmortem RNA-seq defines a consistent ASD molecular phenotype: altered synaptic signaling plus glial reactivity (astrocytes, microglia, recently oligodendrocytes), widespread across cortex including primary sensory areas — not confined to the “social brain.”
iPSC-derived organoid/assembloid models link risk genes to developmental mechanisms (e.g., interneuron-migration deficits, intermediate-progenitor decreases), pointing toward neurogenesis and neurodevelopment as the convergence point.

Notable claims / data

ASD affects ~1-2% of the population; heritability estimates converge on ~60-80%.
Mendelian/de novo rare variants account for ~15-20% of ASD individuals; recessive bi-allelic LOF/missense variants ~3-4% (~2% LOF, ~1-3% damaging missense).
~86% of ASD risk genes also confer risk for broad developmental delay (DD); ASD-preferential genes enrich in maturing excitatory neurons, DD-preferential genes in newly born excitatory neurons and intermediate progenitors (Fu et al. 2022).
Gene-level phenotype splits: KMT2D/KMT2A enrich for DD over ASD, KMT2C more for ASD; BAF member SMARCB1 more DD, SMARCC2 more ASD.
ASD-associated eQTLs enriched in Polycomb repressive complex regions during fetal (not adult) brain; independent studies find eQTL target genes enriched in immune “antigen processing and presenting” pathways.
A CUX1 regulon (SFARI gene, score 2) is enriched in other SFARI genes (CHD2, CHD6, CACNA1A/C, GRIA1, MTOR) and is decreased across ASD cortical cell types including L2/3 excitatory cells (Wamsley et al. 2023).
Assembloid functional screen tested >400 NDD genes for interneuron migration into cortex (Meng et al. 2023); organoid CRISPR screens show convergent intermediate-progenitor defects (Li et al. 2022).
Caveat: findings are review-level syntheses; eQTL/sQTL mapping remains power-limited and highly tissue/timepoint-specific, and primary-glial-dysfunction interpretations are not straightforward.

Connections

walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and — cited as evidence that fetal-brain eQTLs/sQTLs and TF-binding-site enrichment inform ASD disease mechanisms.
gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across — the 800+-sample cortex study showing ASD transcriptomic changes extend to primary sensory regions, reframing the “social brain” hypothesis.
meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact — the assembloid CRISPR screen of >400 disease genes for interneuron-migration deficits highlighted here as a functional-assay advance.

Open questions

Which specific eQTLs/sQTLs act early in development to confer ASD susceptibility, and how do long-read sequencing and cell-type resolution refine sQTL maps?
Do coexpression and biological function cleanly separate rare de novo from rare inherited variants, or is the overlap fundamental?
Is primary glial dysfunction a driver or a sequela of altered neuronal activity in ASD?
How can organoid models bridge early fetal developmental perturbations to postnatal/adult circuit-level pathophysiology, including the intersection of rare and common risk?

wiki/sources/bodelon-2026-native-long-re.title: bodelon-2026-native-long-read-rna-sequencing
wiki/sources/bodelon-2026-native-long-re.content:
Native long-read RNA sequencing of human monocytes reveals activation-induced alternative splicing toward functional isoforms
One-line: Oxford Nanopore native long-read RNA-seq of primary human CD14+ monocytes before/after LPS activation, showing that activation drives widespread isoform switching toward longer, coding-competent, more-translatable isoforms.
Key takeaways

LPS activation of monocytes upregulates splicing-related genes and induces genome-wide alternative-splicing changes, not just gene-level expression changes; pharmacological interference with splicing-factor phosphorylation (CLK / SRPK1 / PP2A inhibitors) dose-dependently altered monocyte activation (TNFα), arguing splicing is functionally required for activation.
Native long-read sequencing avoids amplification bias and reads full-length transcripts, capturing thousands of novel isoforms that short-read methods miss; matched short-read rna-seq was used for splice-junction correction and validation.
Activation shifts isoform usage toward longer isoforms with complete ORFs, fewer retained introns (reduced intron-retention), more exons, longer 3’ UTRs, and greater protein-domain complexity — i.e., toward more “productive” / translatable isoforms.
Integration with Ribo-seq, mass-spectrometry proteomics, and an ESM1b protein language model links these isoform shifts to enhanced translation and higher predicted evolutionary fitness of post-activation protein sequences.
NLRP3 (inflammasome sensor) is a case study: nine novel isoforms detected; activation shifts from exon-skipped / intron-retained variants toward full-length isoforms with all canonical exons and the longest ORF.

Notable claims / data

24,939 expressed isoforms identified in monocytes; 62% absent from GENCODE reference; 48% of novel isoforms predicted protein-coding. 1,238 novel expressed loci (631 predicted coding).
Isoform classification: 4.50% FSM, 13.07% ISM, 42.9% completely novel (NIC/NNC). 74% and 68% of novel isoforms had splice-junction chains absent from TRAILS and FLIBase respectively. Our isoforms significantly longer than GENCODE (Wilcoxon p < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶).
Splice junctions of 83.5% of long-read isoforms (incl. 65.9% of fully novel NIC/NNC) validated in independent short-read datasets (own + GSE103501).
Short-read DE (8 donors/condition): 25.51% genes overexpressed (3,057), 27.99% underexpressed (3,354) after LPS; LPS explained 98% of PCA variance.
Activation: 7,100 differentially expressed isoforms (4,258 novel) vs 2,915 DE genes; 686 isoform-switching events (353 novel); 214 differentially spliced events (reduced intron retention, increased exon inclusion). Gene vs isoform expression change correlated (R² = 0.911).
Motif enrichment (MEME/AME, CISBP-RNA) in exons more included after activation: SFPQ and CPEB2 binding motifs, both overexpressed after LPS.
Genes with >25% increased usage of longest-ORF / productive / Ensembl-canonical isoforms showed significantly higher protein abundance after activation (validated in PXD004352, 12h LPS); higher mRNA–protein correlation than other genes.
Caveats: single 3h time point (6h/12h public datasets used to argue generalizability); THP-1 (cell line) used for Ribo-seq translation evidence; mass-spec n=1 per condition for own data; long-read error rates mitigated by strict CAGE/refTSS-based TSS filtering to avoid RNA-degradation false positives.

Connections

Method stack relies on FLAIR for isoform reconstruction and suppa (SUPPA v2.3) for splicing-event / differential-splicing analysis; also dexseq (via IsoformSwitchAnalyzeR), kallisto-adjacent salmon quantification. SQANTI3 used for transcriptome QC/ORF prediction.
Sibling to human-immune splicing / sQTL work: motivates context-specific splicing relevant to immune-mediated disease (rheumatoid arthritis, SLE, MS) cited via sQTLs — relates to zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls and demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing.
Reduced intron retention + increased exon inclusion findings connect to intron-retention and percent-spliced-in concepts; isoform-functional-consequence analysis complements landscape surveys like park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative.

Open questions

Temporal dynamics: longitudinal isoform profiling beyond the single 3h time point.
Which specific immune splicing factors (beyond SFPQ, CPEB2, MBNL1, QKI) drive activation-induced switching, and are they therapeutic targets (kinase inhibitors, antisense oligonucleotides)?
Single-cell / spatial long-read extension across innate and adaptive immune subtypes.
Whether isoform-ratio-modulating genetic variants (sQTLs) explain disease susceptibility at isoform resolution.

wiki/sources/bond-2012-61-million-person.title: bond-2012-61-million-person-experiment-social-influence
wiki/sources/bond-2012-61-million-person.content:
A 61-million-person experiment in social influence and political mobilization
One-line: A randomized controlled trial delivered voter-mobilization messages to 61 million Facebook users on 2010 US election day, showing that social cues (faces of friends who voted) directly increased real-world turnout and that the behaviour spread through the network, mostly via close friends.
Key takeaways

Randomized 3-arm design on 2 November 2010: a “social message” group (n=60,055,176) shown a vote prompt plus a counter and up to six profile pictures of friends who had clicked “I Voted”; an “informational message” group (n=611,044) with the same message minus friends’ faces; and a control group (n=613,096) with no message.
Three outcomes measured: clicking the “I Voted” button (political self-expression), clicking the polling-place link (information seeking), and validated turnout matched to public voter records (~6.3 million users).
Social cues beat information-only appeals: seeing friends’ faces raised self-expression, information seeking, and, critically, real-world voting. Information-only turnout was statistically identical to the control group.
Contagion was real but concentrated in strong ties: per-friend treatment effects on validated voting were near zero for weak ties and spiked for the top two deciles of user-friend interaction, i.e. “close friends.”
Social transmission outweighed the direct message effect: close friends exerted roughly four times more influence on total validated voters mobilized than the message itself did directly.

Notable claims / data

Direct self-expression effect: social-message users were 2.08% (s.e.m. 0.05%, P<0.01) more likely to click “I Voted” than informational-message users (20.04% vs 17.96%).
Direct validated-vote effect: social message raised turnout by 0.39% (s.e.m. 0.17%, P=0.02) versus control, and by the same 0.39% versus informational message; informational vs control effect was 0.00% (P=0.98).
Per-close-friend effects: +0.099% for expressed voting, +0.224% for validated voting, +0.012% for polling-place search — each outside the simulated null distribution; ordinary (weak-tie) friends showed no effect on private/real-world behaviours.
Network scale: users averaged 149 friends; ~10 were “close” (decile 9, top interaction percentile) versus 139 non-close; 98% of users had at least one close friend; close friendships were only 7% of all friendships yet accounted for all significant contagion of validated voting and search.
Aggregate estimate: message directly produced ~60,000 extra voters and ~280,000 via social contagion, for ~340,000 additional votes (~0.14% of the ~236 million US voting-age population in 2010).
All estimates are intent-to-treat effects (some targets had already voted or logged in too late), so treatment-on-treated effects would be larger. Inference used a network-permutation null distribution validated by Monte Carlo simulation.

Connections

A landmark early computational social science study, using a very large online platform as an experimental substrate — an outlier in this wiki relative to its genomics-heavy sources, but illustrative of large-N randomized experiments and observational-vs-causal inference on human behaviour.
Its methodological core (permutation-based null distributions to recover causal effects in dependent network data) parallels concerns in population-scale human data analysis found elsewhere in the collection.

Open questions

Do these strong-tie contagion effects generalize beyond a single election-day message and beyond the matchable subset of the voter file?
How much of the “online” social influence is really unobserved face-to-face interaction between strong ties that merely has an online representation?
What are the ethical and governance implications of a platform being able to measurably shift real-world turnout for hundreds of thousands of people via message design?

wiki/sources/bonnal-2025-core-splicing-a.title: bonnal-2025-core-splicing-architecture-and-early
wiki/sources/bonnal-2025-core-splicing-a.content:
Core splicing architecture and early spliceosomal recognition determine microexon sensitivity to SRRM3/4
One-line: Massively parallel splicing assays over 28,535 variants show that a microexon’s high vs low sensitivity to the neuronal/endocrine splicing regulators srrm4 is set mainly by its core splicing architecture (splice-site strength, branch-point/pyrimidine tract, exon length) and its intrinsic efficiency at recruiting early spliceosome components — not by SRRM-specific motifs.
Key takeaways

Microexons (3–27 nt) essential for neurons and pancreatic endocrine cells depend on splicing-factors srrm4 for inclusion; two classes exist — highly sensitive (HS, neuronal) and lowly sensitive (LS) — and this study asks what cis-determines the differential dose response.
Uses MaPSy (massively-parallel-reporter-assay) in HEK293 Flp-In T-REx cells with titratable SRRM4 (GFP/LOW/MID/HIGH) over 28,535 variants to test two models: (I) HS/LS share splice architecture but differ in SRRM3/4 recruitment ability; (II) HS/LS differ in core splicing architecture with similar SRRM3/4 effect. Data support model II.
Sensitivity to SRRM4 is evolutionarily conserved across 47 jawed vertebrates (~450 My); orthologous HS microexons show higher inclusion than LS across five vertebrates; swapping human microexon flanking introns for orthologs preserves sensitivity, confirming cis-encoding.
Exon length modulates sensitivity: lengthening a microexon (toward 42 nt) generally enhances recognition and can render HS microexons SRRM4-independent; shortening constitutive exons can make them SRRM4-responsive — length interacts with core splicing to shift the sensitivity state.
Sensitivity is encoded predominantly in the flanking introns (3′ss > 5′ss): swapping intronic sequences between HS and LS events transfers sensitivity; the 5′ss contribution is unexpectedly strong (a previously unappreciated role).
The characteristic UGC motif near the 3′ss facilitates but is NOT strictly required for srrm4 inclusion; UGC context/position is similar between HS and LS, so it does not explain differential sensitivity. HIGH SRRM4 can overcome UGC disruption.
Strengthening the branch point / pyrimidine tract or the 5′ss enables SRRM4-independent inclusion (especially in HS); a mathematical model in which HS and LS microexons differ only in their efficiency of recruiting early spliceosomal components reproduces the data.

Notable claims / data

Classified 71 LS and 73 HS SRRM3/4-dependent microexons from HeLa/HEK293 SRRM3-or-SRRM4-expressing RNA-seq; probed via DNM1 three-exon minigene MaPSy with 93 nt of 3′ intron + 25 nt of 5′.
HS microexons have stronger polypyrimidine tract, weaker 3′ss context, and are more “primed” (shifted right on the PSI–SRRM4 dose curve) — closer to forming productive spliceosome assemblies without SRRM4.
MAXENT5 (5′ss strength) variation strongly and positively correlates with change in inclusion at HIGH SRRM4 for HS/LS; agez and MAXENT3 correlations are much weaker.
In vitro, stronger 5′ss yields stronger spliceosomal A-complex assembly on ITSN1 pre-mRNA in the presence of the SRRM3 eMIC domain — biochemically linking early splice-site recognition to sensitivity.
5′ss strengthening via U1/U6 snRNA complementarity increased both LS and HS microexon inclusion even without SRRM4.

Connections

Deepens the splicing-code for microexons and extends splice-sites / splicing-factors concept pages; the SRRM3/4 (eMIC domain) mechanism connects to neuronal microexon biology in furlanis-2019-landscape-of-ribosome-engaged-transcript-isoforms.
The MaPSy variant-effect approach parallels chiang-2022-mechanism-and-modeling-of-human and begg-2020-concentration-dependent-splicing-is-enabled in the wiki; its length/splice-site-strength determinants inform sequence-based splice predictors reviewed in shen-2026-advances-and-challenges-of-splicing.
Early-spliceosome-recognition framing (U1/U2AF/branch-point) connects to spliceosome assembly and splicing-and-neurodevelopment threads.
Candidate entity pages: SRRM3/4 (eMIC-domain microexon regulators), the titratable SRRM4 MaPSy platform.

Open questions

Can microexon SRRM3/4 sensitivity now be predicted purely from core splicing features (5′ss/3′ss strength, branch point, exon length) in a quantitative model?
Why is the 5′ss so influential for a 3′ss-anchored UGC-regulated event — what is the mechanistic link to eMIC-domain-driven A-complex assembly?
How do these cis-determinants set the correct microexon programs specifically in neurons vs pancreatic endocrine cells with their different SRRM3/4 dosages?

wiki/sources/boutz-2015-detained-introns.title: boutz-2015-detained-introns-are-a-novel
wiki/sources/boutz-2015-detained-introns.content:
Detained introns are a novel, widespread class of post-transcriptionally spliced introns
One-line: Defines “detained introns” (DIs) — a widespread, evolutionarily conserved class of introns that remain unspliced within otherwise fully processed, nuclear-retained poly(A)+ transcripts, and shows that regulated splicing of DIs (e.g. by Clk kinase activity and DNA damage) couples splicing rate to gene expression level independently of NMD.
Key takeaways

Introduces the concept of the detained-intron: a specific internal intron that is spliced more slowly than the other introns in the same transcript, so the transcript accumulates as a nuclear, polyadenylated, incompletely spliced species — a distinct form of post-transcriptional-splicing related to but operationally separable from classic intron-retention.
DIs are common and biologically conserved: ~3150 DIs (4.2% of internal introns in expressed genes) in mouse ES cells at 1% FDR, thousands more in four human cell lines; 721/2262 mappable mouse DIs (32%) are also detained in orthologous human introns (overlap P < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶), arguing for a conserved regulatory function.
Unlike NMD substrates, DI-containing transcripts are not degraded by nonsense-mediated-decay despite frequently containing premature termination codons — Upf1 knockdown does not stabilize them — because they are sequestered in the nucleus where NMD (which requires cytoplasmic translation) cannot act.
DIs have longer half-lives than their neighbors (mean ~29 min vs ~11 min for flanking introns) yet are eventually either spliced or degraded within the nucleus, making the rate of DI splicing a rate-limiting control point for producing mature, exportable mRNA.
clk-kinase activity regulates a specific set of DIs: inhibition with the Clk1/4 inhibitor CB19 significantly changed >300 DIs within 2 h; roughly half showed increased splicing and half increased detention, altering transcript pools of hundreds of genes, especially genes encoding splicing-factors and RNA-processing proteins.
The SR protein srsf4 (Srsf4) is a major Clk substrate (its phospho-state shifts sharply on CB19 treatment) and Srsf3/Srsf4 iCLIP clusters are highly enriched at CB19-responsive RNA-processing DIs, indicating many DIs are direct SR-protein/Clk targets.
DNA damage (doxorubicin) triggers regulated splicing changes in a subset of DIs; ~30% of genes changing after 4 h of Clk inhibition are p53 transcriptional targets, and the p53 negative regulator Mdm4 switches its DI-containing pool toward the NMD isoform upon damage — linking DI splicing to stress and splicing-and-disease responses.

Notable claims / data

Deep sequencing of poly(A)-selected RNA from mouse ES cells (and hESC, HeLa, HepG2, HUVEC) revealed specific internal introns far more abundant than other introns in the same gene; a DESeq-based pipeline (TopHat-classified splicing types) identified them genome-wide.
61% of DIs are constitutive (no evidence of alternative splicing); DIs are ~3-fold more likely than chance to flank alternative exons but are only 9%–17% of any alternative-splicing event class — so DIs are a distinct regulated class, not merely a byproduct of alternative splicing.
Constitutive DIs show higher mean vertebrate sequence conservation (0.096 vs 0.088, P = 3.0×10⁻⁴) and, notably, weaker 5′ and 3′ splice site strength (MaxEnt 8.0 vs 8.5 and 8.1 vs 8.6) than non-DIs; DIs are biased toward the 3′ end of genes (mean relative position 0.69 vs 0.58).
Flavopiridol (transcription block) time courses: DIs decay logarithmically like other introns but slower (mean DI half-life 29 min vs 11 min flanking).
Subcellular fractionation (HeLa/HepG2/HUVEC ENCODE + mESC): DI-containing transcripts are strongly nuclear, forming a population distinct from cytoplasmic-enriched CDS and nucleus-slightly-enriched NMD substrates.
After 2 h CB19: 325 DIs in 295 genes changed significantly; ~188 increased detention, 137 decreased; Clk1/Clk4 DIs decreased (increased splicing → more coding mRNA) while Srsf3/Srsf5 gained poison-exon inclusion → NMD, so total Clk-kinase coding transcript rises while SR-protein coding transcript falls.
36% (115/325) of DIs whose splicing responded to CB19 were bound by Srsf3, Srsf4, or both (P ≤ 2.2×10⁻¹⁶); among RNA-processing gene DIs, 78% (38/49) were SR-protein-bound vs 29% of other DIs.
Adult mouse liver contains 2347 DIs (6.8% of introns in expressed genes); 454 shared with ESCs (P < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶), plus large tissue-specific sets — importantly, many DIs occur in genes expressed in both cell types but detained in only one, evidence of regulated (not merely slow) splicing.
Mdm4 DI increased ~2-fold 4–6 h after doxorubicin, with an isoform switch from coding to NMD; Bclaf1 showed the opposite switch (toward coding), demonstrating bidirectional, gene-specific DNA-damage regulation.

Connections

Sharpens the vocabulary around post-transcriptional-splicing and cotranscriptional-splicing: DIs are transcripts where most introns are spliced (co-transcriptionally) but one “detained” intron is held back, distinguishing “detained” from classic “retained” introns (which may exit to the cytoplasm as exon-containing mRNA and be translated or NMD-degraded).
Directly complements braunschweig-2014-widespread-intron-retention-in-mammals (same era, poly(A)+ RNA): Braunschweig emphasizes NMD/nuclear turnover as the fate of retained introns, whereas Boutz argues DIs are a distinct, NMD-resistant, nuclear pool — the two papers explicitly contrast their intron sets.
Ties splicing regulation to splicing-factors autoregulation: many DIs sit in SR-protein and RNA-processing genes, extending the splicing-code idea that splicing regulators homeostatically control their own poison/NMD-switch exons.
Links splicing-and-disease via p53/Mdm4 and DNA-damage-responsive DI splicing, and supports splicing-therapeutics relevance of Clk-kinase inhibitors as splicing modulators.
Relies on percent-spliced-in-style intron-centric quantification (MISO Ψ for cassette exons; DESeq intron read modeling).

Open questions

What molecular signal marks a specific intron for “detention,” given DIs have weaker splice sites yet require active regulation rather than simply reflecting poor splice-site recognition?
How is the choice between eventual splicing vs nuclear degradation of a detained transcript made, and which nuclear surveillance pathways degrade DIs?
Which additional kinases/signaling inputs (beyond Clk and DNA damage) tune DI splicing under physiological conditions?
How broadly does DI-based regulation control cell-type-specific programs, given the large tissue-specific DI sets in liver vs ESCs?

wiki/sources/braunschweig-2014-widesprea.title: braunschweig-2014-widespread-intron-retention-in-mammals
wiki/sources/braunschweig-2014-widesprea.content:
Widespread intron retention in mammals functionally tunes transcriptomes
One-line: Using high-coverage poly(A)+ RNA-seq across >40 human and mouse cell/tissue types, shows that intron-retention (IR) is far more common in mammals than previously thought (affecting transcripts of most multiexonic genes), that a defined cis “IR code” predicts it, and that IR functionally “tunes” transcriptomes by down-regulating transcripts less relevant to the cell — via NMD, nuclear sequestration, and Pol II pausing.
Key takeaways

IR is surprisingly widespread in mammals: ~53% (human) and 51% (mouse) of introns can be retained at PIR ≥ 10 in at least one sample; ~77% of multiexonic genes contain ≥1 retained intron — comparable in frequency to cassette-exon alternative splicing, overturning the view that IR is the rarest alternative-splicing class in animals.
IR is quantified with percent intron retention (PIR), a metric analogous to percent-spliced-in but for introns: PIR = balanced average of 5′ and 3′ exon–intron junction reads over (exon–exon junction + exon–intron) reads.
A logistic-regression “IR code” of ~136 cis features (elevated C/G content, short intron length, weak 5′/3′ splice sites, flanking-exon length ratios, intron position) reliably discriminates retained from constitutively spliced introns (ROC AUC = 0.79), meaning retained introns are a structurally distinct class, part of the broader splicing-code.
Retained introns fall into three evolutionary classes (Types A–C): Type A = ancestral introns flanked by constitutive exons (most frequent, weakest PIR); Type B = introns from “intronization” of ancestral exonic sequence (shortest, highest C/G, strongest PIR); Type C = introns adjacent to alternative exons (longest, recently evolved).
IR functionally down-regulates gene expression: retained introns are enriched in UTRs/noncoding regions, and IR that introduces a PTC targets transcripts to nonsense-mediated-decay, while PTC status aside, IR causes nuclear restriction/turnover — additively reducing cytoplasmic transcript levels.
The “transcriptome tuning” function: IR preferentially reduces transcripts of relatively low-abundance genes whose products are less relevant to the cell/tissue — e.g. during ES-cell→neuron differentiation IR progressively increases on cell-cycle/DNA-replication genes, suppressing spurious/inappropriate transcripts. Links to splicing-and-neurodevelopment.
IR is mechanistically coupled to RNA Pol II elongation and chromatin (splicing-and-chromatin): retained introns show strong enrichment of elongating Pol II (Ser2-phospho CTD), and slowing elongation (DRB) increases IR, while depleting core spliceosomal snRNPs (SNRPB knockdown) increases IR at introns with high Pol II occupancy — a bidirectional cross-talk / checkpoint model.

Notable claims / data

Analysis of >40 diverse human and mouse cell/tissue RNA-seq datasets; IR detected by aligning reads to exon–intron junctions, intron-midpoint sequences, and exon–exon junctions, requiring balanced 5′/3′ junction coverage to exclude alternative-splice-site and antisense artifacts.
Extrapolated estimates: PIR ≥ 10 potentially in ~63% of human introns / 77% of genes; PIR ≥ 50 in ~9% of introns / 35% of genes.
IR is higher in neural and immune cell types, lower in ES and muscle cells; PIR by RNA-seq correlated with RT-PCR PIR (r = 0.63, P < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶) across 25 validated events.
Cross-species conservation across 11 vertebrates (~440 Myr): IR profiles diverged rapidly in all organs but the nervous system retains the most conserved IR functions; testis is an outlier (high heterogeneous IR); tissue clustering segregates by species except brain, which clusters by tissue.
In Smg1 gene-trap (NMD-deficient) MEFs, PTC-introducing retained-intron transcripts are expressed at higher steady state (P = 5×10⁻⁵) and ~10% show ≥15% PIR increase, confirming NMD contributes to IR-mediated down-regulation.
ENCODE ChIP-seq (K562, CH12): RNA Pol II, H3K27ac, and CHD2 are significantly enriched over retained vs constitutive introns; Pol II-Ser2p (elongating form) shows the strongest enrichment; H3K36me3 is enriched over exons but not retained introns after controlling for expression.
DRB elongation inhibition: 13/18 tested introns showed increased retention (4 with PIR ≥ 10 increase); SNRPB (SmB/B′) knockdown increases PIR preferentially at high-Pol II-occupancy introns.
Retained introns are on average shorter, more C/G-rich, and have weaker splice sites than constitutive introns (all P < 0.001).

Connections

Foundational companion to boutz-2015-detained-introns-are-a-novel: both use high-coverage poly(A)+ RNA-seq and both find pervasive nuclear-retained unspliced introns, but Braunschweig frames the phenomenon as broad IR with NMD as a major fate, while Boutz carves out “detained introns” as an NMD-resistant subclass — the papers explicitly distinguish their intron sets.
Establishes IR as a major branch of the mammalian splicing-code and a driver of splicing-and-neurodevelopment (progressive IR during neuronal differentiation).
Strong support for splicing-and-chromatin / cotranscriptional-splicing: positions Pol II pausing and reduced splicing-factor recruitment as a bidirectional checkpoint controlling IR.
Uses percent-spliced-in-style quantification generalized to introns (PIR); connects to rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1 as a repressive factor) and splicing-factors availability as a control knob.
Methodological predecessor to downstream IR tools (e.g. IRFinder, vast-tools lineage from the Blencowe/Irimia groups).

Open questions

Which cis features and trans factors determine whether a retained intron is destined for NMD vs pure nuclear restriction vs eventual splicing?
How much of the Pol II–IR correlation is causal (pausing → IR) vs consequence (unspliced intron → pausing), and how is the checkpoint reset?
Do the three evolutionary IR types (A/B/C) map onto distinct regulatory or disease-relevant functions?
To what extent does the “transcriptome tuning” (suppressing low-relevance transcripts) generalize across tissues and disease states beyond neuronal differentiation?

wiki/sources/bray-2016-near-optimal-prob.title: bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification
wiki/sources/bray-2016-near-optimal-prob.content:
Near-optimal probabilistic RNA-seq quantification
One-line: Introduces kallisto, a pseudoalignment-based RNA-seq quantification program that is two orders of magnitude faster than alignment-based pipelines while matching their accuracy, removing a major computational bottleneck in transcript-level RNA-seq analysis.
Key takeaways

kallisto replaces base-level read alignment with pseudoalignment: for each read it identifies only the subset of transcripts the read is compatible with, without computing exact base coordinates. This is sufficient for transcript-level quantification because quantification needs to know which transcripts could have generated a read, not where in the transcript it originated.
It builds a transcriptome de Bruijn graph (T-DBG) from k-mers in the reference transcriptome, with a path covering where paths correspond to transcripts. The path cover induces k-compatibility classes on k-mers; intersecting the k-compatibility classes of a read’s constituent k-mers yields the read’s equivalence class.
Abundances are estimated by running the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm over equivalence classes (not individual reads), since equivalence-class counts are sufficient statistics — typically hundreds of thousands of classes instead of tens of millions of reads.
Speed gain is dramatic: ~30 million paired-end reads quantified in <10 min on a standard laptop, versus tens of core-hours for TopHat2 + Cufflinks. This is a splicing-methods tool relevant to isoform-level expression that underlies downstream alternative splicing analyses.
The tool entity is kallisto.

Notable claims / data

Accuracy vs alignment: similar median relative difference to RSEM, EMSAR, eXpress; substantially better than Cufflinks and Sailfish (Fig. 2a). kallisto reported median relative differences in the low range (~0.03-0.06) comparable to the best alignment-based methods, while Cufflinks/Sailfish were markedly worse (~0.21-0.52).
Pseudoalignment specificity/sensitivity: vs Bowtie2 alignments on 20 RSEM simulations of 30M 75-bp paired-end reads (based on GEUVADIS sample NA12716_7), the two agreed exactly for 70.7% of reads; when differing, Bowtie2 reported 8.02 transcripts on average vs 4.96 for kallisto (more specific). Despite being more specific, kallisto had ~100% sensitivity — the true transcript of origin was in the reported set for 99.89% of reads (vs 99.99% Bowtie2).
On real NA12716_7 data: exact agreement 66.22%; for differing reads Bowtie2 aligned 8.94 vs 4.86 transcripts; 86.5% of reads aligned by Bowtie2 vs 90.8% pseudoaligned by kallisto.
Speed: each 30M-read simulation processed on average <7.5 min single-core; total kallisto runtime on the 20 simulated datasets was 11.47 min. A simple word count of a dataset took 75 s (a lower bound), so kallisto’s speed is near-optimal. Memory: max 3.2 Gb RAM per sample; runs on a 1.3-GHz laptop in <10 min.
Allele-specific expression (simulated two-haplotype transcriptome): Spearman correlation 0.833 for kallisto vs 0.848 RSEM, 0.830 eXpress, 0.706 Sailfish — competitive for allele-specific expression. (Caveat: simulation was RSEM-based, which may favor RSEM.)
Bootstrap uncertainty: kallisto resamples multinomially from equivalence-class counts and re-runs EM; each bootstrap ~10 s. On a 216M-read SEQC-MAQCIII human dataset, 40 bootstraps on a single 30M-read sample gave variance correlating R = 0.933 with the across-subsample variance. Naive Poisson/shot-noise variance is shown to be a poor proxy for true variance.
Few parameters: only k-mer length and mean fragment length (the latter estimated at runtime for paired-end reads); performance robust to k chosen. Bias correction uses 6-mers overlapping the 5’ fragment, similar to Roberts et al.
Validation also on SEQC data with qPCR as independent ground truth; sequence-specific bias learning gave a slight improvement in qPCR agreement.

Connections

Methods sibling to rmats and shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection: rMATS detects/quantifies differential alternative splicing events, whereas kallisto provides upstream isoform-level abundance estimates that splicing-quantification pipelines can build on.
Fits the broader splicing-methods tooling landscape; isoform abundances feed percent-spliced-in-style and differential-isoform analyses.
Benchmarked against and contrasted with prior tools: Cufflinks (alignment + EM), Sailfish (k-mer shredding hash), RSEM, eXpress, EMSAR — kallisto’s preservation of k-mer information across reads (unlike Sailfish’s shredding) is the key accuracy advantage.
The bootstrap-based uncertainty quantification is methodologically relevant to any downstream differential-expression or differential-splicing test that needs to factor in inferential variance.

Open questions

How does pseudoalignment perform for splicing-specific tasks (e.g. exon-inclusion / PSI estimation) rather than whole-transcript abundance, where read placement within a transcript matters more?
The authors note kallisto should generalize to other “sequence census” datasets — what is its accuracy on non-RNA-seq assays?
Bias correction is sequence-specific (6-mer priming bias); how much residual positional/fragmentation bias remains and how does it affect isoform-ratio estimates relevant to alternative splicing?
Robustness on highly similar paralogs and complex isoform families remains a limitation shared with all methods — what is the practical impact on splicing-level conclusions?

wiki/sources/brechtmann-2018-outrider-a-.title: brechtmann-2018-outrider-a-statistical-method
wiki/sources/brechtmann-2018-outrider-a-.content:
OUTRIDER: A Statistical Method for Detecting Aberrantly Expressed Genes in RNA Sequencing Data
One-line: outrider — an autoencoder + negative-binomial statistical test that detects aberrant gene-expression outliers in rna-seq for rare-disease diagnostics, controlling for hidden confounders automatically and giving FDR-adjusted p-values. The expression-outlier counterpart to aberrant-splicing detection (cf. absplice2 from the same lab), and a sibling of differential-expression tools like dexseq.
Key takeaways

Targets a different problem than differential expression (dexseq, DESeq2, edgeR): not a fold change between two groups, but an outlier within a population — one sample tested against tens of others, the rare-disease diagnostic setting.
A denoising autoencoder models gene covariation (technical, environmental, common-genetic) to predict context-dependent read-count expectations; counts are then modeled as negative binomial with gene-specific dispersion, and outliers are read counts that significantly deviate → two-sided p-values, Benjamini–Yekutieli FDR.
Fully automated: the autoencoder’s encoding dimension is auto-tuned by maximizing recall of artificially corrupted counts — no manual confounder correction (unlike the prior Kremer/PEER approaches).
An end-to-end R/Bioconductor package: gene filtering, aberrant-sample flagging (>0.5% of expressed genes are outliers), and FDR-based aberrant-gene calls.
Frames RNA-seq as a complementary assay to WES/WGS for rare disease: it surfaces expression outliers (and, with sibling tools, splicing defects and mono-allelic expression) where DNA sequencing leaves variants of unknown significance.

Notable claims / data

Datasets: Kremer rare-disease cohort (119 skin-fibroblast RNA-seq, suspected mitochondrial disorder) + GTEx (250 not-sun-exposed skin). Auto-selected encoding dimension 21 (Kremer) / 45 (GTEx).
Outperformed PCA and PEER (prior covariation-control methods, which assume log-normal): OUTRIDER’s p-values were closer to uniform null, gave more even outlier counts per sample, and recalled injected outliers better — especially for low-expression genes and under-expressed outliers.
On Kremer, recalled all six validated pathogenic events (3 expression outliers, 1 mono-allelic expression, 2 splicing defects — the loss of expression from CLPP/MCOLN1 splice defects was caught); PCA/PEER called 3.8×/7.8× more outliers and missed 1–2 pathogenic events.
Across GTEx tissues, OUTRIDER outliers were enriched for rare (MAF < 0.05) moderate/high-impact variants (VEP) more than PCA/PEER — supporting that the called outliers are genuine regulatory/NMD-related events.
Aberrant-sample counts: 9 (OUTRIDER) vs 18 (PCA) vs 214 (PEER) of 8,166 GTEx samples flagged — OUTRIDER captures the distribution better.
Power: ~50–60 samples suffice to recall most complete-loss pathogenic events; subtler outliers need larger cohorts.

Connections

Part of the wiki’s splicing-methods / RNA-seq analysis toolkit, but for expression rather than splicing — its methodology is explicitly noted as adaptable to splicing outliers (which the Gagneur lab realized as FRASER, the ground truth behind absplice2 / wagner-2025-aberrant-splicing-prediction-during-human).
Builds on the rare-disease RNA-seq diagnostics line (Kremer 2017, Cummings 2017); complements DNA-only WES/WGS by interpreting regulatory/non-coding variants via their molecular consequence — directly relevant to the rare-variant theme in splicing-and-disease and the brain-disease atlases (e.g. walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and).
GTEx’s >85% European composition is flagged as a limitation for the population model — a concrete data-quality-and-bias instance.

Open questions

Could the autoencoder-controlled NB model improve eQTL mapping (the authors note it but did not test)?
Can counts from multiple protocols/platforms be combined so small cohorts merge with GTEx, without batch artifacts confounding the autoencoder?
How best to handle replicates, family structure, and multiple tissues per individual within the outlier framework?
Extending the same outlier methodology to splicing, proteomics, metabolomics, or ATAC-seq features.

wiki/sources/bryois-2022-cell-type-speci.title: bryois-2022-cell-type-specific-cis-eqtls-in-eight
wiki/sources/bryois-2022-cell-type-speci.content:
Cell-type-specific cis-eQTLs in eight human brain cell types identify novel risk genes for psychiatric and neurological disorders
One-line: A single-nuclei cis-eQTL map across eight adult human brain cell types (192 donors) showing that nearly half of genetic effects on expression are cell-type-specific — effects that bulk-tissue eQTLs systematically dilute — and co-localizing these eQTLs with GWAS to nominate disease risk genes and the specific cell types in which they act.
Key takeaways

Single-nuclei RNA-seq + genotyping in 192 adult brains (prefrontal cortex, temporal cortex, deep white matter) yields cis-eQTLs for eight major brain cell types, identifying 7,607 eGenes (4,799 unique genes) at 5% FDR.
46% (3,537/7,607) of cell-type-level eQTLs show cell-type-specific effects, strongest in microglia — direct evidence that aggregating cell types in bulk tissue masks a large fraction of genetic regulation (the bulk-vs-cell-type confounding theme; cf. walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls).
Cell-type-level eQTLs have larger effect sizes and affect more constrained genes than tissue-level eQTLs; a “tissue-like” pseudo-aggregated re-analysis of the same data recovered far fewer eQTLs (3,058 vs the cell-type total) with smaller effect sizes, isolating cell-type resolution (not cohort) as the driver.
Co-localization with GWAS for neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders shows that at most loci a single gene co-localizes in a single cell type, pinpointing both the risk gene and its cellular context (e.g. microglial signals for AD/MS, excitatory-neuron and microglial signals for PD).
Explicitly an expression-only map: the authors flag as a limitation that they “did not measure splicing, which might play an important role in disease, potentially missing a subset of co-localization signals” — making this a complementary counterpart to cell-type/regional splicing-qtl studies and motivating cell-type sQTL work for splicing-and-disease.

Notable claims / data

Cohort/data: single-nuclei RNA-seq + genotyping on 270 brain samples from 148 individuals, integrated with 102 published single-nuclei samples → 192 genotyped donors total. cis window = SNPs within 1 Mb of the TSS; covariates = study, disease status, genotype PCs, expression PCs.
7,607 eGenes at 5% FDR (4,799 unique). Authors estimate the true fraction of testable genes with an eQTL is at least 10–48%, so discoveries are power-limited. eQTL counts track nuclei abundance: 2,734 in excitatory neurons vs only 16 in pericytes.
Replication in bulk cortical Metabrain: 77–100% of SNP–gene pairs replicate at 5% FDR (91–100% concordant direction); neuronal eQTLs replicate slightly better (82%, pi1=86%) than glial (79%, pi1=86%). Effect-size correlation Rb = 0.74–0.94 (neurons 0.85 > glia 0.78).
Effect sizes larger than bulk: mean absolute beta 0.44 (cell-type) vs 0.34 (bulk Metabrain). The within-dataset “tissue-like” aggregation gave 3,058 eQTLs with significantly lower effect sizes — argues bulk RNA-seq underestimates genetic effects.
Positional enrichment: eQTLs enriched near the TSS; 3,027 in gene body, 2,907 upstream, 1,673 downstream; 47–56% of top eQTL SNPs hit the closest gene.
Co-localization: at 61–90% of co-localized loci a single gene co-localized, and usually in a single cell type. Disease examples — AD: APH1B (excitatory + others), NDUFS2/SPPL2A (oligodendrocytes only), ZNF226 at the APOE locus; PD: BIN3/CAMLG/GPNMB (multiple cell types), TMEM163 (microglia only), with complex 17q21 (MAPT/KANSL1) signals attributable to a common inversion; MS: 45 loci, microglial signals at CLECL1, IQCB1, TYMP, VEGFB, etc.
MS context-specificity: of 45 brain co-localized loci, 34 also co-localize in immune cell types; only 11 are brain-exclusive — same haplotype can regulate different genes in brain vs immune contexts, a caution against assuming cell-type assignment from co-localization alone.
Stated limitations: small sample vs bulk eQTL studies (limited power); no splicing measured; few discoveries in rare cell types (16 in pericytes); GWAS reflect disease risk not progression.

Connections

An adult, expression-axis complement to walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and (prenatal bulk eQTL + sQTL): Walker dissects developmental-stage and the splicing layer in bulk tissue, whereas Bryois dissects the cell-type layer in adult tissue but omits splicing — the two confounders (developmental stage, cell-type composition) that bulk QTL maps conflate.
Directly relevant to the bulk-vs-cell-type-confounding theme shared with zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls: both show that the resolution of the assay (region/cell type) determines which regulatory effects and disease co-localizations are even detectable.
The explicit “did not measure splicing” gap motivates cell-type-resolved splicing-qtl mapping; the GWAS co-localization framework here is the same one used to connect splicing-and-disease (sQTL–GWAS co-localization) to risk genes.
Microglia-dominated co-localization for AD/MS positions cell-type eQTLs as a tool for resolving which cell type a non-coding risk variant acts in — a question equally open for splicing QTLs.

Open questions

Cell-type-resolved sQTLs: how much additional, splicing-specific disease co-localization is missed by an expression-only map, and does splicing show the same microglia-skewed cell-type specificity?
Can larger single-nuclei cohorts and targeted enrichment of rare cell types (pericytes, COPs) recover the estimated 10–48% of genes with undetected eQTLs?
For MS loci shared between brain and immune cell types, which context is causal — and can cell-type-specific splicing help disambiguate pleiotropy from genuine multi-context regulation?

wiki/sources/cakir-2020-lifetime-social-.title: cakir-2020-lifetime-social-cost-autism
wiki/sources/cakir-2020-lifetime-social-.content:
The lifetime social cost of autism: 1990–2029
One-line: A US state-by-state cost-of-illness analysis estimating that autism spectrum disorder (ASD) has generated more than $7 trillion in lifetime social costs from 1990–2019, with trillions more projected through 2029.
Key takeaways

Applies a classic cost-of-illness method to autism: estimate the number of ASD cases per state per decade, then multiply by an inflation-adjusted lifetime per-capita cost built from direct medical, special-education, residential-care, and lost-productivity components.
Frames ASD as a chronic neurodevelopment condition often requiring life-long care, and argues that identifying modifiable causes of ASD could yield tangible economic benefit.
Estimated ~2 million new US ASD cases over 1990–2019 with social costs exceeding 7 trillion; an additional ~1 million cases and ~4 trillion are projected for 2020–2029 if prevalence holds flat.
Uses scenario analysis (base case of no prevalence change vs. continued historical increase) borrowed from prior lifetime-cost models; if the historical rate of increase continues, costs could approach nearly $15 trillion by 2029.

Notable claims / data

Prevalence inputs: ADDM/CDC birth-year averages of 7.6 per 1,000 (born 1990–1999) and 14.4 per 1,000 (2000–2009); NHIS-derived 24.7 per 1,000 for 2010–2019. Alternate scenario assumes a 180.77% increase per decade.
Direct medical cost: ~6,142/yr (child, ×15 yrs from assumed diagnosis age 3) and ~4,110.5/yr (adult, ×49 yrs to a life expectancy of 67), totaling ~$293,545 lifetime.
Special-education cost: ~15,114.25/yr using a 40/60 intellectual-disability/no-ID weighting × 13 years (K-12) = ~196,485; a 31/69 split lowers the figure ~2%.
Indirect (lost productivity) cost dominates: ~2,109,358 lifetime per capita, combining lost individual employment (34 yrs × 38,436) and family/caregiver productivity losses; a shifted-household-activity sensitivity raises it ~3%.
Residential/HCBS-waiver care costs computed per state from Medicaid 1915(c) waiver per-enrollee spending (national average ~$56,767 in 2019 dollars); assumes all adults with ASD plus co-occurring ID qualify.
Costs inflated to 2019 dollars via the BLS CPI calculator; per-capita figures drawn from prior US studies (Buescher 2014, Ganz 2007, Leigh & Du 2015, Lavelle 2014, Vohra 2017, etc.).

Connections

Provides an economic/policy framing for autism and neurodevelopment that complements the wiki’s molecular and genetic ASD sources, motivating research into modifiable causes and early intervention.

Open questions

Estimates rest on heterogeneous, non-uniform prior cost studies and strong assumptions (diagnosis at age 3, 67-year life expectancy, employment/care rates); how sensitive is the aggregate to these?
Prevalence increases may partly reflect changing diagnostic criteria and ascertainment rather than true incidence, complicating the projected future-cost scenarios.
The model counts “needs being met” rather than unmet need, potentially understating true social burden (e.g., excluded high EIBI costs).

wiki/sources/cao-2020-immune-checkpoint-.title: cao-2020-immune-checkpoint-molecules-natural-killer
wiki/sources/cao-2020-immune-checkpoint-.content:
Immune checkpoint molecules in natural killer cells as potential targets for cancer immunotherapy
One-line: A review of the inhibitory and activating immune checkpoint receptors that govern natural-killer-cells function, arguing they are druggable targets to restore antitumor immunity in refractory cancers.
Key takeaways

NK cells are innate cytotoxic lymphocytes (~15% of circulating lymphocytes) that kill tumor and infected cells without prior sensitization and without requiring MHC/HLA matching; their effector output reflects a balance of inhibitory versus activating receptor signals.
In the tumor microenvironment, NK cells become dysfunctional through downregulation of activating receptors (e.g., CD226/DNAM-1) and upregulation of inhibitory checkpoints, facilitating tumor immune escape — a form of impaired immune-recognition.
Inhibitory checkpoints reviewed on NK cells: KIR family, PD-1 (CD279), TIGIT, CD96, TIM-3, CD112R (PVRIG), IL-1R8, LAG-3, and NKG2A; most signal via ITIM/ITT motifs that recruit phosphatases (SHP-1, SHP-2, SHIP) to suppress cytotoxicity.
Activating checkpoints reviewed: CD226 (DNAM-1) and NKG2D; inducing these receptors/ligands (NKG2D-ligand α3-domain antibodies, IL-2/IL-15/IL-21, anti-TGF-β agents, demethylating agents) can restore antitumor activity.
Antibody blockade of inhibitory checkpoints (anti-KIR lirilumab, anti-PD-1 sintilimab/pembrolizumab, anti-NKG2A monalizumab) is proposed to reactivate NK-cell antitumor responses, mirroring the T-cell checkpoint-blockade paradigm.

Notable claims / data

PD-1 is highly expressed on CD56dim NK cells in the peripheral blood of ~25% of healthy humans, and elevated on NK cells in ovarian carcinoma, multiple myeloma, Kaposi sarcoma, head and neck cancer, and malignant pleural effusions. PD-1 signals through Y248 (switch motif) engaging SHP-2 to suppress ZAP70, PI3K/AKT, C3G, and ERK.
TIGIT and CD96 both bind CD155 (PVR) and compete with the activating receptor CD226/DNAM-1; antibody blockade of TIGIT or CD96 augments NK antitumor/antimetastatic activity in mouse models. TIGIT is detectable on resting human NK cells but not mouse NK cells.
CD112R (PVRIG) binds PVRL2/CD112 (not CD155) and competes with CD226; blockade — alone or with TIGIT blockade — enhanced trastuzumab-triggered NK antitumor responses in breast cancer preclinically.
IL-1R8 is an NK checkpoint: Il1r8−/− mice show increased NK number/maturity and IL-18-dependent JNK/mTOR activation; genetic IL-1R8 blockade conferred NK-mediated resistance to liver and lung metastasis.
NKG2A/CD94 recognizes HLA-E and strongly inhibits NK granule release via ITIM→SHP-1 (dephosphorylating Vav1); blockade restores NK function. Two clinical-trial tables (preclinical targets and NCT-listed trials for KIR, PD-1, TIGIT, LAG-3, NKG2A, TIM-3) are compiled.
Caveats: the signaling mechanisms of CD96, CD112R, and LAG-3 in human NK cells remain poorly defined; LAG-3 blockade did not affect human NK cytotoxicity in some studies; TIM-3 targeting lacks clear tissue specificity.

Connections

Extends the checkpoint-blockade concept — well established for cytotoxic T cells — to natural-killer-cells, a distinct arm of tumor surveillance.
Frames tumor immune escape as a failure of immune-recognition driven by receptor-signal imbalance rather than antigen loss alone.

Open questions

Which NK-specific checkpoints (CD96, CD112R, IL-1R8) translate from mouse/preclinical findings to human efficacy and safety?
How to achieve tumor-tissue specificity for broadly expressed checkpoints (e.g., TIM-3) to avoid off-target effects on healthy tissue?
Can combined activating-receptor induction plus inhibitory blockade, or CAR-engineered NK cells, outperform single-axis checkpoint therapy?

wiki/sources/capauto-2024-characterizati.title: capauto-2024-characterization-enhancer-activity-early
wiki/sources/capauto-2024-characterizati.content:
Characterization of enhancer activity in early human neurodevelopment using Massively Parallel Reporter Assay (MPRA) and forebrain organoids
One-line: A lentiviral massively-parallel-reporter-assay (lentiMPRA) in iPSC-derived brain-organoids functionally tests ~7000 gene-linked enhancers across neurodevelopmental time, confirming activity for ~35% and providing a gene-regulatory resource.
Key takeaways

Used genomic-integrated lentiMPRA to assay the activity of 6989 putative gene-linked enhancers (7261 total library sequences including 122 positive and 150 negative controls) previously identified by H3K27Ac ChIP-seq + Hi-C in human fetal cortex and forebrain organoids (Amiri et al. 2018).
Enhancers were tested at three time points spanning early neurodevelopment: iPSC stage, terminal-differentiation day 0 (TD0, proliferating progenitors) and TD30 (organoids generating layer 5-6 cortical neurons), interrogating temporal-specific cis-regulatory-elements during neurodevelopment.
A Gaussian mixture model of negative-control activity defined a background distribution; 34.8% of enhancers were MPRA-active in at least one time point, most showing stage-specific activity.
MPRA-active enhancers were linked to genes with significantly higher endogenous expression, and differential MPRA activity correlated (positively or negatively) with differential expression of linked genes across differentiation, connecting reporter activity to gene-regulation.
Delivered as a gene-regulatory resource via the PsychENCODE Knowledge Portal.

Notable claims / data

95.1% (6907/7261) of library sequences recovered by DNA-seq; ~39.9 unique barcodes per enhancer; 94.9% passed QC.
Signal from likely-active sequences was ~55% stronger than background; 1755 core validated enhancers (74.6%) were active at a single time point (193 iPSC-only, 552 TD0, 1010 TD30); 449 shared TD0+TD30. Overall 2352 enhancers validated.
Estimated assay sensitivity only ~35%, with wide overlapping active/background distributions limiting reproducibility; clustering suggested many “inactive” enhancers in cluster 2 were likely true positives missed by low sensitivity.
No significant difference in number of transcription-factor binding sites (FIMO/JASPAR2022) or cognate TF expression between active vs inactive tested enhancers, though positive controls had significantly more binding sites.
79.1% of tested enhancers overlapped external fetal-brain / organoid ATAC-seq datasets; MPRA-active enhancers enriched in the most abundant organoid cell type, radial glial cells (radial-glia).
Example stage-specific hits: an iPSC-only enhancer upstream of G3BP2 (predicted ZNF263 binding); TD0 enhancers linked to CUX1 (predicted ASCL1 binding), RGS4, ANKRD11, ErbB4, FAM107A, P2RX4.
Human iPSC lines (ACE1815, 11251) reprogrammed from skin biopsies of normal individuals; study approved by Yale IRB.

Connections

Applies massively-parallel-reporter-assay as an orthogonal functional readout for cis-regulatory-elements predicted by chromatin-based methods, complementing chromatin-accessibility assays.
Uses forebrain brain-organoids as a developmental model, intersecting bulk and single-cell ATAC-seq to relate enhancer activity to neuronal-cell-types such as radial-glia.
Extends enhancer-target linking (proximity + 3D conformation) toward functional validation relevant to splicing-and-neurodevelopment and broader gene-regulation of the developing human brain.

Open questions

Low (~35%) MPRA sensitivity and transgene silencing over differentiation limit power; how much of the “inactive” fraction is truly inactive versus context-dependent?
Bulk MPRA in heterogeneous organoids biases toward abundant cell types; single-cell MPRA-seq combined with scATAC-seq is needed for cell-type-resolved enhancer dynamics.
270 bp minimal enhancers may omit co-activators or looping-dependent context, potentially causing false negatives.

wiki/sources/capitanchik-2024-from-compu.title: capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the
wiki/sources/capitanchik-2024-from-compu.content:
From computational models of the splicing code to regulatory mechanisms and therapeutic implications
One-line: A review tracing 30 years of computational splicing-code models — from position weight matrices to deep-learning networks like SpliceAI and Pangolin — and how they illuminate splicing regulatory mechanisms and enable rational design of splicing-therapeutics.
Key takeaways

The splicing-code is written in genomic sequence: RNA sequences/structures within exons and introns that determine outcomes (largely via splicing-factors such as snRNAs and rna-binding-proteins) plus DNA sequences that couple splicing to transcription and chromatin. The “ideal” model would predict the full set of transcript isoforms and their frequencies from sequence alone in any cellular context, and approximate underlying biology.
Models evolved through stages: (a) models of core motifs (e.g. MaxEntScan scoring splice-sites); (b) integrative models using ~1,000+ predefined features (Splicing Code, SPANR); (c) end-to-end deep learning that learns features de novo from raw sequence (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice); (d) large omics-prediction models spanning whole loci (Borzoi, BigRNA); (e) self-supervised language models (SpliceBERT, Nucleotide Transformer).
Four core motifs form the minimal genetic code read by the spliceosome: 5′ splice site (consensus G|GU(A/G)AGU, recognized by U1 snRNP), branch point ((C/U)U(A/G)A(C/U)), polypyrimidine tract (bound by U2AF65), and 3′ splice site ((C/U)AG|, recognized by U2AF35). Weaker metazoan core motifs provide flexibility for alternative-splicing.
splicing-regulatory-elements (intronic/exonic enhancers and silencers) act position-dependently and multivalently; the same regulator can enhance or repress depending on binding position relative to splice sites. This combinatorial, cotranscriptional-splicing-coupled logic is what models must capture.
Deep models drive splicing-and-disease applications: SpliceAI and Pangolin predict non-coding cryptic-splicing variants (~10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations); benchmarking informs ClinGen clinical variant classification guidelines.
Splicing is a validated therapeutic target — FDA-approved nusinersen (SSO) and risdiplam (small molecule) promote SMN2 exon 7 inclusion in spinal muscular atrophy; deep models (BigRNA, SpliceAI) are beginning to aid rational design of splice-shifting oligos and splice-gated gene-therapy vectors.

Notable claims / data

Human exons: median length 122 nt (micro-exons as short as 3 nt). Human introns: 30 nt to >1 million nt, median 1,710 nt. Long introns have lower GC than flanking exons; short introns have exon-like high GC and occur in highly expressed genes.
SpliceAI is a 32-layer deep residual CNN trained on human GENCODE; Pangolin uses the same architecture but is trained on multiple species (human, rhesus, mouse, rat) and four tissues (heart, liver, brain, testis), adding tissue-specific splice-site strength outputs.
Context window matters: extending input from 80 nt → 400 nt raised SpliceAI top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95%; extending to 10,000 nt added a further 3–5%. SpliceAI accuracy on lncRNAs (lacking coding bias) was similar to mRNAs (top-k ~84%).
Tissue-specific prediction remains weak: median Spearman correlation with RNA-seq was 0.22 (MTSplice) and 0.43 (Pangolin). DeltaSplice (30,000 nt window, brain RNA-seq from 8 mammals) reached r = 0.69–0.71 on Vex-Seq vs 0.56 for SpliceAI/Pangolin.
Saturation-mutagenesis benchmarking showed all models (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice) predict exonic variants — especially distal ones — much worse than intronic variants: a key area for improvement.
Clinical benchmarks: SpliceAI AUC 0.959 on BRCA1 near-splice-site variants; up to 0.80 (proximal) / 0.95 (distal intronic) on ABCA4; different algorithms win in different genes (SpliceAI best on ABCA4, others better on MYBPC), motivating consensus/integrative models (CADD-Splice, AbSplice, SpliceVault).
Borzoi (built on Enformer) and BigRNA take up to ~0.5 Mb input and predict tissue-specific RNA-seq coverage plus other functional genomics (CAGE, DNase-seq, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, RBP binding); BigRNA reportedly beats SpliceAI on exon-skipping variants and can predict exon-inclusion-promoting SSOs.
In silico mutagenesis of SpliceAI revealed it indirectly predicts nucleosome positions, contributing to exon-boundary determination (splicing-and-chromatin link). Pangolin mutagenesis: mutations nearly always decrease splice-site usage, implying most exons are near-optimal for splicing accuracy.
Interpretability tools (enhanced integrated gradients, Deep SHAP, attention weights, nucleotide dependency maps) applied to models discovered new biology — e.g. EIG on liver exons identified A1CF as a novel liver-specific splicing regulator (later validated); an interpretable model found stem-loops and G-poor sequences drive exon skipping.

Connections

Central reference for splicing-code modelling; complements core-mechanism concepts: spliceosome, splice-sites, splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-factors, rna-binding-proteins, alternative-splicing.
splicing-and-chromatin: DeepCode found H3K36me3 and H3K79me1 most predictive; SF3B1 interacts with nucleosomes; H3K36me3 enriched in (especially constitutive) exons; SpliceAI implicitly learns nucleosome positions.
cotranscriptional-splicing / post-transcriptional-splicing: RNA Pol II elongation speed affects splicing; first-exon splicing needs cap binding complex; last-exon splicing couples to 3′-end cleavage/polyadenylation; splicing inhibits intronic poly(A) use.
splicing-and-evolution: multi-species training (Pangolin, DeltaSplice, SMsplice) leverages functional conservation; developmentally dynamic alternative-splicing events are most conserved across vertebrates; SMsplice found mammals rely more on regulatory elements, plants on core motifs/structure.
splicing-and-disease / splicing-therapeutics: variant diagnostics (ClinGen, ACMG/AMP), SMA (SMN2/nusinersen/risdiplam), Duchenne, ALS (TDP43 cryptic exons; TDP43–RAVER1 fusion proposal), splice-gated vectors designed with SpliceAI.

Open questions

How to close the gap between high black-box accuracy and mechanistic understanding (explainable-AI toolkit underused for splicing vs transcription); over-reliance on black-box models in the clinic is risky.
Context-dependent / tissue-specific alternative-splicing prediction is limited mainly by scarce training data (few alternative events in healthy-tissue RNA-seq; past focus on cassette exons narrowed data).
Predicting full-length transcript isoforms (not just per-splice-site usage) remains unsolved — needs long-read and uniform-coverage single-cell data (Nanopore, PacBio, Smart-Seq3, VASA-seq, SnISOr-Seq, scNanoGPS).
