Need uniform processing, curation and sharing of CLIP/RNA-structure datasets (e.g. Flow, Snakemake/Nextflow workflows); crowdsourced challenges (CAGI, Kaggle/RibonanzaNet) could target alternative-splicing prediction.
Future: multimodal models linking splicing mechanisms (CLIP, RNA structure), outcomes (RNA-seq) and downstream RNA fate (half-life, translation efficiency) in one model.

wiki/sources/cappelluti-2024-durable-eff.title: cappelluti-2024-durable-efficient-gene-silencing
wiki/sources/cappelluti-2024-durable-eff.content:
Durable and efficient gene silencing in vivo by hit-and-run epigenome editing
One-line: Transient LNP-mRNA delivery of programmable epigenetic repressors installs heritable, DNA-break-free DNA-methylation silencing of Pcsk9 in mouse liver that lasts nearly a year, establishing a “hit-and-run” therapeutic paradigm.
Key takeaways

Demonstrates in vivo that transient delivery of epigenome-editing editors can install long-lasting gene silencing, the missing experimental confirmation needed for the therapeutic promise of the approach.
Editors combine three effector domains from an endogenous retrovirus-silencing complex — KRAB, the catalytic domain of de novo methyltransferase DNMT3A (cdDNMT3A), and cofactor DNMT3L — targeted by a programmable DNA-binding domain; durability rests on inherited dna-methylation propagated by endogenous DNMT1.
Zinc-finger proteins (ZFPs) beat dCas9 and TALE DNA-binding platforms for silencing mouse Pcsk9: ZFP-based ETRs were 5.7x and 2.8x more potent than dCas9- and TALE-based architectures respectively.
Introduces evoetr, an all-in-one single-molecule editor that outperforms the three-component combination while avoiding the DNA breaks of conventional genome-editing.
Unlike KRAB-only CRISPRi (crispri-crispra) repressors, which need continuous editor expression, the multi-domain design makes silencing self-sustaining after a single transient dose (“hit-and-run”).

Notable claims / data

A single intravenous dose of LNP-loaded ZFP-ETR mRNAs almost halved (~40% of vehicle) circulating PCSK9 for up to 330 days (nearly one year) in C57BL/6 mice; LDL-cholesterol dropped ~35% at day 30.
Silencing and repressive CpG methylation persisted through forced liver regeneration (partial hepatectomy, robust hepatocyte proliferation), directly corroborating heritability of the installed epigenetic state through active cell division.
Specificity in vitro: ZFP-ETRs deregulated only 8 genes beyond Pcsk9 (none nearby; no spread to the 40 adjacent genes), genome-wide methylation delta <1.2%; EvoETR-8 was cleaner still (Pcsk9 the only significantly downregulated gene).
EvoETR-8 reduced circulating PCSK9 ~75% (vs ~50% for triple-ETR, ~70% for CRISPR–Cas9) to day 43, with ~1.9-fold higher Pcsk9-promoter methylation than triple-ETR, and no detectable genetic alterations — whereas CRISPR–Cas9 imposed a 45% mutational burden at its target.
In vitro-nominated off-target DMRs were confirmed in treated mouse hepatocytes, supporting the predictive value of in vitro screening for off-target nomination.
No promoter methylation changes in lung, spleen, kidney or pancreas, confirming LNP hepatocyte-targeting specificity.

Connections

Direct application of programmable epigenome-editing with a repressive effector payload; contrasts with sequence-altering genome-editing (CRISPR–Cas9 used here as the benchmark/control).
Extends crispri-crispra-style transcriptional repression by adding heritable dna-methylation for durability, addressing the lability of KRAB-only marks in somatic cells.
Situated within gene-regulation: silencing operates on the promoter–enhancer/CpG-island region via coordinated histone-mark exchange plus de novo CpG methylation.
New entity evoetr captures the all-in-one editor design as a reusable tool.

Open questions

Long-term durability of the all-in-one EvoETR beyond 43 days in vivo (the year-long durability was shown for the three-component ZFP-ETRs).
Whether the residual off-target DMRs/DEGs pose safety concerns at clinical scale, and how specificity generalizes beyond Pcsk9.
Translatability of ZFP-based editors and this LNP formulation (which caused transient ALT/AST rises) to human hepatocytes and non-liver tissues.

wiki/sources/castro-2023-challenges-scre.title: castro-2023-challenges-screening-noncoding-variants
wiki/sources/castro-2023-challenges-scre.content:
Challenges in screening for de novo noncoding variants contributing to genetically complex phenotypes
One-line: A critical reassessment showing that the failure to find de novo noncoding variant enrichments in autism is driven not just by sample size but by the quality and disease-relevance of the annotations and the reliability of the variant calls themselves.
Key takeaways

Using whole-genome sequencing from 1,917 SSC quad families (simons-simplex-collection), the authors called 266,685 autosomal de novo single-nucleotide variants (dnSNVs); ~98% fall in noncoding regions, and there is no significant difference in dnSNV count between probands and unaffected siblings.
They recover the known coding signal (predicted loss-of-function coding mutations enriched in probands, FDR p = 0.002) as a positive control, validating their ensembl-vep-based calling and Fisher’s-exact-test screening pipeline.
Across 65 annotation categories, NO noncoding category survives multiple-testing correction — consistent with prior noncoding-variants studies of autism.
The core message: for detecting regulatory dnSNV enrichment, improving annotation quality and disease/tissue relevance matters more than simply increasing the number of families, because current annotations are only marginally more informative than random.

Notable claims / data

Power to detect enrichment at the current sample size: 97% for high-impact coding dnSNVs, but only 27% (missense coding) and 32% (best noncoding category, brain-specific TURF) elsewhere.
Brain-specific TURF scores would need ~10,000 families for 80% power; generic (tissue-agnostic) TURF would need >50,000 families — highlighting the large effect of the variant-prioritization training strategy.
Generic TURF (12% power) underperformed a manually curated fetal-brain-promoter category (13%) despite using more annotations (Wilcoxon p = 6.0e-12); the fetal-brain-promoter category itself would still need ~37k families.
Permutation test (10,000 label shuffles): all three noncoding categories had Z scores well below 2 (fetal brain promoters 0.53, generic TURF 0.48, brain TURF 1.16), versus 3.57 for high-impact coding — even the best noncoding annotation is nearly uninformative for ASD risk.
Variant-set quality matters: the intersection of this study’s dnSNVs with Zhou et al.’s (111,029 variants) gave a stronger disease-impact-score signal (p < 0.0026) than either dataset alone, while disjoint sets were non-significant, implying disjoint calls are enriched for false positives.
Different prioritization scores carry different biases: TURF-brain did better under Fisher’s exact test while the disease impact score did better under Wilcoxon rank-sum, arguing for cross-method comparison.

Connections

Uses tissue-specific variant prioritization (TURF, an extension of RegulomeDB) as a case for cis-regulatory-elements scoring; relates to mutation-effect-prediction tools such as cadd-score and ensembl-vep.
Directly addresses the missing-heritability of autism via noncoding-variants and connects to psychiatric-genetics and genome-wide-association-study signal mapping to noncoding regions.
Touches chromatin-accessibility (DNase-seq) and enhancer/promoter annotation as inputs to regulatory-potential categories.

Open questions

Would stratifying by ASD subclasses reduce the phenotypic heterogeneity that attenuates dnSNV signal?
Can machine-learning prioritization trained on disease-specific (not merely tissue-specific) regulatory variants close the gap, as the disease impact score’s edge over brain-specific TURF suggests?
How should the field standardize de novo variant identification and validation to improve cross-study reproducibility, given that intersecting call sets raised detection power?

wiki/sources/catta-preta-2023-combinator.title: catta-preta-2023-combinatorial-transcription-factor-binding-encodes
wiki/sources/catta-preta-2023-combinator.content:
Combinatorial transcription factor binding encodes cis-regulatory wiring of forebrain GABAergic neurogenesis
One-line: Integrating ChIP-seq for 12 TFs with chromatin state and 3D contacts in E13.5 mouse basal ganglia, the study shows combinatorial TF binding at enhancers encodes GABAergic neurogenesis while repressing alternative fates.
Key takeaways

Twelve TF ChIP-seq datasets (9 homeobox TFs from 6 classes, plus ASCL1, NR2F1, SP9) were merged with H3K4me3 PLAC-seq, ChromHMM chromatin state, and RNA-seq to build a TF-anchored regulatory map of the developing basal ganglia, defining cis-regulatory-elements driving neurogenesis.
K-means clustering of ChIP-seq neighborhood signal split TF-bound loci into distinct pRE clusters; broadly-bound clusters (1_D, 2_D) activate GABAergic differentiation programs (axon guidance, interneuron-migration) while single/few-TF clusters skew toward inactive/repressive states suppressing pallial and excitatory fates.
Sequence analysis supports a “billboard” model of cis-regulatory-grammar: relevant motifs (TAATTA homeobox, etc.) cluster within a core region but show no deterministic spacing/orientation syntax.
Broadly-bound enhancers show exceptional vertebrate conservation, high TAATTA motif density (biased to symmetric/palindromic instances), and complex 3D interactions (chromatin-loops) consistent with biomolecular-condensates-based recruitment of co-factors and RNA Pol.

Notable claims / data

27,398 merged TF-bound loci (22,297 distal pREs + 5,101 promoter-proximal); 57.5% bound by a single TF, only 2.7% bound by 8-12 TFs.
PLAC-seq: 113,048 significant interactions, 13,128 PLAC-seq contacts, 1,974 ensembles; 48% of TF-bound pREs overlap PSCs, 30% within loops.
7 proximal + 18 distal pRE clusters; clusters 1_D/2_D average 6-8 of 12 TFs bound and span all 9 homeobox TFs.
Cell-type overlap (chromatin-accessibility via snATAC-seq): 63% of pREs overlap OCRs; 1_D/2_D overlap 70%/60% of developmental cell-type-specific OCRs.
Conservation: distal cluster PhastCons scores range 365 (near-random) to 662 (1_D, the extreme); strong correlation between number of TFs bound and conservation.
Transgenic enhancer assays: distal pREs with 8-12 TF binding overlap 48% of VISTA subpallial enhancers vs 3% of no-activity elements; 84 novel TF-bound pREs tested, broader-binding clusters enriched for subpallial specificity.

Connections

Provides in vivo mammalian complement to combinatorial cis-regulatory-grammar and enhancer models; contrasts strict-syntax expectations with a billboard architecture.
Ties TF binding to chromatin-loops and enhancer-promoter contact ensembles, relevant to 3D-genome regulation of neurodevelopment.
Enhancer/OCR partitioning by neuronal-cell-types connects to single-cell atlases of prenatal forebrain development (e.g. velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal).

Open questions

Do the broadly-bound “condensate-like” enhancers actually nucleate biomolecular condensates, and is co-occupancy cooperative or competitive at these loci?
How much of combinatorial binding at shared TAATTA motifs is direct vs assembly-driven, given SP9’s largely indirect binding?
Can TF-binding modules predict enhancer activity and cell-type specificity well enough to serve as a general model of GRN wiring beyond basal ganglia?

wiki/sources/chardon-2024-multiplex-sing.title: chardon-2024-multiplex-single-cell-crispra-screening
wiki/sources/chardon-2024-multiplex-sing.content:
Multiplex, single-cell CRISPRa screening for cell type specific regulatory elements
One-line: A scalable framework combining highly multiplexed CRISPR-activation perturbations with single-cell RNA-seq to find cell-type-specific, CRISPRa-responsive cis-regulatory elements and the genes they upregulate.
Key takeaways

Introduces multiple random CRISPRa gRNA combinations per cell, then computationally partitions cells by detected gRNA for differential expression — a single-cell-crispr-screen design that gains power because each transcriptome is informative for many perturbations.
Screens for sufficiency of cis-regulatory-elements (promoters and enhancers) to drive upregulation, complementing prior crispri-crispra necessity screens; extends the multiplex sc-approach previously used for CRISPRi.
The same 493-gRNA library was applied in K562 cells and iPSC-derived excitatory neurons, targeting candidate promoters of 9 haploinsufficient ASD/NDD (autism/neurodevelopment) risk genes plus enhancers and controls.
Enhancer responsiveness to CRISPRa is strongly cell-type-restricted, implying dependence on the cis chromatin landscape and/or trans-acting factors — motivating “cis-regulatory therapy” (CRT) for haploinsufficient disorders.

Notable claims / data

Library: 493 gRNAs = 30 TSS positive controls (from hCRISPRa-v2), 313 candidate promoter gRNAs (50 TSSs of BCL11A, TCF4, ANK2, CHD8, TBR1, SCN2A, SYNGAP1, FOXP1, SHANK3), 100 candidate enhancer gRNAs, 50 NTCs; cloned into the piggyFlex piggyBac transposon vector for high-MOI integration and direct gRNA capture.
K562: 33,944 QC-passing cells, 79% with ≥1 gRNA, mean 2.5 gRNAs/cell and 178 cells/gRNA; 59 activating hit gRNAs at empirical FDR 0.1 (dCas9-VP64 stronger than dCas9-VPR). 45/47 promoter and 9/12 enhancer hits exclusively upregulated the intended target within 1 Mb.
Neurons (WTC11, dox-inducible NGN2 + TMP-inducible ecDHFR-dCas9-VPH): 51,183 cells, 89% with ≥1 gRNA, mean 6.1 gRNAs/cell, 638 cells/gRNA; 17 hits (all promoter/TSS). 16/17 target-specific; 5 gRNAs upregulated TBR1 (not expressed in NGN2 neurons) despite closed chromatin.
Enhancer cell-type specificity: 12/60 K562 hits were enhancers, none were hits in neurons; SCEPTRE reanalysis (covariate-aware) called 21 K562 vs. only 1 neuron enhancer hit. SCEPTRE raised totals to 83 (K562) and 35 (neuron) hits with high overlap to the original approach.
Recovered the therapeutically validated SCN2A “H1 gRNA” exclusively in neurons. Singleton bulk RNA-seq validation: 14/16 experiments matched expected (Pearson r=0.85 vs. single-cell fold changes). Median hit fold-change 1.7; 79/118 SCEPTRE hits reached ≥50% upregulation.

Connections

Builds on multiplex single-cell CRISPRi enhancer screening exemplified by gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements, and on multiplex single-cell CRISPR-screen foundations from dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits and adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening.
Fits the CRISPR functional-genomics landscape reviewed in kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for.
The recovered SCN2A therapeutic gRNA connects to the SCN2A CRISPRa rescue work captured in scn2a-crispra and tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related.
Complements reporter-based dissection of cis-regulatory-elements such as deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory by instead perturbing elements in their endogenous context.

Open questions

What determines “activatability” of a locus (e.g. TBR1 in neurons despite closed chromatin) — chromatin environment vs. the trans-acting factor milieu?
Can CRISPRa-specific gRNA design rules for distal, cell-type-specific enhancers be derived, given current rules are promoter/CRISPRi-centric?
Does treating cells as pseudo-replicates overestimate DE genes, and how far can per-cell gRNA multiplicity and cheaper scRNA-seq scale screens toward the ~660 haploinsufficient disease genes?

wiki/sources/che-2026-multiscale-evoluti.title: che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome
wiki/sources/che-2026-multiscale-evoluti.content:
Multiscale evolution of the 3D genome
One-line: A review proposing a micro-to-macro comparative framework that treats 3D genome-architecture as an evolvable trait — rapidly plastic at the cellular/cancer scale, diverging mainly through cis-regulatory-elements within conserved folding frameworks between closely related species, and broadly diverse across the tree of life yet recurrently convergent at higher-order features (see 3d-genome-evolution).
Key takeaways

The mammalian nuclear genome folds into a hierarchy revealed by chromatin-conformation-capture and imaging: chromatin-loops (~10 kb / ~200 kb), topologically-associating-domains (TADs, ~150 kb / ~1 Mb), and chromatin-compartments (multi-Mb, A/B). These layers dynamically modulate the spatial proximity between cis-regulatory-elements and promoters, shaping gene-expression programs and cellular identity.
The central question is how genome-architecture itself evolves. Most prior work compared isolated samples (developmental stage, age, disease) without tracing origins; the paper synthesizes comparative work into a single multiscale evolutionary framework (3d-genome-evolution).
Microscale (cancer): Architecture evolves on the shortest timescale via clonal selection of genomic mutations and plastic epigenetic reprogramming, rewiring chromatin-loops, topologically-associating-domains, and enhancer–promoter contacts (3d-genome-and-disease). Epigenetic changes are a more plastic but equally heritable (across cell divisions) substrate for selection than genomic mutations.
Closely related species: Divergence operates on a longer timescale with stable inheritance, but is restricted to specific loci, driven primarily by sequence-encoded changes in cis-regulatory-elements / TF-binding sites within conserved folding frameworks (the CTCF–cohesin loop-extrusion machinery). Crucially, the chromatin structures themselves are NOT heritable across species — only the molecular information that re-forms them.
Macroscale (tree of life): Genome architectures show striking diversity, yet recurrent higher-order features (loops, domains, Rabl/rosette configurations) arise via convergent evolution through distinct molecular mechanisms — selection acts on architectural function, not on specific molecular components.
A general trend: more intricate 3D organization parallels increasing organismal complexity (proxied by number of cell types), tied to independently evolved multicellularity — though this correlation is mainly demonstrated within animals and unclear in plants.

Notable claims / data

Hierarchy scales (text gives two scale sets): loops ~10 kb (Fig 1) / ~200 kb (intro); TADs ~150 kb (Fig 1) / ~1 Mb (intro); compartments multi-Mb / whole-genome.
Loop extrusion model: cohesin extrudes DNA loops until blocked by convergent pairs of CTCF-binding sites, forming loops and TADs; transcription-related enhancer–promoter loops in mammals are mediated/stabilized by RNA Pol II and TF binding.
A recent comparative analysis suggests 3D genome organization is evolutionarily more flexible (lower constraint) than many other epigenetic markers.
Primate brain: human-specific chromatin loops (enriched for enhancer–enhancer interactions) upregulate neurodevelopmental genes (axon guidance, synapse assembly); young Alu elements are significantly enriched at human-specific TAD boundaries.
Cancer examples: glioblastoma — pervasive SVs create novel enhancer–promoter loops; familial adenomatous polyposis — global loss of promoter–enhancer loops in early colorectal carcinogenesis; DNMT3A mutations in HSPCs — genome-wide CpG hypomethylation promoting stemness; PML-RARα fusion (t15;17, acute promyelocytic leukemia) — aberrant looping that globally condenses the genome; STAG2 (cohesin) mutation in Ewing sarcoma — genome-wide loss of enhancer–promoter interactions; relapsed pediatric B-ALL — comprehensively restructured architecture.
“Structural bottleneck”: in Kras-driven lung adenocarcinoma and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, a transient stage of nuclear compaction and reduced architectural variability may funnel cells to a higher-fitness state, accelerating malignancy.
Convergence across kingdoms: insects use insulator proteins Su(Hw), Ibf1, Ibf2, Cp190 instead of CTCF; non-bilaterians without CTCF (sea walnut Mnemiopsis leidyi) use zinc-finger TFs CTEP1/2; sea walnut lacks compartments; Mueller’s freshwater sponge (Ephydatia muelleri) lacks TADs; placozoans show promoter hubs; Xenopus laevis shows Rabl configuration.
Plants: Arabidopsis thaliana / A. lyrata show little TAD evidence (later higher-res work found >1000 weaker, smaller ~10 kb domains vs human ~100 kb); TADs clearer in rice, foxtail millet, sorghum, tomato, maize, cotton. No CTCF in plants; boundaries enriched for highly expressed genes. Standard protocols detect only global B (pericentromeric, transposon-rich) and A (gene-dense) compartments; functional local compartments need customized local eigenvalue analysis. Plants use linear gene clustering instead of spatial 3D clustering for co-expression — same goal, distinct mechanism. Chromosome-scale Rabl or rosette (chromocenters + nucleolar telomeres) configurations.
Bacteria: no histones/nucleosomes; nucleoid-associated proteins (NAPs) — H-NS (silencing filaments/bridges), SMCs (loop extrusion ori→ter for segregation), DNA-benders Fis/IHF/HU. Chromosome interaction domains (TAD-analogues, boundaries at highly expressed genes/NAP sites), macrodomains (Ori/Ter via MaoP/MatP), interarm interactions as a secondary Hi-C diagonal (SMC loop extrusion); highly responsive to osmolarity/temperature. E. coli active domains vs repressed compartments show distinct architectures.
Condensin II drives gain/loss and disruption of Rabl-like configurations; a hypothesis links Rabl/rosette structures to cellular totipotency (frequent in plant cells, early mammalian embryos) — unverified.
The author’s companion study (Che et al., 2026, Cell) — “evolution of high-order genome architecture revealed from 1,000 species” — underpins the complexity-correlation and linear-clustering claims.

Connections

First source of a new 3D-genome topic — establishes the controlled vocabulary: genome-architecture, chromatin-compartments, topologically-associating-domains, chromatin-loops, chromatin-conformation-capture, 3d-genome-evolution, 3d-genome-and-disease, cis-regulatory-elements. No prior 3D-genome sources to link or contradict.
The three scales relate as a continuum of decreasing plasticity and increasing constraint: cancer (near-absent negative selection, fully reversible epigenetic exploration) → closely related species (sequence-constrained, heritable only via re-forming information) → kingdoms (functional convergence via distinct machinery). Mechanistic parallels exist between cancer and interspecies divergence (both rewire expression by altering local chromatin at TF-binding sites); the key distinction is heritability of the structures.
Distinct from existing wiki topics (splicing, artificial-intelligence). Tangential bridge: the paper cites RNA splicing and protein disorder alongside genome architecture as regulatory layers supporting organismal complexity — a potential cross-link to the splicing topic.

Open questions

Which architectural components evolve under direct selection vs. emerge as biophysical/polymer byproducts?
How do architectural plasticity and sequence-encoded constraints interact — are folding states explored epigenetically before being stabilized genetically (model’s generality unknown)?
To what extent do transposable elements, TF evolution, and SMC dynamics jointly drive divergence? No unified framework yet quantifies their relative impact.
Are recurrent cross-kingdom features (Rabl configurations, boundaries at active genes) convergent optima or shared ancestry?
Can quantitative, species-aware metrics capture architectural intensity (TAD compaction, loop strength) beyond binary presence/absence? Canonical mammalian pipelines fail on non-mammals (e.g. plant local compartments, dinoflagellate condensed heterochromatin).
Can ancestral 3D-genome states be reconstructed (comparative Hi-C + phylogenetics + machine learning)? What are the limits of architectural reorganization a viable cell tolerates?
The regulatory role of conserved features (the universal SMC family; spontaneous aggregation of repressed transposons forming plant telomeres / mammalian repressed compartments) remains elusive.

wiki/sources/chen-2021-association-mater.title: chen-2021-association-maternal-diabetes-neurodevelopmental-disorders
wiki/sources/chen-2021-association-mater.content:
Association of maternal diabetes with neurodevelopmental disorders: autism spectrum disorders, attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and intellectual disability
One-line: A Swedish national register cohort of 2.37 million births showing that all forms of maternal diabetes raise offspring odds of ASD, ADHD and intellectual disability, most strongly for co-morbid presentations including ID, with paternal comparisons suggesting partial confounding by shared familial (genetic) factors.
Key takeaways

Population-based cohort (“Psychiatry Sweden” register linkage) of 2 369 680 singletons born 1987–2010, followed to 2016, examining maternal type 1 (T1DM), pre-gestational type 2 (T2DM) and gestational diabetes (GDM) versus offspring neurodevelopment disorders: autism spectrum disorder, ADHD and intellectual disability.
All maternal diabetes types were associated with increased odds of NDDs; T2DM showed the strongest associations, and associations were consistently largest for diagnostic groups that included intellectual disability.
A paternal-comparison (family-based) design (twin-and-family-study-design) found paternal T1DM/T2DM also associated with offspring NDDs but more weakly, implying the maternal-diabetes associations are partly confounded by shared familial factors such as genetic liability rather than being purely intrauterine effects.
Timing mattered: GDM diagnosed at 27–30 weeks of gestation carried the greatest risk of NDDs, especially outcomes co-occurring with ID.

Notable claims / data

Diagnosis prevalence: 45 678 (1.93%) ASD, 20 823 (0.88%) ID, 102 018 (4.31%) ADHD; the three disorders overlapped considerably.
Adjusted (Model 2) odds ratios for maternal T2DM: any ASD 1.37 (95% CI 1.03–1.84), any ID 2.09 (1.53–2.87), any ADHD 1.43 (1.16–1.77).
Within ID-containing outcomes, T2DM gave OR 2.42 (1.67–3.49) for “ID without ASD”; T1DM gave OR 1.58 (1.40–1.79) for any ID and 1.54 (1.25–1.90) for ID co-occurring with ASD.
Adjusting maternal-diabetes models for paternal diabetes had “no material effect,” and estimates were robust across sensitivity analyses (missing-data exclusion, gestational-weight-gain adjustment, pre/post-1993 GDM-criteria stratification).
Potential mediators on the causal pathway (maternal hypertensive disease, gestational age, size for gestational age, Apgar) were deliberately excluded from adjustment.

Connections

Complements register/epidemiological perspectives on autism and psychiatric-genetics within brain-development-neurogenomics, framing prenatal metabolic exposure against genetic liability.
The paternal-comparison logic parallels twin-and-family-study-design and liability-threshold reasoning (liability-threshold-model) used to disentangle shared familial confounding in quantitative-genetics.
Situates a non-genomic environmental risk factor alongside the wiki’s molecular accounts of neurodevelopment.

Open questions

How much of the residual maternal-specific association reflects a true hyperglycaemic intrauterine mechanism versus unmeasured shared environment or genetic pleiotropy between diabetes and NDDs?
Why is the 27–30 wkGA GDM window most harmful, and does it mark a developmental critical period or ascertainment/severity artefacts?
Would molecular or genomic data (e.g. polygenic scores for diabetes and NDDs) directly quantify the shared genetic liability the paternal comparison only infers?

wiki/sources/chen-2023-robust-difference.title: chen-2023-robust-differences-cortical-cell-type
wiki/sources/chen-2023-robust-difference.content:
Robust differences in cortical cell type proportions across healthy human aging inferred through cross-dataset transcriptome analyses
One-line: Cross-dataset cellular deconvolution of bulk frontal cortex transcriptomes shows healthy aging is associated with proportionally fewer inhibitory interneurons (esp. SST, VIP) and more astrocytes and other non-neuronal cells, validated against snRNA-seq.
Key takeaways

Aging in human frontal cortex is accompanied by robust, reproducible shifts in the relative proportions of neuronal-cell-types, not just gene-level expression changes.
The largest-magnitude losses are in somatostatin (SST) and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) interneurons; astrocytes and other glia increase with age.
Findings from indirect bulk-tissue deconvolution are confirmed by direct proportion estimates from single-nucleus RNA-seq (rna-seq), supporting that bulk expression differences reflect genuine cell-proportion changes.
Microglia show a suggestive non-linear “U-shaped” (biphasic) relationship with age rather than a monotonic trend.

Notable claims / data

Analyzed 8 bulk gene-expression datasets across 6 cohorts (1142 subjects, 1429 samples), ages of death 15–90 years, from individuals free of psychiatric conditions or brain pathologies; frontal cortex (DLPFC, BA11, BA47, frontal cortex).
Relative cell type proportions (rCTPs) for 13 transcriptomically-defined cell types estimated via markerGeneProfile (MGP), using PCA over novel cell-type marker genes derived from the Allen Institute human neocortex snRNA-seq “subclass” taxonomy (49,495 nuclei).
Cross-dataset mega-analysis (mixed-effects model covarying PMI and sex, subject as random effect): largest negative age effects in SST (std. β = -0.52, FDR = 2.73e-67), VIP (std. β = -0.50, FDR = 2.75e-63), PAX6 interneurons (std. β = -0.49); excitatory neurons more modest (std. β = -0.28).
Non-neuronal cells positively associated with age: pericytes (std. β = 0.36), VLMCs (std. β = 0.35), astrocytes (std. β = 0.30). No significant effect of biological sex on any rCTP.
Microglia: quadratic vs. linear ANOVA p = 1.65e-28; decreasing rCTP in younger group (15–50 yr; std. β = -0.319) then increasing in older group (>50 yr; std. β = 0.276) — among the first reports of a biphasic microglial proportion trajectory.
Validated with 2 snRNA-seq cohorts (MSSM + McLean, 75 controls): SST snCTP std. β = -0.705, PVALB -0.494, VIP -0.355; bulk vs. single-nucleus per-cell-type effect sizes strongly correlated (R = 0.80, p = 0.016).
Cell-type marker genes are largely NOT differentially expressed with age within their own cell type (e.g., 0/8 SST markers significant), arguing that bulk signal reflects proportion shifts, not per-cell expression changes.
MGP and dtangle deconvolution agreed well and generally outperformed MuSiC when benchmarked against snRNA-seq proportions in 41 overlapping MSSM subjects (Spearman rho up to 0.79 for SST).

Connections

Complements wiki material on neuronal-cell-types and cortical composition, extending cell-type resolution to the healthy human aging axis rather than development or disease.
Uses bulk and single-nucleus rna-seq jointly; the concordance between deconvolution and direct nucleus counting is a methodological anchor point for other cell-proportion inference work.
SST/VIP interneuron vulnerability connects to broader interneuron biology themes in the brain-development-neurogenomics topic.

Open questions

The design is cross-sectional (age at death), so within-individual aging trajectories and causality cannot be inferred.
What drives the biphasic microglial pattern, and does it reflect true proliferation/loss versus taxonomy-mapping artifacts in rare cell types?
Excitatory neurons show a bulk decrease but no significant snCTP association — is this a resolution/marker-availability limitation or a real discrepancy?
Relative (not absolute) proportions are estimated, so an increase in one compartment mechanically depresses others; absolute-count validation remains needed.

wiki/sources/chen-2024-increased-enhance.title: chen-2024-increased-enhancer-promoter-interactions-during
wiki/sources/chen-2024-increased-enhance.content:
Increased enhancer–promoter interactions during developmental enhancer activation in mammals
One-line: In vivo capture Hi-C of ~935 validated developmental enhancers across ten mouse embryonic tissues shows enhancer–promoter proximity moderately but consistently rises upon enhancer activation.
Key takeaways

Enhancer capture Hi-C on 935 bona fide developmental cis-regulatory-elements (from the VISTA Enhancer Browser) plus 176 promoters and 87 negative controls across ten E11.5 mouse tissues mapped tissue-resolved enhancer interactomes.
Enhancer–promoter (E–P) and enhancer–enhancer (E–E) interaction frequencies are moderately but consistently increased in tissues where an enhancer is active in vivo, supporting an activation-linked looping model.
Most enhancer contacts fall within topologically-associating-domains (~80% intra-TAD); 61% of enhancers bypass their nearest neighboring gene to reach more distal targets.
Skipped genes are frequently silenced and marked by promoter CpG methylation, not by polycomb (H3K27me3) or heterochromatin (H3K9me3) signals.
Only 13.3% of E–P interactions are tissue-invariant chromatin-loops; these form independently of the enhancer and are likely mediated by adjacent CTCF binding.

Notable claims / data

24,657 significant interactions total (17,988 enhancer-baited): 2,818 E–P, 5,612 E–E, 5,140 enhancer–CTCF; median E–P distance ~410 kb.
E–P interaction increase upon activity: 1.3-fold (neural tube, P=7.3e-11) up to 1.6-fold (forebrain, P=1.03e-42); limb 1.62-fold, face 1.62-fold, heart 1.3-fold; clustering of 969 E–P pairs correlated with activity (logistic regression P=9.7e-46).
Enhancer knockouts validate targets: Δhs654 reduced midbrain Zic4 ~34% (Padj<9.5e-3); Δhs267/hs266/hs853 reduced Mir9-2 precursor ~64% (Padj<7.8e-32) with no Tmem161b change.
3D-FISH super-resolution confirmed decreased E–P interprobe distance in active tissues (e.g. hs266–Mir9-2 P=9.53e-7; hs654–Zic1/Zic4 P=1.18e-4).
Forebrain skipped genes: 52.4% methylated (80.8% avg CpG at TSS), 8-fold lower DNase accessibility and 56-fold lower expression than interacting genes (P<0.0001).

Connections

Provides in vivo, functionally-validated E–P looping evidence complementing the genome-architecture and chromatin-conformation-capture framework of loop extrusion within topologically-associating-domains.
Enhancer bypass of methylated neighbors adds a selectivity mechanism to how cis-regulatory-elements choose targets, relevant to interpretation of noncoding disease variants.

Open questions

What determines the moderate (rather than switch-like) magnitude of the activity-linked interaction increase?
How do the CTCF-anchored invariant loops mechanistically enable or constrain the dynamic E–P contacts formed on activation?
Does promoter CpG methylation cause enhancer bypass or merely correlate with the silenced state?

wiki/sources/chi-2026-single-cell-mappin.title: chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory
wiki/sources/chi-2026-single-cell-mappin.content:
Single-cell mapping of regulatory DNA-protein interactions
One-line: Introduces d-and-d-seq, a single-cell immuno-tethering method that uses an antibody-binding nanobody fused to a cytosine base editor to record weak or transient transcription-factor binding as C-to-U edits, integrable with scATAC-seq, WGS, single-cell genotyping, and 10x Multiome.
Key takeaways

D&D-seq (docking and deamination followed by sequencing) maps DNA-protein interactions in low-input/single-cell primary samples by tethering the double-stranded DNA deaminase DddA (split DddA11) to an antibody-bound target via secondary nanobodies (nb-DddA); cytosine-to-uracil edits near binding sites are read out by sequencing. This sidesteps the high-salt stringency of pA-Tn5 tagmentation (CUT&Tag) that disrupts weak TF interactions.
A split base-editor design gives switch-like control: nb-DddA_NT is catalytically inactive until the DddA_CT C-terminal peptide (plus Zn2+) is added, avoiding nonspecific deamination during DNA binding.
Captures binding of sequence-specific TFs (CTCF, GATA1, GATA2, JUN, SP1, TAL1) and of transient/non-sequence-specific chromatin remodelers (p300 histone acetyltransferase), the latter giving broader footprints.
Compatible with standard single-cell multi-omic workflows; coupling to WGS (PTA) captures CTCF binding outside accessible chromatin, including in H3K9me3/H3K27me3 heterochromatin — relevant to cis-regulatory-elements in inactive compartments.
Integrates with single-cell genotyping (GoT-ChA → “D&D-GoT-ChA”) to link somatic mutations to TF-binding changes; applied to a human clonal-hematopoiesis (CHIP) patient with IDH2 R140Q.
D&D-seq CTCF profiles plus scATAC-seq feed the C.Origami deep-learning model to predict cell-type-specific 3D genome / Hi-C contact maps from single cells, bridging to the 3d-genome field.

Notable claims / data

nb-DddA preferentially deaminates cytosines in TpC (and CC) context, active at 37°C, Zn2+-dependent; recapitulates unmodified-enzyme base-editing activity.
Edit events show a bimodal “molecular footprint” within a 200-bp window centered on HOCOMOCO-defined binding sites; background ATAC regions show no footprint or motif enrichment.
WGS+D&D identified 2,045 CTCF peaks outside open chromatin; 196 inaccessible peaks overlapped H3K9me3/H3K27me3 marks. K562 WGS via PTA confirmed expected karyotype (predicted average ploidy 2.75).
Single-cell cell-line mixing (CA46 n=4,108; K562 n=1,732): CTCF/GATA1 signals retained only in the antibody-matched subpopulation (cell-level specificity). Mean fragments/cell ~5,263 (K562) / 5,437 (CA46).
Specificity benchmark: scD&D-seq CTCF FRiP ~60% (stable from 5,000 down to 10 cells: 56.75–57.00%), vs uliCUT&RUN 13.69–14.04%. CTCF median FRiP 0.57, GATA1 0.43, vs Olig2 scCUT&Tag ~0.22. After motif filtering, 89% of CTCF and 74% of GATA1 D&D peaks overlapped ChIP-seq references.
Logistic/lasso regression on-target peak classifier (trained on 5 bulk CTCF/GATA1 datasets): test AUC 0.84, PR-AUC 0.70; generalized to other TFs and scD&D-seq (AUC 0.73–0.89); “max edits in 100-bp window” the top feature (edits concentrated ±50 bp from motif center due to nanobody tethering constraints).
PBMC study (n=5,358 cells); D&D-C.Origami Hi-C predictions from scD&D-seq comparable to those from ENCODE CTCF ChIP-seq, both beating no-CTCF control (CD8 T cells). Authors caution these 3D results are preliminary.
CHIP IDH2 R140Q sample (VAF 0.15): genotyped 33% (5,291/15,807) of cells via D&D-GoT-ChA; MUT cells enriched in CD8 T subcluster, validated by FACS-sorted bulk nanopore genotyping. After regressing out ATAC fragment counts, WT CTCF edits significantly higher than MUT — consistent with IDH-MUT 2-HG → TET inhibition → CTCF-site hypermethylation reducing CTCF binding. At GIT1 (and CNIH2) loci, MUT T cells showed disrupted TAD-like co-accessibility (Cicero) and altered predicted 3D contacts.
Caveat: edits only detected within ATAC fragments, so editing signal correlates with fragment count (Spearman rho 0.66) — requires normalization. Robust aggregate footprints need ≥250 cells; single-cell signal is sparse (low edits/cell), relying on pseudobulk/metacell aggregation.

Connections

A functional-single-cell-genomics resource; methodological sibling/contrast to CUT&Tag, scCUT&Tag, ChIP-seq, DamID, and uliCUT&RUN for DNA-protein mapping.
Extends single-cell readouts of cis-regulatory-elements to weak/transient factors and to inaccessible chromatin (via WGS), beyond ATAC-peak motif inference.
Links to 3d-genome through C.Origami-based Hi-C prediction from single-cell CTCF + accessibility.
Builds on the same group’s single-cell genotyping lineage (GoT, GoT-ChA).

Open questions

Can algorithms be tailored to single-cell (rather than bulk-trained) 3D-genome prediction? Authors flag current C.Origami use as preliminary.
How to raise per-cell edit density / sensitivity (esp. for GATA1, where scD&D-seq performance was moderate) to reduce reliance on pseudobulk aggregation?
Generalization to chromatin remodelers and low-affinity antibodies at true single-cell resolution; multiplexing several TFs per cell.
Mechanistic confirmation in vivo that IDH2-MUT CTCF loss drives the observed 3D reorganization at GIT1/CNIH2 beyond model-based and co-accessibility inference.

wiki/sources/chiang-2022-mechanism-and-m.title: chiang-2022-mechanism-and-modeling-of-human
wiki/sources/chiang-2022-mechanism-and-m.content:
Mechanism and modeling of human disease-associated near-exon intronic variants that perturb RNA splicing
One-line: A massively parallel splicing assay (MaPSy) over 5,307 branch-site and 5,884 5′-splice-region disease variants that dissects the distinct molecular logic of 3′ vs 5′ near-exon intronic mutations and builds a LASSO model that out-predicts SpliceAI/MMSplice for intronic splicing defects.
Key takeaways

Systematically tests near-exon intronic variants (the redundant, ill-defined branch-site/splice-sites region) with a massively-parallel-reporter-assay (MaPSy) using WT/mutant three-exon minigenes in HEK293T resolved by amplicon sequencing (validation Pearson r=0.89 vs semi-quantitative RT-PCR).
The splice-altering mechanism differs by end: 5′ intronic variants act mainly by splice-site destruction, whereas 3′ intronic variant effects are complex — driven by branch-site dysfunction plus competition among 3′ AG dinucleotides.
Strong splice sites and exonic features buffer splicing against intronic sequence variation — sequence context, not the variant alone, determines impact.
Significant splicing variants are enriched for pathogenic/likely-pathogenic annotations and depleted for benign, are more evolutionarily conserved (PhyloP), and cluster distinctively (5′ variants at the 5′ss; 3′ variants spread ~50 nt into the intron toward the 3′ss).
A key class of 3′ variants creates novel “add-AG” splice sites; the novel 3′ss tends to have a weak canonical 3′ss, a strong predicted upstream branch site, higher exon–intron GC contrast (mean ΔGC 3.5% for novel exons vs 7.3% canonical), and proximity to the 3′ss — reflecting combined BS dysfunction and AG competition.
LASSO regression on molecular features (GC content, MaxEntScan splice scores, BS/polypyrimidine strength, conservation, SNP type, folding energy, rna-binding-proteins sites) independently models 3′ (novel-AG and non-AG) and 5′ variants; the 3′ add-AG model’s top factor is novel 3′ss score, non-AG model’s top factor is WT splicing efficiency.
The model generalizes (AUC 0.962 on held-out data), predicts 5′ variants at >91% sensitivity, out-competes spliceai and MMSplice on intronic-variant sensitivity without sacrificing specificity, and is served as the SpliceAPP web tool (bc.imb.sinica.edu.tw/SpliceAPP).

Notable claims / data

10–30% of pathogenic mutations in rare-disease patients alter splicing; 54% of splicing-disrupting variants in prior MaPSy work were intronic.
Variants collected from HGMD, ClinVar, dbSNP, COSMIC overlapping BS position 0 (branchpoint) and −2/−3 positions; 3′ significant variants show A-to-G and G-to-A transitions best correlate with splicing defects; shorter introns are enriched for regulatory elements near the 5′ss.
WT splicing-efficiency model identifies 3′ss score as the top factor and intron/exon folding energy as a major contributor (Pearson r=0.48); add-AG model r=0.84, non-AG model r=0.78.
Branchpoint distribution genome-wide: canonical A/C branchpoints peak at −25 nt, T at −28; functional branchpoints concentrate −18 to −40 nt upstream of the 3′ss, most conserved at −20 to −30.
Taiwan Biobank (TWB) analysis: splicing-disrupting intronic variants associate with skewed biochemical indices; the CAMTA2 intron-14 branchpoint variant rs72835097 (A→G at −41) associates with hypertension in males and altered platelet counts, and mechanistically causes CAMTA2 exon-15 skipping → frameshift → loss of transactivation.

Connections

Cited directly by shen-2026-advances-and-challenges-of-splicing (ref 41) for the 10–30% pathogenic-splicing statistic; provides the mechanistic/MaPSy grounding for that review’s variant-effect-prediction discussion.
Extends splice-sites, branch-site, and splicing-code concept pages with quantitative branchpoint/AG-competition rules; complements spliceai and MMSplice by targeting the intronic variants those tools handle poorly.
The massively-parallel-reporter-assay design and add-AG mechanism connect to bonnal-2025-core-splicing-architecture-and-early and begg-2020-concentration-dependent-splicing-is-enabled MaPSy work in the wiki.
Candidate entity pages: SpliceAPP (web predictor), SVM-BPfinder (branchpoint scorer used here), Taiwan Biobank.

Open questions

How well do these intronic-variant rules transfer across genomic contexts beyond the minigene/HEK293T system to endogenous tissue splicing?
Can the add-AG / BS-competition features be folded into deep-learning splice predictors to fix their deep-intronic blind spot flagged by shen-2026-advances-and-challenges-of-splicing?
What fraction of population-level “benign” intronic variants (like some TWB BS variants) actually carry subclinical splicing/biochemical consequences?

wiki/sources/chiossone-2018-natural-kill.title: chiossone-2018-natural-killer-cells-other
wiki/sources/chiossone-2018-natural-kill.content:
Natural killer cells and other innate lymphoid cells in cancer
One-line: A Nature Reviews Immunology overview of how natural-killer-cells and the other innate lymphoid cell (ILC) subsets shape antitumour immunity, and the immunotherapies being built to harness them.
Key takeaways

Innate lymphoid cells derive from common lymphoid progenitors but lack rearranged antigen receptors; five groups are defined by cytokine output and transcription factors: NK cells, ILC1s, ILC2s, ILC3s and lymphoid tissue-inducer (LTi) cells. ILC1/2/3 mirror TH1/TH2/TH17 helper subsets.
NK cells contribute to immune-recognition of tumours via cytotoxicity plus cytokine/chemokine release (CCL5, XCL1, XCL2 recruit dendritic cells into solid tumours, correlating with improved survival), and via ‘DC editing’ that supports T cell priming.
Immuno-oncology has focused mostly on T cells, but NK cells are an underexploited compartment; many T-cell-directed strategies (checkpoint blockade, cytokines) also activate NK cells.
The tumour microenvironment drives NK cell dysfunction: activating receptors (NKp30, NKp44, NKp46, NKG2D, DNAM1) are downregulated and inhibitory receptors (NKG2A, KIRs, PD1, TIGIT) upregulated, skewing NK cells to a hyporesponsive phenotype.
Roles of non-NK ILCs are context-dependent and often pro-tumorigenic (e.g. ILC3-derived IL-22 driving epithelial proliferation and metastasis; TGFβ-driven conversion of NK cells into non-cytotoxic ILC1s as an immunoescape route).

Notable claims / data

Mice lacking perforin, NKp46 (NCR1), or TRAIL show faster tumour growth; NK depletion (anti-asialo-GM1 or anti-NK1.1) accelerates MCA-induced fibrosarcomas.
Human selective NK cell deficiency has not shown markedly higher cancer incidence, but cohorts are too small and follow-up too short to conclude; low peripheral NK cytotoxicity correlates with metastasis in head/neck, pharyngeal, colorectal and other tumours.
Prognostic contrast: NK infiltration favours outcome in clear cell renal cell cancer, but has no impact in NSCLC because intratumoural NK activating receptors are strongly downregulated (phenotype, not density, matters).
In allogeneic HSCT, donor inhibitory-KIR / recipient MHC class I mismatch drives a T-cell-independent graft-versus-leukaemia effect and lower AML relapse (Velardi group, 1999); ‘missing ligand’ and ‘donor haplotype’ models refine this but give conflicting clinical results.
Therapeutic classes reviewed: inhibitory-receptor-blocking mAbs (lirilumab vs KIRs — EFFIKIR monotherapy did not improve leukaemia-free survival in elderly AML; monalizumab vs NKG2A, first checkpoint inhibitor targeting both T and NK cells); activating/tumour-antigen mAbs (elotuzumab vs SLAMF7, FDA-approved 2015 for multiple myeloma, acts via ADCC + NK activation); immunomodulatory drugs (lenalidomide, pomalidomide); cytokines (IL-2, IL-2 variants/NKTR-214, IL-15 and superagonist ALT803); TGFβ inhibitors (vactosertib, galunisertib); BiKEs/TriKEs engaging CD16; and adoptive transfer of autologous, allogeneic, cord-blood-derived or CAR-engineered NK cells.
IL-15 is favoured over IL-2 (expands NK cells without Treg expansion), but continuous IL-15 exhausts NK cells — intermittent dosing is proposed.

Connections

Extends the wiki’s natural-killer-cells concept from basic biology into a cancer-immunotherapy setting and grounds tumour-context immune-recognition.
Complements immuno-oncology framing: distinguishes NK/innate cytotoxicity from the more heavily studied T cell checkpoint-blockade paradigm.

Open questions

Does NK-to-ILC1 conversion (TGFβ-driven) occur in humans, and would anti-TGFβ therapy act partly by blocking it?
Are non-NK ILC subsets in human tumours net protective or pro-tumorigenic — most evidence comes from mouse tumour-injection models that may not reflect physiological microenvironments.
What are the optimal dosing schedules, indications and combination partners (e.g. KIR blockade, IL-15) to avoid NK desensitization or exhaustion?
What are the still-unidentified ligands for the natural cytotoxicity receptors (NCRs) on tumour cells?

wiki/sources/choquet-2023-pre-mrna-splic.title: choquet-2023-pre-mrna-splicing-order-is
wiki/sources/choquet-2023-pre-mrna-splic.content:
Pre-mRNA splicing order is predetermined and maintains splicing fidelity across multi-intronic transcripts
One-line: Direct-RNA nanopore sequencing of chromatin-associated RNA shows that post-transcriptional-splicing of multi-intron human transcripts follows a largely predetermined, cell-type-conserved intron-removal order controlled by the U2 spliceosome component, and that this order safeguards splicing fidelity across long pre-mRNAs.
Key takeaways

Uses direct RNA nanopore sequencing (dRNA-seq) of poly(A)-selected chromatin-associated RNA (2.65 M aligned reads, K562/HeLa/iPSC-derived motor neurons) to read splicing status of every intron on individual long molecules — overcoming the pairwise limitation of prior methods.
post-transcriptional-splicing is widespread: >35% of multi-intron chromatin reads are partially spliced (<5% in cytoplasm/whole-cell); 38% of introns are excised post-transcriptionally in ≥10% of reads; >75% of transcripts have ≥1 post-transcriptionally excised intron.
Multi-intron splicing order is largely predetermined — for intron groups of 3–4, one or a few of the possible paths (up to 24 for four introns) dominate; low evenness (Shannon-index-based; median 0.35 for three introns, 0.25 for four) confirms a preferred order vs the near-1 evenness of simulated random orders.
Splicing order is conserved across cell types and stages of motor-neuron differentiation (82% of shared intron groups keep the same top order between K562, HeLa, and sMN timepoints).
Introns flanking alternatively spliced exons (alternative-splicing) tend to be removed last, after their neighbors; strong splice sites are removed early, weak splice sites late; longer introns removed later.
Perturbing the spliceosome U2 snRNA (ASO knockdown / targeted intron perturbation) alters preferred splicing order for many genes and is associated with intron-retention of other introns in the same transcript — linking order to fidelity. U2-sensitive introns are normally removed first/second.
In IFRD2, forcing early removal of specific introns (or CRISPR deletions) induces delayed excision and retention of three proximal introns through two distinct cis-mechanisms — a downstream splice site and RNA secondary-structure changes (DMS-MaPseq shows reduced U2 branch-point accessibility) — demonstrating strong interdependency across neighboring introns.

Notable claims / data

43% of intermediate-isoform chromatin reads with ≥2 remaining introns have middle (non-terminal) introns retained; poly(A) tail length grows as splicing progresses, confirming intermediate isoforms are actively processed (not decay products; unaffected by EXOSC10 depletion).
Splicing order strongly correlates with independent nano-COP co-transcriptional data and short-read nascent-RNA kinetics.
1,616 cassette exons change inclusion across sMN differentiation; mutually exclusive exons (e.g. TPM2 exons 6A/6B) show splicing-order reversal between isoforms/timepoints, with MXE-flanking introns removed last.
U2 snRNA KD raises the splicing index of U2-sensitive introns and does not change RNA Pol II pausing, isolating a direct spliceosomal role in order (vs transcription dynamics).
Genes with retained introns upon U2 KD frequently also show skipped exons on the same molecule (RI|SE and SE|RI co-occurrence), and SE-flanking introns normally excised late are removed earlier when the SE-flanking “early” introns are limiting.

Connections

Central evidence for post-transcriptional-splicing and cotranscriptional-splicing concept pages; links intron-removal order to intron-retention and alternative-splicing outcomes.
The AS-introns-removed-last rule and U2-dependency connect to splicing-factors / spliceosome pages and to microexon/cassette-exon regulation in bonnal-2025-core-splicing-architecture-and-early and furlanis-2019-landscape-of-ribosome-engaged-transcript-isoforms.
The isoform-level, long-molecule readout speaks directly to the isoform-reconstruction gap flagged by shen-2026-advances-and-challenges-of-splicing — sequence predictors that treat splice sites independently miss this cross-intron coordination.
Candidate entity pages: direct-RNA nanopore dRNA-seq splicing-order method, DMS-MaPseq, nano-COP.

Open questions

What defines the “predetermined” order mechanistically — spliceosome assembly kinetics, RNA structure, cis-element spacing, or all three?
How is splicing order encoded in the genome, and can it be predicted from sequence to improve isoform-level models?
Does disrupted splicing order contribute to disease-associated intron-retention and aberrant alternative-splicing beyond U2 perturbation?

wiki/sources/choquet-2025-the-regulation.title: choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of
wiki/sources/choquet-2025-the-regulation.content:
The regulation and function of post-transcriptional RNA splicing
One-line: A review arguing that up to ~40% of mammalian introns are spliced post-transcriptionally — largely while transcripts remain chromatin-associated after polyadenylation — and that the timing of splicing is itself a major, regulated layer of gene expression control.
Key takeaways

splicing occurs both co-transcriptionally (during transcription) and post-transcriptionally (after transcription completes); recent long-read and subcellular methods reveal the timing axis with high spatial/temporal resolution. The spliceosome assembly steps are presumed identical for both modes, but it is unknown whether the post-transcriptional delay occurs before or during early assembly, or what triggers eventual splicing.
Roughly 27–40% of mammalian introns are present at high levels in polyadenylated, chromatin-associated RNA, and >70% of expressed genes carry at least one post-transcriptionally retained intron. This makes chromatin-associated post-transcriptional splicing a common feature of gene expression.
Polyadenylated transcripts can stay on chromatin long after synthesis ends; splicing completion appears to be a rate-limiting step for transcript release from chromatin, controlling when mRNA becomes available for nuclear export and translation.
Most retained introns are eventually excised productively rather than degraded; nuclear decay (via the nuclear exosome / PABPN1 hyperadenylation pathway) is the minority fate, affecting only ~4% of protein-coding genes (PUND transcripts).
Detained introns are a specialized class held in the nucleus until specific signals trigger removal — a regulatory reservoir used in neurons, spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation, muscle stem cells and heat-shock responses.
Splicing timing is shaped by cis features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary structure) and trans factors/RBPs (PTBP1, SR proteins, U2 snRNA, SNIP1), with phosphorylation (CLK/SRPK), methylation (PRMT5, PRMT1/4) and m6A as key modulators.
Introns flanking alternative exons are preferentially spliced post-transcriptionally, plausibly buying time for correct splice-site choice; Pol II elongation rate and co-transcriptional RNA folding feed into these outcomes.

Notable claims / data

Up to 40% of mammalian introns are retained after transcription termination; transcriptome-wide estimates put post-transcriptional splicing at 30–45% of introns (Table 1). Most mammalian introns (~70%) are excised on chromatin.
Subcellular TimeLapse-seq (K562): mRNAs have a median chromatin half-life of ~50 min, some genes >170 min — often exceeding transcription time itself; transcripts spend most of their nuclear life on chromatin, not free in nucleoplasm.
Microscopy: an intron in a β-globin reporter was excised ~10-fold faster and more frequently (87%) after release from the transcription site; for 6 of 10 endogenous human introns, post-transcriptional excision was more probable than co-transcriptional.
Detained introns tend to be shorter, GC-rich, have weaker 5′/3′ splice sites, and are more conserved; ~31% of nuclear-speckle-associated retained introns overlap annotated detained introns vs 12% for lamina-associated ones.
Species differences: Drosophila shows higher splicing completion on chromatin than mouse liver; in Arabidopsis >40% of chromatin RNA is polyadenylated, ~1/3 partially spliced — similar to mammals.
67% of introns flanking alternative exons are excised post-transcriptionally vs 30% of constitutive introns in mESCs.
~20% of human genes have ≥1 intronic poly(A) (PAS) event. Terminal-intron excision is stimulated by 3′-end cleavage/poly(A) signal and may require an optimal poly(A) tail length (PABPN1).
Disease: PRMT5 is aberrantly activated in glioblastoma, excising detained introns in cell-cycle genes to drive proliferation — a hijacked developmental program; PRMT5 inhibition suppresses tumour growth (therapeutic vulnerability). FUS autoregulation via intron retention is disrupted by ALS-causing nuclear-localization-signal mutations.

Connections

Definitive reference for post-transcriptional-splicing and its contrast with cotranscriptional-splicing; central to splicing-and-chromatin (chromatin retention of polyadenylated pre-mRNA, nuclear speckles vs lamina).
Ties alternative-splicing timing to Pol II elongation rate and co-transcriptional RNA structure.
Mechanistic links to splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins, CLK/SRPK, PRMTs, SNIP1, U2 snRNA), and to disease/therapeutics via PRMT5 in glioblastoma and FUS in ALS.
splicing-and-evolution: detained introns and intronic PAS show elevated cross-species conservation, implying selected regulatory roles.

Open questions

What signals trigger the eventual post-transcriptional splicing reaction, and is the delay pre- or post-engagement with the spliceosome?
What regulatory features sort partially spliced chromatin-associated transcripts toward productive splicing vs nuclear degradation?
How do nuclear speckles mechanistically couple transcript localization to splicing timing?
To what degree is m6A a universal splicing modulator vs a context-specific one (most marks are not near splice sites and are added post-transcriptionally)?
Do exons regulated by transcription rate overlap with those regulated by delayed removal of flanking introns?
Can post-transcriptional splicing be therapeutically targeted in diseases where it is dysregulated, e.g. cancers?

wiki/sources/cirnigliaro-2023-contributi.title: cirnigliaro-2023-contributions-of-rare-inherited
wiki/sources/cirnigliaro-2023-contributi.content:
The contributions of rare inherited and polygenic risk to ASD in multiplex families
One-line: Whole-genome sequencing of the largest multiplex-family ASD cohort shows rare inherited variants and overtransmitted polygenic risk combine additively, with language delay flagged as a core biological feature.
Key takeaways

Multiplex families (two or more autistic children) have a genetic architecture distinct from simplex families: rare de novo variation is depleted while rare inherited protein-truncating variants (PTVs) carry real signal — a design lever for autism gene discovery.
Seven previously unrecognized ASD risk genes were identified (PLEKHA8, PRR25, FBXL13, VPS54, SLFN5, SNCAIP, TGM1), five supported by a majority of inherited PTVs — the kind of signal invisible to de novo-focused studies.
ASD polygenic score is overtransmitted from nonautistic parents specifically to autistic children who also carry rare inherited variants, evidence for additive common+rare risk that helps explain reduced penetrance of rare variants in parents (psychiatric-genetics, heritability).
Polygenic overtransmission tracks with language delay and severe social impairment, arguing language is a core biological feature of ASD even though it is not a current clinical criterion.
Risk genes converge on fetal excitatory neurons and interneurons and on synaptic/chromatin biology, consistent with prior neurodevelopment convergence findings.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 1,004 AGRE multiplex families, 4,551 individuals, WGS at mean 37.5x; 1,836 autistic + 418 nonautistic fully-phaseable children after QC (see agre-cohort).
TADA mega-analysis (multiplex cohort combined with SSC/ASC/AGP) identified 74 ASD risk genes at FDR < 0.1; a combined total of 152 known ASD risk genes (KARGs) collated across studies.
54% of the 74 TADA genes had an inherited component; ~27% had >50% inherited PTV support. Validated recent de novo genes KMT2A and MED13L.
Excess of rare inherited PTVs in KARGs in autistic vs nonautistic children (logistic regression P = 0.020, OR = 1.73); rare de novo PTVs in KARGs NOT increased (P = 0.37), attributed to depletion + low power.
No excess of rare inherited, private inherited, or de novo variants in noncoding/promoter/TFBS regions; power calc estimates ~8,000-9,000 phaseable children needed for noncoding association.
pTDT: overall ASD PGS overtransmitted to autistic children (P = 0.005, deviation 0.12); strongest in autistic inherited-PTV carriers (P = 0.05, deviation 0.34), not in de novo carriers (P = 1, small n).
Largest rare-inherited-KARG phenotype effect is on language: median 30-month delay in “first phrase” vs nonautistic siblings; PGS overtransmission seen in language-delayed (first word P = 0.01) and severe-social-impairment (P = 0.05) autistic children; relationship specific to ASD PGS (not educational-attainment, schizophrenia, or bipolar PGS).
Oligogenic/two-hit transmission not a major mode: only 6 autistic children carried two rare inherited KARG PTVs, all sibling pairs from 3 families.

Connections

Extends the polygenic/rare-variant ASD framing in zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of and the developmental-cortex autism work in schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism and velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal.
Cell-type convergence and fetal-cortex expression analyses relate to neurodevelopment and interneuron-migration.
Family/transmission modeling connects to twin-and-family-study-design and quantitative-genetics; polygenic-score transmission relates to genome-wide-association-study and missing-heritability.

Open questions

Do the seven candidate genes replicate in independent multiplex cohorts, given they are flagged as needing confirmation?
What sample sizes make noncoding rare inherited variation tractable, and does its very small effect size reflect biology or power?
Mechanistically, how does overtransmitted common risk combine with rare inherited PTVs in the same child to cross the diagnostic threshold?

wiki/sources/clavell-revelles-2025-long-.title: clavell-revelles-2025-long-read-transcriptomics-of-a-diverse
wiki/sources/clavell-revelles-2025-long-.content:
Long-read transcriptomics of a diverse human cohort reveals ancestry bias in gene annotation
One-line: Long-read RNA-seq of 43 lymphoblastoid cell lines from eight genetically-diverse human populations yields a cross-ancestry transcript annotation (poder-annotation) and shows that reference gene annotations (GENCODE/RefSeq) systematically underrepresent transcripts from non-European individuals — a direct, splicing-level instance of data-quality-and-bias.
Key takeaways

Generated >800 million full-length ONT long-read reads (rna-seq) across 43 LCL samples from 8 populations (Africa, Asia, America, Europe), one of the most deeply-sequenced long-read RNA-seq datasets to date; built PODER (POpulation Diversity-Enhanced long-Read), a final cross-ancestry set of 155,875 transcripts.
Reference annotations are European-biased: non-European samples yield significantly more novel transcripts, and population-specific transcripts found only in non-European populations are enriched in novel ones — a bias not explained by higher African genetic diversity.
The annotation gap has functional consequences: a diverse annotation increases the discovery of allele-specific transcript usage (ASTU), disproportionately benefiting non-European populations (mean 1.24x more testable genes vs 1.15x in Europeans).
Reference-assembly choice also limits splice-junction discovery: personalized and full personal genome assemblies recover transcripts and splice junctions invisible to GRCh38, with the largest gains in African-descent samples.

Notable claims / data

380,519 raw transcript models discarded down to 155,875 after stringent filtering; 41,297 PODER transcripts are novel vs GENCODE v47 (731 mapping to previously-intergenic regions); 31,097 novel transcripts (75.3%) not described by GTEx, ENCODE4, or CHESS3 catalogs.
Transcript models called by four tools merged on intron-chain identity: FLAIR, IsoQuant, ESPRESSO (annotation-guided), and LyRic (annotation-agnostic); monoexonic transcripts removed. SQANTI structural categories used (FSM/ISM/NIC/NNC). SIRV spike-in control: 78.3% FSM, only 5.5% novel splicing (sequencing-error attributable).
2,419 population-specific transcripts (median 302/population, 1.55% of PODER); enriched in lncRNAs, lowly-expressed, shorter. Novel exons carry SNPs with greater allelic divergence between CEU and non-European populations than known exons. 8,740 highly population-specific expressed transcripts (Tau τ ≥ 0.8; 4,154 novel).
476 novel intergenic genes clustered; 41% of novel protein-coding transcripts predicted to encode a genuinely novel amino-acid sequence (12,148 unique novel ORFs from 13,401 transcripts).
ASTU genes from PODER enriched in 10 non-redundant GWAS traits (vs 6 with GENCODE), mostly immune/autoimmune (lupus, Takayasu’s arteritis, rheumatoid arthritis) plus cholesterol/lipoprotein traits with known cross-ancestry differences; LCLs are B-cell lineage.
Personalized-GRCh38 haplotypes recover a median 361 (0.83%) more transcripts and 607 (3.6%) more novel transcripts per sample vs GRCh38; 44.4% of novel personalized-only splice junctions directly attributable to alternative alleles at/near splice sites. Largest gains in African populations (ANOVA p = 0.0004).
Personal genomes contain a median ~156 Mb (~4.7% of GRCh38) of non-reference regions, but these are gene-poor (<2 transcripts/Mb vs ~6/Mb in shared regions), highly repetitive, with little transcriptional activity — most transcriptome diversity is in shared genomic regions. Caveat: single cell type (LCL), small cohort, discrete European/non-European categorization acknowledged as oversimplified.

Connections

A splicing-domain, empirical counterpart to data-quality-and-bias: where that concept (via hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation) documents European dataset dominance harming AI models, this paper shows the bias is baked into the gene annotation itself at the level of alternative-splicing and splice junctions.
Uses LCL samples and a population-genetics framing overlapping splicing-qtl work; ASTU is an allele-resolved transcript-usage analysis related to demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing and regional sQTL variation in zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls.
Methodologically extends large-scale long-read annotation efforts and complements short-read isoform/rna-seq catalogs; PODER positioned as a step toward a human “pantranscriptome.”

Open questions

Are other functional-element catalogs (enhancers, promoters, TF binding, chromatin marks) equally ancestry-biased?
How much further would transcript diversity expand with long-read discovery directly on pangenome graphs (vs haploid linear personal genomes)?
Does the bias replicate across tissues, cell types, and developmental stages beyond LCLs?

wiki/sources/conesa-2016-survey-of-best-.title: conesa-2016-survey-of-best-practices
wiki/sources/conesa-2016-survey-of-best-.content:
A survey of best practices for RNA-seq data analysis
One-line: A broad community review that walks through every major step of an RNA-seq analysis pipeline — experimental design, QC, alignment, quantification, differential expression, alternative splicing, fusion detection, small RNAs, functional profiling, eQTL/sQTL mapping, and data integration — cataloguing the dominant tools and their trade-offs rather than prescribing one pipeline.
Key takeaways

There is no single optimal RNA-seq pipeline; the right choices for library prep, sequencing depth, replicates, mapping, quantification, and DE testing depend on the organism, annotation quality, and biological question.
Experimental-design fundamentals: poly(A) selection vs rRNA depletion (rRNA is >90% of total RNA, mRNA only 1–2%); strand-specific protocols (e.g. dUTP); paired-end and longer reads are preferable for de novo discovery and isoform/alternative-splicing analysis; SE short reads suffice for gene-level expression in well-annotated genomes.
Replicates: minimum three biological replicates for any population inference; without replication, p-values are invalid and analyses are only exploratory. Power depends on effect size, depth, and replicate count.
Three mapping/quantification strategies: (a) map to annotated genome with a gapped/spliced mapper for novel-transcript discovery, (b) map to reference transcriptome with an ungapped mapper for quantification only, (c) de novo assembly when no genome exists.
The alternative-splicing section is the most relevant to this wiki: two method families — isoform-resolution methods (estimate isoform abundance then compare) vs exon/junction-based methods (compare read distributions over exons and junctions directly). rmats and dexseq are cited as exon/junction-based; CuffDiff2 as isoform-based.

Notable claims / data

Expected mapping rates: 70–90% of typical RNA-seq reads map to the human genome (mapper-dependent); transcriptome mapping gives lower total but more multi-mapping reads.
Sequencing depth guidance: ~5M mapped reads can quantify medium-to-high expressed genes; up to 100M for precise low-expression quantification; single cells often profiled at ~1M reads, reliably at 50,000, with cell-type discrimination shown at 20,000 reads.
Statistical power example (Table 1, GTEx whole-blood gene at 70 aligned reads, 30M reads/sample): at fold-change 1.5, power rises from 43% (3 replicates) to 64% (5) to 91% (10); at fold-change 2, 87%/98%/100%.
Technical-replicate reproducibility expected at Spearman R² > 0.9; recommend discarding outliers with >30% disagreement in QC metrics.
DE-method comparisons (Box 4): limma performs well and is fastest; DESeq/edgeR rank genes similarly but are conservative/liberal respectively on FDR; SAMseq controls FDR well but needs ~10 replicates for sensitivity; NOISeq avoids false positives at some sensitivity cost; Cuffdiff/Cuffdiff2 performed “surprisingly poorly,” reflecting the difficulty of transcript-level DE.
Proteomics–RNA-seq correlation is generally low (~0.40), making integration controversial; single-cell transcriptomes typically show only ~3000–8000 expressed genes with 10–50% capture rates.

Connections

Foundational/overview companion to the more specialized splicing-methods sources in this wiki; provides the pipeline context that tool-specific papers assume.
Directly situates rmats (exon-inclusion via junction reads) and dexseq (differential exon usage) within the broader taxonomy of alternative-splicing detection methods — useful cross-reference for those entity pages.
Touches percent-spliced-in-style reasoning implicitly via exon-inclusion-level comparison in junction-based methods.
The eQTL/sQTL subsection (genotype × expression across >50 individuals; splicing QTL via tools like SplicePlot) connects to population-genetics splicing themes, though this review treats it only briefly.

Open questions

Many tools named here (Cufflinks, RSEM, kallisto, STAR, TopHat, SUPPA-era successors, etc.) postdate or were superseded after 2016 — which of the splicing-specific tools (rMATS, DEXseq, DiffSplice, FDM, rSeqDiff, rDiff, DSGSeq) remain current best practice today?
The review flags that isoform-level DE and transcript reconstruction from short reads remain unreliable; how much have long-read (PacBio/Nanopore) approaches, only nascent here, changed the splicing best-practice landscape?
Persistent caveat across the paper: tool results disagree and are parameter-sensitive, especially for low-expression genes — what reproducibility standards have since emerged?

wiki/sources/cortese-2022-association-be.title: cortese-2022-association-between-autism-spectrum
wiki/sources/cortese-2022-association-be.content:
Association between autism spectrum disorder and diabetes: systematic review and meta-analysis
One-line: The first systematic review/meta-analysis of the ASD–diabetes link, finding an apparent association that dissolves once confounders, publication bias, and heterogeneity are accounted for.
Key takeaways

First pre-registered (PROSPERO CRD42021261114, PRISMA 2020) synthesis of whether autism spectrum disorder (ASD) co-occurs with diabetes; 24 studies in qualitative review (3,427,773 individuals; 237,529 with ASD; 92,832 with diabetes), 20 meta-analyzed.
Pooling unadjusted odds ratios gave a significant positive association (OR 1.535, 95% CI 1.109–2.126), but with severe heterogeneity and evidence of publication bias.
After trim-and-fill correction for publication bias, and when restricting to adjusted ORs (excluding hazard ratios), the association became non-significant (adjusted OR 1.528, 95% CI 0.954–2.448) — the authors conclude the link is likely confounded.
Relevant to the broader psychiatric-genetics / neuropsychiatric-comorbidity literature framing ASD as a condition with systemic physical-health comorbidities.

Notable claims / data

Trim-and-fill adjustment of the unadjusted pooled OR drove the effect to null (OR 0.99, 95% CI 0.71–1.38).
Subgroup signal was present in children but not adults; and was significant for type 2 diabetes (OR 2.57, 95% CI 1.65–4.03) but not type 1 (OR 1.35, 95% CI 0.96–1.90).
No significant prospective association of diabetes predicting later ASD (only 2 studies; HR reported as 1.232). A single study of the reverse direction (ASD preceding diabetes) was significant (HR 3.25, 95% CI 2.23–4.75).
Heterogeneity was extreme throughout (I2 up to ~98%); meta-regression on study quality (Newcastle-Ottawa Scale, mean 5.89/10) and publication year found no significant moderators.
Largest included study (Schott et al.) uniquely controlled for intellectual disability when selecting controls, a design difference possibly driving effect-size discrepancies.
Proposed but unconfirmed mechanisms: shared inflammatory/autoimmune factors (type 1); obesity, dyslipidemia, and antipsychotic metabolic side-effects (type 2).

Connections

Extends the ASD comorbidity theme within autism research toward physical/metabolic health rather than the more commonly wiki-indexed molecular and psychiatric-genetics angles.
Methodologically a clinical epidemiology / evidence-synthesis contribution (registry- and survey-based cohorts), distinct from the wiki’s genomic ASD sources.

Open questions

What confounders (demographics, obesity, medication, intellectual disability) actually drive the apparent association, and can a directed-acyclic-graph-guided large registry study resolve it?
Are the child-vs-adult and type-1-vs-type-2 differences real biology or artifacts of sparse, heterogeneous data?
Does the significant single-study ASD-preceding-diabetes direction warrant dedicated prospective study?

wiki/sources/cotney-2015-autism-associat.title: cotney-2015-autism-associated-chromatin-modifier-chd8-regulates
wiki/sources/cotney-2015-autism-associat.content:
The autism-associated chromatin modifier CHD8 regulates other autism risk genes during human neurodevelopment
One-line: ChIP-seq mapping of the strongest autism-risk gene chd8 in human midfetal brain, human neural stem cells, and mouse cortex shows CHD8 directly targets a conserved set of other ASD risk genes during brain development.
Key takeaways

chd8 is the ASD risk gene with the most recurrent de novo loss-of-function mutations; it encodes an ATP-dependent chromatin remodeller binding H3K4me3-marked active promoters. This study asks whether it regulates other autism risk genes in human neurodevelopment.
CHD8 targets were mapped by ChIP-seq in H9-derived human neural stem cells (hNSCs, 9,414 reproducible sites), human midfetal brain at 16-19 post-conception weeks (4,428 sites), and embryonic day 17.5 mouse cortex, defining shared conserved targets.
CHD8 binds primarily promoters of actively transcribed genes: 99% of hNSC-bound promoters carried active marks H3K4me3 or H3K27ac, with binding level correlating with expression; little enrichment for repressive H3K9me3/H3K27me3.
CHD8 targets are strongly enriched for ASD risk genes (from Willsey et al. and Liu et al. gene sets), and this convergence is a conserved feature of mammalian neurodevelopment (gene-regulation).
CHD8 knockdown in hNSCs disproportionately dysregulates the conserved CHD8 targets and the ASD risk genes among them, supporting direct regulation.

Notable claims / data

Of 127 analysed Liu et al. ASD risk genes, 47 are targeted by CHD8 in both human tissues (permutation P < 0.0001); 46 of 116 Willsey et al. genes likewise (P < 0.0001). hNSC-specific targets were NOT enriched (P = 0.9911), highlighting the value of in vivo binding data.
1,910 CHD8-binding sites are shared across human brain, mouse cortex and hNSCs; conserved targets are enriched in ASD risk genes (39 Liu, 37 Willsey; P < 0.0001) and for transcription-regulation / chromatin-modification GO functions.
Two independent shRNA knockdowns of CHD8 in hNSCs showed the conserved target set held the greatest fraction of differentially expressed genes; affected KEGG pathways included cell cycle, p53 signalling and Hippo signalling; ASD risk genes tended to be downregulated.
Adding CHD8-binding (parameter d) to the Liu et al. statistical ASD risk model (DAWN algorithm) improved risk-gene discrimination (d = 1.63, P < 0.001) and newly predicted three risk genes: ASH1L, SPAST and THSD7A.
Supporting predictive value: of 19 later-reported high-confidence ASD risk genes, 15 (79%) are conserved CHD8 targets.

Connections

Central to autism and psychiatric-genetics: positions chd8 as an organizer of an ASD-associated gene co-expression network in human midfetal prefrontal / motor-somatosensory cortex.
