Ties chromatin remodelling and promoter chromatin-accessibility (H3K27ac/H3K4me3 marking) to gene-regulation of ASD risk genes.
Complements gene-network convergence approaches to ASD risk-gene discovery (Willsey/Liu de novo mutation gene sets); relates to the family of statistical ASD risk models like the TADA framework (tada-method).

Open questions

What are the precise molecular mechanisms of CHD8-dependent activation vs repression at target promoters (candidate cofactors CTCF, E2F, YY1, Sp/KLF motifs were enriched)?
How does CHD8 haploinsufficiency alter proliferation/differentiation of neuronal progenitors and downstream cortical cell-type proportions?
Which additional CHD8 targets are undiscovered ASD risk genes, and how do targets of other ASD-associated chromatin modifiers intersect with CHD8’s?

wiki/sources/courchesne-2007-mapping-ear.title: courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development
wiki/sources/courchesne-2007-mapping-ear.content:
Mapping Early Brain Development in Autism
One-line: A landmark review arguing that autism’s neuropathological signature is early brain overgrowth during the first 1–2 years of life (not the loss/degeneration seen decades later), and proposing that an excess of cortical neurons in frontal and temporal regions derails the formation of large-scale, long-distance social-communication networks.
Key takeaways

Most autism neurobiology had studied brains 10–20+ years after clinical onset; this review reorients attention to the critical 1–4 year window when symptoms emerge and neural wiring forms, linking developmental brain biology to a neuropsychiatric disorder (brain-development-neurogenomics).
Central hypothesis: autism involves two (sometimes three) phases of growth pathology — (1) early brain overgrowth at the beginning of life, (2) slowing/arrest of growth in early childhood, and (3) in some individuals/regions, later degeneration by preadolescence.
The mature autistic brain shows neuron loss, glial activation/inflammation, reduced minicolumn size, and near-normal overall size — a picture that contradicts the overgrowth seen in toddlers, implying a dynamic age-dependent trajectory rather than a fixed deficit.
Overgrowth is regionally selective: frontal and temporal lobes and the amygdala overgrow most, occipital cortex least — a gradient that parallels the higher-order social, emotional, language, and communication functions most impaired in autism.
Proposed mechanism: an excess of excitatory pyramidal neurons could produce excess axons/dendrites/synapses/myelin (explaining enlarged gray and white matter), tip the excitation/inhibition balance, and let local/short-distance connectivity out-compete long-distance afferents — weakening large-scale networks (mirror neuron system, resting network) that underlie social cognition.
The cellular/molecular cause of overgrowth was explicitly unknown at the time; the authors call for quantitative stereology in the youngest cases and note that causal (overgrowth-driving) genes may differ from reactive genes responding to the maldevelopment.

Notable claims / data

Epidemiology/context cited: ASD prevalence ~1 in 150 (Fombonne 2005); more heritable than breast/colon cancer, Alzheimer’s, or schizophrenia; first behavioral signs ~9 months–2 years; diagnosis typically 2–4 years; estimated US cost up to ~$35 billion/year.
Literature gap quantified: of ~100 MRI/DTI/postmortem studies in the prior decade, only 6 MRI, 1 DTI, and 0 postmortem studied autistic children at 2–4 years; of 39 postmortem studies, mean autistic case age ≈ 21 years.
Head circumference: at birth ~90–95% of later-autistic neonates have average-to-slightly-small HC and only ~5% are excessively large; by 1–2 years HC becomes abnormally enlarged (Courchesne et al. 2003), replicated by multiple independent groups.
MRI brain volume in young autistic children is enlarged ~5%–12% (vs ~1% average in adolescents/adults); at 2–4 years overall brain volume is ~10% larger than typically developing peers.
Postmortem brain weight: 15% greater in 3- to 5-year-old male autism cases than male controls (1451 g vs 1259 g); the 1451 g toddler average ≈ a normal adult male brain. By adulthood the difference shrinks to ~1%.
Single-case/early findings: Kennedy et al. (2007) found 58% more spindle (von Economo) neurons in a 3-year-old autistic frontal cortex vs a matched control; a 3-year-old showed normal (not reduced) minicolumn size; a 4-year-old showed no Purkinje loss — all opposite to adult-autism findings, though n=1.
Connectivity: one MRI study (Herbert et al. 2004) found excess “radiate”/outer white matter under frontal and temporal cortex in 7- to 11-year-olds; fMRI/EEG consistently report reduced long-distance functional connectivity in autism.
Genetics discussed (all noted as incomplete/uncertain): neuroligin/neurexin synapse genes, de novo CNVs in sporadic cases (Sebat et al. 2007), and MET gene association with reduced MET expression in temporal lobe — but reduced MET would predict reduced growth, which the authors flag as hard to reconcile with overgrowth.
Caveats throughout: many key claims rest on single cases or small samples; no MRI studies of autistic brains under age 2; HC is an imprecise brain-size proxy in adults; cellular/molecular basis of overgrowth entirely unstudied; this is a hypothesis-generating review, not primary data.

Connections

Anchors the human/disease end of brain-development-neurogenomics: connects normal early corticogenesis (rapid dendritic/synaptic growth in the first 2 years) to a neurodevelopmental disorder via a neuron-number hypothesis.
Complements developmental-genomics siblings such as walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and (prenatal cortical eQTL/sQTL map carrying ASD/SCZ risk), li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna — those map the molecular/genetic regulatory layer of developing cortex that could underlie the anatomical phenotypes Courchesne describes.
The excitation/inhibition imbalance and excess-pyramidal-neuron theme situates autism within broader cortical neurogenesis and neuronal cell-type biology rather than a single-synapse account.

Open questions

What is the cellular/molecular basis of early overgrowth — excess neurogenesis, excess/activated glia, excess synapses/minicolumns, premature myelination, or some combination?
Are the neural systems that overgrow the same ones that fail to support emerging social/language skills, and do they later degenerate?
Which genes drive early overgrowth versus react to it, and can early-phenotype (rather than adult-outcome) gene-association studies separate them?
Can sleep fMRI in infants/toddlers map dysfunctional large-scale networks early enough to guide intervention?
Does very early, activity-dependent behavioral intervention stabilize adaptive long-distance connections against local overconnectivity?

wiki/sources/cui-2024-scgpt-toward-build.title: cui-2024-scgpt-toward-building-a-foundation
wiki/sources/cui-2024-scgpt-toward-build.content:
scGPT: toward building a foundation model for single-cell multi-omics using generative AI
One-line: A generative pretrained transformer trained on 33M human cells that transfers to cell annotation, perturbation prediction, batch/multi-omic integration and gene-network inference.
Key takeaways

scgpt is a single-cell foundation model built as a generative pretrained transformer, following the “pretrain universally, fine-tune on demand” paradigm of foundation-models and generative-ai.
Draws the language-biology analogy: as texts are made of words, cells are defined by genes; scGPT jointly learns gene and cell embeddings via a specially designed attention mask over non-sequential omics data.
One pretrained model transfers across five downstream tasks: cell-type annotation, genetic perturbation prediction, multi-batch integration, multi-omic integration, and gene regulatory network inference.
Reports a scaling effect: larger pretraining data yields better embeddings and better downstream performance.

Notable claims / data

Pretrained on 33 million normal (non-disease) human cells from CELLxGENE, spanning 51 organs/tissues and 441 studies (brain 13.2M, blood 10.3M, lung 2.1M, heart 1.8M, etc.).
Cell-type annotation: >0.8 precision for most cell types on human pancreas; ~0.85 accuracy on a multiple-sclerosis dataset; generalized across unseen cancer types on tumor-infiltrating myeloid cells; outperformed TOSICA and scBERT on all metrics.
Perturbation prediction on three Perturb-seq datasets (Adamson 87, Replogle 1,823, Norman 131 two-gene + 105 one-gene perturbations); beat GEARS and linear baseline by 5–20% on Pearson-delta.
In silico reverse perturbation: identified 91.4% relevant perturbations (6.4/7) in top-1 predictions on a 20-gene Norman subset.
Integration: AvgBIO 0.821 on PBMC 10k (5–10% over scVI/Seurat/Harmony); 9% AvgBIO gain over Seurat v4 on BMMC multi-omic (90k cells, 12 donors, 48 cell types); competitive on 10x Multiome and ASAP mosaic data.
Uses human datasets only as method benchmarks (method, not a human-subjects study) — human_research false.

Connections

Extends the single-cell-foundation-models line and is a concrete generative-ai/foundation-models application to omics; related knowledge-graph-augmented variant txpert.
Perturbation benchmarks draw on adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening and the broader Perturb-seq framework (dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, perturb-seq).
Gene-network inference relates to gene-coexpression-network modeling; perturbation-response modeling relates to regvelo and cmonge.

Open questions

How well does pretraining on normal cells transfer to disease-specific regulatory states?
Can attention-derived gene networks be validated causally rather than by correlation?
How far does the data-scaling effect extend, and what are its limits for rare cell types?

wiki/sources/dao-2025-u-rich-elements-dr.title: dao-2025-u-rich-elements-drive-pervasive-cryptic
wiki/sources/dao-2025-u-rich-elements-dr.content:
U-rich elements drive pervasive cryptic splicing in 3′ UTR massively parallel reporter assays
One-line: Revisiting a 3′ UTR massively-parallel-reporter-assay (PTRE-seq), the authors discover that AU-rich elements (AREs) trigger pervasive cryptic splicing — mostly from an unannotated splice donor in the GFP coding sequence to acceptors in the 3′ UTR — which confounds MPRA measurements of post-transcriptional regulation.
Key takeaways

Repurposing the PTRE-seq MPRA (642 reporters combining let-7, Pumilio, Smaug, and ARE regulatory modules in a GFP 3′ UTR), the authors used “extended” primers spanning the full transcript and found widespread cryptic splicing invisible to standard short barcode-based readouts.
Splicing initiates from a weak, unannotated cryptic 5′ donor within the eGFP coding sequence (located at the stop codon) and proceeds to diverse weak 3′ acceptor sites in the reporter 3′ UTRs (splice-sites usage from non-canonical/weak sites).
U-rich sequences / AREs are the principal drivers: splicing depends almost entirely on the presence and position of ARE modules, which act as potent position-dependent regulators of both 5′ and 3′ splice-site choice and overall splicing efficiency (splicing-regulatory-elements).
AREs function context-dependently: an intronic ARE acts as a polypyrimidine tract activating a downstream 3′ acceptor (splicing enhancer), while an exonic/5′-adjacent ARE represses splice-site usage (splicing silencer) — a single ARE mechanism producing both enhancement and repression.
Cryptic splicing has diverse, confounding impacts on reporter expression — both increases (via “RE-excision” removing destabilizing 3′ UTR elements and enhanced production) and decreases — meaning it distorts conclusions drawn from 3′ UTR MPRAs about post-transcriptional regulation.
The cryptic splicing is constitutive and generalizable: reproducible across HeLa, HEK293, SH-SY5Y (neuroblastoma), and U87 (glioblastoma) cells (Pearson R ≥ 0.94), and validated by RT-PCR on individually transfected reporters.
Provides preventative strategies: synonymous recoding of the GFP cryptic donor (e.g. AAG→AAA at −2, CTG→CTA at −4) suppresses splicing without reducing GFP protein levels — a practical fix for MPRA designs.

Notable claims / data

Of 642 reporters, 119 (18%) were spliced at ≥20%, and 28 (5%) nearly completely (>90%); a cryptic 5′ site at the GFP stop codon featured in 165 splicing events (>1% usage).
RNA read categories (HeLa): 19% spliced, 30% full length, 14% palindromic indels (a separate cloning artifact, 78-bp palindromic insertion), 37% other anomalies; the “no spacer” library reproduced splicing (R=0.7) confirming it is intrinsic to the repetitive library, not the palindromic artifact.
Splice sites are weak: the GFP donor is 1st-percentile relative to annotated human donors yet supports up to 100% splicing; 3′ acceptors are 1st–28th percentile by MaxEntScan.
Position rules: a single 3′ UTR ARE module is sufficient to activate strong splicing; more intronic ARE modules → higher splicing at weaker acceptors; a 5′-adjacent (exonic) ARE blocks the downstream P-position donor; splicing occurs at the most proximal AG downstream of the 3′-most ARE; introns shorter than ~70 nt (minimum for spliceosome assembly) go unspliced.
Expression: observed splicing fraction strongly correlates with reporter expression (R=0.74); highly spliced reporters (>90%) show 2–3-fold enhanced production and >4× higher expression than baseline “blank” 3′ UTRs, explained by an “RE-excision” model where splicing removes destabilizing 3′ UTR elements.
MarathonRT/SuperScript II choice affected palindromic-artifact rates; splicing fractions robust across reverse-transcriptases and library preps.
Caveats: findings stem from one repetitive synthetic library (PTRE-seq); the exact quantitative cryptic-splicing rate depends on RT enzyme and library prep; broader implication (that other 3′ UTR MPRAs are affected) is argued but shown for this dataset in depth.

Connections

A cautionary, mechanism-rich study of cryptic splicing and weak splice-sites activation, directly relevant to splicing-methods and massively-parallel-reporter-assay design — cryptic splicing as a confounder of MPRA measurements.
Establishes U-rich/ARE sequences as potent, position-dependent splicing-regulatory-elements acting as both enhancers (polypyrimidine-tract-like) and silencers; connects to rna-binding-proteins (ARE-binding proteins) and percent-spliced-in-style quantification.
The theme of weak/cryptic splice-site activation by U-rich context complements genome-wide cryptic-splicing/mis-splicing findings (relates to garcia-ruiz-2025-splicing-accuracy-varies-across-human) and polypyrimidine-tract recognition mechanisms.
Practical relevance to reporter-based cis-regulatory-elements and 3′ UTR post-transcriptional-regulation studies generally.

Open questions

Which specific ARE-binding RBPs mediate the enhancer vs silencer behavior, and how do they interact with the spliceosome at weak sites?
How many published 3′ UTR MPRA datasets are confounded by undetected cryptic splicing, and how much do their conclusions change once corrected?
Can the position-dependent ARE splicing rules be turned into predictive design rules to engineer or avoid cryptic splicing in reporters and therapeutics?
Does ARE-driven cryptic splicing at weak sites occur in endogenous 3′ UTRs, and does it contribute to human disease mis-splicing?

wiki/sources/dawes-2022-splicevault-pred.title: dawes-2022-splicevault-predicts-the-precise
wiki/sources/dawes-2022-splicevault-pred.content:
SpliceVault Predicts the Precise Nature of Variant-Associated Mis-splicing
One-line: splicevault — an empirical predictor that ranks the four most common unannotated splicing events around a splice site across 335,663 reference RNA-seq samples (300K-RNA Top-4) to predict which mis-splicing outcome a splice-site variant will cause — beating SpliceAI for clinical splice-variant interpretation.
Key takeaways

A different question than “will it mis-splice?” Tools like SpliceAI flag whether a variant disrupts splicing; for essential splice-site (ES) variants that is nearly certain. The hard, clinically decisive question is which event results — exon skipping, cryptic-splice-site activation, intron retention, or a mix — which determines reading frame, PTC, and pathogenicity (ACMG–AMP PVS1).
The empirical insight: the mis-splicing events a variant activates are simply those that already occur naturally around that splice site in deep reference RNA-seq. Ranking the top-4 unannotated events seen across 335,663 GTEx/other samples (“300K-RNA Top-4”) predicts the variant’s outcome — no model training, just observed splice-junction frequencies.
Delivered as the SpliceVault web portal over the 300K-RNA database, for direct splice-site-variant interpretation and classification — a splicing-methods resource on the clinical / diagnostic axis (splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

92% sensitivity predicting the nature of variant-associated mis-splicing.
On 140 clinical cases with RNA testing: correctly identifies 96% of exon-skipping events and 86% of cryptic splice sites — higher sensitivity and positive predictive value than SpliceAI.
Re-analysis surfaced 300K-RNA Top-4 events that had been missed in clinical cases tested before this method existed — i.e. it would have improved past diagnoses.
Motivating gap in prior guidance: revised 2018 PVS1 considered cryptic donors only within 20 nt, but only ~20% of variant-activated cryptic donors fall within 20 nt — empirical reference data captures the rest.

Connections

The outcome-prediction complement to the wiki’s splicing-code/variant-effect tools: spliceai (and absplice2) score disruption; SpliceVault says what mis-splicing happens, from observed data rather than a learned model. Sits with the clinical variant-effect cluster (absplice2, outrider, crosssplice).
Reinforces the splicing-and-disease theme that reference RNA-seq at scale is a diagnostic substrate — conceptually adjacent to the rare-disease RNA-seq diagnostics line (brechtmann-2018-outrider-a-statistical-method, segers-2026-saser-juggling-offsets-unlocks) and to deep-intronic/cryptic-variant work (yano-2025-crosssplice-a-pipeline-for).
Grounded in splice-sites biology: essential GT-AG sites and the wider motif, and the three mis-splicing modes (skipping / cryptic / retention).

Open questions

How well does 300K-RNA Top-4 generalize to tissue-specific or lowly expressed genes where reference junction coverage is thin?
Can empirical event ranking be combined with model-based scores (spliceai) for a best-of-both clinical predictor?
How does performance hold for acceptor vs donor variants and for non-essential extended-splice-site variants?

wiki/sources/dawicki-mckenna-2023-mappin.title: dawicki-mckenna-2023-mapping-ptbp2-binding-in-human
wiki/sources/dawicki-mckenna-2023-mappin.content:
Mapping PTBP2 binding in human brain identifies SYNGAP1 as a target for therapeutic splice switching
One-line: Combines PTBP2 CLIP-seq in human cortex and iPSC-derived neurons with PTBP2-depletion RNA-seq to map the neuronal PTBP2 splicing footprint, then shows PTBP2 drives a nonsense-mediated-decay-linked splicing event that limits SYNGAP1 expression — and that antisense oligonucleotides blocking PTBP2 binding redirect splicing to partially restore SYNGAP1 in patient neurons.
Key takeaways

Defines the direct ptbp2 (neuronal polypyrimidine-tract binding protein) footprint in the human brain by pairing eCLIP-seq in human cortex (Brodmann area 4) and iPSC-derived cortical excitatory neurons with rMATS analysis of PTBP2-depletion RNA-seq — the first comprehensive PTBP2-dependent alternative-splicing map in human neurons and post-embryonic human brain tissue (splicing-and-neurodevelopment).
PTBP2 (paralog of ubiquitous PTBP1) is predominantly neuronal; its target genes are strongly enriched for synaptic functions (SynGO), and it preferentially promotes exon inclusion of AS events in human neurons.
Cross-referencing PTBP2 targets with the Orphanet disease-gene database flags 342 disease-causing genes as PTBP2-regulated (GRIN1, MVD, DNMI, CAMK2B, HNRNPA1, CTNND1, and notably SYNGAP1), positioning PTBP2 as a regulator of neurodevelopmental-disease gene splicing/expression (splicing-and-disease).
Mechanism at SYNGAP1 (a synaptic Ras-GAP whose haploinsufficiency causes a broad neurodevelopmental disorder with seizures, ID and autism): PTBP2 binds SYNGAP1 mRNA and promotes inclusion of an alternative 3′ splice site in Exon 11 that introduces a premature termination codon, triggering nonsense-mediated-decay and restricting SYNGAP1 mRNA and protein — a natural brake on SYNGAP1 dosage.
Antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) that sterically block PTBP binding redirect splicing toward the productive isoform, raising SYNGAP1 mRNA and protein; this validates a therapeutic strategy for a haploinsufficiency disorder by up-regulating the remaining wild-type allele (splicing-therapeutics).
In patient-derived iPSC-neurons (nonsense variants R1240X and K1185X), PTBP2-targeting ASOs partially restored SYNGAP1 expression, and CRISPR correction of the R1240X mutation confirmed the pathogenic effect — the ~50% mRNA reduction indicates efficient NMD of the mutant transcript.

Notable claims / data

The most effective gapmer (“PTBP2 KD”) reduced PTBP2 protein >90% in iPSC-neurons; PTBP1 was high in iPSCs/NPCs and low in neurons while PTBP2 rose with differentiation (confirming the PTBP1→PTBP2 developmental switch).
rMATS (FDR<0.05, |ΔPSI|≥5%): 1,389 genes differentially spliced on PTBP2 depletion across 5 event types, exon skipping most common; 72 disease genes (incl. SYNGAP1, GRIN1) also differentially expressed.
CLIP-seq: 49,875 (cortex) and 57,162 (iPSC-neuron) high-confidence PTBP2 binding sites; predominantly intronic (72%); most enriched motif CUCUCU in both tissues, matching the known PTBP consensus. Positional RBP-Maps show PTBP2 binding <100 nt upstream of a cassette exon promotes its skipping (e.g. DLG4/PSD-95 exon 18).
Overlap of PTBP2 binding between iPSC-neurons and adult human cortex was ~30–40% genome-wide but higher at functionally validated sites, indicating persistence of PTBP2 programs into adulthood.
Cycloheximide (NMD inhibition) increased 3′AS of SYNGAP1 Exon 11, confirming it as an NMD target; PTBP2 KD raised SYNGAP1 protein by 44±14%. Two SYNGAP1 Exon 11 PTBP-binding regions (“Site 1” intronic, “Site 2” exonic) were mapped and ASO-walked at 1-nt resolution; lead ASOs ET-019 (Site 1) and ET-085/ET-020 (Site 2) dose-dependently raised productive splicing, mRNA and protein.

Connections

A translational capstone for ptbp2 biology in splicing-and-neurodevelopment; extends the PTBP1→PTBP2 neuronal switch central to neural alternative-splicing and to reviews like baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (which features ptbp1).
Exemplifies AS-NMD (poison-exons / premature-termination-codon exons) as a dosage-control mechanism and its therapeutic reversal — directly relevant to splicing-therapeutics (Spinraza/SMN2 as precedent) and nonsense-mediated-decay.
The positional PTBP2 rna-splicing-map (upstream binding = repression) parallels the position-dependent logic of hnRNP C in konig-2010-iclip-reveals-the-function-of and Rbfox in lovci-2013-rbfox-proteins-regulate-alternative-mrna; PTBP2’s polypyrimidine/CU-tract binding is mechanistically kin to hnRNP C uridine-tract binding.
SYNGAP1 haploinsufficiency ties to autism and neurodevelopment; the ASO/iPSC-neuron therapeutic framework connects to splicing-and-disease.

Open questions

Given PTBP2’s hundreds of targets, how specific and safe is systemic PTBP2 inhibition versus the gene-specific SYNGAP1-Exon-11 ASOs — what are the off-target splicing consequences in vivo?
Site 2 (exonic) ASOs improved productive splicing without always raising mRNA, hinting at a “no-go decay” / translational mechanism distinct from Site 1 — what is the exact mode of action?
Will the ASO-mediated ~1.5–2.5-fold SYNGAP1 upregulation translate to phenotypic rescue in vivo (the pilot postnatal mouse ICV study), and does the strategy generalize to other haploinsufficient NMD-regulated neurodevelopmental genes?

wiki/sources/de-la-torre-ubieta-2016-adv.title: de-la-torre-ubieta-2016-advancing-the-understanding-of-autism
wiki/sources/de-la-torre-ubieta-2016-adv.content:
Advancing the understanding of autism disease mechanisms through genetics
One-line: Landmark review integrating ASD genetic architecture with neuropathology and model systems to build convergent, mechanistic frameworks for autism and rational therapeutic targets.
Key takeaways

Frames autism as extraordinarily heterogeneous causally—hundreds of genetic forms—yet argues that veritable risk genes give a solid mechanistic grounding for therapy, a core theme of psychiatric-genetics.
Lays out ASD’s genetic architecture across inheritance, variation type, and penetrance: rare high-penetrance syndromes, de novo CNVs/SNVs, rare inherited, and common polygenic variation.
Reconciles competing polygenic vs. major-gene models using heritability estimates, the liability-threshold-model, and twin-and-family-study-design evidence; concludes common additive variation is the largest single component.
Synthesizes convergent neurodevelopment mechanisms—synaptic signaling, protein translation (mTOR/FMRP), Wnt signaling, chromatin regulation (chd8)—as candidate targetable pathways.
Reviews mouse and human hiPSC/hESC/brain-organoids models spanning neurogenesis, radial-glia, interneuron-migration, and synaptic function.

Notable claims / data

ASD prevalence rose to ~1 in 68 US children (<8 yr) by 2016; strong sex bias (1 in 42 males vs 1 in 189 females); intellectual disability comorbid in ~35%.
Known Mendelian syndromes are each rare (none >1% of ASD) but collectively ~5% of ASD cases; ~41% of liability remains “unaccounted.”
Modeled liability contributions (ref. 57): common variation ~49% (predicted), plus smaller de novo, rare inherited, and non-additive components; CNV ~1.28%, syndromic ~3.40%, strongly implicated de novo SNVs ~1.34%.
High-penetrance de novo LGDs (nonsense/frameshift/splice-site) enriched in probands vs unaffected siblings; CHD8 and DYRK1A LGDs seen in ID+ASD but never in unaffected individuals.
Only FDA-approved drugs (risperidone, aripiprazole) target irritability/aggression, not core social deficits; preclinical rescues include rapamycin (mTOR), mGluR5 antagonists (MPEP/CDPPB), IGF-1, and oxytocin.

Connections

Grounds the autism concept page in genetic-architecture terms and connects psychiatric-genetics to the liability-threshold-model, heritability, and missing-heritability debates.
The convergent-pathway argument links chd8 chromatin biology to neurodevelopment, neurogenesis, radial-glia, and interneuron-migration.
Human model-system discussion connects to brain-organoids and neuronal-cell-types as platforms for mechanistic validation.

Open questions

Do the hundreds of genetic forms of ASD converge onto a tractable, small set of targetable pathways, or will many treatments be required?
How much of the ~41% unaccounted liability is common noncoding variation, and can polygenic scores stratify patients for treatment?
Can hiPSC/organoid models faithfully recapitulate fetal cortical phenotypes given their immature, early-developmental identity?

New concepts (plain text, not yet in inventory)
excitatory-inhibitory (E/I) balance; mTOR/FMRP translational control; de novo mutation; copy-number variation; polygenic risk score; likely gene-disrupting (LGD) mutations; Wnt signaling; oxytocin therapeutics; induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) neuronal models

wiki/sources/dear-2024-cortical-gene-exp.title: dear-2024-cortical-gene-expression-architecture-links
wiki/sources/dear-2024-cortical-gene-exp.content:
Cortical gene expression architecture links healthy neurodevelopment to the imaging, transcriptomics and genetics of autism and schizophrenia
One-line: Optimized re-processing of the Allen Human Brain Atlas reveals two new generalizable components of cortical gene expression (C2, C3) beyond the known sensorimotor-association C1, and links these normative developmental transcriptional programs specifically to autism (C1/C2) and schizophrenia (C3) across neuroimaging, differential expression and GWAS. Contributes to brain-development-neurogenomics.
Key takeaways

The first principal component (C1) of cortical transcription is well established as a sensorimotor-to-association “neuronal hierarchy” axis. Standard PCA of the full Allen Human Brain Atlas (AHBA) recovers only C1 robustly; higher components are not generalizable.
Two data-processing improvements — (1) filtering noisy data (low differential-stability genes and under-sampled regions) and (2) using diffusion map embedding (DME) instead of linear PCA — make two higher-order components, C2 and C3, robustly generalizable.
C1–C3 are not just regional cell-composition artifacts: genes weighted positively vs negatively on the same component are anti-correlated within single cells of the same class (scRNA-seq), so they represent transcriptional programs coordinated at the intracellular level.
C1–C3 have distinct developmental phasing (BrainSpan): C1 and C2 are expressed in near-adult form from the first years of life, whereas C3 only emerges during adolescence — convergent with C3’s spatial co-location with a map of adolescent cortical myelination.
Disorder triangulation across three modalities: autism (ASD) is specifically linked to C1 and C2, schizophrenia (SCZ) specifically to C3, consistently across case-control MRI, postmortem differential expression, and GWAS. C3 is framed as a normative adolescent synaptic-pruning/myelination program whose disruption yields atypical supragranular (L2/L3) connectivity in schizophrenia.
The “disorder-second” / development-first framing (asking “what developed differently?” rather than “what is different?”) is positioned as an advance over prior disorder-first imaging-transcriptomics.

Notable claims / data

DME applied to the filtered {137 regions × 7,937 genes} AHBA matrix (top 50% most stable genes; regions sampled in ≥3 of 6 donors). Generalizability g rose from PCA values gC1=0.78, gC2=0.09, gC3=0.14 to gC1=0.97, gC2=0.72, gC3=0.65 (gC4 stayed low at 0.28).
C1–C3 explained 38%, 10% and 6.5% of filtered-matrix variance; strongly weighted (|r|≥0.5) genes: 4,867 (61%) on C1, 967 (12%) on C2, 437 (5.5%) on C3.
Replicated in independent PsychENCODE bulk RNA-seq (11 regions, n=54 controls): regional rC1=0.85, rC2=0.75, rC3=0.73.
GO enrichments: C1 only 15 terms (functionally general; matches prior C1 at r=0.96); C2 59 terms (positive = metabolism, negative = epigenetic/regulatory); C3 111 terms (positive = synaptic plasticity/learning, negative = immune). Higher-order components are more biologically specific.
Cell-type/layer enrichment: C3-positive genes enriched for neurons, synapses, L2/L3 markers; C3-negative for glial (esp. oligodendrocyte) markers. C2 and C3 (not C1) enriched for intelligence/cognition GWAS genes across four studies; all three enriched for educational-attainment GWAS.
Neuroimaging co-location (rejecting a single S-A axis): C1↔fMRI nodal degree (r=0.78, Pspin<0.001) + T1w/T2w + cortical thickness; C2↔MEG theta power (r=0.78, Pspin=0.002) + aerobic glycolysis + cerebral blood flow + cortical expansion; C3↔14–26y change in myelination (ΔMT, r=0.43, Pspin=0.009) + fMRI principal gradient.
Single-cell coupling (~50,000 nuclei, Hodge 2019): positive/negative gene sets strongly anti-correlated within cell class, even within fine subclasses (e.g. L2 VIP interneurons: r=−0.95 for C1, −0.91 C2, −0.69 C3).
Development (BrainSpan, n=35 brains, −0.5 to 40 y): AHBA↔BrainSpan adult correlations rC1=0.96, rC2=0.88, rC3=0.84; but C3 weak in fetal (rC3=0.29) and childhood (rC3=0.47) subsets, while C1/C2 stayed high — C3 top-decile genes peak in adolescence/adulthood.
Disorder links: ASD cortical shrinkage ↔ C1 (r=0.49) and C2 (r=−0.28); SCZ shrinkage ↔ C3 (r=0.43). DEGs: ASD enriched in C1+C2, SCZ in C3, MDD only in C1. GWAS: ASD enriched in C1+C2, SCZ in C3 (replicated with MAGMA/H-MAGMA); MDD and Alzheimer’s GWAS not enriched in any component.
SCZ convergence despite near-zero overlap between primary DEG and GWAS gene sets: C3-positive subsets of both SCZ gene sets are enriched for L2/L3 markers, and C3 co-locates with an MRI map of supragranular thinning in SCZ (r=0.55, Pspin=0.002).

Caveats

Based on bulk-tissue RNA from only six healthy adult AHBA brains; authors expect more than three components exist but their discovery needs higher-granularity data. Gene expression varies with sex, age, genetics and environment — future components likely more individually variable. Co-location is not causation; AHBA is microarray (not RNA-seq).

Connections

A core hub source for brain-development-neurogenomics: ties normative cortical transcriptional architecture to neurodevelopmental disorder genetics (psychiatric-genetics bucket).
Uses the same PsychENCODE/BrainSpan developmental ecosystem indexed by psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource as a replication and developmental-trajectory dataset.
Sibling to developing-brain transcriptomic sources here, e.g. parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna and li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (both used as ASD/SCZ differential-expression inputs in this paper) and the broader PsychENCODE/BrainSpan-anchored set walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform.

Open questions

Are there additional generalizable components (C4+) recoverable only with single-cell or spatially resolved, demographically diverse transcriptomic data?
Does atypical C3 expression mechanistically cause atypical supragranular connectivity in schizophrenia, or is the co-location secondary?
How do the metabolic (C2) and synaptic/immune (C3) programs interact during adolescent plasticity at the cell-type level?
Can the development-first, generalizable-architecture framework be extended to disorders beyond ASD/SCZ (and why does it fail to implicate MDD or Alzheimer’s here)?

wiki/sources/demirdjian-2020-detecting-a.title: demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing
wiki/sources/demirdjian-2020-detecting-a.content:
Detecting Allele-Specific Alternative Splicing from Population-Scale RNA-Seq Data
One-line: Introduces PAIRADISE, a paired statistical framework and software that detects allele-specific alternative splicing (ASAS) by aggregating signals across replicates or individuals in a population, boosting power and uniquely capturing rare-variant effects on splicing.
Key takeaways

ASAS analysis tests differential splicing between the two haplotype-derived mRNA pools within an individual, using heterozygous SNPs to assign RNA-seq reads to alleles; because both alleles share one cellular environment, allelic splicing differences should reflect genetic effects.
Prior ASAS approaches were ad hoc: one-individual-at-a-time tests (e.g., Fisher exact test on inclusion vs. skipping counts) that failed to combine signal across people and were confounded by allele-specific gene expression. PAIRADISE fills this methodological gap.
PAIRADISE reframes ASAS detection as differential alternative splicing with paired replicates: the two alleles of an individual are a pair, and individuals sharing a heterozygous SNP are replicates.
It is a hierarchical model: a binomial models RNA-seq inclusion/skipping read counts (accounting for coverage-dependent estimation uncertainty of percent-spliced-in), and a logit-normal models allele-specific inclusion-level variation across individuals. An additive parameter d_i captures the allelic difference; a likelihood ratio test of d_i = 0 (chi-square, 1 df) gives p values, with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR.
Builds directly on the lab’s prior tools: rmats (AS event detection, length-normalization function, and the rMATS paired model it improves upon), rPGA (allele-specific personal-genome alignment), and GLiMMPS (sQTL testing for orthogonal validation).
A unique advantage over sQTL analysis: ability to detect rare-variant effects on splicing, since ASAS does not require population-level genotype frequency.

Notable claims / data

In simulations vs. paired t test, paired Wilcoxon, rMATS paired test, and Fisher’s combined method, PAIRADISE had the highest AUC/TPR in virtually every setting. With 3 replicates, low variance: AUC 84% (PAIRADISE) vs. 74 / 68 / 63 / 60%; TPR at 5% FPR 61% vs. 26 / 12 / 23 / 4%.
Other models needed roughly 2–3x larger sample sizes to match PAIRADISE’s performance at 3 replicates.
More robust to outliers than a “Fisher’s pooled” strategy; setting si=0 reduces PAIRADISE to an unpaired model similar to EAGLE/rMATS.
GM12878 (6 RNA-seq replicates, single European female): 116 significant ASAS events at FDR <= 10%; 33 in high LD (r2 > 0.8) with GWAS SNPs. 34 of 66 testable events were also significant GLiMMPS sQTLs.
Geuvadis 445 individuals across 5 populations (CEU, FIN, GBR, TSI, YRI): 91 / 82 / 75 / 71 / 143 ASAS events respectively at FDR <= 10%; 13 shared across all five; YRI had the most population-private events (87), many tied to population-specific SNPs (often MAF 0 in Europeans).
ASAS SNPs were significantly enriched for LD with GWAS SNPs vs. random expectation (p < 1e-4 in all six datasets). Example loci: BCL2A1 exon 2 (rs3826007, blood-cell traits / sclerosing cholangitis), LGALS9 exon 6 (rs361497, p=6.7e-16 in CEU; blood protein measurement).
Rare-variant findings: 10 rare-variant-associated ASAS events in GM12878 (e.g., IFI16 rs62621173, CEU MAF 3.4%, p=1.0e-5; FERMT2 rs72686362, MAF ~1%; SCAMP3 rs11557757). Rare-variant events had larger effect sizes (avg allelic PSI difference 25% vs. 18% for common variants in GM12878; Wilcoxon p=0.09).
Conservative design: not constraining si1=si2 reduces detections; the equal-variance user option raised GM12878 from 116 to 133 events (and similarly across Geuvadis populations).

Connections

Methodologically a successor/refinement of the rmats paired model (same lab, Xing); shares the binomial PSI-uncertainty modeling but replaces the correlation-parameter covariance structure with an interpretable additive difference parameter.
Sits alongside sQTL methods (GLiMMPS) as a complementary splicing-methods approach to genetic variation of splicing; the two are orthogonal validation for each other.
Relevant to splicing-and-disease via GWAS colocalization and rare-variant interpretation (e.g., IFI16 linked to Alzheimer age-of-onset).
Tool entity: pairadise.

Open questions

How to model individual-specific allelic differences that depend on covariates (adjacent cis SNPs, trans-regulator levels) — authors propose adding a hierarchical layer but note much larger cohorts are needed.
Short-read limitation: requires a heterozygous SNP outside but near the alternative exon (on the same read); SNPs inside or far from the exon cannot be analyzed — would long-read data relax this?
Extensibility to other allele-specific isoform variation (e.g., allele-specific RNA editing) and to tumor-normal paired cancer designs.

wiki/sources/deng-2024-massively-paralle.title: deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory
wiki/sources/deng-2024-massively-paralle.content:
Massively parallel characterization of regulatory elements in the developing human cortex
One-line: A PsychENCODE2 study that combines lentivirus-based MPRA and deep learning to functionally test 102,767 candidate regulatory sequences and variants in mid-gestation human cortical cells and cerebral organoids, yielding a large catalog of active neurodevelopmental enhancers, disease-associated regulatory variants, and a sequence-to-activity model that predicts variant effects.
Key takeaways

Used lentiMPRA to interrogate the cis-regulatory activity of 102,767 open-chromatin regions (cis-regulatory-elements) in primary GW18 human cortical cells and 10-week cerebral organoids, identifying 46,802 active enhancer sequences — establishing a functional catalog beyond the descriptive massively-parallel-reporter-assay readout.
Two libraries: a differentially accessible (DA) library of 51,495 cell-type-specific candidate enhancers (from developing-cortex scATAC-seq DA peaks, filtered by H3K27ac/PLAC-seq/PCHi-C) and a variant library of 17,069 brain-QTL allele pairs linked to psychiatric disorders.
Found 164 differentially active variants (DAVs) that alter enhancer activity between alleles, nominating candidate causal variants at known disease genes (TBR1, MARK2 for ASD; NFKB2, SUFU for SCZ).
Cerebral organoids produced highly concordant lentiMPRA activity vs primary cells (DA Pearson r = 0.89, variant r = 0.87), supporting organoids as an adequate in vitro model for the developing cortex — a methodological validation relevant to functional-single-cell-genomics.
Trained a sequence-to-activity deep learning model (CNN + recurrent layers) reaching 0.82/0.78 Pearson on held-out chromosome 4, then used in silico mutagenesis (ISM) over 18.4M alleles to learn the regulatory grammar (cis-regulatory-grammar) and classify TFs as activators vs repressors in this neurodevelopmental context.

Notable claims / data

Library QC: >96% of designed oligos recovered, median 56 (DA) / 64 (variant) barcodes per oligo; 97,762 sequences (95%) passed QC; replicate Pearson DA 0.93, variant 0.91.
Active definition = RNA/DNA ratio above the median of positive controls (DA 1.047, variant 1.068); 46,802 active across both libraries, 25,557 above the 75th percentile of positive controls. Active sequences are significantly more conserved (P = 5.8 × 10⁻²⁸) and target higher-expressed genes (P = 6.4 × 10⁻⁶).
Of 46,370 QC-passing DA sequences, 24,218 (52%) were active enhancers; cell-type-specific active fraction ranged 43–62%. Motif enrichment for neurodevelopmental TF families (DLX, LHX, SOX) and universal stripe factors / USFs (EGR1, MAZ, KLF/SP).
Variant library: 15,335 variants had both alleles passing QC, 8,029 active from ≥1 allele; 164/8,029 (2.04%) DAVs at 10% FDR (≈51% down, 49% up) — in line with ~1% of SNPs marked in prior eQTL work. Only 34 DAVs (21%) clearly alter a TFBS by motifbreakR; disrupted TFs form a SOX2/STAT3-centered network.
Independent validation: 11 high-activity DA peaks all drove GFP in cortical slice cultures (3 cell-type-specific, e.g. ulEN-2 co-localizing with SATB2 and looping to MEF2C); 24 sequences tested by luciferase (MPRA–luciferase Pearson 0.63 in bulk; 0.90/0.84 bulk vs purified EN/microglia).
Organoid concordance: ~half of tested sequences active in both contexts; DAV effect sizes highly correlated (r = 0.91, P = 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶) for largest-effect variants, though ≥half of per-context DAVs were not significant in the other context (some opposite-direction, e.g. OLIG2-related rs112049982). BCL6 (organoid) and GLIS3 (primary) identified as differentially expressed TFs explaining context-specific activity.
Deep learning: model outperforms the most comparable model DeepSTARR (0.68 Pearson on fly STARR-seq) in its own setting; ISM predicts 11.6% of oligos can be increased ≥50% and 19.7% decreased ≥50% by a single nucleotide change — many synthetic alleles exceed the tested QTL effects.
Caveats (stated): bulk MPRA cannot make cell-type-specific predictions and limits detection of cell-type-specific allelic effects; MPRA measures sequence activity but not the target gene; episomal/out-of-context testing of 270-bp inserts; adult (not developmental) brain QTLs used for target mapping; modest MPRA–eQTL correlation; microglia under-detected partly due to lentivirus resistance.

Connections

A human, developing-cortex massively-parallel-reporter-assay companion to other MPRA/variant-function work in this wiki, especially groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at (cis-regulatory mutation mapping) and zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory (context-dependent MPRA + snATAC dissecting GWAS risk alleles in liver) — both pair candidate-CRE accessibility maps with allele-resolved reporter assays and motif-disruption analysis to nominate causal noncoding variants.
Same PsychENCODE2 program and developing-brain focus as wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform (xQTL atlas) and psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource; complements the descriptive scATAC-seq of the developing cortex that supplied its candidate sequences.
The sequence-to-activity model and ISM connect this to cis-regulatory-grammar and broader sequence-based prediction in ai-in-genomics.
See entity psychencode2-developing-cortex-lentimpra for the reusable catalog + model deliverable.

Open questions

Can single-cell MPRA and purified/sorted populations recover the cell-type-specific allelic effects that bulk lentiMPRA misses?
Would developmental (rather than adult) brain QTLs and CRISPR/prime-editing screens better confirm endogenous target genes and effect directions for the 164 DAVs?
How transferable is the sequence-to-activity model to other brain regions, developmental stages, or non-human primate iPSC libraries for studying regulatory evolution?

wiki/sources/dent-2025-a-basic-framework.title: dent-2025-a-basic-framework-to-explain
wiki/sources/dent-2025-a-basic-framework.content:
A basic framework to explain splice-site choice in eukaryotes
One-line: Quantifies the usage of individual splice-sites transcriptome-wide across Arabidopsis, Drosophila and humans, treats splice-site usage as a molecular phenotype for GWAS, and shows that a simple ranking of intronic GT[N]4 / [N]4AG hexamers explains most splice-site choice — a shared, conserved “splicing logic” across eukaryotes.
Key takeaways

Introduces splice-site usage (measured by the Splice-site Strength Estimate, spliser/SSE) as a direct, individual splice-site-level phenotype for GWAS, rather than gene-level or event-level splicing measures. This directly links genetic variants to the usage of specific donor/acceptor sites (see splice-sites).
Ran >130,000 GWAS (SpliSER-GWAS) across three species (1001 Genomes Arabidopsis, DGRP Drosophila, GTEx human heart), cataloguing genetic variation associated with individual splice-site usage.
Most genetically controlled splicing variation is cis (78–91% of associations) with no major trans hotspots in any of the three species — i.e., no dominant trans splicing-factor QTLs among common variants.
Splice-site-level GWAS is specific and captures causal variants: because mutations at a splice-site have a causal impact on its usage, the highest-associated SNP is often the splice-site mutation itself (captures up to ~60% of mappable large-effect causal variants).
Genetic variation affecting splice-site choice is concentrated near the splice-site, and intronic regions drive most variation in splice-site usage (stronger and more frequent than exonic effects).
Intronic hexamer ranking is the core determinant: GT[N]4 hexamers explain ~70.8% of donor-site choices on average; [N]4AG / [N]5AG explain ~60% of acceptor choices. This is the smallest intronic k-mer giving comparable cross-species explanatory power.
Hexamer strengths correlate across species and their dendrograms reflect phylogeny, arguing for evolutionary selection on hexamer sequences and a conserved splicing logic (see splicing-and-evolution).
Donor hexamer differences are primarily driven by U1 snRNA base-pairing (strongest hexamer GTAAGT is complementary to U1’s CAUUCA site); other hexamers reflect RBP binding (e.g., GTACAG = DAZAP1 motif; GTCTTT/GTCTTA = PTBP1).

Notable claims / data

Quantified ~200 million splice-site-strength values across 767,199 splice-sites (39,734 genes, three species); 430,345 sites (56.1%) showed >20% usage difference between extreme individuals.
SpliSER-GWAS detected associations at 21,988 sites (4,951 Arabidopsis, 7,328 Drosophila, 9,709 human). cis fractions: Arabidopsis 82.6%, Drosophila male 91.3% / female 91%, human 78.1%.
Compared to a published sQTL analysis on the same data: 82% (32/39) of mappable splice-site mutations were significant in SpliSER-GWAS; 40% gene-level overlap for splicing variation.
Hexamer ranking explained 66% of splice-site choices in a meta-analysis of Rosenberg et al. massively-parallel data (~4,000 competing hexamers), rising to 91% when hexamer strength differences were >0.25.
Validated experimentally with synthetic introns and mCHERRY minigene reporters: “best” (AT-rich, 75%) vs “worst” (GC-rich, 68%) intron; swapping hexamers at donor/acceptor sites of RPS10, CALM3, MYO1SB introns changed splice-site choice and mCHERRY fluorescence as predicted; explained known mutations in FLM (Arabidopsis flowering) and CFTR (rs75039782 pseudo-exon in cystic fibrosis).

Connections

Reframes splicing-qtl analysis: individual splice-site usage as the phenotype gives better specificity for causal variants and distinguishes direct vs indirect (competing-site) effects, versus event/isoform-based sQTL.
Provides an empirical, hexamer-based splicing-code / splice-sites framework, complementary to and outperforming (for splice-site usage) MaxEnt scores and deep-learning tools like spliceai / pangolin in cross-species portability.
The cis-dominance / no-trans-hotspot finding is relevant to how splicing variation contributes to complex traits and splicing-and-disease.
Cross-species hexamer conservation contributes to splicing-and-evolution; the tissue-specificity is argued to act on top of a species-level conserved core via splicing-factors expression.
Uses the spliser method (SSE) as the phenotype quantifier.

Open questions

Do rarer alleles or additional tissues/cell types reveal trans effects that this stringent, single-tissue, common-variant analysis missed?
Can hexamer rankings be developed tissue/condition/sex-specifically and used to rationally engineer desired splice-site strengths for agricultural/medical applications?
How do hexamer rankings integrate with larger-context deep-learning predictors to improve both interpretability and accuracy?

wiki/sources/derafshi-2022-autism-risk-f.title: derafshi-2022-autism-risk-factor-chd8
wiki/sources/derafshi-2022-autism-risk-f.content:
The autism risk factor CHD8 is a chromatin activator in human neurons and functionally dependent on the ERK-MAPK pathway effector ELK1
One-line: Conditional loss-of-function and endogenously tagged chd8 alleles in human iPSC/ESC-derived excitatory neurons show CHD8 is a transcriptional activator recruited to ETS-motif promoters by the MAPK/ERK effector ELK1.
Key takeaways

CHD8, the most recurrently mutated gene in autism, acts as a transcriptional and chromatin activator in human neurons: homozygous knockout causes far more gene downregulation than upregulation and predominantly loss of chromatin accessibility.
CHD8 chromatin targeting is cell-context-dependent (weak correlation r2=0.25 with published neural-progenitor ChIP-seq) and preferentially binds ETS-motif-enriched proximal promoters marked by active histone modifications.
ELK1, the highest-expressed ETS factor in these neurons and the one whose expression correlates with CHD8 in primary human cortex, physically co-IPs with CHD8 and is required to recruit CHD8 specifically to ETS-motif sites.
Findings implicate a MAPK/ERK/ELK1 axis in the pathogenesis of CHD8-mutation neurodevelopmental disorders, offering a possible therapeutic entry point.

Notable claims / data

Model built in human pluripotent stem cells (H9 ESC + one male iPSC line): heterozygous conditional KO by floxing CHD8 exon 4 (AAV-mediated homologous recombination), homozygous cKO by CRISPR/Cas9 indel in the non-conditional allele (clones CR1: 2 bp del, CR2: 10 bp ins, CR3: 7 bp del).
Loss of CHD8 did NOT impair viability, intrinsic/active membrane properties, or synaptic function (mEPSC, evoked EPSC unchanged) in human neurons, unlike lethality seen in mouse embryos.
Heterozygous KO gave only subtle transcriptional changes; homozygous KO gave pronounced changes skewed toward downregulation. CHD8 binding is stronger at promoters of downregulated genes.
ChIP-seq (endogenous C-terminal FLAG-HA tag; two antibodies, Pearson r2=0.80): 80% of CHD8 peaks at promoters; top 30 enriched motifs specific to ETS factors, plus YY1.
ATAC-seq homozygous KO: 1,481 peaks lost accessibility vs 106 gained; lost-accessibility sites enriched for CAAT and GGAA (ETS) motifs; accessibility loss most pronounced at ETS-motif-containing CHD8 target sites.
Autism-risk genes (SFARI) overrepresented among CHD8-regulated genes and largely upregulated in KO; a subset of CHD8-target ASD genes co-express as a module in the developing human fetal cortex (12-37 CPW).
ELK1 knockdown (shRNA) or peptide inhibitor abolished CHD8 binding specifically at ETS-motif peaks; ELF4-control knockdown had no effect; ELK1 overexpression did not increase binding (possibly saturated/phospho-regulated).
Analysis methods: excitatory neurons via Ngn2 induction; RNA-seq aligned to hg19 (TopHat/Bowtie), DESeq2; multivariate HMM (ChromHMM) chromatin-state annotation using public H9-neuron modification data.

Connections

Deepens the wiki’s chd8 entity with a human-neuron loss-of-function mechanism, complementing its established autism / psychiatric-genetics associations.
Adds a chromatin-accessibility and gene-regulation mechanism relevant to neurodevelopment and cis-regulatory-elements: a transcription-factor (ELK1) that guides a chromatin remodeler to its motif sites.
Relevant to neuronal-cell-types: CHD8 is predominantly expressed in excitatory neurons in adult human cortex (single-cell data).

Open questions

Does ELK1 phosphorylation status (MAPK/ERK signaling state) quantitatively gate CHD8 recruitment in vivo?
Why is heterozygous loss (the disease-relevant dosage) so transcriptionally mild while homozygous loss is severe?
Can pharmacological modulation of the MAPK/ERK/ELK1 axis rescue CHD8-mutant transcriptional/chromatin phenotypes?

wiki/sources/desterro-2020-targeting-mrn.title: desterro-2020-targeting-mrna-processing-as-an
wiki/sources/desterro-2020-targeting-mrn.content:
Targeting mRNA processing as an anticancer strategy
One-line: A review arguing that precursor-mRNA processing — alternative-splicing and 3′-end cleavage/alternative-polyadenylation — is deregulated in nearly all cancers, generating cancer-specific mRNAs, and that the spliceosome and its regulators are actionable drug targets, several already in clinical trials.
Key takeaways

Cancer is driven not only by DNA mutations but by aberrant post-transcriptional processing: pre-mRNA alternative-splicing and alternative-polyadenylation are frequently altered in tumours, producing cancer-specific mRNAs that raise oncogene activity or inactivate tumour suppressors (splicing-and-disease).
Mechanistic primer: splicing is catalysed by the spliceosome (five snRNAs U1/U2/U4/U5/U6 + ~200 proteins) through two transesterification reactions; splice-sites (5′SS GU, 3′SS AG, branch point, polypyrimidine tract) are recognized by U1, SF1, U2AF2/U2AF1 and the U2 subcomplex sf3b1/SF3B, and are modulated by splicing-factors — SR proteins (activate) and hnRNPs (repress) depending on binding position (splicing-code).
Splicing is coupled to transcription via the Pol II CTD and can be tuned by elongation rate and chromatin (cotranscriptional-splicing, splicing-and-chromatin).
Tumours can be selectively sensitive to spliceosome inhibition even when splicing is essential to all cells — cell-cycle/apoptosis genes are “weak competitors” for the spliceosome and are hit first, and MYC-driven cancers with high pre-mRNA load are especially vulnerable (splicing-therapeutics).
Recurrent somatic mutations in splicing factors (sf3b1, u2af1, SRSF2, ZRSR2) act as change-of-function heterozygous splicing-factors driver mutations, first found in myelodysplastic syndrome and now across ≥33 cancer types.
Two therapeutic modalities are advancing: (1) small molecules targeting the spliceosome (SF3B pladienolide derivatives, PRMT5/PRMT1 inhibitors, sulfonamides degrading RBM39, SRPK/CLK kinase inhibitors) and (2) splice-switching / decoy oligonucleotide-therapeutics that redirect cancer-associated splicing or polyadenylation.

Notable claims / data

Splicing is altered in nearly all cancers; a comprehensive analysis of 8,705 patients across 32 cancer types found tumours with up to ~30% more alternative splicing than normal tissue, plus tumour-specific events absent from healthy tissue.
Alternative 3′-end formation: ~2,000 clinically relevant APA events identified; cancer-associated APA usually shortens 3′UTRs, upregulating oncogenes (loss of miRNA repression) — CFIm25 (NUDT21) downregulation in glioblastoma shifts to short-3′UTR oncogene isoforms and worse prognosis; intronic polyadenylation truncates tumour suppressors (72% of 190 candidate suppressor genes disrupted this way in CLL).
Concrete oncogenic splicing switches: pyruvate kinase PKM2 (Warburg glycolysis), BCL2L1/BCL-XL (anti-apoptotic), BRCA1-Δ11q and BRCA1-IRIS (PARP/DNA-damage resistance), constitutively active androgen receptor variants (AR-V7, anti-androgen resistance), BIM/BCL2L11 truncation (imatinib resistance), FPGS mis-splicing (methotrexate resistance), aberrant BRAF isoforms (vemurafenib resistance).
SF3B-targeting drugs: pladienolide derivative E7107 showed antitumour activity in xenografts and reached phase I, but caused optic neuritis/visual loss and was suspended; orally available H3B-8800 preferentially kills spliceosome-mutant cells and entered phase I (MDS/AML/CMML). Pladienolides, spliceostatins and sudemycins all bind SF3B and block U2 snRNP–pre-mRNA interaction.
Indirect spliceosome modulation: PRMT5 inhibitors (EPZ015666/GSK3235025, JNJ-64619178, GSK3326595, PF-06939999) and PRMT1 inhibitor GSK3368715 in phase I; sulfonamides (indisulam, E7820, tasisulam) induce DCAF15-mediated degradation of the splicing factor RBM39; SRPK1 inhibitor SPHINX31/SRPKIN-1 and CLK inhibitor T-025 reduce angiogenic/oncogenic splicing; BET inhibitors indirectly affect splicing via elongation.
Splice-switching oligonucleotides (SSOs, 15–30 nt): approved drugs eteplirsen (DMD exon 51 skip, Duchenne) and nusinersen (SMN2 exon 7 inclusion, SMA) are the proof of concept; preclinically SSOs redirect BCLX, STAT3, PKM, MKNK2 (Mnk2b→Mnk2a), HER2/ERBB2, MDM4, BRCA1, INSR, BIM splicing toward tumour-suppressive/pro-apoptotic isoforms. Decoy oligonucleotides sequester SRSF1; ASOs blocking intronic PAS in AR restore full-length receptor and re-sensitize prostate cancer.
Pre-mRNA processing also generates neoantigens (splicing/editing-derived neoepitopes) exploitable for personalized cancer immunotherapy; RNA editing (A-to-I) is likewise deregulated in cancer.

Connections

Companion cancer-focused counterpart to the development/physiology reviews baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and complements the therapeutics angle in splicing-therapeutics; heavily grounds splicing-and-disease.
Deep on spliceosome-mutation biology involving sf3b1, u2af1, SRSF2 and srsf1 — reinforces the driver-mutation framing used across the splicing-QTL / splicing-factor pages (splicing-factors, splicing-qtl).
The mechanism sections map onto spliceosome, splice-sites, splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-code, cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin.
The SSO/decoy sections extend splicing-therapeutics toward oncology and introduce alternative-polyadenylation and oligonucleotide-therapeutics as targetable layers; intron retention as NMD substrate connects to intron-retention and poison-exons.

Open questions

Why are spliceosome-mutant cancers selectively vulnerable to further spliceosome inhibition — the “catastrophic-to-all-cells” paradox — and how to identify responder patients / biomarkers?
What controls the competition between splicing and (intronic) polyadenylation, which remains largely unknown, and can APA be drugged as reliably as splicing?
Can splicing- and editing-derived neoantigens be reliably predicted and turned into personalized cancer vaccines?
How generalizable is the R-loop / genome-instability mechanism linking SF3B1/SRSF2/U2AF1 mutations to oncogenesis, and does ATR inhibition offer synthetic lethality?

wiki/sources/di-lullo-2017-the-use-of-br.title: di-lullo-2017-the-use-of-brain
wiki/sources/di-lullo-2017-the-use-of-br.content:
The use of brain organoids to investigate neural development and disease
One-line: A foundational 2017 review of human PSC-derived brain organoids — how they are generated, the extent to which they recapitulate human cortical development (notably the human-specific outer radial glia / OSVZ), their current limitations, and their emerging use for modelling neurodevelopmental, infectious, psychiatric and neurodegenerative brain disease.
Key takeaways

Brain organoids are 3D structures grown from human pluripotent stem cells (PSCs — including iPSCs and ESCs) that self-organize into tissue resembling discrete brain regions; their key value is modelling human-specific features of brain-development-neurogenomics that mouse models miss. Relevant to the brain-development-neurogenomics topic hub.
Protocols span a spectrum from spontaneous self-organization (medium free of neural-induction signals → diverse regions but high variability and off-target/non-ectodermal cells) to “extrinsic” directed differentiation (exogenous morphogens mimicking endogenous patterning → reproducible, region-specific organoids). Standard neural induction inhibits SMAD signalling, then supplies region-specific morphogens.
The signature human-relevant achievement: organoids generate an outer subventricular zone (OSVZ) populated by outer radial glia (oRGs, a.k.a. basal radial glia), a progenitor class abundant in gyrencephalic primates but largely absent in mouse and tied to the evolutionary expansion of the human cortex.
Single-cell mRNA sequencing is the central tool for validating organoid fidelity — organoid cell identities must be matched transcriptionally against the developing human fetal brain rather than inferred from (disorganized) anatomical location.
“Fusing” pre-patterned dorsal (pallial) and ventral (subpallial) organoids reconstitutes interneuron migration and rudimentary excitatory–inhibitory circuit interactions in vitro.
Disease modelling is the major application: microcephaly, lissencephaly (Miller–Dieker syndrome), Zika virus, autism, Timothy syndrome, and Alzheimer disease have all been modelled, with organoids best suited to disorders manifesting at embryonic/fetal stages.

Notable claims / data

Transcriptional maturity is limited: organoids cultured up to ~100 days resemble human cortex at only post-conceptional weeks 17–24; most protocols correlate only with early-to-mid-gestational stages, a key ceiling on modelling later/postnatal events.
A single-cell study profiled ~80,000 cells from 31 organoids (3 and 6 months); 6-month organoids yielded ~10 distinct cell clusters (astrocytes, neuroepithelial/OPC-like progenitors, neuronal-lineage cells, forebrain-marker-enriched cells, retina-specific cells), with greater diversity than 3-month organoids. Caveats: many cells assigned to mesodermal lineage (protocol variability); organoids in the same bioreactor were similar (batch effects / possible secreted signalling between “sister” organoids); some clusters lacked clear in vivo counterparts.
Reproducibility is a core weakness: organoids from the same cell line/conditions produce different regional identities and cellular heterogeneity, especially under minimal extrinsic patterning. Long-term culture improves correlation between organoid and endogenous molecular signatures.
DNA-methylation analysis showed some epigenomic patterns are conserved between fetal tissue and organoids while others are in-vitro-specific — reinforcing the need for continuous validation.
Functional maturation reported: neurons in dorsal telencephalon organoids (up to 180 days) fire spontaneous action potentials, show synaptogenesis and evoked EPSPs; fused dorsal–ventral organoids show migrated interneuron-like cells receiving/giving synaptic inputs and firing at ~2× the rate of non-migrated cells; electron microscopy confirms structural synapses; retinal-like regions respond to light.
Disease findings: CDK5RAP2-mutant microcephaly organoids show fewer progenitors and premature differentiation; Miller–Dieker (lissencephaly) organoids show mitotic-spindle dysregulation, neuronal-migration defects, neuroepithelial apoptosis, oRG mitotic defects, and non-cell-autonomous WNT-signalling defects; ZIKV selectively targets/destroys neural progenitors (growth reduction, apoptosis), upregulates TLR3, with AXL implicated as a candidate entry receptor (contested across studies — important for glia but apparently dispensable in NPCs); idiopathic-ASD organoids show FOXG1 dysregulation, accelerated cell cycle and excess GABAergic neurons; Timothy-syndrome fused organoids reveal a cell-autonomous interneuron migration defect; familial-AD organoids recapitulate amyloid aggregation, hyperphosphorylated/filamentous tau and endosome abnormalities — phenotypes not seen in mouse FAD models.
Persistent missing/under-represented cell types: organoids lack vascularization (endothelial cells) and microglia, and OPCs are uncommon — a limitation flagged as significant for, e.g., interpreting ZIKV tropism (microglia and astrocytes are vulnerable in primary tissue).

Connections

Anchors the brain-development-neurogenomics hub on the developmental-biology / model-system side: where the developing-brain genomics siblings characterize human fetal cortex from primary tissue and -omics, this review covers the in-vitro stem-cell model used to test mechanisms.
The OSVZ/oRG and cortical-expansion themes connect to the broader developing-brain literature on human-specific neocortical progenitors; single-cell transcriptomics is the shared validation method linking this review to single-cell developing-brain atlases.
Disease sections (autism, schizophrenia, bipolar, lissencephaly) connect to the psychiatric/neurodevelopmental-genetics arm of the hub — organoids are positioned as a complement to population/tissue genetics for testing the cellular consequences of risk variants in polygenic disorders intractable in rodents.

Open questions

Can organoids be matured past mid-gestation to model postnatal events (circuit refinement, synaptic pruning, dendritic growth) and late-onset/neurodegenerative disease?
How to add the missing structural components — vascularization for nutrient delivery (and larger size), microglia, OPCs — via bioengineering or co-differentiation/integration of separately derived cell types?
How reproducible and in-vivo-faithful is organoid cell-type composition, given batch effects, off-target lineages, and weak organoid-vs-fetal transcriptional correlations for some progenitors?
Can organoids recapitulate higher-order human cortical architecture — the radial-glial scaffold transition (continuous→discontinuous, truncated RGs), gyrification (which occurs after neurogenesis), proper six-layer lamination, and canonical intracortical circuitry?
Will disease phenotypes reported in single labs replicate across laboratories to establish genuine biological relevance?

wiki/sources/dickel-2018-ultraconserved-.title: dickel-2018-ultraconserved-enhancers-are-required
wiki/sources/dickel-2018-ultraconserved-.content:
Ultraconserved Enhancers Are Required for Normal Development
One-line: CRISPR deletion of individual and paired ultraconserved forebrain enhancers near mouse Arx yields viable mice with growth and brain-development defects, revealing why these noncoding elements are so conserved.
Key takeaways

Non-coding “ultraconserved” elements act as distant-acting cis-regulatory-elements; their perfect cross-species conservation had puzzled biologists since single-enhancer knockouts (Ahituv 2007) gave viable, phenotypically normal mice.
Focusing on eight ultraconserved sites in an 800 kb X-chromosome region containing the essential neuronal TF Arx, the authors used CRISPR/Cas9 genome-editing to make single and pairwise enhancer deletions.
All single and double knockouts were viable and fertile, refuting both “essential for life” and enhancer-redundancy explanations for the extreme conservation of these noncoding-variants.
Three of four enhancers were nonetheless functionally important: their loss caused growth deficits and neurodevelopment anomalies (altered neuron populations, structural brain defects), supporting negative selection against subtle fitness costs.

Notable claims / data

Locus harbors four of the five longest non-exonic ultraconserved sites in the human genome; pairwise deletion removed up to 2,086 bp (hs119 + hs121) of perfectly human-rodent-conserved sequence.
Single-cell RNA-seq (Drop-seq) on ~4,723 E12.5 forebrain cells: all four telencephalon enhancers (hs119, hs121, hs122, hs123) active in Arx-expressing radial glia/progenitors or immature GABAergic/glutamatergic neurons.
Both double deletions reduced Arx expression (p <= 0.002, edgeR); no other gene within 10 Mb affected, indicating Arx-specific action.
hs121 loss = 62% reduction in striatal cholinergic (ChAT+) neurons; hs119/hs121 = 97% near-complete loss; 21-24% drop in PV+ cortical interneurons; hs119/hs121 double = ~15% body-weight reduction.
Dorsal enhancer hs122 loss = disorganized dentate gyrus, 26% shorter DG, 36% smaller granule cell layer.
Caveat: X-linked locus, only hemizygous males examined; heterozygous/autosomal generality untested.

Connections

Directly probes the function of cis-regulatory-elements and noncoding-variants, complementary to reporter-assay screens like lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric and deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory.
Arx enhancers drive Arx in progenitors and migrating GABAergic neurons, linking to interneuron-migration and neuronal-cell-types biology.
CRISPR/Cas9 in vivo enhancer deletion connects to genome-editing approaches such as park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing and gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements.

Open questions

Do heterozygous losses (X-linked in females, or autosomal enhancers generally) also produce detectable phenotypes?
What behavioral/cognitive consequences follow the observed neuroanatomical changes over the full lifespan?
How general is “subtle fitness cost drives ultraconservation” across the >480 ultraconserved elements genome-wide?

wiki/sources/dixit-2016-perturb-seq-diss.title: dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits
wiki/sources/dixit-2016-perturb-seq-diss.content:
Perturb-Seq: Dissecting Molecular Circuits with Scalable Single-Cell RNA Profiling of Pooled Genetic Screens
One-line: The foundational perturb-seq paper — it pairs a pooled CRISPR screen with a single-cell RNA-seq readout and a regularized linear model (MIMOSCA) so that an entire transcriptome becomes the scalable, high-content phenotype of each genetic perturbation, recovering gene targets, gene programs, cell states, and genetic interactions.
Key takeaways

Introduces perturb-seq, a pooled single-cell CRISPR screen (a single-cell CRISPR screen / pooled CRISPR screen) in which an expressed guide barcode (GBC) on a Pol-II reporter transcript lets droplet scRNA-seq read out which sgRNA each cell received, because the standard sgRNA itself is poorly captured.
Pairs the assay with MIMOSCA, an elastic-net regularized linear model that decomposes each cell’s profile into perturbation effects, technical/biological covariates, and interaction terms — an interpretable approach to single-cell-perturbation-modeling and a concrete case of model-interpretability (per-guide regulatory coefficients).
Demonstrated on ~200,000 cells across six experiments: 24 TFs in mouse bone-marrow dendritic cells (BMDCs) under LPS stimulation, 14 TFs and cell-cycle regulators in human K562 cells.
Recovers the BMDC LPS regulatory circuit: four TF modules (M1–M4) and five gene programs (P1–P5), with model-predicted TF targets strongly supported by independent ChIP-seq binding data.
Tunes multiplicity of infection (MOI) to capture multi-guide cells, enabling analysis of genetic interactions (additive / synergistic / buffering / dominant), some linked to TF co-binding.
Downsampling shows signature/cell-state-level effects are recoverable far more cheaply than single-gene effects, motivating the feasibility of genome-wide and combinatorial screens; conceptually upstream of later single-cell functional screens like zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics and chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory.

Notable claims / data

GBC detection: 94% (92–96%) at MOI 0.63 in K562 TF pool; 98% at MOI 0.35 in the cell-cycle pool; 60% at MOI 1.4 in 3-hr BMDCs (rising to ~70% after filtering low-complexity cells). Number of guides/cell fit a zero-truncated Poisson convolved with a binomial detection process (KS test).
Most guides significantly repressed their on-target gene; detectability depends on the target’s WT expression, capture efficiency, and incomplete nonsense-mediated decay of frameshifted transcripts.
Variance explained in stimulated BMDCs: ~5% by perturbations alone, ~17% adding cell-quality covariates, up to ~20% adding cell-state covariates. An EM-like step estimated that, on average, >66% of cells were effectively perturbed by their delivered guide.
Pilot validation: 80% sensitivity / 90% specificity vs bulk RNA-seq for identifying correctly regulated genes with ~100 single cells/guide.
BMDC circuit: M1 = Stat1/Stat2 (antiviral, P1); M2 = Cebpb/JunB/Rela/Stat3/Hif1a; M3 = Rel/Irf2/Atf3 (repressors of antiviral response); M4 = Spi1/Pu.1, Runx1, Irf4, Nfkb1. Modules M2 and M4 have opposing effects on two mutually exclusive differentiation states wired by positive/negative feedback. Nfkb1 predicted to act as a repressor (p50-p50 homodimer logic).
Genetic interactions classified into 27 categories; pairs with Stat1/2/3, Rela, Nfkb1, Spi1 enriched for interactions, and Nfkb1-containing pairs enriched for buffering; the Rela–Nfkb1 dominant interaction subset was specifically enriched for ChIP co-binding.
K562 results generalize the approach (continuous cell-state covariates via PCA on WT cells); recovers known TF roles (e.g., GABPA represses mitochondrial function p<1e-8; YY1 represses oxidative phosphorylation p<1e-10) and distinguishes cell-cycle regulators (e.g., AURKA/TOR1AIP1 vs CABP7) with similar fitness but distinct mechanisms.
Downsampling guidelines: signature/state-level effects need ~tens of cells and ~400 transcripts/cell; single-gene-level effects need ~100–200 cells/perturbation and ~1,000 transcripts. A back-of-envelope genome-wide design (~100,000 perturbations) is projected at ~0.05/cell, ~1e12-scale cost as drawn — i.e. dominated by, and scaling with, sequencing/cell cost. All steps shown to run in a single pool (chromatin-regulator pilot, 14,000 cells).

Connections

Founding member of the functional-single-cell-genomics topic; the new entity perturb-seq is shared with the companion CRISPRi study (Adamson et al., 2016, this Cell issue).
A canonical reference point for single-cell-perturbation-modeling (MIMOSCA as a linear, covariate-aware, interaction-capable decomposition) and an instance of interpretable modeling (model-interpretability).
Methodologically upstream of later single-cell/spatial functional-genomics screens in the wiki, e.g. zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics and chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory.

Open questions

Does the sparsity/modularity assumption (few perturbations affect any given gene signature) hold broadly enough to make genome-wide and large combinatorial Perturb-seq tractable in practice?
How best to separate true multiplets/multi-infections from doublets and ambient-RNA contamination as MOI is pushed up for higher-order epistasis?
Can the linear MIMOSCA framework be extended to strongly nonlinear regulatory logic, other molecular phenotypes (chromatin, protein), and continuous rather than discrete cell-state covariates without losing interpretability?

wiki/sources/dong-2014-de-novo-insertion.title: dong-2014-de-novo-insertions-and-deletions
wiki/sources/dong-2014-de-novo-insertion.content:
De Novo Insertions and Deletions of Predominantly Paternal Origin Are Associated with Autism Spectrum Disorder
One-line: A robust family-based indel-detection pipeline applied to 787 ASD exome families shows de novo frameshift indels contribute to autism risk and arise mostly on the paternal chromosome.
Key takeaways

De novo frameshift indels are a bona fide autism risk class (OR 1.6), functionally equivalent to de novo loss-of-function SNVs and adding ~3% to the population attributable de novo burden.
Recurrent de novo hits nominate two new ASD genes: KMT2E/MLL5 (a chromatin regulator) and RIMS1 (synaptic vesicle release), reinforcing chromatin-modification and synaptic themes in psychiatric-genetics.
88% of de novo indels arise on the paternal chromosome, mirroring de novo SNVs, yet — surprisingly — with no detectable paternal-age correlation for indels.
Frameshift indel carriers are enriched among FMRP-target genes and are more common in female probands, consistent with a higher genetic liability threshold in girls with autism.
Analysis was performed in the simons-simplex-collection simplex-family cohort, a mainstay resource for de novo mutation studies in neurodevelopment.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 787 SSC families (602 quartets + 185 trios), 2,963 individuals; whole-exome data reanalyzed with a Dindel family-based local-realignment pipeline plus GATK LeftAlignIndels.
307 putative de novo indels submitted for PCR/Sanger confirmation; 146 confirmed de novo (119 coding, 27 intronic); overall 51% confirmation rate; 96% recovery of 54 previously confirmed indels.
Frameshift burden (602 quartets): 47 in probands (0.078/sample) vs. 30 in siblings (0.050/sample), OR = 1.6 (95% CI 1.0–2.7; p = 0.03); brain-expressed genes OR = 1.7. In-frame indels: no excess (OR 1.3, p = 0.28).
Comparison LoF SNVs OR = 2.4 (p = 0.0002); missense SNVs OR = 1.1 (p = 0.07).
Two-hit genes: a gene with ≥2 disruptive de novo mutations has 97.6% probability of ASD association (q = 0.024) vs. 50.4% for a single hit → KMT2E and RIMS1.
FMRP-target enrichment: p = 6×10⁻⁹ (mouse-brain targets), not significant in HEK293; no enrichment in sibling genes.
Sex bias: female probands 0.126/sample vs. male 0.071/sample (OR 1.9; p = 0.02). Parent of origin: 31 paternal vs. 4 maternal (p < 0.001, binomial). No paternal-age effect for indels (p = 0.33), unlike SNVs (p = 0.0002).
Clinical: de novo frameshift indels associated with a 6.3-point FSIQ decrement (p < 0.0001) but explain little IQ variance (R² = 0.004).

Connections

Companion ASD genomics work on the same broad theme of disrupted risk genes and transcriptomic dysregulation: gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across, schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism, zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of.
KMT2E as a chromatin regulator ties into ASD chromatin-modifier genes such as chd8; RIMS1 partners the ASD gene SCN2A in coexpression networks, relevant to tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related.

Open questions

Why do paternally biased de novo indels show no paternal-age correlation while de novo SNVs do — statistical power or a distinct mutational mechanism?
Do de novo missense and in-frame indels also carry ASD risk once cohorts are large enough to power the test?
How do KMT2E and RIMS1 mechanistically converge with established synaptic/chromatin ASD pathways during fetal cortical development?
