wiki/sources/doughty-2024-single-molecul.title: doughty-2024-single-molecule-chromatin-configurations-link
wiki/sources/doughty-2024-single-molecul.content:
Single-molecule chromatin configurations link transcription factor binding to expression in human cells
One-line: Single-molecule footprinting on engineered enhancer/promoter reporters resolves TF, nucleosome, and machinery occupancy per molecule, decomposing gene activation into separable TF binding and potency terms.
Key takeaways

Applies single-molecule footprinting (SMF, GpC methyltransferase NoME-seq) to genome-integrated synthetic reporters in K562 cells to read simultaneous TF, nucleosome, and transcription-machinery occupancy across single chromatin-accessibility fibers, going beyond aggregate ChIP/ATAC-seq.
Non-linear expression vs. number of TF binding sites arises from apparent cooperative TF binding driven by activation-domain-recruited chromatin remodelers evicting nucleosomes, not from direct TF-TF synergy at the promoter.
Decomposes TF “strength” into separable, independently tunable binding and activation (potency) terms; average TF occupancy contributes additively/linearly to promoter activation rate.
Thermodynamic (partition-function) and kinetic (telegraph-model) frameworks quantitatively predict enhancer binding microstates and time-dependent cis-regulatory-elements output.

Notable claims / data

Called binding/nucleosome states across 24,715,362 single molecules; engineered 0-9 TetO sites (21 bp spacing) upstream of minCMV-Citrine at the AAVS1 locus, driven by dox-inducible rTetR-VP48.
For 5xTetO: 73% of molecules TF-bound vs. only 22% with active (nucleosome-free) promoters. Nucleosome Destabilization model (3-param) fits average occupancy r2=0.99 vs. Simple Competition (2-param) r2=0.84 and fails bimodality.
Removing VP48 (rTetR alone) cut TetO occupancy ~35% and abolished active promoters; Simple Competition model then fits, implicating activation domains (not just DNA-binding domain) in in-vivo occupancy.
BAF inhibition (BRM014) reduced both TF occupancy and potency; p300 inhibition (A485) left occupancy/nucleosome-destabilization unchanged (despite loss of H3K27ac) but reduced potency — BAF acts at enhancer + promoter, p300 only at promoter.
Endogenous ISGF3 (IRF9:STAT1:STAT2) reporter: narrow (IRF9, pre-bound inactive) vs. wide (ISGF3, active) footprints decouple accessibility from expression; scrambling ISREs of ISG15/IFI6/USP18 ablates both pre-stimulation accessibility and stimulated expression. ISGF3 potency higher than rTetR-VP48.
Kinetic model: rTetR-VP48 binding t1/2 ~2.1 hr; hours-long delay between binding and activation (promoters integrate over multiple binding events); ISGF3 reaches max potency in ~3 hr vs. >8 hr for rTetR-VP48.

Connections

Complements bulk/aggregate TF-occupancy profiling in loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor with per-molecule resolution.
Mechanistic, synthetic-reporter counterpart to cis-regulatory-elements dissection via reporter assays (e.g. deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory, lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric).
Links chromatin-accessibility state directly to transcriptional output at single-molecule resolution.

Open questions

How do these principles (activation-domain-driven remodeling, additive potency) generalize to native, multi-TF enhancers with heterotypic sites?
Which specific remodelers beyond BAF compensate under BAF inhibition, and how does redundancy shape occupancy?
Can footprint-width discrimination be extended to resolve broader TF-complex assembly states genome-wide?

wiki/sources/driessen-2026-conditional-m.title: driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable
wiki/sources/driessen-2026-conditional-m.content:
Conditional Monge Gap enables generalizable single-cell perturbation modelling
One-line: cmonge is a conditional neural optimal-transport framework that learns a single global transport map conditioned on arbitrary covariates (drug, dose, combinations), matching condition-specific models in-sample and generalizing to unseen drugs from compound structure alone — a parameter-efficient approach to single-cell-perturbation-modeling.
Key takeaways

Builds on the Monge Gap (a regularizer that enforces c-optimality of a directly parameterized transport map via the primal objective), avoiding the instability of ICNN/Brenier dual approaches (CellOT) and enabling conditioning through a contextualized loss — see optimal-transport.
A single conditional model replaces many condition-specific models: nine CMonge-Dose models matched 36 condition-specific Monge models on SciPlex despite using 4× fewer models.
Two drug-condition encodings compared: structure-based RDKit fingerprints (194 features from SMILES) and data-driven mode-of-action (MoA) embeddings (10-dim MDS over pairwise Wasserstein distances of perturbed populations). MoA is strongest, but RDKit closes the gap as conditions scale.
Enables out-of-sample (OOS) prediction for unseen drugs/doses; combinatorial perturbations handled via a permutation-invariant DeepSets average-pooling layer.
Highly parameter-efficient vs single-cell foundation-models and vs chemCPA: CMonge models range from ~23,000 to ~580,000 parameters vs chemCPA’s ~1.37 million.
Validated across two modalities: SciPlex3 scRNA-seq (3 human cancer cell lines, 187 drugs, 4 doses) and the 4i multiplexed protein-imaging dataset (40-plex, 2 melanoma lines, 35 therapies incl. combinations).

Notable claims / data

SciPlex OOS leave-nine-drugs-out, highest dose (10,000 nM): CMonge-DD-MoA R²=0.900, Wasserstein=3.873, MMD=0.013; CMonge-DD-RDKit R²=0.781, MMD=0.020; chemCPA R²=0.760, MMD=0.195 — CMonge widely outperforms chemCPA especially on distributional (heterogeneity) metrics.
At full scale CMonge trained on all 187 drugs / 748 conditions; in-sample achieves avg R² > 0.8 and R² > 0.6 for all drugs with both RDKit and MoA embeddings.
CMonge captures distributional heterogeneity (spread/multimodality) better than 36 condition-specific Monge models (Wasserstein), whereas chemCPA predictions often collapse to the mean.
OOS performance correlates with similarity of the test drug’s effect to nearest training drugs; trametinib (MEK/kinase MoA) and mocetinostat (most distant MoA embedding) were hardest.
Pathway enrichment from learned transport maps recovers ground-truth pathways for trametinib (MAPK signalling); hypergeometric overlap P=1.0×10⁻⁴⁵.
Caveats: small effect sizes in 4i (identity baseline R² > 0.6) limited gains; CMonge struggled on single-drug 4i OOS; highest dose hardest; simple average pooling may miss higher-order synergistic effects; generalization to new cell types / OOD conditions / batch effects remains open.
Implementation: Sinkhorn divergence + Monge Gap regularizer, squared Euclidean cost, 4-layer MLP, GELU, AdamW, OTT-JAX; SciPlex uses a frozen autoencoder to a 50-dim latent where OT operates.

Connections

artificial-intelligence — AI/ML method for biology; a generative/predictive modelling of cell-state distributions.
optimal-transport — extends the Monge Gap (Uscidda & Cuturi) to a conditional/global estimator.
single-cell-perturbation-modeling — sibling methods compared/cited: chemCPA (main conditional baseline), CellOT, scPRAM, scGen, PerturbNet, GEARS; contrasts with single-cell foundation-models (scGPT, scBERT) on parameter efficiency. Shared concept with TxPert and SIGnature.
ai-in-genomics — single-cell RNA-seq perturbation response prediction.

Open questions

How well does CMonge generalize to entirely new cell types and out-of-distribution conditions, beyond unseen drugs/doses within seen cell lines?
Would more expressive permutation-invariant pooling (attention) meaningfully improve combinatorial/synergistic perturbation modelling?
Can unbalanced OT or flow-matching extensions add robustness to outliers, batch effects, and rare conditions?
How does it scale and perform when fine-tuned on giga-scale screens or patient-derived organoids?

wiki/sources/du-2020-nonrandom-occurrenc.title: du-2020-nonrandom-occurrence-multiple-de-novo
wiki/sources/du-2020-nonrandom-occurrenc.content:
Nonrandom occurrence of multiple de novo coding variants in a proband indicates the existence of an oligogenic model in autism
One-line: A combined-cohort burden analysis showing that ASD probands nonrandomly carry two or more genes hit by extreme de novo variants, supporting an oligogenic model tied to lower IQ and tighter functional connectivity.
Key takeaways

Pools coding de novo variants (DNVs) from 4504 autism trios and 3012 control/sibling trios (from the NPdenovo database) to gain statistical power that individual cohorts lack for detecting multiple-hit patterns.
ASD probands show a significant excess of individuals carrying 2+ genes with “extreme” DNVs (loss-of-function or damaging missense), supporting an oligogenic architecture in a fraction of cases rather than pure single-gene causation.
More mutated genes per proband correlate with lower IQ, linking mutational burden to phenotypic severity in neurodevelopment.
Genes co-mutated within the same patient show tighter protein-protein interaction and coexpression connectivity than random pairs, consistent with a convergent gene-coexpression-network.
Extends psychiatric-genetics beyond additive single-DNV models toward multi-locus / epistasis-flavored contributions.

Notable claims / data

From 4504 ASD trios, 5123 coding DNVs (4705 SNVs, 418 indels) retrieved; rate ~1.14 coding DNVs per trio; no case-control difference in synonymous SNV / nonframeshift indel rates (Padjust = 0.37).
Using the TADA Bayesian tool (tada-method) across four background DNV-rate models, 98 genes reached q < 0.1 in ASD but only one gene (SH3D19) in controls; 80 robust genes (identified by ≥3 background rates) used downstream.
23 new candidate genes with recurrent extreme DNVs identified (e.g., VIP with 2 LoF DNVs, ZWILCH, MSL2, LRRC4, CAPRIN1); 6 hit by 3 extreme DNVs.
Burden: more ASD individuals carry 2+ genes with DNVs (OR = 1.3, P = 2.8 × 10⁻⁶); for extreme DNVs the 2+ signal is stronger (2+ vs 0: OR = 2.2, P = 3.3 × 10⁻¹²).
IQ: patients with 2+ mutated genes have significantly lower FSIQ than those with 0 or 1 (2+ vs 0: Padjust = 4.7 × 10⁻³); 2+ carriers depleted in FSIQ > 100 (OR = 0.5) and enriched in FSIQ ≤ 60 (OR = 1.7).
No significant female:male ratio difference across mutation-count groups, arguing against a simple female-protective effect here.
56 “key” ASD genes (gene-56) defined as more confident: extreme DNVs in ~3.2 individuals/gene, enriched in the RVIS/pLI “hot zone,” and enriched (ClueGO) for synaptic, transcriptional-regulation, and chromatin-remodeling modules.
Estimated ~6.3% of sporadic ASD cases in the combined set are affected by multiple genes with extreme DNVs.

Connections

Uses the tada-method (TADA) for gene prioritization; complements SFARI-based known-gene sets.
Fits the autism and psychiatric-genetics hubs; the burden framing contrasts with strictly additive heritability models by invoking multi-hit / epistasis contributions.
Candidate genes enriched in prenatal brain expression, tying to neurodevelopment and convergent gene-coexpression-network biology.
Notes remaining probands may carry noncoding contributions (see noncoding-variants), CNVs, or inherited variants beyond coding DNVs.

Open questions

What is the mechanistic nature of the oligogenic effect (additive vs epistatic vs threshold), and can it be modeled quantitatively?
How much of ASD risk is explained once noncoding DNVs, CNVs, and structural/inherited variation are jointly considered?
Are the 23 new candidates and gene-56 key set reproducible in independent, larger, and more ancestrally diverse cohorts?
Does the IQ-burden relationship reflect causal severity or ascertainment biases in who receives sequencing?

wiki/sources/duan-2008-development-of-ne.title: duan-2008-development-of-neural-stem
wiki/sources/duan-2008-development-of-ne.content:
Development of neural stem cells in the adult brain
One-line: A 2008 review synthesizing how multipotent adult neural stem cells generate new neurons throughout life in the two canonical neurogenic niches of the mammalian brain, and the extrinsic and intrinsic molecular mechanisms governing each sequential step of adult neurogenesis.
Key takeaways

Part of the brain-development-neurogenomics topic: new neurons are continuously generated in the subgranular zone (SGZ) of the dentate gyrus (hippocampus) and the subventricular zone (SVZ) of the lateral ventricles, believed to originate from multipotent adult neural stem cells (NSCs) capable of long-term self-renewal and generating neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes.
Adult neurogenesis recapitulates the full process of neuronal development (proliferation, fate specification, differentiation, maturation, navigation, and synaptic integration) but within the mature CNS environment.
The identity of adult NSCs is debated: the prevailing model holds that GFAP-expressing astrocytes with radial-glial properties are the NSCs in both SVZ and SGZ; CD133+ ependymal cells may serve as injury-activated NSCs; and Sox2+ cells in the SGZ show self-renewal and multipotency at the single-cell level.
NSC development is governed by a local “niche” (astrocytes, endothelial cells, ependymal cells, mature neurons, and immature progeny), and methodological advances (BrdU labeling, onco-retroviral and reporter-mouse labeling, confocal/electron microscopy, electrophysiology, time-lapse imaging) enabled fine-grained characterization of each developmental stage.

Notable claims / data

Two crucial periods identified during adult hippocampal neurogenesis: (1) NMDAR (NR1)-dependent competitive survival when new neurons are 2–3 weeks old — the same window as enriched-environment-induced survival; (2) an NR2B-dependent enhanced LTP critical period when neurons are 4–6 weeks old, coinciding with preferential recruitment of new granule cells into spatial-memory circuits.
Timeline of synaptic integration of new dentate granule cells: dendritic GABAergic inputs form ~1 week after birth, glutamatergic inputs ~2 weeks, perisomatic GABAergic inputs last; axons reach CA3 within ~2 weeks while dendrites reach the molecular layer within ~1 week and elaborate for ≥4 weeks. Olfactory-bulb granule cells receive mitral-cell inputs by ~10 days.
Signaling specifics: Hedgehog signaling marks/maintains quiescent NSC pools; LIF promotes NSC self-renewal and pool expansion in SGZ; astrocyte-derived Wnts instruct neuronal fate while BMPs promote glial fate (antagonized by noggin and neurogenesin-1); Smad4 deletion or noggin infusion in adult SVZ impairs neurogenesis and increases oligodendrocyte production — a noted unreconciled contrast in BMP effects across settings.
Intrinsic regulators: DCX deletion delays RMS migration; DISC1 (and NDEL1) knockdown causes overextended migration, aberrant positioning, limits dendritic outgrowth, and accelerates synapse formation — DISC1 acts as a key “tempo regulator.” GABA depolarizes immature neurons (high chloride) and promotes synapse formation.
Adult neurogenesis differs from early-postnatal neurogenesis by being dynamically tuned by circuit activity (seizures, antidepressants) and by a markedly slower maturation tempo (dendritic development >4 weeks, spine maturation >8 weeks vs ~2 weeks postnatally); pathological acceleration (recurrent seizures, DISC1 knockdown) causes aberrant circuit incorporation.
Caveat: clonal-level tri-potency of a single adult NSC in vivo remains undemonstrated; most evidence is from rodent models, with human adult neurogenesis only beginning to be probed (e.g., human RMS extension, MR-spectroscopy biomarker for live-human progenitors).

Connections

Core foundational page for the brain-development-neurogenomics hub, covering neurogenesis biology and neuronal maturation that underlies later developmental-brain transcriptomic/atlas work.
Mechanistically anchors the disease gene DISC1 (Disrupted-in-Schizophrenia 1) to adult neuronal development, connecting neurogenesis to neuropsychiatric-disease genetics — relevant to psychiatric-genetics siblings in the topic.
Predates and provides cell-biological grounding for the developing-brain genomic resources in the wiki (e.g., the developmental transcriptomic/isoform atlases); this review is the “wet” mechanism side, those are the “omics” side.

Open questions

Can a single adult NSC give rise to multiple neuronal subtypes, and is a tri-potent NSC demonstrable at the clonal level in vivo? (Requires clonal lineage-tracing tools like MADM or NSC-specific Brainbow.)
What environmental cues guide axons/dendrites of newborn neurons, and what are the synaptic outputs/targets of adult-born neurons (electron microscopy + optogenetics needed)?
What is the physiological significance of the slow maturation tempo, and how reconcilable are the divergent BMP/Smad4 effects across SVZ experimental settings?
To what extent does active neurogenesis occur outside the two canonical niches, and how robust is adult human neurogenesis?

wiki/sources/dudnyk-2023-sequence-basis-.title: dudnyk-2023-sequence-basis-transcription-initiation
wiki/sources/dudnyk-2023-sequence-basis-.content:
Sequence basis of transcription initiation in human genome
One-line: Introduces puffin, a deep-learning-inspired explainable model that predicts basepair-resolution transcription initiation signal from promoter sequence and distills a compact set of interpretable sequence rules (10 motifs + 10 initiators + trinucleotides) that explain most human promoters.
Key takeaways

Builds two complementary sequence models: a prediction-focused deep model (Puffin-D, 100kb context) and an interpretation-focused model (puffin) that extracts a small, human-readable rule set, an exercise in model-interpretability applied to gene-regulation.
A simple additive-in-log (multiplicative-in-count) combination of three sequence pattern types explains basepair-resolution initiation: 10 motifs (main drivers), 10 initiators (tune the exact start base), and trinucleotides (residual local dependencies), together defining transcription start sites at three spatial scales.
Learns 10 reproducible motifs de novo from sequence and signal alone, split into direction-specific (TATA, YY1, U1 snRNP, Long Inr) and bidirectional (SP, NFY, ETS, ZNF143, NRF1, CREB) groups; each has a distinct position-specific effect curve characterizing its cis-regulatory-grammar contribution.
Characterizes each motif’s position-specific effect curve for the first time (e.g. YY1 activating immediately upstream and ~200bp upstream; NFY showing ~10.5bp helical periodicity), reframing known cis-regulatory-elements as quantitatively resolvable.

Notable claims / data

Trained on aggregated basepair-resolution initiation maps from five techniques (FANTOM CAGE 6.7B reads, ENCODE CAGE, ENCODE RAMPAGE, GRO-cap, PRO-cap 2.3B reads); Puffin achieves higher basepair-level correlation with experiment than the most-correlated pair of experimental techniques on held-out chromosomes (chr8, chr9).
In silico TF knockout (zeroing a motif’s activation) recapitulated experimental depletion: NFYA knockdown caused the predicted upstream TSS shift; auxin-induced YY1 degron reproduced the predicted (previously unreported) upstream initiation reductions. Motif insertion/deletion edits matched experiment at TSS-level correlation 0.88 and ~0.9 basepair-level.
Motif composition links to expression selectivity: TATA-high promoters are cell-type-specific / high-dispersion, YY1-high promoters are ubiquitous / low-dispersion; a virtual insertion screen (40,000 promoters x 3,500 genomic contexts with Puffin-D) shows the same motifs govern context selectivity.
Explains bidirectional transcription: bidirectional motifs drive both strands at the nascent level, while U1 snRNP acts post-initiation to yield directional mature transcripts; 87.7% of forward/reverse TSS pairs share sequence contribution (r>0.75), rising to 92.6% within 300bp.
Sequence rules conserved across mammals: human and mouse models nearly interchangeable (median correlation 0.967); across 241 Zoonomia-project genomes, position-specific PhyloP conservation mirrors the model’s motif effects and Puffin basepair contribution scores predict per-base conservation.

Connections

A companion to other interpretable sequence-to-function models in the wiki’s AI-in-genomics space; sits alongside chrombpnet and deepc as a deep-learning tool for reading cis-regulatory-elements and cis-regulatory-grammar from DNA.
The U1 snRNP / 5’ splice-site motif’s post-initiation elongation effect touches splice-sites and cotranscriptional coupling of transcription and splicing.
Positioned within gene-regulation and model-interpretability; the web server (tss.zhoulab.io) makes it a reusable puffin resource for promoter analysis.

Open questions

What are the molecular/structural underpinnings of each motif’s position-specific effect curve, and will sequence-level and mechanism-level understanding converge?
How do promoter motif compositions interact with distal enhancers to set the response curve to cell-type-specific activation signals?
Can the explainable modeling approach generalize to other basepair-resolution genomic functions beyond transcription initiation?

wiki/sources/dupas-2026-splice-isoform-p.title: dupas-2026-splice-isoform-perturbation-coupled-to-single
wiki/sources/dupas-2026-splice-isoform-p.content:
Splice isoform-perturbation coupled to single cell transcriptome profiling reveals functions of microexons in neurogenesis and autism-linked pathways
One-line: Develops CHyMErA-seq — a scalable platform coupling CRISPR-Cas9/Cas12a hybrid-guide deletion of individual exons to single-cell transcriptomic read-out — and applies it to systematically delete brain-specific microexons during neurogenesis, revealing convergent roles in the developmental timing of autism-linked gene expression programs.
Key takeaways

CHyMErA-seq couples isoform-resolution exon deletion to single-cell transcriptomics by adapting the CHyMErA hybrid-guide (hgRNA, fused Cas9 + Cas12a guides) system to a modified CROP-seq lentiviral vector, so hgRNAs are transcribed as polyadenylated 3′-UTR reporters and captured in sci-RNA-seq (see single-cell-crispr-screen).
Enhanced editing efficiency: a mutationally-active en(AsCas12a) fused to tandem C-terminal cMyc NLSs with Cas9 gave ~16% higher editing on average; microexon deletions did not affect mESC viability or differentiation.
Targets a critical program of evolutionarily conserved, 3–27-nt microexons — predominantly included in the nervous system and misregulated in autism (see splicing-and-neurodevelopment, autism).
hgRNA library: 500 gRNAs targeting 37 microexons in 32 genes (frame-preserving, expected to add 3–9 amino acids), plus constitutive-exon and intronic/intergenic controls; applied during in vitro glutamatergic neuron differentiation of CGR8 mESCs.
Microexon deletion causes distinct transcriptomic signatures (not equivalent to whole-gene knockout): 10 microexons showed transcriptomic phenotypes; deletions of Bin1, Clasp1, Gfra1, Med23, Ptprf, Ralgapb microexons perturbed cells enriched at late neuroepithelial / early neuronal-differentiation stages.
Perturbed microexons converge on signaling and morphogenesis pathways and on a protein-protein interaction network involved in neuron projection development (Bin1, Clasp1, Gfra1, Ptk2, Ptprf, Ralgapb).
The Gfra1 microexon regulates neuron projection development: its deletion prematurely upregulates neurogenesis programs and produces longer neurites (GFRA1 is the GDNF co-receptor; the microexon sits in a GDNF-binding-domain loop).
The Clasp1(A) microexon modulates neurogenesis via FAK signaling: skipping promotes FAK Y397 phosphorylation and downstream ERK signaling; the neuronal microexon-included isoform attenuates FAK-ERK to time gene expression (validated with dCasRx-RBM25 artificial splicing).
Neuronal microexons control the developmental timing of autism-linked gene expression: microexon deletion prematurely activates ASD-risk gene programs (SFARI genes), and differentially expressed genes overlap ASD-dysregulated regulons (Sox6, Ets2, Egr1, Elk1, Foxp1, Sin3a) in excitatory neurons — several downstream of MAPK-ERK / the Egr1 regulon.

Notable claims / data

~21,000 quality-filtered nuclei at DIV7; 36/37 targeted microexons represented; median 44 cells per microexon-targeting gRNA, median 492 cells per microexon target; 100% library representation (98.8% within 10-fold of mean).
Benchmarking the analysis pipeline against a published single-cell CRISPR dataset recovered 12/15 (80%) of perturbations called by target-gene expression reduction.
Cells expressing guides targeting the same gene showed significantly higher transcriptomic correlation than non-targeting/intergenic controls (P = 7.50×10^-12); effect largely independent of cell number.
Ptk2 (FAK) microexons A/B/C sit proximal to phosphorylated FAK regions; Ptk2 shows an overall convergent transcriptomic signature on microexon deletion.
Gfra1 microexon-deletion neurites significantly longer at DIV7 (n = 212, P = 0.0056); bulk RNA-seq of independent homozygous microexon-deletion lines correlated with CHyMErA-seq (R = 0.14, p = 0.0057).

Connections

A single-cell, isoform-resolution splicing-methods advance — an alternative/complement to Cas13d RNA-targeting isoform knockdown (schertzer-2025-cas13d-mediated-isoform-specific-rna-knockdown-with) and to dCasRx-RBM25 artificial splicing; extends CHyMErA exon-deletion screens to a transcriptomic read-out (single-cell-crispr-screen).
Deepens microexons biology and their role in splicing-and-neurodevelopment and autism (SRRM4/nSR100-regulated program), showing microexons tune developmental timing rather than on/off gene function.
The FAK-ERK and Egr1-regulon convergence connects microexon splicing to MAPK signaling in neuronal differentiation.
Relevant to splicing-and-disease and ASD gene networks (SFARI, cross-referencing ASD post-mortem brain regulon dysregulation).

Open questions

Which additional microexons (beyond the 10 with detectable phenotypes here) have context- or timepoint-specific roles not captured at DIV7?
How do the “push-pull” microexon networks (different microexons in the same PPI network with opposing effects) collectively set neurogenesis timing?
Can CHyMErA-seq scale to thousands of exons and to in vivo / organoid systems for isoform-resolution functional genomics of brain disorders?

wiki/sources/editorial-2025-rna-splicing.title: editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer
wiki/sources/editorial-2025-rna-splicing.content:
RNA splicing — a central layer of gene regulation
One-line: A framing editorial introducing a Nature Reviews online collection, surveying how recent technological and computational advances (cryo-EM, sequencing, machine learning) have deepened understanding of splicing as a central layer of gene regulation.

Note: This is a short framing editorial introducing a journal collection across Nature Reviews Genetics and Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology. It is best used as an overview/orientation piece rather than a primary research source.

Key takeaways

Most eukaryotic genes are organized as a “mosaic” of introns and exons; splicing removes introns and ligates exons to produce mature mRNA, and is essential for accurate translation of DNA sequence information.
The spliceosome is a dynamic ribonucleoprotein complex of five snRNAs and numerous proteins that recognizes conserved motifs at exon–intron boundaries and carries out a two-step transesterification reaction; recent cryo-EM has resolved multiple conformational states.
alternative-splicing (regulated inclusion/exclusion of exons) greatly expands the coding potential of the genome, producing isoforms that affect mRNA stability, localization and translation.
Timing matters: splicing occurs cotranscriptional-splicing or post-transcriptional-splicing, and depends on nuclear compartmentalization via biomolecular condensates such as nuclear speckles and paraspeckles, which concentrate splicing-factors.
Genetic variation disrupting splice-sites or splicing-regulatory-elements underlies splicing-and-disease; splicing-therapeutics (antisense oligonucleotides, small molecules) is an increasingly validated treatment strategy.

Notable claims / data

More than 95% of human multi-intron genes undergo alternative-splicing.
“Split genes” were discovered in 1977 (Richard Roberts and Phillip Sharp), earning the 1993 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.
The spliceosome comprises five small nuclear RNAs (snRNAs) plus numerous associated proteins.
Spliceosome catalysis is a two-step transesterification reaction.
Genetic variants can cause exon skipping, intron retention, or activation of cryptic splice sites; mutations in core spliceosomal proteins can compromise splicing fidelity or efficiency.
The splicing-code is defined as “a set of rules, an algorithm or a computational model that could predict the splice isoforms, and their frequencies, produced from any transcribed gene in a specific cellular context,” encompassing cis-regulatory elements, RNA structure and trans-acting contributors.

Connections

splicing-therapeutics: ASOs and small molecules applied to genetic disorders, neurodegenerative diseases and certain cancers.
splicing-code / machine learning: deep learning is now a powerful tool for modelling the code and predicting variant effects on splicing, though interpretability remains a challenge; this can guide drug development and clinical diagnostics.
splicing-and-evolution: long-read sequencing reveals striking differences in alternative splicing extent between eukaryotes, and heritable variation in alternative-splicing patterns may contribute to adaptive evolutionary change.
splicing-and-chromatin / transcription: long-read sequencing and imaging illuminate splicing timing and its dynamic interplay with transcription and RNA processing.
rna-binding-proteins / splicing-factors organized within phase-separated condensates regulate splicing.

Open questions

How to improve the interpretability of deep-learning models of the splicing code.
How splicing shapes phenotypic outcomes and contributes to human disease (the editorial frames researchers as “poised to further decode” this).
The full regulatory logic linking splicing timing, nuclear compartmentalization, and isoform output.

wiki/sources/eyring-2026-single-cell-pro.title: eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation
wiki/sources/eyring-2026-single-cell-pro.content:
Single-cell profiling of DNA methylation in autism spectrum disorder prefrontal cortex reveals distinct regulatory and aging signatures
One-line: The first population-scale single-cell DNA-methylation-plus-transcriptome study of autistic and neurotypical postmortem prefrontal cortex (snmCT-seq, 49 donors), finding that ASD methylation changes are modest, hit cell-type-specific regulatory elements, are uncorrelated with transcription, and are dwarfed by profound, cell-type-specific aging effects.
Key takeaways

Used snmct-seq to jointly profile DNA methylation (DNAm) and transcript levels from >60,000 nuclei of the prefrontal cortex (Brodmann area 9) in 26 idiopathic autistic and 23 neurotypical control donors (49 total, all male, ages 2–60), making this the first population-scale single-cell DNAm study of autism.
ASD-associated differentially methylated regions (DMRs) were enriched in promoters and cell-type-specific regulatory elements (overlapping cis-regulatory-elements such as fetal brain enhancers and adult cortex candidate cCREs), yet were uncorrelated with gene expression — methylation and transcriptional dysregulation in ASD appear to be largely independent layers.
Age effects on the methylome were far larger than diagnosis effects; within almost every cell type age explained more methylome variance than any other donor feature, concentrated in excitatory neurons, relevant to psychiatric-genetics and neurodevelopment.
DMRs were highly cell-type specific (<2% overlap across cell types) and most enriched in L2/3 IT excitatory neurons and microglia; relates to cell-type-resolved work like gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across and the single-cell ATAC framework of kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and.

Notable claims / data

Dataset: 66,174 nuclei passed methylome QC (median 1,347/donor; avg depth 1.5M reads, 83% genomic DNA); 27,515 nuclei passed RNA QC; 22,712 nuclei had paired RNA + DNAm. Age did not differ between groups (p = 0.65, Wilcoxon). 236 384-well plates; ~2:1 NeuN+/NeuN- ratio. Whole-genome sequencing (40.9x ± 10.3x) was used to genotype-mask high-impact variants that distort bisulfite methylation estimates (e.g. a C>T SNV mimicking loss of methylation).
28 integrated clusters aggregated into subclasses (5 non-neuronal, 10 inhibitory, 12 excitatory); methylome-only label transfer hold-out accuracy >=97.4%. Analyses focused on the 8 most abundant subclasses; cell type explained 80–99% of global methylome variance.
Transcriptomics: largest DGE in Exc L2/3 IT neurons (608 DEGs, padj < 0.05), concentrated specifically in the putative L2 cluster; DEGs enriched in 129 pathways (81% in Exc L2/3) converging on cellular stress / unfolded protein response. ASD transcriptomic effects correlated with a prior single-cell ASD study.
ASD-DMRs: only 244 differentially methylated predefined genes/promoters (1–27 per cell type), but 31,165 de novo CG DMRs (median 132 ± 185 bp), more often hypomethylated in ASD. Promoters were 5.3% of ASD-DMRs vs 2.2% genomic background (Bonferroni p < 0.004). 36% of ASD-DMRs (11,271/31,165) fell in cell-type-aligned candidate cis-regulatory elements from independent adult-brain single-cell ATAC-seq.
TF motifs: TGA*TCA (Fos/AP-1 family — Fos, ATF3, Jun) enriched in MGE-interneuron hypo-DMRs and Exc L2/3 IT hyper-DMRs; corroborated by SCENIC regulon and ChIP-seq overlap. No significant overlap between DEGs and DMR-implicated genes (mGenes); only a small negative correlation between transcript ASD effect and gene-body mCA in Exc L2/3 IT (Pearson r = -0.08, p = 4.1E-7).
Aging: ~1.4 million de novo CpG age-DMRs (spanning 625,306 non-overlapping regions, 10.3% of genome); generally gaining methylation with age, most pronounced in excitatory neurons. mCG and mCA increase with age in excitatory neurons and plateau by ~25 years (in Exc L4/5 IT, 89% of tested bins gained methylation with age). Near-universal hypomethylation at CTCF motifs with age; AP-1/Fos motifs lose methylation while neurogenin/atonal (NEUROD1) and RFX motifs gain it. Microglia/Ets motifs and EGR-in-excitatory-neurons signatures reproduced.
Epigenetic clocks (Horvath pan-tissue; a cortical clock): performance highly cell-type dependent — strong in Exc L2/3, poor in MGE interneurons and microglia; no difference in predicted epigenetic age between ASD and controls in any cell type. Clock-probe overlap with age-DMRs ranged 0–35% by cell type.
Age-dependent ASD effects: 1,644 genes with age-by-diagnosis interactions; largest neuronal/astrocyte ASD effects concentrated in young donors. Age-stratified ASD-DMRs in young Exc L4/5 IT neurons were enriched for neuron projection development, dendrite morphogenesis, and cell differentiation — proposed as a key perturbed process. Example: STXBP1 promoter hypermethylated specifically in Exc L2/3 of young autistic donors.

Connections

Complements bulk and single-cell transcriptomic convergence in ASD cortex (e.g. gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across) by adding a cell-type-resolved methylation layer and showing it is largely decoupled from transcription.
Reinforces L2/3 superficial excitatory neurons and microglia as foci of ASD molecular pathology, a theme across cell-type-resolved cortical genomics; the cis-regulatory framing connects to single-cell ATAC approaches in kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and.
The strong prenatal/fetal-enhancer enrichment of ASD-DMRs links to developmental-stage themes in velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal and early-brain-overgrowth context in courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development.
Age-dependent diagnosis effects and lifespan profiling relate to developmental/polygenic-trajectory framing in zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of, cross-disorder genetics in grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across, and cortical expression-architecture work in dear-2024-cortical-gene-expression-architecture-links.

Open questions

Causal direction is unresolved: are ASD-DMRs causes or consequences, given they hit methylation-sensitive TF motifs but do not track transcription in cis?
Bisulfite cannot distinguish 5mC from 5hmC (the latter dynamic with age and positively correlated with expression), complicating functional interpretation of DMRs.
ASD-DMRs covered too little of the genome to partition ASD heritability — do genetic-risk regions for ASD overlap differentially methylated regions?
Would syndromic ASD (Rett, DNMT3A variants) show a more dramatic methylome signature than the modest idiopathic one seen here? And is there a true age-dependent microglial ASD signature given limited non-neuronal nuclei?

wiki/sources/fair-2024-global-impact-of-.title: fair-2024-global-impact-of-unproductive-splicing
wiki/sources/fair-2024-global-impact-of-.content:
Global impact of unproductive splicing on human gene expression
One-line: Using nascent RNA-seq before decay across population-scale data, this study shows unproductive (NMD-targeted) splicing is ~3× more pervasive than steady-state estimates and that much of alternative splicing’s impact acts through NMD-driven expression changes rather than proteome diversification.
Key takeaways

Measuring splicing before cytoplasmic decay with nascent RNA-seq (naRNA-seq) reveals threefold more unproductive-splicing than steady-state RNA suggests, because nonsense-mediated-decay rapidly removes these transcripts before they are seen (alternative-splicing).
Unproductive splice junctions compound across multi-intronic genes: a low per-intron “error” rate accumulates so that ~15% of transcript molecules from a typical protein-coding gene are unproductive, and long genes / genes with many introns are most affected.
Genewide unproductive-splicing fraction correlates with degradation-rate measures; AS–NMD explains at least ~9% of post-transcriptional gene-expression variance across cell lines — challenging the view that AS mainly diversifies the proteome (poison-exons).
Most alternatively spliced cassette exons are NOT frame-preserving (length divisible by three) in naRNA once NMD is removed, further arguing that much AS is unproductive “noise”/regulation rather than protein-coding diversification.
GWAS trait loci explained by AS are as often associated with NMD-induced expression differences as with protein-isoform usage — reframing how splicing-qtl contribute to disease.

Notable claims / data

~2.3% of splicing events target transcripts for NMD in naRNA vs ~0.55% in steady-state RNA; NMD-factor knockdown (UPF1/SMG6/SMG7) confirms depletion is due to NMD.
Under a binomial model (~2.3% per-intron unproductive rate), ~17% of mRNA molecules of a typical multi-intronic human gene are predicted unproductive; NMD-substrate fraction rises from ~25% (exon-8 genes) to >50% (≥15-exon genes).
Highly expressed and evolutionarily constrained genes have the lowest unproductive-splicing rates (consistent with selection for accurate splicing); a known exception is conserved SR-protein autoregulatory AS–NMD loops.
Risdiplam (a splice-switching small molecule) induces cryptic GA|GU 5′ splice sites genome-wide in a dose-dependent way: 316 induced cassette exons, 92% unannotated, 91% in coding regions, ~61% frameshifting → NMD; the oncogene MYB is dose-dependently downregulated via a risdiplam-induced exon.
219 risdiplam-regulated genes include many “undruggable” classes (transcription factors, etc.), showing splice-switching molecules can modulate genes beyond classic small-molecule targets.
Post-transcriptional (AS-driven) eQTLs: ~half colocalize with an sQTL; splice sites are enriched among post-transcriptional vs transcriptional eQTLs.

Connections

Establishes unproductive-splicing and AS–nonsense-mediated-decay as major regulators of expression, extending poison-exons and intron-retention and reframing splicing-qtl interpretation (much sQTL signal acts through expression).
The risdiplam findings connect to splicing-therapeutics — extending splice-modulator utility to downregulating “undruggable” genes — and to poison-exon autoregulation seen in schmok-2024-large-scale-evaluation-of-the-ability (PRPF39).
Methodologically relies on leafcutter for junction clustering and nascent RNA-seq; complements steady-state alternative-splicing landscape surveys.

Open questions

What fraction of unproductive splicing is genuinely regulated/adaptive versus tolerated molecular noise, gene by gene?
Can the ~2.3% per-intron unproductive “error rate” be predicted from sequence (splice-site strength, intron length)?
How broadly can splice-switching molecules be designed to titrate expression of specific undruggable disease genes via targeted NMD?
How do inter-individual sQTLs acting through NMD contribute to complex-trait heritability?

wiki/sources/fernando-2025-phenotypic-co.title: fernando-2025-phenotypic-complexities-of-rare-heterozygous
wiki/sources/fernando-2025-phenotypic-co.content:
Phenotypic complexities of rare heterozygous neurexin-1 deletions
One-line: Using patient-derived hiPSC neurons, shows that different rare heterozygous NRXN1 deletions perturb its alternative-splicing in cell-type-specific ways — producing loss-of-function in glutamatergic neurons but gain-of-function in GABAergic neurons — arguing that precision medicine for splicing-linked neuropsychiatric disorders must stratify patients by LOF vs GOF mechanism.
Key takeaways

NRXN1 (2p16.3) encodes a presynaptic cell-adhesion protein / synaptic organizer; complex, cell-type-specific alternative-splicing of neurexins tunes synaptic identity and is differentially disrupted by unique, non-recurrent patient deletions (splicing-and-neurodevelopment).
Patient-specific deletions produce divergent, cell-type-specific synaptic outcomes: NRXN1^+/- deletions decrease synaptic activity in glutamatergic (iGLUT) neurons yet increase it in GABAergic (iGABA) neurons.
Two mechanisms coexist across patients: a 5’ deletion causes loss of function (reduced β→α splicing, less wild-type NRXN1α), whereas a 3’ deletion causes gain of function by expressing novel mutant (MT) splice isoforms from the affected allele.
Reciprocal isogenic experiments (shRNA knockdown of wild-type exon-9 isoforms to mimic LOF; shRNA against the mutant exon-20-to-24 junction to ablate GOF isoforms) causally show that aberrant splicing — not just gene dosage — drives the synaptic changes and can reverse the 3’-deletion phenotype.
NRXN1 emerges as a mediator of excitatory–inhibitory balance, a recurrent theme in neuropsychiatric disorders (NRXN1 CNVs carry high odds ratios for schizophrenia, autism, epilepsy, intellectual disability, Tourette syndrome).
Therapeutic implication: restore the NRXN1 isoform repertoire by increasing wild-type and/or ablating mutant isoforms, stratified by whether a patient’s mutation acts via LOF or GOF.

Notable claims / data

hiPSC-derived neurons made by transcription-factor induction: NGN2 → iGLUT (>95% glutamatergic), ASCL1+DLX2 → iGABA (>80% GABAergic). Cases: a ~115-kb 5’ deletion (affecting the α-exon promoter; LOF; bipolar/psychosis, mother–son pair) and a ~136-kb 3’ deletion (affecting alt splice sites SS4/SS5 across exons 21-23; GOF; schizophrenia, monozygotic twins).
LeafCutter (leafcutter) differential-splicing analysis: 5’-Del reduced β→α splicing (ΔPSI -0.187 iGABA; -0.086 iGLUT) and SS4 inclusion; 3’-Del showed a robust increase in a mutant splice junction between exons 20 and 24 (ΔPSI +0.227 iGLUT, +0.23 iGABA) with reduced wild-type SS4/SS5 splicing — consistent with GOF.
Multi-electrode-array recordings: spontaneous network activity fell >40% in both 5’-Del and 3’-Del iGLUT neurons; immature iGABA neurons showed ~twofold increased activity. Passive/intrinsic membrane properties were largely unchanged, so the phenotype reflects synaptic transmission, not excitability.
Synaptic transmission: sEPSC frequency decreased in iGLUT (both deletions); iGABA sIPSC frequency increased in 3’-Del; synaptic-puncta (synapsin-1) changes were bidirectional and matched presynaptic-gene (SynGO) transcriptomic signatures.
Isogenic rescue: shRNA knockdown of constitutively expressed exon 9 (~55% iGLUT, ~75% iGABA) mimicked the 5’-Del LOF; an shRNA targeting the mutant exon-20–24 junction knocked down GOF isoforms ~95% (iGLUT) and reversed 3’-Del synaptic phenotypes toward control.

Connections

A neuropsychiatric-genetics counterpart to synaptic-splicing biology in wang-2024-alternative-splicing-of-latrophilin-3-controls (latrophilin-3) — both show synaptic adhesion molecules whose alternative-splicing sets synapse properties.
Links splicing-and-neurodevelopment to psychiatric-genetics and autism; the LOF-vs-GOF stratification connects to precision-medicine themes and splicing-and-disease.
Uses leafcutter for annotation-free differential splicing; the exon-junction shRNA strategy parallels isoform-specific correction logic in splicing-therapeutics.
Reinforces that unique (non-recurrent) CNVs at a splicing-rich locus demand patient-specific, mechanism-aware interpretation.

Open questions

Can the LOF-vs-GOF distinction be read directly from a patient’s genotype to choose between “add wild-type” and “ablate mutant” therapeutic strategies?
How do these hiPSC-neuron findings map onto mature circuits and the in vivo excitatory–inhibitory balance over development?
Do other neurexins (NRXN2/3) and other synaptic CNV loci follow the same cell-type-divergent, splicing-driven logic?

wiki/sources/fischbach-2010-simons-simpl.title: fischbach-2010-simons-simplex-collection-resource
wiki/sources/fischbach-2010-simons-simpl.content:
The Simons Simplex Collection: A Resource for Identification of Autism Genetic Risk Factors
One-line: A NeuroView describing the Simons Simplex Collection (simons-simplex-collection), a rigorously phenotyped cohort of >2000 simplex autism families assembled by SFARI to find de novo genetic risk variants.
Key takeaways

The SSC targets “simplex” families (one affected proband, unaffected parents and siblings) so that unaffected siblings serve as ideal controls for identifying truly de novo copy-number variants associated with autism; case-control designs are described as less compelling for this purpose.
The rationale rests on the observation that most autism is sporadic and that submicroscopic deletions/duplications (CNVs) present in probands but absent in parents/siblings contribute to risk, though any single de novo CNV is rare (occurring in 1% or fewer of autistic individuals).
The collection prioritizes careful, standardized clinical evaluation because meaningful genotype/phenotype correlation in psychiatric-genetics and neurodevelopment depends on accurate phenotype data.
The SSC is offered as a community simons-simplex-collection resource: DNA, plasma, transformed-lymphoblast cell lines, whole-genome CNV scans, and clinical data are distributed via the SFARI Base software platform.

Notable claims / data

More than 2000 simplex families were recruited across a coalition of ~14 university/hospital clinics (Michigan, Yale, Emory, Columbia, Vanderbilt, McGill/Washington, Harvard/Children’s Hospital Boston, Washington, Illinois-Chicago, Missouri, UCLA, Baylor).
Table 1 summarizes 1887 probands (1631 male, 256 male:female ~6:1); >80% of families include at least one unaffected sibling, and simplex architecture is less certain in trios than quads.
Probands showed relatively severe ASD with relatively little intellectual disability: mean nonverbal IQ ~87.6 (males) / 79.2 (females); ADOS Calibrated Severity Score mean ~7.4 on a 4–10 scale.
Inclusion window age 4–18; exclusions included nonverbal mental age below 18 months, severe neurological deficits, birth trauma/perinatal complications, and fragile X or Down syndrome.
Data quality was unusually high for a multisite study: error rate averaged <0.50 errors per 1000 data points, achieved via ADI-R/ADOS research-reliability training (4–6 months for untrained clinicians), videotape exchange, and logical cross-measure checks by a Michigan consultant team.
Traditional ASD subcategories (Autism vs Asperger vs PDD-NOS) were not useful for describing SSC probands, anticipating the unified “Autism Spectrum Disorders” classification proposed for DSM-V.
Privacy was handled via Global Unique Identifiers (GUIDs) enabling anonymous linkage to other databases such as the NIMH-maintained NDAR.

Connections

Positioned as extending earlier autism repositories including the Autism Genetic Resource Exchange (agre-cohort), the Autism Genome Project, and the NIMH repository, differing in collection, curation, and exclusion criteria.
The de novo variant framing connects to broader interest in noncoding-variants (the paper asks whether risk variants disrupt gene function directly or via regulatory elements in noncoding regions).
Foreshadows the Simons Variation in Individuals Project (Simons VIP), which studies recurrent 16p11.2 deletions/duplications and builds on lessons from assembling the SSC.

Open questions

Which de novo variants are truly autism risk factors — the paper frames the key tests as recurrence, disruption of gene function, number of genes involved, phenotype in mouse/other-species models, and effects of imprinting.
Whether high-throughput DNA sequencing (planned beyond the initial chip-based CNV scans) will reveal small de novo variants and candidate single-nucleotide risk factors below chip resolution.
Whether the multiplicity of variants converges on a few common signaling pathways (e.g., the synaptic membrane-protein system regulating excitation/inhibition balance).

wiki/sources/fu-2014-context-dependent-c.title: fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing
wiki/sources/fu-2014-context-dependent-c.content:
Context-dependent control of alternative splicing by RNA-binding proteins
One-line: A foundational Nature Reviews Genetics review (Fu & Ares) laying out the emerging “rules” by which sequence-specific RNA-binding proteins combinatorially and context-dependently control alternative splicing, and why a simple readable “splicing code” remains out of reach.
Key takeaways

The central thesis is that each alternative splicing event is governed by multiple rna-binding-proteins acting combinatorially, and because every cell type expresses a distinct array of RBPs, the regulatory meaning of any given RNA target sequence is exceedingly cell-type-dependent. RBPs also modulate one another’s binding activities on splicing-regulatory-elements.
There is no clean division of positive vs negative regulators: whether an RBP enhances or silences splicing depends heavily on the location of its binding site relative to the regulated exon (exon vs upstream-intron vs downstream-intron).
A truly readable “splicing code” is not yet attainable from genome sequence: the same short degenerate motif can be bound by multiple proteins/isoforms, the same protein reads the same sequence differently in different cell types, and machine-learning predictions are limited because bulk tissues superimpose splicing responses of multiple cell types.
splicing-regulatory-elements (SREs) are distinguished from the core splicing signals (5’SS, branchpoint, 3’SS) and are subdivided into ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS depending on exonic vs intronic location and enhancer vs silencer function.
Classic trans-acting splicing-factors are the SR proteins (generally exon-binding, purine-rich, positive) and the hnRNPs (e.g. hnrnpa1, ptbp1/PTB, generally repressive), but loss-of-function profiling shows roughly equal numbers of induced inclusion and skipping events for both classes.
Regulators ultimately act through the core spliceosome — by modulating snRNP assembly/maturation, competing for limiting machinery, blocking the exon-to-intron-definition transition, or modulating U1 dynamics.

Notable claims / data

“Exon definition”: a weak 3’SS is more efficiently recognized when a strong 5’SS lies within ~100-150 nucleotides downstream (U1 snRNP at 5’SS and U2 snRNP at 3’SS mutually enhance recognition).
Most regulatory studies focus on 200-300 nt adjacent to splice sites, but remote/distal regulatory sequences can be just as important (Rbfox RNA “bridges”), implying exome sequencing misses many disease-relevant deep-intronic SRE mutations.
Proteomic crosslinking studies indicate up to ~1,000 proteins can be directly crosslinked to mRNA in HeLa cells or mouse ES cells, many lacking canonical RNA-binding domains.
CLIP requires RNA and protein to be within ~1 Å for UV crosslinking; refinements include PAR-CLIP (higher crosslinking efficiency) and iCLIP (individual-nucleotide resolution). Table 1 catalogs CLIP-derived binding motifs for ~25 regulators (e.g. NOVA YCAY; RBFOX1/2 GCAUG; MBNL1/2 YGCY; PTB CU-rich; srsf1 GGAGGA; hnRNP A1 GNNAGN; U2AF65 pyrimidine-rich).
U1 snRNP is ~10-fold more abundant than other splicing snRNPs in mammalian cells; extended U1-5’SS base-pairing (induced by hnRNP L and hnRNP A1) can act as a repressive complex.
Position-polarity rule (first shown for NOVA): intronic binding upstream of an exon represses inclusion while binding downstream activates inclusion; mechanism still unresolved (tethered RBFOX1 work shows distinct domain requirements for activation vs repression).
hnRNP C directly competes with U2AF65 at Alu-associated 3’SSs to protect the transcriptome from Alu exonization.
Signaling-to-splicing: the Akt-SRPK-SR axis transduces EGF signaling to global splicing changes (RNAi of SRPK1/2 abolishes most EGF-induced events); SR protein phosphorylation is controlled by cytoplasmic SRPKs and nuclear CLKs; Ca2+/CaMK IV regulates splicing via the CaRRE motif and hnRNP L.
Caveat threaded throughout: minigene/reporter and in vitro selection results often fail to generalize because one reporter samples only one regulatory context; affinity purification shows SRE-to-RBP relationships are frequently not one-to-one (direct vs indirect interactions are hard to separate).

Connections

This is an upstream/foundational review for the wiki’s regulatory-biology cluster: it gives mechanistic grounding for rna-binding-proteins, splicing-factors, and splicing-regulatory-elements, and complements the other regulation reviews rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
The “splicing code” framing and its limits connect to computational/ML directions discussed in capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the and to the broader cataloguing of RBP motifs (CLIP/Bind-n-Seq) that underpins tools like spliceai.
Specific entities it discusses already have wiki pages: ptbp1, hnrnpa1, srsf1; the proteome-expansion theme links to nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome.
RNA-structure dependence of RBP access connects to RNA secondary structure / dscam-style competing-structure regulation and to G-quadruplex work in peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.

Open questions

What exactly is the molecular mechanism for the intronic position-polarity effect (why upstream = repress, downstream = activate), and which auxiliary proteins mediate it?
How can multi-RBP, cell-type-specific combinatorial codes be deconvolved from bulk tissue data so that a predictive splicing code becomes feasible?
How much disease-relevant regulatory information lies in distal/deep-intronic SREs that exome sequencing cannot capture, and can CRISPR genome engineering in native context map it?
How are RBPs with multiple jobs (splicing plus transcription/stability/localization, e.g. SRSF2 in Pol II pause release) functionally disentangled?
Do kinetic (vs thermodynamic) binding preferences of RBPs account for regulatory specificity currently missed by standard binding assays?

wiki/sources/fu-2022-rare-coding-variati.title: fu-2022-rare-coding-variation-provides
wiki/sources/fu-2022-rare-coding-variati.content:
Rare coding variation provides insight into the genetic architecture and phenotypic context of autism
One-line: Joint TADA analysis of rare PTVs, missense variants and CNVs across 63,237 individuals discovers 72 ASD genes (373 with developmental-delay meta-analysis) and maps them onto distinct fetal cortical cell types.
Key takeaways

Landmark autism rare-variant study (Autism Sequencing Consortium + Broad-CCDG + iPSYCH-BROAD) integrating protein-truncating, missense and copy-number variants at single-gene/exon resolution to sharpen psychiatric-genetics gene discovery.
Extends the Bayesian TADA model to include CNVs, unaffected-sibling de novo variants and gnomAD constraint (LOEUF), yielding 72 ASD genes at FDR ≤ 0.001 (185 at FDR ≤ 0.05).
Meta-analysis with developmental-delay (DD) cohorts partitions neurodevelopment risk genes along a temporal axis: DD-predominant genes act in less differentiated progenitor/immature neuronal-cell-types (neurogenesis-stage), ASD-predominant genes in maturing excitatory neurons.
ASD-predominant genes overlap schizophrenia-associated genes, supporting shared pathways across neuropsychiatric disorders.
Reinforces that most ASD heritability is common/polygenic, but rare de novo loss-of-function variation drives current gene discovery.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 63,237 ASD-cohort individuals (20,627 affected) + 91,605 DD-cohort samples (31,058 affected); 58.7% of family data previously unpublished.
Association-evidence composition: de novo PTVs 57.5%, damaging missense 21.1%, CNVs 8.44% — but CNVs conferred the greatest relative risk.
Exome CNV discovery (GATK-gCNV) benchmarked vs WGS: 86% sensitivity, 90% PPV for rare CNVs spanning >2 exons; 83% sens/97% PPV for de novo CNVs.
3.95% of ASD cases vs 1.39% of unaffected siblings carried a de novo coding CNV (OR 2.91, P = 2.2×10⁻²¹). De novo deletions of a constrained gene (LOEUF <0.4): 9.33-fold enrichment, comparable to a genomic-disorder segment deletion.
Female cases carried more de novo CNVs than males (6.0% vs 3.5%, OR 1.8) — analyses favor a female protective effect over ascertainment bias.
Maternal parent-of-origin bias for de novo CNVs restricted to NAHR-mediated genomic-disorder loci (16p11.2 ~95% maternal); non-NAHR de novo SVs showed paternal bias.
TADA-NDD joint analysis: 373 genes at FDR ≤ 0.001 (664 at FDR ≤ 0.05); 54 genes unique to the joint analysis (14% yield increase). De novo CNVs hitting a TADA-NDD gene: 134 in ASD cases vs 1 in siblings (OR 48.9).
Estimated cross-phenotype sharing: 70.1% of DD genes affect ASD; 86.6% of ASD genes affect DD. 36 ASD-predominant vs 82 DD-predominant genes (posterior >0.99). Example contrast: ARID1B de novo mutations ~7-fold more frequent in DD than ASD; DSCAM evidence is ASD-specific.
Top genes: SCN2A, SHANK3, CHD8, SYNGAP1, ADNP, PTEN, FOXP1, CHD2, POGZ, GRIN2B; PLXNA1 showed missense overtransmission localized to the Plexin domain.

Connections

Successor to and extends the ASC exome studies (e.g. su-2018-alternative-splicing-in-neurogenesis-and context of neurodevelopmental gene function) and complements the CHD8-focused developmental work in nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8.
Cell-type enrichment framework parallels prenatal cortical single-cell atlases used in velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal and disease-critical-cell-type methods in jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types.
Autism gene/expression context overlaps transcriptomic dysregulation work in gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna.
Constraint/rare-variant risk framing connects to heritability and missing-heritability discussions (manolio-2009, zuk-2012).

Open questions

How much additional ASD signal lies in inherited missense variants and intragenic/exon duplications that larger cohorts could resolve?
Can gene-level CNV analysis be operationalized for clinical diagnostic testing given deletion of a constrained gene rivals genomic-disorder segments?
Which of the pleiotropic ASD/DD genes are truly ASD-specific neurobiological drivers (e.g. DSCAM) versus broadly developmental (e.g. ARID1B)?
What mechanism underlies the maternal bias specific to NAHR-mediated genomic-disorder formation?

wiki/sources/fu-2025-single-cell-and-spa.title: fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative
wiki/sources/fu-2025-single-cell-and-spa.content:
Single cell and spatial alternative splicing analysis with Nanopore long read sequencing
One-line: Introduces longcell, a statistical/computational pipeline for isoform quantification from single-cell and spatially barcoded Nanopore long reads that recovers cell barcodes/UMIs, corrects sequencing/truncation/mapping errors, and models splicing heterogeneity within and between cells/spots.
Key takeaways

Long-read single-cell/spatial splicing analysis is hampered by Nanopore’s low per-base accuracy (85–99%), which corrupts UMI recovery and causes read truncation and misalignment; existing tools that ignore the UMI inflate isoform quantification. Longcell tackles these noise sources head-on.
Core preprocessing identifies and corrects UMI scattering — sequencing errors split observed UMIs from one true molecule into many small/singleton clusters, inflating UMI counts (worse at higher PCR amplification). Longcell clusters putative UMIs within meta-isoform groups, then adaptively prunes small clusters.
Uses the UMI cluster (PCR duplicates of one molecule) to build a consensus alignment, correcting wrongly mapped short internal exons (microexons) and 5’/3’ read truncations before isoform assignment.
Downstream, models per-cell percent-spliced-in (ψ) with a Beta-Binomial framework, decomposing exon-usage variation into intra-cell vs inter-cell heterogeneity via a mean-invariant dispersion metric φ; includes a generalized likelihood-ratio test for differential splicing between cell populations.
To avoid biases from incomplete annotations, analyses are run at the meta-splice-site level (splice sites grouped by co-variation across reads) rather than the isoform level.
Applications: spatial isoform switching (mouse olfactory bulb, CRCLM), widespread high intra-cell heterogeneity in highly expressed genes, and recovery of splicing-factors regulatory targets in a single-cell CRISPR perturbation screen validated by targeted sequencing.

Notable claims / data

Misalignment of short internal constitutive exons depressed bulk ψ to an average of 0.67 across 1,146 exons from 325 genes (read count > 30) in a colorectal metastasis sample; Longcell’s mapping correction raised it to 0.92 (expected ≈1). VPS28-201 example: a 29 bp exon misaligned in 20% of reads.
Simulation benchmark (220 cells, 1000 isoforms, 187 genes): Longcell achieved the highest per-cell Spearman correlation with gold standard (mean 0.76 / 0.80 / 0.82 for low/mid/high-quality data); pairing UMI preprocessing with IsoQuant (Longcell+IsoQuant) gave a 13.73% Spearman improvement over IsoQuant alone.
Full-transcriptome simulation (23,560 isoforms, 918 cells, R10 quality): Longcell+IsoQuant best (mean Spearman 0.87); Longcell and wf-single-cell tied second (0.85).
Library-matched PacBio–Nanopore benchmark (18 genes, 110 isoforms, 5,881 Jurkat cells): Longcell gave most accurate per-cell quantification vs Isoseq cross-validation (mean Spearman 0.43, +8.72% over Sicelore2).
MOB Illumina-guided benchmark: Longcell+IsoQuant best (mean Spearman 0.70, +20% over IsoQuant’s 0.58).
For highly expressed genes, most alternatively spliced exons show low φ (low inter-cell, high intra-cell heterogeneity) — isoforms co-expressed at similar ratio within each cell (e.g. RBIS, ZFAS1 at ~1:1). High-φ exceptions show cell-type-specific switching (MYL6 exon 6; Pkm exon 9 continuous switch along neuroblast→glutamatergic differentiation, Pkm-201→Pkm-202).
Spatial: MOB Plp1 isoform switch between olfactory bulb layers reproduced (312 meta-sites in 224 genes differentially spliced across layers, FDR 0.05); Mapre3 shows per-cell exclusive isoform choice uncorrelated with tissue layer.
CRISPR screen: gRNA library targeting 9 splicing factors in naïve/stimulated Jurkat (Cas9+). Detected 7 meta-sites/5 genes (naïve) and 11 sites/6 genes (stimulated) affected (FDR 0.05). HNRNPLL knockout promoted PTPRC exon 4/6 inclusion (PTPRC-214→PTPRC-209, corroborating known biology); novel: PCBP2→DGUOK (validated by targeted sequencing), CELF2→PTS and CELF2→ARHGEF1. Effect sizes correlated between stimulated/unstimulated (p=0.001).
Practical guidance: a mean PCR amplification fold of ~5 already enables consensus correction of many truncation/mapping errors — shallow amplification recommended.
Caveat: all metrics are reference-based; unannotated isoforms (e.g. inferred VIM-new) can confuse all algorithms — motivation for the meta-splice-site strategy.

Connections

longcell sits in the splicing-methods family alongside event/junction callers like rmats, leafcutter, and psi-sigma, but is specialized for single-cell/spatial long-read data and UMI-aware denoising — a niche the bulk tools and the short-read-oriented li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna (LeafCutter) do not cover.
Like LeafCutter, it adopts an annotation-light, sufficient-statistics philosophy (meta-splice-sites vs intron clusters) and a Beta/Dirichlet-style overdispersion model for differential splicing.
Benchmarked against / complementary to IsoQuant, Sicelore2, FLAMES, wf-single-cell, and PacBio Isoseq (all plain text — not yet wiki entities).
Connects alternative-splicing to splicing-factors regulation via single-cell CRISPR, and to spatial transcriptomics (10x Visium).

Open questions

How well does the Beta-Binomial φ metric scale to genes with very low per-cell coverage, where bootstrap CIs are wide?
Can de novo isoform discovery be integrated without the annotation biases illustrated by the VIM-new example?
What drives intra-layer isoform choice (e.g. Mapre3) that is uncorrelated with spatial domain — a regulatory program orthogonal to tissue identity?
Generalization beyond 10x/Visium and Nanopore R10 to other platforms claimed but not benchmarked here.

wiki/sources/fudenberg-2020-predicting-3.title: fudenberg-2020-predicting-3d-genome-folding
wiki/sources/fudenberg-2020-predicting-3.content:
Predicting 3D genome folding from DNA sequence with Akita
One-line: A convolutional neural network (akita) that predicts locus-specific 3D genome-architecture from ~1 Mb of DNA sequence alone, enabling in-silico mutagenesis and variant interpretation.
Key takeaways

akita transforms ~1 Mb (2^20 bp) of one-hot DNA into predicted Hi-C/Micro-C contact maps at 2,048-bp resolution, learning genome-architecture end-to-end from sequence rather than epigenomic inputs — a sequence-to-structure dna-language-models-style approach that, unlike earlier models, can predict effects of DNA variants.
Learned representations recover an orientation-specific grammar for CTCF sites: inverting all CTCF motifs redistributes rather than abolishes contact patterns, whereas mutagenizing them flattens maps — echoing loop-extrusion halting that underlies topologically-associating-domains and chromatin-loops.
Once trained, predictions run in seconds, enabling saturation mutagenesis, GTEx eQTL interpretation, and prediction of engineered structural variants (model-interpretability).

Notable claims / data

Held-out test-set performance: genome-wide 0.14 MSE, 0.61 Pearson R, 0.56 Spearman R — approaching the noise limit between experimental replicates.
Trained on five human datasets (HFF, H1ESC, GM12878, IMR90, HCT116); multi-task training beat single-dataset models. Training set: 7,008 sequences / 419 validation / 413 test.
CTCF motif mutagenesis had >3x the impact of any other JASPAR motif except CTCFL; 19.9% of high-impact single-nucleotide mutations fell outside standard annotation categories, indicating uncharacterized folding-relevant sequence.
SNPs with higher GTEx causal posterior probability showed significantly larger predicted disruptions, both inside and outside CTCF motifs.
Recapitulated a CRISPR/Cas9 ~25 kb boundary deletion at the Lmo2 locus (redundant CTCF sites) and a ~622 kb inversion at the Eph4A locus; cross-species, a human-trained model predicted mouse folding (median Spearman R 0.50), improving when B2 SINE CTCF sites were masked.

Connections

Directly extends the sequence-to-structure modeling lineage; complements che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome on how genome-architecture is organized and evolves.
Shares CNN-on-DNA methodology with sequence models of the dna-language-models family and offers model-interpretability via in-silico mutagenesis, analogous to interpretation methods used for splicing-code models.

Open questions

How to extend predictions beyond the ~1 Mb window to model A/B compartmentalization and inter-chromosomal contacts?
Can architectures that better encode loop-extrusion biophysics improve accuracy and cell-type specificity?
What are the CTCF-independent DNA determinants (the ~20% of high-impact non-annotated variants) of genome folding?

wiki/sources/furlanis-2019-landscape-of-.title: furlanis-2019-landscape-of-ribosome-engaged-transcript-isoforms
wiki/sources/furlanis-2019-landscape-of-.content:
Landscape of ribosome-engaged transcript isoforms reveals extensive neuronal-cell-class-specific alternative splicing programs
One-line: A genome-wide RiboTRAP map of ribosome-associated mRNA isoforms across genetically defined mouse forebrain neuron classes, showing that cell-type-specific alternative-splicing programs selectively remodel synaptic and intrinsic-property proteins to specify neuronal identity.
Key takeaways

Uses RiboTRAP (HA-tagged Rpl22 affinity purification of ribosome-engaged mRNAs) to isolate translated isoforms from cre-defined neocortical and hippocampal populations — Camk2 (CaMKII+ pyramidal), Scnn1a (L4), Sst / Pvalb / Vip interneurons, plus hippocampal CA1/CA3/Grik4/Sst — establishing a validated resource for neuronal-cell-types-specific isoforms.
Two complementary computational analyses (‘Exon’ differential exon usage and ‘Pattern’ splicing analysis over FAST DB annotations) identify hundreds of differentially regulated (DR) splicing events per cell class; RT-PCR validation >90%.
Neuronal transcript isoform profiles reliably distinguish even closely related neuronal-cell-types — e.g. Scnn1a-defined L4 cells carry 310 DR exons in 214 genes; MGE-derived Pvalb vs Sst interneurons differ by 628/719 exons; CGE Vip interneurons show 609 DR exons in 407 genes.
Cassette-exon inclusion is the most frequent DR event type; microexons (3–27 nt) are enriched (3.8–5.3% of DR exons vs 2.8% genome-wide); intron retention is elevated specifically in Vip interneurons.
~30–60% of DR transcript isoforms arise from alternative transcription start sites (TSSs) rather than alternative-splicing, implying cell-type-specific enhancers/promoters drive isoform diversity (e.g. Dlgap1, Rapgef5); alternative last exons (ALEs) modify 3′UTRs in ~20% of events (e.g. Ncam1), controlling localization/protein output.
Differential expression of a hand-curated set of 57 splicing-factors segregates neuron classes: Rbm20 in Pvalb, Ptbp1 in Vip, Rbfox3/NeuN in glutamatergic cells — these are candidate regulators of the cell-class splicing programs.
Splicing-regulated transcripts are overwhelmingly dedicated to synaptic interactions and intrinsic neuronal properties: GO enrichment (2–4 fold above differentially expressed genes) for adhesion complexes (Nrxn3, Nlgn1, Cntnap2), voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, presynaptic release machinery, postsynaptic receptor complexes, scaffolding proteins, potassium channels (Kcnq2, Kcnc2), and cytoskeletal/motor proteins.

Notable claims / data



12,000 genes detected per sample with full-length coverage; paired-end 100 bp, >100 M reads/replicate, n=4 biological replicates per population.


In N2A cells, overexpressed Ptbp1 shifts Gabrg2 and Kcnq2 reporter splicing toward the Vip pattern; Rbfox1/Rbfox3 shift toward the Scnn1a pattern — functionally linking specific splicing-factors to the endogenous cell-type splicing choices.
Splicing-dependent GO enrichment exceeds gene-expression-dependent enrichment, and is not merely a consequence of longer genes having more exons.
Splicing changes did not correlate with gene-expression level changes, indicating the analysis captures splicing regulation independent of transcript abundance.

Connections

A foundational cell-type resource for splicing-and-neurodevelopment and splicing-and-neurodevelopment wiki threads; complements bulk developmental-switch studies of neocortical splicing.
Its splicing-factors-to-cell-type logic (Rbfox, Ptbp1, Nova, Rbm20) and microexon enrichment connect to microexon-regulation work like bonnal-2025-core-splicing-architecture-and-early (SRRM3/4 microexon control) in this ingest group.
Reinforces that rna-binding-proteins terminal differentiation programs, layered on transcription-factor codes, specify neuronal subclasses.
The public isoform look-up tool (scheiffele-splice.scicore.unibas.ch) is a candidate entity resource page.

Open questions

Which distal enhancers/promoters drive the extensive cell-type alternative-TSS usage, and how are they coordinated with downstream splicing?
To what extent do the mapped candidate splicing-factors causally establish (vs merely correlate with) each neuron class’s splicing program in vivo?
How much of the ribosome-engaged isoform diversity is functionally distinct at the protein level for synaptic wiring vs buffered/redundant?

wiki/sources/gallo-2014-glial-developmen.title: gallo-2014-glial-development-crossroads-regeneration
wiki/sources/gallo-2014-glial-developmen.content:
Glial Development: The Crossroads of Regeneration and Repair in the CNS
One-line: A review arguing that the signaling programs governing developmental astrocyte and oligodendrocyte generation are reused during CNS injury repair and regeneration.
Key takeaways

Glia comprise at least 50% of CNS cells and participate in all injury/disease responses; because regeneration often recapitulates development, developmental gliogenesis illuminates glial repair, complementing our knowledge of neurodevelopment and neurogenesis.
Astrocyte specification hinges on a Sox9/NFIA transcriptional cascade at the “gliogenic switch” (best defined in ventral spinal cord), downstream of Shh and Notch; differentiation is marked by GFAP induction driven by CNTF/LIF/CT-1–gp130–JAK/STAT3, plus Notch and BMP signaling.
Reactive astrocytes (GFAP+, hypertrophic, proliferative) are neither simply “good” nor “bad”: the glial scar walls off damage yet inhibits axon regrowth via CSPGs; ablation studies show they are required for repair.
Oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs) arise from multiple ventral (pMN/Olig2) and dorsal domains in spinal cord and sequential forebrain waves (MGE→LGE→cortex); postnatal SVZ and parenchymal OPCs regenerate myelinating OLs after injury.
Distinct glial subtypes reflect embryonic patterning, echoing how developmental origin shapes neuronal-cell-types; astrocyte lineage stages remain poorly defined for lack of markers.

Notable claims / data

Glia-to-neuron ratio: ~1:6 in C. elegans (56 glia : 352 neurons) vs 1:1–4:1 in vertebrates; a single hippocampal astrocyte contacts ~100,000 synapses (Bushong et al., 2002).
Up to 55% of early postnatal cortical GFAP+ astrocytes are generated after migrating out of germinal centers (Ge et al., 2012).
Nine morphologically distinct adult astrocyte classes identified via GFAP/s100b reporters (Emsley & Macklis, 2006).
SVZ OPC proliferation after hypoxia depends on Cdk4; parenchymal/WM OPC proliferation depends on Cdk2/CyclinE (G1-S transition); dorsally derived OPCs generate 10–50% of dorsal OLs.
SCI conditional KOs of BMPR1a vs BMPR1b have opposing effects on scar formation, gliosis, and recovery; STAT3 deletion in astrocytes impairs scar formation and functional recovery.
Caveat: reactive-astrocyte “NSC-like” neurogenic potential rests on in vitro assays prone to Shh-Olig2 deregulation; in vivo lineage tracing still needed. Review excludes microglia (distinct developmental origin).

Connections

Extends developmental frameworks in stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain and duan-2008-development-of-neural-stem toward the glial (non-neuronal) lineage.
Complements su-2018-alternative-splicing-in-neurogenesis-and on molecular control of neural cell-fate transitions.
Relevant to radial-glia as the shared neural-stem-cell origin feeding both neurogenesis and gliogenesis.

Open questions

Do reactive astrocytes genuinely dedifferentiate into neurogenic NSCs in vivo, or only in culture?
How do injury type, location, and astrocyte heterogeneity jointly dictate beneficial vs detrimental repair outcomes?
Which developmental OPC-proliferation pathways (Cdk2/Cdk4, Wnt, p27Kip1, EGF) are the best therapeutic levers for remyelination after perinatal white matter injury?

wiki/sources/gandal-2022-broad-transcrip.title: gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across
wiki/sources/gandal-2022-broad-transcrip.content:
Broad transcriptomic dysregulation occurs across the cerebral cortex in ASD
One-line: Pan-cortical bulk RNA-seq (725 samples, 11 cortical areas) plus snRNA-seq and methylation profiling showing that autism’s molecular pathology is widespread across the cortex — not confined to frontotemporal association areas — follows an anterior-to-posterior gradient peaking in primary visual cortex, attenuates normal regional identity, and reflects cell-type-specific (mainly excitatory-neuron and glial) gene and isoform dysregulation onto which rare and common ASD risk converge.
Key takeaways

Bulk rna-seq of 725 brain samples spanning 11 cortical areas from 112 post-mortem donors (49 idiopathic ASD, 54 neurotypical controls, plus dup15q cases) — a >3-fold increase in samples and regions over prior frontotemporal studies. Extends the developing/diseased-brain transcriptomics program of the lab (brain-development-neurogenomics).
ASD shows widespread transcriptomic change across the entire cortex with an anterior-to-posterior gradient: the greatest differences are in primary visual cortex (BA17), coincident with attenuation of the normal transcriptomic differences between cortical regions (attenuation of regional identity, “ARI”).
Splitting differential expression by feature type reveals distinct transcript- (isoform-) level signals beyond gene-level signals, with transcript changes of greater effect-size magnitude — supporting a substantial role for alternative-splicing and isoform expression in ASD (splicing-and-disease). Co-expression networks built on isoform quantifications “reflect both alternative splicing and alternative promoter usage.”
Single-nucleus RNA-seq (>250,000 nuclei, 6 ASD / 6 control, frontal/parietal/occipital) and methylation cell-type deconvolution show the bulk signature is driven mainly by within-cell-type gene-expression changes (especially excitatory neurons and glia), not by shifts in cell-type proportions.
