wiki/sources/jacobs-2025-widespread-natu.title: jacobs-2025-widespread-naturally-variable-human-exons
wiki/sources/jacobs-2025-widespread-natu.content:
Widespread naturally variable human exons aid genetic interpretation
One-line: Defines and catalogs “naturally variable exons” (NVEs) — exons included in some individuals’ mRNAs but entirely excluded in others — from GTEx transcriptomes of 838 people, showing they are widespread, often unannotated, enriched in constrained genes, and useful for interpreting genetic variants.
Key takeaways

Introduces naturally variable exons (NVEs): cassette/skipped exons or alternative splice sites whose splicing is present in some individuals but absent in others (Ψ ≥ 5% in >1% but <99% of people), a distinct category from “canonical” alternative-splicing (spliced in nearly everyone). This captures human population-level splicing variation.
Identified 57,271 unique NVEs (414,141 NVE-tissue pairs) across 49 tissues; NVEs impact ~75% of protein-coding genes with a median of 3 NVEs per gene, and an individual has ~13,100 on average.
A Bayesian / beta-binomial mixture-of-3-betas model de-noises per-individual Ψ estimates and defines the summary statistic exon frequency (EF) — the estimated proportion of the population that splices the NVE above threshold (analogous to allele frequency).
61% of NVEs are absent from comprehensive reference annotations (GENCODE 45); low-EF NVEs are predominantly (73%) unannotated, high-EF NVEs ~31% unannotated. NVEs expand the covered transcriptome sequence space by ~4 MB.
EF distribution is U-shaped; most low-EF NVEs occur in coding regions (cdsNVEs), while high-EF NVEs are enriched in 5′ UTRs (consistent with recently-evolved exons being 5′-UTR-biased and expression-associated — see splicing-and-evolution).
NVEs preferentially occur in mutationally-constrained genes (lower LOEUF = greater intolerance to loss-of-function), suggesting selection and a role in gene regulation; constrained genes favor lower-Ψ NVEs.
NVEs aid GWAS interpretation: they are enriched among fine-mapped causal variants (above synonymous, below missense), and unannotated NVE splice-region variants add interpretive power; example ASGR1 synonymous variant is both an sQTL and eQTL colocalizing with an NVE affecting cholesterol/heart phenotypes.
Low-Ψ NMD-triggering NVEs (nmdNVEs) can have outsized effects on gene expression: 55% of NVEs and 68% of cdsNVEs are nmdNVEs, and their splicing shifts mRNA output toward unproductive isoforms, reducing expression (relevant to intron-retention/poison-exons-style regulation).

Notable claims / data

Input: ~14,000 GTEx samples, 838 individuals, 49 tissues. 28,694 alternative-splice-site NVEs + 28,577 skipped-exon NVEs.
Fraction of NVEs shared between two unrelated individuals estimated at 28%; median ~3,500 NVEs per tissue.
NVE splice-site motifs match cognate exon motifs closely; MaxEnt splice-site scores of NVEs tend to be slightly weaker than their cognate sites, with the gap shrinking at higher EF.
60% of NVEs are associated with ≥1 sQTL (cis), consistent with GTEx splicing being mostly cis-driven; high-confidence (≥90% PIP) sQTLs preferentially locate near splice sites.
For CDS alternative-splice-site NVEs with NMD potential, sQTL and eQTL effect sizes are negatively correlated across all EF thresholds — supporting expression modulation via productive/unproductive mRNA switching even at low observed Ψ.
Constrained (low-LOEUF) genes show stronger NVE enrichment (KS test p ~10^-212); nmdNVEs tend to have lower Ψ in constrained genes (selection against large expression perturbations).

Connections

Extends alternative-splicing and splicing-qtl work to population-frequency framing (EF as splicing’s “allele frequency”); complements splicevault/cryptic-exon and unannotated-splicing efforts.
The nmdNVE / expression-coupling mechanism connects to nonsense-mediated decay regulation, poison-exons, and dosage control — relevant to splicing-and-disease.
5′-UTR / evolutionary bias of high-EF NVEs ties to splicing-and-evolution (recently-evolved exons).
Enrichment in constrained genes and utility for fine-mapping links splicing variation to complex-trait / disease genetics (genome-wide-association-study, UK Biobank / FinnGen / Biobank Japan fine-mapping).
Conceptually related to dent-2025-a-basic-framework-to-explain (both use GTEx to quantify population splicing variation; NVEs are event-level, splice-site-usage there is site-level).

Open questions

How would NVE evolutionary ages and cross-population differences resolve whether low-EF NVEs are transient noise vs early-stage adaptive exons (needs non-GTEx populations)?
What are the protein-level consequences of cdsNVEs that yield novel isoforms without NMD (not explored here)?
Can a genome-wide NVE atlas across all tissues/cell types (vs one tissue per NVE) reveal additional tissue-restricted or condition-specific splicing variation?

wiki/sources/jagadeesh-2022-identifying-.title: jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types
wiki/sources/jagadeesh-2022-identifying-.content:
Identifying disease-critical cell types and cellular processes by integrating single-cell RNA-sequencing and human genetics
One-line: Introduces sc-linker, a framework that links GWAS disease heritability to cell types and cellular processes by turning scRNA-seq-derived gene programs into SNP annotations (via tissue-specific enhancer–gene maps) and scoring them with stratified LD-score regression (S-LDSC).
Key takeaways

A functional-single-cell-genomics method connecting single-cell expression to human disease genetics: scRNA-seq → gene programs → SNP annotations (through enhancer–gene links) → heritability enrichment via S-LDSC, conditional on the baseline-LD model.
Three families of gene programs capture different facets of cells: (1) cell-type programs (genes specific to one cell type vs others), (2) disease-dependent programs (same cell type in disease vs healthy tissue), and (3) cellular process programs (genes co-varying within or across cell types, recovered by non-negative matrix factorization / NMF).
Key methodological claim: programs are mapped to SNPs using tissue-specific enhancer–gene links (Roadmap Enhancer-Gene Linking and the Activity-by-Contact / ABC model) rather than naive gene windows; tissue specificity of both the program and the enhancer–gene strategy was necessary for correct linking.
Analyzes a single tissue in isolation can mislead (e.g. an enteric neural gene looks colon-specific until CNS cells are added), motivating a multi-tissue framework.
Recovered known biology (proof of principle on blood-cell traits) and surfaced novel, biologically plausible cell–disease links, including GABA-ergic neurons in major depressive disorder, a disease-dependent M-cell program in ulcerative colitis, and a disease-specific complement cascade in multiple sclerosis.
For autoimmune disease, both healthy and disease-dependent immune-cell programs were enriched, but only disease-dependent epithelial programs were prominent — suggesting epithelial cells act in disease response/development rather than initiation.

Notable claims / data

Scale: 17 scRNA-seq datasets across 11 tissues + 6 disease conditions (MS, AD, ulcerative colitis, asthma, IPF, COVID-19); 209 individuals, 1,602,614 cells, 256 annotated cell subsets; 60 GWAS traits (genetic correlation <0.9, average N ≈ 297,000).
Benchmarking: sc-linker outperformed MAGMA gene-set analysis on a sensitivity/specificity index across five blood-cell traits matched to immune cell types (erythroid→RBC count, megakaryocytes→platelet count, monocytes→monocyte count, B/T cells→lymphocyte %).
Brain/psychiatric (prefrontal cortex, k=73,191, n=10): MDD and BMI enriched specifically in GABA-ergic neurons; insomnia, schizophrenia, intelligence in glutamatergic neurons; neuroticism in both. MDD GABA-ergic E-score 4.0 (P=1.0×10⁻⁴); top genes TCF4 (rank 1), PCLO (rank 4). Finer NMF programs localized signal to neuron subtypes (MDD→SST+/PVALB+; BMI→LAMP5+/VIP+).
Disease-dependent programs differed substantially from healthy or disease cell-type programs alone (avg Pearson r=0.16 healthy, 0.29 disease). MS: enrichment in disease-dependent GABA-ergic neurons and microglia (microglia top genes MERTK rank 2, TREM2 rank 4); AD microglia disease-dependent E-score 9.1 (PICALM, APOE, APOC1).
Reproducibility: results recapitulated in independent datasets — immune programs avg correlation 0.78 (same cell type) and disease enrichments 0.86 (E-score); brain programs 0.77 same-type / −0.21 otherwise.
Caveats stated by authors: cell types/states not exhaustive; enhancer–gene links limited to Roadmap + ABC; focus is genome-wide heritability rather than individual loci; the sensitivity/specificity index can penalize biologically real “other” enrichments.

Connections

A core functional-single-cell-genomics integration method; complements single-cell-only or GWAS-only approaches by joining them through epigenomic enhancer–gene maps.
Brain/psychiatric findings (GABA-ergic neurons in MDD; microglia in MS/AD) are relevant to developing-brain and neuropsychiatric-genetics siblings such as walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis.
Contrasts with gene-window gene-set methods (MAGMA, RSS-E, LDSC-SEG) by substituting enhancer–gene SNP linking.

Open questions

Can finer/more exhaustive cell states and improved enhancer–gene-linking strategies (beyond Roadmap/ABC) sharpen or shift the enrichments?
Which specific causal genes and loci (rather than genome-wide heritability) drive each program–disease link, and can perturbation experiments validate them?
Does the epithelial “response not initiation” pattern generalize across more autoimmune diseases and tissues?

wiki/sources/jbara-2023-rbfox2-modulates.title: jbara-2023-rbfox2-modulates-a-metastatic-signature
wiki/sources/jbara-2023-rbfox2-modulates.content:
RBFOX2 modulates a metastatic signature of alternative splicing in pancreatic cancer
One-line: In pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, where no driver mutation explains metastasis, the splicing factor rbfox2 is identified as a metastatic suppressor whose loss unleashes a pro-metastatic alternative-splicing program in RHO-GTPase / cytoskeletal genes (notably mprip).
Key takeaways

Analysis of alternative-splicing across 395 primary and metastatic PDA tumours defined a differential-splicing signature that separated tumours by metastatic stage far better than gene-expression clustering — establishing splicing as a metastasis-associated regulatory layer independent of driver mutations (KRAS/SMAD4/CDKN2A/TP53).
De novo motif analysis of metastasis-associated events was enriched for the rbfox2 binding motif (GCAUG), nominating this RNA-binding protein / splicing factor as the regulator.
rbfox2 acts as a metastatic suppressor: overexpression in a metastatic PDX cell line sharply reduced migration, colony formation and lung metastasis, while CRISPR knockout / EIJ-sgRNA silencing in primary PDA lines increased metastatic potential — without changing proliferation.
RBFOX2 protein (not mRNA) is lower in metastatic than primary patient samples, owing to enhanced protein degradation — contrasting with oncogenic splicing factors srsf1/SRSF6 that are upregulated in many cancers.
RBFOX2 target genes are enriched in RHO-GTPase pathways governing cytoskeletal organization and focal adhesion; its RRM (RNA-recognition motif) domain is required for tumour-suppressor activity.
The key effector event is inclusion vs skipping of mprip exon 23: RBFOX2 promotes inclusion; the skipped isoform (more abundant in metastases, lower PSI) is oncogenic and correlates with worse survival.

Notable claims / data

~8,000 significant differential splicing events between primary and metastatic groups; 114 RBFOX2-dependent events regulated reciprocally by overexpression and knockout. Event types: single-exon skipping 45%, alt 5’ ss 16%, alt 3’ ss 14%, mutually exclusive exons 7%, multiple exon skipping 9%, intron retention 9%. The GCAUG motif was over-represented upstream of the 5’ splice site of dependent exons.
Of 87 RBFOX2-regulated genes, 35 were previously reported RBFOX2 targets and 37 were differentially spliced in patients; overlap enriched in RHOA/CDC42/RAC1 pathways.
RBFOX2(ΔRRM) failed to rescue the metastatic phenotype, showing the RRM (and thus direct splicing activity) is essential; epistasis experiments modulating three targets (MPRIP, MYL6, CLSTN1) simultaneously showed no additive effect.
mprip exon 23: low-PSI (skipped) tumours had median survival ~52 months shorter (hazard ratio 8.66). Forcing exon 23 inclusion via a CRISPR sgRNA (DS-24) that disrupts the intronic GCAUG RBFOX2 motif reduced migration and lung metastasis. The exon-23-skipped MPRIP isoform has an AlphaFold-predicted C-terminal α-helix that reroutes binding toward RAF-MAP kinase / cell-cycle proteins.
RHO-pathway inhibitors MBQ167 (dual RAC/CDC42, mbq167) and azathioprine (RAC1 blocker) selectively inhibited migration and lung metastasis of RBFOX2-depleted cells — proposed therapeutic candidates for metastatic PDA.

Connections

A cancer counterpart to the neuronal rbfox2 / RBFOX-family splicing logic; complements the developmental RBP review baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (which discusses RBFOX-depleted neurons).
Parallels inoue-2019-spliceosomal-disruption-of-the-non-canonical: both show a single splicing factor driving cancer through specific mis-splicing, and both point toward splicing-targeted therapy (splicing-therapeutics).
Co-authored by Adrian Krainer (of spliceai/smn2 antisense-oligonucleotide fame); links percent-spliced-in quantification (PSI-sigma) to clinical outcome.
Reinforces splicing-and-disease and the theme that splicing, not expression, encodes tumour-progression state.

Open questions

What drives the selective post-translational degradation of RBFOX2 protein in metastatic cells, and can it be blocked to restore tumour suppression?
Do the RBFOX2-regulated cytoskeletal splicing events generalize to other metastatic carcinomas beyond PDA?
Can RHO-pathway inhibitors (MBQ167, azathioprine) or direct correction of MPRIP exon-23 splicing translate to patient benefit?

wiki/sources/jeste-2014-disentangling-th.title: jeste-2014-disentangling-the-heterogeneity-of-autism
wiki/sources/jeste-2014-disentangling-th.content:
Disentangling the heterogeneity of autism spectrum disorder through genetic findings
One-line: A clinical review arguing that genetic findings can parse the phenotypic heterogeneity of autism spectrum disorder into more homogeneous, treatment-relevant subgroups.
Key takeaways

Frames autism as “the autisms”—a heterogeneous group whose biological variability can be dissected using psychiatric-genetics, moving from gene to brain to behaviour.
Argues that rare, large-effect mutations (not common variants) are the most robustly identified genetic risks, and that single-gene (Mendelian) disorders offer tractable models of ASD neurodevelopment and drug targets.
Reviews three organizing themes: single-gene disorders (TSC, fragile X, Rett, neurofibromatosis), the male sex bias / female-protective effect, and neurological comorbidities (epilepsy, motor impairment, sleep disturbance).
Emphasizes the heritability of ASD (recognized since the earliest twin studies) and that endophenotypes preceding diagnosis could feed back to identify common genetic syndromes.

Notable claims / data

Contributory mutations identified in >20% of individuals with ASD; 10–20% carry de novo mutations detectable by current testing; no single mutation accounts for >1% of cases (extreme genetic heterogeneity).
Male:female ratios range 1.33:1 to 15.7:1, dropping toward 1:1 in severely intellectually disabled populations; female-protective effect (FPE) supported by greater genetic load in affected females and higher symptom scores among siblings of female probands.
Neurological comorbidity prevalences: insomnia 53–78%; epilepsy 5–46% (epileptiform EEG abnormalities up to 60%); ASD in up to 50% of TSC children.
TSC (TSC1/TSC2) upregulates mTORC1; mTOR inhibitors (e.g., rapamycin) reverse cognitive/social deficits in mouse models; everolimus FDA-approved (2010) for subependymal giant cell astrocytoma in TSC.
Clinical genetic-testing guidance: chromosomal microarray (aCGH or SNP array) as first-tier for all ASD; ACMG-estimated diagnostic yield ~40%, with clinical exome sequencing adding an estimated 15–25%.
Sibling recurrence risk highly variable: ~6% average (Danish twin study) up to 32% for a next male child in a family with two affected boys.

Connections

Provides clinical-genetics context for autism as a wiki topic and complements molecular/transcriptomic ASD work in the brain-development-neurogenomics bucket (e.g., schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism, gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across, parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna).
The heritability / rare-vs-common-variant framing links to missing-heritability and twin-and-family-study-design discussions in psychiatric-genetics.
CHD8 and other high-confidence ASD risk genes discussed elsewhere (nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8) exemplify the single-gene-disorder-to-mechanism strategy this review advocates.

Open questions

Can genetic classifiers for diagnosis/prognosis/treatment stratification be validated once sample sizes are adequate, without population-structure confounding?
Are sex differences in ASD driven by biological mechanisms (FPE, sex chromosomes, fetal testosterone) or ascertainment/diagnostic bias?
Does early, mechanism-based or antiepileptic intervention (e.g., prophylactic vigabatrin in TSC) improve long-term cognitive/social outcomes?

wiki/sources/jovasevic-2024-formation-me.title: jovasevic-2024-formation-memory-assemblies-through-dna-sensing
wiki/sources/jovasevic-2024-formation-me.content:
Formation of memory assemblies through the DNA-sensing TLR9 pathway
One-line: In mice, learning drives persistent double-stranded DNA breaks in discrete hippocampal CA1 neurons that trigger a TLR9 innate-immune inflammatory pathway required to recruit those neurons into memory circuits.
Key takeaways

Contextual fear conditioning (CFC) induces, hours after learning, discrete clusters of excitatory CA1 neurons with persistent double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) breaks, nuclear envelope ruptures, and perinuclear release of histone and dsDNA fragments — a form of learning-related genomic stress distinct from the fast, minutes-scale DNA breaks previously tied to immediate-early gene induction.
The released extranuclear dsDNA activates the endosomal DNA sensor TLR9 and its downstream NF-κB signalling, giving a subset of neurons an inflammatory phenotype. This links neuronal activation to innate immune sensing rather than the classical neuronal-activation/immediate-early-gene program.
Neuron-specific knockout/knockdown of Tlr9 in CA1 impairs context memory and blunts CFC-induced gene expression changes, showing the inflammatory response is causal for memory formation, not a side effect of DNA damage.
TLR9 has an essential role in centrosome function — DNA damage repair, ciliogenesis, and build-up of perineuronal nets — positioning the centrosome as a hub for memory-related DNA repair.
Sensing is specifically TLR9-mediated: the cGAS–STING pathway is dispensable (Sting1-knockout mice and cGAS–STING inhibitors leave memory intact).

Notable claims / data

Bulk hippocampal RNA-seq contrasting recent (96 h) vs remote (21 days) memory found 847 differentially expressed genes (440 up); 96 h profile dominated by immune-response genes for nucleic-acid sensing and cytokine release (71 up, 11 down), with TLR9/NF-κB the most prominent functional cluster.
Extranuclear dsDNA isolated after CFC: 53 cloned fragments from 25 unique genomic sequences, all genomic (none mitochondrial), predominantly non-coding, sized 50–300 bp; frequency of CG-containing (putative TLR9-activating) sequences rose transiently from 33% to 77%.
γH2AX-marked dsDNA breaks were neuron-specific (absent in astrocytes/microglia). Sharp nuclear foci peaked 1–3 h; large (≥4 µm) fuzzy foci persisted 6–96 h and co-localized with centrosome markers (centrin, γ-tubulin) and the NHEJ mediator 53BP1 (non-homologous-end-joining), indicating pericentrosomal DNA repair.
DDR and immediate-early-gene populations were largely non-overlapping: only 20% of γH2AX+ nuclei were Fos+; γH2AX+ neurons showed significantly smaller Fos responses upon memory reactivation than PRAM–GFP-labelled (Fos-promoter) neurons.
Neuron-specific Tlr9 deletion (AAV9 Syn-cre in Tlr9 fl/fl mice) reduced freezing across context tests (day 1/7/28) and impaired trace fear conditioning, but left hippocampus-independent delay fear conditioning intact; replicated with Syn-driven Tlr9 shRNA. Astrocytic (GFAP-cre) and microglial (CSF1R-inhibitor depletion) manipulations did not reproduce the deficit.
snRNA-seq (29 clusters; highest diversity among 12 excitatory CA1 clusters) showed Tlr9-KO abolished CFC-induced gene expression; conserved CFC-induced genes involved ER chaperones/vesicle trafficking supporting TLR9 folding (HSP90B1) and vesicle acidification (ATP6V0C), and were stronger in doublecortin-positive (DCX+) neurons.
Pharmacology/DNase manipulations: TLR9 antagonist ODN2088 impaired CFC; Dnase2 knockdown (blocking generation of TLR9-activating fragments) impaired memory; TREX1 overexpression / cGAS–STING inhibitors (RU-521, H-151) and Sting1 loss had no effect.

Connections

A topical outlier for this section: it ties learning-induced neuronal-activation to innate immune DNA sensing and non-homologous-end-joining DNA repair in adult neurons.
The authors frame compromised TLR9 as a gateway to genomic instability underlying neurodegeneration and cognitive impairment relevant to psychiatric-genetics and accelerated senescence.
The DCX+ neuron findings connect to adult neurogenesis debates in the hippocampus.

Open questions

What determines which discrete CA1 neurons undergo sustained dsDNA breaks and the inflammatory TLR9 fate versus the immediate-early-gene fate?
How does centrosome-localized DDR mechanistically drive ciliogenesis and perineuronal-net formation that stabilize memory circuits?
Does the same TLR9 memory mechanism operate in human neurons, and can maintaining TLR9 signalling integrity prevent neurocognitive decline as the authors propose?
Why is TLR9 required but cGAS–STING dispensable, given both sense cytosolic dsDNA?

wiki/sources/kalsotra-2011-functional-co.title: kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated
wiki/sources/kalsotra-2011-functional-co.content:
Functional consequences of developmentally regulated alternative splicing
One-line: A 2011 Nature Reviews Genetics review surveying how regulated alternative splicing networks reshape transcriptomes during development, differentiation, and cell stress across yeast, worms, flies, and vertebrates, and what physiological consequences those splicing transitions actually have.
Key takeaways

Frames the central question of the field: of the ~19,000 alternatively spliced human genes, what fraction of mRNA isoforms have a real functional impact? Much diversity is low-abundance, non-conserved stochastic noise, but a growing set of transitions are physiologically determinative.
Distinguishes two roles for alternative-splicing: determinative (the splicing change itself drives the biological outcome) versus permissive (splicing provides mRNA/protein variability that a downstream mechanism then uses).
Splicing transitions accompanying physiological change are disproportionately conserved (>40% of heart/muscle transitions conserved between mammals and birds, vs <20% genome-wide human-mouse), arguing for functional importance.
Genome-wide studies repeatedly find two largely separate gene sets: one regulated mainly at the level of total mRNA expression, the other mainly at the level of splice-isoform choice without changing total output.
A splicing regulatory network is defined as the set of events directly regulated by one RNA-binding protein; ~50 mammalian auxiliary regulators are characterized, each governing hundreds of events but determinative for only a subset.
Reviews concrete networks across biology: yeast meiosis (Mer1/Nam8/Tgs1), cell cycle/apoptosis (SRSF1, BCL-X, MCL1), stem-cell pluripotency (OCT4, RBFOX2, PTB/nPTB), EMT (SRSF1, ESRP1/2, FGFR2), heart development (CELF/MBNL/SR switch), brain development (DSCAM1, ROBO3, NOVA, RBFOX1), and stress responses (heat shock, genotoxic stress).

Notable claims / data

Alternative splicing affects ~90% of human genes, ~60% of Drosophila genes, ~25% of C. elegans genes (RNA-seq estimates).
In Drosophila development (27 stages) >60% of alternative splicing undergoes developmental change; in C. elegans (17 stages) >30% does, often in coordinated co-regulated sets.
Budding yeast: only 5% of genes (290 of 6,000) have introns, but introns are enriched in highly expressed genes — >1/4 of the ~38,000 RNAs transcribed per hour are spliced. ~45 intron-containing genes are inefficiently spliced in vegetative growth; 13 of 20 intron-containing meiotic genes are efficiently spliced only during meiosis. At least three overlapping meiotic splicing programs (Mer1 activates 4 single-intron genes; Nam8- and Tgs1-dependent subnetworks).
Cell cycle: a genome-wide RNAi screen with BCL-X/MCL1 splicing reporters found knockdown of 52 genes induced pro-apoptotic splicing of both; aurora kinase A loss degrades SRSF1; importantly the splicing response preceded mitotic arrest (parallel, not secondary).
ESCs: splice-isoform diversity is high in undifferentiated cells and decreases on differentiation. OCT4 yields three isoforms (OCT4A/B/B1) with distinct functions; RBFOX2 is essential for human ESC viability but not for differentiated/transformed cells. PTB represses neuronal nPTB via NMD-coupled exon-10 skipping; miR-124 silences PTB — first miRNA→splicing-network hierarchy.
EMT: SRSF1 upregulation produces pro-invasive ΔRON (RON exon-11 skipping); ESRP1/2 downregulation switches CD44v→CD44s (essential for EMT); FGFR2 IIIb/IIIc mutually exclusive choice is steered by ESRPs, RBFOX2, and PTB recruited via the H3K36me3 mark through adaptor MRG15 — linking the histone code to splicing.
Heart: postnatal development shows three temporally coordinated splicing-transition sets; >40% conserved mammal-to-bird. CELF1/CELF2 drop >10-fold postnatally while MBNL rises ~4-fold; forcing CELF gain + MBNL loss in adult hearts reverts >half the transitions to the embryonic pattern. miR-23a/miR-23b drive the >10-fold CELF1/CELF2 downregulation (Dicer1 deletion re-expresses fetal isoforms). SR-protein KOs (SRSF1, SRSF2 conditional → dilated cardiomyopathy by ~8 weeks; SRSF10 constitutive → mid-gestation lethality) each misregulate only a few unique transcripts.
Brain — permissive: Drosophila Dscam1 produces >19,000 extracellular variants; individual neurons express 14-50 stochastically; a single isoform suffices for homophilic repulsion but >1,000 isoforms are required population-wide for self/non-self discrimination. ROBO3’s two isoforms act as a binary midline-crossing switch (spatial restriction is post-transcriptional, not splicing-based).
Brain — determinative: NOVA regulates ~700 splicing events; uses a conserved YCAY positional activity code shared with Drosophila Pasilla; NOVA2 KO disrupts Dab1/reelin-pathway splicing causing neocortical migration defects. RBFOX1 (A2BP1) controls neuronal excitation; linked to epilepsy, intellectual disability, and autism.
Stress: heat shock inhibits splicing partly via rapid SRSF10 dephosphorylation (reversed within ~1 h) and partly via nuclear stress bodies (peak ~3 h, reversed over 10-12 h). Genotoxic stress: UV-induced MDM2/MDM4 exon skipping removes the p53-binding region (activating p53); two co-transcriptional mechanisms — reduced EWS-RNAPII coupling (more skipping) and slowed RNAPII elongation (more inclusion).
AS-NMD (alternative splicing coupled to nonsense-mediated decay): general mammalian rule — a stop codon >50 nt upstream of the last intron triggers NMD. SR and hnRNP gene families autoregulate via ultraconserved-element AS-NMD switches with “polar” feedback (SR activators: activation→NMD; hnRNP repressors: repression→NMD).
Caveat (Box 3): many RBP binding sites may be non-functional “sinks” that sequester the regulator rather than directing splicing — paralleling the miRNA-target paradox. Cell-heterogeneity caveat: <20% of heart cells are cardiomyocytes, so whole-tissue splicing transitions can be ambiguous; single-cell transcriptomics is proposed as the fix.

Connections

Foundational/contextual companion to existing review-type sources in this wiki: lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — this 2011 review predates and underpins much of their framing of regulatory networks and physiological splicing.
Deep on splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins and the splicing-code; the SRSF1, hnRNPA1, PTBP1, and DSCAM entities all appear as central examples: srsf1, hnrnpa1, ptbp1, dscam.
Touches splicing-and-chromatin (FGFR2/H3K36me3/MRG15), cotranscriptional-splicing (RNAPII elongation rate, EWS), splicing-and-evolution (conserved YCAY code, NOVA functional divergence), and splicing-and-disease (RBFOX1/autism-epilepsy, cardiomyopathy, cancer/EMT).
Complements method-oriented sources capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the and sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying: it relies heavily on CLIP + splicing microarrays/RNA-seq + computational network inference (Box 1), the experimental substrate those methods/tools formalize.

Open questions

Which binding sites of an RBP are genuine “physiological targets” versus inert molecular “sinks,” and what criteria (conserved sites, robust loss/gain-of-function response, regulation during conserved physiological change) reliably distinguish them?
For each network, is a natural change in RBP activity actually used to drive the transition in vivo, versus the regulator merely being permissive/required?
How are multiple stress mechanisms with different kinetics (e.g., SRSF10 dephosphorylation vs nuclear stress bodies) integrated during recovery?
Is alternative splicing “reset” during somatic-cell reprogramming to pluripotency, as transcription is?
How will single-cell transcriptomics resolve which cell type within a heterogeneous tissue actually undergoes a measured splicing transition?

wiki/sources/kampmann-2020-crispr-based-.title: kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for
wiki/sources/kampmann-2020-crispr-based-.content:
CRISPR-based functional genomics for neurological disease
One-line: A single-author review arguing that CRISPR-based genome editing and gene-expression control (CRISPRi/CRISPRa), deployed in massively parallel genetic screens in human iPSC-derived neurons, glia and organoids, is the key to moving from disease-associated genetic variants to causal mechanisms and therapeutic targets in neurological disease.
Key takeaways

The bottleneck in neurological disease is not variant discovery but functional interpretation: genome-wide-association-study catalogues grow fast, yet the causal genes, cell types and mechanisms lag behind. The review frames seven “key mechanistic questions” (which gene? which process? loss vs toxic gain of function? which cell types? what sensitizes/protects cell types? when in life does the variant act? how do modifiers/environment interact?).
noncoding-variants are hard to interpret: the nearest coding gene is often wrong. Example — an Alzheimer disease risk locus first attributed to CELF1 was later shown to affect SPI1 (PU.1); enhancer variants act cell-type-specifically (e.g. BIN1 in microglia but not neurons/astrocytes).
CRISPR provides a programmable toolkit spanning DNA and RNA: homology-directed repair, NHEJ knockout, dual-cut deletions, base editing, prime editing, CRISPRi (dCas9-KRAB) and CRISPRa (dCas9-activator), and RNA-targeting Cas (Cas13) for knockdown, splice manipulation and RNA modification.
These perturbations scale into pooled genetic screens (survival/proliferation, insult-sensitivity, FACS-based, single-cell RNA-seq / perturb-seq, optical) and arrayed screens (needed for non-cell-autonomous / morphological / electrophysiological phenotypes).
The most powerful application couples CRISPR screens to human iPSC-derived disease models — patient vs isogenic-control neurons, glia, brain-organoids and assembloids — enabling causal tests in the relevant human cell type rather than in cancer lines or mouse models.
Modelling disease-associated gene-expression changes with CRISPRi/CRISPRa can pinpoint the causal changes among many correlated ones, and modifier screens can reveal mechanisms and determinants of cell-type-selective vulnerability.

Notable claims / data

Limitations of prior models are argued in detail: human vs mouse differences (dopamine levels in nigral neurons; von Economo neurons absent in mice; microglial expression differences; SNCA A53T causes PD in humans but is wild-type residue in mice), and the correlative, non-perturbable nature of post-mortem human tissue. Non-human primates model genome/circuitry better but are costly and ethically constrained.
CRISPRi can achieve stringent knockdown (>99% in some cases) of coding and non-coding RNAs, is inducible/reversible, and has substantially fewer off-target effects than RNAi; it acts at transcription start sites so partial knockdown enables study of essential genes.
CRISPRa (dCas9-activator, or dCas9-p300 for distal enhancers) gives gain-of-function; CRISPRi and CRISPRa often yield complementary/opposite phenotypes (e.g. sensitization vs desensitization to toxins).
Example screens with neurological relevance: a screen for modifiers of toxic dipeptide repeats (C9orf72 ALS/FTD) pointed to nucleocytosolic transport and to protective knockdown of TMX2; tau-spreading screens implicated heparan sulfate proteoglycans, LRP1, and the ESCRT complex; α-synuclein modifiers included DCLK1 and THAP11; DDX3X as repressor of C9orf72 RAN translation; TM2D3 in Aβ-aggregate uptake.
iPSC CRISPRi screens in neurons found the neuronal essential-gene set is distinct from cancer/iPSC essential genes; recovered MAP3K12 (DLK) as a neuronal death regulator; UBA1 is broadly expressed yet its loss-of-function links to neurodegeneration. CRISPRa screens found loss of prosaposin causes neuron-specific lipofuscin formation, iron trapping and ferroptosis.
Author states his group established the crisprbrain data commons to compare functional-genomics screens across differentiated human cell types, and implemented the first large-scale CRISPR screens in iPSC-derived neurons.
Unexpected finding: applying CRISPRi/a to hundreds of neurodegeneration-associated genes showed increased NQO1 expression is neurotoxic rather than neuroprotective.

Connections

Extends single-cell-crispr-screen / perturb-seq and pooled-crispr-screen concepts specifically into iPSC-derived neurons and glia; complements dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening, gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements.
Connects genome-editing and noncoding-variants interpretation to disease-causal gene identification via genome-wide-association-study.
Touches splicing-therapeutics / splicing manipulation: notes RNA-targeting dCas can alter alternative splicing (e.g. tau).
Relevant to brain-development-neurogenomics and psychiatric-genetics / autism via iPSC modelling of neurodevelopmental disorders and schizophrenia-variant eQTL screens.

Open questions

Which iPSC-derived cell types can we actually make? Many disease-relevant types lack differentiation protocols; iPSC-neurons resemble young neurons while ageing is a top risk factor.
Transgene silencing during iPSC differentiation threatens durable CRISPRi/a machinery expression.
How to model non-cell-autonomous, circuit-level and tissue-context phenotypes at screen scale (organoids/assembloids are promising but immature)?
Can prime editing enable large-scale introduction of patient variants into iPSCs for phenotypic screens?

wiki/sources/kang-2011-spatio-temporal-t.title: kang-2011-spatio-temporal-transcriptome-human-brain
wiki/sources/kang-2011-spatio-temporal-t.content:
Spatio-temporal transcriptome of the human brain
One-line: A landmark exon-level atlas of gene expression and exon usage across 16 regions and 15 developmental periods of the human brain, delivering the reusable human-brain-transcriptome-atlas.
Key takeaways

Generated exon-level transcriptome plus genotyping from 1,340 samples of 57 post-mortem brains (5.7 post-conceptional weeks to 82 years), sampling 16 regions including 11 neocortex areas across a 15-period system — a foundational resource for human neurodevelopment.
86.1% of 17,565 surveyed protein-coding genes were expressed in at least one region/period, and ~90% of expressed genes were differentially regulated at the whole-transcript or exon level across regions and/or time.
Spatio-temporal differences were overwhelmingly prenatal: the bulk of temporal regulation occurred during fetal development, with regional transcriptomes becoming more similar to each other after birth.
The transcriptome is organized into 29 co-expression modules (gene-coexpression-network) with distinct spatio-temporal patterns and hub transcription factors (e.g. TBR1, FEZF2, FOXG1, SATB2, EMX1).
alternative-splicing via differential exon usage is pervasive and developmentally/spatially regulated (e.g. ANKRD32 isoform gradient in mid-fetal frontal cortex; sex-biased NLGN4X exon 7 usage).

Notable claims / data

Region and age contribute more to global transcriptome differences than sex, ethnicity or inter-individual variation (MDS/PCA).
Cerebellar cortex (CBC) had the most distinctive transcriptional profile among regions; primary visual cortex (V1C) was most distinctive among neocortical areas.
Spatial vs temporal regulation: 70.9% of expressed genes were spatially differentially expressed between regions vs 89.9% temporally between periods; only ~0.7% of temporal regulation fell in adulthood.
Differential exon usage affected 13,647 (90.2%) of expressed genes; 83.0% of these showed temporal exon-usage changes during fetal development.
159 sex-biased genes identified (13 Y, 9 X, 137 autosomal); 76.7% male-biased, most pronounced prenatally. Y-genes (PCDH11Y, RPS4Y1, DDX3Y, etc.) showed no compensatory X-paralog upregulation in females.
Expression trajectories validated against independent non-transcriptome data: DCX in hippocampus (r=0.946), dendrite growth (r=0.810/0.700), synaptogenesis (r=0.940).
cis-eQTLs (SNPs within 10 kb of a TSS/TES) detected for 39 NCX, 8 HIP, 4 AMY, 2 STR, 6 MD and 5 CBC genes; associated SNPs enriched near transcription start/end sites (e.g. rs10785190–GLIPR1L2).
Limitation: bulk dissected tissue dilutes cell-type-specific signal; sample/region numbers insufficient for the full eQTL range; array platform misses low-expressing transcripts (sequencing suggested as follow-up).

Connections

Provides a developmental-atlas backbone complementary to the psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource for studying human neurodevelopment and psychiatric-genetics.
Co-expression hub genes and disease-linked trajectories (ASD, schizophrenia) connect to autism and psychiatric-disorder gene-expression studies.
Exon-usage findings relate to alternative-splicing and its regulation across brain development.

Open questions

How much of the observed bulk-tissue dynamics reflect changing cellular composition versus per-cell regulation — resolvable only with cell-type-specific/single-cell approaches?
Do the identified cis-eQTLs and their developmental context mediate disease risk, and how do they extend to trans-eQTLs at larger sample sizes?
Which spatio-temporal exon-usage events are human-specific relative to non-human primates?

wiki/sources/karollus-2023-current-seque.title: karollus-2023-current-sequence-based-models
wiki/sources/karollus-2023-current-seque.content:
Current sequence-based models capture gene expression determinants in promoters but mostly ignore distal enhancers
One-line: A benchmark showing that state-of-the-art sequence-to-expression deep models (esp. Enformer) capture causal promoter signals but massively under-weight distal enhancers and eQTLs.
Key takeaways

Confronts state-of-the-art sequence-based models of transcription (Enformer, Basenji1/2, Xpresso, Expecto) with two observational RNA-seq datasets and five deep perturbation assays via in silico “reproductions” of experiments, probing whether they learn genuine cis-regulatory-grammar rather than evolutionary correlations.
Enformer captures causal determinants of human promoters well — including single-nucleotide variants, TF-binding-site effects, and core-promoter-motif perturbations from a massively-parallel-reporter-assay — demonstrating real signal about promoter cis-regulatory-elements.
Models fail to capture the causal effect of enhancers and distal noncoding-variants; the predicted impact decays as ~1/distance, so most validated enhancers and eQTLs have negligible influence on predictions.
Uses in silico mutagenesis and window-masking as a model-interpretability probe: Enformer’s distal two-thirds of its 196 kb receptive field adds only ~1% explained variance.
Diagnoses the root cause as an escalating class imbalance — the ratio of true regulatory elements to enhancer-like candidates worsens rapidly with distance from the TSS.

Notable claims / data

Enformer predicts endogenous between-gene expression well: median GTEx tissue r = 0.79; Cardoso-Moreira developmental r = 0.77; between-tissue log-fold-change r = 0.64. Mean-absolute error ~2-fold vs ~1.5-fold typical between-tissue variation.
A tiny 1001 bp window (~0.5% of receptive field) already recovers ~two-thirds of full-sequence explained variance; expanding 1→40 kb helps, distal two-thirds adds only ~1%.
Promoter tasks: Weingarten-Gabbay MPRA promoter r = 0.68; TF-motif backgrounds r = 0.75/0.73; TATA-shift RPLP0 r = 0.9.
Enhancer failure: Bergman et al. plasmid MPRA — promoter strength r = 0.81 but enhancer strength r = 0.137; promoters drive 90% of predicted variance vs 54% of measured. CRISPRi (crispri-crispra) validated enhancers — measured median knockdown effect ~20% but Enformer predicts only ~4%; for ~60% of genes no candidate exceeds 10% predicted effect.
Distal eQTLs: predicted effect <1% beyond 1 kb (median 0.5% change) with no such decay in measured GTEx normalized effect sizes.
Enformer’s promoter-enhancer logic is largely multiplicative (77% of variance as product of promoter × enhancer × background strength), qualitatively matching Bergman/Hong experimental findings.
Enformer beats Basenji2 even when restricted to the same ~40 kb window and on receptive-field-irrelevant tasks (plasmid saturation mutagenesis), suggesting parameter count + transformer architecture — not receptive field — drives gains.

Connections

A skeptical evaluation of sequence-based deep models complementary to broader dna-language-models and foundation-models framings in the wiki’s AI bucket; relevant to whether such models learn causal grammar vs correlation.
Enhancer-vs-promoter and long-range folding limits connect to genome-architecture and chromatin-conformation-capture (paper proposes adding Hi-C prediction as an auxiliary task).
The model-interpretability via in silico mutagenesis parallels interpretation-based scoring approaches elsewhere in the wiki (e.g. gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting).
eQTL / variant-effect angle relates to mutation-effect-prediction and noncoding-variant prioritization work.

Open questions

Is the distal-element failure driven by the “enhancer code” (identifying functional elements) or the “folding code” (linking enhancers to target genes)?
Can new data — more cell types, more species, or scaled perturbation assays — plus Hi-C integration or ABC-style hybrids fix long-range prediction?
Does the observed locus-specific miscalibration of variant effects reflect the model or the plasmid assay?

wiki/sources/katz-2010-analysis-and-desi.title: katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna
wiki/sources/katz-2010-analysis-and-desi.content:
Analysis and design of RNA sequencing experiments for identifying isoform regulation
One-line: Introduces MISO (mixture-of-isoforms), a Bayesian statistical model that quantifies alternative-splicing and isoform expression from RNA-seq with confidence intervals, detects differential regulation, and yields design guidelines for paired-end RNA-seq experiments.
Key takeaways

MISO recasts isoform quantification as a Bayesian inference problem: it models the generative process of RNA-seq read sampling (an isoform is chosen by its length-rescaled abundance, then a read is sampled uniformly along the mRNA) and infers the posterior over isoform abundances Ψ.
Beyond the standard splice-junction estimator (Ŷ_SJ), MISO’s estimator Ŷ_MISO also uses reads on flanking constitutive exons (latent information) and paired-end insert-length distributions, both unbiased estimators of percent-spliced-in.
Incorporating mRNA fragment (insert) length distribution from paired-end data substantially improves splicing estimates and gives concrete RNA-seq design guidelines (favor paired-end, longer inserts of ~300+ nt, low dispersion, and matched insert sizes across compared libraries).
MISO detects differentially regulated exons/isoforms via a Bayes factor (BF) computed from the posterior over ΔΨ using the Savage-Dickey density ratio.
Applied to hnRNP H knockdown human cell data, MISO implicated the splicing factor hnRNP H1 in regulation of alternative cleavage and polyadenylation (APA), validated by CLIP-seq.
Exon-centric (two-isoform) case has an analytic solution; the general isoform-centric case uses a hybrid MCMC sampler (Metropolis-Hastings + Gibbs with a logistic-normal proposal on the simplex). Implements miso.

Notable claims / data

For a gene at median GA2 coverage (~220 RPK), the s.d. of estimated Ψ dropped more than twofold, from 0.21 (Ŷ_SJ) to 0.09 (Ŷ_MISO).
Against qRT-PCR for 52 alternatively skipped exons, Ŷ_MISO gave Pearson r = 0.87; restricting to high-confidence exons with 95% CI width < 0.25 (n = 23) raised it to r = 0.96.
Differential-splicing detection: 100% of exons (6/6) with BF ≥ 20 were confirmed differentially regulated by qRT-PCR vs 21% (4/19) with BF < 20 (P < 0.0004, Fisher’s exact). Overall ~15% of alternative exons changed at BF ≥ 20 on hnRNP H knockdown.
CLIP-seq validation: percentage of hnRNP-H-enhanced exons among those with flanking-intron CLIP tags rose from 60% to >90% as BF threshold increased, plateauing near BF = 5 (5:1 odds); effect stronger for downstream-intron binding and reversed for exonic CLIP tags.
Paired-end with insert-length info reduced Ψ error by ~2-5x; at 200 RPK, error fell from ~8% (standard) to ~4%, and to ~2% at higher coverage.
Low-dispersion paired-end length distributions raised the fraction of assignable reads from ~15% to >50%, potentially tripling splicing information at fixed depth. Libraries here had dispersion d between 0.6 and 1.5.
Caveat: read-coverage fluctuations along exons depend on fragment size, so comparisons are most accurate when compared libraries share similar insert lengths; very short mRNAs (a few hundred bases) lose ~20-40% coverage in longer-insert libraries. Genes with multiple distant alternative events still need longer reads/inserts to resolve.

Connections

A foundational isoform-quantification method predating rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); both estimate percent-spliced-in and differential splicing but rMATS uses a replicate-aware likelihood-ratio framework whereas MISO uses single-sample Bayes factors.
Fits the methods/tools strand of the wiki (splicing-methods); relevant to alternative-splicing quantification and splicing-factors mechanism studies.
The hnRNP H application connects splicing-factor biology to alternative cleavage and polyadenylation; complements RBP-mechanism sources.
Builds on the earlier Jiang & Wong (2009) alternative-splicing quantification method, extending it with paired-end information.

Open questions

How well does the single-sample Bayes-factor differential test hold up versus replicate-based approaches when biological variability is high?
What is the practical magnitude/impact of increased local read-coverage fluctuations at insert lengths well beyond 300 nt?
Can short-read paired-end data, even with insert-length modeling, ever resolve isoforms in genes with many distant alternative events, or is long-read sequencing strictly required?

wiki/sources/keeling-2023-horizontal-gen.title: keeling-2023-horizontal-gene-transfer-eukaryotes
wiki/sources/keeling-2023-horizontal-gen.content:
Horizontal gene transfer in eukaryotes: aligning theory with data
One-line: A review arguing that horizontal gene transfer (HGT) is far rarer in eukaryotic nuclear genomes than in bacteria/archaea, because eukaryotic phagocytosis and endosymbiosis increase exposure to foreign genes but reduce the necessity for them.
Key takeaways

HGT (non-sexual movement of genetic information between genomes) reshaped bacterial and archaeal genomes profoundly, but its role in eukaryotes has been debated and is much smaller in magnitude.
The intuitive “you are what you eat” hypothesis predicts that phagocytosis and endosymbiosis should raise HGT frequency; genomic data do not support this — the disconnect is the paper’s central puzzle.
The author’s core argument: eating other cells increased the chance of acquiring genes but obviated the necessity, because selection in most eukaryotes operates on variation (cell structure, behaviour, developmental/regulatory change) not readily supplied by HGT.
The review reframes the field’s question from “did HGT happen in eukaryotes?” to “how did it shape their ecology and evolution?”

Notable claims / data

At most only a few percent of genes in a nuclear genome are detectably derived from HGT — several-fold lower than in most bacterial/archaeal genomes. Most such surveys count only bacteria-to-eukaryote transfers (easier to detect), so they underestimate the true total.
Two famous high-HGT claims proved to be methodological artefacts: the human genome (twice claimed to carry scores of foreign genes) and tardigrades (~one-fifth of genes claimed from HGT). Bdelloid rotifers’ unusually high HGT proportion, by contrast, has survived re-analysis.
Many genuine eukaryotic HGTs are small, functionally important “pulses” tied to ecological transitions (e.g. anaerobic protists gaining metabolic pathways from bacteria; adaptation to parasitism; thermal/carbohydrate adaptation).
Fungi and fungus-like oomycetes are “bacterial-like” exceptions: they feed by extracellular heterotrophy (no phagocytosis), fit metabolic networks like bacteria, and accordingly show prevalent metabolism-related HGT. Plants also acquired substantial foreign genes affecting photosynthesis, development and pathogen conflict.
Massive amounts of organelle/endosymbiont DNA integrate into host nuclear chromosomes on lab timescales, but nearly all of it is non-functional — functional transfer frequency stays low.
Terminology proposal (three distinct processes): EGT (endosymbiont-derived protein targeted back to the same endosymbiont); hetero-EGT (endosymbiont-derived gene, protein targeted elsewhere); hetero-EPT (protein targeted to an endosymbiont but the gene came from elsewhere).
Nuclear genomes with secondary/tertiary plastids show no “excess” of plastid-lineage genes beyond organelle-function genes, and lineages that fully lost an organelle leave no detectable genetic “footprint” — undercutting hetero-EGT as a null assumption and as a test for a lost organelle.
In recently integrated endosymbionts (e.g. the rhizarian amoeba Paulinella’s chromatophore, insect obligate symbionts, the trypanosome Angomonas), most host-encoded symbiont-targeted proteins are NOT derived from the symbiont — i.e. dominated by hetero-EPT, not classical EGT.

Connections

Complements wiki material on genome architecture and evolution by contrasting eukaryotic nuclear genome dynamics with prokaryotic gene flux.
Proposes a “cyclic targeting-ratchet” model of organelle integration in which host protein-targeting evolves early (during a “farming” phase of delayed digestion) before an endosymbiont is permanently fixed, reversing the classical “linear” (gene-transfer-first) model.

Open questions

How much HGT actually occurs between two eukaryotes (vs the better-studied bacteria-to-eukaryote direction)?
What are the actual mechanisms of transfer (vesicles, transposable elements, tunnelling nanotubes, viral transduction) — rarely known?
How variable is HGT’s impact across the vast, under-sampled diversity of eukaryotes, given that single-genome sampling poorly represents lineages as diverse as all animals combined?

wiki/sources/kelley-2021-human-brain-org.title: kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular
wiki/sources/kelley-2021-human-brain-org.content:
Human brain organogenesis: Toward a cellular understanding of development and disease
One-line: A Cell Leading Edge review (Stanford, Pașca lab) arguing that the convergence of single-cell genomics and stem-cell-based 3D tissue modeling (organoids and assembloids) is enabling a new cellular/molecular understanding of human-specific brain-development-neurogenomics — progenitor expansion, neurogenesis/gliogenesis, brain evolution, and neuropsychiatric disease.
Key takeaways

The human brain forms from a neuroepithelial tube into ~170 billion cells of diverse types over a uniquely protracted developmental course; most neurodevelopmental principles were extrapolated from non-primate models because human tissue (especially late gestation) is inaccessible. Two technological waves — single-cell molecular profiling and iPSC-based 3D culture — are now opening human-specific biology.
Progenitor expansion is a key driver of human cortical enlargement. Ventricular radial glia (vRG) give rise to intermediate progenitor cells (IPCs) and outer radial glia (oRG); the outer subventricular zone (oSVZ), populated by oRG, is the defining feature of large mammalian cortices and its thickness correlates with cortex size. scRNA-seq has assigned molecular identities to morphologically-defined progenitors (oRG, vRG, IPCs) across cortex, hippocampus, striatum, midbrain, cerebellum.
Neurogenesis is protracted and regionally staggered (begins ~4 PCW in brainstem/spinal cord, continues postnatally in cerebellum); non-neuronal cells (oligodendrocytes, astrocytes, microglia, vascular) are ~half of adult CNS cells and are generated largely after neurogenesis. In humans (not rodents) oRG are a source of OPCs via a “pre-OPC” state, coordinating oligodendrogliogenesis with expanded neurogenesis.
Human cellular innovations: cell types are broadly conserved across human/mouse, but humans show compositional shifts (relative increase in cortical GABAergic interneurons; expanded cerebellar nuclei) and putative human/primate-enriched types (rosehip layer-1 interneurons, TAC3+ striatal interneurons, von Economo neurons, Betz cells, enlarged interlaminar astrocytes). Human supragranular pyramidal neurons are larger, more branched, and show novel dendritic calcium waves and HCN1-mediated membrane differences.
Molecular regulation: bulk transcriptomes show ~two-thirds of protein-coding genes are developmentally regulated, with two major temporal transitions (early-fetal PCW 6–10 regional-identity establishment; a massive late-fetal transition spanning prenatal→postnatal). Brain gene expression evolves slowly; many bulk human-vs-macaque “differences” prenatally reflect cellular composition rather than cell-intrinsic molecular change.
Molecular innovations in human brain evolution come via (1) human-specific gene duplications (NOTCH2NL, ARHGAP11B, TBC1D3 → progenitor/oRG expansion; SRGAP2C → protracted spine maturation) and (2) human accelerated regions (HARs) and divergent regulatory enhancers (e.g. FZD8 enhancer, PPP1R17 via HARs; PDGFD, mTOR signaling enriched in human RG).
3D modeling: iPSC-derived organoids (guided vs unguided differentiation) and assembloids (fusion of regionalized organoids and multi-lineage assembly) recapitulate progenitor diversity, regional identity, gliogenesis, myelination, circuit formation, and migration — and can model neuropsychiatric/neurodevelopmental disease (brain-development-neurogenomics).
Disease: rare coding/CNV variants (ASD, epilepsy, developmental delay) converge on maturing glutamatergic/GABAergic neurons; common noncoding variants are annotated via eQTLs and chromatin maps (the psychENCODE Consortium linked schizophrenia variants to adult excitatory-neuron genes; AD signals enrich in microglial regulatory regions). Organoid/assembloid models reveal disease phenotypes (Timothy syndrome, 22q11.2, Phelan-McDermid, Miller-Dieker, microcephaly, Huntington’s, Down syndrome).

Notable claims / data

~170 billion cells of diverse types in the human brain; ~two-thirds of protein-coding genes developmentally regulated in neocortex (Li et al., 2018).
RG scaffold becomes discontinuous ~17–24 PCW (vRG truncate to tRG; oRG become the primary migration scaffold).
Human-specific duplications: NOTCH2NLB overexpression clonally expands cortical progenitors via Notch activation; NOTCH2NL deletion → premature neuronal maturation. ARHGAP11B (mice/marmoset) and TBC1D3 increase oRG and neocortical expansion/folding. SRGAP2C in mice extends dendritic-spine formation and increases intracortical connectivity + sensory learning.
HAR-linked PPP1R17 modulates progenitor cell-cycle progression (mirrors human protracted development); a human-accelerated FZD8 enhancer (WNT) increased brain size in mice.
TAC3+ striatal interneurons ≈ one-third of sampled interneurons in marmoset and human, with no apparent mouse homolog (Krienen et al., 2020). Rosehip layer-1 GABAergic interneuron lacks a mouse homolog (Boldog et al., 2018).
In vitro maturation tracks in vivo timing: organoid electrical spiking noted after ~3 months; cortical organoids cultured up to 21 months show postnatal hallmarks (e.g. NMDA-subunit composition changes); transplanted human neurons assayed to 11 months. A reported organoid metabolic/stress signature (Bhaduri et al., 2020) was argued to be culture-microenvironmental and homeostatic, not deleterious cell stress (Gordon et al., 2021).
Disease modeling examples: cortico-spinal-muscle assembloids form a functional di-synaptic circuit driving muscle contraction (Andersen et al., 2020); Timothy syndrome (CACNA1C) assembloids show inefficient interneuron migration (Birey et al., 2017); 22q11.2 neurons show increased excitability/impaired Ca2+ partly via DGCR8 loss; Phelan-McDermid cortico-striatal assembloids show reduced network synchronization only after cortical fusion; human fetal Huntington’s tissue shows progenitor cell-cycle/polarity/ciliogenesis deficits (Barnat et al., 2020).
Caveats stressed throughout: bulk-vs-single-cell composition confounds in cross-species comparisons; organoids lack microglia/vasculature/sensory input and show batch variability (especially unguided); colocalization/association ≠ causality; functional validation of common-variant effects remains hard and difficult to scale to hundreds of variants.

Connections

The NEW topic hub: this is a foundational developmental-neurobiology review for brain-development-neurogenomics — covers cell types, neurogenesis, brain evolution, and psychiatric-disease genetics in one synthesis.
Cites and is conceptually upstream of bulk developmental-transcriptome work in this wiki: li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (psychENCODE/BrainSpan; the “two-thirds developmentally regulated” and late-fetal transition claims), walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and (fetal-brain eQTLs for annotating disease common variants), parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (ASD cortical molecular changes).
Relates to single-cell developmental brain atlas siblings here, e.g. wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of and zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory, and to cis-regulatory disease mapping in groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at.
The psychENCODE work it cites underlies the existing resource page psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource.
Touches splicing-adjacent themes (alternative splicing analyses contribute to the late-fetal molecular transition) but is not a splicing paper.

Open questions

What underlies the protracted human developmental time course, and is the massive late-fetal molecular transition driven by birth/environment or pre-programmed intrinsic programs?
Are prenatal cross-species cell-type differences genuinely smaller than adult ones, or an artifact of sample size/coverage?
For most human-enriched differentially expressed genes, the causal noncoding regulatory changes remain unknown — how to systematically map HAR/enhancer effects to cell-type expression?
Can in vitro systems model the specified-neuron → mature-circuit transition (synaptic plasticity, experience-dependent wiring, complex morphology) and scale common-variant functional testing (e.g. “village-in-a-dish”)?

wiki/sources/kent-2010-bigwig-bigbed-ena.title: kent-2010-bigwig-bigbed-enabling-browsing
wiki/sources/kent-2010-bigwig-bigbed-ena.content:
BigWig and BigBed: enabling browsing of large distributed datasets
One-line: Introduces the bigwig-bigbed (BBI) file formats and tools that let the UCSC Genome Browser display terabyte-scale next-generation sequencing results by fetching only the data a given view needs.
Key takeaways

Next-generation sequencing produces terabyte-sized datasets whose visualization via custom-track upload previously timed out; installing a local browser copy is costly. BigWig and BigBed solve this by deferring bulk transfer to on-demand fetches.
The bigwig-bigbed files are compressed binary indexed files holding data at several resolutions; only the data supporting the current browser view is transmitted, giving fast remote access to large distributed datasets.
BigBed derives from BED text tables (first three fields: chromosome, start, end; optional custom fields documented via autoSql); BigWig derives from fixedStep/variableStep wiggle or bedGraph text and maps a floating-point value onto genomic positions.
Files are created with command-line utilities (bedToBigBed, wigToBigWig, bedGraphToBigWig) plus a chrom.sizes file, then hosted on an HTTP/HTTPS/FTP site and referenced by a custom-track line; parsing utilities (bigWigSummary, bigWigInfo, bigBedToBed, etc.) inspect or reverse the binary files.

Notable claims / data

After conversion, the browser upload phase takes under 1 s even on home/remote networks (well within the UCSC 300-s limit), with only ~0.5–1.0 s of on-demand transfer overhead depending on hosting location.
Indexing uses a single-dimensional R tree (chosen over binning schemes and nested containment lists for scale-invariant performance and fewer network round trips); a B+ tree maps chromosome names to integer IDs, and the combined structure is called a cirTree in the source. The index is typically less than 1% of the data size because only every 512th item is indexed by default.
Data regions (not the index) are compressed with zlib deflate in blocks of 512 items, balancing random access against compression ratio; a local cache stores 8 Kb blocks in sparse files so repeat and multi-user views avoid re-fetching. A single remote round trip is still required to check whether the file changed.
The formats are similar to BAM files (binary/compressed/indexed SAM) but target wiggle graphs and BED-style tables; the authors adopted BAM for short-read alignments and added HTTPS support to BAM via the BBI transfer/cache layers to avoid format proliferation.
Readers/writers are portable C; as of February 2010 nearly 1300 files had been displayed since June 2009, with Perl bindings (Bio-BigFile) and an in-progress Java implementation for the Integrative Genomics Viewer.

Connections

A foundational genome-browser data-format tool underpinning distributed visualization workflows, relevant to any rna-seq or sequencing pipeline whose coverage/annotation tracks are shared through the UCSC Genome Browser.

Open questions

How do BBI formats and their remote-access model scale to modern cloud object storage and much larger cohorts than the ~1300 files reported in 2010?
Could the alignment/sequence-quality information of BAM be folded into a BigBed extension, and is avoiding format proliferation still the right tradeoff?

wiki/sources/kernohan-2024-expanding-dia.title: kernohan-2024-expanding-diagnostic-toolbox-for-rare
wiki/sources/kernohan-2024-expanding-dia.content:
The expanding diagnostic toolbox for rare genetic diseases
One-line: A Nature Reviews Genetics review of the current and emerging technologies used to diagnose rare genetic diseases — from targeted, exome and short-read genome sequencing through long-read sequencing, optical genome mapping and functional multi-omics (RNA-seq, methylation, metabolomics, lipidomics, proteomics, MAVEs) — framing how to pick, interpret and integrate tests when >50% of patients still remain undiagnosed.
Key takeaways

Rare diseases (often <1 in 2,000) collectively affect >400 million people worldwide, ~80% are genetic, and OMIM lists >6,300 disease–gene associations; yet fewer than 50% of patients obtain a molecular diagnosis with current methods. Diagnosis matters clinically (prognosis, targeted treatment, stopping ineffective therapy, reproductive/predictive counselling).
Rare-disease genetics has moved through eras — karyotyping (1950s) → Sanger (1970s) → triplet-repeat/mtDNA/imprinting (1990s) → CNV microarray/MLPA (2000s) → next-generation sequencing (NGS, 2010s). Today a mix of traditional cytogenetics and NGS is standard, with long-read sequencing and optical genome mapping emerging.
Variant classes each carry distinct detection/interpretation challenges: SNVs, indels (1–50 bp), short tandem repeats (STRs), and structural variants (SVs ≥50 bp, incl. CNVs, rearrangements, mobile-element insertions). The diagnostic path is a four-step process: clinical consultation → test selection/sampling → lab testing + bioinformatics/reporting → clinical correlation.
Three NGS testing choices — small gene panels vs exome sequencing vs genome sequencing — traded off against cost and pretest probability. Defined phenotypes suit panels; genetically heterogeneous or unclear presentations favour genome-wide exome/genome. Testing strategy also spans singleton vs trio (trio speeds analysis and interpretation but can expose non-parental relationships).
Emerging DNA-interrogation tools push past short-read limits: long-read genome sequencing (PacBio ~10–100 kb; Oxford Nanopore ~10–30 kb; 10x linked reads ~50 kb) enables mapping of non-unique regions, repeat-expansion sizing, SV detection, phasing, de novo haplotype-resolved assembly, and simultaneous epigenetic-mark detection; optical genome mapping (OGM) is an imaging (not sequencing) method for SVs from ~500 bp to whole chromosomes.
A parallel wave of functional assays interprets variants of unknown significance (VUS) rather than finding them: RNA sequencing (expression outliers, aberrant splicing, allele-specific expression), epigenomic/dna-methylation episignatures, metabolomics, lipidomics, proteomics, and multiplexed assays of variant effect (MAVEs). The authors argue these omics succeed mainly as secondary functional assays, not primary variant discovery.
The forward vision is integrated multi-omics (not just parallel cross-referencing), AI-assisted cross-omic variant nomination, and global data sharing + matchmaking (Matchmaker Exchange) to solve the unsolved ~50%.

Notable claims / data

Coverage of coding bases: exome ~95%, genome ~98%; typical read depth exome ~100×, genome ~30×, mosaic panels ~500–1,000×. Genome sequencing detects SVs but generally only >10 kb, and short reads (~50–300 bp) cannot reliably resolve repeat expansions.
Diagnostic-yield gain of genome over exome sequencing is described as “thus far underwhelming” — most diagnoses and novel gene discoveries remain achievable with current exome technology — yet genome sequencing is expected to supersede exome for its noncoding reach and shorter processing time.
~15% of rare-disease-causing variants alter mRNA splicing, a portion outside the canonical splice site (>±2 bp) where effects are hard to predict — motivating RNA-seq. Published RNA-seq cohort studies report diagnostic yields of 7.5–36.0%; the main utility is splicing analysis / VUS resolution (expression and untargeted analyses are hampered by high variability and weak control cohorts).
Metabolomics: one untargeted study accurately diagnosed 16 inborn errors of metabolism; coupling exome sequencing with metabolomics gave clarity in ~43.5% of cases and raised diagnostic yield by 12.3%. >2,000 “metabolic genes” and >19,000 small molecules; >100 rare diseases relate to lipid synthesis/remodelling.
DNA-methylation “episignatures” defined for many disorders (e.g. Sotos, Kabuki, CHARGE, Rubinstein-Taybi, ATR-X, 16p11.2 deletion) can both reclassify a VUS as pathogenic/benign and flag candidate diagnoses in unsolved patients.
MAVEs (saturation mutagenesis + multiplexed phenotyping) build variant-effect maps; the Atlas of Variant Effects aggregates data from >400 researchers across >30 countries.
Control-cohort scale for DNA is far ahead of other omics: gnomAD v2.1.1 has 125,748 exomes + 15,708 genomes (GRCh37), v3.1.2 has 76,156 genomes (GRCh38); RNA/protein/metabolite reference sets lag and are confounded by demographics/environment. Genome-in-a-Bottle provides DNA truth sets but comparable benchmarks are missing for the new omics.
Data sharing / matchmaking: >60 million people projected to have had exome/genome sequencing for healthcare by 2025; the Matchmaker Exchange connects 8 matchmakers, >15,000 unique genes submitted from 105 countries representing 137,000 unsolved cases, with ≥600 novel disease–gene discoveries and ~15% of submitted genes leading to publications. Reanalysis of prior exome/genome data is a strongly recommended, high-yield first step for negative cases.

Connections

This is the diagnosis half of the genome-medicine (crispr topic) diagnosis→therapy arc. It pairs with the therapy-side reviews pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of (CRISPR editing toolbox) and stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of (clinical interpretation of editing off-targets); together they trace a rare-disease patient from molecular diagnosis to genome-editing-based treatment.
Extends noncoding-variants: the review repeatedly names deep-intronic and intergenic SNVs as the frontier where annotation is “incredibly limited,” positioning RNA-seq (aberrant splicing/expression outliers) and ENCODE-type regulatory maps as the interpretive bridge — the clinical-diagnostics counterpart to the wiki’s splicing/splicing-qtl and regulatory-variant threads.
Reinforces dna-methylation as a clinical readout (episignatures) beyond its regulatory-biology role elsewhere in the wiki.
Touches the transcriptomics thread relevant to long-read-rna-seq: the review notes long-read platforms can detect epigenetic marks alongside sequence and enable haplotype-resolved assembly, and frames RNA-seq splicing analysis as the highest-yield functional assay.

Open questions

Will integrated (vs merely coordinated/sequential) multi-omics pipelines — likely AI-assisted — become scalable enough for routine clinical use, or is the per-sample analysis time an insurmountable bottleneck?
Noncoding interpretation: can regulatory-region annotation advance enough for genome sequencing to realize its promise beyond being “an improved-coverage exome”?
Control cohorts for RNA/protein/metabolite/lipid data lag far behind gnomAD and lack age/sex-matched, tissue-relevant references and truth sets — how are these built, and how are underrepresented ancestries included?
Which emerging technology belongs where in a standardized “rare disease diagnostic algorithm,” and how is equitable global access achieved given that millions still have no genetic testing at all?
Health-economics/real-world-evidence gaps block reimbursement — how is clinical utility demonstrated for tests from small, potentially unstable start-up manufacturers?

wiki/sources/kilpinen-2013-coordinated-e.title: kilpinen-2013-coordinated-effects-of-sequence-variation
wiki/sources/kilpinen-2013-coordinated-e.content:
Coordinated Effects of Sequence Variation on DNA Binding, Chromatin Structure, and Transcription
One-line: Allele-specific profiling in human lymphoblastoid cell lines shows sequence variants coordinately perturb TF binding, histone marks, and transcription, with TFs as the primary drivers.
Key takeaways

Genetic variation at noncoding-variants in cis-regulatory-elements produces abundant allele-specific (AS) activity that is coordinated across regulatory layers, not just gene expression.
Transcription factors are the primary mediators of sequence-specific regulation; histone modifications more often reflect, rather than define, the primary regulatory event (context-dependent behavior).
AS effects show local, short-range, and long-range (haplotypic) coordination, and are enriched at DNase I sensitivity QTLs (dsQTLs) and eQTLs, linking them to chromatin-accessibility and allele-specific-open-chromatin.

Notable claims / data

Profiled LCLs from two parent-offspring trios (+8 unrelated individuals): ChIP of 5 histone PTMs (H3K4me1/me3, H3K27ac/me3, H4K20me1), 3 TFs (TFIIB, PU.1, MYC), POLR2B; plus GRO-seq, DNase I-seq, CTCF ChIP-seq; mRNA for all 14.
AS activity: 11-12% for TFs, 6-30% for hPTMs at accessible heterozygous sites; fewer AS effects in mRNA (5%) than in nascent GRO-seq transcripts (27-28%), reflecting posttranscriptional buffering.
Most TF AS binding is motif-independent (70% PU.1, 97% MYC not explained by consensus-motif disruption); homotypic motif clusters buffer effects of motif-disrupting SNPs (41% vs 25% allelic effects; P=0.0087).
Significant parental transmission for all three TFs (r=0.44-0.75, P≤0.02) and mRNA/GRO-seq (r=0.46-0.50); weaker for active histone marks (r=0.12-0.21), stronger near eQTLs/dsQTLs (r=0.31-0.57).
Long-range haplotypic coordination extends up to ~200 kb (H3K4me1/H3K27ac with PU.1); >90% of coordination events are positive (same haplotype). Companion report (McVicker et al.) maps >1200 histone-mark/Pol II QTLs in Yoruba LCLs.

Connections

Complements MPRA and eQTL-mapping work on how regulatory SNPs act; shares the allele-specific-signal logic used in allele-specific splicing/chromatin studies in the wiki.
Relates to functional-genomics annotation of cis-regulatory-elements and to interpretation of noncoding-variants via chromatin-accessibility.

Open questions

To what extent are histone-mark changes causal vs. consequential across cell types beyond LCLs?
Can the motif-independent majority of AS TF binding be explained by cooperativity/chromatin context, and does this generalize to disease-relevant loci?

wiki/sources/kim-2020-extraction-of-dist.title: kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell
wiki/sources/kim-2020-extraction-of-dist.content:
Extraction of Distinct Neuronal Cell Types from within a Genetically Continuous Population
One-line: In mouse primary visual cortex (V1), layer 2/3 cortico-cortical projection neurons that look like a single transcriptomic cluster split into distinct cell types once anatomical projection target and feedback connectivity are added — showing gene expression alone cannot fully annotate cortical cell types and linking to brain-development-neurogenomics.
Key takeaways

Single-cell transcriptomics yields >100 putative cortical cell-type clusters, but cortico-cortical projection neurons (CCPNs, the intratelencephalic class) collapse into surprisingly few clusters despite projecting to >10 different higher visual areas (HVAs) — a mismatch between genetic clusters and anatomical diversity.
Combining projection-dependent single-nuclei RNA-seq with monosynaptic rabies tracing, the authors show V1→AL versus V1→PM L2/3 neurons differ systematically in gene expression even though they form ONE transcriptomic cluster (“continuous variability,” not discrete clusters).
These neurons obey a “like-to-like” feedback rule: each population receives long-range cortical feedback selectively from the same HVA it projects to.
Central conclusion: connectivity is an essential feature for cell-type identification; gene-expression analysis in isolation is insufficient to define cortical neuron types — a caution for purely transcriptomic atlases of the brain.
Developmental framing: L2/3 V1 neurons share birthdates and neurogenic environment yet acquire distinct adult expression+connectivity, suggesting genetic programs and activity-dependent mechanisms jointly establish the gene-expression/connectivity relationship.

Notable claims / data

Prior transcriptomic taxonomy (Tasic et al., 2018): 111 V1 clusters (60 inhibitory, 34 excitatory); excitatory split into 11 SCPN (5 PT + 6 CT) and 14 CCPN/IT clusters. Inhibitory neurons and SCPNs show cluster–anatomy concordance; CCPN clusters map to layers, not within-layer anatomy.
Triple retrograde CTB tracing: most V1 neurons project to a single area (single-color labeling: V1→AL 76.01%±2.09%, V1→PM 84.08%±3.89%, V1→LM 81.14%±4.31%). Among double-labels, AL+LM most common (19.47%±3.29%), AL+PM rare (3.84%±0.90%) — so V1→AL vs V1→PM are largely non-overlapping populations.
Laminar bias: V1→AL/V1→RL (anterior-targeting) enriched in lower L2/3 and rare in L5/6; V1→PM/V1→P/POR (posterior-targeting) enriched in upper L2/3 and most abundant in L5. Two-way ANOVA p<0.0001; 55,742 retrograde-labeled neurons across 12 mice.
snRNA-seq: AAVretro-Cre + Sun1-sfGFP reporter, FANS-sorted GFP+ nuclei, SMART-seq v4 / Nextera XT, Illumina HiSeq 4000, ~1.6M uniquely mapped reads/nucleus. tSNE shows two clusters = L2/3 vs L5+6; V1→AL and V1→PM do NOT form sub-clusters, but enrich toward opposite ends of the single L2/3 cluster.


800 genes differentially expressed between L2/3 V1→AL and V1→PM (Zinbwave-EdgeR / Zinbwave-DESeq2 zero-inflated negative binomial models). Four neuronal genes (Astn2, Kcnh5, Grm1, Cntn5) validated by retrograde labeling + smFISH; differences persisted even for intermingled neurons at matched depth (100–200 µm from pia in L2/3upper), ruling out pure laminar artifact. Caveat: substantial overlap in expression distributions — differences are statistical, not categorical.


These projection-based differences do NOT correspond to previously reported unsupervised L2/3 clusters — i.e. clustering without projection labels misses them.
Rabies feedback tracing (SepW1-Cre for L2/3, Tlx3-Cre for L5; EnvA+RVdG-eGFP, oG glycoprotein): L2/3 neurons received ~half their HVA feedback from their own target area (V1→AL 50.99%±4.47% from AL; V1→PM 40.97%±11.49% from PM). V1→PM received virtually no input from AL; V1→AL got only 10.28%±5.08% from PM. L5 mirrored this (V1→PM L5 54.47%±21.82% from PM; V1→AL L5 no detectable PM input). Feedback interpreted as consistent with activity-dependent Hebbian “like-to-like” wiring.

Connections
