Given the decoupling of GWAS-risk modules from postmortem-DEX modules, are disease-relevant splicing changes primary (developmental) or secondary (reactive) — and at which developmental window do they act?

wiki/sources/li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-.title: li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-m6a-promotes-local
wiki/sources/li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-.content:
Super-enhancer RNA m6A promotes local chromatin accessibility and oncogene transcription in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
One-line: In pancreatic cancer, m6A methylation of super-enhancer RNAs recruits an H3K4 methyltransferase that opens local chromatin-accessibility and drives oncogene transcription.
Key takeaways

Maps the landscape of super-enhancer RNA (seRNA) m6A modification in PDAC and defines a CFL1–METTL3–seRNA m6A–YTHDC2/MLL1 regulatory axis linking the epitranscriptome to the epigenome at cis-regulatory-elements.
The actin-depolymerizing protein CFL1, overexpressed in tumor, acts as a non-canonical METTL3 cofactor guiding m6A deposition onto seRNAs — an unexpected role for an RNA-binding cytoskeletal protein in the nucleus.
m6A seRNAs are read by YTHDC2 (not YTHDC1), which recruits the H3K4 methyltransferase MLL1 (KMT2A) to catalyze H3K4me3 cotranscriptionally, augmenting local chromatin-accessibility and downstream oncogene expression.
Provides a disease-context example of noncoding-RNA m6A modification as a layer of gene-regulatory control over super-enhancer function.

Notable claims / data

18,311 super-enhancers annotated from H3K27ac ChIP-seq; 17,467 seRNAs expressed in >10% of samples; 5,375 (30.77%) carry m6A. m6A-seq run on 65 PDAC (33 paired + 32 unpaired) and 33 normal tissues.
Of 410 differential m6A seRNAs, 398 (97.07%) were hyper-m6A in PDAC vs normal; CFL1 RNA scored higher than all known m6A writers for correlation with hyper-m6A seRNA levels.
CFL1-KO in PANC-1 lowered m6A on 3,217 (77.07%) seRNAs, overlapping 69.10% of PDAC hyper-m6A seRNAs; METTL3-KO hypomethylated 83.99% of seRNAs. CFL1 binds METTL3 via its ZnF1 domain (aa 259–298), RNA/DNA-independent.
CFL1-KO reduced H3K4me3 at 2,382 peaks (262 increased); 66.54% of CFL1-related hypo-m6A seRNAs overlapped decreased-H3K4me3 regions. YTHDC2-KO decreased 2,229 H3K4me3 peaks; YTH-pocket mutation W1360A abolished m6A-seRNA recognition.
ATAC-seq: 12,201 / 16,832 / 22,201 regions lost accessibility on CFL1 / YTHDC2 / MLL1 KO, coregulated at 10,300 shared ATAC-loss regions (intron / distal intergenic / promoter). 539 target genes enriched in hypoxia, mTORC1, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, glycolysis, EMT pathways.
Clinical: high CFL1 associated with shorter overall survival across two patient sets (combined n=227; HR 2.58, 95% CI 1.85–3.59); 16 hyper-m6A seRNAs correlated with shorter progression-free survival. CFL1/METTL3/YTHDC2 KO suppressed proliferation, migration, invasion, metastasis.
Caveat: total m6A-seq captures only steady-state RNA, missing degraded nascent seRNAs; authors flag MINT-seq as needed for precise nascent seRNA methylome profiling.

Connections

Extends the theme of super-enhancers and cis-regulatory-elements regulating gene expression, adding an RNA-modification control layer.
Complements work on chromatin-accessibility as a readout of local epigenetic state, here driven by seRNA m6A rather than DNA sequence.
Illustrates rna-binding-proteins (CFL1 as writer cofactor, YTHDC2 as reader) mediating chromatin remodeling.

Open questions

Does seRNA m6A modulate R-loop / three-stranded DNA–RNA hybrid homeostasis at enhancers (as suggested by AANCR/APOE eRNA precedent)?
How general is the CFL1–YTHDC2/MLL1 axis across other cancers with CFL1 overexpression, and is it therapeutically targetable?
What determines seRNA-specific vs mRNA-specific METTL3 targeting given CFL1’s selective effect on seRNA (not global mRNA) m6A?

wiki/sources/li-2024-combinatorial-genet.title: li-2024-combinatorial-genetic-strategy-exploring
wiki/sources/li-2024-combinatorial-genet.content:
A combinatorial genetic strategy for exploring complex genotype–phenotype associations in cancer
One-line: A high-MOI barcoded-lentiviral-library method that transforms primary mouse organoids into genotypically/phenotypically diverse tumors, then uses single-cell and spatial barcode sequencing to deconvolve which combinations of oncogenic events drive specific cancer phenotypes.
Key takeaways

Delivers many cancer-associated genetic events (ORF gain-of-function and shRNA loss-of-function) at once into primary epithelial cells at high multiplicity of infection, moving beyond one-driver-at-a-time cancer models.
Enforced cell–virus contact in a constrained Matrigel volume achieves near-complete transduction of mouse bladder urothelial (mBU) and prostate epithelial (mPE) organoids, delivering 10–20 lentiviral copies per cell.
Each lentivirus carries matched 10-nucleotide barcodes at the 5′ and 3′ ends, so clonal architecture is recoverable by single-cell DNA amplicon sequencing (Mission Bio Tapestri) or, spatially, by laser capture microdissection plus bulk barcode sequencing.
Biological selection in NSG mice yields tumors recapitulating human bladder/prostate cancer subtypes; barcode deconvolution links specific mutation combinations to specific histologies.
Conceptually complementary to pooled perturbation screens (pooled-crispr-screen, perturb-seq) but uses random high-MOI lentiviral integration for combinatorial genotypes selected in vivo rather than programmed single perturbations.

Notable claims / data

Tumor formation efficiency: 80% (16/20) for mBU + bladder pool (BU-LVP, 33 genes); 38% (18/47) for mPE + prostate pool (PE-LVP, 24 genes). Latency mean 4.2 months (bladder), 8.9 months (prostate).
In one bladder tumor, three dominant clones were resolved; papillary urothelial carcinoma was uniquely associated with Fgfr3 S243C, shAtm and Zfp703 on a shared background (Ywhaz, Pik3ca E545K, Pparg, Pvrl4). Activating FGFR3 enrichment in papillary urothelial carcinoma matches human data (TCGA-BLCA).
A defined 5-gene pool (Fgfr3 S243C, Ywhaz, Pik3ca E545K, Pparg, Pvrl4) reproduced papillary urothelial carcinoma with inverted growth in independent experiments, validating the combinatorial association.
The Fred Hutch Bladder Tumor (FHBT) series spans urothelial, papillary, squamous and sarcomatoid histologies; RNA-seq (BASE47 predictor, Consensus Molecular Classifier, GSEA) placed them onto human TCGA-BLCA subtype space with conserved EMT signatures in sarcomatoid tumors.
A prostate tumor with rare pleomorphic giant cell carcinoma showed striking enrichment of shKmt2c in the giant-cell clones, consistent with KMT2C’s role in the DNA damage response.
Initial pools over-represented shRNA vectors >10-fold vs ORF vectors (shorter inter-LTR length packages better); producer-cell surface-area adjustment normalized representation.
Caveat: single-cell dissociation loses spatial context (recovered only via LCM); the study used no custom code and relied on the commercial Tapestri platform. Deposited data: GEO SuperSeries GSE229783.

Connections

A functional single-cell cancer-genomics assay that reads out combinatorial genotypes at single-cell resolution, adjacent in spirit to single-cell-perturbation-modeling and pooled screen readouts.
Uses cancer organoids as the transformation substrate and bulk/LCM rna-seq for molecular subtyping.
Contrasts with programmed perturbation platforms (perturb-seq, pooled-crispr-screen): here genotypes arise from random high-MOI integration and are shaped by in vivo tumorigenic selection rather than designed guide libraries.

Open questions

How scalable is barcode deconvolution to larger gene pools and higher-order interactions before clonal complexity outstrips single-cell sampling depth?
Can the random-integration/selection design distinguish causal driver combinations from passenger co-occurrence without extensive defined-pool validation?
Does the approach generalize to epithelial tissues beyond bladder/prostate, and to spatially resolved rather than dissociation-based single-cell readouts?

wiki/sources/li-2026-the-contribution-of.title: li-2026-the-contribution-of-short-tandem
wiki/sources/li-2026-the-contribution-of.content:
The contribution of short tandem repeats to splicing variation in the human cortex
One-line: A human dorsolateral-prefrontal-cortex (DLPFC) sQTL study that, for the first time at scale and including homopolymers, maps short tandem repeats (STRs) whose copy number controls nearby alternative splicing — yielding the splicestr catalog of 146,969 STR–splice associations, fine-mapped candidate-causal repeats, an RBP-binding mechanism, and co-localization with brain-disorder GWAS.
Key takeaways

Built a brain splicing-qtl resource from 336 HBCC DLPFC samples (194 controls, 142 bipolar/schizophrenia cases) with both SNP genotypes and deep RNA-seq; imputed 445,720 autosomal STRs from a SNP-STR reference panel and tested STR dosage vs exon percent-spliced-in (PSI) for STRs within 100 kb of a splice event’s constitutive exons.
Identified 146,969 splicestrs at 5% FDR (51,343 unique STRs × 25,230 splicing events) from 6,143,556 tests; splicing quantified with rmats across SE/MXE/A3SS/A5SS/RI event classes (see rna-seq).
Key contribution over prior brain STR–splicing work: it retains homopolymers (~50% of all STRs), which Cui et al. excluded, and uses larger/deeper data than the GTEx-based Hamanaka et al. study — so the majority of associations are novel.
Three orthogonal fine-mappers (ANOVA, FINEMAP, Lasso) prioritized candidate-causal repeats: 21,893 leniently (≥1 method) and 1,313 strictly (all three) fine-mapped spliceSTRs. Candidate-causal repeats are longer, T-rich, and enriched within 100 bp upstream of the affected exon — consistent with shaping the polypyrimidine tract.
A mechanistic subset acts via rna-binding-proteins: fine-mapped spliceSTRs overlap RBP eCLIP binding sites, and repeat length predicts RBP binding probability — recapitulating HNRNPL–(AC)n and TARDBP–(GT)n relationships.
Co-localization with GWAS for 6 brain disorders linked spliceSTRs to disease, recapitulating a (GT)n spliceSTR for PLEKHA1 (Alzheimer’s) and identifying a novel intronic (AGG)n spliceSTR controlling SEPTIN3 exon-2 skipping co-localized with schizophrenia (see splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

Replication: of HBCC spliceSTRs testable in 4 GTEx tissues (n=34,828 overlapping), effect sizes correlate strongly (Pearson r > 0.7, P < 1×10⁻¹⁰⁰ all tissues); strongest with GTEx brain frontal cortex BA9 (r=0.79), weakest with whole blood (r=0.70). Cross-study overlap was small (1,067–4,825 shared) due to differing TR-callers (rmats + HipSTR here vs GangSTR / intron-based elsewhere) but shared effect sizes still correlate (r > 0.47).
Of 25,230 events with a significant spliceSTR, all but 44 also had a significant SNP signal; those 44 STR-only events were weak and dropped — motivating joint STR+SNP fine-mapping.
Fine-mapping counts: 17,524 pass ANOVA (FDR<10%), 4,647 pass FINEMAP (PIP>0.5), 7,830 pass Lasso. Strict set most enriched within target-gene transcribed regions (OR=2.2, P=5.3×10⁻³⁶) and especially ≤100 bp upstream of the affected exon (OR=25.9, P=2.8×10⁻¹⁵). CG-rich units (CCG OR=10.6, CGG OR=5.7) enriched; consistent T-rich strand bias on the coding strand.
RBP binding (eCLIP, 129 RBPs, K562+HepG2): leniently fine-mapped spliceSTRs enriched at binding sites of 32 RBPs (6 for the strict set). 15 repeat-unit–RBP pairs (4 units, 13 RBPs) enriched; (T)n most common (7 RBPs). Length→binding associations: 17/98 pairs significant; HNRNPL–(AC)n positive (adj P=3.1×10⁻³⁰), TARDBP–(GT)n positive (adj P=9.9×10⁻⁵); some negative associations possibly mapping artifacts.
GWAS co-localization (PP.H4>50% + spliceSTR in LD r²>0.1 with lead variant): 6,111 event–trait pairs tested → 96 gene-trait pairs (11 strict); schizophrenia had the most (60). PLEKHA1 exon-12 skipping ↔ AD (PP.H4=88.97%; (GT)n at chr10:122429495, FINEMAP PIP=0.96, r²=0.71 with rs7908662). SEPTIN3 exon-2 skipping ↔ SCZ (PP.H4=65.3%; intronic (AGG)n at chr22:41978894, strictly fine-mapped, 2.7 kb upstream); exon-2 PSI and the excluding isoform differ between cases and controls (Mann-Whitney P=1.3×10⁻³ and P=0.02). The (AGG)n repeat overlaps no analyzed RBP site but AGG repeats can form g-quadruplex structures, a proposed alternate mechanism.
Methods stack: Beagle 5.2 STR imputation (hg19→hg38 LiftOver); STAR 2.7.9a + GENCODE v38 alignment, RSEM isoform quantification, rmats v4.1.2 PSI; OLS (statsmodels) with age/sex/read-count/10 genotype-PCs/20 PEER-factor covariates; GTEx replication via HipSTR + TRtools genotyping.
Caveats (authors): single brain region, modest n, limited power for rare events; mostly European ancestry; imputation limited to short-read-spannable repeats (no large expansions / VNTRs); RBP mechanism needs experimental validation; eCLIP positional biases may inflate downstream-shift patterns.

Connections

STR-based counterpart to the SNP/indel brain sQTL atlas zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls and the population-blood sQTL→pQTL→disease resource tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants (which it shares a near-identical title with); together these extend splicing-qtl from SNPs toward repeats. See also park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative.
Splicing quantified event-wise with rmats (PSI), unlike the intron-ratio approaches in the studies it benchmarks against — a methodological axis affecting cross-study overlap.
Mechanistic core (repeat length tunes rna-binding-proteins affinity, e.g. HNRNPL/TARDBP; polypyrimidine-tract length) connects to splicing-factors and to RBP-binding resources like encode-rbp-map / van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional.
Disease relevance (Alzheimer’s, schizophrenia) sits in splicing-and-disease; the SEPTIN3 (AGG)n → g-quadruplex hypothesis offers a non-RBP, structure-based route.
Introduces the splicestr entity (the catalog/resource and the term itself).

Open questions

Will direct STR genotyping from WGS/long reads (vs short-read-limited imputation) recover large expansions and VNTRs missed here, and change the spliceSTR landscape?
Does the SEPTIN3 (AGG)n spliceSTR drive schizophrenia risk through G-quadruplex formation, and can this be validated experimentally?
How much of the catalog replicates in cell-type-resolved or multi-region brain data, given the single-region, bulk DLPFC design?
Beyond RBP-affinity, how many spliceSTRs act via DNA/RNA secondary structure or folding energy — a mechanism the authors flag as untested genome-wide?

wiki/sources/liang-2026-single-cell-mult.title: liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron
wiki/sources/liang-2026-single-cell-mult.content:
Single-cell multiomics of neuron activation reveals context-specific genetics of brain disorders
One-line: Single-nucleus RNA+ATAC multiomics of iPSC-derived excitatory and inhibitory neurons from 100 donors, stimulated with KCl (0/1/6 h) to model neuronal activation, showing that most genetic effects relevant to neuropsychiatric disorders (NPD) are context-specific — eQTLs, caQTLs, allele-specific open chromatin (ASoC), and cTWAS-prioritized risk genes appear or strengthen only upon activation, with caQTLs explaining a far larger share of NPD heritability than eQTLs and activation implicating cholesterol/lipid metabolism in disease.
Key takeaways

Models activity-dependent gene regulation at population scale: glutamatergic (iGlut) + GABAergic (iGABA) neurons differentiated from 100 human iPSC lines, cocultured with rat astrocytes, depolarized with 53 mM KCl for 1 or 6 h, then assayed by single-nucleus multiomics (snRNA-seq + snATAC-seq) at 0/1/6 h — the resting post-mortem brain misses biology that only switches on with activity.
1,053,422 nuclei (651,012 passed QC) resolve three neural subtypes — GABA, NEFM⁺ Glut (npglut), NEFM⁻ Glut (nmglut) — each showing widespread activation-induced transcriptomic + epigenomic change: early response genes (ERGs, peak ~1 h, e.g. FOS, NPAS4) then late response genes (LRGs, peak ~6 h, e.g. BDNF, VGF).
Gene expression and chromatin accessibility are often discordant — “epigenetic memory” (open chromatin persists after expression drops) and “chromatin priming” (accessibility opens before expression rises), complicating the activation regulatory code (chromatin-accessibility).
ERG clusters are enriched for cholesterol-biosynthesis genes and NPD GWAS signal; LRG clusters for axon guidance / synaptic processes — surfacing lipid metabolism as an activation-regulated axis of NPD.
Activation-dependent gene regulatory networks (GRN inference) highlight ASD-risk transcription factors MEF2C, TCF4, RORB, MLXIP; CRISPR knockout of RORB, MEF2C, TCF4 validated predicted ASD-target dysregulation (autism).
Mapped stimulation-specific molecular QTLs: eQTLs, chromatin-accessibility QTLs (caQTLs), and ASoC variants — many detectable only after activation. caQTLs explained ~25% of SCZ heritability vs ~10% for eQTLs, and stimulation-specific QTLs carried stronger GWAS enrichment.
Causal-TWAS (ctwas) integrating these QTLs with GWAS prioritized putatively causal NPD risk genes predominantly under stimulated conditions (e.g. CPT1C for SCZ), and SCZ patient–derived neurons up-regulated cholesterol synthesis upon activation.

Notable claims / data

Cohort/assay: 100 iPSC lines → Day-35 iGlut (NEUROG2) + iGABA (ASCL1+DLX2) neurons + rat astrocytes; 53 mM KCl, 0/1/6 h; 10x Genomics Single-Cell Multiome (GEX+ATAC). Subtype counts: GABA n=251,501; npglut n=159,735; nmglut n=137,564. iGlut/iGABA most resemble second-trimester–to–2-year human neurons.
OCR–gene links: 8,522 OCR–gene pairs (4,017 genes) in GABA, 6,921 (3,625) nmglut, 7,492 (3,651) npglut; 43–49% of “coactivation” OCR–gene pairs overlap activity-by-contact (ABC) enhancer–gene pairs (>2.5-fold enrichment). ~150,000–300,000 OCR peaks across types/times, 10–22% stimulation-specific; only DA peaks at 1/6 h (not static peaks) showed NPD GWAS enrichment.
GRN/TF: 145 candidate early-response TF regulators (half shared across all cell types); only 6 of 64 late-response TFs shared across cell types (late response is more cell-type-specific, as with LRGs). GABA-specific TCF4 (SCZ master regulator) elevated even before stimulation.
eQTL: 1,316–4,113 eGenes per context (cell type × time); 8–11% of eGenes only in stimulated states; eGene numbers larger from stimulated conditions; 19–61% of eGenes shared with GTEx brain; effect sizes correlate r ≈ 0.71–0.76 with GTEx cerebellum. Dynamic eGenes: 288–976 across contexts; dynamic eQTLs more cell-type-specific and less shared with GTEx than static — i.e. activation reveals QTLs missed by bulk brain.
caQTL/ASoC: 1,800–11,600 cPeaks (≥1 caQTL) per context, >2× more upon stimulation; 5,461–9,832 caQTLs dynamic. 4,900–18,700 ASoC variants across nine contexts; caQTL vs ASoC effect sizes r=0.82; ASoC enriched 30–80-fold in eQTLs of the matching context. Stimulation-specific ASoC enriched in enhancers and AP-1 (FOS/JUNB) motifs at 1/6 h vs ASCL1/DLX1/2, CUX2 at 0 h.
Heritability: caQTLs explained substantially larger NPD h²g than eQTLs (≈25% vs ≈10% for SCZ cPeaks), even controlling for eQTL effects — chromatin variants capture regulatory effects expression QTLs miss.
cTWAS: SCZ had most candidate genes (48 at PIP>0.5; 19 high-confidence PIP>0.8). Most SCZ/NPD cTWAS genes are “dynamic” (PIP higher in stimulated states). Only a few overlap GTEx-brain-derived TWAS genes (e.g. FOXN2, NPIPB2, TDRD6, ZNF823) — stimulation-driven eQTLs discover risk genes static brain QTLs miss. MADL1L SCZ cPeak driven by a single caQTL.
Cholesterol/SCZ: CPT1C (carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1C) is a SCZ cTWAS gene whose eQTL effect appears chiefly at 1 h stimulation in nmglut; it maintains neuronal lipid metabolism for synaptic activity. SCZ C6 ERG module (cholesterol-biosynthesis-enriched, NPD-GWAS-enriched): 5 of 6 cholesterol genes (e.g. INSIG1, HMGCS1, MSMO1, HMGCR, CYP51A1) differentially expressed in SCZ neurons only upon activation; SREBF1/SREBF2 TFs (lipid/cholesterol regulators) have targets enriched for ASD genes.
Caveats (authors): iPSC QTL mapping may be underpowered; KCl is a strong, possibly non-physiological proxy for in vivo neural activation; only two stimulation time points; some activity-dependent genes (e.g. FOS) show diurnal rhythmicity that could confound; statistical prioritization ≠ definitive causal inference — functional validation still required.

Connections

Activation as the missing context. This is the wiki’s first population-scale activity-dependent genetics dataset — it operationalizes neuronal-activation and supplies the dynamic / context-specific QTL evidence that static post-mortem brain QTL atlases (e.g. bryois-2022-cell-type-specific-cis-eqtls-in-eight, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls) structurally cannot see.
Chromatin > expression for heritability. caQTLs/ASoC explaining ~2.5× more SCZ h²g than eQTLs extends chromatin-accessibility as the prime substrate for noncoding-variant interpretation, and speaks to the missing-heritability debate (context-specific regulatory variance is part of the “missing” share).
Causal-gene prioritization. ctwas here is the disease-mapping engine, a cousin of TWAS/isotwas; it complements direct functional assays (massively-parallel-reporter-assay, pooled-crispr-screen) — and the paper’s own CRISPR-KO validation of RORB/MEF2C/TCF4 closes that loop for autism.
Cholesterol thesis. cholesterol-and-brain-disorders connects an unexpected metabolic axis (lipid/cholesterol synthesis, CPT1C) to SCZ/ASD, joining the synaptic/developmental story that dominates psychiatric-genetics.
A resource-tagged iPSC-neuron activation atlas, sibling to the chromatin-accessibility / single-cell atlases the artificial-intelligence topic’s models learn from.

Open questions

Does KCl depolarization faithfully recapitulate physiological/in-vivo neural activity, and would patterned or receptor-driven stimulation (and more time points) reveal additional dynamic QTLs?
Is cholesterol/lipid dysregulation a cause or consequence of NPD-relevant neuronal activation — and could cholesterol-targeting drugs (the CPT1C/SREBF axis) act on clinical symptoms?
How much further NPD “missing heritability” is hidden in other unmodeled contexts (developmental stage, other cell types, other stimuli)?
Will functional validation confirm the cTWAS-prioritized causal genes (e.g. CPT1C, MADL1L, ZNF823) beyond statistical prioritization?

wiki/sources/liang-2026-single-cell-reso.title: liang-2026-single-cell-resolution-of-splicing-regulation
wiki/sources/liang-2026-single-cell-reso.content:
Single-cell resolution of splicing regulation in peripheral blood mononuclear cells uncovers heterogeneity-driven mechanisms underlying human complex traits
One-line: A single-cell splicing-QTL atlas of ~980 European individuals from the OneK1K cohort, mapping cell-type-specific, sex- and age-biased splicing-qtl across 13 immune cell types and colocalizing them with GWAS loci for 30 complex traits to show splicing as a mechanism linking susceptibility variants to disease.
Key takeaways

Builds the first European-ancestry atlas of alternative-splicing and splicing-qtl across peripheral immune cells at single-cell resolution, leveraging the OneK1K cohort (980 individuals with matched genotyping + scRNA-seq) across 13 immune cell types (functional-single-cell-genomics).
Identifies 3,337 independent cis-sQTLs in 2,721 cis-sGenes, with subsets showing distinct sex-biased (18) and age-biased (144) patterns — interindividual variation in splicing was more strongly influenced by age than sex.
Colocalization with GWAS for 30 polygenic traits found 3,297 significant shared signals across the 13 cell types; autoimmune diseases (especially Type 1 Diabetes) showed markedly higher enrichment than metabolic or oncologic traits, demonstrating splicing regulation as a route from genome-wide-association-study susceptibility loci to complex traits (splicing-and-disease).
cis-sQTLs and cis-eQTLs are largely distinct classes: ~half of cis-sGenes overlap cis-eGenes but 84% of overlapping gene–cell-type combinations involve completely distinct variant sets, and 80% of paired eQTL/sQTL variants are in limited LD (R²<0.8). cis-sGenes skew toward lncRNAs and neuro-related pathways; cis-eGenes skew protein-coding and immune pathways.
trans-sQTL analysis identified 602 trans-sGenes (76% cell-type-specific); ~60% of lead trans-sQTL variants lie within 1 Mb of a cis-eGene, supporting distal splicing regulation via proximal transcriptional control.
A worked trans mechanism: genetic variants exert distal effects on RPS24 splicing by cis-regulating expression of the splicing cofactor IVNS1ABP, which interacts with the splicing-factors HNRNPK to regulate splicing (rna-binding-proteins) — supported by eCLIP, motif scanning and mediation analysis.

Notable claims / data

Data processing: 483,968 autosomal SNPs (1,037 samples) imputed to 5,580,512 SNPs (R²>0.8, MAF>0.05); 960,589 cells from 980 samples after QC, clustered into 13 usable immune cell types (B/T/NK/monocyte subsets). Median 2,015 AS genes detected per cell type; 82% of exonic bases covered; 26% of reads junction-spanning (3′ droplet scRNA-seq).
Splice-junction validation: 49.5% of OneK1K junctions matched GENCODE donor+acceptor exactly, 9.7% novel; 94.3% documented in Snaptron; canonical motif enrichment (AG|GU 5′SS, AG|G 3′SS).
Recapitulated known AS: CTLA4 exon skipping generating soluble sCTLA-4 peaks in CD4Naive; dynamic AS in monocyte cM→ncM differentiation (LST1 exons 5-6 linear inclusion; AIF1 alternative 5′SS quadratic).
RBP motif analysis of sVariant-flanking sequence showed altered binding for 130 RBPs; SR and hnRNP families exert strong effects across all cell types; HNRNPL/HNRNPLL modulate CD8B splicing in CD8GZMH cells.
Replication: OneK1K independent cis-sQTLs replicated strongly in GTEx whole blood (mean π1 = 0.82); replicated sQTLs were more cross-cell-type and larger effect. Saturation model predicts a ceiling of ~742 detectable cis-sGenes per cell type.
GWAS colocalization exemplars: SP140 (Crohn’s disease) isoform ENST00000343805 lacking exon 7 → NF-κB de-repression; CLEC2D variant-4 (T1D) retained in ER, lacks C-type lectin domain, weakens CD161 checkpoint; CASP8 DED-only isoform (breast cancer) attenuates apoptotic signaling; SP3 short isoform (height); FDPS junction (Crohn’s, age-specific, strongest in young — absent from GENCODE); SYNRG rs12944821 creates a canonical 3′SS across 11 cell types (lymphocyte count).
Study caveats stated by authors: no wet-lab validation; imputed GWAS stats miss many splice-site bases; Northern European ancestry limits generalizability.

Connections

A population-scale, immune-focused splicing-qtl resource complementing the small-molecule and disease reviews in this ingest (desterro-2020-targeting-mrna-processing-as-an, nikom-2023-alternative-splicing-in-neurodegenerative-disease); grounds how splicing mediates genome-wide-association-study risk, extending splicing-and-disease.
Methodologically overlaps the percent-spliced-in / junction-quantification framing and uses snaptron and DEXSeq-style testing; sits alongside other single-cell functional-genomics work under functional-single-cell-genomics.
The cis-sQTL vs cis-eQTL distinction reinforces that splicing is an independent regulatory layer (a recurring wiki theme from baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator); RBP regulators (SR/hnRNP, HNRNPK, HNRNPL, IVNS1ABP, ptbp1) tie to splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins.
Introduces new entities: the onek1k-cohort (980 European individuals, matched genotype + scRNA-seq) and the splicing cofactor ivns1abp.

Open questions

Which colocalized splicing events are causal for disease vs bystander, given no wet-lab validation was performed?
How much do imputation gaps at splice-site bases hide the true fraction of GWAS variants acting via canonical splice-motif disruption — would WGS recover more?
Do these European-ancestry sQTL maps transfer to other ancestries, and how do sex/age-biased sQTLs interact to shape demographic disparities in autoimmune risk?
Is the IVNS1ABP→HNRNPK→RPS24 trans-regulatory axis (RPS24 cancer-associated microexon, EMT) generalizable as a template for distal splicing control across the genome?

wiki/sources/liao-2023-draft-human-pange.title: liao-2023-draft-human-pangenome-reference
wiki/sources/liao-2023-draft-human-pange.content:
A draft human pangenome reference
One-line: The Human Pangenome Reference Consortium’s first-draft hprc-pangenome — 47 phased diploid assemblies from genetically diverse individuals — that replaces the single-mosaic GRCh38 reference to reduce reference bias in variant discovery.
Key takeaways

Presents 47 fully phased, diploid assemblies from genetically diverse individuals; assemblies cover >99% of expected sequence per genome and are >99% accurate at structural and base-pair levels. Samples were drawn largely from 1000 Genomes lymphoblastoid cell lines with parental WGS for haplotype phasing.
Adds 119 Mb of euchromatic polymorphic sequence and 1,115 gene duplications relative to GRCh38; roughly 90 Mb of the additional bases derive from structural variation, addressing the “streetlamp effect” observational bias of a single reference.
Using the draft pangenome to analyse short-read data reduced small-variant discovery errors by 34% and increased structural variants detected per haplotype by 104% versus GRCh38-based workflows.
Three graph-construction methods were applied and compared: Minigraph (SV-only), Minigraph-Cactus (MC, base-level), and PanGenome Graph Builder (PGGB, reference-free all-to-all alignment).

Notable claims / data

Assembly quality: haploid X-containing assemblies averaged 3.04 Gb (99.3% of T2T-CHM13); average contig NG50 of 40 Mb (vs 56 Mb for GRCh38); Yak k-mer QV averaged 53.57 (~1 error per 227,509 bases); average switch error 0.67%, Hamming error 0.79%.
Flagger (a new read-based misassembly-detection pipeline) flagged only 0.88% (26.4 Mb) of each assembly as unreliable; estimated correct assembly of 95.4% of alpha satellites, 97.7% of segmental duplications, 94.3% of VNTRs.
Graph variation: MC graph held ~22M small variants and ~67,000 SVs (PGGB 21M/73,000; Minigraph 75,000 SVs); ~90 Mb of non-reference sequence in SV sites (MC). Alu, L1 and ERV SVs were largely biallelic; VNTRs frequently had 3+ alleles.
Short-read genotyping: Giraffe + DeepVariant on the MC graph made 21,700 errors on HG005 (30x) vs 36,144 for GRCh38/BWA-MEM and 26,852 for Dragen; in challenging medically relevant genes, SNP F1 reached 0.985 vs <0.976. DeepTrio further reduced HG005 errors to 10,098.
Community resource: Giraffe + DeepVariant applied to 3,202 1000 Genomes samples yielded on average 64,000 more small variants per sample than the 1KG catalogue; PanGenie genotyped SVs (413,809 SV alleles) with high leave-one-out concordance. Detected gene conversion at RHCE exon 2 (~25% of haplotypes) and resolved KCNE1 despite a GRCh38 false duplication.

Connections

A population-scale reference resource whose improved genotyping in complex/medically relevant loci and noncoding regions relates to work on noncoding-variants (noncoding-variants) and to population-scale variant catalogues used in genome-wide-association-study (genome-wide-association-study) contexts.
Built as a subset of the planned HPRC panel of 700 haplotypes across 350 individuals; complements the single-haplotype T2T-CHM13 finished genome.

Open questions

How well will graph-based references be adopted by standard clinical and GWAS pipelines given the added computational complexity of the three graph representations?
The draft omits much heterochromatic/centromeric sequence from the MC graph; how will completeness and reference bias change as the panel scales to 350 individuals?
Which graph-construction philosophy (reference-anchored MC vs reference-free PGGB) best balances usability, accuracy and unbiased representation of diverse haplotypes?

wiki/sources/lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-compre.title: lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method
wiki/sources/lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-compre.content:
PSI-Sigma: a comprehensive splicing-detection method for short-read and long-read RNA-seq analysis
One-line: Introduces PSI-Sigma, a splicing-detection tool with a new PSI index (PSIΣ) that quantifies Percent Spliced-In for individual alternative exons across all isoforms, benchmarked against rMATS, SUPPA2 and Whippet using real (non-simulated) RNA-seq data from synthetic RNA Sequins.
Key takeaways

PSI-Sigma computes percent-spliced-in using a modified PSI (Σ) equation that considers splice-junction reads of all isoforms between two constitutive exons, so a single alternative exon can carry multiple PSI values depending on the isoform it belongs to.
Unlike two-isoform tools (rmats, MISO) and isoform-resolution tools (SUPPA2), PSI-Sigma handles multiple-exon-skipping (MES) events and intron-retention (IR) events, where other tools fail.
Benchmarking used real RNA-seq data from spliced synthetic genes (RNA Sequins, 164 polyA RNAs in two mixtures with known concentration ratios = ground truth), avoiding the in silico-simulation limitation of prior splicing-methods evaluations.
PSI-Sigma also works on nanopore long-read RNA-seq (the other three tools are short-read only), demonstrated on U87 glioblastoma RNA spiked with RNA Sequins.
Tool entity: psi-sigma. Software at github.com/wososa/PSI-Sigma.

Notable claims / data

Short-read RNA Sequins benchmark (DPSI ≠ 0 criterion): recall — PSI-Sigma 1.0, Whippet 1.0, rMATS 0.64, SUPPA2 0.50; precision — PSI-Sigma 0.53, Whippet 0.40, rMATS 0.56, SUPPA2 0.52. rMATS/SUPPA2 missed up to ~50% of AS genes (cannot detect MES; actual recall for MES events was 0 for both).
DPSI accuracy vs ground truth: PSI-Sigma most accurate (r = 0.9441, P = 3.93e-30); SUPPA2 least accurate (r = 0.5700). For 8 IR events, PSI-Sigma highest correlation (r = 0.9465).
False positive rate at 10% DPSI cutoff: PSI-Sigma 0.12, Whippet 0.28, rMATS 0.08, SUPPA2 0.18; at 30% cutoff: PSI-Sigma 0, Whippet 0.04, rMATS 0, SUPPA2 0.10. PSI-Sigma FPRs differ significantly from Whippet (P=0.0002) and SUPPA2 (P=3.09e-8) but not from rMATS (P=0.0721).
Human transcriptome (Ensembl 94): PSI-Sigma identified MES events in 7029 genes (90.18% protein-coding) and IR events in 9175 genes vs only 3574 for rMATS v4.0.2; PSI-Sigma recovered 98.35% of rMATS’s IR genes plus 5660 additional. Validated AFMID exon-6 isoform switch in HCC (TCGA-LIHC, 50 normal + 369 HCC) where rMATS/Whippet could not report the two opposite DPSI values.
Long-read: nanopore GridION runs gave 2.20M (Run 1) and 2.51M (Run 2) reads; error rate ~12.5% vs <0.2% short-read; only 48 reads (0.0022%) of Run 1 ambiguous between human/Sequins genomes. Long-read DPSI vs concentration r=0.9245; short- vs long-read gene-expression r=0.9045. ~0.76M long reads covered ≥1 read for 11,420 protein-coding genes; ~1M long reads ≈ 10–100× fewer reads needed than short-read for PSI analysis.
Speed/caveat: PSI-Sigma single-threaded (~10 h for a 3-vs-3 comparison on pre-aligned BAMs) vs Whippet ~5 h and rMATS v4.0.2 ~30 min (8 threads). Whippet generates no P-values; PSI-Sigma and rMATS give adjusted P-values for multi-sample statistical comparison.

Connections

Direct benchmark competitor to rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); both perform similarly on single-alternative-exon events, but PSI-Sigma extends to MES/IR.
Methodological sibling to other splicing-quantification tools catalogued under splicing-methods; central concept is percent-spliced-in (PSI/DPSI).
Touches splicing-and-disease / cancer biomarkers via AFMID, TERT, SRSF2 examples and TCGA-LIHC analysis; mentions splicing factors srsf1 (SRSF1 last-intron IR) and SRSF2.
Methods/tools-survey context overlaps with sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.

Open questions

Does PSI-Sigma’s per-isoform PSI generalize to whole human cohorts at scale, given its lack of multithreading?
How does the ~12–13% nanopore error rate cap reliable IR/MES detection as long-read depth and base-calling improve?
Could rMATS/SUPPA2 close the MES/IR gap by adopting the PSIΣ index, as the authors suggest?
How robust is the RNA Sequins ground truth as a general benchmark for newer splicing tools beyond the four compared here?

wiki/sources/lindhout-2024-a-molecular-a.title: lindhout-2024-a-molecular-and-cellular-perspective
wiki/sources/lindhout-2024-a-molecular-a.content:
A molecular and cellular perspective on human brain evolution and tempo
One-line: A Nature Perspective arguing that human brain specializations arise through a progressively slowed developmental tempo — for which the authors coin the terms “bradychrony” (slowed) and “tachychrony” (accelerated) — acting together with human-specific genetics, gene-regulatory rewiring and cellular innovations across distinct stages of neurodevelopment.
Key takeaways

Frames human brain evolution as a multi-stage developmental process (neural stem cell proliferation → neurogenesis → neuronal maturation), where most differences from other species are quantitative shifts in conserved, homologous cell types rather than wholly novel cells.
Introduces two new terms — bradychrony (slowed tempo of a developmental event between species or brain regions) and tachychrony (accelerated tempo) — to replace the loosely-used “neoteny,” arguing developmental delay is not merely retention of juvenile traits but a pervasive slowing throughout embryonic and postnatal development.
Human developmental tempo has become progressively more bradychronic along the lineage (rodents → primates → great apes → humans), and the degree of slowing correlates with brain size, cell number/diversity and connectivity; tempo differences appear to be cell-intrinsic.
Later neuronal-maturation stages (synaptic pruning, myelination) are the most disproportionately protracted in humans, suggesting this extreme bradychrony is a relatively recent evolutionary event.
Molecular drivers are dual: human-specific protein-coding changes/gene duplications (TKTL1, NOTCH2NL, SRGAP2C, ARHGAP11B) and non-coding gene-regulatory changes (human-accelerated regions/HARs, hCONDELs, enhancer rewiring), validated increasingly via in vitro models, comparative functional genetics and high-throughput single-cell profiling.
A central theme: extended developmental windows give more room for environmental influence, behavioural plasticity and cultural transmission, but also heighten susceptibility to neuropsychiatric disease.

Notable claims / data

The human brain is ~3× larger than chimpanzee/gorilla/bonobo brains; hominin endocranial volume rose stepwise from ~450 cm³ (Australopithecus) → ~600 cm³ (early Homo) → ~1,000 cm³ (H. erectus) → 1,400–1,500 cm³ (H. sapiens / Neanderthals).
The human neocortex underwent ~50% more expansion relative to the rest of the brain vs other primates; white matter expands disproportionately (universal power law across mammals), with extra human-specific prefrontal white-matter increase implying more connectivity.
Interneuron fraction: ~15% of cortical neurons in mouse (invariant across areas) vs 30–40% in primate association cortex; humans exceed marmosets in motor-cortex interneuron number, indicating a recent lineage change.
Tempo metrics: at birth the human brain is 28% of adult size vs 40% in chimpanzee; in vivo neurogenic window ~4 months in humans (~2× macaque, ~10× mouse); neural-stem-cell/tangential-expansion phase ~4 weeks human vs 2.5 weeks macaque vs 2 days mouse. Full brain development (gastrulation→myelination completion) takes ~30 years in humans (~half the lifespan) vs ~30% of lifespan in chimp/macaque and ~5% in mouse.
Maturation tempo: initial synaptogenesis ~5 yr (human) / 4 yr (chimp) / 3 mo (macaque); late maturation (pruning + myelination) ~30 yr (human) / 10 yr (chimp) / 4.5 yr (macaque). Within humans, prefrontal cortex is slowest (synaptogenesis peaks ~15 months postnatal, pruning to ~30 yr) vs auditory cortex (peak ~3 months, pruning to ~12 yr).
Tempo is cell-intrinsic: species-specific timing is preserved when human neurons are xenografted into mouse or co-cultured with NHP neurons; candidate mechanisms include protein stability/turnover, mitochondrial metabolism (its stimulation accelerates human neuronal maturation), and a chromatin-modifier epigenetic barrier (EZH2, EHMT1/2, DOT1L) gating maturation timing.
Protein-coding human-specific drivers: TKTL1 (Lys→Arg, post-Neanderthal) boosts basal radial glia output; NOTCH2NL paralogues expand progenitors and are linked to brain-size/autism structural variants; SRGAP2B/C modulate synaptic maturation and prolong SYNGAP1 upregulation (bradychronic spines); ARHGAP11B enlarges/gyrifies neocortex in mouse and marmoset.
Regulatory drivers: HARs are the fastest-evolving conserved non-coding elements, many act as neurodevelopmental enhancers (e.g. HARE5 enhancer of FRIZZLED8 accelerates progenitor cell cycle and enlarges mouse brain); a hominin-specific SOX5-binding-site deletion in a CBLN2 enhancer drives human-specific prefrontal CBLN2 upregulation and spine formation. Caveat: even the strongest human adaptive-trait cases have largely relied on correlative evidence, now beginning to shift toward functional validation.

Connections

brain-development-neurogenomics — this Perspective is a foundational conceptual anchor for the topic: it organizes brain evolution by developmental stage and supplies the bradychrony/tachychrony vocabulary.
Pairs with single-cell atlases of the developing human brain as the conceptual/evolutionary framing for their cell-type findings — e.g. the protein-level companion wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of (which independently flags the intermediate-progenitor→neuron transition as disease-vulnerable, consistent with this paper’s emphasis on conserved-cell-type shifts and neurodevelopmental disease susceptibility).
Touches developmental gene-regulation themes shared with the developing-brain expression/isoform siblings walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna.

Open questions

What are the global vs stage-specific molecular clocks setting tempo, and how much of human bradychrony follows allometric scaling vs is slower than expected for our size?
Are human-specific maturation features (extended axons, dendritic arborization, spine number) shared with other great apes or truly human-specific, given the ethical/technical limits on great-ape fetal tissue?
How do structural variants and 3D-genome reorganization (enhancer hijacking near HARs) causally rewire neurodevelopmental gene regulation in their endogenous context, beyond reporter-assay evidence?
Do cerebellar and thalamic (non-cortical) cell types diversify in human evolution, and how does neuronal activity feed back to shape these protracted developmental processes?

wiki/sources/liu-2018-statistical-framew.title: liu-2018-statistical-framework-mapping-risk
wiki/sources/liu-2018-statistical-framew.content:
A Statistical Framework for Mapping Risk Genes from De Novo Mutations in Whole-Genome-Sequencing Studies
One-line: Introduces tada-a, a Bayesian framework that extends de novo mutation gene mapping into non-coding sequence by learning informative functional annotations, and applies it to autism WGS trios.
Key takeaways

Presents tada-a (TADA-Annotations), a major advance over the coding-only tada-method, modeling de novo mutation counts as Poisson with position-level rates elevated inside risk genes in proportion to functional annotations.
Designed for noncoding-variants: it incorporates many overlapping annotations (conservation, brain enhancer marks, splicing predictions) and learns from data which are enriched for pathogenic mutations, avoiding arbitrary variant categories or weights.
Combines coding and non-coding signal at the gene level by multiplying Bayes factors, letting a WGS study borrow strength from published whole-exome (WES) results, and supports meta-analysis across studies with study-specific background mutation models.
Applied to 300+ autism-affected family trios across five WGS studies, it links risk to brain H3K27ac active-enhancer marks (a cis-regulatory-elements / chromatin-accessibility signal) and to variants damaging splicing.
Estimates that regulatory non-coding de novo mutations contribute roughly a third of de novo autism risk, and nominates four novel ASD risk genes at FDR < 0.1.

Notable claims / data

Analyzed five WGS studies (314 affected subjects; 57-63 DNMs/subject; Sanger validation 85-94%) plus a 700-subject non-ASD control cohort for burden analysis; includes 32 unpublished Han Chinese ASD trios.
After feature selection over 12 annotations, only brain H3K27ac, brain H3K27ac + GERP > 2, and SPIDEX-predicted splicing were significant, with jointly estimated relative risks of 1.54, 3.42, and 3.22 respectively.
Non-coding SNVs (less-conserved regulatory, conserved regulatory, and splicing) explain ~38% of de novo autism risk; the authors call this conservative. Each individual mutation class explains only a small fraction of total genetic risk.
Four “novel ASD genes” identified: NRXN1, APBB1, TANC2, PNPLA7; three (NRXN1, APBB1, TANC2) are FMRP targets, haploinsufficient, and highly brain-expressed (hypergeometric p = 0.00032, 0.00293, 0.049).
Running TADA-A on control data yields relative risks near or below 1 for all annotations, supporting ASD-specificity of the signal.
Baseline mutation rates taken from a trinucleotide table (Samocha et al.); DNMs collapsed into 50-bp windows for calibration; genes assigned priors converted from published WES Bayes factors (0.06 baseline ASD-gene fraction).

Connections

Directly generalizes tada-method and uses the cadd-score as one of its 12 candidate annotations.
Treats ASD liability as a continuous trait and partitions variance using quantitative-genetics calculations on a liability scale, connecting to the liability-threshold-model.
Motivated by genome-wide-association-study evidence that disease variants often lie in non-coding regulatory sequence; relevant to variant-effect-prediction and psychiatric-genetics.
Explores recurrent de novo SNVs within topologically-associating-domains and brain enhancers as additional units of analysis.

Open questions

The ~38% non-coding risk estimate is deliberately conservative; the true contribution and the cell types most relevant to autism etiology remain to be pinned down with larger cohorts.
SPIDEX-based splicing annotation captures signal but many bases near exon/intron junctions do not regulate splicing; better splicing predictors could sharpen this.
With only ~300 trios non-coding signals are weak and reliant on informative priors from WES; how much of the framework’s power depends on those priors versus WGS data alone?

wiki/sources/liu-2024-single-cell-rna-se.title: liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral
wiki/sources/liu-2024-single-cell-rna-se.content:
Single-cell RNA sequencing of peripheral blood links cell-type-specific regulation of splicing to autoimmune and inflammatory diseases
One-line: A population-scale single-cell splicing study of ~1 million PBMCs from 474 healthy Eastern/Southeastern/South Asian donors (the aida cohort) that maps cell-type-specific, sex-biased and ancestry-biased alternative splicing plus 11,577 cis-sQTLs, and links these to autoimmune/inflammatory disease heritability — validating an Asian-specific TCHP sQTL for Graves’ disease.
Key takeaways

Uses 10x Genomics 5′ scRNA-seq plus the endogenous “exon painting” phenomenon (stochastic mRNA cleavage and recapping creating multiple 5′ ends per isoform) to gain exon coverage that standard 3′-biased droplet scRNA-seq lacks, enabling splicing-qtl mapping at single-cell resolution across 21 PBMC subtypes from 474 donors.
Two-level alternative splicing (see alternative-splicing) quantification: SpliZ at the single-cell level (median 1,146 AS genes/cell, 4.3× the OneK1K estimate of 267) and leafcutter at the pseudobulk level (median 7,721 AS genes/pseudobulk, saturating at ~11,500 genes; R²=0.92 sigmoid fit to cell count).
Identifies 11,577 independent cis-sQTLs (10,874 protein-coding + 703 lncRNA sGenes, FDR<0.05; ~39.9% of tested genes), 607 trans-sGenes (FDR<0.01) and 107 dynamic sQTLs along B cell differentiation; most differential splicing and many sQTLs are cell-type-specific.
A cell-type-specific trans-regulatory relationship: a cis-eQTL for HNRNPLL (a master regulator of T cell activation-induced splicing) colocalizes with a trans-sQTL for PTPRC/CD45 (rs6751481, H4≈1.00 in naive CD4+ T), with higher HNRNPLL expression driving shorter PTPRC isoforms — recapitulating the CD45RA→CD45RO isoform switch in T cell development.
cis-sQTL effects are enriched in autoimmune/inflammatory disease heritability (S-LDSC: SLE, atopic dermatitis, Graves’ disease, RA highest); colocalization with 20 Asian-focused GWAS traits nominates 563 putative effector genes (see splicing-and-disease).
Functional validation: an Asian-specific sQTL (rs74416240) disrupting the 5′ splice site of TCHP exon 4 colocalizes with Graves’ disease GWAS; a K562 minigene assay confirmed the alternative (A) allele induces intron retention while the reference (G) allele yields the normal isoform.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: AIDA Data Freeze v.1 — 474 donors (Japan, Korea, Singapore: Singaporean Chinese/Malay/Indian), avg 1,959 cells/donor, avg 53,846 reads/cell of which 46.9% overlapped a splice junction; median 85.3% of exonic bases covered per gene (IQR 51.8–97.3%).
QC/replication: 59.3% of pseudobulk introns canonical in GENCODE v.32; 85.9% of short-read junctions replicated in PacBio MAS-seq long-read data (4 donors); ~93%/98.8%/99.5% of junctions seen in >1,000/>100/>10 Snaptron samples. cis-sQTLs replicated at π1 ≈ 0.70–1 across BLUEPRINT, DICE, GTEx whole-blood/LCL and ImmuNexUT, with ~14.3% new vs ImmuNexUT (largest existing PBMC sQTL set).
Sex/ancestry splicing: 48 sex-biased differential splicing events (DSEs) across 32 genes (e.g. female-biased FLNA isoform ENST00000498491 in T/NK cells); 1,031 ancestry-biased DSEs across 509 genes. SNP rs11064437 disrupts the 3′ splice site of SPSB2 exon 2 (AG|C→AA|C) with MAF declining EAS 30.7% → SEA 13.9% → SAS 3.4%; the EAS-biased alt intron was absent from GENCODE.
sQTL context-dependence: 27 sex-biased sQTLs across 20 genes; 8 Malay-biased and 19 Indian-biased cis-sQTLs; allelic heterogeneity up to 16.1% of sGenes with ≥2 independent variants. Sample size (r=0.95) and library size (r=0.96) independently drive sQTL discovery.
Dynamic sQTLs across B-cell pseudotime (naive → IGHMhi memory → IGHMlo memory, 52,964 cells): 865 dynamic introns (3 modes: stepwise/linear/quadratic; e.g. PAX5A↓/PAX5B↑, PTPRC exon 4↓, DOCK8 exon 1 transient) and 107 dynamic sQTLs (62 strengthening, 37 weakening, 11 transient); CD83 dynamic sQTL colocalizes with RA (H4=0.980), BCL2A1 with lymphocyte count (H4=0.848).
trans-sQTLs are more cell-type-specific than cis: 393/607 (64.7%) appear in only one cell type; 17 cis-eQTL↔trans-sQTL colocalization events (H4>0.75). A lead-SNP switch between naive CD4+ T (rs6751481, promoter) and CD4+ TEM (rs74258942, enhancer; Asian-biased) confirmed by SuSiE fine-mapping; SMR links HNRNPLL expression to memory CD4+ T proportion (P=9.21×10⁻⁷, HEIDI P=0.103).
TCHP/Graves’: lead GWAS risk variant rs74416240 has an East-Asian-biased minor allele (AIDA + 1000 Genomes), sits in the TCHP exon 4–5 5′ splice site, and shows cell-type-specific GD-GWAS/sQTL colocalization across seven cell types.

Connections

A single-cell, immune-cell, population-genetics application built on existing splicing-quantification tooling — pseudobulk via leafcutter (cf. li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna) rather than a new algorithm; the disease-genetics angle parallels zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain-region sQTLs) but resolves cell types instead of tissue regions and adds ancestry diversity.
Extends splicing-qtl and splicing-and-disease into single-cell, multi-ancestry territory; complements the population-splicing line of park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative and disease-splicing reviews (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer).
Mechanistically centers RNA-binding-protein trans-regulation (HNRNPLL→PTPRC) relevant to rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors; splice-site-disrupting variants (SPSB2, TCHP) connect to splice-sites.
Introduces the aida atlas as a reusable multi-ancestry immune single-cell + sQTL resource.

Open questions

Does the East-Asian-biased TCHP exon 4 sQTL causally raise Graves’ disease risk in vivo, and through what TCHP isoform function?
How much of the disease heritability enrichment attributable to sQTLs is orthogonal to eQTLs in these same cell types, and which effector genes among the 563 are druggable?
Can full-length single-cell technologies eventually replace the “exon painting” trick, and would they recover isoforms missed by 5′ coverage?
How well do sQTLs and ancestry-biased splicing events discovered in Asian donors transfer to (or differ from) European-ancestry cohorts like OneK1K?

wiki/sources/logsdon-2024-variation-and-.title: logsdon-2024-variation-and-evolution-of-complete
wiki/sources/logsdon-2024-variation-and-.content:
The variation and evolution of complete human centromeres
One-line: Long-read sequencing completes every centromere of a second human genome (CHM1), establishing a first baseline for centromeric sequence, structural and epigenetic variation and, with orthologous primate centromeres, a model for centromere evolution.
Key takeaways

Completely sequenced and assembled all centromeres from a second human genome, the complete hydatidiform mole cell line CHM1, using ~66-fold PacBio HiFi and ~98-fold Oxford Nanopore (ONT) ultra-long coverage, and compared it to the finished T2T-CHM13 reference (see chm1-centromere-assemblies).
The two sets of centromeres show at least a 4.1-fold increase in single-nucleotide variation relative to their unique flanks and vary up to 3-fold in size, establishing centromeres as among the most rapidly mutating regions of the genome.
45.8% of centromeric sequence cannot be reliably aligned with standard methods because of the emergence of new alpha-satellite higher-order repeats (HORs); alignability drops further (53.2-55.3%) across 56 diverse human genomes.
DNA methylation and CENP-A chromatin immunoprecipitation define the kinetochore site; 26% of centromeres differ in kinetochore position by more than 500 kb, decoupling epigenetic position from underlying sequence (relates to dna-methylation).
Sequencing 31 orthologous centromeres from chimpanzee, orangutan and macaque reveals near-complete turnover of alpha-satellite HORs across ~25 million years, with idiosyncratic species-specific architectures.

Notable claims / data

Assembly base accuracy estimated at >99.9999% (QV > 60); validated with VerityMap, GAVISUNK (SUNK-based) and an independent Verkko assembly (>99.99% identity).
CHM1 alpha-satellite HOR arrays are ~1.3-fold larger on average than CHM13; 16 of 23 chromosomes are larger in CHM1, chromosome 21 showing the greatest difference (3.6-fold). Human HOR arrays range from 0.03 Mb (chr 4) to 6.5 Mb (chr 11).
Mean sequence identity of alignable HOR arrays is 98.6 +/- 1.6%, versus 99.8 +/- 0.4% for monomeric/diverged alpha-satellite and 99.9 +/- 0.3% approaching euchromatin; chromosomes 19 and X are most conserved.
Eight chromosomes (5, 7, 8, 10-14) have distinctly different HOR array structures; novel HOR variants (e.g. chr 5 D5Z2 four- and six-monomer variants) arose from deletions of an ancestral ten-monomer HOR and form discrete “evolutionary layers”.
Neither CHM1 nor CHM13 is a majority-best match across HPRC/HGSVC genomes; 46.9% of tested haplotypes match CHM1 better than CHM13 - a single genome cannot capture human centromeric diversity.
Species differences: chimpanzee HOR arrays average 67.8% of human size; orangutan arrays are mosaic patchworks of 80-90%-identical blocks; macaque centromeres are large (12.2 +/- 1.6 Mb) dimeric arrays. Chimpanzee chr 5 is unexpectedly SF5-composed.
Phylogenetic reconstruction supports limited-to-no recombination between the short (p) and long (q) arms across centromeres, and a monophyletic origin for novel HORs, enabling estimation of saltatory amplification and mutation rates.
Caveat: both CHM1 and CHM13 are hydatidiform moles (aberrations of development) propagated in culture; structures need confirmation in additional human samples.

Connections

Extends the telomere-to-telomere reference effort beyond the single T2T-CHM13 genome, addressing centromeres that were the most gap-ridden regions and excluded from the Human Pangenome Reference Consortium (HPRC) pangenome.
Complements the topic of genome sequencing and references by providing a second complete, allelically distinct centromere baseline for structural-variation calling against 56 HPRC/HGSVC genomes.

Open questions

How much additional centromeric diversity exists across many individuals per chromosome, beyond two mole-derived haplotypes?
What functional consequences follow from >500 kb shifts in kinetochore position and divergent CpG methylation over structurally identical HORs?
Can saltatory-amplification and mutation-rate estimates from monophyletic novel HORs be generalized to predict per-chromosome evolutionary trajectories?

wiki/sources/lord-2020-autism-spectrum-d.title: lord-2020-autism-spectrum-disorder
wiki/sources/lord-2020-autism-spectrum-d.content:
Autism spectrum disorder
One-line: Authoritative Primer synthesizing the epidemiology, genetics, neurobiology, diagnosis, and lifespan management of autism spectrum disorder (ASD), framing it as a highly heritable, heterogeneous neurodevelopmental condition.
Key takeaways

ASD is a highly heritable neurodevelopmental disorder defined by impairments in social communication plus restricted/repetitive behaviours and sensory anomalies beginning early in life; the wiki concept page is autism.
Genetics is dominated by rare, de novo, heterozygous coding mutations (single-base to large CNVs) rather than the common-variant architecture typical of adult-onset psychiatric-genetics; >100 genes/loci now confidently associated.
Autism risk genes converge on two broad functional buckets — synaptic structure/function and chromatin modification/transcriptional regulation — and on a mid-fetal glutamatergic cortical neuron nexus (spatiotemporal convergence).
Prospective “baby sibling” studies show brain-structural and EEG differences at 6 months, before behavioural symptoms emerge, hinting at cascading developmental mechanisms rather than deterministic gene→synapse→symptom models.

Notable claims / data

Worldwide prevalence just under 1% (2010 Global Burden of Disease: ~52 million people, 1 in 132); higher estimates in high-income countries; male:female ratio 2:1 to 5:1 (~4:1).
No clear evidence of a real increase in community prevalence 1990–2010; apparent rises reflect administrative case-finding, not true incidence. Prevalence in mental-health inpatient settings 4–9.9%.
heritability estimated ~40–90% from twin/family studies; among the most heritable common medical conditions.
De novo mutations contribute ~3% of population-level risk but are markedly enriched clinically: ~10–20% of autistic people carry a contributory de novo point mutation or CNV, rising to ~20–30% with added risk factors (female, intellectual disability, multiple unaffected siblings, seizures).
A single de novo germline loss-of-function point mutation can convey more risk than the top decile of schizophrenia polygenic risk. Monogenic syndromes (fragile X, tuberous sclerosis, Rett/MECP2, Angelman) account for <10% of clinical cases.
Highest-confidence idiopathic-autism gene-therapy targets named: SCN2A and CHD8 (see chd8).
Neurobiology: greater cortical surface-area growth 6–12 months and larger brain volume 12–24 months in later-diagnosed toddlers; EEG/MEG converge on excitation/inhibition imbalance and reduced long-range functional connectivity; no MRI or EEG measure is yet a validated biomarker (replication/heterogeneity problems).
Environmental associations (contributory, not causal): advanced parental age, birth hypoxia, maternal obesity, gestational diabetes, short interpregnancy interval, prenatal valproate. Clearly NOT associated: vaccination.

Connections

Complements chd8-focused developmental work in nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8 and SCN2A restoration in tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related.
Provides clinical/genetic framing for autism transcriptomic and developmental studies: schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism, zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of, velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal.
Genetic-architecture themes connect to psychiatric-genetics and cross-disorder overlap discussed in grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across.

Open questions

How do rare large-effect mutations mechanistically produce human social disability given their pleiotropy across brain regions and development, and why the strong sexual dimorphism?
Can spatiotemporal convergence (mid-fetal glutamatergic neurons, striatum) be leveraged for shared-mechanism therapeutics across genetically distinct cases?
Which early (pre-symptomatic) MRI/EEG signatures are genuine endophenotypes versus average-brain artefacts, and can large-scale collaboration yield generalizable biomarkers?

wiki/sources/loupe-2024-multiomic-profil.title: loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor
wiki/sources/loupe-2024-multiomic-profil.content:
Multiomic profiling of transcription factor binding and function in human brain
One-line: A large multiomic ChIP–seq resource (braintf) mapping occupancy of >100 transcription factors directly in human postmortem brain tissue and FANS-sorted neuronal/glial nuclei, integrated with ATAC-seq, RNA-seq and DNA methylation to model TF function and to link specific TFs (notably TBR1, PKNOX1) to neuropsychiatric disease risk.
Key takeaways

First and largest direct measurement of TF binding in human postmortem brain: ChIP–seq for 106 total TFs (96 in bulk, 76 in sorted nuclei) plus histone marks across nine brain regions from four neurotypical donors, paired with ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, phased WGS and DNA methylation — packaged as the braintf resource.
FANS sorting (NeuN+ neurons, Olig2+ oligodendrocytes, NeuN−/Olig2− microglia+astrocytes) de-confounds cell-type signal versus bulk homogenate; for nearly all TFs profiled, no prior brain binding data existed in ENCODE.
Identifies high-occupancy target (HOT) sites — loci bound by many TFs — that are promoter/CpG-island biased and largely cell-type-shared; they carry little TF-specific information and bias both motif discovery and computational TF-binding predictors. Removing them sharpens motif recognition for low-GC factors.
Neuronal TFs SATB2 and TBR1 bind unique, HOT-depleted cis-regulatory-elements and promote neuronal gene expression; 31% of TF-bound regions have no ENCODE cCRE annotation and are enriched for neuron-specific regulatory elements/pathways.
Stratified LD score regression links TF binding sites — especially in NeuN+ neurons (e.g. PKNOX1, TBR1, BCL11A) — to heritability for neuropsychiatric disorders (schizophrenia, bipolar), more strongly than histone marks or ATAC-seq; Alzheimer’s risk localizes to NeuN−/Olig2− (e.g. REST).

Notable claims / data

Union of TF peaks across the four large regions: 239,361 distinct regions spanning 6.94% of hg38 (text also cites 236,311 in Fig. 1); ~49% distal enhancer-like (dELS), 8% promoter-like (PLS), 31% unannotated (“none”). Promoters shared across more cell types than enhancers (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶).
“None” regions enriched for neuron-specific elements: odds ratio 2.9 for overlap with NeuN+ vs bulk ATAC-seq (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶) vs 1.1 (Olig2+) and 1.5 (NeuN−/Olig2−).
TF co-association: PCA on ChIP signal (first 20 PCs = 83.76% variance) yields three clusters — Cluster 1 low promoter-preference/high signal, Cluster 3 promoter-biased (H3K4me3-overlapping), Cluster 2 intermediate (TBR1–SATB2 node). Recovers known interactions (CTCF–RAD21 cohesin, EHMT2–REST, NFIB–NFIC).
HOT sites = top 10% of merged loci by number of bound TFs; 66% are promoter-like CpG-island sequences (OR = 3.81, P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶); shared HOT sites enriched for housekeeping-gene promoters (OR = 8.245). SATB2 binds few-TF regions; HCFC1 binds predominantly HOT sites.
Motifs: from JASPAR 2022, peak motif-match proportion ranged 0.8–86.5%; of 49 factors, 24 were most enriched for their expected motif. Common motifs (e.g. THAP11, NRF/SP families) are GC-rich; removing HOT sites improved enrichment for low-GC motifs (e.g. NFIB motif rose to top-ranked MEME call).
Predictive models (Virtual ChIP–seq, 16 overlapping TFs): bulk-DLPFC auPRs correlated with GM12878 cell-line auPRs (r = 0.69, P = 0.0093); CTCF/NRF1 well predicted, EGR1/REST poorly. auPRs drop sharply when HOT sites removed — models are biased toward easily-predicted promoter/HOT sites and miss neuron-enriched “none” CREs.
Gene-expression modeling (NeuN+ linear models controlling for cobound TF count): most TFs positively affect expression, with largest effect at non-HOT promoters; at HOT promoters individual TFs have minimal effect. TAF1 most enriched for upregulated genes; EHMT2/SIRT1 repressive; TBR1/ZBTB7B dual activator/repressor.
Cell-type-biased TFs: SATB2/TBR1/BCL11A (neurons), OLIG2/SOX8 (oligodendrocytes), MITF (microglia/astrocytes). Example: a NeuN+ PKNOX1 peak overlaps a schizophrenia risk locus at the 3′ end of TSNARE1, linked to neuronal ADGRB1 and ARC expression.
Caveats: only four donors; ChIP–seq quality/skewness covaries with peak count and signal-to-noise; HOT sites should NOT be universally excluded (unlike ENCODE excluded-regions list) since they capture real GC-rich TF behavior; target-gene/distal links rely on a prior single-cell multiomic study (Anderson et al. 2023).

Connections

A functional-single-cell-genomics / regulatory resource: complements zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory and groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at — all interpret noncoding cis-regulatory-elements, but this one supplies direct TF occupancy maps (ChIP–seq) rather than MPRA-measured activity.
Sibling human-brain regulatory/psychiatric-genetics source to li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (PsychENCODE) and zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory; uses cell-type FANS sorting like the sorted-nuclei strategy in kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell.
Conceptually adjacent to TF/RBP binding-map efforts such as encode-rbp-map (van Nostrand RBPs) — both build occupancy maps to assign regulatory function, but BrainTF targets DNA-binding TFs in brain tissue rather than RNA-binding proteins.
Relevant to the developing/diseased-brain hub brain-development-neurogenomics via neuronal TFs (SATB2, TBR1, BCL11A, NEUROD1) with established roles in cortical neuron development.

Open questions

Can the BrainTF occupancy maps improve in silico TF-binding predictors for the neuron-enriched, unannotated (“none”) CRE category that current models miss?
Do TF–disease enrichments replicate with more donors and additional disease GWAS, and can dual-role TFs (TBR1, ZBTB7B) be resolved into activator vs repressor contexts?
How do these bulk/sorted-nuclei TF maps relate to single-cell-resolution regulatory states, and to allele-specific binding from the phased WGS generated here?

wiki/sources/lovci-2013-rbfox-proteins-r.title: lovci-2013-rbfox-proteins-regulate-alternative-mrna
wiki/sources/lovci-2013-rbfox-proteins-r.content:
Rbfox proteins regulate alternative mRNA splicing through evolutionarily conserved RNA bridges
One-line: CLIP-seq mapping shows that more than half of Rbfox1/Rbfox2 binding sites are located deep (>500 nt) in introns rather than near exons, and that these distal, evolutionarily conserved GCATG sites regulate splicing at a distance — in some cases via a long-range RNA–RNA base-pairing “RNA bridge” that folds the distal Rbfox site next to the exon.
Key takeaways

Genome-wide CLIP-seq of rbfox (Rbfox1 and Rbfox2) in mouse brain finds that >50% (62%) of Rbfox binding sites are distal — located within introns >500 nt from any annotated exon — overturning the assumption that splicing regulators act mainly through proximal intronic sequence (<500 nt).
Distal Rbfox sites carry the canonical UGCAUG/GCATG motif, are evolutionarily conserved across mammals (and often birds), and are preferentially enriched around alternatively spliced exons — hallmarks expected of functional splicing-regulatory-elements, marking distal introns as reservoirs of conserved cis-elements.
Distal sites regulate splicing following the same position-dependent rule as proximal ones (an rna-splicing-map): upstream Rbfox binding correlates with exon repression, downstream binding with exon inclusion — shown by RNA-seq in Rbfox1/Rbfox2 knockout brain and RBFOX-overexpressing 293T cells.
Proof of principle in two human genes: distal RBFOX sites control splicing of KIF21A exon 23 and ENAH exon 11a, validated in endogenous transcripts with antisense morpholinos (including vivo-morpholinos in mouse) that block the distal sites.
Introduces the RNA bridge mechanism (rna-structure-and-splicing): for ENAH exon 11a, a conserved long-range RNA–RNA secondary structure base-pairs across the intron to position the distal Rbfox site close to the exon; disrupting the duplex abolishes Rbfox-mediated inclusion, and compensatory mutations restore it.
Demonstrates a previously unknown, RNA-structure-mediated mechanism by which a distally bound RNA-binding protein can act on a splice site far away.

Notable claims / data

CLIP-seq yielded 2,071,607 (Rbfox1) and 2,451,256 (Rbfox2) nonredundant genomic alignments (mouse mm9); CLIPper called 10,062 Rbfox1 clusters (3,490 genes) and 7,466 Rbfox2 clusters (2,672 genes), 1,901 genes bound by both.
62% of clusters were in distal intron, only 8% in proximal intron; TGCATG was the single most enriched hexamer in conserved regions both proximal and distal (P < 0.01).
GCATG sequences conserved across multiple genomes (mouse/human/rat/dog) were ~3.5× more likely to be Rbfox-bound than mouse-only GCATGs; <15% of bound sites contained an evolutionarily conserved GCATG, yet these drove regulation.
Rbfox1/Rbfox2 loss in brain differentially spliced 620/934 exons (379 in common); of exons that changed in both KOs, 210 of 379 moved in the same direction. Rbfox binds a kilobase from the autoregulatory RRM-encoding exons of Rbfox1 and Rbfox2 themselves.
KIF21A exon 23 has two distal RBFOX sites ~42 nt apart, ~3.3 kb downstream of the exon and ~550 nt upstream of the next exon; a morpholino against the first cut inclusion from ~39% to ~18%. For ENAH exon 11a the only conserved GCATG motifs sit ~1 kb downstream; a group of three is conserved in ≥28 mammals and in birds.

Connections

Establishes rbfox as a central sequence-specific splicing regulator and extends the position-dependent rna-splicing-map logic first built for Nova and for hnRNP C in konig-2010-iclip-reveals-the-function-of.
The RNA-bridge finding is a cornerstone for rna-structure-and-splicing: long-range secondary structure, not just linear motif position, determines regulatory competence.
Distal-intronic, deeply conserved cis-elements connect splicing-and-evolution with splicing-regulatory-elements; Rbfox1 (RBFOX1) locus variants link to autism/epilepsy and FSHD, tying to splicing-and-disease and splicing-and-neurodevelopment.
Methodologically part of the CLIP-seq lineage in splicing-methods alongside iCLIP/eCLIP; the ENAH exon 11a switch is a known epithelial–mesenchymal-transition marker.

Open questions

How common genome-wide is the RNA-bridge mechanism versus distal sites that act through other means (e.g. local conformation), and can conserved long-range duplexes be predicted a priori?
Distal-site directionality of splicing effects looked more complex than for proximal sites — what additional rules govern activation vs repression from a distance?
Do other splicing factors also use conserved distal intronic sites and RNA bridges, making distal introns a general regulatory layer rather than an Rbfox peculiarity?

wiki/sources/lu-2021-wider-field-of-view.title: lu-2021-wider-field-of-view
wiki/sources/lu-2021-wider-field-of-view.content:
A wider field of view to predict expression
One-line: A short News & Views on enformer, a transformer-based sequence-to-expression model that improves accuracy by attending to distal regulatory elements up to 100 kb away.
Key takeaways

Deep learning can predict gene expression, chromatin-accessibility, and histone modifications across cell types from DNA sequence alone, but prior state-of-the-art models could not capture interactions with distal regulatory elements tens of kilobases away.
Enformer (Avsec et al.) borrows the transformer architecture from natural language processing, using self-attention layers to associate each locus with all other positions, letting information flow between distal elements up to 100 kb on either side of a target locus.
This large receptive field lets Enformer identify sparsely distributed distal elements (cis-regulatory-elements) more easily than convolutions, which have local receptive fields and need many stacked layers to reach far.
The model outputs thousands of predicted genomic tracks (transcription-factor and histone ChIP-seq, DNase-seq, ATAC-seq, and CAGE) for hundreds of human and mouse cell types.

Notable claims / data

Enformer integrates information from up to 100 kb on either side of the target locus, versus ~32 kb (Basenji2, via dilated convolutions) and ~20 kb (ExPecto) for predecessors; the model lineage runs Basset → Basenji2/ExPecto → Enformer.
Attention weight between two positions is a similarity of their latent representations; because every position is directly linked to all others, distal effects are captured without deep convolutional stacks.
Enformer substantially outperforms the prior state of the art at predicting CAGE-based tissue-specific expression, where expression depends strongly on distal elements.
Its attention focuses on cell-type-specific promoters, enhancers, and insulators, and can be exploited to identify enhancer-promoter interactions.
Enformer predicts more accurately than prior methods whether a natural variant or a CRISPR-perturbed enhancer will significantly change expression, relevant to noncoding-variants and personalized genomic medicine.

Connections

Extends sequence-based dna-language-models and foundation-models thinking to expression prediction, using the same attention machinery that powers language models.
Relevant to annotating disease-associated noncoding-variants via mutation-effect-prediction on regulatory attributes.
Ties into 3d-genome-and-disease: distal regulation is shaped by 3D contacts, and predicted DNA contacts could further improve insulator/distal modeling.

Open questions

A key caveat: these models cannot predict for cell types absent from training; their utility is identifying regulatory elements and variant effects, and a new approach is needed for novel cell types.
Could integrating sequence-predicted 3D DNA contacts improve modeling of insulators and long-range regulation?
Applying further interpretation methods (model-interpretability) could clarify additive, cooperative, or competitive sequence syntax within enhancer-promoter interactions across cell types and species.
Running such models in reverse could enable design of synthetic sequences with desired cell-type-specific behavior.

wiki/sources/manolio-2009-finding-the-mi.title: manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of
wiki/sources/manolio-2009-finding-the-mi.content:
Finding the missing heritability of complex diseases
One-line: The landmark review that named and framed the “missing heritability” problem — common-variant GWAS explain only a small fraction of the heritability estimated from family/twin studies — and catalogues candidate explanations and research strategies.
Key takeaways

Defines the central puzzle of human missing-heritability: hundreds of common variants found by GWAS confer only small risk increments (typically odds ratios ~1.1–1.5) and together explain only a small portion of the heritability estimated from family and twin studies.
GWAS rests on the “common disease, common variant” hypothesis — that common diseases are partly attributable to allelic variants present in >1–5% of the population. The review argues this hypothesis is incomplete, not wrong: common variants matter but do not account for the bulk of heritability.
Catalogues candidate sources of the missing variance: (1) many more common variants of even smaller effect; (2) rarer variants (MAF 0.5–5%, or <0.5%) of possibly larger effect, poorly tagged by SNP arrays; (3) structural variation / copy-number variants (CNVs); (4) gene–gene (epistasis) and gene–environment interactions; and (5) inadequate accounting for shared environment or overestimated heritability.
Frames the contributions to phenotype using risk-allele frequency vs effect size (Fig. 1): GWAS so far has only populated the common-variant/modest-effect corner, leaving low-frequency and rare-but-large-effect regions largely unexplored.
Proposes concrete strategies: larger GWAS and meta-analyses, targeted and whole-genome sequencing (e.g. 1000 Genomes, extreme-phenotype sequencing), better CNV detection, family-based designs (which enrich for rare variants and enable identity-by-descent and parent-of-origin analyses), and improved phenotyping including non-European populations.
Notes the heritability denominator itself can be questioned: narrow-sense heritability can be inflated by non-additive effects (dominance, epistasis) and shared environment — though IBD-based estimates (e.g. height ~0.8 from sib IBD sharing) agree with traditional ones, suggesting at least some estimates are not over-inflated.

Notable claims / data

Human height: estimated heritability ~80%, yet at least ~40 loci identified at the time explained only ~5% of phenotypic variance despite studies of tens of thousands of people.
Table 1 examples of heritability explained by identified loci: age-related macular degeneration ~50% (5 loci), Crohn’s disease ~20% (32 loci), systemic lupus erythematosus ~15% (6 loci), type 2 diabetes ~6% (18 loci), HDL cholesterol ~5.2% (7 loci), height ~5% (40 loci), early-onset myocardial infarction ~2.8% (9 loci), fasting glucose ~1.5% (4 loci).
Most common variants individually confer small risk (OR ~1.1–1.5; “large effect” defined loosely as ≥twofold).


80% of associated variants fall outside coding regions, emphasizing non-coding and regulatory variation.


The vast majority of GWAS were done in European-ancestry populations; populations of recent African ancestry (greater genetic diversity, shorter LD blocks) could increase yield of rare variants and narrow association regions.
Age-related macular degeneration is highlighted as the best example of a common disease whose heritability is substantially explained by a small number of common variants of large effect; Crohn’s disease is the opposite case (many variants, modest fraction explained).
