In S. cerevisiae, Hub1 is dispensable for general splicing and for canonical 5′ splice site usage, but is specifically required for allele-specific-splicing/alternative splice-site selection at divergent 5′ splice sites — offering a mechanism for how spliceosomes tolerate and select non-consensus splice-sites.
The Hub1-guided selective-splicing mechanism is conserved (also seen for S. pombe genes such as CDC2 and ZAS1), suggesting relevance to alternative splicing in eukaryotes broadly.

Notable claims / data

Immunoprecipitation and GST pull-downs confirm direct Hub1–Snu66 binding via HIND; Hub1 co-localizes with Snu66 in nuclear speckles in human cells.
Crystal structures (1.4 Å) of Hub1 bound to each of the two Snu66 HIND peptides: the Hub1 fold is highly similar to ubiquitin (r.m.s.d. 0.88 Å to 1UBI) and SUMO, with a ββαβαβ topology; the bound peptide forms an ~11-residue helix.
The Hub1–HIND interface (~500 Å²) uses a salt bridge (Snu66 Arg16/47–Hub1 Asp22) plus hydrophobic contacts; mutating Hub1 Asp22 or Snu66 Arg abolishes binding. Hub1–HIND affinity is high (Kd ≈ 0.6 and 1.7 µM) versus weak (Kd ≥ 100 µM) canonical ubiquitin-receptor interactions.
Hub1–HIND binding is distinct from SUMO–SIM binding despite using a similar surface of the ubiquitin fold; the interaction mode is novel among ubiquitin-like proteins.
Δhub1 and Δsnu66 mutants are synthetically sick/lethal with mutant alleles of several spliceosomal genes (e.g. prp38 P1381L); the Δhub1 prp8* lethality is not rescued by a Hub1 variant defective in Snu66 binding (D22A), confirming splicing relevance.
Splicing microarrays: Δhub1 cells show splicing defects for only a small subset of transcripts (mainly RPL34B); Δsnu66 has broader defects, worsened by additional Hub1 loss.
Reporter assays (RP51*–LacZ): efficient use of a non-canonical 5′ splice site (GUAUAU) requires Hub1; SRC1 alternative splicing via overlapping upstream/downstream 5′ splice sites is Hub1-dependent, and the characteristic differential SRC1 splicing is cell-cycle regulated (enriched in G2).

Connections

Expands splicing-factors and spliceosome biology by adding a non-covalent ubiquitin-like regulator that tunes splice-sites usage — a mechanistically distinct layer from cis-element/splicing-code models (cf. barash-2010-deciphering-the-splicing-code).
Provides a molecular basis for alternative-splicing at divergent/non-canonical 5′ splice sites, relevant to how metazoan spliceosomes exploit 5′ splice-site plasticity.
Introduces the entities hub1 (UBL5), snu66 (SART1), and the hind motif to the wiki’s molecular-machinery vocabulary; connects to Prp38 and Prp8 core spliceosomal factors.
Complements the theme that spliceosome composition/modification (not only RNA cis-elements) governs splicing outcomes — a bridge to splicing-and-chromatin/co-transcriptional regulation themes elsewhere in the wiki.

Open questions

Does the mammalian Hub1 orthologue (UBL5) similarly regulate SART1-dependent alternative 5′ splice site choice in human cells and disease?
Mechanistically, how does non-covalent Hub1 binding to a disordered Snu66/HIND region change spliceosome catalysis to permit non-canonical 5′ splice sites?
What determines the cell-cycle (G2) enrichment of Hub1-dependent SRC1 splicing, and is this a general mode of cell-cycle-coupled splicing?
Beyond splicing, do Hub1’s reported roles (cell-cycle, polarized growth, mitochondrial UPR) intersect with its spliceosomal function?

wiki/sources/mitra-2024-single-cell-mult.title: mitra-2024-single-cell-multi-ome-regression-models
wiki/sources/mitra-2024-single-cell-mult.content:
Single-cell multi-ome regression models identify functional and disease-associated enhancers and enable chromatin potential analysis
One-line: SCARlink is a gene-level regularized Poisson regression that predicts single-cell expression from scATAC tiles and, via Shapley values, links cell-type-specific enhancers to genes.
Key takeaways

scarlink models expression of each gene from the chromatin-accessibility of its genomic context (500 bp tiles, +-250 kb around the gene body) using non-negative regularized Poisson regression, jointly modeling all tiles instead of pairwise peak-gene correlations and avoiding dependence on peak calling.
Regression coefficients and per-cell-type Shapley values flag genomic tiles as putative cis-regulatory-elements (enhancers) in a cell-type-specific way, even though the model never sees cluster labels.
Predicted enhancer tiles are validated by promoter capture Hi-C (a chromatin-conformation-capture assay) and are strongly enriched for fine-mapped noncoding-variants from genome-wide-association-study and eQTL analyses, prioritizing causal cell types for disease loci.
Non-negativity plus a log-link GLM keeps the model interpretable, tying into model-interpretability via exact/approximate Shapley attribution.
SCARlink-predicted expression yields a robust chromatin-potential vector field for developmental trajectory inference; authors note enhancer-gene links could feed functionally driven transcriptome-wide-association-study methods.

Notable claims / data

Applied to 6 multi-ome datasets: PBMC (10X), BMMC (NeurIPS), mouse skin (SHARE-seq), developing human cortex, pancreas, pituitary gland; top ~5,000 variable genes filtered to 393-1,655 per dataset.
Beat ArchR gene score on all high-coverage datasets (signed-rank P < 8.35e-114 PBMC, < 3.24e-200 BMMC, < 1.15e-61 cortex); better per-gene correlation for 57.0% PBMC / 56.8% BMMC / 24.4% cortex genes at FDR<0.05. Comparable on sparse pancreas/pituitary; beat DORC scores on mouse skin (P < 3.1e-18).
Gene-linked tiles: 5.5x-7.5x enrichment of fine-mapped GWAS variants (17,769 variants, 82 UK Biobank traits) vs LD/MAF/distance-matched common variants; 12x-20x enrichment of fine-mapped whole-blood eQTLs in PBMC; higher enrichment than ArchR peak2gene throughout.
S-LDSC: 1.2x higher meta-analyzed heritability enrichment than ArchR across 104 traits; joint analysis showed 1.9x higher disease information.
Case studies: asthma variant rs112401631 linked to CCR7 in T-cell subsets; IGF-1/T2D variant rs12454712 linked to BCL2 in pituitary stem cells and somatotropes.
Caveats: non-negative coefficients cannot capture repressive elements; performance degrades with scATAC/scRNA sparsity; chromatin potential recovered the correct cortex trajectory only after restricting to a 470-gene subset.

Connections

Directly compared against ArchR gene scores; complements peak-based enhancer-gene linking such as kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and.
Shapley-based cell-type enhancer maps and disease-heritability framing parallel jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types and the sc-linker approach from the same lab.
Enhancer-gene regulatory linking is an alternative route to inferring regulatory networks, adjacent to gene-coexpression-network methods; downstream use in transcriptome-wide-association-study connects to isoform/expression TWAS work like bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic.

Open questions

Can interaction terms or neural architectures over tiles improve gene-level prediction without losing interpretability?
How to integrate DNA-sequence models (shared across genes) to capture regulatory grammar in enhancers?
When must genes be pre-selected for chromatin potential to reflect known biology, and can this be automated?

wiki/sources/mohanraju-2016-diverse-evol.title: mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic
wiki/sources/mohanraju-2016-diverse-evol.content:
Diverse evolutionary roots and mechanistic variations of the CRISPR-Cas systems
One-line: A landmark Science review that lays out the two-class / six-type classification of prokaryotic CRISPR-Cas adaptive immune systems, argues for a modular evolutionary scenario in which class 2 effectors (Cas9, Cpf1, C2c2) arose repeatedly from mobile genetic elements, and connects this mechanistic diversity to the genome-editing toolbox.
Key takeaways

CRISPR-Cas is a prokaryotic adaptive, heritable immune system found in most archaea and almost half of bacteria; it complements innate defenses (receptor masking, restriction-modification, DNA interference via prokaryotic Argonaute, BREX/PGL, abortive infection) in the constant virus-host arms race.
Immunity runs in three stages: adaptation (fragments of invader DNA captured as spacers into the CRISPR array), expression (array transcribed into pre-crRNA and processed into mature crRNAs), and interference (crRNA-guided Cas nucleases degrade the cognate invader) (see genome-editing).
The systems split into two classes subdivided into six types and 19 subtypes: class 1 (types I, III, IV) uses multi-subunit effector complexes (e.g. Cascade, Cmr); class 2 (types II, V, VI) uses a single large multidomain effector protein (cas9, cpf1, C2c2).
Evolution is strongly modular: adaptation and effector modules recombine freely across loci. The ancestral CRISPR-Cas is inferred to be class 1, derived from a self-synthesizing (casposon) transposon plus an innate-immunity locus; class 2 effectors arose by independent replacement of the multi-subunit effector with nucleases from distinct mobile genetic elements (transposons/toxin-antitoxin systems).
Self/nonself discrimination hinges on the protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) in type I and II systems, whereas type III systems use a different mechanism (targeting nascent transcripts, tolerating self via lack of PAM at the CRISPR array).
Class 2 single-protein effectors are what made programmable genome editing practical: Cas9 (type II) and Cpf1 (type V-A) are the headline tools, plus dCas9 for CRISPRi/a, imaging, and base/epigenome editing.

Notable claims / data

PAM is a short 2-to-7-nt motif; its presence in the target and absence in the host array enforce nonself targeting and avoid autoimmunity (self-targeting).
Cas1-Cas2 form the core adaptation machinery across nearly all CRISPR-Cas types; Cas1 is the integrase/nuclease, Cas2 largely structural (its nuclease activity is dispensable). Crystal structure of Cas1-Cas2 bound to dual-forked DNA solved (PDB 5DQZ, Fig. 3A).
Naive vs primed adaptation: primed adaptation (a positive-feedback loop that acquires new spacers from previously encountered elements, tolerating mismatches) has been demonstrated in type I systems; it uses Cascade + Cas3 + Cas1-Cas2 and reduces viral escape.
crRNA biogenesis differs by class: class 1 relies on the metal-independent endoribonuclease Cas6 for pre-crRNA processing (type I-E Cas6e leaves an 8-nt 5’ handle); type II uses tracrRNA + host RNase III + Cas9; Cpf1 has intrinsic RNase activity and needs no tracrRNA, processing its own crRNA (5’ hairpin).
Cas9 (type II): two nuclease domains, HNH (cleaves target strand) and RuvC (cleaves nontarget strand), producing a blunt double-strand break ~3 nt from the 3’ end of the protospacer; recognizes a 3’ G-rich PAM; PAM readout by Arg1333/Arg1335.
Cpf1/Cas12a (type V-A): single RuvC-like nuclease domain, needs no tracrRNA, recognizes a 5’ T-rich PAM, and generates staggered cuts with 4-5-nt 5’ overhangs (sticky ends) outside the heteroduplex.
C2c2/Cas13 (type VI): effector with two HEPN domains giving RNA-guided RNase activity; targets ssRNA; once activated becomes a nonspecific “collateral” RNase causing toxicity/dormancy — coupling immunity to programmed cell death.
Type III (Csm/Cmr) complexes are transcription-dependent DNA nucleases that also cleave the target RNA (Cas7-like backbone subunits Csm3/Cmr4 provide the RNase activity at 6-nt intervals); they do not use PAM-based discrimination.
Structures anchor the mechanistic claims: Cascade (type I-E, PDB 4QYZ), Cmr (type III-B, 3X1L), Cas9 (type II-A, 4OO8), Cpf1 (type V-A, 5B43) (Fig. 5).
Applications: first multiplex Cas9 genome editing in human cells reported 2013; dCas9 used for transcriptional activation/repression (CRISPRi/a), fluorescent labeling, histone-modifying recruitment, gene-circuit building, antimicrobials, and large-scale gain/loss-of-function screening; “double-nicking” with paired Cas9 nickases improves specificity; a claimed “MIMIVIRE” CRISPR-like defense in a mimivirus is flagged as needing caution (locus lacks CRISPR repeats and Cas1).

Connections

Provides the CRISPR-Cas mechanistic foundation underlying the CRISPR-screen entities in this wiki: pooled-crispr-screen, single-cell-crispr-screen, perturb-seq, and CRISPR-based reporter/editing methods like crispey.
The genome-editing applications section is the technology backbone for the genome-editing concept and for functional-genomics perturbation approaches (see single-cell-perturbation-modeling).
Introduces the two headline effector entities cas9 and cpf1 as programmable nucleases; contrasts their PAM, tracrRNA requirement, and cut geometry.

Open questions

Ecological roles of CRISPR-Cas beyond defense (biofilm formation, differentiation, pathogenicity) and the extent of nondefense/regulatory functions.
The still-elusive connection between the adaptation stage and interference, especially self/nonself discrimination in primed spacer acquisition.
Discovery and dissection of newly found types (IV, V, VI) whose biological roles and mechanisms remain largely unknown; abundance and diversity of anti-CRISPR proteins.
For therapeutics: efficient, tissue-specific delivery of Cas9/Cpf1 and guides; long-term effects of Cas9 expression in eukaryotic cells; ecological/ethical implications of Cas-based gene drives.

wiki/sources/moore-2020-expanded-encyclo.title: moore-2020-expanded-encyclopaedias-of-dna-elements
wiki/sources/moore-2020-expanded-encyclo.content:
Expanded encyclopaedias of DNA elements in the human and mouse genomes
One-line: ENCODE phase III (ENCODE3) capstone: ~6,000 new datasets across many assay types, integrated into a registry of ~927k human and ~340k mouse candidate cis-regulatory-elements queryable via SCREEN.
Key takeaways

Delivers the screen-ccre-registry — a categorized, tiered registry of candidate cis-regulatory-elements in human and mouse, with a web server (SCREEN) for user-defined access.
Massively expands maps of chromatin-accessibility (DNase-seq DHSs and ATAC-seq), histone-mark and TF ChIP-seq, dna-methylation (WGBS), chromatin looping, and RNA transcription — with a new emphasis on primary cells/tissues and mouse fetal development.
Adds a previously unannotated element class — genomic recognition sites for rna-binding-proteins — via eCLIP on 150 RBPs, complementing the companion binding-and-function map (van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional).
Fetal mouse accessible-chromatin orthologues are enriched for human disease-associated noncoding-variants in a tissue-restricted manner, linking the resource to complex-trait genetics.

Notable claims / data

5,992 new experimental datasets in phase III (4,834 human, 1,158 mouse); >4-fold more cell/tissue types and >2-fold more datasets than prior phases.
Registry: 926,535 human cCREs (7.9% of GRCh38) and 339,815 mouse cCREs (3.4% of mm10), from 2.2M/1.2M rDHSs.
Reference indices of ~3.6M consensus human DHSs and ~1.8M mouse DHSs; ~4.4M human DNase I footprints from >300 deep DNase-seq biosamples.
ChIP-seq mapped 662 chromatin-associated proteins in reference cell types; Hi-C for 33 human cell/tissue types; ChIA-PET (RAD21) in 24 cell lines; WGBS across 48 human types and 12 mouse tissues × 9 stages.
ENCODE-Roadmap Encyclopedia now spans 503 cell/tissue types from >1,369 biosamples; cCREs classified into PLS/pELS/dELS/DNase-H3K4me3/CTCF-only and data-support tiers 1a/1b/2.

Connections

The cCRE registry operationalizes cis-regulatory-elements genome-wide using integrated chromatin-accessibility and histone-mark signals; sits alongside dna-methylation and RBP-site annotations.
eCLIP RBP layer connects directly to van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional and the study of rna-binding-proteins in post-transcriptional regulation.
Fetal accessible-chromatin enrichment for noncoding-variants parallels wiki work on cell-type-specific regulatory annotation of GWAS/disease variants.

Open questions

How complete is the cCRE registry across untested biosamples, and how many tier-2 (provisional) cCREs will be promoted as data fill in?
How well do candidate elements’ predicted functions (enhancer/promoter/insulator) hold up under direct functional validation (e.g. MPRA, CRISPR perturbation)?

wiki/sources/mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-.title: mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian
wiki/sources/mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-.content:
Mapping and quantifying mammalian transcriptomes by RNA-Seq
One-line: Foundational RNA-Seq paper that maps and quantifies mouse transcriptomes by deep short-read sequencing, introducing the RPKM normalization measure and the erange analysis pipeline, and demonstrating direct, microarray-free detection of splice events at genome scale.
Key takeaways

Establishes RNA-Seq as a method to digitally quantify transcript prevalence by deeply sequencing cDNA (41–52 million mapped 25-bp reads) from poly(A)-selected adult mouse brain, liver and skeletal muscle.
Introduces RPKM (reads per kilobase of exon model per million mapped reads) as a normalized read-density measure reflecting molar transcript concentration, enabling transparent within- and between-sample comparison.
Introduces the erange software pipeline to assign unique reads, allocate ambiguous “multireads”, detect splice-crossing reads, and cluster reads outside known gene models (RNAFAR features) into candidate exons/genes.
Detects splicing directly by mapping splice-crossing reads against a library of all known UCSC mm9 gene-model splices — a capability not readily provided by expression microarrays or SAGE.
Finds alternative splicing prominent: 3,462 genes expressed two or more splice isoforms across the three tissues; 1.45 × 10^5 distinct splices observed out of ~2 × 10^5 possible.
Beyond known annotations, the data suggest revised gene models: ~3,000 extended/new 3′ UTRs, ~3,000 new/extended 5′ exons (alternate promoters), ~17,000 candidate new exon features, 596 new candidate transcripts, and new candidate microRNA precursors (e.g., mir-124-1).

Notable claims / data

Linear dynamic range across five orders of magnitude in RNA concentration using in-vitro Arabidopsis/lambda RNA standards (~0.5–50,000 transcripts per cell; R^2 > 0.99).
Technical replicate reproducibility R^2 = 0.96 (brain); ~93% of uniquely mapped reads fall on exons (which are <2% of the genome), ~4% intronic, ~3% intergenic (rough upper bound on noise).
At ~40 M mapped reads, detection is robust at ≥1.0 RPKM for a typical 2-kb mRNA (~80 reads, P < 10^-16); 3 RPKM ≈ one transcript per liver cell; 1 RPKM ≈ one transcript per cell in C2C12.
A splice is detected with 95% confidence if its transcript is represented at >11 RPKM in a 40-M-read transcriptome; splice events identified effectively for transcripts >15–25 RPKM. Splice detection for low-abundance isoforms is sporadic — these are minimum estimates of alternative splicing extent.
Multiread handling: 13–24% of mappable reads are multireads (match 2–10 loci); discarding them (Solexa default) drastically undercounts paralog families (e.g., ubiquitin B: 97% multireads). erange redistributes multireads proportionally to unique+splice reads, changing quantification >30% for 28% of genes above 5 RPKM in muscle and improving correlation with Affymetrix data (R^2 0.62 → 0.69).
Caveats: data are not strand-specific; long-range splice contiguity / full isoform quantification is not attempted (reported per-locus); low-prevalence RNAs from minority cells in complex tissues (e.g., brain) remain inaccessible at current cost/capacity.

Connections

A foundational methods/resource source for the splicing topic: it pioneered direct sequencing-based splice detection that later, splicing-focused tools build on (cf. shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection and rmats for differential alternative-splicing quantification from RNA-seq).
RPKM is an early quantification metric conceptually upstream of isoform-ratio metrics such as percent-spliced-in used by later splicing tools.
Belongs alongside other splicing-methods sources; provides the experimental/computational substrate (RNA-seq read mapping, splice-junction libraries) that downstream alternative-splicing analysis depends on.

Open questions

How would prevalence-normalized input RNA plus paired-end and longer (e.g., 454) reads enable a comprehensive, isoform-resolved splice map — the explicit extension the authors propose?
Can long-range splice contiguity (full isoform assembly) be recovered, which this per-locus RPKM approach deliberately does not address?
How do sequence-error-driven false positives in gene families affect quantification, and how much do longer/higher-quality reads and probabilistic transcriptome models mitigate them?

wiki/sources/nadig-2024-transcriptome-wi.title: nadig-2024-transcriptome-wide-characterization-genetic-perturbations
wiki/sources/nadig-2024-transcriptome-wi.content:
Transcriptome-wide characterization of genetic perturbations
One-line: Introduces trade, a statistical framework that estimates the transcriptome-wide distribution of true differential-expression effects from noisy Perturb-seq data, enabling power-robust comparison of genetic perturbations.
Key takeaways

Single-cell CRISPR screens like perturb-seq are noisy; conventional per-gene differential expression and correlation give results that depend heavily on statistical power. TRADE borrows the population-parameter philosophy of human genetics (like SNP-heritability and genetic correlation) to estimate effect-size distributions instead of thresholding.
TRADE fits a mixture model (via ash) to per-gene effect estimates and standard errors from DESeq2 pseudo-bulk, yielding the “transcriptome-wide impact” (variance of true log2FC) — an estimator stable across sampling depths.
It derives interpretable metrics: transcriptome-wide impact, an effective number of differentially expressed genes (π_eff), two flavors of gene-set enrichment, and a bivariate transcriptome-wide impact correlation.
Many true transcriptional effects are individually non-significant but detectable in aggregate.

Notable claims / data

Across a genome-scale pooled-crispr-screen (CRISPRi) in K562 (9,866 genes), only 36% of transcriptome-wide impact was explained by FDR-significant genes; in four essential-gene screens the significant fraction was even lower (K562-Essential 18%, RPE1 35%, Jurkat 13%, HepG2 14%).
A typical gene perturbation affects an estimated median π_eff = 45 genes, whereas a typical essential-gene perturbation affects a median of over 500 genes.
Illustrative contrast: GATA1 knockdown (108 cells) had TI = 0.4 (avg |log2FC| 0.63) vs EIF4A3 knockdown (only 7 cells) TI = 0.004 — near-null once sampling variance was modeled, despite near-identical raw estimate distributions.
Transcriptome-wide impact was far more reproducible across replicate experiments than DEG counts (TI R2 = 59.7% vs DEG R2 = 28.4%); replicate TI correlation median 0.90 vs raw log-fold-change correlation median 0.16.
Constrained genes were enriched ~1.57x for perturbation impact but depleted (0.40x) for perturbation response; strong-growth-effect (essential) genes 4.22x impact / 0.71x response; highly-expressed genes enriched for both (2.26x impact, 4.44x response).
Cross-cell-type impact correlations tracked cell-line biology (K562/Jurkat 0.74, HepG2/RPE1 0.75; RPE1 most idiosyncratic). 56% of perturbations correlated highly across all four lines; 44% correlated more within similar pairs.
Dosage (allelic-series) analysis: SOX9 and Polycomb dTAG depletion gave nonlinear dose-response TI curves; weak vs strong perturbations were only moderately correlated (r=0.60, 0.48), implying qualitatively different responses. Cross-dosage correlation reflects how much response curves vary across target genes.
Reanalyzing Gandal et al. (2018) case/control brain differential expression, TRADE transcriptome-wide impact correlations between neuropsychiatric diagnoses exceeded Spearman point-estimate correlations for 9/9 trait pairs, and exceeded the genetic correlations — while correctly staying low for a non-psychiatric control (IBD). Cross-assay (microarray vs RNA-seq) correlations were high (ASD 0.96, BPD 0.91, SCZ 0.78).

Connections

A statistical-inference tool for single-cell-perturbation-modeling and single-cell-crispr-screen data; complements perturb-seq atlases.
Adapts heritability / transcriptome-wide-association-study-style population-parameter estimation (threshold-free effect-size distributions) to RNA-seq differential expression.
Neuropsychiatric application connects to psychiatric-genetics and autism transcriptomic overlap.

Open questions

TI estimates carry a slight downward bias at finite sample size for very small effects; how much residual bias remains in low-power screens?
Whether elevated cross-diagnosis transcriptomic correlations reflect shared downstream disease biology or general expression/diagnosis confounders remains unresolved.
Extending systematic TRADE comparison across larger, more diverse perturbation atlases and primary tissues.

wiki/sources/nambiar-2022-crispr-based-g.title: nambiar-2022-crispr-based-genome-editing-through
wiki/sources/nambiar-2022-crispr-based-g.content:
CRISPR-based genome editing through the lens of DNA repair
One-line: A mechanistic review arguing that every CRISPR editing outcome is dictated by which cellular DNA-repair pathway resolves the editor-induced lesion, and that steering repair-pathway choice is the master lever for precise editing.
Key takeaways

Reframes all five major CRISPR editing modalities as lesion-generators whose outcomes are decided downstream by DNA repair: nuclease DSBs, nickase SSBs, base-editor deaminations/abasic sites, prime-editor DNA flaps, and transposase/recombinase integration intermediates. The editor “writes”; the homology-directed-repair vs non-homologous-end-joining (and MMEJ/SSA/MMR/BER) balance “decides.”
DSB repair-pathway choice hinges on DNA end resection: fast Ku/DNA-PKcs/LIG4-driven non-homologous-end-joining gives error-prone indels, while MRN-CtIP → EXO1/DNA2-BLM resection commits to microhomology-mediated end joining (Polθ), single-strand annealing (RAD52), or homology-directed-repair (RAD51). HDR is largely error-free but confined to S/G2.
Precision editing is boosted by tilting repair toward HDR: inhibiting NHEJ (SCR7 on LIG4, NU7441/M3814 on DNA-PKcs), blocking the resection antagonist 53BP1 (i53, dn53BP1, RAD18-derived e18), overexpressing/fusing resection & recombination factors (CtIP, EXO1, RAD52, POLD3) to cas9, cell-cycle synchronization to S/G2, and chromatin manipulation (Table 1 catalogs ~dozens of such strategies).
Base editors deliberately co-opt or block repair: cytosine base editors (CBE) fuse a cytidine deaminase (APOBEC1/3, AID, CDA) to nickase cas9 plus a UGI peptide that inhibits UNG to protect the U:G intermediate; adenine base editors (ABE) use an evolved TadA and need no glycosylase inhibitor because inosine is poorly excised. The non-edited-strand nick recruits MMR/EXO1 to make the edit permanent.
Non-canonical base editors weaponize BER: CGBEs remove UGI (adding UNG/UdgX) so abasic sites are bypassed by TLS polymerase REV1 to yield C→G transversions; AFID systems chain deaminase + glycosylase + AP-lyase + cas9 cut to make predictable small deletions; DddA-derived DdCBE edits double-stranded (and mitochondrial) DNA that R-loop editors cannot reach.
Prime editing (nickase cas9–reverse-transcriptase + pegRNA) creates a 3’ DNA flap; outcome depends on flap resolution (FEN1 excises the 5’ flap to install the edit) and is actively suppressed by mismatch repair — hence PE4/PE5 add dominant-negative MLH1 (MLH1dn) to block MMR-mediated reversion.

Notable claims / data

~10,000 abasic sites form in every human cell each day; abasic-site handling (HMCES shielding, BER, TLS) governs base-editing purity and byproducts.
MMEJ deletions correlate positively with GC content, microhomology length, and proximity to the cut; SSA uses long homologies (hundreds of nt) vs MMEJ’s 5–25 bp microhomologies.
End-joining outcomes at cas9 cuts are reproducible and sequence-context-dependent — enough to train predictive models (inDelphi, FORECasT, SPROUT, Lindel).
Base-editing activity window is ~5–8 bp for Cas9 CBE/ABE at a fixed distance from the PAM; bystander edits within the window are a key limitation.
Safety liabilities of DSB editing: large deletions (>250 bp), gross chromosomal rearrangements/chromothripsis, micronuclei, loss-of-heterozygosity in embryos (POU5F1 cuts gave 4–20 kb deletions), and p53 activation (even Cas9 alone without gRNA), raising selection-for-p53-mutant concerns in ex vivo therapy.
Repair-seq (Hussmann et al., 2021) is highlighted as the high-throughput screen mapping which end-processing enzymes generate which DSB repair products; Polλ promotes mutagenic fill-in inhibited by NHEJ factors.
Integration tools: INTEGRATE (type I-F, TniQ, no Cas3) is more efficient/purer than CAST (type V-K, Cas12k); neither is scarless (transposon-end duplication). Prime-editor-installed attB/attP + Bxb1 serine recombinase enables gene-sized insertions.

Connections

Deep DNA-repair companion to the broader-scope CRISPR reviews already in the wiki: pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of and wang-2023-crispr-technology-a-decade-of; complements the mechanism/biology of CRISPR systems in hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance and mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic.
The variant-scale readout of these editors (base/prime editing saturation mutagenesis, screens) connects to functional-genomics framing in kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for and application breadth in barrangou-2016-applications-of-crispr-technologies-in.
Class-1 Cascade/cas3 and catalytically dead dcas9 appear as the enzymatic building blocks (nickase/dead variants) underlying DSB-free editors; Cas12a (cpf1) noted as the workhorse type-V nuclease.
Off-target detection assays cataloged elsewhere in the wiki (e.g. GUIDE-seq, CIRCLE-seq) are the empirical flip side of the rearrangement/large-deletion risks this review formalizes mechanistically.

Open questions

Molecular details of SSTR remain unresolved: which resection machinery, and how strand invasion / D-loop extension / second-end capture actually proceed for ssODN-templated repair.
Cell- and tissue-specific regulation of DSB repair is poorly understood, blocking clinical translation — e.g. HDR is nonfunctional in neurons, forcing error-prone end-joining insertion.
Can recombination-promoting phage single-strand annealing proteins (1,000-fold HDR boost in bacteria) or G1-phase HDR restoration (phospho-mimetic CtIP + BRCA1-PALB2-BRCA2) be made efficient and non-toxic in mammalian/non-dividing cells?
No tool yet combines transition + transversion edits simultaneously; multi-step base editing is low-efficiency. Prime editing’s efficiency, pegRNA design complexity, and in vivo delivery remain the main barriers.
Genome-integrity impact of transient repair-modulator expression (e.g. 53BP1 loss → hyper-resection/SSA) and of persistent SSBs/abasic sites needs systematic evaluation before therapeutic use.

wiki/sources/nava-2025-dominant-variants.title: nava-2025-dominant-variants-in-major-spliceosome
wiki/sources/nava-2025-dominant-variants.content:
Dominant variants in major spliceosome U4 and U5 small nuclear RNA genes cause neurodevelopmental disorders through splicing disruption
One-line: De novo variants in the spliceosomal snRNA genes RNU4-2 (U4) and RNU5B-1/RNU5A-1 (U5) cause neurodevelopmental disorders by disrupting 5′ splice-site recognition; variant location within U4 structural domains dictates phenotype severity, and the disorder carries a distinct DNA-methylation episignature.
Key takeaways

Investigating de novo variants in 50 snrna-encoding genes across a French cohort of 23,649 rare-disease patients (plus international cases) identified 145 unreported pathogenic RNU4-2 variants and 21 individuals with variants in RNU5B-1 / RNU5A-1 (encoding U5) — implicating the spliceosome’s core snRNAs in disease.
Pathogenic variants typically arise de novo on the maternal allele and cluster in regions critical for splicing: RNU4-2 variants map to stem III and the T-loop/quasi-pseudoknot, which position the U6 ACAGAGA box for 5′ splice-site recognition (splice-sites).
Variant location predicts severity: T-loop/RBM42-interaction variants (including the recurrent n.64_65insT) give severe phenotypes, while stem III variants (n.62T>C, n.76C>T, n.72_73del) give milder disease — a genotype–phenotype continuum (splicing-and-neurodevelopment).
RNA-seq of patient lymphocytes shows RNU4-2 variants cause aberrant alternative 5′ splice-site usage: a shared signature that shifts toward existing cryptic isoforms, correlating with clinical severity; 30% of these events are out-of-frame yet largely escape nonsense-mediated-decay.
The study establishes RNU5B-1 as a new NDD gene and RNU5A-1 as a strong candidate; RNU5 variants cluster in U5 stem loop 1 (which recognizes the 5′ exon), and RNU4-2 patients carry a distinct DNA-methylation episignature useful for variant classification.

Notable claims / data

150 individuals with 22 distinct RNU4-2 variants; 106 (71%) had the recurrent n.64_65insT insertion; variants occurred de novo in 55/56 informative cases; parental origin was maternal in 47/51 phased cases.
This defines/expands ReNU syndrome (OMIM 620851): NDD with intellectual disability (65.2% severe/profound), microcephaly (74%), epilepsy (57.6%), hypotonia, short stature, and prenatal cerebral abnormalities/IUGR in ~60% of severe cases.
rMATS-turbo on 19 patients vs 21 controls found 111 significantly altered 5′ splice sites; decreased 5′SS had high SpliceAI scores (usage shifts not restricted to weak sites), while increased 5′SS had lower SpliceAI scores (median 0.52) and were mostly absent from controls (cryptic).
14 NDD-associated genes showed 5′SS defects, including three KMT2-family H3K4 methyltransferases (KMT2A, KMT2C, KMT2D — Wiedemann-Steiner, Kleefstra, Kabuki syndromes).
Episignature: 147 differentially methylated positions distinguish 35 RNU4-2 patients from 45 controls; a cross-validated classifier reached AUC 0.91 (100% sensitivity for n.64_65insT); the RNU4-2 episignature is distinct from the KMT2A/KMT2D episignatures.
U5 variants: 18 NDD probands with RNU5B-1 de novo variants (enriched in a small conserved 5′-loop-1 region; Fisher P = 6.1×10⁻⁵ vs non-NDD); RNU5A-1 n.40_41insA affected both 5′SS and 3′SS.

Connections

Adds noncoding spliceosomal snRNA genes to the catalog of Mendelian splicing-and-disease genes, alongside protein splicing-factor disorders; RNU4-2 is now among the most frequent monogenic NDD causes.
Mechanistically ties disease to 5′ splice-sites recognition failure in the U4/U6.U5 tri-snRNP, complementing spliceosome biochemistry; a recent report on USP39 deficiency causing similar 5′SS abnormalities is cited as convergent.
Uses rmats-turbo and spliceai to quantify and interpret aberrant 5′SS usage; the episignature approach links dna-methylation to splicing-gene variant classification.

Open questions

Why do out-of-frame aberrant transcripts largely escape NMD, and does that shape which splicing defects are pathogenic?
What explains the strong maternal-allele origin and recurrence of specific insertions (n.64_65insT) — germline selection, replication/methylation context?
Do other spliceosomal snRNA genes (U1, U2, U6) harbor undiscovered NDD variants, and how should such noncoding variants be classified clinically?
How do snRNA variants perturb splicing in neurons specifically, given analyses were done in lymphocytes?

wiki/sources/neil-2025-poison-exons-tuni.title: neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing
wiki/sources/neil-2025-poison-exons-tuni.content:
Poison exons: tuning RNA splicing for targeted gene regulation
One-line: A review (from Remix Therapeutics) of poison exons (PEs) — alternatively spliced exons whose inclusion introduces a premature termination codon that routes the host mRNA to nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) — covering their role in endogenous gene regulation, their misregulation in disease (notably neurodevelopmental disorders and cancer), and how splice-modulating ASOs and small molecules exploit them to drug “undruggable” targets.
Key takeaways

Poison exons couple alternative-splicing to NMD (“AS-NMD”): inclusion of a PE inserts a premature termination codon (PTC), and because exon-junction complexes (EJCs) downstream of a stalled ribosome recruit UPF1/SMG1, the transcript is preferentially degraded. This makes PE inclusion a tunable knob on host-gene abundance, a form of post-transcriptional-splicing regulation rather than proteome diversification.
PEs are detected via short-read rna-seq (exon causing a PTC >50 nt upstream of an EJC), but many are efficiently degraded and only surface under NMD perturbation (UPF1/SMG5/6/7 knockdown or chemical NMD inhibition), EJC RNA-immunoprecipitation, capture sequencing of NMD intermediates, or long-read-rna-seq; computational/ML methods increasingly predict functional PEs.
PE misregulation drives disease in both cis (SRE/splice-sites mutations shifting PE inclusion) and trans (splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins dysfunction). Neurodevelopmental disorders are especially affected because of long introns and embedded regulatory elements.
PEs are a therapeutic opportunity (splicing-therapeutics): ASOs can skip a pathogenic PE to restore expression (haploinsufficiency), or small molecules can induce PE inclusion to degrade a disease driver — analogous to PROTAC/molecular-glue targeted protein degradation but acting on RNA.
Two mechanistically distinct splice modulators of SMN2 are approved (nusinersen, risdiplam) and ~6 PE-targeting drugs are in clinical trials across oncology and non-oncology indications, signaling a maturing modality.

Notable claims / data

90–95% of human genes produce multiple isoforms via alternative splicing; large-scale reporter assays imply >1 million autonomous exons in the human genome.
~15% of all mRNAs are NMD targets; ~5% of exons (in ~13% of genes) show differential expression attributable to AS-NMD; many genes incorporate PEs into >20% of their expressed transcripts. PE enrichment is highest in introns with weak splice sites and in long introns.
PEs are over-represented in NMD-deficient RNA-seq datasets and show stronger predicted splice-site strength and greater cross-species conservation than “noise” cryptic events. A CRISPR screen estimated up to 50% of PEs may affect cellular fitness; computational models suggest possibly >1 million PEs in the human transcriptome.
Neuronal development: ~3000 alternatively spliced exons are coordinated by antagonistic RBPs and ~33% of such cassette exons are predicted to trigger NMD on inclusion or exclusion. Disease examples: a PE in FLNA (periventricular nodular heterotopia); TDP-43 mislocalization in ALS causing cryptic exon inclusion incl. a UNC13A PE that drives NMD-mediated loss of UNC13A.
Cancer: loss-of-function RBM10 (lung adenocarcinoma) and gain-of-function sf3b1 (myeloid cancers, altered 3’ splice-site selection / frameshifts) reshape the NMD-targeted transcript landscape; SR-protein PEs are autoregulated and dysregulated in tumors.
Therapeutics (Table 1): ASOs — STK-001/zorevunersen skips a SCN1A PE for Dravet syndrome (Phase 1/2a–2/3); STK-002 reduces an OPA1 PE for ADOA; smn2-targeting eteplirsen (DMD, approved) and nusinersen (SMA, approved). Small molecules (U1 snRNP–5’ss molecular glues) — risdiplam (approved, SMA), branaplam (HD; VIBRANT-HD halted for peripheral neuropathy), PTC518 (HD; ~43% mHTT reduction in CSF at 10 mg), SKY-0515 (HD/PMS1; ~72% HTT mRNA reduction in blood at 9 mg), REM-422 (MYB degrader, ACC/AML/MDS, Remix), RGT-61159 (MYB, Rgenta). Beyond PEs: BPN-15477/PTC258/PTC680 for ELP1 (familial dysautonomia), SKY-1214 inducing FANCL/FANCI exon skipping.
NMD escape: some PE-containing transcripts evade NMD and translate potentially harmful truncated peptides — PE inclusion is not always simple loss-of-function.

Connections

Mechanistically a deep dive on the AS-NMD / poison-exon motif introduced more briefly in baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator; both frame poison exons as a way splicing tunes abundance rather than protein form.
Disease framing complements scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (RNA mis-splicing as a disease mechanism) — here specialized to PTC/NMD-coupled events and their druggability.
The neurodevelopmental/ALS examples (FLNA, UNC13A, TDP-43 cryptic exons) connect to brain-splicing siblings such as humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas.
Names trans-acting regulators already in the wiki: sf3b1 (gain-of-function 3’ splice-site shifts) and the splice-modulator target smn2.

Open questions

Of the vast number of PEs, which trigger efficient degradation, and how can NMD penetrance be predicted up front to pick tractable targets?
Which splicing regulatory elements are most amenable to selective small-molecule/ASO modulation, and how can off-target effects of motif-targeting small molecules be predicted?
Can splice-switching small molecules rescue low gene expression (via PE skipping) as effectively as ASOs while keeping small-molecule delivery advantages?
Can paralog-selective targeting be achieved by exploiting faster-evolving intronic SREs between near-identical proteins?

wiki/sources/nepal-2023-alternative-prom.title: nepal-2023-alternative-promoters-cpg-depleted
wiki/sources/nepal-2023-alternative-prom.content:
Alternative promoters in CpG depleted regions are prevalently associated with epigenetic misregulation of liver cancer transcriptomes
One-line: CAGE-seq across 100 hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) liver samples shows tumor-specific alternative promoters fire from CG-poor regions that selectively lose DNA methylation, reshaping cancer transcriptomes.
Key takeaways

Alternative promoters are a pervasive mode of gene-regulation in liver cancer: 4083 alternative promoters (1031 novel) across 2926 multi-promoter genes were annotated from 100 CAGE-seq libraries (50 tumors + 50 matched tumor-adjacent), largely undetected in normal liver.
Multi-promoter genes are enriched for oncogenic processes and overwhelmingly associated with overall survival (P = 1.06E−247, Fisher’s exact test); ~50% of alternative promoters are unexpressed in normal liver vs ~15% of reference promoters, marking tumor-specific activation.
Alternative promoters have a distinct regulatory architecture: narrow/“sharp” nucleosome-depleted TSSs, CpG island depletion (only 48.6% overlap CGIs vs 80% of reference promoters), positionally constrained TATA box, and enrichment for tissue-specific liver cis-regulatory-elements (HNF1A, HNF4A; NFATC2 unique to nonCGI alternative promoters).
A hierarchical dna-methylation retention mechanism explains activation: CG-poor regions rapidly lose methylation while CG-rich intragenic CGIs retain it, governed by differential SETD2, H3K36me3, DNMT3B, and TET1 binding.
Selective demethylation of CG-poor regions opens chromatin-accessibility and permits alternative transcription; activation of a downstream/upstream alternative promoter frequently downregulates the reference promoter.

Notable claims / data

42,804 high-confidence consensus transcript clusters were derived from 1.4 billion mapped CAGE tags; 90% supported by FANTOM5 peaks and/or ENCODE/TCGA open chromatin.
Genes classified as single-promoter (12,493; 80.3%) vs multi-promoter (2926; 19.7%); 65% of alternative promoters lie downstream of the reference promoter.
Reference promoters are broadly (constitutively) expressed; alternative promoters are expressed either constitutively or only in patient subsets, and are significantly upregulated in tumors (P = 0.00012) while reference promoters trend down.
Novel alternative promoters are even more CGI-depleted than annotated ones (P = 1.9E−61) and have significantly lower observed/expected CG ratio (P = 9.1E−21).
NonCGI alternative promoters carry enhancer-like histone signatures (low H3K4me3, high H3K4me1); intragenic CGIs stay hypermethylated (high DNMT3B, low TET1/5hmC) and therefore silent.
Mechanism validated in SETD2-knockdown cells (global H3K36me3 loss, higher retention in CG-rich regions) and in SETD2-mutated patients (n = 15) vs wild-type (n = 362): mutants are significantly hypomethylated, with demethylated CpGs enriched in CG-rich gene bodies.

Connections

Extends dna-methylation and chromatin-accessibility themes to a cancer-epigenetics setting, tying methylation loss causally to promoter activation.
Complements cis-regulatory-elements and gene-regulation pages by detailing how promoter architecture (sharp vs broad, CGI vs nonCGI) differentiates reference from alternative transcription start sites.
The SETD2/H3K36me3/DNMT3B axis links transcription elongation to co-transcriptional methylation maintenance, relevant to splicing-and-chromatin discussions elsewhere in the wiki.

Open questions

Are CG-poor alternative promoters drivers or passengers of hepatocarcinogenesis, and do they offer therapeutic or prognostic targets?
How generalizable is the CG-density-dependent demethylation hierarchy beyond liver cancer (the ATAC analysis spans pan-cancer TCGA but methylation mechanism is liver-focused)?
What determines which tumor-specific alternative promoters attenuate their host reference promoter versus act independently?

wiki/sources/neumann-2024-brainwide-sile.title: neumann-2024-brainwide-silencing-prion-protein
wiki/sources/neumann-2024-brainwide-sile.content:
Brainwide silencing of prion protein by AAV-mediated delivery of an engineered compact epigenetic editor
One-line: Introduces charm-epigenetic-editor, a compact enzyme-free epigenetic editor that recruits endogenous DNMT3A to methylate and durably silence the prion gene, achieving up to ~80% brainwide reduction of prion protein in mice after a single systemic AAV injection.
Key takeaways

CHARM (Coupled Histone tail for Autoinhibition Release of Methyltransferase) silences genes through programmable dna-methylation without overexpressing any catalytic domain: a histone H3 tail plus the noncatalytic Dnmt3l domain (D3L) recruits and activates the cell’s endogenous full-length DNMT3A by releasing its autoinhibition (the H3K4me0-reading ADD domain).
Prnp is a strong target for durable epigenome-editing: PrP is nonessential (knockouts well-tolerated across mammals), and lowering neuronal PrP halts and can reverse prion disease, so simply reducing expression is therapeutic.
Unlike transient CRISPRi (dCas9-KRAB) repression, methylation-based silencing (CRISPRoff and CHARM) is durable through cell division; CHARM matched or exceeded CRISPRoff while being far smaller.
CHARM is DNA-binding-agnostic (works with dCas9, zinc-finger proteins/ZFPs, and TALEs); ZFP versions (ZFcharm) are compact enough to fit within the ~5 kb AAV genome limit, unlike the ~6.2 kb CRISPRoff.
Self-silencing CHARMs (ZFP motif placed around the editor’s own promoter) autonomously deactivate after silencing their target, temporally limiting expression to curb antigenicity and off-target activity in nondividing neurons.
Potentially generalizable to other neurodegenerative diseases driven by toxic protein aggregation.

Notable claims / data

In vivo: systemic retro-orbital AAV-PHP.eB delivery of Prnp-targeting ZFcharm (1.5 x 10^13 vg/kg) to adult mice, harvested 6 weeks later, gave a 70-90% drop in Prnp transcripts and 60-80% reduction in PrP protein across whole-brain hemispheres; abstract summarizes as up to ~80% brainwide reduction.
KRAB-free ZFcharm was also highly effective, indicating DNA methylation alone suffices to silence Prnp in the brain. No adverse effects at any dose; doubling dose gave only mild improvement.
Nanopore native-DNA sequencing confirmed CpG-island methylation around the Prnp TSS in vivo; HCR RNA-FISH showed Prnp silencing in the vast majority of Uchl1+ neurons (postmitotic cells).
These knockdown levels far exceed the therapeutic threshold cited: 50% ASO knockdown extended survival across five prion strains, and as little as 21% delayed symptom onset.
Mechanistic dissection in HEK293T CLTA reporter cells: silencing requires unmethylated H3K4 (H3A4 mutant loses activity) and DNMT3A specifically (not DNMT3B); H3 tail and D3L act synergistically. Optimization covered linker length (40-aa maxiflex), D3L orthologs (best: Apodemus sylvaticus AsD3L), and a 30-aa H3.1 tail.
The catalytic D3A domain used by CRISPRoff/ZFPoff was overtly cytotoxic when overexpressed, whereas D3A-free CHARM constructs showed no significant viability loss; RNA-seq showed high on-target specificity for Prnp.
Compactness enables split-intein trans-splicing, multiplexing up to three ZFcharms, tamoxifen-inducible ERT2 nuclear localization, and WPRE3 stabilization; authors note compatibility with lipid nanoparticle mRNA delivery.

Connections

Extends the epigenome-editing and dna-methylation silencing paradigm exemplified by CRISPRoff, contrasting with transient crispri-crispra repression.
A genome-editing-free alternative: avoids double-strand breaks / DNA edits and the production of altered mRNAs, positioned against nuclease and base-editing approaches for neurodegeneration.
New entity introduced: charm-epigenetic-editor.

Open questions

In-depth in vivo toxicity and off-target profiling remain critical next steps before therapeutic use.
Whether >80% brainwide PrP knockdown translates to extended survival in prion-infected mice (not tested here) and durability beyond 6 weeks.
Whether human PRNP can be silenced as effectively in vivo, and design of cross-reactive ZFcharms using human/nonhuman-primate promoter homology.
Optimal self-silencing kinetics to first establish heritable repression before the editor shuts itself off.

wiki/sources/nguyen-2025-aberrant-splici.title: nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease
wiki/sources/nguyen-2025-aberrant-splici.content:
Aberrant splicing in Huntington’s disease accompanies disrupted TDP-43 activity and altered m6A RNA modification
One-line: Mechanistic multi-omics study showing that mutant-HTT-driven aberrant splicing in Huntington’s disease arises from TDP-43 loss of function coupled with altered m6A RNA modification, validated across HD mouse models and human patient brain.
Key takeaways

HD aberrant splicing is dominated by increased exon exclusion (~70% of skipped-exon events) in both cortex and striatum, conserved from R6/2 mice to human HD patient cortex.
Two HTT-interacting rna-binding-proteins are implicated as upstream regulators of HD exon skipping: TDP-43 (UG/GU-rich UGUGU motif) and the m6A writer METTL3 (DRACH motif), both identified via de novo motif enrichment on excluded exons.
TDP-43 shows loss of nuclear function in HD: reduced nuclear localization, cytoplasmic phosphorylated-TDP-43 aggregates, and attenuated binding to dysregulated/aberrantly spliced target RNAs — recapitulating cryptic-exon (poison-exon-like, AS-NMD) biology seen in ALS/FTD.
m6A modification is reduced at specific sites on HD-dysregulated RNAs (including sites adjacent to TDP-43 binding), linking the epitranscriptome to TDP-43 activity; the authors argue m6A loss precedes/underlies decreased TDP-43 binding.
A novel neuropathological feature — nuclear pTDP-43 “aggregation-like (AL) bodies” co-localizing with HTT — is reported in HD patient neurons, distinct from canonical HTT inclusions.
Reinforces splicing-and-disease and links HD to the broader TDP-43 proteinopathy family (ALS, FTLD, AD, Parkinson’s). See scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.

Notable claims / data

DGE (R6/2, 3-month, n=10, sex-balanced): striatum 1,309 down / 648 up; cortex 1,253 down / 462 up (adj P<0.05, log2FC>1); 65.8% striatal-cortical DEG overlap, 95.4% same direction. All 266 genes of the HD266ssg striatal signature dysregulated (265 in expected direction).
rMATS AS (FDR<0.05): 9,033 RNAs / 3,620 genes in cortex; 10,074 RNAs / 3,854 genes in striatum. ~70% of skipped-exon events = higher exon exclusion in HD. ~60% of AS-containing genes also up/downregulated.
Human HD (rMATS on published datasets): prefrontal cortex BA9 (grade III/IV, 20 HD, 49 controls) 12,469 AS events; anterior cingulate cortex (6 HD, 6 controls) 7,730 AS events — exon exclusion increase confirmed.
Long-read (PacBio Iso-seq) validation: 1,840/2,311 (79.6%) cortex and 2,083/2,634 (79.1%) striatum significant exon exclusions confirmed; ~57% of novel splice junctions confirmed. Single-nuclei RNA-seq + DESJ-detection localized most splicing changes to inhibitory MSNs.
Unannotated/novel splicing: ~50% of R6/2 AS changes novel; MAJIQ and LeafCutter detected hundreds of novel events. 55% overlap (192/348) with TDP-43-knockdown splicing changes (Šušnjar et al.). Novel events drive expression changes in both directions (e.g. Cntn6 cryptic exon up; Ssbp2 unannotated exon lost).
TDP-43 eCLIP-seq (R6/2 + NT, n=8): ~80% binding overlap HD vs NT (most targets unchanged); binding decreased on downregulated genes and aberrantly spliced cassette-exon transcripts, not on upregulated genes. TDP-43 binds ~30% (79/266) of HD striatal signature genes. Caveat: binding distribution unexpectedly enriched in CDS/3’UTR (not intronic), attributed to cytoplasmic-mRNA enrichment in the protocol.
TDP-43 ASO knockdown (~50% KD) in WT mouse striatum clusters transcriptionally with R6/2 HD; siRNA TDP-43 KD in isogenic HTT-18Q vs mHTT-50Q iPSC-derived MSNs: 68.7% (382/556, P=1.5e-189) of KD-downregulated transcripts overlap HD-down genes; STMN2, UNC13B, CAMK2B downregulated in both.
m6A: Mettl3 nuclear expression significantly decreased in R6/2 cortex and striatum (Rbm15 down in cortex); global m6A by LC-MS unchanged (P=0.0567), but m6A eCLIP-seq showed DEGs carry more m6A sites than non-DEGs (downregulated genes highest), yet those sites are LESS methylated in HD. TDP-43 binding peaks center on m6A sites more sharply than HNRNPC/HNRNPL. Human HD caudate: m6A IF signal significantly increased (P<0.0001) with cytoplasmic METTL3 mislocalization.
Human TDP-43 pathology: nuclear TDP-43 significantly reduced in 5/5 HD SFG patients (P<0.0001); rare cytoplasmic pTDP-43 (S409/S410) aggregates; pTDP-43 AL bodies (~3 µm, in MAP2+ neurons) significantly increased in HD (P=0.0146), containing N-terminal TDP-43 and HTT.

Connections

Direct splicing-and-disease sibling to scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease — provides a concrete neurodegenerative mis-splicing mechanism (cryptic/poison-exon, AS-NMD).
Methodologically uses rmats, majiq-v2 and leafcutter for AS and novel-splicing detection, plus eCLIP and PacBio long-read RNA-seq; complements brain long-read work in humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas.
Shares the TDP-43/RBP-regulation theme with baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and the developmental-splicing framing of kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated.
Human-brain disease counterpart to the brain-sQTL/atlas siblings (zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls, park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative).
Couples splicing regulation to the m6A epitranscriptome and to rna-binding-proteins biology — an axis adjacent to splicing-code and rna-structure-and-splicing.

Open questions

Causal ordering: does m6A loss truly precede TDP-43 dysregulation, or are they co-dependent? (Inferred, not directly demonstrated.)
What is the composition/core of the novel nuclear pTDP-43 AL bodies (PML-like, nucleolar, or RNA-free anisosomes)?
Which additional cryptic-exon-regulating RBPs cooperate with HTT beyond TDP-43?
Can pharmacologic control of m6A restore TDP-43 splicing function as an HD therapeutic? (splicing-therapeutics direction.)
Why does the TDP-43 eCLIP binding distribution differ (CDS/3’UTR vs intronic) from prior maps — protocol artifact or HD-specific biology?

wiki/sources/nikom-2023-alternative-spli.title: nikom-2023-alternative-splicing-in-neurodegenerative-disease
wiki/sources/nikom-2023-alternative-spli.content:
Alternative splicing in neurodegenerative disease and the promise of RNA therapies
One-line: A review synthesizing how dysregulated alternative-splicing contributes to the major neurodegenerative diseases (Alzheimer, Parkinson, ALS–FTD, repeat-expansion disorders, Huntington) and how splice-switching oligonucleotide-therapeutics are being deployed to correct mis-splicing pathology.
Key takeaways

Alternative splicing is disproportionately abundant and evolutionarily conserved in the brain; ~90% of multi-intronic human genes are alternatively spliced, and neural-specific AS scales with vertebrate brain complexity — making the CNS especially vulnerable to splicing defects (splicing-and-neurodevelopment).
Mis-splicing is a broad, converging mechanism across neurodegeneration (neurodegeneration): disease-associated splicing typically produces truncated/aberrant proteins or shifts the reading frame to create premature termination codons that trigger nonsense-mediated-decay, lowering functional protein.
Regulation framing: splice-sites recognized by the spliceosome are tuned by splicing-regulatory-elements (ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS) read by splicing-factors — SR proteins (generally activators) and hnRNPs (generally repressors), plus context-dependent RBPs RBFOX1/2, FUS, TDP-43 (rna-binding-proteins); the SR-activate/hnRNP-repress dichotomy is an oversimplification.
Alzheimer disease: the 3R-tau/4R-tau ratio set by MAPT exon 10 splicing is a central axis — ~1/3 of >50 MAPT mutations alter exon 10 and imbalance in either direction impairs microtubule assembly and axonal APP transport; APP, BACE1, PSEN1/2, ApoER2 and APOE are also mis-spliced.
ALS–FTD: TDP-43 (TARDBP), FUS and C9ORF72 converge on pathological splicing; nuclear TDP-43 loss depresses cryptic exons in STMN2 and UNC13A and mis-splices HNRNPA1, tying cytoplasmic RBP aggregation (upstream) to splicing dysregulation (downstream).
Repeat-expansion disorders act largely through toxic RNA foci that sequester Muscleblind-like proteins (MBNL1/2), e.g. myotonic dystrophy DMPK CTG expansion → CLCN1 exon 7a mis-splicing (PTC/NMD) → myotonia; spinocerebellar ataxias and Huntington (CAG-length-dependent HTT mis-splicing) follow related logic.
Splice-switching ASOs are the clinical proof of concept: nusinersen (SMN2 exon 7 inclusion, SMA) and eteplirsen (DMD exon 51 skip, Duchenne) are approved; but several neurodegenerative ASO trials (HTT-lowering, C9ORF72) failed, underscoring that clinical success hinges on identifying the precise pathogenic mechanism and target validation.

Notable claims / data

MAPT exon 10 encodes the second of four microtubule-binding repeats; inclusion gives 4R-tau, skipping gives 3R-tau; adult human brain expresses 3R and 4R at roughly equal levels while only 3R is expressed developmentally. Exon 10 splicing is regulated by SR proteins (SRSF1/2/9 promote inclusion; SRSF3/4/6/7/11 repress), RBFOX3/NeuN, PTBP1 (via miR-124), and SFPQ.
APP is spliced into APP695/APP751/APP770; exon 7 encodes a Kunitz-type protease inhibitor domain (present in APP751/770, absent in neuronal APP695); APP695 overexpression is more amyloidogenic; RBFOX1 (downregulated in AD dorsolateral prefrontal cortex) normally represses APP exon 7.
BACE1 pre-mRNA yields BACE1-501 (full active) plus BACE1-476/457/432 with markedly reduced β-secretase activity; HNRNPA2/B1 downregulation on cholinergic dysfunction shifts toward amyloidogenic isoforms.
TDP-43 loss induces cryptic exon inclusion in STMN2 (cryptic exon 2a with premature poly(A) → truncated stathmin-2, impaired axonal function) and UNC13A (128-bp cryptic exon → NMD); TDP-43 aggregates found in >90% of ALS and up to 50% of FTD.
Myotonic dystrophy type 1: CTG expansion in DMPK 3′UTR forms hairpins sequestering MBNL1 → CLCN1 exon 7a inclusion shifts frame → PTC → NMD → truncated CLCN1, reduced chloride conductance, myotonia. Type 2: CCTG expansion in ZNF9/CNBP intron 1.
Huntington: CAG-length-dependent aberrant HTT exon1–exon2 splicing produces a short polyadenylated transcript translating to a pathogenic exon-1 HTT protein; PTBP1 and SRSF6 implicated; a small molecule promoting HTT cryptic-exon inclusion lowers mutant HTT.
ASO mechanisms: (1) RNase-H1-recruiting degradation, (2) steric-block splice modulation of ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS, (3) microRNA sequestration, (4) translation blocking. Six splice-switching ASOs are FDA-approved for neurological disease (four for DMD, nusinersen for SMA, one for Batten disease). An APOER2 exon-19-inclusion ASO improved cognition in AD mice; an APP exon-17 ASO cut Aβ42 ~60% in wild-type mice.

Connections

Neurodegeneration-focused companion to the development/tissue review baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and the cancer-focused desterro-2020-targeting-mrna-processing-as-an; all three converge on splice-switching oligonucleotide-therapeutics (nusinersen/eteplirsen as shared exemplars) and splicing-therapeutics.
Deep grounding for splicing-and-neurodevelopment, splicing-and-disease and neurodegeneration; the intron-retention / cryptic-exon → nonsense-mediated-decay motif recurs throughout and links to poison-exons.
Heavy on RBP regulators overlapping wiki entities: ptbp1, hnrnpa1, RBFOX family, TDP-43/TARDBP, FUS, MBNL1/2 (rna-binding-proteins, splicing-factors).
The regulatory-element schema maps to splicing-regulatory-elements, splice-sites, splicing-code and spliceosome; repeat-expansion RNA-foci mechanism connects to rna-structure-and-splicing.

Open questions

Which specific splicing factors drive MAPT exon 10, SNCA, and other disease-gene mis-splicing in vivo, at cell-type resolution — the cell of origin for observed splicing changes in the brain is usually unspecified?
Are the vast transcriptome-wide splicing alterations seen in AD/PD/HD post-mortem tissue causal or bystander, and which are the safe mechanistic “picks”?
Why did highly anticipated HTT-lowering and C9ORF72 ASO trials fail while SMA/DMD succeeded — what distinguishes a validated pathogenic splicing target?
Can RNA-targeting CRISPR or spliceosome-mediated trans-splicing (SMaRT) mature into safer alternatives to repeated ASO dosing for repeat-expansion and proteinopathy diseases?

wiki/sources/nilsen-2010-expansion-of-th.title: nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome
wiki/sources/nilsen-2010-expansion-of-th.content:
Expansion of the eukaryotic proteome by alternative splicing
One-line: A foundational Nature Insight review arguing that alternative splicing is a principal source of proteomic diversity in multicellular eukaryotes, and that splice-site choice is governed by kinetic competition rather than dedicated tissue-specific regulators alone.
Key takeaways

Mammals have far fewer genes (~20,000) than the ~100,000 once estimated; the “missing” information is supplied largely by alternative-splicing, which lets one gene yield many mRNAs and proteins.
High-throughput sequencing indicates 95–100% of multi-exon human pre-mRNAs are alternatively spliced; isoform counts per gene range from two to several thousand.
Nearly all alternative splicing reduces to four basic modules: alternative 5′ splice-site choice, alternative 3′ splice-site choice, cassette-exon inclusion/skipping, and intron retention (see splice-sites).
Only a small number of splicing-factors regulate large numbers of events; most regulators (rna-binding-proteins) such as SR proteins and hnRNPs are ubiquitously expressed, with few truly tissue-specific exceptions (NOVA, nPTB/PTBP2, FOX1/FOX2, ESRP1/ESRP2, nSR100/SRRM4).
The simple “yin–yang” model (SR-protein enhancers vs hnRNP-bound silencers) has predictive value but greatly understates the biochemical complexity of regulation.
The authors advance a kinetic view: transcription rate, chromatin/histone marks, spliceosome component levels, intron size, and competition between splice sites jointly determine splice-site choice.

Notable claims / data

Drosophila dscam can generate 38,016 distinct mRNA isoforms from 95 alternative exons in four mutually exclusive clusters (12, 48, 33 and 2 variant exons), far exceeding the ~14,500 genes in the organism; 19,008 distinct extracellular domains arise via isoform-specific homophilic binding.
Human KCNMA1 (SLO) can produce >500 mRNA isoforms from one pre-mRNA via combined alternative 5′/3′ splice sites and cassette exons.
D. melanogaster mod(mdg4) makes 28 mRNAs via trans-splicing of common exons (one pre-mRNA) to variable exons on separate pre-mRNAs.
Dscam exon-6 mutually exclusive splicing is directed by competing intronic RNA secondary structures (a docking site pairing with one selector sequence per variant); hnRNP HRP36 (HRB87F) represses all 48 exonic sequences, and co-depleting an SR protein with HRP36 restores correct splicing — showing non-selected exons are not dormant but kinetically out-competed.
Constitutive exons carry more H3K36me3 than alternative exons; slowing RNA polymerase via this mark or heterochromatin shifts inclusion — proposed exon-”tethering” model where slow transcription favors inclusion.
Caveats: extent of “constitutive” vs regulated splicing is unknown; functional relevance of most isoforms is unproven (e.g. Drosophila CadN exon 18A/18B phenotype is rescued by the alternate isoform); cross-species comparisons (worm/fly vs human) rest on far less data.

Connections

Provides the conceptual backbone for many sibling pages: complements review-style sources lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Its kinetic/co-transcriptional framing prefigures cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin; its RNA-secondary-structure argument links to rna-structure-and-splicing and peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
The “splicing code” question it poses anticipates splicing-code and modern predictors such as spliceai.
Discusses canonical regulators with existing entities: srsf1 (SR/SF2/ASF family), ptbp1 and nPTB, plus dscam as the flagship diversity example; relates to splicing-regulatory-elements and cis-regulatory-elements.
Evolutionary plasticity discussion connects to splicing-and-evolution.

Open questions

Can the absolute extent of alternative splicing actually account for organismal complexity across species?
How many mRNA isoforms are functionally relevant versus incidental?
Will a reliable, computable “splicing code” emerge, and how much do deep-intronic elements contribute to splice-site choice?
Do tissue-specific transcription-rate / chromatin differences (rather than tissue-specific regulators) drive a large fraction of regulated splicing, e.g. between brain/muscle and low-AS tissues?

wiki/sources/nitahara-2026-defective-ven.title: nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8
wiki/sources/nitahara-2026-defective-ven.content:
Defective ventral neurogenesis due to midfetal Chd8 mutation drives autistic-like behavior in mice
One-line: Using time- and cell-type-controlled Chd8 mouse alleles, the authors pinpoint the critical window (midfetal, ~E14.5) and cell population (ventral/ganglionic-eminence progenitors of interneurons and oligodendrocytes) whose accelerated differentiation drives autistic-like behavior — and show that restoring CHD8 in those cells rescues it.
Key takeaways

Identifies both a critical period and a critical cell type for autism-like behavior caused by chd8 haploinsufficiency: Chd8 heterozygous mutation induced at embryonic day (E) 14.5 or earlier produces the phenotypes, but induction at E17.5 or later does not.
The primary developmental defect is accelerated cell-cycle exit and differentiation of ventral (ganglionic eminence) progenitor cells — the source of GABAergic interneurons and oligodendrocytes — with dorsal (cortical excitatory) neurogenesis relatively spared.
Genetic rescue is spatiotemporally matched: restoring CHD8 in neural stem cells at E14.5 (not E17.5), or specifically in ventral progenitors via Olig1-Cre, ameliorates the behavioral abnormalities and normalizes ventral differentiation.
Behavioral phenotypes (abnormal social contact, increased anxiety) are dissociable from macrocephaly — the conditional inductions caused behavior changes without macrocephaly, and the ventral rescue fixed behavior without fixing macrocephaly.
Downstream consequence in the adult brain is impaired inhibitory function: weakened GABAergic control in medial prefrontal cortex, reduced perisomatic VGAT+ puncta, and region-specific transcriptomic/connectivity changes in interneurons and oligodendrocytes.

Notable claims / data

Critical period. Nestin-CreERT2 tamoxifen induction at E11.5 or E14.5 (but not E17.5 or P1–3) caused reduced social contact and increased anxiety (light-dark, elevated plus-maze, open-field). Example: social-interaction test at E11.5 gave exact P = 7.7 × 10⁻⁴. Neural-stem-cell-specific (Nestin-Cre) heterozygosity reproduced global-heterozygote phenotypes (anxiety, macrocephaly) but not deficits in cognitive flexibility, stereotypy, or three-chamber sociability indices.
Lineage tracing. Rosa26-tdTomato reporter: E14.5 induction labeled telencephalon (olfactory bulb, cortex, striatum, hippocampus) but not thalamus; cortex and striatum were the regions downregulated between E14.5 and E17.5, matching the behavioral window.
scRNA-seq (P5 telencephalon, tdTomato+ cells). 8,681 control vs 8,940 mutant cells; 12 clusters. Mutant had higher proportions of endothelial cells, OPCs, and oligodendrocytes (exact P = 3.1 × 10⁻¹⁰, 3.1 × 10⁻²², 7.9 × 10⁻⁹) and lower excitatory neurons and microglia. GSEA showed mostly downregulated cell-type gene programs but upregulated inhibitory-neuron and oligodendrocyte differentiation programs. Representative DEGs (many ASD-associated): Nrxn1, Nlgn1, Neurod1 (excitatory); Auts2, Nrxn3, Gphn (inhibitory); Olig1, Tcf4, Nfia (OPC).
Trajectory analysis. RNA velocity + CellRank: mature inhibitory-neuron (Gad1) and oligodendrocyte (Mbp) populations were significantly larger in mutant, while mature excitatory (Rbfox1) populations were similar — i.e., a fate shift toward ventral-derived mature cells.
EdU pulse-chase (embryo). 6 h pulse at E16.5: significantly more Ki67⁻ EdU⁺ (cell-cycle-exited) cells in the ganglionic eminence but not cortex. 24 h pulse (E15.5→E16.5): more differentiating DLX1+ interneurons and PDGFRα+ oligodendrocyte progenitors in the GE of mutants; cortical DCX+ differentiation unchanged.
Spatial transcriptomics (adult, CosMx 1000-plex, 50,505 cells, 14 clusters). Cortical L2–4 neurons: increased immediate-early genes, decreased synapse/GABA-receptor genes. Oligodendrocytes: coordinated decrease in myelin genes Mag, Mog, Plp1 (cortex-striatum fold-change correlation P = 6.0 × 10⁻²⁴). Interneurons: Slc32a1 (VGAT) downregulated. NeuronChat: decreased GABA signaling / interneuron→excitatory communication in cortex, but increased MSN/ChAT/interneuron communication in striatum.
Functional deficits. Fiber photometry + optogenetics in mPFC (ChrimsonR in VGAT+ neurons, GCaMP6f readout): mutants showed a larger Ca²⁺ response over the first 300 ms, reflecting loss of the late-phase synaptic inhibitory undershoot — i.e., weakened inhibitory connectivity. MEA of primary cultures: mutant excitatory neurons (E13.5 cortex) had reduced mean firing rate; mutant inhibitory neurons (E13.5 GE) had shorter axonal branch length. Immunostaining: reduced perisomatic VGAT+ puncta per NeuN+ soma.
Rescue. Chd8+/LSL mice (CRISPR/Cas9 LSL cassette between exons 8–9) reproduce ASD-like behavior + macrocephaly. Nestin-Cre restoration attenuated both; timed Nestin-CreERT2 restoration at E14.5 or before (not E17.5+) rescued behavior without affecting macrocephaly (e.g., light-dark E14.5 exact P = 9.8 × 10⁻⁵). Ventral-specific Olig1-Cre restoration rescued behavior (male social-interaction P = 2.6 × 10⁻⁵) and normalized DLX1+/PDGFRα+ ventral differentiation.

Connections

Assigns the ASD-relevant action of chd8 to ventral progenitors, complementing human/organoid evidence: postmortem cortex snRNA-seq shows inhibitory-neuron/OPC changes in ASD, and CHD8-disrupted cerebral organoids overproduce inhibitory neurons — cited as cross-species support (related to meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact and schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism).
A “when + which cell type” causal-mapping strategy that parallels late-rescue logic in other ASD genes — cf. adolescent allele reactivation in scn2a-conditional-ki-mouse (tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related).
Many implicated DEGs (Nrxn1/3, Nlgn1, Auts2) are recurrent ASD risk genes, tying this developmental mechanism to the broader transcriptomic dysregulation seen across autism (gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across).
Reinforces interneuron-migration / ventral-telencephalon biology as central to autism neurodevelopment and psychiatric-genetics.

Open questions

Datasets are cross-sectional snapshots (embryonic GE, P5 telencephalon, adult cortex/striatum); the authors caution they do not establish lineage-level causality across stages.
The P5 interneuron fraction was unchanged despite prenatal overdifferentiation — possibly masked by the physiological early-postnatal wave of cortical interneuron death; the true magnitude of interneuron overproduction is unresolved.
Which downstream circuit change (inhibitory hypofunction vs oligodendrocyte/myelin deficit) is most responsible for behavior, and whether the transient developmental “advancement → later hypofunction” pattern is the operative mechanism.
Behavioral rescue implies a degree of plasticity — how far the therapeutic window extends, and whether postnatal intervention could work, remains open.

wiki/sources/nord-2020-neurobiological-f.title: nord-2020-neurobiological-functions-of-transcriptional
wiki/sources/nord-2020-neurobiological-f.content:
Neurobiological functions of transcriptional enhancers
One-line: A 2020 Nature Neuroscience review placing genome-scale CNS enhancer-mapping data into decades of mechanistic enhancer biology, tracing how enhancers direct gene expression across the neuronal lifespan and how enhancer sequence variation drives brain-disorder risk.
Key takeaways

Enhancers are the most common regulatory elements in mammalian genomes (hundreds of thousands predicted in brain); they are cis-regulatory-elements whose activity depends jointly on encoded sequence (TF motifs) and trans/epigenetic context — the same element can behave differently by cell type, developmental stage, and chromosomal insertion site.
The modern “enhancer” is largely a biochemical proxy: open chromatin-accessibility (DNaseI/ATAC-seq), histone marks (H3K27ac + H3K4me1 = active; H3K4me1 alone = poised), dna-methylation (mCG and neuron-specific mCH), enhancer RNAs (eRNAs), and enhancer–promoter contacts mapped by chromatin-conformation-capture (Hi-C, ChIA-PET).
Enhancer–promoter looping is mediated by TF-recruited Mediator and CTCF, constrained within topologically-associating-domains; super-enhancers may act via phase-separated condensates (biomolecular-condensates) while sporadic loci show transcriptional bursting.
Across the neuronal lifespan enhancers are dynamically licensed then decommissioned: pioneer/identity TFs open chromatin, p300/CBP deposit active marks, and LSD1 + DNA methylation lock in silenced states — 90% of mouse forebrain putative enhancers change H3K27ac across development.
Enhancers set which activity-inducible programs a neuron can run (neuronal-activation): synaptic activity induces eRNAs that bind NELF and modulate p300/CBP, tuning bursting of immediate-early genes (Fos, Npas4); Fos itself recruits Brg1 to remodel enhancer accessibility.
Most disease-associated risk variants are noncoding-variants in putative enhancers; both rare mutations and common SNPs implicate enhancer dysregulation across psychiatric-genetics (schizophrenia, bipolar, MDD, autism).

Notable claims / data

Comparison of H3K27ac across seven mouse forebrain stages (E11.5→P56) found ~90% of putative enhancer loci change dynamically; in fetal human cortex only ~1/3 of putative regulatory regions had static accessibility between germinal zone and cortical plate.
Single-locus concordance is poor: only some epigenomic signatures (e.g. p300 in mouse brain) have high sensitivity for reporter-validated enhancer function; predicted enhancers frequently fail to drive expression in reporter assays.
Redundancy is the norm — most genes are regulated by multiple enhancers even within one cell type/stage; a single enhancer can be pleiotropic across cell types (the one-enhancer-per-domain schematic is an oversimplification).
Disease examples: point mutation at an SHH brain enhancer → holoprosencephaly; VIPR2 non-coding deletions → schizophrenia; CACNA1C intron-1 SNP alters enhancer-reporter activity (schizophrenia/bipolar); FKBP5 SNPs alter promoter–distal-element interaction (stress disorders); a 7p21 non-coding variant changes CTCF-mediated looping to the TMEM106B promoter (frontotemporal lobar degeneration).
Emerging statistical evidence for enrichment of de novo mutations in distal enhancers and promoter-proximal enhancers in simplex autism, in human accelerated regions in consanguineous ASD, and in conserved fetal-brain enhancers in developmental delay.
Functional-validation toolkit surveyed: massively-parallel-reporter-assay, CRISPR–Cas9 enhancer mutagenesis (genome-editing; NHEJ/non-homologous-end-joining vs homology-directed-repair knock-in, the latter infeasible in post-mitotic CNS cells), and epigenome editing via dCas9–KRAB/p300 (crispri-crispra).
Proof-of-concept therapeutic: dCas9-based synthetic activator targeted to a hypothalamic Sim1 enhancer rescued Sim1 expression and prevented obesity in a haploinsufficiency mouse model.
Caveat: narrative review (submitted 2019, VOL 23 Jan 2020), not a primary study; many claims from mouse/in-vitro models, and the authors stress function-based screens had “yet to be widely applied in the brain” as of writing.

Connections

Bridges enhancers × brain: complements the chromatin/accessibility view of brain regulation in kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and and the TF-occupancy profiling of loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor.
Function-based enhancer screening it anticipates is realized in deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory, sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel, lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric, and CRISPR enhancer scanning gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements.
Non-coding disease-variant interpretation theme shared with marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding and groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at.
Enhancer evolution and 3D-genome framing connect to che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome; prenatal cell-type context to velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal.

Open questions

How much true enhancer–promoter specificity exists, and how is it actualized at sequence/molecular/structural levels?
How can TF–enhancer interactions and their functional impact be predicted well enough to reverse-engineer CNS transcriptional control across the lifespan?
Which of the many rare/common non-coding variants in any genome are causal, and how do context- and activity-dependent effects — invisible to bulk or immortalized-cell assays — contribute to phenotype?
Can high-throughput, function-based enhancer screening and in-vivo CRISPR editing be scaled to the endogenous chromatin context of the brain?
