wiki/sources/nott-2019-brain-cell-type-s.title: nott-2019-brain-cell-type-specific-enhancer
wiki/sources/nott-2019-brain-cell-type-s.content:
Brain cell type–specific enhancer–promoter interactome maps and disease-risk association
One-line: Cell-type-resolved epigenomic and chromatin-loop maps of the human brain that assign noncoding disease-risk variants to specific cell types, showing psychiatric-disorder variants act in neuronal regulatory elements while Alzheimer’s disease variants concentrate in microglia enhancers.
Key takeaways

Nuclei of four major human brain cell types (PU.1+ microglia, NEUN+ neurons, OLIG2+ oligodendrocytes, LHX2+ astrocytes) were FACS-sorted from resected cortex of six individuals and profiled by chromatin-accessibility (ATAC-seq) plus H3K27ac and H3K4me3 ChIP-seq to define active promoters and enhancers.
Active promoters are largely shared across cell types, whereas active enhancers are mostly cell-type-specific, so cell-type identity is captured predominantly in the enhancer repertoire.
Partitioned-heritability (genome-wide-association-study LDSC) analysis showed strong enrichment of psychiatric-disorder and behavioral-trait heritability in neuronal enhancers/promoters, while Alzheimer’s disease risk was most enriched in microglia regulatory elements — a cell-type assignment invisible to bulk-brain annotations.
PLAC-seq (proximity-ligation-assisted H3K4me3 ChIP-seq) mapped 219,509 promoter-to-distal-region chromatin-loops, connecting disease-risk noncoding-variants in cell-type-specific enhancers to their target gene promoters.
Deleting a 363-bp microglia-specific enhancer harboring the AD-risk variant rs6733839 ablated BIN1 expression in stem-cell-derived microglia but not neurons or astrocytes, functionally validating the interactome.

Notable claims / data

Profiling sorted nuclei expanded the total count of putative brain enhancers by 87% over bulk-brain (PsychENCODE) enhancers, though 94% of bulk enhancers overlapped the nuclei set.
2,954 super-enhancers were identified across microglia, neurons, and oligodendrocytes; 83% had PLAC interactions and linked to promoters with elevated H3K27ac.
AD fine-mapping yielded 261 credible-set variants; PLAC linking identified 41 AD-risk genes across cell types, 25 in microglia (14 undetected in other cell types); a broader analysis flagged 134 putative risk genes.
PLAC loops revised variant interpretation: reassigning variants away from the nearest gene (e.g., SLC24A4 variants linked to ATXN3/TRIP11/CPSF2), extending gene sets (CLU locus adding TRIM35, CHRNA2, SCARA3, CCDC25), and revealing cell-type-specific susceptibility at PICALM and BIN1.
Microglia AD-risk genes formed a protein-protein interaction network centered on APOE and enriched for immune-function ontologies.
Caveat: small sample size (six donors) limits inter-individual analysis and may explain non-overlap of some risk alleles with the regulatory atlas; data restricted to healthy resected cortex, not diseased tissue.

Connections

Provides cell-type-resolved cis-regulatory-elements and 3D interactome maps complementing bulk-tissue regulatory atlases of the human brain.
Links noncoding-variants from genome-wide-association-study loci to target genes and to specific brain cell types, relevant to both psychiatric-genetics and neurodegeneration (Alzheimer’s disease).
Uses chromatin-loops and genome-architecture to resolve enhancer–promoter assignment, a resource for interpreting brain-disease risk.

Open questions

Do these regulatory maps hold in diseased tissue and in disease-relevant microglial subsets (e.g., amyloid-plaque-associated microglia)?
How much does inter-individual variation shape enhancer selection and function beyond what six donors can reveal?
Which additional AD-risk genes and mechanisms emerge once nuclear-sorting is refined to rarer, informative cell subsets?

wiki/sources/nurk-2022-complete-sequence.title: nurk-2022-complete-sequence-of-a-human
wiki/sources/nurk-2022-complete-sequence.content:
The complete sequence of a human genome
One-line: The Telomere-to-Telomere (T2T) Consortium presents t2t-chm13, the first gapless 3.055-billion-base-pair assembly of a human genome, closing the ~8% of the reference that had remained unsequenced since 2000.
Key takeaways

The prior GRCh38 reference (BAC-based, mosaic-haplotype) left ~151 Mbp of unknown sequence across pericentromeric/subtelomeric regions, segmental duplications, ampliconic gene arrays, and rDNA arrays. T2T-CHM13 finishes these via genome-assembly of the near-homozygous CHM13 hydatidiform-mole cell line.
Assembly leveraged complementary long reads: PacBio HiFi (20-kbp, 0.1% error) for differentiating near-identical repeat copies, and Oxford Nanopore ultralong (>100-kbp) reads for spanning repeats. A HiFi string graph was built from homopolymer-compressed, error-corrected reads; ambiguous tangles were resolved manually with ONT-read multiplicity.
The completed regions include all centromeric satellite arrays, recent segmental duplications, and the short arms of all five acrocentric chromosomes — regions previously inaccessible to sequence-based analysis.
rDNA arrays (219 complete copies, 9.9 Mbp) could not be resolved by the string graph and required specialized sparse de Bruijn graphs plus ONT-derived “morph” clustering.
T2T-CHM13 is a more complete and representative reference for both short- and long-read variant calling across all ancestries, reducing spurious variant calls.

Notable claims / data

Assembly comprises 3,054,815,472 bp of nuclear DNA (chr1-22 + chrX; chrY absent) plus a 16,569-bp mitochondrial genome. Contig NG50 rises to 154.26 Mbp (vs 56.41 Mbp for GRCh38); gap and unplaced bases drop to zero.
Adds/corrects 238 Mbp of non-syntenic sequence (76% centromeric satellites, 19% non-satellite segmental duplications, 4% rDNA); 182 Mbp has no primary alignment to GRCh38 and is exclusive to T2T-CHM13.
Annotation totals 63,494 genes and 233,615 transcripts (19,969 protein-coding); 3604 genes are CHM13-exclusive, and 1956 genes (99 protein-coding) lie in regions with no primary alignment to GRCh38.
Consensus accuracy estimated at ~1 error per 10 Mbp; only 0.3% of the assembly is flagged as potential-issue bases, versus ~8% for GRCh38.
CHM13 is largely of European ancestry (with Neanderthal introgression); estimated ~400 diploid rDNA copies (409 ± 9 by ddPCR). Reanalysis of 3202 1000 Genomes short-read datasets showed simultaneous reduction of false-negative and false-positive variant calls.
The five acrocentric short arms (combined 66.1 Mbp) are unusually similar to one another (median 98.7% identity in 5-kbp windows), consistent with recurrent ectopic recombination in the nucleolus.

Connections

Provides the completed human reference underlying downstream studies of noncoding-variants and cis-regulatory-elements in previously inaccessible repeat regions.
The assembly resource complements long-read transcriptome methods (Iso-seq was used here for annotation support) relevant to long-read-rna-seq work elsewhere in the wiki.

Open questions

Reproducible coverage anomalies within certain satellite arrays are attributed to sample-prep/sequencing/base-calling bias rather than assembly error, but the specific mechanisms remain to be characterized.
Three of the longer rDNA arrays (chr13, 15, 21) capture chromosome-specific morphs but are arranged as model sequences, since ONT reads could not fully resolve their ordering.
The assembly is a single homozygous genome lacking chromosome Y; a diploid, pan-genome complement is needed to represent full human variation.

wiki/sources/olson-2023-variant-calling-.title: olson-2023-variant-calling-benchmarking-era
wiki/sources/olson-2023-variant-calling-.content:
Variant calling and benchmarking in an era of complete human genome sequences
One-line: A methods review of how long reads, deep learning, de novo assembly and pangenomes extend germline variant calling into repetitive, medically relevant regions, and how new benchmark sets and comparison tools reveal method strengths and limits.
Key takeaways

Small-variant calling from short reads (150-250 bp) is now highly accurate in mappable regions, but short reads fail where a read is shorter than a repeat or duplication (segmental duplications, LINEs, STRs, VNTRs, telomeres, satellite DNA up to 30 Mb).
Long reads solve much of this: PacBio HiFi (highly accurate 15-20 kb circular-consensus reads) and ultralong ONT reads (>2 Mb, N50 >100 kb) together enabled the first telomere-to-telomere (T2T-CHM13) human assembly, which corrected GRCh38 errors and added ~7% of previously unassembled repetitive sequence.
Deep learning (mostly CNNs, e.g. DeepVariant) has become the dominant variant-calling/filtering architecture because openly available benchmarks provide millions of training variants; it enables rapid adoption of new sequencing technologies.
Three broad workflow axes: read mapping vs de novo assembly, and linear vs pangenome (graph) reference. Haplotype-resolved (“diploid”) assembly plus pangenome alignment is now the leading way to resolve complex variation in the hardest regions.
Benchmarks (a variant VCF plus a benchmark-regions BED) let any lab run a characterized sample (e.g. GIAB HG002) and measure recall and precision against a shared truth set; the Genome in a Bottle (GIAB) benchmarks anchor the field.

Notable claims / data

One in seven pathogenic variants can be difficult to detect with standard short-read sequencing (large clinical-lab analysis).
Segmental duplications and tandem repeats accounted for >90% of large deletions found by long reads but missed by short reads.
GIAB germline benchmarks grew from 77% of autosomal GRCh37 bases (2014) to 88% (2016-2019) to 94% (2020-2021); the v4.2.1 benchmark still excludes 8% of GRCh38 sequence on top of the 7% missing from GRCh38.
In the first precisionFDA Truth Challenge, short-read callers reached >99.9% SNV and >99% indel accuracy inside v3.2 regions, yet concordance between top callers OUTSIDE those regions was <80%; against the harder v4.2.1 benchmark, SNV accuracy of earlier top submissions dropped up to 10-fold.
Variant accuracy can differ by orders of magnitude by variant type and genome context, so stratifying performance (GA4GH/GIAB stratifications) is essential; aggregate metrics overestimate genome-wide accuracy.
Benchmarking tools: hap.py with the vcfeval engine is GA4GH best practice for germline small variants; Truvari and hap-eval handle structural variants at varying stringency. No standards yet exist for representing many complex variants, complex SVs, or extra copies of segmental duplications.
Broad informed consent matters: GIAB trios are consented for public and commercial sharing (enabling spike-ins, mimicked FFPE/cfDNA), whereas NA12878’s 11 children and current tumour-normal pairs (e.g. COLO829) are restricted (dbGaP), limiting somatic benchmarking.

Connections

Complements the wiki’s genome-assembly material by focusing on how assembly quality feeds variant calling and benchmark construction.
Relevant to noncoding-variants and other variant-interpretation topics, since medically relevant genes (SMN1/SMN2, HLA, KIR, LPA, CYP2D6, RHCE) sit in exactly the repetitive regions that short reads miss.
The RIDE (reliable identification of errors) design principle and the Shneiderman “overview, zoom-and-filter, details-on-demand” curation workflow (via IGV) are methodological ideas transferable to other benchmarking efforts.

Open questions

How to represent and benchmark complex variants, satellites and copy-number-variable segmental duplications when no standard representation (VCF SVTYPE ambiguity: INS vs DUP vs BND vs TRA) exists?
How to build benchmarks across diverse ancestries (few exist for African samples, where pangenomes most improve calls) and for the X/Y chromosomes and centromeric arrays newly opened by T2T?
Which future trajectory wins: linear reference (T2T-CHM13/GRCh38), population-specific reference, reference flow, or graph-based pangenome, and what benchmarking each will need?
Consented tumour-normal cell lines with public data are still missing for robust somatic benchmarks.

wiki/sources/oneill-2024-parse-seq-a-cal.title: oneill-2024-parse-seq-a-calibrated-multiplexed-assay
wiki/sources/oneill-2024-parse-seq-a-cal.content:
ParSE-seq: a calibrated multiplexed assay to facilitate the clinical classification of putative splice-altering variants
One-line: Introduces Parallel Splice Effect Sequencing (ParSE-seq), a barcoded minigene massively-parallel-reporter-assay that quantifies percent-spliced-in for hundreds of variants at once and is calibrated against ClinVar controls to the ACMG “strong” evidence level, applied to the arrhythmia gene SCN5A.
Key takeaways

ParSE-seq is a multiplexed, calibrated functional assay for splicing-and-disease: variants are placed in a barcoded triplet-exon minigene, pooled, transfected, and read out by RNA-seq to compute percent-spliced-in (PSI) per barcode (splicing-methods).
The minigene uses a modified pET01 vector with the test SCN5A exon flanked by rat insulin exons and up to 250 bp of native intronic sequence on each side; 18-mer barcodes in the downstream exon are linked to constructs by PacBio long-read sequencing and to splicing outcomes by short-read RNA-seq.
Run in two cell types — HEK293 and disease-relevant iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes (induced-pluripotent-stem-cells / iPSC-CMs) — with high concordance between cell types (ΔPSI_norm R²=0.84) and between replicates.
Calibrated against 69 ClinVar benign/pathogenic controls using the ClinGen/ACMG OddsPath framework: reached PS3 (strong pathogenic, OddsPath 21.1) and BS3 (strong benign) evidence strength in iPSC-CMs.
Reclassified 29 of 34 conflicting-interpretation (CI) variants and 11 of 42 variants of uncertain significance (VUS) — 8 VUS to likely pathogenic/benign categories and 28 CI to likely benign — reducing clinical uncertainty.
Beyond canonical splice-sites, many synonymous and missense variants disrupted splicing; missense variants can be miscalled “normal” by cDNA-based patch-clamp assays that cannot see splicing.
Validated the mechanism at the endogenous locus: CRISPR-edited iPSC-CMs for missense c.4220C>G (p.Ala1407Gly) showed a 31-bp out-of-frame exon truncation, NMD degradation (increased by cycloheximide), and abrogated sodium current — a splice-altering (not protein-altering) loss-of-function.

Notable claims / data

Library: 244 SCN5A variants (258 SNVs + 12 indels) across 19 of 27 coding exons; 172 exonic, 98 intronic; three source categories (212 clinically observed + 55 SpliceAI-predicted high-impact + ClinVar controls).
3238 barcodes linked to 284 unique inserts (median 11 barcodes/insert); barcode frequencies well correlated between PacBio and Illumina (R=0.82).
iPSC-CM assay: near-perfect control concordance — 24/25 P/LP variants abnormal, 21/22 B/LB variants normal; HEK assay similar (OddsPath_Pathogenic 26.13, OddsPath_Benign 0.032).
105/94 → 104/78/42 (HEK/iPSC-CM) variants yielded determinate normal/abnormal/indeterminate calls; no discordance in direction between the two cell types for variants determinate in both.
Aberrant splicing modes observed: exon skipping, exon truncation, intron retention, sometimes multiple events per variant; SpliceAI predicted many but not all exact molecular outcomes (a variant with a predicted event could produce a different cryptic site or event class).
In silico correlation with ΔPSI_norm: SpliceAI Spearman ρ=−0.81 (best), Pangolin −0.67, AbSplice −0.61; 39/41 exonic variants with SpliceAI >0.80 disrupted splicing (high positive predictive value).
c.4220C>G had near-normal electrophysiology in a cDNA patch-clamp assay (which lacks introns) but abolished current at the endogenous locus — a concrete demonstration that cDNA assays can misannotate splice-altering missense variants.
ΔPSI_norm thresholds derived from B/LB controls (mean −2.1%, SD 17%): z<−3 (≈−53%) abnormal, z>−1 (≈−19%) normal, in-between indeterminate; FDR<0.1 required.
Caveats: single-exon-per-construct design (misses multi-exon skipping context); some exons unstudyable (non-canonical AC/AT splice sites, high-GC synthesis failures, exon 15); minigene may miss native long-range regulatory context; benign functional evidence applied only to synonymous/intronic variants (a normal splice result doesn’t exclude protein dysfunction for missense).

Connections

A calibrated, ACMG-aware splicing-methods complement to saturation-mutagenesis and other multiplexed splicing assays; extends minigene assays to clinical variant classification for splicing-and-disease.
Directly benchmarks in silico splicing predictors — relates to spliceai, splicing-code, and variant-effect-prediction — showing SpliceAI’s high but imperfect concordance and proposing experiment to prioritize which predicted splice-disruptors to test.
Reinforces (as in other sources) that synonymous and missense variants outside the 2-bp canonical splice-sites are an under-recognized loss-of-function mechanism; PSI as the quantitative readout links to percent-spliced-in.
Uses induced-pluripotent-stem-cells-derived cardiomyocytes as a disease-relevant cellular context and CRISPR endogenous-locus editing to confirm splice-driven loss-of-function.

Open questions

How well does the single-exon minigene generalize to genes where multi-exon or long-range intronic regulation dominates (e.g. TTN, large exons)?
Can prospective SpliceAI/Pangolin/AbSplice prioritization plus ParSE-seq scale to genome-wide clinical variant triage across many Mendelian disease genes?
For missense variants, how should splice-normal but potentially protein-altering results be integrated so benign evidence is not over-applied?
Would combining ParSE-seq (RNA level) with complementary protein-function MAVEs give a complete functional picture for ion-channel and other loss-of-function genes?

wiki/sources/osterwalder-2018-enhancer-r.title: osterwalder-2018-enhancer-redundancy-provides-phenotypic-robustness
wiki/sources/osterwalder-2018-enhancer-r.content:
Enhancer redundancy provides phenotypic robustness in mammalian development
One-line: In vivo mouse enhancer-deletion series showing that multiple enhancers with overlapping activity near the same gene act redundantly, buffering limb development against loss of any single enhancer.
Key takeaways

Distant-acting tissue-specific cis-regulatory-elements outnumber genes ~10-fold; this study tests whether that regulatory complexity is functionally redundant rather than each enhancer being individually essential.
23 CRISPR-Cas9 mouse deletion lines across seven limb-development loci; none of ten single-enhancer deletions caused any detectable limb morphological abnormality, despite all enhancers being highly conserved with robust limb activity.
Deleting pairs of enhancers near the same gene (Gli3, Shox2) produced clear skeletal phenotypes, demonstrating enhancer redundancy at the level of organismal morphology.
Redundancy operates via additive effects on gene expression level: in a target-gene-sensitized (heterozygous) background, even single enhancer deletions caused limb abnormalities.
A genome-wide correlation analysis shows redundant enhancer landscapes are pervasive near developmental genes, a mammalian analog of invertebrate shadow enhancers.

Notable claims / data

Ten individual enhancer knockouts: no abnormalities in bone number, shape, length, position, or mineralization; 9/10 showed no significant target-gene expression change in E11.5 limbs.
Double knockout (DKO) at Gli3 (mm1179 + hs1586) reduced Gli3 expression in the hand plate and caused forelimb-specific preaxial polydactyly (digit-1 duplication); DKO at Shox2 (hs741 + hs1262) reduced Shox2 expression and femur ossification (femur:tibia P = 4.12 x 10^-9). The hs1467-mm636 pair (2 of 3) showed no DKO phenotype.
Fluorescent enhancer-reporter transgenes (mm1179-GFP, hs1586-mCherry, hs741-GFP, hs1262-mCherry) showed double-positive cells, confirming cellular-level activity overlap of redundant enhancer pairs.
Sensitized backgrounds: over a single functional Gli3 or Shox2 allele, loss of either single enhancer produced phenotypes more severe than the gene heterozygote; loss of both enhancers over a null allele approached homozygous-gene-loss severity.
Genome-wide: H3K27ac ChIP-seq / RNA-seq correlations across 12 mouse tissues (2-3 timepoints) assigned enhancers to target genes within the same TAD; limb transcription-factor genes had a median of ~8 enhancers; 1,058 genes were associated with 5+ enhancers of putatively redundant (same-tissue) activity.
Capture-C confirmed physical enhancer-target interaction for at least 6 of the tested enhancers; knockouts generated via NHEJ (non-homologous-end-joining) deletion breakpoints.

Connections

Provides an in vivo functional counterpoint to enhancer prediction from chromatin-accessibility and histone marks, addressing what fraction of predicted cis-regulatory-elements are individually essential.
Enhancer-to-gene assignment within TADs relates to chromatin-conformation-based regulatory-landscape mapping.
Bears directly on interpreting noncoding-variants: authors argue most enhancer loss-of-function variants cause at most subtle human phenotypes, so enhancer-linked disease more likely stems from gain-of-function or enhancer-repositioning mutations.
Demonstrates in vivo enhancer dissection by genome-editing with paired sgRNAs.

Open questions

What determines whether a redundant enhancer pair is phenotypically buffering (Gli3, Shox2) versus dispensable in combination (hs1467-mm636)?
If individually deletable enhancers are dispensable under lab conditions, what selective pressure maintains their strong evolutionary conservation — subtle fitness contributions, or additional tissue-specific functions?
How does additive dosage buffering scale for genes with 5+ redundant enhancers, and where are the functional thresholds?

wiki/sources/pacesa-2024-past-present-an.title: pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of
wiki/sources/pacesa-2024-past-present-an.content:
Past, present, and future of CRISPR genome editing technologies
One-line: A comprehensive Cell Review (from the Jinek lab) mapping the past, present, and future of CRISPR-based genome-editing — nucleases, base/prime editors, epigenome/RNA editors, their limitations, and therapeutic and research applications.
Key takeaways

CRISPR-Cas systems are prokaryotic adaptive immune defenses against phages/mobile elements that store invader-DNA fragments in repetitive arrays; transcription and processing yield crRNAs that guide Cas proteins to cleave complementary nucleic acids. Repurposing this RNA-guided cleavage for genome-editing followed the 2012 dual-RNA (crRNA+tracrRNA) mechanism and the single-guide RNA (sgRNA) simplification, enabling a “one-nuclease-one-guide-RNA” programmable editor.
Two archetypal nucleases anchor the field: SpCas9 (from Streptococcus pyogenes; NGG PAM; blunt DSBs 3 nt upstream of PAM via HNH + RuvC domains) and Cas12a (type V; TTTV PAM, no tracrRNA required, self-processes its crRNA, generates staggered 5’ overhangs, and has trans-cleavage ssDNA activity exploited for diagnostics).
Edits arise from cellular DSB repair: error-prone end-joining (non-homologous end joining/NHEJ, MMEJ) produces indels for gene knockout, whereas homology-directed repair (HDR) with a donor template enables precise correction — but HDR is largely restricted to dividing cells (S/G2 phases) and is inefficient in post-mitotic cells like neurons.
The review frames four principal limitations of first-generation CRISPR editors — specificity (off-target activity), targeting scope (PAM requirement), control of editing outcomes (reliance on stochastic DSB repair), and delivery — each addressed by specific technological solutions (Figure 2).
“Second-generation” editors bypass DSBs: base editors (BEs) (nCas9 fused to cytidine or adenosine deaminase → C-to-T/CBE or A-to-G/ABE, later expanded to A-to-Y, C-to-G, etc.) and prime editors (PEs) (nCas9-reverse transcriptase + pegRNA; insertions ~40 bp, deletions ~80 bp, edits within ~30 bp of the nick).
Beyond editing DNA sequence: CRISPRi/CRISPRa (dCas9-KRAB / dCas9-VP64 transcriptional modulators), RNA editors (Cas13-ADAR for A-to-I, plus m6A epitranscriptomic editors), and epigenome editors (CRISPRoff/CRISPRon for heritable silencing/reactivation without altering DNA sequence).
Applications span reverse genetics (isogenic disease models in cells/organoids/animals), forward genetics (pooled CRISPR screens, lineage tracing, molecular recorders), and therapeutics (ex vivo edited cells + in vivo LNP/AAV delivery).

Notable claims / data

First CRISPR-based human therapy approved in late 2023; ex vivo editing for sickle cell disease and β-thalassemia (via BCL11A erythroid-enhancer disruption in CD34+ HSCs by Cas9 RNP) approved in Europe, UK, and USA.
SpCas9 NGG PAM occurs roughly every 8 nucleotides on average; alternative-PAM engineered variants (VQR/EQR/VRER → NGAN/NGNG/NGCG; xCas/near-PAMless NRN/NRRN designs) expand scope but relaxed PAM targeting lowers specificity.
High-fidelity SpCas9 variants (eSpCas9, SpCas9-HF1, HypaCas9, Sniper2L) reduce off-targets via two approaches: structure-based rational mutation of DNA/protein contacts and directed evolution; off-target profiles remain target-dependent and no variant is universally applicable.
Off-target tolerance: Cas9 tolerates a considerable number/variety of guide-target mismatches; most potential off-targets are merely bound, not cleaved, due to intrinsic cleavage checkpoints; in vivo off-target cleavage frequency is consistently lower than with isolated genomic DNA.
Prime editing can introduce insertions up to ~40 bp and deletions up to ~80 bp; the MMLV RT was engineered across PE generations for thermostability/processivity; PE still can generate on-target DSBs/indels.
AAV cargo limit is ~4.7 kb; SpCas9 CDS is ~4.2 kb and its sgRNA ~100 nt, so SpCas9 barely fits a single AAV — motivating compact Cas9 orthologs (SauCas9, CjeCas9, NmeCas9, GeoCas9) and Cas12-family enzymes; LNPs (Cas9 mRNA + synthetic sgRNA) are a non-viral alternative, notably for liver via LDL-receptor uptake.
In vivo clinical proof-of-concept: LNP-delivered Cas9 mRNA + sgRNA knockdown of TTR in the liver reduced serum TTR for transthyretin amyloidosis — described as the first successful systemically delivered in vivo CRISPR therapy; ABE targeting of PCSK9 lowers LDL cholesterol (NHP + phase I).
Multiplexed editing: up to 62 unique genomic sites inactivated in a single cell line via distinct sgRNAs (e.g., endogenous retrovirus inactivation for xenotransplantation).
Emerging (third-generation) tools for large-payload insertion and DSB-free regulation: CRISPR-associated transposons (CASTs), engineered CRISPR integrases (PE-installed recombinase att sites), DNA polymerase editors (e.g., click editing, tethered DDPs), retroelement/TPRT-based insertion (silkworm R2), and minimal ancestral RNA-guided nucleases (IscB/TnpB → OMEGA/HEARO systems; Fanzors in eukaryotes; ultracompact Cas12f, 422 aa).
Artificial intelligence is flagged as an increasingly central force: predicting editing outcomes and off-target activity, and de novo design of novel nucleases and guides via protein design methods — with caveats about training-data quality and black-box interpretability.

Connections

Extends the genome-editing concept page with a structured taxonomy (nucleases → base/prime editors → transcriptional/RNA/epigenome editors → transposon/integrase/polymerase tools).
Underpins functional-single-cell-genomics: CRISPR screening is the engine behind pooled-crispr-screen, single-cell-crispr-screen, and perturb-seq — the review names Perturb-seq, CROP-seq, and Mosaic-seq as single-cell CRISPR readouts, connecting to existing sources dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening, and gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements.
Complements splicing-therapeutics: shares the therapeutic frame (SCD/β-thalassemia, TTR amyloidosis) with antisense/splicing-modulator approaches for genetic disease.
Ties to the wiki’s ai-in-genomics and foundation-models / protein-language-models threads via AI-driven nuclease and guide design.

Open questions

Delivery is repeatedly named the primary bottleneck — which non-viral (LNP, virus-like particles, cell-penetrating peptides, contractile injection systems) approaches will generalize beyond liver/blood to brain, muscle, and other tissues?
Can DSB-free editors (BEs, PEs, epigenome editors) fully eliminate the genotoxic risks (large deletions, chromosomal rearrangements, p53 activation) that motivated their development?
How reliably can AI models predict off-target activity and design novel editors given limited/biased training data and interpretability limits?
Germline editing: the review states current methods are not sufficiently safe/efficacious for reproductive use — what governance and technical thresholds would change that calculus?

wiki/sources/pampari-2024-chrombpnet-bia.title: pampari-2024-chrombpnet-bias-factorized-base-resolution
wiki/sources/pampari-2024-chrombpnet-bia.content:
ChromBPNet: bias factorized, base-resolution deep learning models of chromatin accessibility reveal cis-regulatory sequence syntax, transcription factor footprints and regulatory variants
One-line: A lightweight base-resolution deep-learning model that deconvolves enzyme bias from regulatory sequence to learn TF motif syntax, footprints, and predict noncoding variant effects on chromatin accessibility.
Key takeaways

chrombpnet models base-resolution DNase-seq/ATAC-seq accessibility profiles as a function of local DNA sequence, explicitly factoring out assay-specific DNase-I/Tn5 enzyme cleavage bias to recover the true regulatory signal.
Enables de novo discovery of compact TF motif lexicons and cooperative cis-regulatory-grammar plus precision TF footprints, without relying on pre-defined motifs the way classical footprinting does.
Despite its small size, it is competitive with much larger sequence dna-language-models at predicting the effect of noncoding-variants on chromatin-accessibility, pioneer TF binding, and reporter activity across cell contexts and ancestry.
Uses model-interpretability (contribution scores) to explain how disease-associated variants disrupt regulatory syntax, helping prioritize GWAS and de novo rare-disease variants in accessible cis-regulatory-elements.

Notable claims / data

Predicts total counts and profile shape well: Pearson r = 0.70 +/- 0.02 (p < 1e-10) across held-out chromosomes; peak classification auROC = 0.98 +/- 0.001 (AP = 0.42 ATAC / 0.46 DNase).
On chromatin-QTL variant effect prediction it matches or beats Enformer; identified and corrected flaws in Enformer’s published variant scores (recomputed as allelic logFC over local 2 Kb windows).
Defines variant scores including log fold-change (logFC) of predicted coverage and Active Allele Quantile (AAQ).
On the CAGI5 MPRA benchmark (>30,000 single variants) it matched Enformer within +/- 0.05 Pearson; enrichment for fine-mapped variants reached up to 70-fold at stringent score thresholds.
Applied to fetal heart/brain pseudobulk ATAC to prioritize de novo variants in patients; example variant reduced predicted accessibility by 40%.

Connections

A deep-learning complement to massively-parallel-reporter-assay approaches for decoding cis-regulatory-elements; benchmarked against CAGI5 MPRA data.
Related in spirit to kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and and loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor on single-cell ATAC and TF regulation.
Complements deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory and lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric on experimental readouts of regulatory variant effects.
Feeds genome-wide-association-study interpretation by scoring noncoding-variants in accessible chromatin.

Open questions

How well do single-cell/pseudobulk ChromBPNet models transfer to sparsely-covered rare cell types?
Can bias-factorized base-resolution models be extended to link accessibility syntax to 3D genome architecture and long-range regulation?
How to integrate ChromBPNet variant scores with expression/gene-level effects for full noncoding interpretation?

wiki/sources/pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-.title: pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing
wiki/sources/pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-.content:
Deep surveying of alternative splicing complexity in the human transcriptome by high-throughput sequencing
One-line: The first mRNA-Seq survey of alternative splicing across normal human tissues, which raised the estimate of alternatively spliced multiexon human genes to ~95% and put the count of intermediate- to high-abundance alternative splicing events at roughly 100,000.
Key takeaways

First analysis of alternative splicing complexity in human tissues using mRNA-Seq (Illumina Genome Analyzer) data, profiling six normal tissues: whole brain, cerebral cortex, heart, skeletal muscle, lung and liver, with datasets of 17–32 million 32-nt reads.
Combining mRNA-Seq with EST-cDNA data, the authors estimate that transcripts from ~95% of multiexon genes undergo alternative splicing — a substantial jump over the prior microarray/EST-based estimate of ~74% (two-thirds of genes).
New (previously uncatalogued) splice junctions were detected in roughly 20% of surveyed multiexon genes, many of them tissue specific, demonstrating that EST-cDNA catalogues substantially undercount alternative splicing.
mRNA-Seq exon inclusion estimates (a precursor notion of percent inclusion / percent-spliced-in) correlate well with an established quantitative alternative splicing microarray system, validating sequencing as a quantitative readout for splicing levels.
A foundational data point for the field’s view of alternative-splicing pervasiveness; relates to broader splicing-methods and the transition from microarray to sequencing-based splicing measurement.

Notable claims / data

Junction libraries mined from 15,702 multiexon UniGene clusters: 257,257 “known” junctions (EST/cDNA-supported) and 2,459,306 candidate “new” junctions (all hypothetical 5’-to-3’ splice-site pairings).
A logistic-regression classifier (trained on known vs. control “reverse” junctions, five features) achieved 94% sensitivity at 95% specificity by tenfold cross-validation.
Across the six tissues combined, 128,395–130,854 (49.9–50.9%) of the 257,257 known junctions were detected, versus only 121–135 (0.04–0.052%) of control junctions; each tissue contributed 18–31% of detected known junctions.
4,294–11,099 new splice junctions detected, corresponding to one or more new junction in 2,948 (18.8%) to 3,788 (24.1%) of surveyed genes.
Combining EST, cDNA and mRNA-Seq, >85% of multiexon genes contain at least one alternative splicing event; at high read coverage (16 to >500 reads/100 nt) AS events are detectable in 92–97% of multiexon genes — hence the ~95% headline.
Alternative splicing events per gene increase near-linearly (not quadratically) with exon number; median 0.5–0.75 AS events per exon at 32–256 reads/100 bases. From 175,944 exons across 15,702 genes, they predict ~88,000–132,000 AS events of comparable abundance in major human tissues, i.e. at least ~7 AS events per multiexon gene.
mRNA-Seq vs. microarray percent-inclusion correlation: r = 0.80 (n = 1,548) at a ≥20-read threshold, rising to r = 0.85 (n = 546) at ≥50 reads.
Caveats: estimates given as ranges depending on inclusion/exclusion of “repeat” junction reads; the survey covers only six tissues, and authors predict the new-junction count would at least double with fuller coverage. Quantitative validation relied on unpublished microarray data and supplementary methods. mRNA-Seq datasets deposited at GEO accession GSE13652.

Connections

A foundational counterpart to method-centric splicing sources in the wiki such as shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection; this paper is the earlier “how pervasive is human AS” landmark that later quantification tools build on.
The exon-inclusion measurement validated here is conceptually the basis for percent-spliced-in, later formalized in tools like rmats.
Broad context for alternative-splicing and splicing-methods; complements review/overview sources like marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative and lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative by supplying the empirical pervasiveness estimate they cite.

Open questions

How much higher does the AS-gene fraction and event count go with deeper coverage and many more tissues (the authors expect new-junction counts to at least double)?
How many “new” junctions are functional/translated versus noise or low-abundance transcriptional leakage?
Why does selection appear to limit splicing complexity (near-linear, not quadratic, growth) in large genes — what constrains AS frequency per exon?
How well do these early short-read (32-nt) junction-based estimates hold up against full-length / long-read isoform sequencing?

wiki/sources/pao-2025-dawn-of-gene-editi.title: pao-2025-dawn-of-gene-editing
wiki/sources/pao-2025-dawn-of-gene-editi.content:
The Dawn of Gene Editing (Breakthrough, Ch. 7)
One-line: A popular-science book chapter narrating the six-decade path from the
1910 description of sickle cell disease to Casgevy/CTX001 — the first approved CRISPR
therapy — told as the “detective story” of one tiny scrap of DNA (the BCL11A erythroid
enhancer).

Note on source type: this is a narrative book chapter (Pao, Breakthrough,
2025), not primary literature or a peer-reviewed review. Ingested as a one-off
exception to the wiki’s no-books rule at the user’s request; treat its claims as
journalistic science-history framing, and defer to the peer-reviewed sources
(stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of,
villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome,
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of) for technical detail.

Key takeaways

The disease. Sickle cell disease is an autosomal-recessive condition (two mutant
copies of HBB, encoding β-globin) first described in 1910 (Herrick/Irons, patient
Walter Clement Noel); sickled red cells clog vessels, causing agonizing pain crises,
organ damage, stroke and early death. Millions are affected, predominantly people of
African descent (~150,000 affected births/year in Nigeria alone). Treatment has barely
advanced in a century.
The evolutionary puzzle & the clue. The mutation persists because sickle-cell
trait (one copy) protects against malaria (Anthony Allison’s hypothesis). The
therapeutic clue: fetal haemoglobin (HbF) — made before birth, then switched off —
does not sickle. People with hereditary persistence of fetal haemoglobin (HPFH) who
keep making HbF are protected from both sickle cell disease and β-thalassaemia.
The target gene. Stuart Orkin and Vijay Sankaran identified BCL11A as the master
repressor that flips haemoglobin production from fetal to adult at birth. Knocking down
BCL11A (specifically via its erythroid enhancer, to spare its other roles)
reactivates HbF — turning a lifelong disease into a manageable one.
The edit. CTX001 / Casgevy (casgevy) uses cas9 to disrupt the BCL11A
erythroid enhancer in a patient’s own CD34⁺ haematopoietic stem cells ex vivo; the
edited cells are reinfused and repopulate the marrow, switching HbF back on. It is a
genome-editing cure via targeted gene disruption (a non-homologous-end-joining
knockout of a regulatory element), not gene correction.
The approval. Developed by CRISPR Therapeutics and Vertex; Victoria Gray was the
landmark first sickle cell patient treated. Casgevy became the first approved CRISPR
medicine (UK/US 2023, β-thalassaemia 2024) — the “dawn of gene editing” in the clinic.

Notable claims / data

Sickle cell disease was described scientifically in 1910; a century later acute pain
crises are still managed largely with warmth, fluids and analgesia.
Hydroxyurea (a chemotherapy drug that boosts HbF) remains one of the few approved
sickle-cell treatments — foreshadowing HbF reactivation as the therapeutic strategy.
The therapeutic “prize” is a short non-coding regulatory sequence (the BCL11A
erythroid enhancer) — it encodes no protein, underscoring the importance of
noncoding regulatory DNA.

Connections

The human-story companion to the wiki’s technical CRISPR-therapy sources:
stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of analyzes Casgevy’s
off-target profile (variant-aware nomination), while
villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome and
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of cite BCL11A-enhancer disruption as the
archetypal approved ex vivo Cas9 therapy.
Reinforces the crispr topic’s mechanism → tool → therapy arc from the patient/
clinical end, and pairs with kernohan-2024-expanding-diagnostic-toolbox-for-rare as
the therapy side of the diagnosis→therapy story.

Open questions

Cost and access: ex vivo HSC editing is a bespoke, expensive procedure — how does an
approved CRISPR cure reach the African populations who bear most of the disease burden?
Durability and safety of enhancer-disruption knockouts over a patient’s lifetime
(addressed technically in the peer-reviewed sources above, not this chapter).

wiki/sources/parikshak-2013-integrative-.title: parikshak-2013-integrative-functional-genomic-analyses-implicate
wiki/sources/parikshak-2013-integrative-.content:
Integrative Functional Genomic Analyses Implicate Specific Molecular Pathways and Circuits in Autism
One-line: Maps ASD and intellectual-disability risk genes onto human cortical developmental coexpression networks, showing ASD genes converge on distinct synaptic and early-transcriptional modules and superficial-layer glutamatergic circuits, with a molecular signature distinct from ID.
Key takeaways

Builds signed WGCNA gene-coexpression-network modules (17 total) from BrainSpan human neocortex RNA-seq (PCW 8 to 12 months postnatal), capturing biological processes of neurodevelopment; 12 modules are reproducible, GO-enriched, and protein-protein-interaction-enriched.
Curated ASD risk genes (SFARI ASD, 155 genes) and unbiased dysregulation genes (asdM12 from Voineagu et al. 2011) both converge on the same three synaptic modules M13, M16, M17 despite only 15 genes overlapping between the two lists — evidence of biological convergence in heterogeneous autism.
Rare de novo protein-disrupting/missense variants (RDNVs) instead concentrate in two early fetal transcriptional-regulation modules, M2 and M3, enriched for chromatin remodelers (BAF/SWI-SNF complex).
Intellectual-disability (“ID all”, 401 genes) shows NO enrichment in any module, establishing a molecular distinction between ASD and ID that supports phenotypic specificity — relevant to psychiatric-genetics.
ASD-associated modules are linked by translational regulation (FMRP CLIP targets enriched in M2/M16/M17) and shared transcription-factor coregulation (17 TFs including MEF2A/MEF2C, SATB1, FOXO1, ELF1 bridging anticorrelated modules).
At a circuit level, ASD genes are enriched in superficial cortical layers (L2-L4) and upper-layer glutamatergic projection neuron markers (neuronal-cell-types), whereas ID genes trend toward deeper layers.

Notable claims / data

42% of asdM12 and 25% of SFARI ASD genes fall into M13/M16/M17; SFARI ASD most overrepresented in M16 (p=0.0024, OR=2.9).
asdM12 strongly enriched in M13 (OR 3.6), M16 (OR 3.9), M17 (OR 2.5), each FDR<0.05.
Combined across four exome studies (965 probands, 565 siblings), 52 RDNV genes in M2 (p=9.6e-6, OR 2.0) and 61 in M3 (p=8.5e-7, OR 2.1); silent RDNVs and sibling variants show no enrichment (key mutability control).
19% (113/598) of RDNV-affected genes coexpress in M2/M3; these genes are more dosage-sensitive (higher probability of haploinsufficiency), consistent with heterozygous state in probands.
FMRP targets: M16 enrichment OR 5.7 (p=2.4e-29). ChIP data support many predicted TF binding sites (39% MEF2A, 23% MEF2C, 87% ELF1).
Common-variant lists (SFARI/asdM12) affect synaptic modules; larger-effect noninherited RDNVs affect early transcriptional modules — but not absolute (M16 carries SCN2A, SHANK2, NRXN1 RDNVs).
M2 peaks later and enriches in cortical plate; M3 peaks earlier and enriches in germinal zone (VZ/SZ), consistent with proliferation-to-differentiation timing.

Connections

Uses the ASD cohort/database landscape overlapping the simons-simplex-collection exome-sequencing studies feeding the RDNV analysis.
Complements neurogenesis and progenitor-proliferation biology via the early M2/M3 chromatin-remodeling modules.
Coexpression-network convergence framework is a methodological anchor for later network-based psychiatric-genetics gene-set analyses in autism.

Open questions

How do the synaptic (M13/M16/M17) versus early-transcriptional (M2/M3) axes causally interact to produce ASD phenotypes rather than more severe or ID outcomes?
Are the predicted TF-coregulation links (e.g., MEF2C, SATB1) mechanistically required, and do they operate in specific cell types or lineages?
Does the superficial-layer glutamatergic enrichment reflect a developmental origin or a mature circuit vulnerability?

wiki/sources/parikshak-2016-genome-wide-.title: parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna
wiki/sources/parikshak-2016-genome-wide-.content:
Genome-wide changes in lncRNA, splicing, and regional gene expression patterns in autism
One-line: Post-mortem transcriptome study of the largest autism cortex cohort to date, showing that ASD molecular pathology converges across lncRNA dysregulation, downregulated alternative splicing of neuron-specific/activity-dependent exons, and attenuated frontal–temporal regional gene-expression differences.
Key takeaways

rRNA-depleted RNA-seq of 251 post-mortem samples (frontal cortex, temporal cortex, cerebellum) from 48 individuals with ASD and 49 controls; differential gene expression (DGE) in cortex replicated prior microarray data (R² = 0.60).
ASD cortex shows 584 upregulated and 558 downregulated genes (FDR < 0.05, linear mixed-effects model); downregulated set enriched in neuronal genes (e.g. PVALB, SYT2 interneuron markers), upregulated set enriched in microglia/astrocyte genes. No significant DGE in cerebellum — cortex is selectively vulnerable.
A comprehensive alternative-splicing analysis found 1,127 differential splicing events in 833 genes in cortex (none in cerebellum). Most events are exclusion of neuron-specific exons; the differential splicing signature is not driven by DGE, consistent with splicing being an independent link to disease risk (splicing-and-disease).
Differential splicing correlates with cortical expression of neuronal splicing factors and overlaps experimentally defined targets of Rbfox1, SRRM4, and ptbp1 (PTBP1); events are enriched for neuronal activity-dependent exons, supporting an excitation/inhibition-balance model of ASD.
60 lncRNAs are in the DGE set; many are brain-enriched and primate-specific (e.g. LINC00693, LINC00689, both upregulated in ASD but normally downregulated during development; several interact with miRNA-processing complexes or FMRP).
Attenuated cortical patterning (ACP): 523 genes differ between frontal and temporal cortex in controls but not in ASD; the transcription factor SOX5 (sole ACP-set member among enriched TFs) loses its correlation with predicted targets in ASD, and SOX5 overexpression in human neural progenitors induced synaptic genes and repressed proliferation.
A genetically defined ASD subtype, chromosome 15q11.2–13.1 duplication (dup15q, n = 9), shows cis upregulation of the 15q region plus genome-wide (trans) DGE and differential splicing highly concordant with idiopathic ASD (R² = 0.64–0.65) but of greater magnitude — biological validation of the shared signature.
WGCNA identified 24 modules; 6 ASD-associated (3 up / 3 down). Downregulated modules are synaptic/neuronal, upregulated modules immune/glial (microglial), and the two anticorrelate. CTX.M19/CTX.M20 trajectories diverge between ASD and controls over the first two decades of life.

Notable claims / data

Cortex DGE replication R² = 0.60 (P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶) against independent microarray samples; signal present in >2/3 of ASD samples.
1,127 differential splicing events / 833 genes in cortex; reproduced with an alternative pipeline (OLego-based) and validated by RT–PCR.
Splicing-factor perturbation overlaps: Rbfox1 knockout, SRRM4 overexpression, and PTBP1 knockdown all significantly overlap ASD differential splicing (e.g. fold enrichments ~2.0–2.4, P down to 1.7 × 10⁻⁵ for inclusion/exclusion subsets).
PC1 of protein-coding DGE, lncRNA DGE, and differential splicing are highly correlated (R² > 0.8), indicating molecular convergence is a “unitary phenomenon.”
ACP set: 551 vs 51 genes show frontal–temporal DGE in controls vs ASD respectively; SOX5 DGE set enriched in ACP set (P = 1.1 × 10⁻⁷, Fisher’s exact). Δmedian SOX5–target correlation R drops by 0.24 in ASD.
dup15q vs ASD effect-size concordance: DGE R² = 0.64 (slope 3.7), differential splicing R² = 0.65 (slope 3.2), both P < 2.2 × 10⁻¹⁶.
Module CTX.M24 enriched for ASD rare mutations and lncRNAs and fetal-cortex genes; CTX.M20 (overlapping the ACP set) enriched for ASD common-variant polygenic risk (FDR < 0.1), linking polygenic risk to regional attenuation.
Caveat: many observed changes may be compensatory/consequential rather than causal; ASD GWAS at the time was underpowered, limiting common-variant conclusions.

Connections

Direct disease sibling for splicing-and-disease — splicing alterations link to common disease risk independently of expression changes.
Brain/neurodevelopment context shared with kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated AS) and the developing-brain proteome work wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of.
Splicing-factor regulatory programs (Rbfox1, PTBP1, neuronal microexons via SRRM4) connect to baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and mis-splicing-in-disease reviews scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.
Brain-region splicing variation relates to zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain sQTL) and microglial isoform work humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia being the upregulated cell type here).
Uses percent-spliced-in (ΔPSI) metrics and rna-seq throughout.

Open questions

Which splicing/lncRNA changes are causal vs compensatory responses to a primary genetic/prenatal insult?
How would larger, better-powered ASD GWAS sharpen the link between common polygenic risk and the CTX.M20/ACP regional-attenuation module?
What are the functions of the primate-specific brain lncRNAs (LINC00693, LINC00689) and their role as candidate ASD risk loci?
Do the activity-dependent splicing changes reflect altered excitation/inhibition balance mechanistically, and can the early-developmental trajectory window (first decade) be targeted for intervention?

wiki/sources/park-2009-chip-seq-advantag.title: park-2009-chip-seq-advantages-challenges
wiki/sources/park-2009-chip-seq-advantag.content:
ChIP–seq: advantages and challenges of a maturing technology
One-line: A foundational methods review of chromatin immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing (ChIP–seq), covering experimental design and computational analysis for genome-wide profiling of DNA-binding proteins, histone modifications, and nucleosomes.
Key takeaways

ChIP–seq sequences immunoprecipitated DNA fragments directly instead of hybridizing them to a microarray (ChIP–chip), giving single-nucleotide resolution, less noise, greater coverage, and a larger dynamic range. It has become an indispensable tool for studying gene-regulation and epigenetic mechanisms.
The value of any ChIP experiment depends crucially on antibody quality; in the modENCODE Drosophila effort the author found 20–35% of commercially produced antibodies tested were unsatisfactory.
A matched control sample (input DNA, mock IP, or nonspecific IP) is essential to distinguish real peaks from artefacts of uneven shearing, repeat copy-number errors, and amplification bias; input DNA is the near-universal choice.
Peak calling exploits the strand-specific pattern: 5′-end reads form paired positive- and negative-strand distributions flanking the binding site, and profiles are built by shifting or fragment-extending the tags.

Notable claims / data

Sonication shears chromatin to 200–600 bp; sequencing libraries are size-selected to ~150–300 bp. Only ~48% of the human genome is non-repetitive, yet 80% is mappable with 30 bp reads and 89% with 70 bp reads.
Genome Analyzer / SOLiD runs generated 100–400 million reads, with ~60–80% uniquely alignable; a single Illumina lane went from 4–6 million to 8–15 million reads as the platform improved.
ChIP–seq needs far less material than ChIP–chip: ~10–50 ng DNA versus >2 μg, with fewer amplification rounds and thus lower PCR-bias artefact potential; a typical ChIP uses ~10^7 cells.
Whether a “saturation point” of discovered binding sites exists depends on scoring: with statistical significance alone new peaks keep appearing (e.g. STAT1 targets rise steadily), but a fixed fold-enrichment threshold does produce saturation. The author proposes a minimal saturated enrichment ratio (MSER) as a depth-of-sequencing metric, linearly related to read count.
Enriched regions come in three shapes — sharp (transcription factors, regulatory marks), broad (domain-marking histone modifications), and mixed (e.g. RNA polymerase II) — and most early peak callers were built for sharp peaks. A Poisson model on tag counts captures both fold ratio and absolute counts; significance is reported via false discovery rate / q value.
mNase digestion maps nucleosomes more precisely than sonication but has stronger sequence bias; native (N-ChIP) versus crosslinked (X-ChIP) protocols trade off artefacts.

Connections

Complements array-based chromatin-accessibility and DNase I hypersensitivity mapping approaches for locating regulatory DNA and cis-regulatory-elements.
Motif discovery from top peaks (MEME, MDscan, Weeder, W-ChIPMotifs) and integration with RNA–seq expression data are framed as the route to reconstructing gene regulatory networks, tying ChIP–seq output back to gene-regulation.
Positions ENCODE, modENCODE, and the NIH Roadmap Epigenomics Program as the first large-scale epigenome-profiling efforts built on this technology.

Open questions

No community consensus on which control type is most appropriate, nor on which data levels (image, tag, alignment) to archive given terabyte-scale per-run output.
How to build a peak caller that automatically handles sharp, broad, and mixed enrichment without prior knowledge of the signal type.
FDR estimates from read-randomization null models can be off by an order of magnitude because neighbouring tags are not independent — what null distribution is realistic?
Reducing input cell numbers toward single-cell characterization, and lowering cost/access so ChIP–seq fully supplants ChIP–chip.

wiki/sources/park-2018-the-expanding-lan.title: park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative
wiki/sources/park-2018-the-expanding-lan.content:
The Expanding Landscape of Alternative Splicing Variation in Human Populations
One-line: A review (from the Xing lab) of how population-scale RNA-seq, sQTL/ASAS analysis, massively parallel reporter assays, and machine/deep learning have revealed widespread naturally occurring genetic variation in alternative splicing across human populations, and its link to GWAS traits and disease.
Key takeaways

Genetic variation in splicing-regulatory-elements is pervasive in human populations; many sQTL SNPs are enriched at the 5’ and 3’ splice-sites but a large fraction lie outside them, modifying exonic/intronic enhancers/silencers or rna-binding-proteins binding sites.
Splicing quantitative trait locus (sQTL) analysis treats percent-spliced-in (PSI) values as quantitative traits tested against genotypes; allele-specific alternative splicing (ASAS) is a complementary cis-only approach using heterozygous SNPs to compare the two haplotypes within one individual.
sQTLs frequently colocalize with GWAS signals, arguing that alternative-splicing is a primary causal mechanism (not a secondary effect) linking genetic variation to complex traits and disease — comparable to or greater than eQTL effects (Li et al. 2016).
Massively parallel reporter assays (massively-parallel-reporter-assay) plus minigene reporters allow rapid screening of which variants causally alter splicing; deep learning models the “splicing code” to predict PSI and variant effects.
RNA-seq-based splicing analysis is emerging as a diagnostic and prognostic tool (rare-disease diagnosis, cancer survival prediction).

Notable claims / data

cis splicing mutations are estimated to constitute 15%–60% of human disease mutations.
Population sQTL studies (Table 1): Montgomery et al. (LCLs, n=60, 110 sQTL events); Pickrell et al. (LCLs, n=69, 187 sQTL genes at 10% FDR); Lappalainen/Geuvadis (LCLs, n=462, 639 trQTL genes); Battle et al. (whole blood, n=922, 1,370 sQTL genes at 5% FDR, 159 in high LD with GWAS SNPs); Fadista et al. (pancreatic islets, n=89, 371 sQTLs in T2D loci); Li et al. family study (LCLs, n=17, 261 sQTLs at 50% FDR, enriched for rare variants); GTEx pilot (43 tissues, 1,641 samples/175 individuals, ~1,900 sQTL genes/tissue with Altrans and ~250 with sQTLseekeR; tissue-specific sQTLs only 7%–21%); Chen et al. (immune cells, n=197, >2,000 sQTL genes/cell type); Pala et al. (Sardinia leukocytes, n=624, 6,768 sQTLs); Takata et al. (brain prefrontal cortex, n=206, 1,595 sQTLs enriched for schizophrenia loci).
Worked sQTL/GWAS examples: SP140 exon 7 (silent exonic SNP rs28445040; T allele lowers inclusion; in LD with GWAS SNPs for CLL, MS, Crohn, IBD) and ERAP2 exon 10 (intronic 5’SS SNP rs2248374 activating a cryptic 5’SS → PTC → NMD; linked to Crohn/IBD). Both causal variants are silent-exonic or intronic and missed by common variant-annotation tools.
MaPSy (Soemedi et al.) tested 4,964 exonic disease mutations: ~10% perturbed splicing in both in vivo and in vitro assays vs ~3% of common SNPs (likely a lower-bound, single cell type HEK293). Rosenberg et al. tested >2 million synthetic minigenes for alt 5’/3’ splice site selection.
Deep learning splicing-code models (Frey/Xiong et al.) achieved R²=0.65 between predicted and empirical PSI on independent data and predicted misregulated splicing in 19 ASD candidate genes.
Rare-disease diagnosis proof-of-concept (Cummings et al.): muscle RNA-seq of 63 patients vs 184 GTEx controls yielded genetic diagnoses for 35% of previously undiagnosed individuals (e.g., recurrent COL6A1 deep-intronic GC→GT creating a novel 5’SS).
Notes the FDA approval of nusinersen (antisense oligonucleotide) correcting splicing in spinal muscular atrophy.

Connections

Methodologically anchors event-based tools cited elsewhere in the wiki: miso, rmats, MAJIQ, suppa, and PSI (percent-spliced-in); transcript-level quantitation via kallisto/bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification.
Complements deep-learning splice-prediction work centered on spliceai and the splicing-code; this review predates SpliceAI but reviews its lineage (Barash splicing code, AVISPA, Leung/Xiong deep models, BRIE).
Extends splicing-and-disease and splicing-and-evolution into the population-genetics regime; relates to cis-regulatory-elements and ai-in-genomics.
Same Xing lab as shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (rMATS); ships only the helper rmats2sashimiplot as a Web Resource, not a new analysis tool.

Open questions

How many GWAS signals across all complex traits are ultimately splicing-causal, once full ASAS + long-read coverage and additional tissues are layered in?
Can predictive models incorporate chromatin state, epigenetic marks, and 3D genome organization to capture co-transcriptional splicing regulation?
How well do MPRA/HEK293 reporter results generalize to the cell-type-specific splicing of disease-relevant tissues?
What is the contribution of non-SNP variants (indels, short tandem repeats) to population-scale splicing variation?

wiki/sources/park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-.title: park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing
wiki/sources/park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-.content:
In vivo neuronal gene editing via CRISPR–Cas9 amphiphilic nanocomplexes alleviates deficits in mouse models of Alzheimer’s disease
One-line: A non-viral, amphiphilic peptide (R7L10)–Cas9 ribonucleoprotein nanocomplex edits Bace1 in post-mitotic neurons of the adult mouse brain, lowering amyloid-beta pathology and rescuing cognitive deficits in two Alzheimer’s disease mouse models with minimal off-target effects.
Key takeaways

Demonstrates efficient genome-editing in post-mitotic adult neurons in vivo — a setting where HDR-based correction fails and only NHEJ-mediated indel gene disruption is practical — using a delivered Cas9 protein–sgRNA RNP rather than a virus.
Delivery vehicle is the amphiphilic peptide R7L10 (a poly-arginine/poly-leucine peptide) that self-assembles with Cas9-sgRNA into ~125 nm spherical nanocomplexes (cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex); this avoids viral integration and the persistence of Cas9 expression.
Target gene is Bace1 (beta-secretase 1), required to generate amyloid-beta (Aβ); knocking it down in neurons is a therapeutic strategy for Alzheimer’s disease.
Therapeutic efficacy shown in two models: the 5xfad-mouse-model (five familial AD mutations) and an App knock-in model (humanized App with Swedish/Iberian/Arctic mutations) — Aβ plaque and Aβ42 reduced, cognitive/memory deficits alleviated.
Comprehensive off-target profiling (WGS, WES, digenome-seq, Cas-OFFinder) found no genome-wide elevation of mutation rates from Cas9 nanocomplex activity.

Notable claims / data

Nanocomplex physical characterization: ~125 nm uniform spheres (DLS/AFM/SEM); STEM-EDS confirmed N (peptide) + P (sgRNA) colocalization; positive ζ-potential and stable size across R7L10:Cas9-sgRNA weight ratios.
Cas9 protein persisted ~1 week post-injection and declined to undetectable by 3 weeks in brain (transient exposure, unlike viral expression).
In vitro editing: on-target indels at Bace1 and Th with very low off-target; also achieved HDR-mediated 2A-eGFP-IRES-Puro knock-in at the TH locus in human NSC-derived neurons.
In vivo editing efficiency (Surveyor / deep sequencing): ~26% indel at Bace1 and ~31% at Th in WT hippocampus/midbrain; ~45% editing rate at Bace1 target region by deep sequencing in nanocomplex-treated brains.
In 6-month-old 5XFAD hippocampus: ~70% decrease in Bace1 expression in CA3; significantly decreased APP β-cleavage products (βCTF, C99); reduced Aβ plaque and Aβ42 secretion.
Off-target (Table 1, n=3): control vs Cas9-Bace1 brains had similar SNV/indel counts; e.g. WGS 686 vs 775 unique SNVs and 347 vs 408 indels; none of these fell in top-100 predicted off-target sites; no large-scale rearrangements or CNVs; Digenome-seq showed uniform genome-wide cleavage patterns with clean on-target and no off-target double-peaks validated.
Behavior: Cas9-Bace1-treated 5XFAD mice showed increased contextual fear-conditioning associative learning, rescued Y-maze alternation, shorter Morris water maze escape latency, and more time in target quadrant vs 5XFAD controls.
Multiple deliveries into hippocampal CA3 more efficiently suppressed Bace1 and disease phenotypes than a single injection, suggesting multi-targeting for translation.
No increase in inflammation (Iba1, Tnf-α, Il-12A, Itgam, Cd86, Cd68), toxicity (blood urea nitrogen), or apoptosis (cleaved caspase-3) in nanocomplex-treated brains.
Aβ suppression by Cas9-Bace1 was more efficient than by chemical BACE1 inhibitors.

Connections

Extends genome-editing into non-dividing neural tissue via non-viral RNP delivery — complements virus-mediated CRISPR approaches by removing integration/long-term-expression risks.
Relevant to neurodegeneration and Alzheimer’s therapy; contrasts with pharmacological BACE1 inhibition.
The R7L10 amphiphilic peptide delivery (cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex) is a reusable method for CNS RNP delivery beyond this paper.
Off-target validation stack (digenome-seq + WGS/WES + Cas-OFFinder) is a template for rigorous in vivo editing safety assessment.

Open questions

Gene editing is irreversible — authors caution about rare/very rare off-target or long-term effects; longer-term and non-human primate safety studies are needed.
Delivery cannot target widespread neuronal dysfunction, so the approach is proposed as best suited to focal neurodegenerative disease (e.g., Parkinson’s), not late-stage widespread AD.
Cell-type-specific delivery systems are needed to restrict editing to desired brain cell types.
Optimal timing, dosing, and number of deliveries for durable phenotype rescue remain to be defined.

wiki/sources/patowary-2024-developmental.title: patowary-2024-developmental-isoform-diversity-in-the
wiki/sources/patowary-2024-developmental.content:
Developmental isoform diversity in the human neocortex informs neuropsychiatric risk mechanisms
One-line: A PsychENCODE2 long-read-rna-seq (PacBio Iso-Seq) full-length isoform atlas of the developing human neocortex (mid-gestation, progenitor vs neuron zones, tissue + single-cell), cataloguing 214,516 isoforms (72.6% novel) and using this isoform-resolved annotation to reprioritize rare de novo neurodevelopmental-disorder variants — the Gandal/de la Torre-Ubieta developmental counterpart to bulk short-read brain studies. (sciso-portal)
Key takeaways

Long-read full-length transcriptome of microdissected germinal zone (GZ, progenitor-enriched) and cortical plate (CP, neuron-enriched) from PCW15–17 human neocortex (6 donors bulk + 3 donors single-cell, >7,000 cells via ScISOr-Seq) — the first systematic isoform-diversity map of developing human cortex.
214,516 isoforms, 72.6% novel vs Gencode v33; >7,000 novel spliced exons; the transcribed genome extended by ~27 Mb; 92,422 isoforms with protein-coding potential, 35,467 with mass-spec peptide support (new proteoforms).
Isoform switching drives cell fate. GZ→CP differential transcript usage (DTU) in 2,679/10,809 multi-isoform genes; 1,669 genes switch isoform with no change in overall gene expression — a regulatory layer invisible to gene-level analysis (alternative-splicing, cf. percent-spliced-in). Switches alter ORF length, NMD sensitivity, and 3′UTR length (DPUI lengthening in neurons).
RBP-driven networks: isoform-usage networks are organized by rna-binding-proteins rather than cell type — PTBP1, SRRM4, RBFOX1/2/3, ELAVL1/2, CELF2 — and implicate understudied RBPs (SAFB, UTP18, SRSF9) in corticogenesis (splicing-factors).
Disease mechanism: isoform number/complexity per gene strongly predicts NDD-risk-gene status; dynamic isoform expression/usage is the feature most enriched across neuropsychiatric disorders; the isoform-resolved annotation reprioritizes thousands of rare de novo ASD/ID/NDD variants to more-severe consequences and reveals more cryptic splice variants than previously reported (splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

Technology: PacBio HiFi Iso-Seq (>33M CCS reads), >99% full-length alignment vs ~85% for short-read; median read 2.99 kb. Single-cell via ScISOr-Seq (Drop-seq + UMIs), barcodes matched to prior short-read scRNA-seq.
Validation: ~80% of novel TSSs supported by CAGE/ATAC; ~91% of novel 3′ ends by polyA sites/motifs; 74% of novel junctions in Intropolis; RT-PCR validation of novel exons.
NDD-risk genes have significantly more isoforms (OR 1.56–1.8, controlling for length/expression). 319 multiexonic genes match no existing model (256 antisense, 63 intergenic), e.g. an 18-exon antisense gene to ASD-risk DPYSL2.
Single cell: early-stage excitatory neurons show greatest isoform diversity; isoform-centric clustering reveals previously uncharacterized cell states across 16 cell types.
Resource portal: sciso.gandallab.org/ (sciso-portal).

Connections

The developing-brain, long-read complement to the wiki’s short-read brain work: it directly addresses the cell-type-confounding and isoform-resolution open questions that bulk studies (walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis) leave open — here splicing is resolved to full-length isoforms and to GZ/CP + single cells.
Same lab lineage as isoform-level disease mapping (bhattacharya-2023-isoform-level-transcriptome-wide-association-uncovers-genetic, isoTWAS) and the ASD cortical transcriptome (gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across); shares the long-read-isoform-atlas goal with heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform (aged cortex) but maps development.
RBP-network thread connects to encode-rbp-map and splicing-factors; variant-reannotation connects to splice-variant prediction (spliceai, absplice2).

Open questions

Does isoform diversity at later developmental stages and across cortical regions differ from this mid-gestation GZ/CP snapshot?
How many of the 150k+ novel isoforms are functional vs low-abundance noise — and which novel proteoforms are translated in vivo?
Can isoform-resolved variant reannotation raise the diagnostic yield for unsolved NDD cases (cryptic splice variants), and does it generalize beyond ASD/ID gene sets?
Would matched genotypes/WGS turn this isoform atlas into developmental isoform-QTL / sQTL maps?

wiki/sources/pawitan-2004-estimation-of-.title: pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and
wiki/sources/pawitan-2004-estimation-of-.content:
Estimation of genetic and environmental factors for binary traits using family data
One-line: A general GLMM/liability-threshold method, fitted by likelihood over arbitrary extended-family structures, for partitioning a binary trait into maternal, foetal, and environmental variance components — demonstrated on Swedish birth-registry pre-eclampsia data.
Key takeaways

Treats a binary disease phenotype as a thresholded unobserved normal “liability”, fitting it directly as a probit-link generalized-linear-mixed-model — a liability-threshold-model embedded in a variance-components-model. This handles arbitrary family structures (not just twins/relative-pairs) and incomplete outcome data.
Motivated by the limits of classic twin-and-family-study-design: twinning is rare so twin studies are underpowered for common conditions (PE prevalence ~4%), and nuclear families alone cannot separate maternal vs foetal genetic effects or genetic vs shared-environment effects. Extended family data (e.g. families of sib-pairs) breaks these confoundings.
Inference is by direct (exact) likelihood: the random effects are integrated out, reducing each family’s contribution to an orthant probability of a multivariate normal, evaluated via a fast Cholesky-based Monte Carlo algorithm (Genz). Parameters estimated by a derivative-free simplex; the authors note ongoing work on a hierarchical-likelihood approach to avoid the integration.
heritability is defined on the liability scale as h² = σ²_g/(σ²_g + σ²_e + 1), consistent with standard biometrical-genetics quantitative-genetics; with the probit link it is the proportion of liability variance due to additive genetic effects.
Families of sib-pairs (mothers who are full sisters, fathers unrelated, etc.) let the model separate maternal genetic (m), foetal genetic (f), common-family-environment (e), and common-sibling-environment (s) components, and can even split the foetal effect into maternal- vs paternal-transmitted contributions (f_m, f_p).

Notable claims / data

Twin study (Ros et al., ref [6]) of PE: 917 MZ + 1199 DZ Swedish female twin pairs, only 8 concordant MZ / 2 concordant DZ affected pairs → heritability estimate 0.57 but with 95% CI (0.0–0.71) — illustrating the power problem.
Familial clustering far exceeds chance: among women with 2 pregnancies, 570 had PE in both vs 68 expected under randomness; among 3-pregnancy women, 21 PE-in-all-three vs <1 expected. Clustering by fathers is similar (536 vs 63), showing nuclear data cannot separate maternal from paternal genetic contribution.
Application data: all Swedish pregnancies 1987–1997, first 3 known same-partner pregnancies; 239,193 family-pairs, 774,858 pregnancies, 20,358 pre-eclamptic. Mother–mother, father–father, and mother–father sib-pair groupings used.
PE risk is much higher in first (13,072/320,512 = 4.1%) than subsequent (7,286/454,346 = 1.6%) pregnancies; modelled via separate prevalence parameters φ₁, φ₂ orthogonal to the variance components.
Model 2 estimates (liability variance components): maternal σ²_m = 1.08 (0.99–1.18), foetal σ²_f = 0.63 (0.32–0.81), family-env σ²_e = 0.40 (0.33–0.51), sibling σ²_s = 0.02 (0.0–0.03). Maternal heritability h²_m = 1.08/(1.08+0.63+0.40+0.02+1) = 35%; foetal 20%, family-env 13%, sibling 1%.
Model 3 (split foetal effect) shows no evidence of unequal maternal vs paternal foetal contribution (Δ −2logL = 2.5, 1 df). Model 4 (later-pregnancy genetic/family interaction terms) is not a significant improvement (Δ = 0.1).
Conclusion: PE has both maternal and foetal genetic origin (part of the foetal effect from paternal genes), plus a real common-family environment effect but no detectable sibling-environment effect; goodness-of-fit (χ² = 656.3, 434 df for patterns with expected freq > 5) shows Model 2 explains most but not all variation.

Connections

Extends the same GLMM/liability logic used in rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and (which fits twin/family biometrical models with gllamm) from twins/relative-pairs to arbitrary extended pedigrees, and replaces approximate quadrature with exact orthant-probability likelihood.
Shares the variance-components-model foundation that mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models frames as the path-analysis ↔ VCM equivalence; here the same additive-genetic correlations (MZ=1, DZ/full-sibs=0.5, cousins=0.125) are encoded directly in random-effect correlation (R) matrices rather than path diagrams.
The probit/liability-threshold framing makes binary biometrical-genetics a special case of the generalized-linear-mixed-model, and the heritability definition aligns with classical quantitative-genetics (Falconer; Sham 1998).

Open questions

Can the hierarchical-likelihood (h-likelihood) formulation, flagged as ongoing work, deliver the same estimates without the Monte Carlo integration and known to fail for small clusters under PQL/APHL?
How to incorporate continuous fixed covariates without the data-grouping step that the current efficient likelihood computation requires?
Non-additive genetic effects (dominance, gene–gene, maternal×paternal interaction) are confounded with the family-environment effect here — what design or data resolves them?
Given that the estimated genetic components still leave PE largely unexplained, how do specific molecular PE polymorphisms map onto these liability-scale variance partitions?

wiki/sources/perochon-2023-early-detecti.title: perochon-2023-early-detection-of-autism
wiki/sources/perochon-2023-early-detecti.content:
Early detection of autism using digital behavioral phenotyping
One-line: A multiclinic prospective study showing a tablet app (sensetoknow) that quantifies autism-related behaviors via computer vision achieves high, demographically fair diagnostic accuracy for toddler autism screening in primary care.
Key takeaways

Combines multiple digital behavioral phenotypes (gaze, facial dynamics, head movements, response to name, blink rate, motor/touch) rather than a single signal, better capturing the heterogeneous presentation of autism.
Deployed the sensetoknow app during routine pediatric well-child visits, targeting the toddler age when screening is normally done.
Uses XGBoost over 23 phenotypes with SHAP-based model interpretability, giving individualized, auditable explanations for each prediction.
Sensitivity was consistent across sex, race and ethnicity, addressing known disparities of questionnaire-based screening.

Notable claims / data

Sample: 475 toddlers (17–36 months; 269 boys, 206 girls); 49 diagnosed autistic, 98 developmental/language delay (DD–LD), 328 neurotypical.
Autism vs neurotypical: AUC = 0.90 (CI 0.87–0.93), sensitivity 87.8%, specificity 80.8%, NPV 97.8%, PPV 40.6%; rises to AUC 0.93 on high-confidence administrations.
Autism vs nonautism (DD–LD + neurotypical): AUC = 0.86, sensitivity 81.6%, specificity 80.5%.
Combining app + M-CHAT-R/F: AUC = 0.97, sensitivity 92.1%, specificity 91.8%.
Nine autistic children who screened M-CHAT-R/F-negative were correctly flagged by the app.
Top SHAP predictors: facing forward during social movies (|SHAP| 11.2%), percent gaze at social stimuli (11.1%), response-to-name delay (7.1%); feature interactions contributed 35.4% of prediction.
Context: prior M-CHAT/F primary-care study (>25,000 children) had sensitivity 39.0%, PPV 14.6%; eye-tracking-alone screening had specificity 98.0% but sensitivity 17.0%.

Connections

Applies model-interpretability (SHAP) and multimodal-learning to a clinical autism screening problem.
Complements molecular/genetic autism work such as schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism with a behavioral-phenotyping detection angle.
Relates to early-brain-development framing in courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development.

Open questions

PPV remains modest (40.6%) at population prevalence — how to triage app-positive children without over-referral?
Generalizability beyond a single US health system, and to home/non-clinic administration.
Whether digital phenotypes track intervention response or predict subtypes, not just presence of autism.

wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.title: peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes
wiki/sources/peynet-2026-emerging-role-o.content:
Emerging role of G-quadruplexes in alternative splicing regulation
One-line: A mechanistic review arguing that RNA G-quadruplexes (rG4s) are an emerging class of secondary-structure regulators of alternative-splicing, acting position-dependently near splice-sites to recruit or block splicing factors, with strong links to cancer and neurodegeneration and a therapeutic angle via G4 ligands.
Key takeaways

g-quadruplex structures are noncanonical four-stranded folds in guanine-rich nucleic acid: guanines pair via Hoogsteen (not Watson–Crick) bonds into stacked G-quartets, stabilised by K+ cations. They form in both DNA (dG4s) and RNA (rG4s) and act as rna-structure-and-splicing elements distinct from sequence motifs.
rG4s regulate alternative-splicing in a position-dependent way: located near donor (5’ ss) or acceptor (3’ ss) sites, they either recruit rG4-binding splicing-factors that redirect the spliceosome to proximal sites, or remodel pre-mRNA to mask/expose splice sites — functioning as enhancers or silencers (splicing-regulatory-elements).
This is a structure-based complement to the splicing-code: rG4s overlay sequence-defined splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements, and their effect depends on dynamic folding equilibria rather than a fixed consensus.
Many examples span all five major ASE types (exon skipping, intron retention, alternative 5’/3’ ss, mutually exclusive exons); rna-binding-proteins including the hnRNP family, FMRP, AFF family, SRSF1, and RBM25 read these rG4s.
dG4s also influence splicing indirectly via cotranscriptional-splicing: by slowing RNA Pol II elongation (and via R-loops on the non-template strand), they alter exon inclusion (Box 3), separate from the direct rG4 effects.
rG4-controlled splicing is heavily implicated in splicing-and-disease, especially cancer (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x, BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) and neurodegeneration (BACE1/Alzheimer, FMR1/Fragile X).
