G4 ligands (e.g. PhenDC3, pyridostatin, GQC-05) are model splicing-therapeutics that stabilise/unfold rG4s or block G4-binding proteins; effects across ASEs are often contradictory, so no general rule yet exists.

Notable claims / data

Scale: ~100,000 proteins from ~20,000 human genes via AS. ~60% of human transcripts contain ≥1 putative quadruplex-forming sequence (PQS); ~1,200,000 PQSs (300,000 unique motifs) across transcripts and splice junctions; canonical folding rule d(GGG+N1–7)x4 predicts ~400,000 dG4s in the genome.
A threefold enrichment of G4s near splice junctions, preferentially on the non-template (coding) strand (implying the corresponding rG4 matters); G4-at-ss enrichment is conserved in vertebrates but not invertebrates (possibly because AS matters less there).
FMR1 (Fragile X): two rG4s in exon 15 inhibit inclusion; FMRP binds them via its RGG motif and modulates its own splicing, favouring minor ISO2/ISO3 isoforms (autoregulatory feedback). ~15 loop mutations (ΔG4) altered exon-15 splicing without changing translation.
AFF family (AFF1–4; AFF2/FMR2 → FRAXE intellectual disability) bind rG4s via a conserved C-terminal domain (not RGG), localise to nuclear speckles, and broadly modulate AS.
CD44: intron-8 rG4 (element I-8) promotes the epithelial CD44v isoform; hnRNPF regulates it (depletion accelerates EMT). Alkaloids emetine and cephaeline modulate inclusion by unfolding rG4s (~60% of emetine-affected ASEs are skipped exons).
EWS-FLI (Ewing sarcoma): exon-8 rG4 promotes exon exclusion (keeping the oncoprotein); hnRNPH1 destabilises the rG4. Pyridostatin (PDS) blocks hnRNPH1 binding and reduces viability of EWSR1-fusion cells.
BCL-2: exon-3 rG4 favours skipping → proapoptotic BCL-2β; interplay with three nearby m6A sites; SRSF1 (an rG4-binding factor) resolves the rG4. Bcl-2β sensitises breast cancer cells to chemotherapy.
hTERT: intron-6 rG4s act as intronic splicing silencers; ligands Bisquinolinium 12459, CX-5461, and PhenDC3 shift splicing toward the inactive β transcript (telomestatin/BRACO19 had no effect — ligand selectivity).
TP53: intron-3 rG4 promotes intron-2 retention → dominant-negative ΔNp53/Δ40p53; ligand 360A shifts toward full-length p53. A common intronic polymorphism disrupting this rG4 is linked to increased cancer risk.
BCL-x (“rG4 saga”, Figure 1): exon-2 GQ-2/Q2 rG4 (overlaps the Gt16 G-tract) downstream of the alternative 5’ ss controls Bcl-xL (antiapoptotic) vs Bcl-xS (proapoptotic) balance. Splicing factor RBM25 binds Q2 directly via its arginine–glutamate (RE) domain — a novel G4-binding domain — and this binding is required for Bcl-xS production. Ligands GQC-05, PhenDC3, and the novel PhenDH8/PhenDH9 promote Bcl-xS; a genome screen for the Gt16-like G-tract found 3374 exons in 2707 genes.
Other cases: PAX9 intron-1 rG4 (positively regulates splicing; conserved in placental mammals; affects dentition — splicing-and-evolution); IGFN1 intron-15 (renal/breast cancer, PDS); CD133 introns 3/7 (cancer stem cells, RHPS4); BACE1 exon-3 alternative 5’ ss (hnRNPH/hnRNPL; Alzheimer); TRα/THRA alternative 3’ ss (G30 element, only suggestive evidence); vertebrate mirtron 5’-arm rG4s aiding mirtron biogenesis.
Mechanism (Box 6, splicing-and-chromatin/condensate angle): rG4–protein interactions may nucleate liquid–liquid phase separation (LLPS) at nuclear speckles, concentrating splicing factors — proposed but not yet demonstrated for the validated rG4 cases.

Connections

Structure vs sequence: extends splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements from a sequence-motif view to a secondary-structure view — rG4s are folded ESE/ISE/ESS/ISS-like elements whose activity is position-dependent and dynamic, complementing the splicing-code.
Sits in the same RNA-structure thread as Lee 2015 (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative), which discusses RNA secondary structure masking splice sites; this review supplies the G4-specific molecular detail and the new [[rna-structure-and-splicing]] entity.
splicing-and-disease: a curated cancer-centric catalogue (EWS-FLI, hTERT, TP53, BCL-x/BCL-2, CD133, IGFN1) plus neurodegeneration (BACE1, FMR1).
splicing-therapeutics: positions G4 ligands as splicing modulators; relates to BH3-mimetic (venetoclax) and SF3B1-modulator strategies, and to G4-inducing ASOs (G-ASOs) for exon skipping in Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
cotranscriptional-splicing: dG4/R-loop effects on Pol II elongation (Box 3) tie G4 biology to the transcription–splicing coupling thread.

Open questions

The molecular mechanisms of rG4-mediated AS, and the role of phase separation, remain largely unproven (mostly hypothesised models).
Which methods can confirm that an in-silico PQS actually forms a functional rG4 in cellulo and controls a specific gene’s AS (single-RNA-molecule monitoring is lacking).
G4 ligand selectivity is a major limitation: hundreds of thousands of competing dG4s/rG4s exist, effects across ASEs are often antinomic, and no general predictive “G4–ligand code” yet exists.
Is rG4-mediated splicing a general feature of apoptosis-regulating genes with opposing isoforms (e.g. BCL-x, MCL-1)?
Systematic identification of the full set of rG4-binding RBPs that reshape the AS landscape, and the influence of surrounding chemical RNA modifications (e.g. m6A), are still needed.

wiki/sources/pigini-2026-poison-exon-spl.title: pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in
wiki/sources/pigini-2026-poison-exon-spl.content:
Poison exon splicing in the human brain: a new paradigm for understanding and targeting neurological disorders
One-line: A genome-wide catalog of human poison exons (PEs) — alternative exons whose inclusion creates premature stop codons and triggers NMD — mapping their brain-specific and developmental splicing and 1,625 pathogenic variants that disrupt them, with CRISPR prime-editing validation in iPSC-derived neurons.
Key takeaways

Poison exons are conserved exons that, when included, introduce a premature termination codon (PTC) and trigger nonsense-mediated decay (NMD), down-tuning protein output. They are a specialized case of alternative-splicing coupled to NMD (the “AS-NMD” / poison-exon mechanism) and a recurring theme in splicing-and-disease.
A comparative analysis of 957 eukaryotic transcriptomes (Ensembl) shows Homo sapiens has the highest enrichment of NMD-targeted isoforms; 39.6% of human protein-coding genes give rise to >=1 predicted NMD isoform. The authors release the prediction pipeline as the tool evonmd.
Systematic in-silico annotation against Gencode v26 identified 9,814 putative human PEs (17.8% of 55,150 annotated AS events); 59.2% arise from cassette exons, the rest from alternative 3’/5’ splice-site usage.
Using rna-seq from GTEx and BrainSpan, they found 83 PEs uniquely detected in brain, 973 PEs differentially spliced in brain vs other tissues, and 1,284 PEs differentially spliced across brain development (before vs after birth). Brain-expressed genes are depleted (not enriched) for PEs, suggesting tight regulatory pressure.
Integrating ClinVar pathogenic variants with spliceai predictions flagged 1,625 pathogenic variants predicted to affect splicing of 743 PEs, enriched for seizures/epilepsy, intellectual disability, neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative disorders.
CRISPR prime editing in HEK293T and iPSC-derived i3N neurons functionally validated a subset; the deep-intronic NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T (Leigh syndrome) variant increased PE inclusion, reduced NDUFAF6 protein, and impaired mitochondrial membrane potential.

Notable claims / data

Evolutionary scan: average 2.67% of coding genes per genome produce >=1 NMD isoform; vertebrates (3.7%) and plants (3.7%) highest, fungi (0.6%) and protists (0.1%) lowest. In humans, NMD isoforms = 20.1% of all protein-coding isoforms (14.3% under stringent TSL 1-3 filtering); 39.6% of genes have >=1 NMD isoform (30.3% under TSL 1-3). Robust to BUSCO completeness >=90% filtering — not an annotation-depth artifact.
PE definition (stringent, to minimize NMD escape): exon <=300 bp; not overlapping 5’/3’ UTR; on inclusion generates a PTC >150 bp downstream of TSS, >50 nt upstream of the last exon-exon junction, and outside the terminal exon.
CHX (cycloheximide) NMD-stabilization assay confirmed 87.5% (14/16) of putative PEs trigger NMD in HEK293T cells; same 87.5% rate confirmed in CHX-treated neurons. Non-responders (SMPD4, WDR45) reflect transcript-context dependence — downstream exon inclusion restores frame.
Disease ontology enrichment (UniProt KW): PE-containing genes enriched in epilepsy (137 genes), intellectual disability (313), neurodegeneration (181). KEGG: metabolic pathways (572 genes, FDR=0.00069), spliceosome (61), mRNA surveillance (43) — consistent with splicing factors autoregulating via PE inclusion (SRS/SF3 factors).
GTEx: 17,382 libraries, 54 tissues, 13 brain regions, 838 donors. BrainSpan: 607 libraries, 41 individuals, 16 brain regions, 10 pcw to 40 yr. PE inclusion correlated inversely with host-gene expression. Splicing factors RBFOX1, NOVA1, UPF3B enriched for brain-specific PEs (p=4e-6, hypergeometric). NOVA1 PE inclusion high in iPSCs, declines during neuronal maturation (validated by RT-PCR + CHX).
Variant pipeline: SpliceAI score cutoff |0.2| (positive = increased PE inclusion); 45,691 intronic vs 22,991 exonic candidate variants. Pangolin cross-validation showed strong concordance; SpliceAI higher sensitivity. ODiseA ontology mapping: 596 variants linked to seizures, 262 to epileptic syndromes.
Prime editing: 13 pegRNAs across 12 genes; 10/13 >5% efficiency, 9 >10% (avg 22.9%). Five representative validated variants — TAFAZZIN/TAZ c.284+110G>A (SpliceAI 0.57, +0.12 PSI), NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T (0.23, +0.13 PSI), SLC12A6 c.2633-1G>A (0.99, +0.04 PSI), TSC2 c.4850-1G>A (0.99, +0.06 PSI), COQ9 c.521+1del (0.67, -0.28 PSI).
NDUFAF6 deep dive: c.420+784C>T retains 124 intronic nt, creating a PTC in exon 4. Clonal i3N neurons: WT PSI 0.13, heterozygous (C/T) 0.37, homozygous (T/T) 0.94. Homozygotes showed reduced NDUFAF6 protein (Western), impaired mitochondrial membrane potential (TMRM), and smaller/fragmented cell clusters; heterozygotes intermediate. Authors state this is the first functional characterization of NDUFAF6 deficiency from PE mis-splicing in a human neuronal model.

Connections

Direct sibling to disease/mechanism sources: scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (NMD-inducing cryptic exons repressed by TDP-43; this paper extends to a genome-wide PE catalog) and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (AS as a regulator of gene expression).
Developmental-splicing angle complements kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated AS) and the brain-proteome work wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of.
Brain transcriptomic siblings: humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia isoforms), zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (splicing-qtl in brain).
Methodologically anchored by spliceai (variant-effect prediction; Pangolin used as cross-check) and introduces the new tool evonmd.
Therapeutic framing (NMD-sensitive PEs as targetable defects via RNA-based correction, e.g. ASOs) ties to splicing-therapeutics; the SCN1A “20N” PE / Dravet syndrome and SMN2 precedent sit in this lineage (smn2).

Open questions

How many of the 9,814 putative PEs are bona fide regulatory switches vs annotation artifacts of low-abundance isoforms (the authors caution annotation depth inflates human counts)?
Short-read PSI measures only overall exon inclusion, not transcript-context PE usage — long-read RNA-seq could resolve which isoforms actually carry the PTC.
Why does the heterozygous NDUFAF6 line show intermediate PE inclusion and MMP deficit but no significant protein reduction? What sets the dosage threshold for phenotype?
Can PE-targeting ASOs that block aberrant inclusion correct deep-intronic variants like NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T in patient-derived neurons?

wiki/sources/piven-2017-conceptual-frame.title: piven-2017-conceptual-framework-early-brain-behavior
wiki/sources/piven-2017-conceptual-frame.content:
Toward a conceptual framework for early brain and behavior development in autism
One-line: An expert review integrating prospective infant-sibling brain-imaging and behavioral data into a post-natal developmental cascade model of autism, where early progenitor over-proliferation and cortical surface-area hyper-expansion precede brain overgrowth and emergent social deficits.
Key takeaways

Defining features of autism are not present at 6 months but emerge in the latter first and second years, consolidating between 18–36 months, so infancy is a pre-symptomatic window preceding diagnosis.
Proposes a temporal cascade: increased proliferation of neural progenitor cells → non-uniform hyper-expansion of cortical surface area (year 1) → altered experience-dependent neurodevelopment → brain-volume overgrowth (year 2) → disrupted refinement of neural circuits → emergence of autistic social deficits.
Frames autism primarily as an early sensory-motor-attention impairment (visual orienting, sensorimotor deficits at 6–12 months) linked to neurogenesis dysregulation, with social deficits as a secondary downstream consequence.
Draws convergent support from genetically defined syndromes (16p11 deletion, PTEN, chd8 mutations) and rodent/iPSC models implicating Wnt/β-catenin signaling, mTOR, and intermediate progenitor cells.

Notable claims / data

~10–20% of younger siblings of autistic probands themselves meet diagnostic criteria; brain enlargement present in ~15% of autistic children by a >1.5 SD cross-sectional cutoff.
Hazlett/Gu prospective imaging (6, 12, 24 months): HR infants later diagnosed with autism showed increased cortical surface-area growth rate from 6–12 months (greatest in right middle occipital gyrus / left cuneus, visual cortex) but no cortical-thickness differences; brain overgrowth appeared only by 24 months.
Surface-area growth rate (6–12 mo) correlated with total brain-volume growth rate (12–24 mo); brain-volume change (12–24 mo) associated with severity of social deficits on ADOS, but no relation to repetitive behavior.
Emphasizes accelerated within-individual growth rate (rather than cross-sectional enlargement) as potentially relevant to a larger fraction of affected individuals; notes etiologic heterogeneity (e.g., DYRK1-linked microcephaly, decreased volume in 16p11 duplication).
Mechanistic candidates: β-catenin/Brn2/Tbr2 cascade, ARHGAP11B, FGF signaling, XAV939-induced intermediate-progenitor amplification producing excess layer 2/3 excitatory neurons and autism-like behavior; mTOR-driven decreased spine pruning reversed by rapamycin; IGF-1 rescue of ASD-derived iPSC neuron deficits.
Caveat: lissencephalic rodent models may not capture gyrencephalic human surface-area expansion; many infant-sibling studies limited by <20 HR-ASD subjects and reproducibility concerns.

Connections

Centers on autism and situates progenitor over-proliferation within neurogenesis, radial-glia biology, and broader neurodevelopment.
Genetic risk discussion connects to chd8 and to psychiatric-genetics; interneuron findings touch interneuron-migration.
iPSC/organoid evidence (Mariani, Marchetto) relates to brain-organoids as a model system for early ASD neurogenesis.

Open questions

Do accelerated brain-growth-rate findings replicate, and are they specific to autism versus other neurodevelopmental disorders (e.g., Fragile X, language/learning disorders)?
What is the causal role of early sensory/visual-orienting deficits versus primary progenitor over-proliferation in initiating the cascade?
How generalizable is the idiopathic high-familial-risk framework across autism’s etiologic heterogeneity, including microcephalic subgroups?
Can early, sensory-focused interventions during the pre-symptomatic window alter the developmental trajectory?

wiki/sources/plaschka-2018-prespliceosom.title: plaschka-2018-prespliceosome-structure-provides-insights-into
wiki/sources/plaschka-2018-prespliceosom.content:
Prespliceosome structure provides insights into spliceosome assembly and regulation
One-line: A near-atomic cryo-EM structure of the yeast prespliceosome (A-complex) showing how U1 and U2 snRNPs jointly select and position the 5′ splice site and branch point, and revealing structural roles for alternative-splicing factors LUC7L and TIA-1 in splice-sites selection.
Key takeaways

Reports the cryo-EM structure of the S. cerevisiae prespliceosome (A-complex) assembled on UBC4 pre-mRNA at near-atomic resolution (U1 snRNP ~4.0 Å; U2 regions 4.9–10.4 Å), a previously poorly understood early step of spliceosome assembly.
The A-complex is the focal point for regulation by alternative-splicing factors: U1 and U2 snRNPs together recognize the two reactants of branching — the splice-sites 5′ splice site (bound by U1) and the branch-point sequence (bound by U2) — and hold them for delivery into the assembling spliceosome.
The structure explains how the splicing factors Luc7 (human LUC7L, LUC7L2, LUC7L3) and Nam8 (human TIA-1) act structurally, both linked to human disease (splicing-factors).
Luc7 and its human homologues stabilize the U1 snRNA–5′SS helix without base-pairing to the 5′SS — they cradle the helix phosphate backbone — providing a mechanism for selecting weak 5′ splice sites and bypassing the requirement for the Prp28 helicase.
Nam8/TIA-1 binds uridine-rich intron sequences downstream of the 5′SS (predominantly via its RRM2 domain), promoting use of weak 5′SSs and meiotic pre-mRNA splicing.
The model provides working mechanisms for 5′SS transfer to the U6 ACAGAGA region and for conversion of the prespliceosome into the activation-competent B-complex, defining a new checkpoint for spliceosome activation.

Notable claims / data

The final A-complex model comprises 34 proteins, U1 and U2 snRNAs, and the pre-mRNA substrate; it lacks the mobile cap-binding complex, Prp5, and the U1 subunit Prp40.
U1 and U2 snRNPs bind pre-mRNA in a parallel manner; the pre-mRNA 5′SS and branch-point branching reactants are positioned ~150 Å apart in the A-complex, with ~40 nucleotides of the UBC4 intron looped out between them.
The first ~18 nucleotides of U1 snRNA (disordered in the free U1 snRNP) become ordered in the A-complex by pairing with the pre-mRNA 5′SS; a newly ordered Yhc1 (human U1C) peptide contacts the 5′SS phosphate backbone at the +5/+6 positions.
The U1 snRNP core is highly similar to its human counterpart (Snp1/U1-70k, Mud1/U1A, Yhc1/U1C) plus peripheral yeast proteins Luc7, Nam8, Prp39, Prp42, Snu56, Snu71.
U2 snRNP has a bipartite architecture (SF3b-containing 5′ region + U2 3′ domain/SF3a 3′ region); the Prp39–Prp42 heterodimer anchors the U2 3′ domain to U1 snRNP via the U2A′ homologue Lea1 (human U2A′), at two interfaces (A and B).
Modelling suggests the U2 snRNP positions U1 snRNP to deliver the 5′SS–U1 helix to the exposed U6 ACAGAGA stem in the tri-snRNP (only ~20 Å from where Prp28 is likely to mediate 5′SS transfer), and that repositioning the Brr2 helicase onto U4 snRNA coincides with U1 snRNP release — a proposed molecular checkpoint for successful 5′SS transfer and B-complex formation.

Connections

A structural-biology counterpart to the mechanism overviews in baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and desterro-2020-targeting-mrna-processing-as-an (which describe the ordered snRNP assembly U1→SF1/U2AF→U2→tri-snRNP); this paper resolves the A-complex step at atomic detail — deepens spliceosome and splice-sites.
The Luc7/Nam8 findings tie structural mechanism to splicing-factors and human disease (LUC7L2 in myeloid malignancy; TIA-1 in Welander distal myopathy) — connects to splicing-and-disease and to the SF3b module (sf3b1) that is drugged by pladienolides.
The weak-5′SS-selection theme informs how alternative-splicing and the splicing-code are read at the very first committed step, and the RNA-helix stabilization connects to rna-structure-and-splicing.

Open questions

How exactly do the human U1–U2 snRNP interfaces differ from yeast, and does human PRPF39 obligately contact the U2 3′ domain as its yeast homologue Prp39 does?
What is the full trajectory of Prp5-driven remodelling that dissociates it and allows stable U2–branch-point pairing, and how is it structurally coupled to A-complex maturation?
Can the proposed Brr2-repositioning / U1-release checkpoint be captured structurally to confirm the 5′SS-transfer proofreading mechanism?
How do LUC7L family members differentially select 5′ splice sites in human alternative splicing, and how do their disease mutations perturb this?

wiki/sources/pollex-2024-enhancer-promot.title: pollex-2024-enhancer-promoter-interactions-become-more
wiki/sources/pollex-2024-enhancer-promot.content:
Enhancer–promoter interactions become more instructive in the transition from cell-fate specification to tissue differentiation
One-line: Capture-C on ~600 activity-characterized Drosophila enhancers/promoters shows E–P regulation shifts from permissive (proximity uncoupled from activity) during cell-fate specification to instructive (proximity coupled to activity) during terminal tissue differentiation.
Key takeaways

Directly tests the relationship between enhancer–promoter proximity and activity by starting from cis-regulatory-elements with characterized in vivo activity, then measuring their proximity as they transition OFF-to-ON or ON-to-OFF — an orthogonal design that avoids biasing toward already-active or already-proximal loops.
During specification (6–8 h), myogenic and neuronal cells have remarkably similar E–P topologies (1,157 shared invariant interactions vs 245 differential), i.e. chromatin-loops are largely permissive and uncoupled from lineage-specific activity.
During terminal differentiation (10–12 h), many new, more distal interactions emerge whose proximity changes track activity changes — an instructive mode confirmed by concordant chromatin-accessibility (DHS) and H3K27ac signal at the interacting “other end.”
Most E–P interactions stay within a TAD, though some cross ten-plus boundaries as megabase-scale loops.
Insulator binding (CTCF, BEAF-32, Su(Hw)) does NOT explain differential E–P interactions — 74–79% of significant interacting regions bind none of the three, and interaction changes are uncorrelated with insulator-binding changes.

Notable claims / data

583 baits passed QC (303 enhancers, 276 promoters, 4 both); Capture-C with a 4 bp cutter, 2 replicates × 5 developmental contexts = 20 datasets; nuclei FANS-purified to >95% (Mef2+ myogenic, Elav+ neuronal).
24,012 high-confidence interactions (18,252 unique), defined by CHiCAGO score ≥5 + DHS overlap + 2 kb–10 Mb distance filter.
Interaction complexity rises with development: median 1 interaction/bait at 6–8 h vs 7 at 10–12 h; total high-confidence interactions ~1,000→3,000 (2–3 h→6–8 h) and ~3,000→9,000 (6–8 h→10–12 h).
Stringent differential set: 4,348 differential interactions (2,858 unique; 5% FDR, >0.7 log2FC) and 6,853 constant (3,164 unique). 68% (myo) and 74% (neuro) of differential interactions occur in the 6–8 h→10–12 h transition; gains dominate (78% muscle, 91% neurons).
Dendrogram/PCA: myoblasts and neuronal cells are closer to each other at 6–8 h than to their own 10–12 h differentiated counterparts.
DHS and H3K27ac changes at the “other end” are concordant with interaction-frequency changes, but a DHS/accessibility gain alone does not force a new loop (interactions often skip a DHS) — binding of specific factors, not mere accessibility, regulates loops.
Motif analysis implicates Trl/GAF and Clamp broadly, and lineage TFs (Mef2 in muscle, l(3)neo38 in neurons) in instructive loop formation; insulator motifs not enriched.

Connections

Extends the permissive-vs-instructive loop debate for cis-regulatory-elements and chromatin-loops using chromatin-conformation-capture (Capture-C), complementing wiki pages on 3D genome architecture and topologically-associating-domains.
Pairs with chromatin-accessibility as a regulatory readout; relevant to studies linking chromatin state to gene regulation across development.

Open questions

Which specific factors (beyond Trl/GAF, Clamp, lineage TFs) actively drive instructive loop formation during differentiation, and how?
Why is the permissive mode favored during specification and the instructive mode during terminal differentiation — does permissive poising enable cell-fate plasticity while instructive coupling ensures tissue diversification?
Do these Drosophila-derived modes generalize to vertebrate developmental systems and to inducible/stimulus-driven gene expression?

wiki/sources/polymenidou-2011-long-pre-m.title: polymenidou-2011-long-pre-mrna-depletion-and-rna
wiki/sources/polymenidou-2011-long-pre-m.content:
Long pre-mRNA depletion and RNA missplicing contribute to neuronal vulnerability from loss of TDP-43
One-line: A Cleveland/Yeo-lab Nature Neuroscience study mapping the genome-wide RNA targets of the ALS/FTLD protein TDP-43 by CLIP-seq, showing that TDP-43 binds UG-rich distal intronic sites and is required to sustain the levels of very long, brain-enriched pre-mRNAs — many encoding synaptic proteins — and that its loss causes widespread missplicing.
Key takeaways

TDP-43 is an RNA-binding protein whose mutation causes amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS); its normal function is largely nuclear and its disease pathology involves nuclear clearance plus cytoplasmic aggregation, consistent with a loss-of-function contribution.
CLIP-seq in adult mouse brain identified TDP-43 binding in 6,304 genes (39,961 clusters, ~84% of all targets captured); binding is strongly UG-rich / (UG)n-repeat and is predominantly in distal introns (93% of sites intronic, 63% >2 kb from the nearest exon–intron boundary).
This distal-intron binding is strikingly different from the exon-proximal “RNA maps” of classic splicing-factors like Nova and Fox2, and unlike Argonaute/miRNA binding which concentrates at 3′ UTRs.
Antisense-oligonucleotide (ASO) knockdown of TDP-43 in mouse striatum to ~20% of normal changed 601 mRNA levels (362 up, 239 down) and altered 965 splicing events — establishing TDP-43 as a regulator of both RNA abundance and alternative-splicing.
The core disease-relevant mechanism: TDP-43 binding to long introns sustains the mRNA levels of genes with exceptionally long introns; downregulated genes had introns >6-fold longer than unaffected/upregulated genes and 74% of downregulated genes were direct TDP-43 targets.
Downregulated, long-intron TDP-43 targets are enriched for synaptic-activity genes implicated in neurological disease (Grin2a/NMDA receptor 2A, Grik2/GluR6, Cacna1c, Kcnma1, neurexins, neuroligin-1) — a route from TDP-43 loss to neuronal vulnerability.
TDP-43 autoregulates its own synthesis by binding and enhancing splicing of an intron in the 3′ UTR of its own transcript, triggering nonsense-mediated decay — a negative feedback loop on TDP-43 levels.

Notable claims / data

Two CLIP-seq replicates yielded 5.3M and 12.0M reads; 90% of experiment-1 binding sites overlapped experiment 2 (vs 8% for randomized clusters).
57% of clusters contained ≥4 GUGU tetramers; binding strength correlated with number of UG tetramers, but only ~3% of transcribed 300-nt stretches containing ≥3 GUGU tetramers were actually bound — so UG-content alone is not sufficient to specify binding.
Brain-enriched genes have significantly longer introns (P < 6 × 10⁻⁶), and intron length was conserved between mouse and human orthologs, suggesting the long introns carry important regulatory information.
Of downregulated genes with >100-kb introns, 100% (19/19) were direct TDP-43 targets; of >10-kb-intron downregulated genes, 96% (123/128) were direct targets.
203 cassette exons were differentially spliced on TDP-43 depletion (93 included, 110 excluded); sortilin 1 (Sort1, the progranulin receptor) had the highest splice-index change, involving exon 18.
GO analysis: downregulated (TDP-43-bound) genes enriched for ion transport / synaptic transmission; upregulated genes (mostly not TDP-43-bound) enriched for immune/inflammatory response — consistent with an indirect, secondary effect.

Connections

Provides the archetypal example of a splicing/RNA-metabolism regulator whose disease relevance runs through long-intron, brain-specific transcripts — a distinctive “RNA map” contrasting the exon-proximal maps discussed in fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing and vuong-2016-the-neurogenetics-of-alternative-splicing.
Links splicing-and-disease to neurodegeneration (ALS/FTLD) and to splicing-and-neurodevelopment via synaptic-gene targets; the Sort1/progranulin connection ties to FTLD genetics.
The autoregulatory NMD loop connects to poison-exon / poison-exons and NMD-based autoregulation seen in other splicing factors (e.g. PTBP2, SR proteins).
Methodologically anchors CLIP-seq target mapping used broadly across the wiki’s rna-binding-proteins pages; connects to tdp-43 as a disease entity.

Open questions

What is the mechanism by which TDP-43 binding to distal introns sustains mature mRNA levels of long genes (splicing efficiency? stability? transcript integrity?)?
How much of ALS/FTLD neuronal vulnerability is attributable to loss-of-function (long-gene depletion) vs cytoplasmic gain-of-function aggregation?
Do the human orthologous long-intron targets recapitulate the mouse dependency in patient tissue, and can ASO-based restoration protect neurons?
Why is the UG-content-to-binding relationship so sparse — what additional determinants (structure, cofactors) specify actual TDP-43 occupancy?

wiki/sources/povysil-2019-rare-variant-c.title: povysil-2019-rare-variant-collapsing-analyses-complex-traits
wiki/sources/povysil-2019-rare-variant-c.content:
Rare-variant collapsing analyses for complex traits: guidelines and applications
One-line: A methods review laying out best-practice guidelines for gene-based rare-variant “collapsing” analyses that detect an excess of rare, deleterious variants in cases versus controls to implicate disease genes for complex traits.
Key takeaways

Where common-variant genome-wide-association-study designs increasingly yield small-effect signals broadly spread across the genome (the omnigenic model), rare-variant sequencing studies detect larger-effect variants that more directly implicate core disease genes, addressing part of the missing heritability (missing-heritability) of complex traits.
The gene-based collapsing approach bins all “qualifying variants” (variants passing a set of quality, annotation, and frequency filters) within a gene as equivalent, then tests case-vs-control counts of individuals carrying at least one qualifying variant — a simple yet effective alternative to burden and variance-component tests (C-alpha, SKAT, SKAT-O).
Collapsing works best under high allelic heterogeneity (e.g., haploinsufficient genes), where many distinct loss-of-function alleles confer equivalent risk; it flags presence/absence of any LOF variant rather than modeling each variant individually.
The single most important lever for power is restricting to the rarest (“ultra-rare”) variants — those absent from >250,000 aggregated reference exomes (gnomAD + DiscovEHR + Bravo/TOPMed) — because the strongest disease-risk signal concentrates in the rarest MAF bins.
Reliable variant annotation matters: missense deleteriousness predictors (CADD (cadd-score), PolyPhen-2, SIFT, REVEL, PrimateAI) and splice predictors (TraP, SpliceAI) help enrich for functional variants; canonical splice-site variants are among the qualifying LOF classes.

Notable claims / data

Conventional exome-wide significance threshold is α = 0.05/19,000 ≈ 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ (≈19,000 protein-coding genes), further divided by the number of collapsing models tested.
Synonymous variants serve as a near-neutral negative-control class: no significant case-control difference in exome-wide synonymous tallies confirms a homogeneous cohort; deviation flags bias.
Under sparse, imbalanced case-control data, chi-squared/logistic asymptotics fail; Fisher’s exact test, Firth-corrected logistic regression, or saddle-point-approximation score tests are preferred, and permutation-based (1,000-permutation) empirical expected P-value distributions replace the conventional QQ/lambda inflation check.
Forest-plot reanalysis of three published studies (genetic generalized epilepsies, non-acquired focal epilepsies, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis) shows the ultra-rare “PolyPhen-2 probably damaging” missense bin (MAF <0.0002%) carries the strongest, most significant enrichment (e.g., IPF PTV bin log-OR 5.45, P = 6×10⁻¹⁶).
Applied across sample sizes from <300 cases / 4,000 controls (IPF) to 7,000 cases / 13,000 controls (Alzheimer disease), and extended to phenome-wide UK Biobank analyses (thousands of phenotypes, >40,000 individuals, with replication).
Regional missense-intolerance scores (MTR, MPC, CCRs, LIMBR, subRVIS) can act as an added qualifying-variant filter: e.g., for PCDH19, all ClinVar pathogenic missense variants fall in a single intolerant exon while control (DiscovEHR) variants spread across tolerant regions.

Connections

Contrasts the rare-variant paradigm against the common-variant genome-wide-association-study and the missing-heritability (missing-heritability) problem in quantitative-genetics.
Uses linear-mixed-model methods to handle cryptic relatedness and family structure in the test cohort.
Relies on variant-effect prediction (mutation-effect-prediction) tools including CADD (cadd-score) to select functional qualifying variants; canonical splice-site LOF alleles link to splice-sites.
Notes applications and enrichment findings in psychiatric-genetics (schizophrenia) and de novo promoter-variant signals in autism.
Discusses (and flags as still-immature) extension of collapsing/burden frameworks to regulatory noncoding variants (noncoding-variants), where single-variant effects are weaker and true burden aggregation may matter more.

Open questions

Which regional-intolerance measure and threshold maximize collapsing power, and whether optimal thresholds differ by disease severity, population frequency, and genetic architecture.
How to define the aggregation units and functional-enrichment metrics for whole-genome collapsing over noncoding regulatory sequence (candidate metrics: GERP++, Orion, ncRVIS, enhancer domain score).
How to make joint-calling and statistical testing scalable and standardized for biobank-scale cohorts (UK Biobank, TOPMed), including single-sample haplotype-calling strategies to combine independently sequenced cohorts.
How to properly resolve phase for recessive/compound-heterozygous models and tame the combinatorial explosion of digenic/oligogenic tests (biologically informed gene sets as one solution).

wiki/sources/preissl-2023-characterizing.title: preissl-2023-characterizing-cis-regulatory-elements-single-cell
wiki/sources/preissl-2023-characterizing.content:
Characterizing cis-regulatory elements using single-cell epigenomics
One-line: A review of single-cell epigenomic and multi-omic technologies for annotating cell type-specific cis-regulatory-elements, and an assessment of their readiness for human tissue profiling.
Key takeaways

Cell type-specific gene expression is governed by cis-regulatory-elements (CREs) — promoters, enhancers, insulators, and less-characterized silencer/tethering elements — whose activity is read out through epigenomic features: chromatin-accessibility, dna-methylation, histone modifications, and 3D chromatin conformation.
Bulk cCRE catalogues (ENCODE, Roadmap; see screen-ccre-registry) classify millions of candidate CREs as promoter-like, enhancer-like, or CTCF/insulator-like from co-occurring accessibility, DNA hypomethylation, and histone marks (H3K4me3, H3K4me1, H3K27ac), but lack cell-type resolution and miss rare or uncharacterized cell types.
Single-cell epigenomics overcomes cellular heterogeneity: it deconvolutes bulk signal into per-cell-type CRE activity, detects rare/unknown cell types, links distal enhancers to target promoters, and distinguishes whether bulk changes reflect CRE-activity shifts, cell-state transitions, or cellular-composition changes.
Three barcoding strategies span a throughput/coverage trade-off: (1) miniaturized plate/tube/microfluidic assays (hundreds–thousands of cells, high coverage), (2) droplet-based platforms (~10,000 cells, up to ~50,000 with sample indexing), and (3) single-cell combinatorial indexing / split-pool (50,000–300,000+ cells, cost-effective but complex).
Assays now exist for each epigenomic modality — DNA methylation (scBS/scWGBS/snmC-seq), accessibility (scATAC-seq and many sci/split-pool variants), histone marks and DNA-binding proteins (scCUT&Tag, scChIC-seq, CoBATCH), and chromatin architecture (scHi-C, Dip-C, sciHi-C, scSPRITE) — plus multimodal methods co-profiling epigenome and transcriptome.

Notable claims / data

Data sparsity is worse for single-cell epigenomics than transcriptomics: most cell types carry only two DNA copies per feature, and the genome has ~63,000 genes but millions of potential regulatory elements, yielding low library complexity and coverage per cell.
Sparsity can be mitigated by raising assay sensitivity or cell numbers; recent throughput gains (droplet/combinatorial barcoding) especially help complex tissues like brain, whereas maximizing per-cell coverage matters when cells are scarce (e.g., embryonic development).
Plate-based single-cell WGBS is low-throughput but can detect up to 50% of CpG dinucleotides per cell; snmC-seq2 automation processes 3,072 cells (eight 384-well plates) per experiment.
DNA methylation’s relationship to transcription is more complex than simple repression: cytosine methylation blocks binding of many transcription factors but facilitates others, and non-CG methylation in neurons and ESCs mediates local repression via MeCP2.
Doublets / barcode collisions are a key confounder, assessable via species-mixing experiments; assays optimized on cell lines or PBMCs often need re-optimization for new tissues and storage conditions.

Connections

Grounds the epigenomic-mark logic used across the wiki: histone modifications, chromatin-accessibility, and dna-methylation as the readouts that define cis-regulatory-elements.
3D-genome context: insulators demarcate topologically-associating-domains boundaries via CTCF, constraining enhancer–promoter chromatin-loops measured by chromatin-conformation-capture (Hi-C).
Motivates interpretation of noncoding-variants and complex-trait risk loci by resolving which cell types a candidate CRE is active in.
Complements bulk resources such as the screen-ccre-registry and multimodal/multimodal-learning approaches that jointly model epigenome and transcriptome.

Open questions

How ready are these single-cell assays for routine primary human tissue profiling given sparsity, tissue-specific optimization, and storage constraints?
Which analytical tools best call and characterize cell type-specific CRE activity from sparse single-cell data, and how should enhancer–promoter linkages be validated?
How can silencer and tethering elements — still poorly characterized — be systematically detected at single-cell resolution?

wiki/sources/quinones-valdez-2025-long-r.title: quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis
wiki/sources/quinones-valdez-2025-long-r.content:
Long-read RNA-seq demarcates cis- and trans-directed alternative RNA splicing
One-line: Introduces isolaser, a single-sample long-read RNA-seq method that calls variants, phases haplotypes, and tests allelic linkage of splicing to cleanly separate cis-directed (genetically driven) from trans-directed alternative splicing events — applied to ENCODE human/mouse data, HLA genes, and Alzheimer’s disease brains.
Key takeaways

Splicing is jointly controlled by cis-regulatory elements and trans-acting factors (mainly rna-binding-proteins); separating events that are primarily cis- vs. trans-directed in a given sample clarifies the genetic basis of disease. Long reads phase a heterozygous SNP into two haplotypes (H1/H2), and cis-directed exons show exon inclusion almost exclusively in one haplotype while trans-directed exons show similar inclusion across both.
isolaser (isoform-Level analysis of Allele-Specific processing of Exonic Regions) is a one-stop tool: (1) de novo variant calling via de Bruijn graph local reassembly + multi-layer-perceptron false-positive filter, (2) gene-level phasing via weighted k-means read clustering (haplotagging), (3) linkage test between haplotypes and exonic parts using adjusted mutual information (AMI). Crucially it needs NO pre-phased whole genome.
Works on a single sample (a major advantage for allele-specific splicing); an isoLASER-joint mode merges multiple samples to prioritize candidate functional variants (Splicing-Associated Variants, SAVs) and recover cis-events untestable in one sample (e.g. driven by homozygous variants), effectively achieving an sQTL-type analysis with very small cohorts.
Operates on exonic parts (non-overlapping exonic segments from collapsing isoforms, ~100–200 nt) rather than whole isoforms — granular, event-level resolution that is more sensitive than isoform-level allelic methods like LORALS when a gene has many combinatorial AS events.
Striking biological finding: clustering samples by AMI of splicing linkage segregates them by donor identity, not tissue of origin — even though PSI splicing profiles cluster by tissue. Genetic background shapes an individual’s overall splicing linkage; cis-directed events are individual-specific and maintained across that donor’s tissues.
Resolves allele-specific splicing in the highly polymorphic HLA system (hard with short reads), enables HLA typing from RNA, and surfaces cis-directed events in Alzheimer’s-relevant genes (MAPT, BIN1, MS4A6A, HLA-DQA1).

Notable claims / data

Variant calling: MLP classifier training AUC 0.92–0.99 (ROC) / 0.86–0.99 (PR). On HG002 long-read RNA-seq, isoLASER matched or beat GATK HaplotypeCaller, DeepVariant, and Clair3 on F1 but with superior precision.
Phasing: >99% of heterozygous variants phased by HapCUT2 or the HG002 diploid (T2T) assembly were consistently phased by isoLASER; switch-error rates 0.15% (vs HapCUT2) and 0.1% (vs HPRC). No variants phased by isoLASER only.
Cis-event definition: AMI above 99th percentile of a simulated unlinked background (coverage-dependent cutoff fit by 3rd-degree polynomial) AND |delta PSI| > 5% between haplotypes; trans = below 95th percentile; in between = ambiguous.
Scale: 2–946 cis-directed events per sample. Human tissues 2047 unique cis exonic parts / 1203 genes; mouse tissues 4679 / 2341; human cell lines 3312 / 1703; mouse cell lines 1341 / 778. ~13% of cis exonic parts are novel (not in GENCODE). F1 hybrid mice show more cis-events than F0 inbred (more heterozygosity).
isoLASER identified substantially more allele-specific-splicing genes than LORALS in both ENCODE and HG002 data; example WASHC2C has one isoLASER cis exonic part but none of its 4 transcripts showed LORALS-detectable allelic bias.
Considering all human tissues jointly, 37% of testable events were cis-directed in ≥1 tissue, 46% trans, 17% ambiguous (single-sample: 15% cis / 68% trans). 4–29% of single-sample trans events became cis at the joint level.
SAVs of cis-events had significantly lower GTEx splicing-qtl p-values than matched controls; their delta PSI correlated with the GTEx sQTL genotype beta (R = -0.37, p = 4.5e-08; inverse because GTEx/LeafCutter uses intron usage). 44 SAVs were known to alter RBP binding (allele-specific-binding study); motif enrichment near SAVs implicated HNRNP D/L/K, KHSRP, SFPQ; DeepRiPe predicted SAVs significantly alter RBP-RNA binding vs controls (KS p = 4.79e-12).
HLA: isoLASER HLA typing concordant with HG002 diploid MHC assembly and arcasHLA on 70 short-read samples. HLA-C alleles show allele-specific patterns (C04/C06 exon 5 skipping; C03 alt 3’ splice site in exon 6 ~25% usage; C07 none). AlphaFold shows cis-events reshape the protein (MHC-I C-terminus elongated by 6 aa; exon-5 exclusion deletes a transmembrane helix).
Alzheimer’s: in ENCODE PacBio DLPFC (5 AD / 4 control) plus an Oxford Nanopore frontal-cortex set (6 AD / 6 control), 7 HLA genes showed AD allele-specific splicing. SAVs in MS4A6A, BIN1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DQA1 passed the AD GWAS significance threshold (5e-8). UBC had the highest delta-delta PSI (ddPSI): an intron showed 100% retention with the T allele in AD but the linkage was reversed in controls (C allele 100% retention); the event removes one ubiquitin domain. ~15% (1201) of SAVs were rare/ultra-rare (gnomAD MAF < 1e-2 / 1e-3), a regime sQTL studies cannot address.

Connections

A long-read, single-sample counterpart to short-read allele-specific-splicing tools like pairadise (see demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing) — both detect rare-variant effects that population splicing-qtl mapping misses, but isoLASER needs no near-exon SNP on the same short read.
Benchmarked against LORALS (isoform-level allelic linkage) and against GTEx sQTLs computed with LeafCutter (see li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna); positioned as a viable small-cohort alternative to sQTL mapping (cf. zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls).
Quantifies events with percent-spliced-in (delta PSI between haplotypes) and centrally analyzes intron-retention (UBC event).
RBP-binding interpretation connects to the allele-specific-binding / rna-binding-proteins literature (DeepRiPe, RBNS); related to large RBP binding maps (see van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional).
Tool entity: isolaser. Builds on the same lab’s scAllele variant-calling framework.

Open questions

Do non-consecutive cis-directed events within one gene share a common causal variant (genetic co-regulation of splicing)?
Older Nanopore chemistries force SNP-only analysis with external validation — how much sensitivity is lost, and does newer chemistry close the gap with PacBio HiFi?
Can isoLASER-joint with much larger cohorts yield shorter, more robust SAV lists (fine-mapping toward causal variants)? Not evaluated here.
Functional validation: do the predicted HLA/UBC protein-structure changes have measurable cellular or disease consequences?

wiki/sources/rabe-hesketh-2008-biometric.title: rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and
wiki/sources/rabe-hesketh-2008-biometric.content:
Biometrical Modeling of Twin and Family Data Using Standard Mixed Model Software
One-line: Shows that biometrical variance-components-model decomposition of twin and family data into additive-genetic (A), common-environment (C), dominance (D), and unique-environment (E) components can be fitted with standard mixed-model software (Stata’s gllamm, R/S-PLUS) rather than specialized SEM software, via parameterizations needing only a few random effects so the approach extends to categorical phenotypes.
Key takeaways

Twin/family designs are usually fit as structural-equation-models in specialized software like mx-software; this paper recasts the same biometrical models as linear-mixed-models (continuous phenotypes) and generalized-linear-mixed-models (categorical phenotypes), making them accessible in Stata, R/S-PLUS, SAS, SPSS.
The core challenge is imposing the covariance restrictions predicted by quantitative-genetics (e.g., MZ twins correlate 1 on additive genetic effects, DZ twins 1/2) within software that does not allow random-effect covariance matrices to depend on covariates or nonlinear constraints on parameters.
Two parameterizations are proposed. Parameterization 1 reproduces the genetic covariance structure directly using dummy-variable tricks (3 random effects for ACE/ADE), giving a one-to-one correspondence between random-effect variances and the A/C/D/E variance components.
Parameterization 2 is a plain three-level random-intercept model needing only 2 random effects, achieved by letting a level-2 random-effect variance be shared by MZ twins (via an artificial cluster identifier) but unique to DZ individuals; variance components of interest are then linear combinations of model variances.
Fewer random effects matter most for categorical/dichotomous phenotypes, where the GLMM likelihood has no closed form and random effects must be integrated out numerically — dimensionality of the integral is often far smaller than in the SEM approach for large families.
Applied to continuous birth weight (ACE model on Norwegian nuclear families) and dichotomous depression (ACE/ADE models on Virginia Twin Registry twin-pairs).

Notable claims / data

ACDE model is not identified from twins reared together alone; in practice the special cases ACE and ADE (and nested AE, CE) are fitted.
Birth weight (1000 nuclear families, Medical Birth Registry of Norway): fit with Stata’s xtmixed / R-S-PLUS lme. Additive-genetic SD σ_A = 315.06 g (SE 16.12), unique-environment SD σ_E = 365.40 g (SE 12.41); heritability ĥ² = σ²_A/(σ²_A+σ²_E) = 0.43; 95% CI 0.35–0.50 via probit transformation and delta method.
Depression (DSM-III-R, Virginia Twin Registry, Caucasian female twin-pairs): estimated by maximum likelihood with adaptive quadrature in gllamm. ACE common-environment variance from parameterization 2 was negative (−0.12) and constrained to exactly zero (AE model) in parameterization 1; AE heritability ĥ² = σ²_A/(σ²_A+1) = 0.43, 95% CI 0.31–0.53. ADE gives σ²_A = 0.54, σ²_D = 0.25.
A practical feature: parameterizations with direct correspondence between variance components and parameters let software that forbids negative variances enforce the genetically required nonnegativity; parameterization 2 does NOT impose nonnegativity, so a negative σ²_C estimate signals the ACE model is inappropriate and suggests switching to ADE without re-estimation.
Also gives parameterizations for siblings-plus-cousins (sharing grandparents) and nuclear families (mother, father, two children), with the relevant A and C covariance matrices.
Categorical phenotypes handled via a probit threshold/liability model; estimation needs numerical or Monte Carlo integration. Notes adaptive quadrature (gllamm, SAS PROC NLMIXED) is superior to ordinary quadrature, and warns penalized quasi-likelihood (PQL) performs poorly for family data with large/unequal cluster sizes.
Caveat: likelihood-ratio tests for variance components do not follow the conventional χ² null; correct null is a mixture of χ² distributions (cites Dominicus et al. 2005).

Connections

Explicitly builds on mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models: McArdle & Prescott (2005) propose a similar parameterization to “parameterization 1,” but theirs requires four random effects for twin models whereas this paper’s needs three (parameterization 1) or only two (parameterization 2) — a deliberate reduction in random-effect dimensionality for computational efficiency.
Positions itself against pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and: Pawitan et al. (2004) wrote a custom program for a nonstandard GLMM allowing random-effect correlations to depend on covariates; this paper instead achieves the same restrictions within standard GLMM software. It notes Pawitan et al. nicely demonstrate dominance and maternal effects, which these standard-software parameterizations can also incorporate.
Contrasts with two other prior attempts: Guo & Wang (2002) ignore the restrictions to use standard LMM software; van den Oord (2001) uses standard LMM software but needs complicated constraints, parameters that do not map to quantities of interest, and risks theoretically impossible (improper) models.
Strongly tied to twin-and-family-study-design and heritability estimation; both example analyses report ĥ² for the additive-genetic component.

Open questions

The mixture-χ² null for variance-component LRTs is flagged but not applied to the worked examples — how much would correctly testing C=0 (ACE vs AE) in the depression data change conclusions?
Extensions noted but not developed: incorporating measured genotypes at specific loci, genotype-environment interaction, epistasis (genotype × genetic), and higher-level environmental random effects (e.g., neighborhoods) — all within standard mixed-model software.
Mx (mx-software) still uniquely supports sibling-interaction, homogamy, and complex parent–child resemblance models the authors say cannot be fit with standard GLMMs; what is the boundary of what the mixed-model reframing can reach?
For large families with categorical phenotypes, how far does the reduced integral dimensionality of parameterization 2 actually extend feasible model size versus SEM/Mx?

wiki/sources/radrizzani-2023-selection-o.title: radrizzani-2023-selection-on-synonymous-sites
wiki/sources/radrizzani-2023-selection-o.content:
Selection on synonymous sites: the unwanted transcript hypothesis
One-line: A Perspective proposing that much of the pervasive selection on human synonymous sites is best explained not by translational selection but by the need to suppress “unwanted” RNAs (spurious transcripts, mis-spliced forms, transposable element and viral transcripts).
Key takeaways

Classical translational selection (favouring codons matching abundant tRNAs) is not readily seen in humans owing to small effective population size (Ne), yet 10-20% of human synonymous sites still show selection — the paper reframes this as gene-regulation against unwanted transcripts.
With low Ne, humans carry abundant “junk” DNA and error-prone processes, so the dominant expression problem is unwanted RNA: spurious transcripts from degenerate transcription factor binding sites, transposable element and viral transcripts, and mis-spliced forms from weak human splice sites (a problem tied to alternative-splicing and splicing-and-evolution).
Two solution modes: reduce creation (transcriptional suppression, more accurate splicing) or set “traps” (degradation via RNases/exosome, physical retention preventing nuclear export). Both are codon-content-sensitive, so synonymous mutations can affect them.
The best-supported case is selection on synonymous sites to preserve exonic splice enhancers (a class of splicing-regulatory-elements) near exon ends and to avoid cryptic splice sites, both linked to splice-sites, rna-binding-proteins binding, and splicing-and-disease.
Nucleotide “fingerprints” mark nativity: high-GC (but low-CpG) content, intron presence/position, and small exons signal native mRNAs, whereas AT-rich, CpG-rich, UpA-rich, intronless or long-exon transcripts are treated as suspicious.

Notable claims / data

Of 15.6 million human transcription factor binding sites, fewer than 13% are conserved; >20% of all (and >85% of primate-specific) binding sites derive from transposable elements; 83% of lncRNAs have exonized transposable elements.
Estimates of exonic point mutations that disrupt splicing range from 20% to nearly 100%; ~10-30% of disease mutations act through disrupting splice motifs such as ESEs.
Multiple quality-control filters all act against A/U/UpA/CpG-rich transcripts: NEXT/PAXT complexes and nuclear exosome, ZAP (targets CpG), RNase L (targets UpA), HUSH complex (silences long intronless/long-exon A-rich transcripts), paraspeckles and stress granules (trap dsRNA/AU-rich), and NMD/no-go/non-stop/codon-optimality decay.
NXF1 exports single-exon, long-exon and A/U-rich multi-exon transcripts, whereas the TREX complex exports spliced GC-rich transcripts — the three “unwanted” signatures map onto the NXF1 route.
Fewer than 0.4% of native exons (in ~3.5% of genes) are long (>1,500 bp) and A-rich enough to be HUSH targets; ~a quarter of these encode zinc finger proteins.
Higher GC3 content raises protein expression of intronless GFP transgenes; explains high-GC3 of intronless Sry and of retrogenes vs their intron-containing parents.
GC-biased gene conversion acts opposite to mutation bias and may itself have evolved to preserve GC content as a nativity signal.
Design-rule parallel: RNA vaccines and transgenes benefit from the same synonymous-site modifications (high-GC, low-CpG, pseudouridine/base modification to evade U/CpG-sensing filters).

Connections

Extends splicing-and-evolution and splicing-and-disease: positions selection against mis-splicing and cryptic splice-sites as a major, Ne-dependent form of synonymous-site selection, complementing work on noncoding-variants and mutation-effect-prediction.
Relates ESE preservation to rna-binding-proteins (serine-arginine-rich splicing regulatory proteins) and to cotranscriptional-splicing efficiency differences between mRNAs and lncRNAs.
Notes elevated transposable element transcription and RNA-surveillance failure in neurodegeneration, cancer and autoimmune disease; touches rna-editing (ADAR on IRAlus) as part of the trapping machinery.

Open questions

Why is no quality-control filter perfect (e.g. intronless histones and GPCRs still expressed; GC-poor L1 evades all filters)? Is leakiness fundamental or a model weakness?
Why do mammals both suppress high-CpG transcripts (via ZAP) yet use unmethylated CpG islands as nativity signals?
How far does the hypothesis extend across taxa, and can 5’ GC3 content or quality-control-filter inventories test its phylogenetic reach?
What fraction of disease-causing synonymous mutations act via splicing vs RNA structure, stability, miRNA sites or translation kinetics, given profound ascertainment bias?

wiki/sources/rahimikollu-2024-slide-sign.title: rahimikollu-2024-slide-significant-latent-factor-interaction
wiki/sources/rahimikollu-2024-slide-sign.content:
SLIDE: Significant Latent Factor Interaction Discovery and Exploration across biological domains
One-line: Introduces slide, an interpretable, distribution-free machine learning method that discovers significant standalone and interacting latent factors driving outcomes in high-dimensional multiomic data, with identifiability guarantees and rigorous FDR control.
Key takeaways

SLIDE addresses core challenges of multiomic analysis: differing data modalities, multicollinearity (which inflates regression coefficient variance and deflates P values), and large numbers of irrelevant features. It is presented as one of the few methods that provides meaningful inference for high-dimensional data without sacrificing predictive power — a form of model-interpretability and multimodal-learning applied to ai-in-genomics.
The method has three stages: unsupervised latent-factor discovery via the LOVE decomposition (X = AZ + E) with theoretical identifiability guarantees, knockoffs-based selection of significant standalone latent factors, and knockoffs-based selection of significant pairwise interacting factors capturing nonlinear relationships.
Unlike factor analysis or non-negative matrix factorization, SLIDE’s latent factors are uniquely identifiable without assumptions on the data-generating mechanism, permit overlap and correlation, and require only anchoring by two pure variables per factor — enabling meaningful regression and inference on the factors.
SLIDE adapts the knockoffs framework (typically used on observed variables) to latent factors, using a stability parameter (‘spec’) over repeated knockoff applications to converge on a small, actionable set of factors with false discovery rate control.

Notable claims / data

On simulated data, SLIDE performs as well as ER, LASSO, PLSR and PCR when no interaction terms are present, and consistently outperforms them when interaction terms exist. It predicts using only a small number of prioritized latent factors, whereas most compared methods use the full feature set.
Systemic sclerosis scRNA-seq (24 subjects, severity measured by Modified Rodnan Skin Score): SLIDE outperformed five of seven benchmarks (PLSR, PCR, PHATE-regression, MOFA+-regression, VAE) and matched LASSO and ER, but did so using only nine latent factors versus ER’s full set. It recovered nine known SSc drivers versus four (MOFA+) and two (VAE).
SLIDE recapitulated canonical SSc mechanisms (myeloid/fibroblast inflammatory states, SFRP2 fibroblasts, interferon signaling) and uncovered novel ones: an unelucidated keratinocyte role (validated by protein staining) and an HLA-signaling-dependent interaction between myeloid/endothelial inflammation and keratinocyte-fibroblast-endothelial crosstalk.
In a ‘human perturbation experiment’ using 10 subjects from a tofacitinib trial, SLIDE honed in on the known IL6/JAK/STAT mechanism of treatment response, predicting week-24 outcomes from week-6 scRNA-seq data.
SLIDE generalized across spatial modalities — 10X Visium, Slide-seq, MERFISH and CODEX — recapitulating immune cell partitioning (B cells at borders, CD4 T cells and dendritic cells centrally) in a murine asthma/allergy lymph-node model using only spatial-location labels, and separating glutamatergic neuron subclasses in murine primary motor cortex.
Beyond recovering canonical markers, SLIDE distinguished genes with purely linear, purely nonlinear, or mixed relationships to outcome (e.g. APOE and S100A9 showed both linear and nonlinear MRSS relationships), capturing signals missed by standard differential expression and regression methods.

Connections

Benchmarked against other latent-factor methods (MOFA+, VAE, PHATE, PCR, PLSR, LASSO); the essential regression (ER) baseline is a prior latent-factor regression framework it builds on. Relates to broader themes of single-cell-perturbation-modeling in that it extracts interpretable structure from single-cell and spatial omics.
The interpretability-versus-prediction tradeoff it targets is central to model-interpretability within ai-in-genomics.

Open questions

How does SLIDE scale to atlas-scale datasets with millions of cells and many modalities simultaneously?
The knockoffs adaptation to latent factors relies on preserving the covariance structure; how sensitive are the discovered interactions to the choice of stability parameter across noisier real-world datasets?
Can the pairwise-interaction framework be extended to higher-order (three-way and beyond) interactions while retaining FDR guarantees?

wiki/sources/rajderkar-2023-cell-type-an.title: rajderkar-2023-cell-type-and-tissue-specific
wiki/sources/rajderkar-2023-cell-type-an.content:
Cell Type- and Tissue-specific Enhancers in Craniofacial Development
One-line: A tissue- and single-cell-resolution catalogue of ~14,000 human craniofacial enhancers, cross-validated in mouse, to interpret noncoding variants behind facial variation and birth defects.
Key takeaways

Builds a comprehensive map of cis-regulatory-elements active during human face development (Carnegie stages 13-23, gestational weeks 4-8), combining H3K27ac ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, and RNA-seq from primary human embryonic face tissue.
Pairs bulk human enhancer calls with single-cell mouse data (scanfacex transcriptome atlas + ScanFaceN enhancer catalog) to assign cell-type-specific in vivo activity to enhancers via chromatin-accessibility.
Intersects the enhancer set with facial-shape and craniofacial-disease genome-wide-association-study signals, nominating noncoding-variants in enhancers as candidate contributors to normal variation and orofacial clefts.

Notable claims / data

13,983 reproducible human candidate enhancers (H3K27ac in >=2 samples); 6,718 of 10,893 CS18-23 regions (61.7%) also show accessible chromatin.
Transgenic mouse reporter assays: 60 candidates tested, 16 previously-unknown craniofacial enhancers validated in vivo; 153 predicted enhancers overlap prior VISTA craniofacial elements.
87% (12,179/13,983) of human enhancers are sequence-conserved to mouse; 59% of those (8,257) show mouse enhancer signatures (functionally conserved), while 28% are human-specific by epigenomic signature. Overall 56% of human enhancers are functionally conserved.
scRNA-seq: 57,598 mouse face cells (e11.5-e13.5), 16 annotated cell types (ScanFaceX). snATAC-seq: 41,483 cells, 20 clusters, 16,564 DARs (10,038 retained after label transfer).
GWAS: 1,404 lead SNPs -> 27,386 LD SNPs; 209 enhancers overlap 605 LD SNPs (43 enhancers/102 disease SNPs; 176 enhancers/515 normal-variation SNPs).
hs2656 (near POP1/NIPAL2/KCNS2, 8q22.2 cleft region) drives nasal/maxillary activity for the human but not the mouse ortholog (mm2280), confirming a human-specific enhancer.

Connections

Extends the general theme of cis-regulatory-elements and enhancer catalogues; complements MPRA-style and single-cell ATAC approaches to regulatory annotation elsewhere in the wiki.
Uses chromatin-accessibility (snATAC-seq) plus label transfer to give cis-regulatory-elements cell-type resolution, a strategy shared with other single-cell regulatory-genomics sources.
The genome-wide-association-study intersection to prioritize noncoding-variants parallels disease-critical cell-type and TWAS work in the collection.

Open questions

Which of the 209 GWAS-overlapping enhancers are causal for specific clefts or facial traits, and via which target genes?
Do the 28% human-specific (non-mouse-conserved) enhancers reflect true human regulatory innovation, or limits of the mouse assay?
Can enhancer-reporter + scRNA-seq scale beyond three enhancers to systematically resolve in vivo cell-type specificity genome-wide?

wiki/sources/rhine-2025-neuronal-aging-c.title: rhine-2025-neuronal-aging-causes-mislocalization-of
wiki/sources/rhine-2025-neuronal-aging-c.content:
Neuronal aging causes mislocalization of splicing proteins and unchecked cellular stress
One-line: Using transdifferentiated aged human neurons, this study shows that neuronal aging depletes and mislocalizes spliceosome components (including TDP-43) to the cytoplasm, driving widespread aberrant alternative-splicing and chronic, unresolvable stress granules — a mechanistic bridge from aging to neurodegeneration.
Key takeaways

Directly transdifferentiated fibroblast-to-neurons (Tdiff, “aged”) retain aging hallmarks, unlike iPSC-derived neurons (fetal-reset) — an efficient system to study aging RNA biology; validated in 1.5/6/24-month mouse brain and aged human brain tissue.
Aged neurons are broadly depleted of rna-binding-proteins, most enriched for spliceosome components (top de-enriched KEGG terms: spliceosome, mRNA surveillance, RNA transport); proteomic depletion occurs despite unchanged transcript levels.
Core splicing-factors mislocalize from nucleus to cytoplasm in aged neurons — the U1 complex (SNRNP70, SNRNPA), tri-snRNP (PRPF8, SNRNP200), and direct AS regulators tdp-43 and TIA1 — while FUS stays nuclear (DNA-damage role).
Nuclear depletion of tdp-43 (the protein aggregated in >95% of ALS and >50% of Alzheimer’s) reduces its intronic binding (>2.5-fold drop at ALS-linked cryptic-exon splice sites in STMN2 and UNC13A) and dysregulates splicing of ~500 transcripts, many neuronal cytoskeletal genes (e.g. AGRN exon 35/36 exclusion, DNM1, MAPT exons 3/6 inclusion).
Cytoplasmic spliceosome components normally get sequestered into stress granules on stress, but aged neurons form chronic gel-like stress granules (assembled independently of G3BP1, elevated phospho-eIF2α) that persist even at baseline and fail to resolve after acute arsenite/thapsigargin stress.
Chronic stress granules trace to malfunctioning ubiquitylation machinery, poor HSP90α chaperone activity, and priority given to clearing misfolded mitochondrial (oxidative-phosphorylation) proteins over RNA biology — HSP90α inhibitor ganetespib alone induces stress-granule formation.
The aging human brain independently displays the same signature: RBP dysregulation, cytoplasmic tdp-43 foci enriched in old-age (~80 y/o) vs mid-age (~40 y/o) cohorts, and chronic-stress phospho-eIF2α elevation.

Notable claims / data



4,000 transcripts differentially expressed between Tdiff and iPSC-derived neurons; upregulated pathways include immune response and lysosome; aged neurons retain aging-linked genes and senescence marker p16INK4A.


eCLIP of tdp-43 shows reduced intronic binding (fewer introns bound) despite retaining the canonical GU-rich motif; TDP-43-bound intron loss maps to ~500 differentially spliced transcripts (cassette-exon inclusion and exclusion changes).
Age-dependent tdp-43 mislocalization confirmed in mouse motor cortex: nuclear at 1.5 months, cytoplasmic foci by 24 months; cerebellum eCLIP shows age-dependent loss of intronic binding.
HSP90α and VCP/FAF2 (ubiquitin machinery for stress-granule dissolution) are among the most depleted G3BP1/TDP-43 interactors in aged neurons; ubiquitylation is broadly increased (unfolded-protein accumulation).

Connections

Grounds the splicing-and-disease / neurodegeneration axis in a concrete aging mechanism; the STMN2 and UNC13A cryptic exons connect to ALS/FTD tdp-43 proteinopathy literature.
A trans-acting complement to sequence-only splice predictors: shen-2026-advances-and-challenges-of-splicing explicitly flags loss of tdp-43/RBP context as a blind spot of cis-only models — this paper shows why that context matters.
Links biomolecular-condensates (stress granules) to spliceosome dysfunction; connects to the rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors concept pages.
Candidate entity pages: TDP-43, G3BP1, HSP90α, the transdifferentiation aging-neuron model.

Open questions

Is spliceosome depletion a cause or consequence of chronic stress-granule sequestration in aged neurons?
Can restoring HSP90α/VCP-mediated ubiquitylation or dissolving chronic stress granules rescue nuclear tdp-43 and correct aberrant splicing?
How generalizable is the transdifferentiation “aged” phenotype across neuron subtypes and to sporadic (non-familial) neurodegeneration?

wiki/sources/richards-2015-standards-and.title: richards-2015-standards-and-guidelines-for
wiki/sources/richards-2015-standards-and.content:
Standards and Guidelines for the Interpretation of Sequence Variants: A Joint Consensus Recommendation of the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics and the Association for Molecular Pathology
One-line: The foundational ACMG-AMP consensus that standardized how clinical laboratories classify Mendelian sequence variants into five significance tiers using weighted evidence criteria — see acmg-amp-guidelines.
Key takeaways

Establishes a five-tier terminology — pathogenic, likely pathogenic, uncertain significance, likely benign, benign — recommending that “mutation” and “polymorphism” be replaced by “variant” plus a modifier, always reported with respect to a condition and inheritance pattern.
Provides two criteria sets: pathogenic (very strong PVS1, strong PS1-4, moderate PM1-6, supporting PP1-5) and benign (stand-alone BA1, strong BS1-4, supporting BP1-6), combined via scoring rules into the acmg-amp-guidelines classification.
Frames evidence types including population frequency, computational prediction, functional studies, segregation, and de novo status; it is the reference framework for variant-effect-prediction and mutation-effect-prediction in clinical genetics.
Explicitly scoped to germline, primarily Mendelian variants; not for somatic, pharmacogenomic, or complex non-Mendelian variants.

Notable claims / data

Developed by a 2013 workgroup of ACMG, AMP, and CAP members, informed by two community surveys (over 100 labs solicited; responses from over 45 then over 33 labs) plus workshops with audience-response weighting.
“Likely pathogenic” and “likely benign” are defined as greater than 90% certainty — a common but explicitly arbitrary threshold, slightly lower than the IARC 95% level, chosen because clinicians tolerated a marginally higher error rate.
Most missense-prediction algorithms are only 65-80% accurate against known disease variants and tend toward low specificity (over-prediction of deleteriousness); splice-site predictors show ~90-100% sensitivity but only ~60-80% specificity.
Because in silico tools share underlying data, predictions from multiple programs count as a single combined evidence piece, and should never be the sole basis for a clinical assertion.
Population-frequency thresholds: allele frequency above 5% is stand-alone benign support (BA1); frequency greater than expected for the disorder is strong benign support (BS1).
PVS1 (null variants) requires caution: last-exon or last-50bp penultimate-exon truncations may escape nonsense-mediated decay, and alternatively-spliced or transcript-confined exons complicate interpretation.
BP7 addresses synonymous variants — splicing defects beyond the canonical splice-sites consensus can be pathogenic, so synonymous changes are only likely benign when computational splice prediction (BP4) shows no impact.

Connections

Serves as the clinical downstream framework that motivates variant-effect-prediction and mutation-effect-prediction tools; scores like cadd-score and predictors such as spliceai feed the PP3/BP4 computational-evidence criteria.
Its treatment of noncoding-variants and splice-disrupting changes connects to splicing-and-disease and the assessment of splice-sites.
Recommends deposition of variant classifications and evidence into shared databases to improve inter-laboratory concordance.

Open questions

How to move from expert-weighted qualitative criteria toward quantitative, calibrated probabilities of pathogenicity for each of the five tiers.
How the framework should evolve for genes and diseases where criterion applicability and weight vary, and for non-coding and complex-trait variants outside its intended Mendelian scope.
How to reconcile discordant classifications across laboratories and databases that lack primary evidence review.

wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.title: rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing
wiki/sources/rogalska-2022-regulation-of.content:
Regulation of pre-mRNA splicing: roles in physiology and disease, and therapeutic prospects
One-line: A review of recent advances in the molecular mechanisms that distinguish introns from exons and regulate alternative-splicing, connecting these to human disease and to the first generation of splicing-therapeutics.
Key takeaways

Intron removal is carried out by the spliceosome, comprising 5 snRNP complexes (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) plus more than 150 additional proteins, through a two-step transesterification reaction; cryo-EM has now captured snapshots of splicing at many assembly and catalytic steps. A principle of accuracy is that intron boundaries are recognized multiple times.
splice-sites in higher eukaryotes are remarkably diverse and often resemble non-splice-site sequences, so recognition depends on base pairing of splicing-factors/snRNPs plus context; U1 snRNA base-pairing defines 5’ss “strength”, and BP/PPT/3’ss recognition by SF1, U2AF1/U2AF2 and U2 snRNP (SF3B1/PHF5A) defines the 3’ end.
splicing-regulatory-elements (ESE/ESS, ISE/ISS) recruited by rna-binding-proteins (SR proteins, hnRNPs, RBM, CELF) tune splice-site recognition and exon definition; the same motif can act positively or negatively depending on position (the splicing-code).
Mutations in pre-mRNAs and core splicing-factors drive splicing-and-disease: genetic disorders, neurodegeneration and cancer (SF3B1, U2AF1/2, U1 snRNA mutations).
splicing-therapeutics are now clinical reality: antisense oligonucleotides (nusinersen, eteplirsen), the small molecule risdiplam, engineered U1 snRNAs, and SF3B1/RBM39-targeting drugs in oncology.
cotranscriptional-splicing is widespread but not universal; Pol II elongation rate, splicing-and-chromatin (histone marks, nucleosome positioning, CTD), nuclear compartmentalization, RNA modifications and RNA structure all shape outcomes — alongside post-transcriptional-splicing (e.g. detained introns).

Notable claims / data

Massively parallel assay of all 32,768 possible NNN/GYNNNN 5’ss sequences confirmed U1-base-pairing relevance but revealed strong context effects; ~90% of BRCA2 5’ss mutations in breast cancer affect splicing. G at +1 is required for catalysis; pyrimidine at +2 present in 99.6% of 5’ss.
Spinal muscular atrophy: caused by loss of SMN1; nusinersen (approved 2016) blocks an ISS to boost SMN2 exon 7 inclusion via quarterly intrathecal injection. Risdiplam (FDA approval 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone, “repairs” the U1–SMN2 exon 7 bulge by aiding U1C zinc-finger contact.
Familial dysautonomia: IKBKAP intron 20 +6 mutation causes exon skipping; therapeutic strategies include ASOs, modified U1 snRNAs, kinetin, and RECTAS (enhances SRSF6 phosphorylation).
SF3B1 mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts, 15–36% of eye melanomas, and are among the most common single mutations in CLL; K700E correlates with worse prognosis in CLL but better in MDS. SF3B1 mutations activate cryptic 3’ss 10–30 nt upstream and converge on repressing tumour suppressor BRD9 via a “poison exon”.
U2AF1 S34F/Y vs Q157P/R mutants change which flanking-nucleotide 3’ss are selected. SF3B1 inhibitors (H3B-8800, in trials; pladienolide B; spliceostatin A; sudemycins) block the open→closed BP-recognition conformational change; indisulam degrades RBM39 (synthetic lethality).
Splicing modulators generate tumour neoantigens, combinable with anti-PD1 checkpoint blockade. Eteplirsen targets ESEs to skip a mutant dystrophin exon in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, restoring an in-frame shorter functional protein. Pladienolide B reprograms mouse pluripotent cells to totipotent blastomere-like cells.
Two-thirds of all possible mutations in an alternative exon affect inclusion (vs constitutive exons). Internal exons are constrained to ~50–250 nt by exon definition; microexons (~3–27 nt) use SRRM4-coordinated recognition relevant to autism.
m6A regulates splicing: at 3’ss AG it inhibits U2AF1 binding (SAM-synthetase feedback in C. elegans and via METTL16 in humans); readers YTHDC1 (recruits SRSF3, antagonizes SRSF10) and hnRNPG/RBMX modulate exon inclusion.

Connections

alternative-splicing diversifies the proteome and regulates expression via PTC introduction and NMD (~30% of AS events introduce PTCs); links to splicing-and-evolution (joint evolution of splicing and protein codes; circular RNAs from back splicing).
splicing-code prediction: decades of tools from SpliceSiteFinder (1987) through SpliceAI, MMSplice, Pangolin; saturation mutagenesis and AI methods are the path forward.
splicing-and-chromatin: MRG15 links H3K36me3 to PTB recruitment; genome-edited histone marks induce splicing changes during EMT.

Open questions

How widespread is the functional relevance of alternative-splicing versus transcriptome noise, and how much variability exists cell-to-cell?
Do introns have autonomous functions beyond being removed by-products?
How general is cotranscriptional-splicing, and do cell-type- or substrate-specific spliceosomes exist?
How can splice sites be reliably predicted from genomic sequence, and how do lncRNAs contribute to splicing regulation?

wiki/sources/rolland-2023-phenotypic-eff.title: rolland-2023-phenotypic-effects-of-genetic-variants
wiki/sources/rolland-2023-phenotypic-eff.content:
Phenotypic effects of genetic variants associated with autism
One-line: Across 13,000+ autistic and 210,000+ undiagnosed individuals, rare loss-of-function variants in autism genes carry small but significant effects on cognition and socioeconomic status even in people without an autism diagnosis.
Key takeaways

Reframes autism genetics away from a categorical diagnosis: the same rare loss-of-function (LoF) variants that raise autism odds also shift cognitive and socioeconomic traits in undiagnosed carriers, arguing for studying variant effects beyond the clinical label.
