The discovery of splicing arose from comparing late adenovirus cytoplasmic mRNAs to the viral DNA, revealing that the mRNA body was assembled from non-contiguous DNA segments (Berget et al., 1977; Chow et al., 1977) — establishing the “split gene” structure and explaining the earlier hnRNA phenomenon.
Introns are pervasive in cellular genes (the average gene has ~8 introns; the primary transcript is typically 4-10x larger than the final mRNA) and bounded by a limited set of conserved consensus sequences common to vertebrate, plant, and yeast cells.
alternative-splicing lets one gene encode many proteins: ~1 in 20 genes is alternatively spliced, and the fibronectin gene alone yields ~20 distinct proteins through cell-type-specific exon inclusion/skipping.
The spliceosome is an ~60S complex assembling a substrate pre-mRNA with snRNPs (U1, U2, U4, U5, U6), splicing pre-mRNA in two transesterification steps through a lariat intermediate (2’-5’ branch at an intron adenosine).
Spliceosomal splicing chemically and evolutionarily resembles self-splicing group I and especially group II introns; the snRNAs may function as “a group II intron in pieces” forming the catalytic core.

Notable claims / data

~25% of human globin-gene mutations underlying thalassemia disrupt sequences specifying correct RNA splicing — splicing fidelity is a genetic constraint (splicing-and-disease).
~100 yeast genes (~2% of the genome) encode products important for splicing; temperature-sensitive PRP (pre-RNA-processing) mutants accumulate unspliced pre-mRNA and define specific transitions in the spliceosome cycle.
The spliceosome assembly cycle proceeds through biochemically distinguished complexes CC (commitment) → A → B1 → B2 → C1 → C2 → I, with U2 binding stabilized by the polypyrimidine-tract-binding protein U2AF (Arg-Ser-rich) and the U1-70 kDa protein.
Stereochemistry with chiral phosphorothioates (Moore & Sharp, 1993): both splicing steps are single transesterifications with inversion, and the Sp diastereomer is active in BOTH steps — implying two distinct catalytic centers (unlike group I, where the two steps are forward/reverse at a single center).
Pyruvate kinase has 9 coding-region introns in chicken but none in bacteria/yeast; intron positions are non-random relative to protein secondary structure and one is shared with alcohol dehydrogenase, supporting an exon-shuffling / ancient split-gene “progenote” hypothesis.
snRNPs concentrate in 20-50 nuclear “speckles” and in coiled bodies, are tethered to a nuclear matrix, and pre-RNA appears to be processed as it moves along curvilinear tracks toward nuclear pores (~4000 pores per mammalian nucleus).

Connections

Foundational ancestor to the entire splicing topic: it predates and underpins later mechanistic and regulatory reviews like lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
The Arg-Ser (SR) splicing factors it describes (SF2/ASF, SC35) connect to the splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins entities such as srsf1; U2AF anticipates u2af1.
The splice-sites consensus and U1/U2 base-pairing it lays out is the conceptual basis for modern splicing-code and spliceai work.
Its evolutionary argument relates to splicing-and-evolution and exon-shuffling themes echoed in wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.

Open questions

Why have introns persisted during evolution, given they drift rapidly and can be lost via cDNA-mediated gene conversion?
What is the mechanism transporting spliced mRNA from sites of transcription/splicing to nuclear pores?
How do the hypothetical PRP protein functions (RNA helicases, RNA-binding, protein-protein interaction) map onto specific spliceosome-cycle transitions?
Do U2AF and U1-70 kDa communicate directly across the intron during commitment-complex formation?

wiki/sources/shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-.title: shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection
wiki/sources/shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-.content:
rMATS: Robust and flexible detection of differential alternative splicing from replicate RNA-Seq data
One-line: The methods paper for rMATS — a hierarchical statistical model
that detects differential alternative-splicing from replicate RNA-seq
data by jointly modeling within-replicate estimation uncertainty and
between-replicate variability, and the canonical event-based ΔPSI
tool referenced throughout splicing-methods. See entity rmats.
Key takeaways

The gap it fills. First-generation tools (MISO, SpliceTrap, ALEXA-seq,
rSeqDiff) only compare two samples with no replicates; replicate-aware tools
(Cufflinks, FDM, DiffSplice, DEXSeq) fail to model the estimation
uncertainty of isoform proportion in individual replicates, and none
handle paired designs. rMATS targets both.
Lineage. rMATS = replicate MATS, the successor to MATS (Shen et al. 2012,
a Bayesian two-sample method); it replaces sampling-based p-values with a
faster likelihood-ratio test.
Hierarchical model. Two stacked layers per event:
a binomial for read-count–driven estimation uncertainty within each
replicate, and a logit-normal for variability among replicates —
estimated simultaneously. The metric is the exon inclusion level ψ
(percent-spliced-in).
Length normalization → all event types. A function f(ψ) corrects for the
effective lengths of the inclusion vs skipping isoforms, letting rMATS use both
splice-junction reads and exon-body reads and handle all five canonical AS
event types: skipped exon (SE), alternative 5′/3′ splice-sites (A5SS/A3SS),
mutually exclusive exons (MXE), and retained intron (RI).
Paired model. A bivariate-normal layer with an exon-specific correlation
ρᵢ models matched pairs (e.g. case–control / tumor–normal); it reduces to the
unpaired model when ρ=0 and boosts power for correlated pairs.
Flexible hypothesis test. A likelihood-ratio test for |Δψ| > c where the
threshold c is user-defined (e.g. 5%) — not merely the equality test
|Δψ| > 0 used by every other method. This tests for splicing changes of a
chosen magnitude.

Notable claims / data

Real-data validation (unpaired). Prostate cancer cell lines PC3E
(epithelial) vs GS689 (mesenchymal), 6 unpaired replicates, 746M paired reads →
721 differential AS events at |Δψ| > 5%, FDR ≤ 1%. RT-PCR on 34 events
validated 32/34 = 94%; RNA-seq vs RT-PCR Δψ correlation r = 0.96.
Paired model (TCGA ccRCC). 65 tumor–normal matched pairs → paired rMATS
found 315 differential exon-skipping events vs 304 for unpaired (a
superset); the 11 paired-only events are subtle-but-consistent (smaller |Δψ|
but also smaller cross-pair SD). Tumor splicing resembled the mesenchymal GS689
profile (consistent with ccRCC’s mesenchymal phenotype).
Benchmark vs JSD methods. rMATS AUC 86% > Cufflinks 83% >
DiffSplice 81%; the gap widens at low false-positive rate and with
outlier/low-coverage replicates (TPR improvement up to +19 pp). Mechanism:
rMATS down-weights low-count replicates, whereas Jensen–Shannon-divergence
methods weight all replicates equally regardless of read depth.
Experimental-design findings (a second contribution beyond the tool):

Replicates are essential; pooling/merging RNA before sequencing is
not recommended — at fixed total reads, replicate analysis always beats
pooled, and pooled data is highly sensitive to outliers.
The optimal sample-size vs sequencing-depth trade-off depends on
within-group variability: at low SD (0.01–0.02) ~3 replicates suffice; at
high SD more replicates are needed (up to +31% power). Over-splitting a
fixed budget into too many replicates reduces power by raising per-replicate
uncertainty. A low-coverage pilot to estimate variability is advised.


I/O. Inputs: raw reads + genome + transcript annotation (TopHat used for
mapping in the paper); outputs per-event P value + FDR. Source:
rnaseq-mats.sourceforge.net.

Connections

Primary source for splicing-methods — the “rMATS-style event callers”
already referenced there; the empirical, event-based counterpart to the
long-read / single-cell toolkit in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Defines the working metric percent-spliced-in (ψ/PSI, ΔPSI) used across
the splicing literature in this wiki.
Cancer splicing — its EMT (epithelial↔mesenchymal) and tumor–normal use
cases connect to splicing-and-disease.
Complements, does not overlap, the sequence-prediction line. rMATS
quantifies splicing from observed reads; splicing-code models
(spliceai, from capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the)
predict splicing from sequence — measurement vs prediction.

Open questions

Two-isoform limit. rMATS models two isoforms per region; the authors note
the binomial could be generalized to a multinomial for complex events
yielding >2 isoforms.
Bias correction. rMATS uses raw counts; prior bias-correction methods gave
no improvement in their hands, but the framework can ingest adjusted counts if
shown to help.
Beyond splicing. The isoform-ratio framework is claimed to extend to
alternative polyadenylation and RNA editing — untested here.

wiki/sources/shen-2026-advances-and-chal.title: shen-2026-advances-and-challenges-of-splicing
wiki/sources/shen-2026-advances-and-chal.content:
Advances and challenges of splicing prediction with AI
One-line: A 2026 Nature Genetics review tracing the evolution of computational splicing-code models from statistical heuristics to AI foundation models, dissecting the design axes (training data scale, output resolution, event quantification, model complexity) that shape predictive performance and translational utility.
Key takeaways

Frames the history of alternative-splicing prediction as five architectural eras: probabilistic/machine-learning motif models → CNNs → transformers → genomic foundation models, and organizes every tool by two axes — input context window and output predictive scope (splice-site vs acceptor/donor vs tissue/cell-specific).
Splicing prediction is a five-stage deep-learning pipeline: (1) input formulation over the raw A/C/G/T(U) alphabet, (2) sequence encoding (one-hot vs dense/tokenized embeddings), (3) representation learning via CNN / transformer / hybrid / foundation architectures, (4) mapping features to biological outputs (splice-site probability or percent-spliced-in), and optionally (5) variant-effect-prediction via in silico mutagenesis.
Output resolution is a fundamental design choice with three tiers — sequence-level (one score per input, e.g. MaxEntScan’s 9 bp window), segment-level (per-word/bin scores, e.g. GENA-LM, Borzoi’s 32-bp bins), and nucleotide-level (per-base, e.g. spliceai, SpTransformer, Pangolin, AlphaGenome) — the last being ideal for resolving closely spaced splice-sites but treating each base independently, hindering isoform reconstruction.
Event quantification ranges from categorical labels (splice site yes/no; acceptor vs donor) to continuous metrics (percent-spliced-in, splice-site strength/SSE, RNA-seq coverage profiles), with tissue-specificity quantified via z-score frameworks (SpTransformer across 15 tissues) or usage filters (MTSplice).
Model scale has exploded from ~0.7 M parameters (spliceai) to 0.5–2.5 B for foundation models (nucleotide-transformer), with training cost rising from two consumer GPUs (SpliceAI) to 64 H100s (AlphaGenome) — but the authors’ own benchmarking finds larger models do NOT guarantee better splicing prediction; smaller task-specific models often remain preferable.
Input context has grown from <9 bp to >10 kb–1 Mb to capture distal splicing-regulatory-elements, enabled by dilated convolutions (spliceai), linear-complexity attention (SpTransformer’s Sinkhorn transformer), and tokenization schemes (k-mer, Byte Pair Encoding in GENA-LM) that trade single-nucleotide resolution for scalability.
Applications span splicing-and-disease variant annotation (10–30% of pathogenic mutations affect splicing), splice-driven neoantigen discovery for immuno-oncology (NeoSplice, SpliceMutr), and splicing-therapeutics design (ASO tools like Eskip-Finder; CRISPR-GPT for experiment automation).

Notable claims / data

spliceai uses a CNN-based ResNet with dilated convolutions (~0.7 M params), trained on two GTX 1080 Ti GPUs; its ΔScore classified 1,273,003 ClinVar SNVs at AUROC 0.98 (per SpTransformer re-analysis). SpliceAI’s original de novo analysis used mutations from 3,953 autism probands, 4,293 Deciphering Developmental Disorders probands, and 2,073 controls.
Pangolin combines 40 SpliceAI-like submodels (~28 M params total) across 116 samples spanning human, rhesus, mouse; SpTransformer draws from 17,382 GTEx samples across 53 tissues + 2 cell lines and flags tissue-specific events above the 99th percentile z-score.
nucleotide-transformer pretrained on 3,202 human genomes + 850 species; ranges 0.5–2.5 B params using 6-mer tokenization; trained on 128 A100s over 28 days across 16 nodes. AlphaGenome processes up to 1 Mb, compressing 128 bp per embedding unit.
Box 2 formalizes variant-effect scores: MaxEntScan uses log-odds over 9-nt (donor)/23-nt (acceptor) windows; SpliceAI’s ΔSplice = max of four Δ values (acceptor gain/loss, donor gain/loss); SpTransformer builds a tissue z-score null from 27,843 tissue-non-specific variants out of 734,509,842 GTEx SNVs.
Nusinersen (Spinraza) is cited as the flagship splicing-therapeutics success, restoring SMN2 exon 7 inclusion for spinal muscular atrophy; 11 ASO drugs are FDA-approved so far.

Connections

Provides a unifying map for the many splice-prediction tools already in the wiki: spliceai, absplice2, and would-be entity pages for SpTransformer, Pangolin, MMSplice, MaxEntScan, AlphaGenome, Borzoi, nucleotide-transformer, SpliceBERT, DNABERT, GENA-LM, CADD-Splice, MAGPIE, MTSplice.
Directly cites chiang-2022-mechanism-and-modeling-of-human (ref 41) as evidence that 10–30% of pathogenic mutations affect splicing — a Phase-A companion paper in this same ingest group.
Complements method-comparison pages like alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast and the splicing-methods concept; frames the splicing-code as the central modeling target.
Its “limitations” section (deep-intronic variants, isoform-level reconstruction, trans-acting/rna-binding-proteins context, multimodal integration) motivates long-read and RBP-CLIP approaches like eCLIP, Bambu, IsoQuant.

Open questions

Can any single architecture jointly capture cis-elements AND trans-regulatory context (RBP abundance, PTMs, tissue programs) that sequence-only models structurally miss?
How to reconstruct full-length isoforms from nucleotide-level splice-site calls — will integration with long-read-rna-seq (Bambu, FLAIR, IsoQuant) bridge the gap?
Deep-intronic variant prediction remains poor because training databases are biased toward junction-proximal mutations — how to close this data gap?
Does the field’s push toward billion-parameter foundation models actually pay off for splicing, given the authors’ own finding that smaller task-specific models often win on efficiency and accuracy?

wiki/sources/shin-2023-rare-variation-in.title: shin-2023-rare-variation-in-noncoding-regions
wiki/sources/shin-2023-rare-variation-in.content:
Rare variation in noncoding regions with evolutionary signatures contributes to autism spectrum disorder risk
One-line: Rare, likely-recessive inherited variants in three classes of evolutionarily-flagged noncoding regions (HARs, Vista Enhancers, conserved neural enhancers) contribute to ASD risk, with contribution strongly modulated by family structure.
Key takeaways

Examines three classes of noncoding regions as candidate ASD-risk cis-regulatory-elements: Human Accelerated Regions (HARs; human positive-selection signal), neural Vista Enhancers (VEs; experimentally validated), and conserved neural enhancers (CNEs; conserved + human-constrained + predicted brain enhancers) — spanning human-brain-evolution and cross-species conservation.
Rare, recessive, inherited noncoding-variants in all three classes are enriched in autism probands, but the effect size tracks family structure: largest in consanguineous families, smaller in male cases of non-consanguineous multiplex cohorts, and only detectable in females in simplex families.
Used campra (capture-based MPRA) to show VEs (24.4%) and CNEs (21.5%) act as enhancers in N2A cells more often than HARs (14.2%); a mutagenesis variant of the assay showed HAR single-nucleotide variants gain activity nearly as often as they lose it.
Functionally validated patient variants near ASD gene IL1RAPL1 (HAR3091, HAR3094) and neurobehavioral gene SIM1 (a VE) that alter enhancer activity; used crispri-crispra (dCas9-KRAB) to show HAR3094 regulates IL1RAPL1 in iPSC-derived neurons.
Implicates both human-evolved and evolutionarily-conserved regulatory regions in ASD, nominating a set of candidate patient variants and new candidate genes for further study.

Notable claims / data

Cohorts: >16,600 samples / >4,900 probands across HMCA (consanguineous, 193 probands / 662 individuals), NIMH (660 multiplex + 1,911 simplex probands), and SSC (Simons Simplex Collection; 2,167 probands / 8,186 individuals). Analyzed 3,171 HARs, 2,057 VEs, 7,386 CNEs.
HMCA (WGS, AF < 0.005, conserved bases): HAR OR = 2.142 (adj p = 0.001), VE OR = 2.074 (adj p = 0.004), CNE OR = 1.546 (adj p = 0.041). Estimated ASD contributions of ~9.9% / 11.1% / 10.0% for HARs / VEs / CNEs.
NIMH males (AF < 0.001): HAR OR = 1.262 (adj p = 0.050), VE OR = 1.255 (adj p = 0.030); CNEs not enriched. Estimated contribution 2.6% (HAR) / 3.7% (VE) — similar to the 3–5% from rare recessive coding variants, smaller than the ~10% in the consanguineous cohort. Larger effect in multiplex than simplex families.
SSC (simplex): essentially no enrichment overall; a significant excess only in HAR rare recessive variants in female cases (OR = 2.657, adj p = 0.044; ~1.7% contribution), consistent with a female protective effect.
caMPRA mutagenesis: 1,281 variants across 485 HARs; 81.5% no significant change, 10.8% increased, 7.6% decreased activity — contrasting prior mutagenesis studies where most changes decreased expression.
HARs/VEs/CNEs enriched near ASD-associated (SFARI) genes (adj p < 10^-29), DECIPHER developmental-disease genes, and dosage-sensitive loss-of-function-intolerant genes (pLI > 0.9; adj p < 10^-17). Chimp vs human HAR3091/HAR3094 differ in regional (telencephalon vs midbrain) enhancer activity, showing evolutionary rewiring.
Caveat: preprint (not peer-reviewed); enhancer assays in N2A cells / episomal reporters lack native chromatin context; individual elements are underpowered for element-level statistical significance.

Connections

Extends noncoding-variants and cis-regulatory-elements framing into recessive/inherited ASD risk, complementing the common-variant MPRA of lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric and regulatory-variant MPRA of deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory.
Shares massively-parallel-reporter-assay methodology (via the campra capture-based variant) with those studies but targets rare inherited noncoding variation rather than common GWAS variants.
Uses crispri-crispra for enhancer-gene validation, as in CRISPR CRE-mapping work such as groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at.

Open questions

Do HAR/VE/CNE recessive contributions replicate and reach element-level significance in larger consanguineous WGS cohorts?
Which specific target genes and cell types mediate the regulatory effects, and how do human-specific HAR activity changes causally alter social behavior?
How much ASD risk resides in noncoding classes beyond these three evolutionarily-defined sets, and via non-enhancer regulatory modalities?

wiki/sources/shine-2024-co-transcription.title: shine-2024-co-transcriptional-gene-regulation-in
wiki/sources/shine-2024-co-transcription.content:
Co-transcriptional gene regulation in eukaryotes and prokaryotes
One-line: A comparative review of how RNA processing — splicing, 3′-end cleavage/polyadenylation, capping, RNA folding — is coordinated with transcription in both eukaryotes and prokaryotes, framed around recent sequencing and imaging methods that capture transient nascent-RNA intermediates.
Key takeaways

Many RNA processing steps occur during transcription. In eukaryotes cotranscriptional-splicing is the central theme; the review draws striking parallels with prokaryotic co-transcriptional translation (ribosomes trailing RNA polymerase) and shows neither coupling is universal.
Two modes of co-transcriptional splicing recur across species: rapid splicing (exon ligation soon after the 3′ splice site emerges from Pol II) and delayed splicing (exon ligation after Pol II transcribes ~2 kb of downstream intron); these mirror prokaryotic “tightly coupled” versus “runaway transcription” translation modes.
New nascent-RNA long-read sequencing methods (LRS of nascent RNA, nano-COP, POINT-nano, CoLa-seq, dNET-seq) and metabolic-labelling/imaging approaches map Pol II position relative to splicing/cleavage status, allowing inference of event timing and order.
Transcription elongation rate shapes outcomes: the “kinetic competition” model links fast Pol II to alternative-exon skipping and slow Pol II to weak-splice-site recognition/exon inclusion; elongation speed also biases alternative polyadenylation (APA) site choice.
U1 snRNP emerges as a general Pol II elongation factor (beyond its splicing role): it suppresses premature cleavage/polyadenylation (telescripting) and propels Pol II through AT-rich introns, reframing intron–exon Pol II density differences as a GC-content effect.
RNA structure regulates processing co-transcriptionally; the only known eukaryotic riboswitches act on splicing, and heat-melting intron structures (e.g. tomato HSFA2) drive stress-responsive intron retention. Relevant to rna-structure-and-splicing.
Under cellular stress, 3′-end cleavage is suppressed, causing transcriptional readthrough and downstream-of-gene (DoG) transcripts — now considered a hallmark of the mammalian stress response.

Notable claims / data

Budding yeast: spliced fraction plateaus by the time Pol II is ~130 nt downstream of an intron (single-molecule intron tracking); a fast Pol II mutant substantially delays splicing completion.
A typical mammalian gene has 8–10 introns with internal exons of ~150 nt; introns constitute roughly 90% of the transcript that must be removed.
Mouse erythroleukaemia (MEL) cells: 75% of splice-junction-to-nearest-Pol-II distances are ≤300 nt (rapid splicing). CoLa-seq in human K562: most introns spliced before Pol II transcribes 1.5 kb downstream of the 3′ splice site.
POINT-nano (human) and dNET-seq (Drosophila): both rapid splicing and delayed splicing (~2 kb downstream) detected; nano-COP detected abundant splicing at 2 kb (human) and 4 kb (fly) downstream.
Operationally, the review sets thresholds: rapid co-transcriptional splicing while Pol II is <1.5 kb into the gene, delayed while >1.5 kb, and post-transcriptional after Pol II completes the gene (Fig. 2a). Caveat: distance-based and time-based (intron half-life) estimates cannot yet be directly compared owing to variable elongation rates.
U1 snRNP binding propels Pol II elongation ~six times faster over AT-rich introns than GC-rich exons; the ~sixfold elongation-rate range in mammals is better explained by GC vs AU content than by intron–exon architecture per se (TT-seq + PRO-seq “elongation index”).
Cryo-EM captured a human Pol II–DNA–nascent-RNA–U1 snRNP complex; the U1-70K subunit contacts RPB2/RPB12, and binding does not require a 5′ splice site in the nascent RNA.
“All-or-none” pre-mRNA processing: nascent transcripts are either fully spliced+cleaved or fully unspliced+uncleaved despite high overall efficiency (e.g. β-globin ~50/50); a β-thalassaemia IVSI-110 minigene mutation that strengthens a 3′ splice site increased both splicing and cleavage efficiency, evidencing splicing–cleavage coupling.
Methodological caveats flagged: metabolic labelling with uracil analogues enriches intron-containing transcripts and can slow splicing; PCR/size bias favours detection of rapidly spliced (shorter) products; reverse transcription limits study to ~6 kb transcripts; DoGs can reach up to 200 kb.
Prokaryotic contrasts: in B. subtilis RNA polymerase moves ~2× faster than ribosomes (“runaway transcription”), decoupling transcription and translation, contradicting the E. coli textbook tight-coupling model; <20% of E. coli operons have single defined start/end sites.

Connections

Anchors and complements cotranscriptional-splicing and post-transcriptional-splicing with concrete kinetic measurements and the rapid-vs-delayed dichotomy.
Strongly relevant to splicing-and-chromatin: histone post-translational modifications and nucleosome-induced Pol II pausing near the first 5′ splice site, FISH-detected stiff chromatin loops at transcribed long genes, and nuclear-periphery localization of long introns.
Connects to rna-structure-and-splicing (riboswitches, heat-melting intron structures, DMS/SHAPE probing) and to splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins (U1/U2 snRNP, SR proteins, PTB, U2AF, SF3B1, ELAV).
The spliceosome and splice-sites framing connects to mechanistic exon-definition and “first come, first served” / out-of-order splicing debates.
Among sibling sources, pairs naturally with rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of (mechanistic/regulatory reviews) and with sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying (methods, including the nascent-RNA LRS this review builds on).
The sf3b1 entity is directly invoked: CDK11 phosphorylates SF3B1 (U2 snRNP branchpoint selection), tying transcript elongation to splice-site definition.

Open questions

What molecular mechanism enforces all-or-none pre-mRNA processing and coordinates splicing across multiple introns within one transcript?
Can distance-based (Pol II position) and time-based (metabolic labelling) splicing measurements be unified, and can local Pol II elongation rates be measured in absolute time units in vivo?
Does pre-mRNA processing feed back on Pol II in eukaryotes the way co-transcriptional translation feeds back on RNA polymerase in prokaryotes?
How are long introns spliced — do current LRS methods (length-biased toward short transcripts) underestimate delayed/long-intron splicing?
Does intramolecular base pairing in nascent RNA block cleavage-site recognition, and do DoG intergenic regions have intron–exon structure / undergo splicing?
How general is post-transcriptional 3′-end cleavage (observed upon DNA damage) across biological contexts?

wiki/sources/shukla-2011-ctcf-promoted-r.title: shukla-2011-ctcf-promoted-rna-polymerase-ii-pausing
wiki/sources/shukla-2011-ctcf-promoted-r.content:
CTCF-promoted RNA polymerase II pausing links DNA methylation to splicing
One-line: Provides the first evidence that the DNA-binding protein CTCF promotes inclusion of weak upstream exons by inducing local RNA Pol II pausing, and that DNA methylation — by blocking CTCF binding — reciprocally controls splicing, giving a mechanistic link between heritable epigenetic marks, transcription elongation, and alternative-splicing outcomes.
Key takeaways

Establishes that CTCF (CCCTC-binding factor) can directly regulate alternative-splicing by binding within a gene body and promoting inclusion of a weak upstream exon — a “non-insulator” intragenic function of CTCF (splicing-and-chromatin).
The mechanism is local RNA Pol II pausing: CTCF binding at an exon acts as a roadblock that slows elongating Pol II, giving the co-transcriptional spliceosome more time to recognize the upstream weak splice site — a concrete instance of the kinetic-coupling model of cotranscriptional-splicing.
Demonstrated in the well-studied CD45 (PTPRC) model of developmentally regulated exon 5 inclusion: CTCF binds CD45 exon 5 DNA and promotes exon 5 inclusion; CTCF depletion reduces exon 5 inclusion and reduces Pol II at that site.
DNA methylation inhibits CTCF binding (5-methylcytosine at CpGs in the exon 5 CTCF site), so methylation and CTCF binding have reciprocal effects on exon 5 inclusion — connecting dna-methylation to splicing outcomes.
This provides a mechanistic basis for developmental / heritable epigenetic regulation of splicing: differential DNA methylation during lymphocyte maturation retunes intragenic CTCF binding and thereby exon inclusion.
The effect is genome-wide, not CD45-specific: CTCF ChIP-seq + RNA-seq show CTCF bound downstream of an alternative exon generally promotes that exon’s inclusion, providing temporal context for spliceosome assembly at weak upstream splice sites.
In vitro reconstitution shows CTCF binding to a Pol II ternary elongation complex on naked DNA is sufficient to cause pausing (relieved by elongation factor TFIIS), establishing CTCF as an autonomous, context-independent impediment to transcription.

Notable claims / data

CTCF ChIP-qPCR: strong CTCF binding at CD45 exon 5 in cells transcribing exon-5-containing isoforms (murine splenocytes, RBhigh cells); binding correlates with exon 5 inclusion (RBhigh vs RBlow B-cell lines).
CTCF knockdown (shRNA) in bimodal BL41 and BJAB B cells reduced CD45RB expression and exon 5 inclusion, and reduced Pol II (elongating Ser2-phospho CTD) at exon 5 — without changing overall CD45 levels.
In vitro transcription with a purified yeast Pol II elongation complex + recombinant CTCF on a synthetic template: CTCF binding causes Pol II pausing immediately upstream of the CTCF site; TFIIS substantially reduces pausing — showing CTCF can autonomously promote (but not fully arrest) pausing without nucleosomes/chromatin.
CD45 mini-gene with mutated exon 5 CTCF site (I3-I7*CTCF) lost CTCF binding, showed reduced exon 4/5 and 5/6 junctions and increased 4/6 skipping, and lost Pol II enrichment at exon 5 — genetically linking CTCF site → pausing → inclusion.
MedIP: 5-methylcytosine is enriched at CD45 exon 5 in RBlow (exon-5-excluding) cells and in mature CD45RO⁺ primary T cells, inversely correlated with CTCF binding; DNMT1 depletion reduced methylation, restored CTCF binding, restored Pol II pausing, and enhanced exon 5 inclusion.
Genome-wide (CTCF ChIP-seq + RNA-seq, MISO in BL41/BJAB, plus CD4⁺ T-cell Pol II data): CTCF bound within ~1 kb downstream of an alternative exon is associated with increased inclusion of that exon; Pol II is more enriched at downstream CTCF sites, consistent with the pausing model.
~40–45% of CTCF binding sites are intragenic, and CTCF ChIP-seq shows ~40–70% conservation across tissues — implying broad, tissue-variable potential to influence splicing.

Connections

A foundational splicing-and-chromatin / cotranscriptional-splicing source: shows a DNA-binding/epigenetic factor controlling splicing through Pol II elongation kinetics, complementary to braunschweig-2014-widespread-intron-retention-in-mammals (Pol II pausing ↔ intron retention) — both invoke elongation-rate control of splicing.
Directly connects dna-methylation to alternative-splicing, adding an epigenetic axis to the splicing-code beyond RNA cis-elements (cf. barash-2010-deciphering-the-splicing-code).
Uses the CD45/PTPRC exon 5 system, a classic model of lymphocyte-development-regulated splicing; introduces the entity ctcf and the CD45 splicing model to the wiki.
Supports the kinetic-coupling / “window of opportunity” model in which slow elongation favors weak-splice-site (upstream exon) inclusion — relevant to splice-sites strength and splicing-factors recruitment timing.
Relevant to splicing-and-disease: aberrant DNA methylation and CTCF binding in cancer could redirect splicing via this mechanism (authors note widespread methylation/CTCF changes in cancer).

Open questions

How general is CTCF-mediated splicing control across the genome, and what fraction of tissue-specific alternative exons depend on intragenic CTCF pausing?
Does DNA methylation dynamically retune CTCF-dependent splicing in normal development beyond CD45 / lymphocyte maturation?
How does CTCF-induced pausing integrate with histone marks and nucleosome positioning at exons to set the final splicing outcome?
To what extent does aberrant methylation/CTCF binding in cancer drive pathological splicing, and is it therapeutically targetable?

wiki/sources/sibley-2016-lessons-from-no.title: sibley-2016-lessons-from-non-canonical-splicing
wiki/sources/sibley-2016-lessons-from-no.content:
Lessons from non-canonical splicing
One-line: A review cataloguing the many “non-canonical” splicing events revealed by improved transcriptome sequencing — cryptic exons, microexons, recursive splicing, retained introns, exitrons, circular RNAs, chimeric/trans-spliced transcripts and atypical splice sites — their mechanisms, evolutionary role as a source of new transcripts (especially from transposable elements), their quality control, and their relevance to disease and therapy.
Key takeaways

Non-canonical splicing covers events that use cryptic splice-sites located far from annotated exons, or that deviate from the standard rules: cryptic exons, microexons, recursive splice sites, retained introns, exitrons, circular RNAs (circRNAs), chimeric RNAs and atypical splice sites. New RNA-seq methods + tailored aligners revealed these on a transcriptome-wide scale.
Exon definition — combinatorial recognition by U1/U2 snRNPs of the spliceosome, the U2AF complex and SR proteins bound to exonic enhancers — maintains fidelity and explains why most cryptic splice sites do not fire; many non-canonical events require unconventional exon definition.
Cryptic exons frequently arise from transposable elements (TEs), especially antisense Alu elements whose poly(U) tracts recruit splicing factors (U2AF2, TIA). They are repressed by RBPs (HNRNPC, NOVA, RBFOX2, TDP43); loss of repression can convert them into regulated alternative-splicing exons. Many introduce premature termination codons (PTCs) and are degraded by nonsense-mediated decay (NMD).
Microexons (<30 nt) are preferentially included in neurons (~60%), conserved, length usually a multiple of three (preserving the ORF), and enriched in synapse/axonogenesis genes; promoted by SRRM4/nSR100, which binds the long polypyrimidine tract upstream. SRRM4 is reduced in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), lowering microexon inclusion.
Recursive splicing removes a long intron in steps via recursive splice (RS) sites — a 3′ then reconstituted 5′ splice site — and in vertebrates requires a cryptic “RS exon” for exon definition. Found in extremely long introns of brain-expressed axon-guidance/cell-adhesion genes.
Retained introns result from inefficient splicing (intron-retention); characterized by weak splice sites, high GC content, short length; can retain mRNA in the nucleus, trigger NMD, or yield truncated proteins, and serve gene-regulatory roles (e.g., granulocyte differentiation).
Exitrons are introns within annotated protein-coding exons (cryptic introns); their removal changes protein structure or causes frameshifts rather than typically introducing PTCs.
circRNAs form by back-splicing (head-to-tail), promoted by flanking inverted Alu repeats or RBPs (QKI, MBNL); types include exonic, exon–intron and intronic circRNAs. Mostly rare, some highly tissue-specific (e.g., neuronal dendrites). CDR1-AS/ciRS-7 sponges miR-7 via ≥63 sites.
Chimeric RNAs arise from cis-splicing (read-through of adjacent genes) or trans-splicing (exons from distant loci); the same chimeras can come from physiological trans-splicing or, in cancer, from chromosomal fusions (JAZF1–SUZ12, PAX3–FOXO1).
Atypical splice sites: >99% of introns use the major U2 spliceosome; the minor U12 spliceosome has longer 5′SS/branch-point consensus; GC-5′SS are the commonest atypical U2 sites; A-to-I editing and shifted/bulged snRNA base-pairing also enable recognition.

Notable claims / data

Introns make up ~23% of the human genome; >500,000 non-annotated splicing events found in mouse/human RNA-seq.


330,000 antisense Alu elements sit in introns of protein-coding genes; TEs comprise up to two-thirds of the genome; >1.5 million degenerated LINE sequences but fewer than 100 retrotransposition-competent.


Recursive splicing: 197 RS sites in Drosophila, 11 confirmed in humans (419 putative before requiring co-transcriptional “saw-tooth” patterns) — likely underestimates.
923 exitrons identified within annotated human protein-coding exons.
~1/3 of disease-causing mutations disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (likely an underestimate; excludes synonymous exonic variants). About half of disease-linked cryptic exons derive from TEs (mainly Alu).
Disease examples: U1 snRNP repressive-site deletions activate cryptic exons in ataxia telangiectasia (ATM) and Laron syndrome; Alu U-tract deletion in PTS → hyperphenylalaninaemia; cryptic Alu exon in DMD → Duchenne muscular dystrophy; HTT triplet repeats activate a cryptic polyadenylation site → toxic exon-1 peptide in Huntington disease; TDP43 loss derepresses cryptic exons (ATG4B) in ALS-FTD; minor-spliceosome mutations cause U12-intron retention diseases.
Cancer: widespread intron retention (linked to spliceosome competition under high transcription, tumour-suppressor inactivation, SF3B1/U2AF1 mutations causing cryptic 3′SS via alternative branch points); MYC-driven tumours are vulnerable to spliceosome inhibition (apoptosis), a therapeutic angle.
Therapies: splicing-factor modulation; antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) and modified U snRNAs to redirect splicing (β-thalassaemia, SMA); bifunctional ASOs; CRISPR-Cas9 exon skipping; spliceosome-mediated RNA trans-splicing (SMaRT); engineered artificial circRNAs as sponges/aptamers/ribozymes.

Connections

Anchors the “non-canonical splicing” axis around the core spliceosome, splice-sites, alternative-splicing and intron-retention concept pages.
Quality-control/NMD and disease themes overlap with scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Co-transcriptional “saw-tooth” detection of recursive splicing connects to ameur-2011-total-rna-sequencing-reveals.
Senior author Jernej Ule; first author Sibley also led the recursive-splicing discovery (Sibley et al., Nature 2015) cited within.

Open questions

How many RS sites genuinely regulate alternative splicing, and what is recursive splicing’s physiological function (steric blocking did not reduce overall long-intron splicing)?
What are the disease roles of circRNAs and RS-site variants, still largely unexamined?
How did conserved RBPs (e.g., HNRNPC) govern the transition of TE-derived cryptic exons into regulated alternative exons across vertebrate evolution?
Genome-wide (not just exome) sequencing is needed to capture deep-intronic variants activating cryptic splicing; and how chromatin/transcription kinetics couple to non-canonical splicing control.

wiki/sources/singhal-2024-banksy-unifies.title: singhal-2024-banksy-unifies-cell-typing
wiki/sources/singhal-2024-banksy-unifies.content:
BANKSY unifies cell typing and tissue domain segmentation for scalable spatial omics data analysis
One-line: banksy is a spatial-clustering algorithm that solves both cell typing and tissue-domain segmentation in one framework by augmenting each cell’s own transcriptome with a descriptor of its local neighborhood, and it scales to millions of cells.
Key takeaways

BANKSY embeds each cell in a product space combining its own expression with two neighborhood features — the weighted mean neighborhood expression and an azimuthal Gabor filter (AGF) that captures the local expression gradient and is invariant to sample rotation — representing cell state and microenvironment respectively.
A single mixing parameter λ ∈ [0, 1] switches modes: small λ emphasizes a cell’s own transcriptome (cell typing; λ = 0 reduces to conventional nonspatial clustering), while larger λ weights the neighborhood signature to cluster cells by tissue domain. This unifies two previously distinct spatial-clustering problems within one machine-learning framework.
Because cells of the same type need not be physically proximal, BANKSY handles intermingled and spatially separated cell types, unlike methods that penalize distant cells for being dissimilar.
After feature augmentation, dimensionality reduction and graph construction, clustering uses scalable graph-partitioning (Leiden by default; Louvain, model-based and k-means also offered), giving effectively linear O(n) complexity in cell number — the same as nonspatial clustering.
Validated across diverse modalities: RNA imaging (MERFISH, MERSCOPE, VeraFISH, STARmap), RNA sequencing (Slide-seq v1/v2, Visium, CosMx) and protein imaging (CODEX). Relevant to spatial multimodal-learning and neuronal-cell-types mapping.
Interoperable with Seurat (R), SingleCellExperiment (R) and SCANPY (Python) workflows.

Notable claims / data

On Slide-seq/Slide-seq v2 mouse cerebellum, BANKSY recovered eight cell-type clusters matching RCTD deconvolution (vs six for nonspatial clustering) without a reference dataset, and clusters showed higher point-biserial correlation with RCTD weights and lower cross-connectivity (more contiguous layers).
Detected previously undescribed niche-dependent states: split mature oligodendrocytes into white-matter (MOD-wm) vs gray-matter (MOD-gm) subtypes in MERFISH mouse hypothalamus; found cycling epithelial cells in a 677,451-cell MERSCOPE human colon tumor dataset too large for competing methods; identified submucosa-enriched M2 macrophages in human colon CosMx.
Domain segmentation on the 12-section Visium human DLPFC benchmark: BANKSY was statistically tied with the top deep-learning methods GraphST and STAGATE by median ARI and beat Giotto, BayesSpace, SpaGCN and SpiceMix. On STARmap 1,020-gene mouse visual cortex it had the highest ARI (0.720). On simulated data it exceeded ARI 0.8 on domain finding across all gene subsets (others below 0.6) and was the most accurate for cell typing.
Unlike the deep-learning methods (SpaGCN, STAGATE, GraphST), BANKSY was highly robust to random-seed choice, and robust across sweeps of λ, kexpr, kgeom and principal-component count.
Scalability (16-CPU 128-GB machine): BANKSY was the only method reaching 2 million cells within 16 h; summarized as 10–1,000× faster and 2–60× more scalable than existing spatial clustering algorithms, and on CPU it was faster and 8× more scalable than GPU-run competitors.
Additional uses: spatial quality control (flagging necrotic/out-of-focus regions), and spatially aware batch correction (BANKSY → Harmony on 12 DLPFC datasets outperformed BayesSpace with/without Harmony).

Connections

A spatial extension of single-cell rna-seq clustering; the neighborhood-augmentation idea is explicitly analogized to using context to define word meaning in NLP (word2vec-style embeddings), a form of spatial multimodal-learning.
Refines mapping of neuronal-cell-types by separating anatomically distinct but transcriptomically similar neurons (e.g. hippocampal CA3 vs somatosensory cortex).

Open questions

Could the BANKSY embedding be extended to downstream spatial tasks such as cell–cell signaling inference and differentiation-trajectory analysis, as the authors suggest?
How well does simple multisample concatenation overcome batch effects versus explicit spatially informed correction across more heterogeneous cohorts?
What is the optimal principled way to choose λ when no ground-truth domain annotations exist?

wiki/sources/sloan-2014-mechanisms-astro.title: sloan-2014-mechanisms-astrocyte-development
wiki/sources/sloan-2014-mechanisms-astro.content:
Mechanisms of astrocyte development and their contributions to neurodevelopmental disorders
One-line: A review arguing that astrocytes are active choreographers of neural circuit formation, and that defects in the timing or function of astrocyte development (astrogenesis) may underlie many neurodevelopmental disorders long assumed to be purely neuronal.
Key takeaways

Neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes share a common neuroepithelial origin; neural stem cells first undergo a purely neurogenic phase (neurogenesis) and later switch to a gliogenic phase, a temporal program recapitulated in ES/iPSC models.
Astrocytes arise from multiple sources: directly from radial glia (radial-glia), a putative astrocyte-restricted progenitor, or local proliferation of newly born astrocytes; recent lineage tracing shows most postnatal cortical glia come from clonal divisions of early differentiated astrocytes that specify spatial domains.
Gliogenesis is driven by extrinsic IL-6-family cytokines (CNTF, LIF, CT-1) signaling through LIFRb/gp130 and the JAK-STAT pathway, plus BMP and Notch signaling, all converging on a STAT3:p300/CBP transcriptional complex activating GFAP and S100b.
Intrinsic chromatin state gates competence: dna-methylation of the GFAP promoter blocks astrocyte fate even when STAT3 is active; the Notch effector NFIA displaces DNMT1 to demethylate the promoter and relax chromatin-accessibility for transcription — a form of developmental gene-regulation.
Aberrant timing of the neurogenic-to-gliogenic switch (too few or precocious astrocytes) or primary astrocyte dysfunction can each disrupt neural circuits, linking astrocyte biology to autism and the neurodevelopmental origins of psychiatric disease.

Notable claims / data

Human astrocytes are over 20-times larger by volume and contact up to 10-times more synapses than individual rodent astrocytes; human brain shows elevated expression of astrocyte-derived synaptogenic proteins, suggesting a non-neuronal contribution to human cognition.
Gliogenesis is inhibited during the neurogenic phase by the proneuronal factor Ngn1 sequestering p300/CBP away from STAT3, and by BDNF/SHP2-Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK signaling that dephosphorylates STAT3. Constitutively active SHP2 (PTPN11) mutations cause Noonan syndrome; mouse models show excessive neurogenesis and decreased astrogenesis.
RASopathies (Noonan, Neurofibromatosis-1, Costello, Cardiofaciocutaneous syndromes) show abnormal precocious astrocyte development and share cognitive impairment. In Down syndrome (trisomy 21), trisomic OLIG2 overexpression may drive premature exit from neurogenesis and precocious gliogenesis.
Direct causal evidence in monogenic autism: MECP2-deficient (Rett syndrome) astrocytes fail to support normal neuronal dendritic morphology non-cell-autonomously, and astrocyte-specific MECP2 re-expression rescues a subset of Rett symptoms; Fragile-X astrocytes delay dendritic maturation in co-culture.
Using RNA-Seq of purified mouse cell types, 30 of the top 46 autism-linked genes from GWAS (genome-wide-association-study) (65%) are expressed in astrocytes, close to the 77% in neurons; some (Kir4.1, SYNE1, TSPAN7) are astrocyte-enriched.
Observational data implicate astrocytes in schizophrenia and major depression: altered GFAP/AQP4/CX43/EAAT2 expression, reduced glial numbers in anterior cingulate, consistent with the glutamate dyshomeostasis hypothesis.

Connections

Extends the wiki’s neurodevelopment and neurogenesis threads to the glial/astrocyte side of the neurogenic-to-gliogenic switch.
Provides a cell-type-of-action lens on autism and psychiatric-genetics risk genes, complementing genome-wide-association-study-based gene discovery.
Chromatin/methylation gating of the GFAP promoter connects to dna-methylation, chromatin-accessibility, and developmental gene-regulation themes.

Open questions

How developmentally and functionally heterogeneous are astrocyte populations, and how similar are human vs rodent astrocytes in complex disorders like autism?
Which specific qualities of astrocyte dysfunction actually contribute to aberrant neural function during development?
Can human iPSC-derived astrocytes from affected patients confirm rodent observations and enable therapeutic screens? New markers of astrocyte maturation and diversity are needed.

wiki/sources/sokolova-2024-deep-learning.title: sokolova-2024-deep-learning-sequence-models
wiki/sources/sokolova-2024-deep-learning.content:
Deep Learning Sequence Models for Transcriptional Regulation
One-line: Troyanskaya-lab review of sequence-based deep learning models that predict epigenetic marks, gene expression, and 3D genome organization directly from DNA to interpret noncoding variant effects.
Key takeaways

Sequence-based DNNs learn the regulatory code directly from DNA, enabling prediction of the functional consequences of any noncoding-variants—even rare or never-observed ones—beyond what quantitative genetics alone can resolve.
Two dominant architectures: CNNs (spatially local motif scanning, akin to position weight matrices) and transformers whose self-attention captures long-range enhancer/insulator dependencies; hybrid CNN-then-transformer designs combine both.
Because models are never trained on variant data, they generalize to unseen sequence and support in silico mutation-effect-prediction, key for fine-mapping causal common variants and characterizing rare ones.
A dedicated section surveys model-interpretability for regulatory DNNs across four families: in silico mutagenesis, attention weights, convolutional kernel analysis, and gradient-based saliency (integrated gradients, DeepLIFT).
Frames the field’s trajectory toward genomic foundation-models and DNA language models (dna-language-models) pretrained on unlabeled sequence and fine-tuned across tasks, aiming to decode cis-regulatory-grammar at scale.

Notable claims / data

Model scale progression in chromatin profiling: DeepSEA (919 → 2,002 profiles) → Sei (21,907 profiles for histone marks, TFs, chromatin accessibility; mostly from the Cistrome Project); DeepBind covered 538 TFs and 194 RNA-binding proteins.
Gene-expression models (Table 1): ExPecto/ExPectoSC (40 kb TSS-proximal, modular DeepSEA→linear), Basenji 1/2 (131 kb, CAGE peaks), Enformer (200 kb, transformer, best variant-effect prediction), Xpresso (10.5 kb, sequence-only + mRNA half-life features).
3D genome models scale from ~1 Mb (DeepC 5-kb bins, Akita 2-kb bins) to whole-chromosome (Orca, up to 256 Mb via multiscale hierarchical CNN + horizontal checkpointing); applied to structural variants in leukemia, leukodystrophy, and Neanderthal-vs-modern-human contrasts.
Interpretability caveats noted: attention-weight ranking outperforms magnitude; convolutional kernel analysis ignores fully connected layers so misses interactions; ReLU discontinuities create saliency artifacts.
Review (human_research:false); no new data generated. Corresponding author Olga Troyanskaya (Princeton / Flatiron Institute); Sokolova, Chen, Hao contributed equally.

Connections

Post-transcriptional counterpart to this review’s transcriptional focus: closes by pointing to sequence models of splicing and RNA-binding-protein regulation, tying to the wiki’s splicing corpus (e.g. capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the on splicing computational models).
Proposes benchmarking regulatory models against massively-parallel-reporter-assay readouts and CRISPR-interference enhancer knockdowns—linking to lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric and deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory.
Complements gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting on interpreting sequence-model predictions for gene importance.

Open questions

Can training on personalized/population genomes (rather than a single reference) meaningfully improve variant-effect direction prediction?
What benchmark of regulatory-variant pathogenicity would let the field judge readiness for personalized-medicine deployment?
How should transcriptional-regulation models be integrated with splicing/RBP and coding-variant models for whole-genome interpretation?

wiki/sources/sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-t.title: sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying
wiki/sources/sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-t.content:
Tools and tactics for studying alternative splicing
One-line: A methods/tools review of the modern toolkit for studying alternative-splicing — short- and long-read sequencing (bulk, single-cell, spatial), CRISPR-based functional assays, minigene/reporter assays, population/sQTL genetics, and deep-learning splicing-code models — organized around the goals of building a complete mRNA-isoform atlas, testing isoform function, and decoding the genetic control of splicing. Complements the wiki’s biology-focused sources by cataloguing how splicing is actually measured and perturbed.
Key takeaways

splicing-methods for alternative-splicing have evolved through a timeline (Fig. 2): splicing/AS discovery (1977) → RT-PCR (1989) → ESTs (1991) → exon-junction microarrays (2003) → high-throughput RNA-seq (2008) → long-read full-length sequencing (2013) and single-cell isoform RNA-seq → spatially resolved full-length mRNA sequencing (2023). Two landmark RNA-seq studies estimated 92-95% of human multi-exon genes undergo AS.
AS forms catalogued: cassette-exon inclusion/skipping, alternative 5’/3’ splice-sites (exon elongation/truncation), intron retention, plus alternative TSS and polyadenylation; these are frequently coordinated, and long-read data show coordinated/independent splicing of distant exons and that intron-removal order need not follow transcription order (cotranscriptional-splicing).
Short-read RNA-seq analysis splits into isoform-based vs count-based (exon-based and event-based) methods; event-based tools compute per cent spliced-in (PSI) and compare ΔPSI. Splice-aware aligners (STAR, HISAT2), pseudo-aligners (Salmon, Kallisto) and de novo assemblers (Trinity, Oases, SPLASH) are standard. Benchmarks show substantial tool-to-tool discrepancy → orthogonal validation needed.
Long-read platforms (PacBio SMRT/HiFi, ONT) directly capture full-length isoforms and exon connectivity, resolving complex patterns short reads cannot; ONT direct RNA-seq additionally reads native RNA + modifications (m6A) without RT/PCR.
CRISPR-based functional assays directly test isoform function: Cas9 splice-site/exon editing and pooled fitness screens, base editors for precise splice-site/enhancer disruption, and Cas13/dCasRx for RNA-level, isoform-specific, reversible perturbation and SRE mapping (splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-factors).
Genetic control is mapped via sQTL analysis, allele-specific expression (ASE), and massively parallel reporter assays (MPRAs); a recent GTEx v8 analysis found a substantial fraction of trait/disease variants act through splicing.
Deep-learning splicing-code models progressed from feature-based predictors to CNNs (spliceai, Pangolin) and newer multimodal/transformer/foundation models (DeltaSplice, Borzoi, DNABERT, SpliceBERT, AlphaGenome); none yet capture full cell-type/condition-specific isoform repertoires.
Central open debate: distinguishing functional isoforms from pervasive “splicing noise” — many AS isoforms are low-abundance and may be nonfunctional mis-splicing, but a subset is biologically meaningful.

Notable claims / data

Platforms (Table 2): Illumina >99.9% base accuracy, reads 50-300 bp; PacBio HiFi ~99.8% accuracy, 10-25 kb reads; ONT cDNA reads 10-20 kb avg (>1 Mb possible), ONT direct RNA ~1-1.3 kb avg (some >20 kb). PacBio/ONT continuous reads average 10-20 kb.
Library prep matters: rRNA is ~80% of total RNA; polyA selection generally preferred over rRNA depletion for splicing; UMIs remove PCR duplicates; strand-specific and paired-end improve junction calling. Typical AS depth 30-60M reads/sample; 100-200M for low-abundance isoforms or poorly annotated genomes.
Short-read limit: <300 bp reads vs ~3 kb average human mRNA → poor full-length reconstruction.
Large isoform-atlas efforts: ONT RNA-seq of GTEx samples found >70,000 novel transcripts; PacBio added to ENCODE4 to catalogue isoforms across 81 human/mouse samples (preprint). Ouro-Seq built a mouse single-cell long-read splicing atlas of >100,000 cells across 12 organs.
Single-cell: droplet 3’/5’ methods (10x Chromium) capture only transcript ends — insufficient for junctions; full-length plate methods (Smart-seq2/3, RamDA-seq, VASA-seq, FLASH-seq) enable event-level AS in single cells. Single-cell long-read methods: ScISOr-Seq2, scTaILoR-seq (>1,000 genes), HIT-scISOseq, MAS-ISO-seq, Ouro-Seq, snISOr-Seq. m6Anet quantifies m6A from ONT direct RNA.
Targeted long-read: nanopore amplicon sequencing resolved ~8,000 full-length cDNAs from four Drosophila genes incl. Dscam1 (most alternatively spliced gene known); RNA CaptureSeq enriches selected transcripts.
Spatial: MERFISH/seqFISH (imaging), barcoding methods (original 2016 spatial transcriptomics, Slide-seq, Seq-Scope, Stereo-seq, Stereo-cell); Spl-ISO-Seq did spatial long-read isoforms at 10-µm resolution (human cortex > white matter splicing regulation); Longcell pipeline handles single-cell/spatial Nanopore barcode/UMI errors.
Proteomics: most shotgun workflows use trypsin and miss splice-unique peptides; a study using six proteases + deep LC-MS found most frame-preserving AS isoforms are translated. Ribosome profiling and polyribosome long-read RNA-seq probe which isoforms are translated; nanopore protein sequencing emerging for proteoforms.
Functional-prediction tools: NEASE (pathway/PPI interpretation of AS), FAScore (ML predictor of functional exon-skipping), ORF cloning for high-throughput isoform interaction profiling (alternative isoforms share <half their binding partners). AlphaFold3 enables AS structural impact studies.
CRISPR examples: sgRNAs at MPRIP exon 23 splice sites → skipping → metastasis; targeting an RBFOX2 motif → inclusion → metastasis suppression (pancreatic cancer). Base-editor screen showed TAF5 exon 8 AS affects transcription. SpliceRUSH = high-throughput dCas13 SRE mapping; dCasRx fused to splicing factors (e.g. dCasRx-RBM25) activates exon inclusion at scale.
Reporter assays: splicing reporters tested >2 million random sequences for splice-site selection (predicts variants in SMN1, SMN2, CFTR, BRCA2); an MPRA of ~5,000 exonic disease mutations found ~10% disrupt splicing vs only 3% of common SNPs. EXSISERS quantifies endogenous protein isoforms (e.g. MAPT exon 10) via intein splicing in patient iPS cells.
sQTL/ASE tools: scNanoGPS (genotype + splicing from single-cell nanopore), LORALS (allele-specific long-read analysis). siRNA knockdown of 305 spliceosome/regulatory proteins reconstructed splicing-factor functional networks.
Splicing-code benchmark: spliceai (CNN, splice-site positions) and Pangolin (quantitative, multi-tissue) achieve highest accuracy among variant predictors, but performance is limited especially for exonic variants.

Connections

Methods companion to the wiki’s mechanism/code sources. Directly complements capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the on the splicing-code and spliceai/Pangolin (both flag exonic-variant and tissue-specific prediction as the weak spots; both name DeltaSplice, Borzoi, SpliceBERT, foundation models). This paper situates those models as the “decode” layer atop the sequencing/perturbation toolkit.
Provides the experimental backbone for the AS functionality debate: long-read isoform atlases reveal vast diversity, while CRISPR screens, ORF-interaction assays, proteomics (six-protease MS, ribosome profiling) and FAScore are the tactics for separating functional isoforms from mis-splicing noise (its closing “Do rare isoforms matter?” question).
post-transcriptional-splicing / cotranscriptional-splicing: long-read data show intron-removal order ≠ transcription order and coordinated distant-exon splicing; subcellular-fractionation long-read RNA-seq links isoforms to translational fate; ONT direct RNA couples isoform structure to m6A epitranscriptome.
splicing-and-disease: minigene/MPRA validation, sQTL/ASE genetics, CRISPR isogenic iPS-cell models (photoreceptors, cardiomyocytes); examples in NRXN1, NDUFB10, MAPT, and neurodevelopmental/neuropsychiatric risk via long-read neocortex isoform mapping.
splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins / splicing-regulatory-elements: SpliceRUSH and dCasRx-activator screens, the 305-factor siRNA network study, and RBFOX2 editing are the tools for dissecting regulator logic.
Modern instances of this toolkit in the wiki (long-read-rna-seq / single-cell / splicing-qtl era): fu-2025-single-cell-and-spatial-alternative (longcell, single-cell/spatial AS), quinones-valdez-2025-long-read-rna-seq-demarcates-cis (isolaser, cis/trans phasing → allele-specific-splicing), clavell-revelles-2025-long-read-transcriptomics-of-a-diverse (annotation/ancestry bias), kim-2025-nanopore-direct-rna-sequencing-of (direct RNA-seq), humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas & liu-2024-single-cell-rna-sequencing-of-peripheral (isoform/sQTL atlases), tokolyi-2025-the-contribution-of-genetic-determinants (population e/sQTL), and the clinical venkatasubramanian-2025-splicing-regulatory-dynamics-for-precision (oncosplice).

Open questions

Long-read platforms still need higher accuracy/throughput for complete cell-type-specific isoform annotation from low-input RNA; low-abundance transcripts remain low-precision and require validation.
Need integrated computational frameworks jointly modelling transcription initiation, splicing and 3’-end formation, and spatial methods at true isoform-level single-cell/subcellular resolution.
Splicing is highly dynamic, but time-resolved sequencing and methods to identify transient/partial splicing states are lacking.
Prediction goal unmet: a model that outputs the complete isoform repertoire and relative abundances per gene in any cell type/signalling context (likely needs foundation models integrating chromatin, RBP occupancy, RNA structure, epitranscriptome).
Moving from descriptive cataloguing to interpretable, mechanistic models of splicing control via large-scale perturbation + RNA-protein/structure/nuclear-organization mapping.
The core unresolved question: how to distinguish functional isoforms from pervasive splicing noise.

wiki/sources/spielmann-2022-computationa.title: spielmann-2022-computational-experimental-methods-classifying-variants
wiki/sources/spielmann-2022-computationa.content:
Computational and experimental methods for classifying variants of unknown clinical significance
One-line: A commentary on how computational prediction and high-throughput experimental assays can be combined with clinical knowledge and machine learning to interpret and rank the growing flood of variants of uncertain significance (VUSs).
Key takeaways

The bottleneck in genomic medicine is no longer variant discovery but interpretation: as of the January 2022 ClinVar release, of >1.1 million clinically asserted variants, >400,000 are VUSs, and VUSs have been the largest class since ~2015.
Diagnostic yield from exome/genome sequencing plateaus at 40%-60%, partly because analysis stays focused on coding sequence (~1% of the genome) while most common-disease and many rare-disease causal variants fall in noncoding regions.
Computational scores split into “specific” predictors tuned to one molecular function (missense, synonymous, splice) and broadly applicable combined metrics; the paper frames variant-effect-prediction and mutation-effect-prediction across coding, splicing, and regulatory classes on a common scale.
Combined scores like Eigen, LINSIGHT, and cadd-score integrate many partially correlated annotations so variants of different molecular function can be ranked together, but they inherit ascertainment bias from training data.
Multiplex assays of variant effects (MPRA and related methods) offer scalable “next-generation functional tests”; variants are engineered and read out in a pooled format via massively parallel sequencing.
noncoding-variants and regulatory sequence (5%-20% of the genome) remain the weakest area for sequence-based effect prediction, tied to incomplete mechanistic understanding of gene regulation and 3D genome architecture.

Notable claims / data

An individual genome yields ~3-4 million short variants (SNVs, MNVs, indels <50 bp) plus ~15,000 structural variants; considering variant combinations genome-wide is intractable, so filtering (e.g., by pedigree affected/unaffected status) is essential.
gnomAD v3.1 reports >759 million short-sequence variants across >76,000 genomes.
Under the current ACMG guidelines, computational evidence counts only as “supporting”; only coding sequence can be clinically classified.
Deep mutational scanning was introduced by Fowler et al. (2010), profiling >600,000 variants of the human WW domain to map mutational effects at every position.
Saturation genome editing (CRISPR-Cas9 + multiplex homology-directed repair) applied to 13 critical BRCA1 exons covered 96.5% of possible SNVs; of ~4000 tested variants, 25% of VUSs and 49% of conflicting-report variants were flagged nonfunctional, with estimated >95% accuracy predicting functional outcome in BRCA1.
With genome sequencing, de novo variants rise to 70-100 per trio, making one-by-one experimental follow-up infeasible and motivating multiplexed assays.
Structural variants, though the smallest class by count (<20,000 per individual), affect more nucleotides than all other classes combined and are computationally hard because effects (dosage, 3D regulatory disruption) span large regions.
CAGI challenges provide blinded benchmarking (train set plus hidden test set revealed only after the challenge) to prevent overfitting during method evaluation.

Connections

Frames the clinical-interpretation use case for sequence-based scoring tools such as cadd-score and, for splice effects, tools like spliceai.
Central to variant-effect-prediction and mutation-effect-prediction: motivates specialized-plus-combined scoring strategies and cautions about training-data ascertainment bias.
Positions MPRA-style multiplex assays and cis-regulatory-elements readouts as the experimental complement to computation for noncoding-variants.
Regulatory and noncoding interpretation ties to 3D genome architecture and TAD-boundary disruption as disease mechanisms.

Open questions

Will advances in protein-structure prediction (e.g., AlphaFold2) actually improve pathogenic missense classification beyond already-strong existing scores?
How can regulatory / noncoding effect prediction be improved when no single score or annotation reliably predicts experimental regulatory readouts?
How should large-scale functional data (MAVEs, DMS) be integrated with machine learning and clinical evidence to move computational predictions from “supporting” to primary evidence in ACMG-style frameworks?
How can circularity and ascertainment bias (variants clustering around long-studied, highly conserved genes) be corrected so predictors generalize to underexplored genes and variant classes?

wiki/sources/stark-2019-integrating-geno.title: stark-2019-integrating-genomics-healthcare-global
wiki/sources/stark-2019-integrating-geno.content:
Integrating Genomics into Healthcare: A Global Responsibility
One-line: AJHG commentary arguing that clinical genomic medicine is a global responsibility, reviewing four national genomic-medicine initiatives (UK, France, Australia, US) and laying out a roadmap for international sharing of standards, tools, evidence, and data.
Key takeaways

Genomic sequencing is rapidly moving from research into routine clinical care; over the next 5 years genomic data from more than 60 million patients is expected to be generated within healthcare.
Since 2013, governments of at least 14 countries have invested over US$4 billion in national genomic-medicine initiatives to move testing from centers of excellence into mainstream medical practice.
Real barriers to implementation are less technical than systemic: data integration/interpretation, workforce capacity, public acceptability, weak evidence for clinical utility and cost effectiveness, and ethical/legislative issues.
Two priorities dominate the roadmap: building the evidence base (clinical utility, cost effectiveness, long-term outcomes) and enabling responsible genomic data sharing across borders.
The Global Alliance for Genomics and Health (GA4GH) is positioned as the standards-setting backbone, with Genomics England, Australian Genomics, and All of Us acting as early GA4GH Driver Projects.

Notable claims / data

Diverse national models: proof-of-principle rare-disease/cancer programs (UK, France, Australia, Saudi Arabia, Turkey); population-based sequencing with return of results (US, Estonia, Denmark, Japan, Qatar); infrastructure/standards focus (Switzerland, Netherlands, Brazil, Finland).
China Precision Medicine Initiative: a 15-year, ~US$9.2 billion project aiming to sequence 100,000,000 genomes by 2030 — dwarfing others.
UK: Genomics England (2013, ~US$415M) met its 100,000-genome target in December 2018; trio-based rare-disease interpretation; NHS Genomic Medicine Service launched Oct 2018 with a reimbursement-linked test directory; government plans to sequence 5,000,000 genomes over 5 years.
France: Plan France Médecine Génomique 2025 (~US822M first 5 years, ~US282M from industry); target of 235,000 genomes/year by 2020 (20,000 rare disease + 50,000 metastatic/refractory cancer).
Australia: Australian Genomics (2014), a partnership of 78 organizations; AU25M NHMRC funding leveraging AU100M state funding; singleton rare-disease testing; AU$500M over 10 years committed for a Genomics Health Futures Mission. Evidence shows genomic sequencing increases diagnostic yield while potentially reducing diagnostic costs.
US: NHGRI programs since 2011; All of Us (US500M initial appropriation plus US1.455B commitment) engaging 1,000,000 diverse volunteers; open tools include Phenotype KnowledgeBase, Clinical Decision Support KnowledgeBase, IGNITE SPARK Toolbox, and ClinGen.
Private sector: Geisinger MyCode (exome sequencing with Regeneron), Foundation Medicine, and DTC firms (23andMe, Ancestry), with variable clinician/public responses to DTC genomics.
Cautions: risk of overpromising (“genohype”), pressure to exempt genomic testing from rigorous evidence, delayed program evaluation, siloed data, and time-limited funding threatening sustainability. It takes an average of 17 years for research evidence to reach clinical practice.

Connections

Complements the wiki’s genome-sequencing-and-references topic by framing the policy, workforce, and data-governance layer around clinical whole-genome/whole-exome sequencing rather than the algorithms themselves.
Data-sharing themes (federated analysis without moving data across borders, interoperable formats, consent standards) connect to knowledge databases and variant interpretation resources like ClinVar and Matchmaker Exchange.
Emphasis on representative population reference datasets to improve variant interpretation globally links to concerns about database diversity and equitable precision medicine.

Open questions

How can national initiatives, most on time-limited funding, transition to sustainable healthcare resource allocation?
What standard outcome criteria and evaluation methodologies should be agreed on to measure clinical utility across disease groups, funding contexts, and health systems?
Can federated data-sharing models satisfy strict national regulatory frameworks while maintaining public trust?
Are high-income-country implementation strategies transferable to low- and middle-income settings, and how should representativeness of reference datasets be ensured?

wiki/sources/stark-2019-rna-sequencing-t.title: stark-2019-rna-sequencing-the-teenage-years
wiki/sources/stark-2019-rna-sequencing-t.content:
RNA sequencing: the teenage years
One-line: A broad Nature Reviews Genetics survey of how RNA-seq has matured over its first decade — from the baseline short-read bulk differential-gene-expression assay to long-read and direct RNA-seq, single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, and the computational pipelines behind them — providing the technological backdrop against which short-read differential-splicing/isoform analysis operates.
Key takeaways

RNA-seq’s primary, routine application remains bulk differential gene expression (DGE); the core workflow (RNA extraction → mRNA enrichment or rRNA depletion → cDNA synthesis → adaptor-ligated library → ~10-30M reads, usually Illumina → alignment/quantification/normalization/statistics) has not changed substantially since the earliest publications. Almost 100 distinct methods have been derived from the standard protocol.
Short-read cDNA sequencing (Illumina) generated >95% of published RNA-seq data on the SRA and is treated as the baseline technology, but it struggles with isoform-level resolution: reads that do not span splice junctions cannot be unambiguously assigned to a specific isoform, complicating alternative-splicing quantification (see percent-spliced-in and splicing-methods).
Long-read cDNA methods (PacBio Iso-Seq, Oxford Nanopore) and direct RNA-seq (dRNA-seq, ONT) sequence full-length molecules, removing the assembly/inference step and reducing the high false-positive splice-junction detection rate seen with short-read tools; dRNA-seq additionally retains base modifications and enables poly(A) tail-length estimation.
Over 90% of human genes are alternatively spliced into two or more expressed isoforms — the central motivation for isoform-aware library prep and analysis covered throughout the review.
The review surveys the four computational phases of DGE (alignment/assembly, quantification, filtering/normalization, statistical modelling) and the major tool families at each, several of which are also the standard substrate for differential-splicing work.

Notable claims / data

Illumina short reads account for >95% of public RNA-seq data; an Illumina run yields 10^9-10^10 reads vs 10^6-10^7 per run for PacBio/ONT — a throughput gap of roughly two orders of magnitude that currently keeps long reads from supplanting short reads for large DGE experiments.
50% of human transcripts are >2,500 bp (range 186 bp to 109 kb); PacBio Iso-Seq generated full-length reads for transcripts up to ~15 kb, synthetic long reads reconstruct contigs up to ~4 kb, and dRNA-seq read lengths are ~1,000 bp (max >10 kb).
Long-read platforms have error rates 1-2 orders of magnitude higher than mature Illumina; PacBio circular consensus sequencing (CCS) can drive errors down to short-read levels or lower at the cost of throughput.
Replication matters more than read depth/length for confidence: a 48-replicate yeast study found DGE tools detected only 20-40% of differentially expressed genes with just 3 replicates, suggesting a minimum of ~6 biological replicates (a later study argues 4 may suffice if biological variance is measured in a pilot).
Read depth: 10-30M reads/sample is the accepted range for bulk eukaryotic DGE, but as few as 1M reads can give comparable abundance estimates for the most highly expressed half of the transcriptome; 50-bp single-end is adequate for DGE but compromises isoform/splice-junction detection vs 100-bp paired-end.
The quantification step has the largest impact on final results (greater than choice of aligner); read-count tools (HTSeq, featureCounts) discard multi-mapped/ambiguous reads, while RSEM (expectation-maximization) and alignment-free tools (Kallisto) handle them differently.
Caveat: RNA degradation produces a controllable 3’ bias in short reads but cripples long-read full-length analysis; spike-in controls (ERCCs, SIRVs, Sequins) normalize better at gene level than transcript/isoform level.

Connections

Provides the sequencing-technology and pipeline context underlying splicing-focused methods in this wiki: short-read spliced aligners and junction-counting are the substrate for tools like rmats (see shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); isoform/exon quantification underpins percent-spliced-in and splicing-methods.
Complements the splicing-tooling surveys sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the by situating splicing analysis within the broader RNA-seq method landscape (long-read isoform discovery, dRNA-seq, single-cell, spatial).
Relevant to mechanism/regulation reviews (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative) as the measurement layer through which alternative-splicing biology is observed.
The long-read/dRNA-seq emphasis foreshadows the move toward direct isoform-resolved splicing measurement that short-read splicing-methods only approximate.

Open questions

When and in which niches will long-read and dRNA-seq throughput improve enough (≥2 orders of magnitude) to make differential isoform/splicing analysis routine and replace short-read inference?
How much does the choice of quantification and normalization method bias downstream alternative-splicing conclusions, given that quantification dominates final-result variance?
Can direct RNA-seq’s retention of base modifications and poly(A) tail length open splicing-coupled epitranscriptomic regulation (e.g., the observation that intron-retaining transcripts carry marginally longer poly(A) tails) to systematic study?

wiki/sources/stewart-2025-measurement-an.title: stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of
wiki/sources/stewart-2025-measurement-an.content:
Measurement and clinical interpretation of CRISPR off-targets
One-line: A clinically minded Perspective from the Somatic Cell Genome Editing Consortium arguing that CRISPR therapies need not be “perfect” (zero off-target), and offering a practical benefit–risk framework plus a catalog of detection methods for nominating, verifying, and prioritizing genomic off-targets from bench to bedside.
Key takeaways

Central thesis: demanding zero off-targets is biologically and clinically unrealistic; the Cas enzyme inherently tolerates guide RNA–target mismatches, so off-targets should be measured and weighed against disease threat rather than treated as automatic disqualifiers (relates to genome-editing).
Risk tolerance scales with disease threat. For life-threatening/fatal genetic disease (“cost of doing nothing = certain death”), high risk tolerance is warranted; palliative/disease-modifying uses are held to a higher safety bar. Analogy drawn to chemotherapy, radiation, CAR-T (4–16% secondary cancer), lentiviral gene therapy, and anti-amyloid antibodies.
Off-target = any unintended genomic change, not just guide-similar sites: includes structural variants, translocations, truncations, aneuploidy, chromothripsis, loss of heterozygosity, large inversions/deletions, plus guide RNA–independent base-editor deaminase edits.
Two-step detection workflow: (1) nominate candidates via in silico prediction + naked-DNA/cell-free biochemical assays, then (2) empirically verify in human cells/primary cells at relevant depth. Naked-DNA methods overestimate true off-targets (false-positive rates 27–95% vs <20% for cellular).
Provides a framework (Table 2) organized as Identification (on-target characterization, off-target nomination+verification, patient-population variants) and Evaluation (potency/persistence, DNA structure, cancer risk, functional risk, delivery integration, long-term monitoring), noting where the FDA concurs.

Notable claims / data

Naked-DNA off-target assays overestimate true sites “by hundreds or thousands”; reported false-positive discovery rates 27–95% (naked DNA) vs <20% (cellular) — testing/validation in human cellular systems is deemed critical.
Human samples are required: animal models are unsuitable for off-target detection due to human–species sequence divergence, especially in noncoding regions.
