Casgevy case study: original reference-genome in silico predictions were not seen in cell assays, but additional off-targets were found via the variant-aware tool crisprme, accounting for human population polymorphisms — motivating patient-population variant-aware nomination.
Germline editing: FDA placed a clinical hold on Verve’s in vivo PCSK9 base-editing trial pending germline quantification. In NHP/mouse studies (Verve PCSK9; Intellia hereditary angioedema, transthyretin amyloidosis), no sperm editing detected; ovarian bulk editing seen but no germline transmission to progeny — attributed to lipid-nanoparticle failure to penetrate dense tissue rather than innate germline protection.
Zero tolerance recommended for known oncogene/tumor-suppressor off-targets; cross-reference the cosmic-database and use deep sequencing to lower detection thresholds. AAV integration at cut sites is flagged as an additional oncogenic route.
Functional-impact triage: distinguish coding vs noncoding, use ensembl-vep and cadd-score; distinguish off-target (any cell type) from off-organ editing; kidney/liver (blood-filtration/clearance organs) flagged as susceptible even when not targeted.
FDA recommends 15-year long-term follow-up; authors argue current funding/logistics make this hard for small/bespoke single-dose rare-disease trials and call for a centralized, government-sponsored VAERS-like reporting system and a field-wide database of functionally consequential genomic changes.
Physiological vs supersaturating dosing: most assays use supersaturating editor doses that overestimate rates; authors recommend profiling at physiological doses in cells with the matching in vivo DNA repair system (plus supersaturating runs to surface rare cancer-causing edits).

Connections

Extends genome-editing from a discovery/functional-genomics tool toward clinical therapeutic safety assessment — a clinical/regulatory lens absent from CRISPR-screen sources like pooled-crispr-screen.
In silico off-target predictors (cas-offinder, crispor, deepcrispr, elevation-crispr, crisprme) connect to ai-in-genomics and mutation-effect-prediction (VEP/CADD as deleteriousness predictors over noncoding-variants and cis-regulatory-elements).
Cataloged detection assays are candidate entities: guide-seq, circle-seq, change-seq, digenome-seq, one-seq, site-seq, discover-seq, induce-seq, cast-seq — a methods layer the wiki did not previously cover.
Shares the wiki’s neurogenomics thread indirectly: senior author Clelland (Weill Institute for Neurosciences, UCSF) works on CRISPR for the CNS — links conceptually to neurodevelopment / CNS gene therapy (framework is disease-agnostic here).

Open questions

How sensitive is cell-free DNA for capturing off-targets from inaccessible solid organs in living patients — the paper flags this as unresolved but desirable.
What standardized thresholds and platforms will make “off-target cancer mutation detection” comparable across editors and targets?
How will regulators ultimately treat germline editing once delivery vectors become potent enough to penetrate gametes — the paper notes “no clear guidance” yet.
Will unbiased whole-genome off-target sequencing become the default as sequencing cost falls, replacing biased nomination-then-verify workflows?

wiki/sources/stiles-2010-the-basics-of-b.title: stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain
wiki/sources/stiles-2010-the-basics-of-b.content:
The Basics of Brain Development
One-line: A foundational review of mammalian (largely human) brain development spanning macroanatomic, cellular, and molecular levels, framing development as dynamic, adaptive, gene-environment-interactive processes within genetically constrained but constantly changing contexts — a primer that anchors the brain-development-neurogenomics topic.
Key takeaways

Human brain development is protracted: it begins in gestational week 3 (GW3) with differentiation of neural progenitor cells and extends at least through late adolescence, arguably across the lifespan. Both gene expression and environmental input are essential, but neither is “prescriptive or determinative” — the central thesis is interactionist, not deterministic.
Genes are inert templates whose only direct product is protein; proteins (not genes) are the active agents in signaling cascades. Genes do not “directly create” traits like eye color, intelligence, or behavior — there is only an indirect, multi-step relationship between gene sequence and developmental outcome.
The mature brain has >100 billion neurons making >60 trillion connections (each neuron connecting to >1,000 others). Gray matter = neuronal cell bodies (neocortex, subcortical nuclei); white matter = myelinated axon tracts. Cortical folding (gyri/sulci) is an evolutionary adaptation packing a large cortex into a small cranial vault constrained by the birth process.
Embryonic period (conception–GW8): gastrulation transforms a 2-layer into a 3-layer embryo; neuroectodermal stem cells (the neural progenitor cells) arise via molecular signaling from the primitive node. The neural tube forms E20–27 (anterior neuropore closes E25, posterior E27), then balloons into 3 primary then 5 secondary brain vesicles establishing the rostral-caudal CNS plan.
Neural patterning is “continuous specification and refinement.” Early neocortical area identity is set by opposing transcription-factor gradients — Emx2 (high posterior/medial) vs Pax6 (high anterior/lateral), later joined by Coup-TF1 and SP8 — whose combinatorial levels assign motor (M1), somatosensory (S1), and visual (V1) cortex; mouse knockouts shift area sizes accordingly.
Fetal/cellular processes: neuron production begins E42 (shift from symmetric to asymmetric progenitor division) and is largely complete by midgestation (cortical neurogenesis done ~E108). Neurons migrate radially (somal translocation, then radial-glial guides — the glia being the progenitors themselves) and tangentially (interneurons from the ganglionic eminences). Cortex builds “inside-out” in six layers; Cajal-Retzius cells secrete Reelin to signal migration stop. Transient subplate neurons scaffold thalamocortical/corticothalamic pathway formation (complete by GW26).
Regressive events are normal and essential: apoptosis eliminates ≥50% (up to ~70% in some cortical regions) of neurons prenatally, regulated by competition for neurotrophic factors (neurotrophic hypothesis). Postnatally, synaptic exuberance peaks at ~2× adult synapse density then is pruned across childhood/adolescence; transient connections (corpus callosum, thalamocortical, corticospinal) are eliminated.
Postnatal/glial and imaging: neurogenesis continues only in the subventricular zone (→ olfactory bulb) and hippocampal dentate gyrus; glial progenitors (esp. OPCs) proliferate and migrate for a protracted period, myelinating into adulthood. MRI/DTI reveal childhood-adolescent gray-matter thinning, white-matter growth, and rising fractional anisotropy (FA) as fiber tracts mature.
Experience is constitutive: Greenough’s “experience-expectant” (normal early input needed for normal organization) vs “experience-dependent” (lifelong learning) framework; enrichment/deprivation, ocular-dominance-column shifts (Hubel & Wiesel), and Sur’s rewired auditory cortex acting as a visual area all demonstrate developmental plasticity.

Notable claims / data



100 billion neurons; >60 trillion synaptic connections; each neuron connects to >1,000 others (Pakkenberg & Gundersen 1997).


Brain reaches ~90% of adult volume by age 6 and increases ~4-fold during the preschool period (Reiss et al. 1996; others).
Neuron production starts E42; cortical neurogenesis complete ~E108 (Clancy et al. 2001). Symmetric progenitor division (E25–E42) expands the pool; asymmetric division (from E42) yields one progenitor + one neuron.
Neural tube closure: anterior neuropore E25, posterior neuropore E27; embryo grows from 3–5 mm (end of neurulation) to 27–31 mm by GW8 (~tenfold).
Apoptosis affects ≥50% of neurons in many regions, reaching ~70% in some cortical regions (Rabinowicz et al. 1996); rates in the ventricular zone rise across corticogenesis.
Synapse counts plateau at nearly twice adult levels, then decline through childhood/adolescence (Bourgeois & Rakic 1993; Huttenlocher & Dabholkar 1997).
Primary sulci form GW14–26; longitudinal fissure begins ~GW8 and completes GW22; secondary sulci GW30–35; tertiary sulci from GW36 into postnatal life.
Thalamocortical/corticothalamic pathways form in the late second trimester, complete by GW26; TC axons wait ~4 weeks on subplate neurons before connecting to cortical layer 4.
DTI: FA reaches asymptote earliest in projection, then commissural, then association fibers (association continuing into adulthood); Lebel et al. show robust FA increases ages 5–12 with wide individual variability.
Caveat (author-stated): most human postnatal timelines are extrapolated from rodents and limited human postmortem tissue, so timing and relative ordering of postnatal proliferation/migration/differentiation/regression in humans remain uncertain.

Connections

Anchors the new brain-development-neurogenomics topic hub: provides the canonical biological scaffold (neurogenesis, migration, cortical lamination, patterning, pruning, myelination) that the molecular/genomic developing-brain sources build on.
Developmental-regulation sibling: pairs with kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated alternative splicing) — this review supplies the cellular/temporal context (fetal→postnatal transitions) for those splicing programs.
Disease and regional-patterning context for parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (autism cortex): the Emx2/Pax6 arealization and experience-dependent neocortical refinement here underlie that study’s “attenuated cortical patterning” framing.
Developing-brain genomic/resource siblings: walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform, humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia/OPC-relevant), and the developing-brain proteome wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of — all profiling the molecular layer of the developmental stages this review describes anatomically.
Cell-type framing: the inhibitory interneuron origin (ganglionic eminences) and OPC/oligodendrocyte lineages described here motivate single-cell developing-brain atlases such as psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource.

Open questions

Which postnatal proliferation/migration/regression timelines, currently extrapolated from rodents, actually hold in humans? (Author-flagged uncertainty.)
What signaling pathways drive progenitor fate restriction over corticogenesis (cell-intrinsic vs host-environment cues)? Mechanisms remain “poorly defined.”
How much MRI cortical “thinning” is genuine regressive loss of neuronal processes vs intracortical myelination reclassifying gray as white? The relationship between cortical change and fiber-tract myelination is still unresolved.
What is the full functional scope of neuron-glia (OPC/oligodendrocyte/astrocyte) interactions, given evidence some OPCs form synapses and signal directly?
How do molecular/genomic developing-brain datasets map onto the specific cellular milestones (E42 neurogenesis onset, GW26 thalamocortical completion, postnatal pruning) catalogued here?

wiki/sources/storey-2002-direct-approach.title: storey-2002-direct-approach-to-false-discovery
wiki/sources/storey-2002-direct-approach.content:
A direct approach to false discovery rates
One-line: Foundational statistics paper that reframes multiple-testing error control by fixing the rejection region and estimating the error rate, introducing point-estimation of the positive false discovery rate (pFDR) and the q-value.
Key takeaways

Inverts the traditional multiple-testing paradigm: rather than fixing an error rate alpha and estimating the rejection region (as sequential p-value methods like Benjamini-Hochberg do), the method fixes the rejection region and then estimates its corresponding error rate. This turns the problem into familiar point estimation, enabling confidence intervals and estimation of the true-null proportion.
Formalizes the positive false discovery rate (pFDR), the expected proportion of false positives among rejections conditioned on at least one rejection having occurred, and argues it is the more appropriate quantity than the ordinary false-discovery-rate because plain FDR is only controlled at level alpha/Pr(R>0).
Uses information in the observed p-values to estimate the number of true null hypotheses (pi_0), yielding a less stringent, more powerful procedure while retaining conservative bias (“control” in expectation). Power increases with more tests, the opposite of classical behavior.
Introduces the q-value, the pFDR analogue of the p-value: for each observed statistic it is the minimum pFDR attainable over nested rejection regions containing it. It removes the need to fix either the rejection region or the error rate beforehand.
Shows the Benjamini-Hochberg step-up method falls out naturally as a special case (taking the most conservative estimate pi_0 = 1), giving insight into why that procedure works.

Notable claims / data

Estimator of pi_0: pi_0_hat(lambda) = #{p_i > lambda} / ((1 - lambda) m) for a well-chosen tuning parameter lambda; a bootstrap MSE-minimization procedure (Section 9) picks lambda automatically.
pFDR estimate: pFDR_hat(gamma) = pi_0_hat(lambda) gamma / (Pr_hat(P <= gamma){1 - (1 - gamma)^m}); the estimator Q_hat is shown to be a maximum likelihood estimate of a limiting quantity that is a tight upper bound on pFDR (Theorem 5, corollary 1).
Simulation (m = 1000 tests, N(0,1) vs N(2,1), 1000 iterations, lambda = 1/2): the proposed method yields a substantial power increase over Benjamini-Hochberg, with one case giving over an 800% (eight times) increase in power. Estimated FDR was within about 0.1% of the true FDR and always conservative.
Near gamma -> 0, pFDR estimate goes to pi_0_hat(lambda) (a sensible conservative value) whereas the FDR estimate is driven to 0 merely because the region is small; the q-value stays steady, calibrating where enough information exists to make good statements. This is the main argument for pFDR/q-value over FDR near the origin, where the most significant p-values lie.
Theorem 2 establishes conservative bias (analogue of strong control) for all pi_0; Theorem 3 shows truncating estimates at 1 reduces mean-squared error.
Method assumes independent (or exchangeable, same-null-distribution) p-values; dependence effects are argued negligible for large m, with a companion treatment (Storey and Tibshirani) for strong dependence. Suggested application fields include genomics, wavelet analysis, and data mining.

Connections

Directly develops the pFDR and q-value framework foundational to genomics and high-dimensional inference used across biometrical-genetics and genome-wide-association studies.
Complements the companion paper in this batch giving the Bayesian interpretation of the positive false discovery rate.

Open questions

The exact operating characteristics of the calculated q-values (q_hat) are left as an open problem; simulations show conservative behavior but no formal analysis.
Whether better estimators of pFDR exist, and whether optimality theorems for estimating pFDR can be proven within specific frameworks.
Extension beyond independent p-values to general dependence and nonparametric situations (treated separately).

wiki/sources/storey-2003-positive-false-.title: storey-2003-positive-false-discovery-rate
wiki/sources/storey-2003-positive-false-.content:
The Positive False Discovery Rate: A Bayesian Interpretation and the q-value
One-line: Introduces the positive false discovery rate (pFDR) and the q-value, showing that under a mixture model the pFDR equals a simple Bayesian posterior probability and giving each tested statistic a multiple-testing significance measure analogous to the p-value.
Key takeaways

Defines the positive false discovery rate as pFDR = E[V/R | R > 0], a modification of Benjamini & Hochberg’s false discovery rate (FDR = E[V/R | R > 0]·Pr(R > 0)) that conditions on at least one rejection having occurred, avoiding the ill-defined R = 0 case.
Central result (Theorem 1): when test statistics are i.i.d. from a mixture (1 − H)·F0 + H·F1 with prior null probability π0, the pFDR over a fixed significance region Γ equals the posterior probability Pr(H = 0 | T ∈ Γ) = π0·(Type I error) / [π0·(Type I error) + π1·(Power)]. This does not depend on the number of tests m.
Introduces the q-value: q-value(t) = inf over nested significance regions containing t of pFDR(Γα), i.e., the minimum pFDR incurred when calling a statistic with value t significant. Under the mixture assumptions it is the minimum posterior Pr(H = 0 | T ∈ Γα) — a “Bayesian posterior p-value” that reverses the roles of {T ∈ Γ} and {H = 0} relative to the p-value.
pFDR bridges frequentist and Bayesian views: it is a compound multiple-testing error measure usable by both, and connects to classification theory via a dual quantity, the positive false nondiscovery rate (pFNR = E[T/W | W > 0] = Pr(H = 1 | T ∉ Γ)).

Notable claims / data

The q-value is NOT a “pFDR-adjusted p-value”: because the pFDR cannot be controlled by a sequential p-value procedure, no adjusted p-value can be defined from it.
Proposition 1 / Theorem 2: the q-value uses the same significance region as the p-value provided G1(α)/α is decreasing in α (holds when the alternative-p-value c.d.f. G1 is concave, e.g., for likelihood-ratio rejection regions). The q-value can be computed from either the original statistics or their p-values.
Under weak dependence (Theorem 4), the realized proportion of false discoveries, the FDR, and the pFDR all converge simultaneously over all significance regions to the same Bayesian posterior probability as m → ∞; holds for finite-block dependence and stationary ergodic sequences. Simulation (statistics correlated in blocks of 10, π0 = 0.8) shows pFDR_m converging to the limiting value (e.g., 0.137 at α = 0.005) with good agreement by m = 5000 even at correlation ρ up to ~0.8.
The Bayes rule set Bλ = {t : π0·f0(t) / [π0·f0(t) + π1·f1(t)] ≤ λ} minimizes both the Bayes error and the weighted average (1 − w)·pFDR(Γ) + w·pFNR(Γ) over all measurable Γ (Theorem 6, via the Neyman–Pearson lemma).
A cautionary remark: controlling FDR ≤ 0.1 when Pr(R > 0) = 0.5 only controls pFDR ≤ 0.2 — a real confusion that arose in a published genetic-dissection analysis.
Application: DNA microarray experiment (>6,000 genes, four irradiated vs four untreated human cell arrays from Efron, Tibshirani, Storey & Tusher 2001); argues that if only one per-gene significance measure is reported, it should be the q-value, since it accounts for multiple comparisons whereas the local posterior Pr(Hi = 0 | Ti = ti) does not.

Connections

Provides the statistical foundation for FDR/q-value thresholds pervasively used in genomics analyses across this wiki (differential expression, genome-wide-association-study, splicing-qtl and other large-scale association testing where thousands of hypotheses are tested simultaneously).
The mixture-model / posterior-probability framing links multiple-testing error control to Bayesian inference and classification theory, relevant to statistical practice in quantitative-genetics and psychiatric-genetics.

Open questions

The clean Theorem 1 posterior form requires i.i.d. statistics from a mixture; how far can real-data dependence structures deviate before the weak-dependence convergence of Theorem 4 fails?
The paper elucidates pFDR properties rather than estimation; practical accuracy depends on estimating π0, G0, G1, developed in companion work (Storey 2002a; Storey, Taylor & Siegmund 2004).
How best to jointly report the local posterior Pr(Hi = 0 | Ti = ti) and the q-value, given both carry complementary information.

wiki/sources/storey-2003-statistical-sig.title: storey-2003-statistical-significance-genomewide-studies
wiki/sources/storey-2003-statistical-sig.content:
Statistical significance for genomewide studies
One-line: Proposes the q value, a false-discovery-rate-based measure of significance, as a practical and automatically calibrated criterion for genomewide studies that test thousands of features simultaneously.
Key takeaways

Genomewide studies test thousands of features (genes, nucleotide words, SNP markers) against a null hypothesis; traditional per-test p-value cutoffs and familywise-error control (e.g. Bonferroni) are either too liberal or, in the many-true-alternatives setting, far too conservative.
The paper reframes significance around the false discovery rate (FDR) — the expected proportion of truly null features among those called significant — as a sensible balance between true and false positives, a concept also central to the related storey-2002 and storey-2003 work in this batch.
The q value assigns each feature its own FDR-based significance: it is the minimum FDR (technically minimum positive FDR) at which that feature can be called significant. Thresholding q values at level alpha yields a set of significant features whose expected false-positive proportion is alpha.
Crucially the q value is NOT the probability a given feature is a false positive; it is the expected proportion of false positives among all features as or more extreme.
A fully automated estimator computes q values from a list of p values, plus an estimate of pi-0 (proportion of truly null features) using a natural cubic spline fit of pi-0(lambda) evaluated at lambda approaching 1. The QVALUE software implements this.

Notable claims / data

The q value is the FDR analog of the p value: p value = minimum false positive rate at which a feature is called significant; q value = minimum positive FDR. The conditional probabilities are swapped (Pr(null | significant) for pFDR vs Pr(significant | null) for the false positive rate).
pi-0 estimator: #{p_i > lambda} / (m(1 - lambda)). On the Hedenfalk et al. breast-cancer microarray data (3,170 genes after filtering), lambda = 0.5 and the automated spline method both give pi-0 = 0.67, implying at least 33% of genes are differentially expressed between BRCA1- and BRCA2-mutation-positive tumors.
Thresholding q values at 0.05 yields 160 significant genes, of which about 8 are expected false positives; 117 of the 160 are overexpressed in BRCA1 tumors, supporting prior DNA-repair/apoptosis claims.
Under “weak dependence” (dependence whose effect vanishes as feature count grows to infinity), two large-m guarantees hold: calling all features with q <= alpha gives FDR <= alpha, and estimated q values are simultaneously conservative for the true q values across all features at once.
The original Benjamini-Hochberg FDR method assumes pi-0 = 1 and is too conservative for genomics: controlling FDR at 0.03/0.05/0.07 finds 80/160/231 genes with the proposed method vs only 21/88/153 with the earlier method — a substantial power loss.
Motivating examples span differential expression, exonic splicing enhancer (hexamer) detection (Fairbrother et al., 238 significant hexamers), yeast expression-QTL linkage (Brem et al.), and transcription-factor binding sites (Lee et al.) — all cases where investigators used multiple awkward p-value cutoffs the q value replaces with one interpretable measure.

Connections

Directly extends the false-discovery-rate and q-value framework developed in the companion storey-2002 and storey-2003 papers ingested in this batch.
Provides a multiple-testing significance measure applicable to genome-wide-association-study scans and to splicing-qtl / eqtl mapping, where thousands or millions of feature-marker p values must be thresholded.
Relevant to quantitative-genetics and biometrical-genetics workflows that screen many loci or features simultaneously.

Open questions

Whether the weak-dependence assumption holds must be assessed per application; strong or long-range dependence between features can degrade q-value accuracy.
Correctly calibrated p values are a load-bearing assumption; miscalibrated (non-uniform under the null) p values require erring toward overestimation.
The paper computes pi-0 only for the microarray example and notes it would be interesting to compute analogous truly-alternative-proportion estimates for the other genomewide examples.

wiki/sources/su-2018-alternative-splicin.title: su-2018-alternative-splicing-in-neurogenesis-and
wiki/sources/su-2018-alternative-splicin.content:
Alternative Splicing in Neurogenesis and Brain Development
One-line: A 2018 review surveying how alternative splicing, driven by neuron-specific splicing regulators, controls each step of nervous-system development — stem-cell self-renewal, neuronal fate specification, migration, and synaptogenesis — and how splicing defects can cause brain disorders.
Key takeaways

Alternative splicing occurs at exceptionally high frequency in brain tissue and contributes to cell-fate decisions, neuronal migration, axon guidance, and synaptogenesis. ~95% of human multi-exon genes are alternatively spliced.
Splicing outcomes depend on the interplay of trans-acting splicing regulators (splicing-factors) and cis-elements, modulated by transcription rate, histone modifications, and chromatin structure (splicing-and-chromatin).
Timely, cell-type-specific expression (and post-translational modification) of neuron-specific splicing regulators is the central organizing theme: developmental “switches” of regulators reprogram the splicing landscape during fetal-to-adult transitions.
A sequential ptbp1 → PTBP2 (nPTB) switch during the progenitor-to-neuron transition is a master driver; many regulators (RBM4, Nova/Nova2, the Rbfox family, nSR100/SRRM4, srsf1, Sam68, Quaking) act on specific neuronal targets.
Alternative splicing-coupled nonsense-mediated decay (AS-NMD), including poison-exon inclusion, is used to post-transcriptionally tune levels of key neuronal mRNAs.
Splicing also reprograms epigenetic enzymes (G9a, LSD1), metabolism (PKM2→PKM1 switch), centriolar dynamics (Ninein), and synaptic adhesion (neurexins/neuroligins) during neurogenesis.

Notable claims / data

~95% of human multi-exon genes undergo alternative splicing (citing Pan et al. 2008 pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing; Wang et al. 2008 wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human).
PTBP1→PTBP2 switch: PTBP1 suppresses PTBP2 exon 10 inclusion, producing an NMD-targeted isoform, restricting PTBP2 in non-neuronal cells/NPCs; PTBP1 is itself downregulated by neuron-specific miR-124. nSR100/SRRM4 promotes PTBP2 exon 10 inclusion to maintain PTBP2 in neurons.
RBM4 acts like PTBP1 (CU-rich preference) to suppress PTBP1/PTBP2 exon 11/10 in myoblasts; during neuronal differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells, RBM4 induces skipping of mammalian-specific PTBP1 exon 9, yielding a PTBP1 isoform with compromised splicing activity (Su et al. 2017). PTBP2 lacks an exon-9 equivalent, so is resistant to this RBM4 control.
Rbfox family members autoregulate to produce a truncated-RRM dominant-negative isoform; RBFOX3 switches from truncated to full-length in a development-dependent manner, required for late neuronal differentiation.
Table 1 catalogs regulator→target effects on neuronal phenotypes: nSR100/SRRM4→Protrudin (neurite outgrowth); Ptbp1&2→PSD-95/Dlg4 exon 18 (glutamatergic neuron development); Ptbp1→Flna poison exon (brain malformation); Ptbp2→Dnm1 exons 9a/9b (seizures, behavioral deficits); Nova2→Dab1 exon 7bc/9bc (radial + Purkinje migration); Rbfox3→Numb exon 12 (differentiation); Rbfox1→Snap25 exons 5a/5b (seizure); SRSF1→ApoER2 exon 19 (synapse formation); hnRNP H1/H2→TRF2 exon 7 (differentiation); RBM4→Numb (differentiation, outgrowth).
Metabolic switch: embryonic PKM2 → adult PKM1 (mutually exclusive exons) during brain development; RBM4 antagonizes PTBP1 to promote the PKM2→PKM1 switch, raising oxygen consumption and facilitating neuronal differentiation. Quaking (Qki5) maintains neural stem cells by preventing the PKM2→PKM1 switch.
Migration: Nova2 suppresses Dab1 exon 9b/c; Nova2 knockout causes cortical and cerebellar migration defects via aberrant exon-9bc Dab1. SRSF1 inhibits ApoER2 exon 19 inclusion (reduced in Alzheimer’s brain); antisense oligonucleotides blocking SRSF1 sites show therapeutic potential.
Disease links cited but not emphasized: familial dysautonomia (IKBKAP 5′ splice-site mutation → NMD), RBFOX1 in autism spectrum disorder, Flna mis-splicing in periventricular nodular heterotopia.
Caveat: this is a narrative review (mostly mouse/cell-line mechanistic studies plus some human organoid/fetal neocortex data), not a primary study; no new dataset or quantitative meta-analysis.

Connections

Companion developmental-splicing reviews: Baralle and Giudice 2017 (baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator, cited directly) and Kalsotra & Cooper-style developmentally regulated splicing (kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated).
Mechanistic backbone overlaps the proteome-diversity framing of nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome and the human-tissue isoform landscape of wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human / pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing.
Disease/mis-splicing sibling: scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (familial dysautonomia, AS-NMD themes echo here).
Brain alternative-splicing landscape sibling: park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative.
Core regulator ptbp1; also touches srsf1 (ApoER2) and Drosophila dscam-style cell-specific wiring (here exemplified by Dscam2).

Open questions

How exactly do the four Numb isoforms (exons 3/9) differentially modulate Notch to set NPC fate — the mechanism remains incompletely known.
Are splice isoforms of synthetic/metabolic enzymes (e.g., PKMs) coordinately switched in response to dynamic neuroenergetic demands (glutamatergic stimulation, hypoxia)?
Can single-cell and in situ / spatial sequencing resolve alternative splicing at the single-neuron level across complex brain networks, and reveal pathological splicing programs cell-by-cell?
Whether RBM4 promotion of Nova-1 exon 4 inclusion (shown in brown adipocytes) also operates in neurons is unresolved.

wiki/sources/sugathan-2014-chd8-regulate.title: sugathan-2014-chd8-regulates-neurodevelopmental-pathways-associated
wiki/sources/sugathan-2014-chd8-regulate.content:
CHD8 regulates neurodevelopmental pathways associated with autism spectrum disorder in neural progenitors
One-line: Suppressing the strong-effect autism gene chd8 to heterozygous-loss levels in human neural progenitor cells, then integrating RNA-seq with genome-wide CHD8 ChIP-seq, reveals that CHD8 drives a large network of mostly indirect neurodevelopmental gene-expression changes on which many ASD genes converge.
Key takeaways

chd8 is an ATP-dependent SNF2-family chromatin remodeler and one of the most penetrant autism risk genes; the authors mimic heterozygous loss-of-function via lentiviral shRNA knockdown (38-69% reduction) in human iPSC-derived NPCs (line GM8330-8).
A limited set of direct CHD8 regulatory targets produces a much larger secondary network of expression changes, connecting CHD8 to broad neurodevelopment and gene-regulation.
Genes indirectly down-regulated (lacking CHD8 binding) are enriched for synapse formation, neuron differentiation, cell adhesion, and axon guidance; directly CHD8-bound genes are enriched for chromatin modification and transcriptional regulation.
ASD genes converge on shared pathways: SFARI/AutismKB genes enrich among indirectly down-regulated loci, while de novo loss-of-function ASD genes enrich among CHD8-bound genes, mapping onto distinct fetal gene-coexpression-network modules.
In vivo chd8 suppression in zebrafish reproduces the human macrocephaly phenotype via disturbed early neurogenesis.

Notable claims / data

CHD8 suppression altered expression of 1,756 genes (nominal P<0.05; 369 at q<0.05), 64.9% up-regulated (1,140 up vs 616 down).
ChIP-seq with three independent antibodies gave 7,324 replicated binding sites (MACS2, q<0.05) marking 5,658 genes; 83% of sites fall in active TSS chromatin states (H3K4me3) vs 1% genome-wide.
Only 522 CHD8-bound genes were differentially expressed (9.2% of bound genes; 29.7% of DE genes), indicating most expression changes are indirect.
ASD-gene enrichment: down-regulated genes P=3.25x10-8 (OR 2.78, SFARI/AutismKB); indirectly down-regulated & unbound P=1.09x10-9 (OR 3.39). De novo LoF ASD genes (Willsey et al.) enriched among CHD8-bound genes (P=4.34x10-3) but not down-regulated set.
Strong cancer enrichment among CHD8-bound genes (TCGA P=1.55x10-58, OR 1.80; also Lawrence and COSMIC sets), largely independent of differential expression, suggesting cell-type-specific regulation.
Specificity: across 184 NHGRI GWAS Catalog gene lists, no trait approached ASD/cancer significance (top was “height”, P=4.46x10-6); schizophrenia and bipolar sets enriched among down-regulated unbound genes.
Top motifs at CHD8 sites match CTCF (a known interactor) and YY1; strongest down-regulated neurodevelopmental genes include SCN2A, DLG2, SHANK3, LAMA4, NCAM1, MEGF10.
Zebrafish: splice-blocking morpholinos caused 12% head-size increase (P<0.0001), ectopic HuC/D in 65% of morphants, and increased phospho-histone H3+ proliferating cells (518 vs 408, P=0.0018).

Connections

Extends chd8 biology from a genetic risk factor to a mechanistic hub linking chromatin regulation and neurodevelopment.
Complements coexpression-based ASD studies by adding causal perturbation, connecting to gene-coexpression-network and psychiatric-genetics framings of autism.
Situates CHD8 among early fetal transcriptional regulators acting on later synaptic modules, relevant to neurogenesis and progenitor (radial glia) biology.

Open questions

Which direct CHD8 target(s) mediate the large indirect down-regulation of neurodevelopmental genes?
Why do most cancer-associated CHD8-bound genes show no expression change in NPCs — what cell-type or cofactor specificity governs CHD8 regulation?
How do direct (early fetal) versus indirect (later synaptic) mechanisms combine across differentiation stages and brain tissue beyond a single NPC line?

wiki/sources/sun-2023-crosstalk-between-.title: sun-2023-crosstalk-between-rna-m6a
wiki/sources/sun-2023-crosstalk-between-.content:
Crosstalk between RNA m6A and DNA methylation regulates transposable element chromatin activation and cell fate in human pluripotent stem cells
One-line: In human embryonic stem cells, the RNA m6A reader YTHDC2 keeps the primate-specific transposable element LTR7/HERV-H active by recruiting the DNA demethylase TET1, and this axis restrains neural differentiation.
Key takeaways

Introduces CARGO-BioID (cargo-bioid), a CRISPR/dCas9-TurboID proximity-proteomics method that captured 144 proteins enriched at LTR7/HERV-H loci in hESCs, including the m6A reader YTHDC2.
YTHDC2, an RNA-binding protein with an m6A-binding YTH domain, occupies 1,307 LTR7 elements; occupied LTR7s carry higher H3K27ac and greater chromatin-accessibility but lower H3K9me3.
Reveals crosstalk between RNA m6A and DNA 5mC: YTHDC2 recruits the demethylase TET1 to strip dna-methylation from LTR7/HERV-H, preventing epigenetic silencing.
TEs act here as co-opted cis-regulatory-elements; the YTHDC2/LTR7 axis inhibits the switch from pluripotency toward the neuronal fate (neurogenesis).

Notable claims / data

YTHDC2 knockout reduced HERV-H, Gag, Pol and Env RNA by ~60–80%, and nascent HERV-H transcripts to ~20% of control; loss was transcriptional, not from RNA decay or loss of pluripotency.
KO caused gain of H3K9me3 (1,573 LTR7s) and loss of H3K27ac (1,552 LTR7s); YTHDC2-regulated LTR7s (1,661) overlap the 1,307 YTHDC2-bound LTR7s (chi-squared P < 0.0001).
Rescue: WT YTHDC2 restored HERV-H; the m6A-binding-dead YTHDC2ΔYTH failed to rescue, while helicase-dead E317Q rescued partially — m6A binding is indispensable.
6,172 YTHDC2 RIP-seq peaks (97.9% in repeats); HERV-H and LTR7 RNAs are the two most enriched repeat RNAs; a canonical m6A GGACU motif is enriched (P = 1e−131 RIP; 1e−116 eCLIP); methylated RNA probe binds YTHDC2 more strongly by EMSA.
YTHDC2 KO or LTR7 CRISPRi (dCas9-KRAB) raised neuronal markers ~6–8-fold (neural induction) without disrupting self-renewal; the LTR7/HERV-H-derived lncRNA LINC-ROR is a downstream effector restraining neural differentiation.

Connections

A regulation-of-TE/epigenetics counterpart to splicing-and-chromatin work in the wiki; connects RNA modification machinery to dna-methylation and chromatin-accessibility control of cis-regulatory-elements.
The hESC-to-neuron fate angle links to neurogenesis and neurodevelopmental differentiation themes.

Open questions

Is the YTHDC2/TET1 crosstalk generalizable to other TE families or somatic/disease (e.g. cancer) contexts beyond hESCs?
How is m6A deposition on HERV-H RNAs itself regulated, and does YTHDC2 helicase activity matter in other settings?
What is the full effector set downstream of LINC-ROR governing the pluripotency-to-neuron switch?

wiki/sources/sun-2024-strategies-for-dis.title: sun-2024-strategies-for-dissecting-the-complexity
wiki/sources/sun-2024-strategies-for-dis.content:
Strategies for dissecting the complexity of neurodevelopmental disorders
One-line: A Girirajan-lab review cataloging the experimental toolkit — model organisms, human iPSCs, single-cell sequencing, and massively parallel reporter assays coupled with CRISPR — used to functionally dissect the genetic complexity of neurodevelopmental disorders (NDDs).
Key takeaways

NDDs are genetically heterogeneous: the same individual can carry many variant types — SNVs, CNVs, simple tandem repeats (STRs), and polygenic common variants (PRS) — inherited or de novo, in coding and noncoding regions. Analogized to Kurosawa’s Rashomon, where each variant type independently “explains” the disorder.
Three genetic architecture models are proposed for NDDs: monogenic (single large-effect variant, e.g. ADNP → Helsmoortel–Van Der Aa syndrome, MECP2 → Rett, KDM5B recessive), oligogenic (two-plus moderate-effect rare variants jointly cause disease, e.g. Bardet–Biedl, Hirschsprung), and polygenic (many common small-effect variants; autism genome-wide-association-study loci + PRS enrichment).
Model organisms are a “Swiss army knife”: C. elegans, Drosophila, Xenopus laevis/tropicalis, zebrafish (Danio rerio), and mouse trade off human-genome conservation against experimental throughput/cost. Higher-conservation models permit deeper brain-function study; lower-conservation ones enable higher-throughput candidate-gene screens.
Human iPSC models fill the gaps left by cross-species genome differences: patient-derived or CRISPR-isogenic lines differentiated (2D or 3D) into NPCs, neurons, astrocytes, microglia, oligodendrocytes, and brain organoids for cell-type-specific effects.
Single-cell sequencing (scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq) resolves cellular heterogeneity, cell-type composition shifts, and developmental trajectories; combining it with CRISPR screens (Perturb-seq, CROP-seq) enables high-throughput, single-cell-resolution functional characterization of NDD gene pleiotropy.
MPRAs functionally test thousands of noncoding regulatory variants in parallel; lentiviral (LV-MPRA, lentiMPRA) and AAV-MPRA variants extend them into neuronal contexts and in vivo brain development.

Notable claims / data

Individuals with the 22q11.2 deletion plus higher common-variant burden show more severe schizophrenia/cognitive defects than deletion alone — evidence common variants modulate NDD trajectory.
C. elegans: McDiarmid et al. tested 26 behavioral phenotypes across 135 strains of autism-gene orthologs; Wong et al. screened missense variants in 11 autism genes via CRISPR knock-in.
Drosophila: RNAi screen of 26 22q11.2 orthologs found night owl regulates sleep via GABAergic signaling and Nf1; Iyer et al. measured 565 pairwise RNAi knockdowns of 16p11.2 orthologs, finding epistatic proliferation effects.
Zebrafish: Mendes et al. found brain-volume/microglia/dopaminergic activity changes in CHD8/SCN2A/POGZ mutants; a screen of 162 interactions among 19 16p11.2 homologs found doc2a–fam57ba nonadditive interaction.
Mouse: landmark study identified Tbx1 as causal for 22q11.2 heart defects; increasing locus coeruleus noradrenergic activity rescued delayed motor learning in a 16p11.2 deletion model.
Drug repurposing in models: Hope et al. screened 1040 FDA drugs in Drosophila for NGLY1 deficiency; Hoffman et al. profiled 550 psychoactive compounds against cntnap2ab zebrafish, implicating GABA/NMDA dysregulation.
~99% of the human genome is noncoding; Brandler et al. (829 autism families) and Turner et al. (516 families, 640 de novo regulatory variants) show noncoding-variant enrichment near NDD genes; 71% of tested rare human-enhancer variants had pathogenic roles in mouse limb development.
Box 1: RareComb (Pournaraja & Girirajan) uses the Apriori algorithm to find rare-variant combinations enriched in cases; polygenic modeling via BOLT-LMM; a convolutional neural network (Liu et al.) modeled nonadditive SNP effects to distinguish ADHD cases. Integrative modeling (Antaki et al.) explained ~4% of autism phenotype variance combining de novo, inherited rare, and PRS.
Box 2: mechanics of Perturb-seq (gRNA barcodes/GBCs + scRNA-seq) vs CROP-seq (U6-driven gRNA in the 3’ LTR, no GBC, but limited to single gRNA by insertion-size constraints).

Connections

Complements lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric and deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory on massively-parallel-reporter-assay for psychiatric/regulatory variants.
Overlaps with single-cell functional-genomics sources: single-cell-crispr-screen, pooled-crispr-screen, and the perturb-seq entity.
Genetic-architecture framing (monogenic/oligogenic/polygenic, PRS, missing-heritability) connects to psychiatric-genetics and genome-wide-association-study pages; oligogenic/RareComb work ties to epistasis.
iPSC/organoid content links to brain-organoids, neurogenesis, neuronal-cell-types, and neurodevelopment; autism focus links to autism.
Noncoding-variant functional assessment connects to noncoding-variants and cis-regulatory-elements; CRISPR tooling to genome-editing.

Open questions

Do current genetic models accurately reflect the true complexity/mechanisms of NDDs? How to identify variant combinations that exacerbate vs suppress phenotypes from high-dimensional data?
What roles do environment, somatic mosaicism, non-neuronal cell types (glia, astrocytes), and gene regulatory networks play?
Can distinct disease trajectories be mapped to genetic disruptions at specific developmental stages/cell types, toward patient-specific therapeutic targets?
Is the proposed omnigenic model (all expressed genes act in concert) a better frame for complex NDDs than monogenic/oligogenic/polygenic?

wiki/sources/suresh-2023-comparative-sin.title: suresh-2023-comparative-single-cell-transcriptomic-analysis-primate
wiki/sources/suresh-2023-comparative-sin.content:
Comparative single-cell transcriptomic analysis of primate brains highlights human-specific regulatory evolution
One-line: Single-nucleus atlases of the middle temporal gyrus from five primates are aligned into 57 homologous cell types to distinguish deeply conserved transcriptional programmes from a small set of genes showing human-specific expression and co-expression divergence.
Key takeaways

Aligning single-nucleus MTG atlases of human, chimp, gorilla, macaque and marmoset (spanning ~45 million years) yields 57 homologous cell types shared across all five primates, providing a common sample space to compare orthologue expression, relevant to human-brain-evolution and neuronal-cell-types.
Orthologue expression profiles are generally well conserved, and the degree of conservation recapitulates known primate phylogeny; marker genes and transcription factors are especially conserved, indicating a shared molecular landscape of the cortex.
Gene co-expression neighbourhoods (built as gene-coexpression-network from pseudobulk cell types) are highly conserved not only across primates but across metazoa, with high conservation even in fruit fly and roundworm.
Integrating single-cell expression divergence with cross-species co-expression conservation isolates 139 genes with human-specific functional divergence, which tend to evolve under relaxed mutational constraint.
Genes with divergent human expression are enriched for synapse function and are associated with brain disorders, connecting the findings to psychiatric-genetics.

Notable claims / data

574,156 nuclei passed QC (341,469 excitatory, 158,188 inhibitory, 74,499 non-neuronal); each species categorized into 3 classes and 24 subclasses.
Semi-supervised MetaNeighbor mapped 594 within-species clusters to 86 cross-species clusters; 57 were shared by all five primates (>80% of each species’ clusters), with cross-validation mean adjusted Rand index = 0.999.
14,131 human genes with 1:1 orthologues in all non-human primates (from OrthoDB) were scored with an “expressolog score” (AUROC over 57 matched cell types); scores range from 0.93 (human vs great apes) to 0.8 (human vs marmoset).
24% of genes (3,383 of 14,131) show extensive human/non-human differences; of these, 98% diverge in only one cell class and nearly half diverge in non-neuronal cell types.
Only 139 of 3,383 genes (<1% of orthologues) show both divergent expression and divergent co-expression connectivity exclusive to humans; average co-expression conservation across distant species is 0.89, making this a conservative estimate.
Named human-specific candidates include NHEJ1, GTF2H2, C2 and BBS5, which show higher tolerance to inactivation.
Isoform-level expressolog analysis (SSv4 data, 28 cell types in human, chimp, gorilla) gave AUROC = 0.56, only slightly above chance, reflecting extensive isoform switching across apes.

Connections

Uses alternative splicing / isoform diversity across primates as a secondary axis, touching alternative-splicing and splicing-and-evolution.
Builds on meta-analytic co-expression resources (CoCoCoNet, Gemma) that aggregate bulk rna-seq to test co-regulation conservation, complementing the gene-coexpression-network framing.
Human-specific divergent genes overlap cortex-specific expression QTLs and brain-disorder gene sets, linking cortical neurodevelopment to disease.

Open questions

Do the 139 human-specific genes reflect adaptive selection or drift under relaxed constraint, and can this be tested directly?
Would deeper single-cell sequencing and more high-quality primate datasets refine cell type-specific co-expression rewiring beyond current statistical power?
How does pervasive isoform switching across apes shape function when isoform expression profiles are only weakly species-specific?

wiki/sources/szabo-2016-detecting-circul.title: szabo-2016-detecting-circular-rnas-bioinformatic
wiki/sources/szabo-2016-detecting-circul.content:
Detecting circular RNAs: bioinformatic and experimental challenges
One-line: A review of why genome-wide detection of circular RNAs (circRNAs) from RNA-seq is hard, cataloguing the experimental and bioinformatic biases that produce false positives and the statistical approaches needed to control them.
Key takeaways

circRNAs arise from back-splicing, a covalent linkage between a downstream 5’ splice site and an upstream 3’ splice site (the reverse arrangement of canonical linear splicing), and lack poly(A) tails and 5’ caps. Their pervasive expression was only discovered in 2012, having been masked by poly(A) selection in standard rna-seq library prep and by bioinformatic filters in mainstream splice-detection algorithms.
circRNAs are an ancient, broadly conserved feature: expressed across the eukaryotic tree (humans to flies/worms, plus yeast, plants, amoeba, Plasmodium). Most are non-coding; only CDR1as and SRY have a known function (microRNA sponges).
Accurate detection of even linear spliced alignments is an unsolved problem (algorithms disagree on recall/FDR even with a near-identical reference genome), and circRNA detection inherits all of these difficulties plus its own.
Many circRNA-specific tools exist (MapSplice, find_circ, segemehl, CIRI, circExplorer, circRNA_finder, KNIFE, DCC, etc.), but they show little overlap in predictions — up to 40% of circRNAs are reported by only a single algorithm — and there is no gold standard for assessing genome-wide sensitivity/specificity.
The authors argue that read count is a poor predictor of whether a junction (linear or circular) is truly expressed, and push for statistical scoring (FDR estimation) over hard read-count thresholds.

Notable claims / data

circRNA abundance is roughly 2–4% of total mRNA in cells (~1–3% of mRNA level in common cell lines), higher in some cell types such as platelets; sizes range from <100 nt to >4 kb, most commonly a few hundred nt, usually 2–3 exons in humans.
Experimental biases: poly(A) selection depletes circRNAs; rRNA depletion retains them; RNase R (a 3’→5’ exonuclease) digests linear RNA and enriches circRNAs; oligo(dT) priming biases against circRNAs while random priming does not.
Artefacts mimicking back-splices: reverse-transcriptase template switching can account for 34–55% of detected isoforms for some genes (worse for genes with shared homologous exons); ligation can generate chimeric cDNAs; RT strand displacement / rolling-circle amplification inflates abundance estimates for small circles. Filtering out low-read circRNAs does not eliminate these.
Benchmarks found 31–76% of circRNAs detected in an rRNA– library were not re-detected in enrichment libraries, and 12–28% were depleted by RNase R (i.e. false positives). Algorithms with highest specificity had lowest sensitivity and vice versa.
RNase R validation is itself unreliable: validated circRNAs (CDR1as, NCX1, a CAMSAP1 intron-retention isoform, MAN1A2, Drosophila Pangolin/Ank2) are RNase R–sensitive, and <50% of circRNAs resistant in one replicate are resistant in a second from the same lab.
Statistical point: the 95% CI for the RNase R+/RNase R– read ratio is [0.75–1.33] at 100 reads but [0.23–4.34] at 5 reads — so naive fold-change comparisons without confidence intervals and depth normalization give discrepant conclusions. The authors recommend the circular-to-linear splicing ratio (normalization-free) and consider depletion in poly(A)+ libraries a better false-positive proxy than failure to enrich under RNase R.
A statistical approach relaxing the canonical U2 requirement uncovered the first circRNAs spliced by the minor U12 spliceosome (e.g. RANBP17, which produces three distinct circular isoforms from one locus). Lariats (5’–2’ linkage) are a confounding RNase R–resistant species but constitute <0.17% of reported circRNAs.
Conserved, algorithm-robust observations: ubiquity of circRNA expression, no correlation between linear and circular levels from the same gene, enrichment of longer flanking introns, and no global enrichment of microRNA-binding sites.

Connections

A methods/bias review squarely in the splicing-methods and data-quality-and-bias space; complements rna-seq and contrasts with the linear-isoform reconstruction problem also relevant to long-read-rna-seq and heberle-2024-mapping-medically-relevant-rna-isoform.
Frames circRNAs as a class of alternative-splicing output that escaped detection, extending the alternative-splicing landscape surveyed in park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Mechanistically depends on splice-sites (canonical U2 GU–AG vs minor U12 signals) and the spliceosome; the U12-spliced circRNA finding ties circRNA biogenesis to minor-spliceosome usage.

Open questions

Can a genome-wide gold standard (e.g. in-vitro-transcribed circRNA ground-truth libraries, plus simulators that mix annotated/unannotated circular and linear isoforms) be built to benchmark sensitivity vs specificity?
What statistical normalization is appropriate for matched RNase R+/– and poly(A)+/– libraries so enrichment/depletion inferences are valid?
Do circRNAs have genuine biological function, and is genome-wide conservation real or an artefact of confounders such as Simpson’s paradox?
Are there yet other overlooked classes of linear/circular RNA isoforms — motivating reference-free approaches to quantify the full RNA repertoire of a cell?

wiki/sources/sziraki-2023-a-global-view-.title: sziraki-2023-a-global-view-of-aging
wiki/sources/sziraki-2023-a-global-view-.content:
A global view of aging and Alzheimer’s pathogenesis-associated cell population dynamics and molecular signatures in human and mouse brains
One-line: A whole-brain single-cell atlas built with the EasySci combinatorial-indexing platform that maps rare cell subtypes expanded or depleted in aging and Alzheimer’s disease across mouse and human brains.
Key takeaways

Introduces easysci, a low-cost single-cell combinatorial-indexing method (EasySci-RNA and EasySci-ATAC) recovering full gene-body coverage, enabling exon-level subclustering.
Builds a mouse brain atlas of 31 main neuronal-cell-types and 359 subclusters, resolving extremely rare populations (e.g., 21 pinealocytes, 35 tanycytes).
Links neurogenesis decline to aging: OB and dentate-gyrus neuroblasts/progenitors are among the most depleted subtypes across the adult lifespan.
Connects neurodegeneration to specific rare subtypes via two AD models, including a disease-associated microglia (DAM) and reactive oligodendrocyte expansion.
Paired chromatin-accessibility and rna-seq profiling validates cell subtypes and identifies subtype-specific TF regulators.

Notable claims / data

~1,469,111 mouse single-cell transcriptomes and 376,309 ATAC profiles from 20 mice across 5 conditions; ~20 billion paired-end reads.
EasySci-RNA prepares ~1M transcriptomes for ~US $700 library-prep cost; median 935 UMIs and 3,918 ATAC fragments per nucleus at shallow depth.
Aging (6→21 mo): 29 significantly changed subclusters; 14 expanded (incl. MG-9 DAM, Apoe+ Csf1+; reactive OLG-7, C4b+ Serpina3n+), >20 depleted.
7,135 aging-associated DE signatures across 359 subclusters.
Two AD models — EOAD 5xfad-mouse-model (16 changed subclusters) and LOAD APOE4/Trem2R47H (14) — share depletion of a mitochondrial-gene-rich CPEC-4 subtype and expansion of CPEC-6, IMN 1-13 (Col25a1+) and septal IMN 2-9.
118,240 human brain single-cell transcriptomes profiled to discern cell- and region-specific transcriptomic changes tied to AD pathogenesis.

Connections

Method contrast with droplet single-cell atlases such as velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal; complements exon-aware isoform work in the wiki’s splicing corpus.
Uses the 5xfad-mouse-model as its early-onset AD testbed, shared with park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing.
Extends single-cell brain cell-type taxonomy discussed in masland-2004-neuronal-cell-types and zeng-2017-neuronal-cell-type-classification-challenges.

Open questions

Do the mouse aging/AD rare-subtype dynamics (e.g., CPEC-4 depletion) map onto the human brain data, and are they causal versus reactive?
How does isoform/exon-level heterogeneity captured by full-gene-body coverage refine cell-type definitions beyond gene counts?
Are reactive oligodendrocyte and DAM expansions around the neurogenic niche druggable entry points for slowing age-related decline?

wiki/sources/takumi-2018-cnv-biology-in-.title: takumi-2018-cnv-biology-in-neurodevelopmental-disorders
wiki/sources/takumi-2018-cnv-biology-in-.content:
CNV biology in neurodevelopmental disorders
One-line: A review of copy number variants (CNVs) in autism and neuropsychiatric disorders, focusing on 15q11-13 and 16p11.2, and how human genetics, systems biology, and animal/cell models converge on shared pathophysiology.
Key takeaways

Copy number variants (deletions, duplications, inversions ranging Kb to Mb) contribute to human diversity and to disease, with many pathogenic CNVs shared across autism, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder — a core theme in psychiatric-genetics.
CNV pathogenesis is not simply a single driver gene: multifactorial effects, epigenetic alteration (dna-methylation, acetylome changes), and chromosomal structural changes all shape phenotypes, so copy number does not reliably predict expression level.
CNVs arise via non-allelic homologous recombination (NAHR) between low-copy repeats; mosaic CNVs are abundant in the human brain and may arise in progenitor cells during neurodevelopment.
Integrative research combining human genetics, systems biology, and animal/cell models (including CRISPR/Cas9 chromosome engineering, a genome-editing application) is needed to dissect mechanisms.

Notable claims / data

CNV incidence in disease is rare (e.g. <1% in ASD) but highly penetrant; ASD prevalence ~1 in 68 children (US), male-biased (~1 in 42 boys vs 1 in 189 girls); monozygotic twin concordance 60-90%.
Large-scale studies converge on recurrent loci: 15q11-13, 16p11.2, 7q11.23, 1q21.1, 2p16.3 (NRXN1), 22q11.21, 3q29, 22q13.33 (SHANK3); one WGS resource found 189/2620 (7.2%) subjects carry pathogenic CNVs.
15q11-13 dup: maternal duplications are recognized high-penetrance ASD risk; paternal duplications, once considered benign, are pathogenic with ~20% penetrance. The patDp/+ (paternal 15q dup) mouse — the first CNV mouse model, via chromosome engineering — shows impaired social interaction, abnormal USV, altered dendritic spine turnover, and reduced brain serotonin; developmental serotonin recovery rescues phenotypes.
16p11.2: KCTD13 proposed as a major driver (zebrafish), but Kctd13 KO mice show no brain-size increase; KCTD13-Cul3-RhoA and MAPK3/ERK hubs implicated. Ingenuity Pathway Analysis of 271 genes from 9 CNVs found top canonical pathways Rho-family GTPase signaling (p=1.04E-3) and RhoA signaling (p=3.58E-3).
UBE3A (maternally expressed) drives Angelman syndrome; targets include Ephexin-5, Arc, ALDH1A2/retinoic acid, and BMAL1 (circadian). Topoisomerase and G9a/EHMT2 inhibitors can unsilence paternal alleles — candidate epigenetic therapies. Dup15q postmortem brain shows global DNA hypomethylation of synaptic genes (whole-genome bisulfite seq).

Connections

Extends psychiatric-genetics and autism to structural-variant biology, complementing wiki coverage of ASD transcriptomics and single-nucleotide risk.
The imprinting/epigenetic emphasis (DNA hypomethylation, H3K27ac) ties to dna-methylation as a modifier of copy-number effects.
dCas9-Tet1/dCas9-Dnmt3a proposed for therapeutic methylation editing connects to genome-editing.

Open questions

How does serotonin signaling causally shape social behavior (possibly via oxytocin)?
Since gene expression is not linearly tied to copy number, which epigenetic and 3D-structural mechanisms explain CNV phenotypes?
Can dosage compensation or methylation editing become viable CNV therapeutics given the difficulty of tight in vivo expression control?
How much do mouse genetic background and reproducibility confound CNV behavioral models?

wiki/sources/tamura-2025-crispr-activati.title: tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related
wiki/sources/tamura-2025-crispr-activati.content:
CRISPR activation for SCN2A-related neurodevelopmental disorders
One-line: A proof-of-concept that CRISPR activation (CRISPRa) of the endogenous functional allele can rescue neurophysiological deficits caused by SCN2A haploinsufficiency — in mice (including systemic AAV delivery that protects against induced seizures) and in human stem-cell-derived neurons — showing that upregulating an existing gene copy is a viable therapeutic route where classical gene replacement fails.
Key takeaways

The problem: haploinsufficiency. Most single-gene neurodevelopmental disorders act via haploinsufficiency — one of two gene copies is non-functional and the residual copy makes too little mRNA/protein. SCN2A haploinsufficiency is one of the most frequent causes of NDD, often with autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disability, and (in some children) refractory epilepsy.
Why not gene replacement. SCN2A encodes the neuronal voltage-gated sodium channel Na_v1.2; its coding sequence is ~6 kb, exceeding the ~4.7 kb rAAV packaging capacity (minus ~1,700 bp for regulatory elements). 117 of 227 haploinsufficient NDD genes exceed rAAV cargo limits, ruling out traditional AAV gene replacement.
The approach: cis-regulation therapy (CRT) via CRISPRa. A nuclease-dead Cas9 (here Staphylococcus aureus sadCas9) fused to the transcriptional activator VP64, plus an sgRNA targeting the Scn2a promoter, upregulates the endogenous allele rather than delivering a new gene copy (see scn2a-crispra, genome-editing).
Rescue in a conditional mouse. In an adolescent Scn2a conditional knock-in mouse (scn2a-conditional-ki-mouse; Scn2a+/βKI), Cre-mediated reactivation of the silenced allele around P30 restored intrinsic dendritic excitability, AP dV/dt, dendritic Ca2+ transients, and AMPA:NMDA ratio toward wild-type — demonstrating rescue is possible even at adolescent stages.
rAAV-CRISPRa is therapeutic in mice. Stereotaxic mPFC injection of rAAV sadCas9-VP64 + Scn2a-sgRNA into constitutive Scn2a+/− mice upregulated Scn2a mRNA ~1.5-fold and restored AP dV/dt, dendritic Ca2+ influx, and AMPA:NMDA ratio in mCherry-positive pyramidal neurons.
Systemic delivery works and prevents seizures. Intravenous (tail-vein / retro-orbital) rAAV-CRISPRa packaged in the blood-brain-barrier-penetrant PHP.eB serotype gave widespread brain expression, rescued electrophysiology, and reduced 4-aminopyridine (4-AP)-induced ictal spike frequency in Scn2a+/− mice to WT-like levels.
Translates to human neurons. SCN2A-rAAV-CRISPRa rescued blunted AP electrogenesis, low peak dV/dt, and ~19% axon-initial-segment lengthening in SCN2A+/− hESC-derived excitatory neurons.
Well tolerated / safety ceiling. Overexpression is a concern because SCN2A gain-of-function missense variants cause epilepsy; but CRISPRa raised Na_v1.2 only ~1.75× in WT and did not increase network excitability or seizure burden — the authors argue a physiological ceiling (auxiliary subunits, scaffolds, post-translational modification) caps effective Na_v1.2 density.

Notable claims / data

387 high-confidence NDD genes; 227 act via haploinsufficiency; 117 exceed rAAV cargo — motivating cis-regulation therapy.
SCN2A LoF incidence ≈ 300 births/year in the USA (~1 in 12,000).
Screened 10 sgRNAs (mouse Scn2a / human SCN2A promoters) with the Broad GPP tool in Neuro-2a / SH-SY5Y; selected guides increased Scn2a mRNA ~2-fold (mouse) and ~1.3–1.5-fold (human).
Activator = sadCas9-VP64 (3.3 kb), compact enough for rAAV; three guides increased mRNA and were AAV-DJ packaged.
Off-target check: RNA-seq (Neuro-2a, PHP.eB, systemic) found minimal off-target expression; Scn2a was the only upregulated Scn sodium-channel gene in its topologically associated domain; a few viral-response genes (Zfp697, Ifi44l, Csmd1) up, plus predicted (Cas-OFFinder) off-targets Ptpn14/Ptchd4 — none with major brain roles.
Compartmental NEURON models predicted AP peak dV/dt scales ~linearly with Na_v1.2 membrane density (0–200% of WT); empirical data matched, but treated cells never exceeded WT dV/dt.
AAV transgene expression persisted to 460 days (~16 months) by RT-qPCR — durable.

Connections

Central worked example of cis-regulation / CRISPR-activation gene therapy — complements genome-editing approaches that edit rather than modulate; the dCas9-VP64 activator here contrasts with nuclease and base/prime editors.
A concrete NDD-therapeutics counterpart to the descriptive/genetic brain-disorder work in the wiki (e.g. zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain, velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal); here the mechanism is engineered rescue rather than variant discovery.
Uses the same CRISPR-effector logic family as pooled functional screens like dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits and gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements — but as a therapeutic activator (VP64) targeted to a single promoter in vivo, not a screen.
Precedent within the field: prior Colasante/Yamagata dCas9-Sox/Scn1a activation for Dravet syndrome (refs in paper); this extends CRT to an excitatory-neuron sodium-channel haploinsufficiency and to systemic AAV.

Open questions

What is the critical developmental window? Rescue worked at adolescence, but NDD treatment “would probably be most beneficial when administered early, ideally before symptom onset” — timing vs. outcome remains open.
Because current sgRNAs upregulate both alleles, the strategy suits pure LoF variants but not variants where the mutant allele promotes gain-of-function — allele-specific targeting would be needed there.
Long-term safety of dCas9 immunogenicity, AAV toxicity, PHP.eB efficacy in primates (vs. mice), and off-target CRISPRa at scale still require non-human-primate assessment.
Does raising Na_v1.2 rescue non-electrophysiological, behavioral/circuit-refinement aspects of SCN2A NDD beyond the seizure and excitability phenotypes tested?

wiki/sources/tanudisastro-2024-sequencin.title: tanudisastro-2024-sequencing-characterizing-short-tandem-repeats
wiki/sources/tanudisastro-2024-sequencin.content:
Sequencing and characterizing short tandem repeats in the human genome
One-line: A methods review comparing short-read and long-read sequencing modalities and computational tools for genotyping short tandem repeats (STRs), a highly polymorphic and clinically important but historically under-characterized class of human variation.
Key takeaways

Short tandem repeats are 1–6-bp motif repeats occupying ~3–5% of the human genome (vs 1.5% for protein-coding genes); over 1 million variable loci exist, expansions in at least 60 cause disorders (Huntington disease, fragile X), and others influence complex traits like height.
Standard short-read sequencing struggles at STRs because of mapping ambiguity in repetitive regions, PCR stutter, GC bias, and the inability to size alleles longer than the read (~150 bp) or fragment length — many pathogenic expansions exceed these and can approach 10,000 bp.
Short-read callers fall into read-length-restricted tools (LobSTR, RepeatSeq, HipSTR, popSTR), tools that estimate alleles beyond read length via flanking/repetitive read classes (GangSTR, ExpansionHunter, STRetch, exSTRa, TREDPARSE), and catalogue-independent novel callers (ExpansionHunter Denovo, STRling); all require statistical inference to handle stutter.
Long-read sequencing (Oxford Nanopore, PacBio HiFi) spans very large alleles (>10 kb), yields haplotype-resolved genotypes, detects non-canonical motifs, and natively reads DNA methylation; sequence-based tools (PacmonSTR, RepeatHMM, Tandem-genotypes, TRGT, TRiCoLOR, RepLong, Straglr) and nanopore signal-based tools (NanoSatellite, STRique, DeepRepeat) trade off accuracy, cost, and speed.
Genome-wide STR genotyping links repeat-length dosage to gene expression (eSTRs) and traits; STRs sit near regulatory sequences, can act as cis-regulatory-elements, and their noncoding intronic/intergenic variation may explain part of the missing-heritability of polygenic traits identified by GWAS.

Notable claims / data

STR mutation rate is up to 10^4 times that of SNPs, so STRs are less likely to be in LD with nearby SNPs and can carry orthogonal association signal — only 14.2% of lead UK Biobank trait SNPs/indels were in LD with lead STRs/SVs.
popSTR was used to genotype STRs genome-wide across 150,119 UK Biobank individuals; EnsembleTR harmonizes ExpansionHunter, GangSTR, HipSTR and adVNTR calls as an ensemble approach.
Some STR loci increase transcription-factor binding affinity up to ~70-fold; eSTRs can have several times the effect size of the lead associated SNP.
eSTR effects are not always linear: FMR1 alleles of 55–200 CGG repeats raise transcription while >200 repeats silence via hypermethylation; the LPA kringle IV-2 VNTR shows a reversing inverse association at shorter alleles.
STR-containing transcripts can dysregulate mRNA processing including alternative splicing by altering splice acceptor/donor sites or sequestering rna-binding-proteins; STRs can influence local dna-methylation of CpG islands.
Disease/trait links: STR variants in autism are mostly paternally inherited expansions; rare expansions in schizophrenia (psychiatric-genetics) are enriched in postsynaptic/brain-expressed genes; microsatellite instability guides therapy in colorectal and endometrial cancer.
Targeted long-read enrichment (CRISPR–Cas9 adapter ligation; ONT ReadUntil/adaptive sampling) boosts on-target coverage — ReadUntil gave ~4.5× enrichment over standard long-read WGS for neurological STR loci.
Caveats: no community consensus on QC, catalogue/locus-boundary definitions (e.g., competing HTT repeat definitions), concordance criteria, or validation; STRs have high Mendelian-violation rates; long-read base-calling error and stutter still limit accuracy; STR software suffers from “abandonware.”

Connections

Sits in the genome-sequencing-and-references topic as a survey of sequencing-modality and reference/catalogue considerations (Tandem Repeats Finder catalogues, GRCh38 vs Telomere-to-Telomere and Human Pangenome graph references).
Complements existing wiki coverage of STR effects on splicing (a separate source covers that specialised topic); here the emphasis is on genotyping methodology rather than splicing mechanism.
Relevant to genome-wide-association-study, missing-heritability, noncoding-variants, cis-regulatory-elements, and rna-binding-proteins as an under-explored variant class for trait mapping.

Open questions

Can the community converge on consensus catalogues, QC guidelines, and validation truth sets so STR call sets from different tools and cohorts become comparable?
Will long-read WGS become cost-effective enough for population scale, and can base-calling error (especially ONT) and stutter be reduced sufficiently for clinical-grade STR calling?
How do STR effects on gene expression play out in cell-type-specific and developmental contexts (requiring single-cell approaches), and how much nonlinearity and sequence-interruption effect remains undiscovered?
Can a single highly accurate telomere-to-telomere WGS assay eventually capture STRs alongside all other variant classes, removing the need for separate STR-specific pipelines?

wiki/sources/tau-2010-normal-development.title: tau-2010-normal-development-of-brain-circuits
wiki/sources/tau-2010-normal-development.content:
Normal Development of Brain Circuits
One-line: A wide-ranging developmental-neuroscience review that traces how human neural circuits are built, refined, and pruned from the first synapse through adolescence, integrating histology, cellular/molecular biology, and neuroimaging to provide a “normal” baseline against which the developmental origins of neuropsychiatric illness can be read.
Key takeaways

Frames brain function as residing in neural circuits — from local microcircuits (clusters of interconnected neurons) to distributed networks of brain regions — that are neither present at birth nor fixed through life, but are sculpted by genetic programs interacting with environment and experience along a largely predictable timeline. Relevant to the brain-development-neurogenomics hub as a circuit-level scaffold for cell-type and disease-genetics work.
Lays out the earliest events: neural-tube formation at gestational age (GA) weeks 2–3; three then five vesicles forming forebrain/midbrain/hindbrain by week 4; rapid neuroblast proliferation in the ventricular zone (germinal matrix) by GA weeks 5–6; differentiation into neurons vs macroglia by GA week 8.
Describes radial (inside–out) migration along radial glia (deeper layers first), versus tangential migration of GABAergic interneurons from the ganglionic eminences; radial glia can themselves give rise to neurons. Migration peaks GA weeks 12–20, largely complete by 26–29.
Tracks transient embryonic scaffolds — preplate (first synapses ~GA week 5, “pioneer neurons”), subplate (up to 5× thicker than cortical plate at GA weeks 18–22, a placeholder synaptic hub for thalamocortical afferents that later dissolves), marginal zone, and the cortical plate (generates laminae II–VI; marginal zone forms layer I).
Details the canonical six-layer cortical microcircuit (best defined in visual cortex): layer IV receives thalamic input; layers II/III make cortico-cortical projections; layers V/VI project subcortically (V to brainstem/midbrain/basal ganglia, VI to thalamus); inhibitory interneurons gate the excitatory flow. Cells organize into radial ontogenetic columns/minicolumns; clonally related pyramidal neurons preferentially wire to one another.
Emphasizes the progressive vs regressive balance: exuberant synaptogenesis (peaking ~GA week 34 at ~40,000 new synapses/second) counterbalanced by two apoptotic waves (proliferative cells ~GA week 7; postmitotic cortical-plate neurons GA weeks 19–23) and later activity-dependent synaptic/axonal pruning. Up to ~half of neurons born during neurogenesis die by the end of adolescence.
Covers postnatal trajectories via neuroimaging: brain reaches ~70% adult size by age 1 and ~80% by age 2; region-specific synaptic-density peaks (visual cortex 140–150% of adult levels at 4–12 months; prefrontal cortex peaks later, ~8 months onward); myelination proceeds posterior-to-anterior and subcortical-before-cortical; cortical gray matter later thins “back-to-front,” interpreted as combined pruning + myelination and treated as a marker of maturation.
Connects circuit maturation to executive function development (working memory in dorsolateral PFC / frontoparietal network; self-regulation/cognitive control in frontostriatal circuits) and to pediatric psychiatric disorders, illustrating the “normal baseline” frame with autism, Fragile X, childhood-onset schizophrenia, ADHD, OCD, and Tourette’s.

Notable claims / data

Synaptic density within the cortical plate grows ~4% per week until GA weeks 26–28; peak synaptogenesis at GA week 34 produces ~40,000 new synapses per second, continuing into early postnatal life.
Neuron number peaks at GA week 28, yet as many as half of neurons produced during neurogenesis are eliminated by apoptosis by the end of adolescence; two discrete apoptotic waves (GA week 7 onward; peak GA weeks 19–23).
Brain volume: ~36% of adult size at 2–4 weeks postnatal; ~70% by year 1, ~80% by year 2; cortical volume rises ~88% in year 1, ~15% in year 2. Total brain volume increases ~3-fold between GA weeks 29 and 41; gray matter goes from 35% to 50% of total in that window; myelinated white matter rises from 1% to 5% of brain volume between GA weeks 36–40.
Visual-cortex synaptic density peaks at 140–150% of adult levels between 4 and 12 months; PFC synaptogenesis peaks ~8 months, later than sensory cortex.
Brain glucose metabolism (PET) reaches ~2× adult levels by 4–5 years, stays high to 9–10 years, then declines to adult levels by 16–18 years; ~40% of synapses are eliminated during adolescence via activity-dependent removal of excitatory connections, while GABAergic interneuron levels stay fairly stable.
Critical-period claims (caveat: largely from sensory systems / animal models): uncorrected congenital cataracts by 6 months or strabismus by 7 years cause irreversible visual deficits; binocular-vision column formation depends on LTP/LTD/pruning driven by retinal activity (Hebbian).
Disease vignettes tie genes/exposures to circuit defects: lissencephaly (reelin-pathway, cytoskeletal, glycosyltransferase mutations); L1CAM mutation → CRASH syndrome and alcohol-sensitive cell-adhesion (fetal alcohol syndrome); Fmr1 silencing → LTP/LTD imbalance in Fragile X; childhood-onset schizophrenia shows accelerated/excessive cortical thinning correlating with symptom severity; ADHD shows delayed cortical maturation.
Major stated caveat: human knowledge is “highly imperfect,” extrapolated largely from cross-sectional rodent data; most pediatric imaging makes developmental inferences from cross-sectional samples; histological correlates of many imaging measures remain undefined. Calls for multimodal longitudinal imaging plus postmortem molecular study of the same tissue, and nonhuman-primate longitudinal work.

Connections

Provides the circuit-/imaging-level developmental backbone for the brain-development-neurogenomics hub; complements the molecular and cell-type-resolved developing-brain siblings such as parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (autism cortex transcriptome, neuronal exon exclusion, excitation/inhibition imbalance — the molecular counterpart to this review’s pruning/interneuron themes) and the psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource developmental atlas.
The review’s emphasis on developmentally timed, region-specific synaptic and splicing-factor activity links naturally to splicing-and-neurodevelopment (e.g. neuron-specific exon programs, Rbfox/PTBP1-type switches referenced in sibling sources).
A purely review-level, human-and-animal synthesis with no new dataset/tool — best read as orienting context alongside the data-generating sources, not as a primary resource.

Open questions

What are the precise cellular/molecular determinants of macroscopic imaging signals (cortical thinning, DTI/MRS changes), which the review repeatedly flags as “speculative”?
How do critical/sensitive periods for behavioral traits and psychopathology map onto the well-characterized sensory/motor critical periods, which remain comparatively undefined?
Can multimodal longitudinal imaging combined with postmortem molecular profiling of the same tissue (and longitudinal nonhuman-primate studies) replace hazardous cross-sectional rodent extrapolation for human circuit development?
What drives the large excess production of neurons/glia followed by apoptosis, and how does this regressive program interface with the cell-type-specific genetic programs studied elsewhere in this topic?

wiki/sources/theodoris-2023-transfer-lea.title: theodoris-2023-transfer-learning-enables-predictions
wiki/sources/theodoris-2023-transfer-lea.content:
Transfer learning enables predictions in network biology
One-line: Geneformer, a transformer pretrained on ~30M single-cell transcriptomes, transfers self-supervised knowledge of gene network dynamics to boost predictions in data-limited settings.
Key takeaways

Introduces geneformer, a context-aware attention-based single-cell foundation model pretrained via masked learning on Genecorpus-30M (29.9M human single-cell transcriptomes).
Demonstrates the foundation-models transfer-learning paradigm in biology: one self-supervised pretraining phase is fine-tuned toward diverse downstream tasks using only limited task-specific data.
Encodes gene network hierarchy directly in attention weights in a fully self-supervised way, an example of model-interpretability where transcription factors and central regulatory nodes are preferentially attended.
Enables in silico deletion / in silico treatment as single-cell-perturbation-modeling, recovering known TF-target connections and nominating therapeutic candidates without perturbation data.

Notable claims / data

Genecorpus-30M: 29.9M cells from a broad range of tissues; malignant/immortalized cells excluded to avoid network rewiring.
Architecture: rank value encoding, input size 2,048 (covers 93% of encodings), 256 embedding dims, 6 transformer encoder layers, 4 attention heads/layer, feed-forward size 512; 15% of genes masked during pretraining.
