Lead candidate: FGFR1 c.449-6G>A (chr8:38428099 C>T) creates an AG in the AG-exclusion zone, activating a weak acceptor only in early forebrain development (wpc 4–6) and kidney, not after birth — consistent with the Kallmann-syndrome / hypogonadotropic-hypogonadism mechanism. Confirmed positive control: ARFGEF1 c.4960-1G>A in a solved intellectual-disability case.
Worked example SSBP3 exon 6: brain-included at 4 wpc, removed by birth; nearby SNVs scored high early, low in adult — Pangolin/MMSplice scores are stage-invariant while AbSplice2 tracks the SpliceMap reference-PSI change.

Connections

Builds directly on the deep-learning splice-site model spliceai (Pangolin shares SpliceAI’s architecture/context window but adds quantitative RNA-seq usage across 4 tissues / 4 mammals) — this paper swaps SpliceAI for Pangolin inside the AbSplice integration.
Squarely in splicing-and-disease: a variant-interpretation tool for rare-disease diagnostics, with a strong brain/neurodevelopment emphasis.
Sibling to brain/disease sources here: humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (which likewise leans on SpliceAI/Pangolin delta scores for variant interpretation), zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (brain tissue-specificity of splicing regulation), and scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (mis-splicing as a disease mechanism).
Relates to developmentally regulated alternative splicing as a theme — cf. kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated — here operationalized for variant scoring via developmental SpliceMaps.

Open questions

Larger developmental cohorts (e.g. upcoming developmental GTEx) would yield more robust SpliceMaps and capture weakly spliced junctions present in few individuals; the modular framework needs only new SpliceMaps, no retraining.
The framework is bounded by the underlying sequence model: tissue-specific splice sites underrepresented in annotation-trained models (e.g. SpliceAI) cannot be recovered; foundation models + broader training data may help.
Many NGRL candidates are heterozygous in biallelic-mechanism genes — predicted splicing disruptions may be real but not the primary phenotype driver; experimental validation of developmental-stage-specific splicing effects is still needed.
Using the max score across tissues is explicitly NOT the intended use (lower precision); interpretation depends on having a SpliceMap for the relevant tissue/stage.

wiki/sources/waldron-2016-splicing-linki.title: waldron-2016-splicing-linking-genetic-variation
wiki/sources/waldron-2016-splicing-linki.content:
Splicing: linking genetic variation and disease
One-line: A short Research Highlight summarizing a Science study (Li et al.) showing that RNA splicing is a primary link between common genetic variation and complex disease.
Key takeaways

Noncoding variants contribute to complex traits, but their mechanisms are unclear; the highlighted study maps how variants act across the full chain of splicing and gene regulation, from chromatin to protein.
In human lymphoblastoid cell lines (LCLs), the authors quantified eight molecular phenotypes (chromatin modifications, chromatin accessibility, DNA methylation, mRNA levels, transcription rate, RNA decay rate, translation levels, protein levels) and mapped QTLs for each.
Splicing QTLs (splicing-qtl) emerge as a major, partly chromatin-independent route by which variants drive complex traits and splicing-and-disease.

Notable claims / data

65% of eQTLs exert their primary effect on expression at the chromatin level; the remaining 35% are enriched in transcribed regions, acting on transcriptional/post-transcriptional processes in a chromatin-independent manner.
The novel tool LeafCutter identified 2,893 sQTLs; most had no effect on overall expression, but 89% directly affected predicted protein sequences.
sQTLs were modestly enriched for chromatin-level phenotypes, giving direct evidence that splicing can be altered at the chromatin level.
In GWAS data (rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, Alzheimer disease, schizophrenia, height, BMI), sQTLs were enriched comparably to eQTLs — and even more strongly for multiple sclerosis.

Connections

Concentrates the disease-genetics angle of splicing-and-disease via the lens of splicing-qtl.
Complements the sQTL/genetic-control work in walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and.
The original Science article introduces the LeafCutter tool (see entity leafcutter).

Open questions

How generalizable are LCL-derived sQTLs to disease-relevant tissues such as brain or immune cells?
What mechanisms link chromatin-level changes to altered splicing outcomes?

wiki/sources/walker-2019-genetic-control.title: walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and
wiki/sources/walker-2019-genetic-control.content:
Genetic Control of Expression and Splicing in Developing Human Brain Informs Disease Mechanisms
One-line: The first genome-wide map of splice QTLs (and the largest eQTL map) in mid-gestational human cortex (201 brains), showing that prenatal-specific regulatory variation — splicing as much as expression — carries substantial neuropsychiatric disease risk, and integrating it with GWAS via TWAS to nominate dozens of novel candidate genes.
Key takeaways

A well-powered splicing-qtl/eQTL study of 201 mid-gestational human brains identifies 7,962 eQTLs (6,546 eGenes) and 4,635 sQTLs (2,132 sGenes), including several thousand prenatal-specific regulatory regions absent from adult brain datasets.
sQTLs and eQTLs are mostly independent regulatory layers: half of sGenes are also eGenes and 67% of sQTLs also affect total expression, but only 22% of eQTLs affect splice-junction usage, and the eSNP/sSNP for a shared gene are typically far apart — consistent with splicing being a distinct axis of genetic control (allele-specific-splicing).
Variation in splicing carries as much, if not more, disease risk than expression: sQTL regions are enriched for SCZ, educational-attainment, and (trending) ASD/ADHD GWAS signal, with ASD GWAS only trending in sQTLs (not eQTLs) — extending the link between splicing-and-disease into human brain development.
Genetic control of expression and splicing is strongly developmental-stage-dependent: prenatal eQTLs carry significant SCZ SNP-heritability while adult-brain eQTLs (GTEx, PsychENCODE) do not reach significance, and prenatal+adult annotations explain disease risk additively — arguing these epochs capture distinct, complementary risk.
TWAS integrating prenatal QTLs with GWAS nominates dozens of new candidate risk genes for schizophrenia (62 genes + 91 introns) and intracranial volume, several invisible to adult-brain TWAS (e.g., INO80E, TMEM219, NT5DC2, GLN3/PBRM1 splicing at SCZ loci).
The data are released as a browsable atlas via the depict-viewer web portal (gene/SNP summary-level eQTL and sQTL), making this a reusable developmental-brain QTL resource.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 233 prenatal cortical RNA-seq samples QC’d to 201 individuals; 15,925 genes and 6.6M SNPs. Ancestry: 40% Mexican, 25% African-American, 14% European-Mexican, 8% ≥3-ancestry admixed, 6% Chinese, 5% African-American-Mexican, 2% European.
eQTLs: cis-eQTLs via FastQTL; 6,546 eGenes (FDR < 0.05) + 1,416 secondary eQTLs = 7,962. Reproducible: 94% of eGenes also found by EMMAX, 84% in subpopulation meta-analysis. >20% of eQTLs within 10 kb of TSS; 70% within 100 kb; enriched in prenatal open chromatin (OR = 4.42) and distal eQTLs in Hi-C 3D contacts (OR = 2.96). eSNPs enriched in binding sites for 39 TFs/DBPs (ELK4, NRF1, SMARCC1/2, CHD8). eGenes more loss-of-function tolerant.
sQTLs: annotation-free intron-excision clustering (LeafCutter approach; see leafcutter) found 92,449 excision clusters; 4,635 sQTLs (5% FDR) in 2,132 genes — 71% annotated introns, 27% novel cryptic introns. 42% of sSNPs within 10 kb of the splice junction; 64% lie within the gene body. sSNPs enriched in binding sites for 36 RBPs including HNRNPH, ATXN2, SRRM4. Proof-of-principle: TRMT1 (intellectual disability) sSNP falls in an SRRM4 CLIP-seq peak and SRRM4 overexpression alters TRMT1 intron splicing.
Stage/tissue specificity: prenatal-specific eQTLs have larger effect sizes (p < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶) and lower LoF tolerance (pLI) than tissue-shared eQTLs. Only ~1/3 of eGenes overlap GTEx adult cortex; >1,000 eGenes prenatal-specific vs PsychENCODE (which is ~10× larger). Only 24% of prenatal sGenes are adult sGenes.
SCZ SNP-heritability (LDSR): prenatal eQTLs 3.7% (p = 9.1×10⁻⁴) and prenatal sQTLs 2.1% (p = 6.6×10⁻³) — both significant, both higher than adult eQTLs (non-significant). Adult sQTLs do contribute (2.3%); prenatal+adult sQTLs additive to 3.5%. Inflammatory bowel disease used as negative control (no enrichment).
WGCNA: 19 co-expression modules; SCZ GWAS enriched in the “blue” (neurogenesis: DLX1, FGF2, LHX6) module and ASD trending in “yellow” (gene regulation in developing neurons; upper-layer cortical projection neurons: MEF2, HNRNPH3, FOXP4). Common and rare variation converge within modules per disorder; ASD implicates superficial cortical neurons. SCZ and ASD affect distinct developmental modules.
SCZ TWAS: 62 genes + 91 introns (P_Bonferroni < 0.05); 8 genes + 6 introns also pass SMR+HEIDI. 23 new SCZ risk loci not GWAS-significant (5 prenatal-unique). Highlighted: INO80E and TMEM219 at 16p11.2; NT5DC2 (expression), GLN3 and PBRM1 (splicing) at a chr3 SCZ locus; AP3B2, MPHOSPH9 splicing shared with adult. FOCUS fine-mapping: credible set of 155 genes (~3/locus).
ICV TWAS: 7 genes + 8 introns; LRRC37A prioritized at the 17q21 inversion locus; NT5C2/USMG5 splicing at chr10.
Caveats stated: cross-stage sQTL comparison is imperfect because splicing-detection methods differ between studies; prenatal-specific QTL counts are likely underestimates given the ~10× smaller sample vs adult datasets; bulk tissue; TWAS is sensitive to tissue source and fine-mapping shows many loci need experimental dissection (e.g., 16p11.2 CNV contains 29 genes).

Connections

A developmental-stage counterpart to zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (GTEx brain-region sQTLs): both establish splicing-qtl as a major disease-relevant layer, but Walker maps the prenatal epoch and ties sQTLs to SCZ/ASD/ICV via TWAS, whereas Zhang dissects regional ubiquity vs specificity in adult brain.
The annotation-free intron-excision quantification is the leafcutter strategy (Li et al. 2018), quantifying splicing as intron usage rather than percent-spliced-in of predefined exons.
sSNP/RBP enrichment (HNRNPH, SRRM4, ATXN2) connects to splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins; the SRRM4/microexon thread relates broadly to neuronal splicing programs and aligns with disease-splicing reviews like scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.
Mechanistically situates squarely in splicing-and-disease: prenatal sQTLs carry SCZ/ADHD heritability and ASD GWAS trends, arguing mis-regulated splicing in early corticogenesis contributes to neuropsychiatric risk.
SF3B1 appears as a shared prenatal/adult SCZ TWAS gene here (see sf3b1), one of 11 genes overlapping prenatal and high-confidence adult brain TWAS.
The new resource is the depict-viewer developmental-cortex transcriptome browser.

Open questions

How much of the bulk-tissue sQTL signal is cell-type-specific, and would single-cell or purified-cell-type prenatal data reassign signals (e.g., the superficial-neuron ASD module)?
Will the trending ASD/ADHD sQTL enrichments reach significance as GWAS sample sizes grow, sharpening the claim that splicing carries more disease risk than expression?
How comparable would cross-stage sQTL maps be if the same splicing-detection method were applied to prenatal and adult cohorts (the authors flag the current comparison as method-confounded)?
At complex loci (16p11.2 CNV, 17q21 inversion) where fine-mapping yields multi-gene credible sets, which nominated genes/introns are causal under functional validation?

wiki/sources/wang-2008-alternative-isofo.title: wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human
wiki/sources/wang-2008-alternative-isofo.content:
Alternative isoform regulation in human tissue transcriptomes
One-line: A foundational deep-sequencing (mRNA-Seq) survey of 15 human tissues and cell lines that established alternative splicing as essentially universal (92-94% of human genes) and showed that most splicing and polyadenylation events are tissue-regulated and coordinately controlled.
Key takeaways

mRNA-Seq of 10 diverse human tissues plus 5 mammary epithelial / breast-cancer cell lines (32-bp cDNA reads, 12-29 million reads per sample, >400 million reads total) produced a digital inventory of gene and mRNA isoform expression.
Mapping reads to exon-exon junctions estimated that 92-94% of human multi-exon genes undergo alternative splicing; ~86% of all human genes produce two or more appreciable isoform populations (minor isoform >=15%). This is the headline claim that made alternative splicing “nearly universal.”
Most alternative splicing and alternative cleavage/polyadenylation (APA) events vary between tissues; variation between individuals was roughly twofold-to-threefold less common than variation between tissues.
Defined the “per cent spliced in” (PSI / Psi) metric as the ratio of inclusion-read density to the sum of inclusion and exclusion read densities — a quantity now central to splicing quantification (see percent-spliced-in).
“Switch-like” exons (large between-tissue PSI differences) show higher sequence conservation in the exon and flanking introns and preferentially preserve the reading frame, indicating they generate full-length protein isoforms rather than truncations.
Provides early genome-scale “tissue RNA maps” linking regulatory motif position to tissue-specific activity for factors such as FOX-1/FOX-2 and PTBP1 (see ptbp1).

Notable claims / data

~60% of reads mapped uniquely to the genome (up to 2 mismatches), +4% to splice junctions; ~two-thirds of reads assigned unambiguously; estimated mis-mapping ~0.1%; only ~3% of reads from rRNA. Read density >100-fold higher in exons than introns/intergenic.
Asymptotic estimate of alternatively spliced multi-exon genes: ~98% excluding cell lines, ~100% with all samples; 94% of RefSeq genes are multi-exon. No alternative splicing detected in the 6% single-exon genes.
1,413 high-confidence new exons identified (FDR ~1.5%) plus thousands of putative new junctions — showing mRNA-Seq’s discovery power, with the caveat that very deep coverage is needed for low-abundance isoforms.
A comprehensive set of ~105,000 events of eight types (skipped exon, retained intron, A5SS, A3SS, MXE, AFE, ALE, tandem 3’ UTR) was assembled; >22,000 found tissue-regulated (Fisher’s exact test, 5% FDR), far exceeding prior sets numbering in the hundreds to low thousands. Estimated true tissue-regulation frequencies ranged 52-80% across event types.
Inter-individual variation assessed via six cerebellar cortex samples from unrelated men: ~10-30% of events showed individual-specific variation (depending on type), broadly consistent with a prior ~21% estimate but below tissue-level variation (47-74%). Caveat: differentially expressed isoforms reflect mixed cell-type composition and inter-individual differences, not pure cell-intrinsic regulation.
Example: SLC25A3 mutually exclusive exons 3A/3B — 3A dominant in heart and skeletal muscle, 3B in testes/liver, consistent with heart/muscle symptoms of exon-3A mutation. TPM1 exon 2: PSI 2% in heart vs 95% in skeletal muscle.
Switch score for MXEs shifted higher than for skipped exons (K-S test P = 3.7e-5). UGCAUG (FOX-1/FOX-2 motif) enriched in introns downstream of exons with increased inclusion in heart/muscle/brain/cerebellum; PTBP1/PTBP2-like UCUCUC/CUCUCU motifs upstream of cerebellum-included exons.
Evidence for coordinated splicing-APA regulation: SVD of inclusion ratios cleanly separated cell lines from tissues across all event types; pairwise SVD distances for splicing events and tandem-3’UTR events were highly correlated. Splicing-associated motifs (UGCAUG, (CUG)n for CELF/MBNL, ACUAAC for QKI with intronic S:B >7:1) showed high conservation in both introns and 3’ UTRs, suggesting dual splicing / 3’UTR roles for these factors.

Connections

A foundational anchor for the splicing topic: the empirical basis behind the “nearly universal” framing in reviews such as lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative and rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Operationalizes the percent-spliced-in metric used by downstream quantification tools like rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); this paper is methodologically upstream of those PSI-based event detectors and of splicing-methods.
Connects regulatory motif maps to splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins, notably ptbp1 and the FOX family, and to splicing-regulatory-elements / cis-regulatory-elements via conserved intronic and 3’UTR motifs.
Supports the splicing-and-evolution hypothesis that alternative splicing drives mammalian phenotypic complexity.
Complements wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source on isoform diversity as a source of functional variety.

Open questions

How much of the detected “alternative splicing” reflects allele-specific / individual-genotype effects rather than true tissue regulation, given the single-donor-per-tissue design?
Are FOX, CELF/MBNL and QKI genuinely “moonlighting” in 3’UTR/APA roles, or do the conserved 3’UTR motifs reflect distinct factors with identical specificity? Direct functional validation of coordinated splicing-APA regulation is needed.
How do bulk-tissue cell-type composition differences confound the tissue-specific isoform estimates — a question later addressable with single-cell isoform sequencing.
What is the mechanistic basis for tandem-3’UTR tissue regulation (APA choice vs differential isoform stability)?

wiki/sources/wang-2008-splicing-regulati.title: wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts
wiki/sources/wang-2008-splicing-regulati.content:
Splicing regulation: From a parts list of regulatory elements to an integrated splicing code
One-line: A 2008 RNA review by Wang & Burge framing the long-term goal of a predictive “splicing code,” synthesizing what was then known about splicing cis-regulatory elements (ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS), their context-dependent activities, and early attempts to integrate them into splicing-simulation algorithms.
Key takeaways

Outside the core splice site motifs, most of the information needed for splicing is held in exonic/intronic cis-regulatory elements that recruit sequence-specific RNA-binding proteins to activate or repress adjacent splice sites — i.e. the splicing-regulatory-elements view of the splicing-code.
In mammals, splice sites are predominantly recognized in pairs across the exon via exon definition (short exons, large introns); intron definition dominates in most other metazoans, with pufferfishes intermediate — supporting exon definition as the critical regulated step in mammalian splicing regulation.
Core splice signals carry only about half the information needed to accurately define exon/intron boundaries; the rest comes from SREs that distinguish authentic exons from abundant “decoy” splice sites and pseudoexons.
ESEs mostly recruit SR proteins (via RRM/RS domains) to promote splicing; ESSs are typically bound by hnRNP-class repressors such as hnRNP I and hnRNP A1.
SRE activity is strongly context-dependent — both location-dependent (same motif acts as enhancer or silencer depending on exonic vs intronic position) and gene-dependent (activity in one gene lost when moved to another). RNA structure and trans-factor availability modulate which of the many candidate motifs are actually functional.
Proposes a “bottom-up” program: catalogue cis-elements, learn their context-dependence rules, and integrate them into simulation algorithms — embodied here by the exonscan splicing simulator.

Notable claims / data

Typical human exons are ~50-250 bp, separated by introns of hundreds to thousands of bp, with introns averaging >90% of the primary transcript.
Core human splice site motifs estimated (via Monte Carlo motif-insertion simulations) to contain only ~half the information needed to define boundaries (Lim & Burge 2001).
A cell-based fluorescence-activated screen (FAS) identified 133 active ESS decanucleotides in human cells (Wang et al. 2004); FAS-ESS elements help distinguish authentic exons from pseudoexons and regulate alt splice-site usage and intron retention.
~1000 hexamers estimated to be able to function as either ESSs or ESEs (Stadler et al. 2006), so every pre-mRNA carries many candidate motifs, only a fraction recognized — analogous to TF-binding-site over-prediction near promoters.
Intronic regions flanking alternative exons are far more conserved than those around constitutive exons, out to ≥150 bp (Sorek & Ast 2003); UGCAUG (Fox-1/2 site) repeatedly emerges as the most conserved near-AS motif and is conserved from nematodes to mammals.
The Nova “RNA map” (Ule et al. 2006) predicts the direction of Nova-dependent regulation with high accuracy — a key demonstration that a factor’s full set of activities can be rationalized; YCAY clusters act as ESS, ISE, or ISS depending on position.
Caveat: the authors note their own analyses found statistically- vs experimentally-derived ESE and ESS sets to be largely non-overlapping, arguing against a single sequence commonly being both ESS and ESE — in tension with the “ESR” claim (Goren et al. 2006).
Caveat: array-based global AS platforms identify a subset of events with high confidence but have an unknown, probably substantial false-negative rate.

Connections

Foundational/conceptual companion to other splicing-code and regulation reviews in the wiki: rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the (which extends the cis-element/RBP “map” idea into the modern computational/CLIP era).
Predates and motivates modern sequence-to-splicing predictors; the exonscan log-odds simulator is an intellectual ancestor of deep models like spliceai.
Discusses many RBP entities already in the wiki: ptbp1, hnrnpa1, srsf1 (SR proteins), and the smn2 therapeutic-target intronic elements; also touches dscam mutually-exclusive splicing via intronic secondary structure (rna-structure-and-splicing).
Relates to cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin by framing the splicing regulatory network as a subnetwork of gene regulation coupled to Pol II.

Open questions

How can one predict which of a pre-mRNA’s many candidate motifs will actually be recognized in a given cell type/context — the central barrier to a working splicing code?
How general is the role of pre-mRNA secondary structure in gating SRE/splice-site accessibility, and how to measure RNA structure in vivo at high throughput (SHAPE then only in vitro)?
How to generalize cis-elements, factors, and context rules learned in one cell type or developmental stage to others?
Will the splicing code resemble the authors’ analogy — less a simple table (like the genetic code) and more an “IRS form 1040” of conditional tables and arithmetic?

wiki/sources/wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolut.title: wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolutionary-tool
wiki/sources/wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolut.content:
RNA-Seq: a revolutionary tool for transcriptomics
One-line: Foundational 2009 perspective introducing RNA-Seq as a deep-sequencing approach to transcriptome profiling, laying out the workflow, advantages, and computational challenges — including junction-read detection of splicing — that underpin most later splicing-by-RNA-seq methods.
Key takeaways

RNA-Seq converts an RNA population (total or poly(A)+) into a library of adaptor-tagged cDNA fragments that are deep-sequenced (reads typically 30-400 bp, single- or paired-end), then aligned to a reference or assembled de novo to yield a genome-scale transcription map of structure and expression.
Relative to hybridization (tiling microarrays) and Sanger EST/tag methods (SAGE, CAGE, MPSS), RNA-Seq offers single-base resolution, very low background, large dynamic range, low RNA input, and the ability to distinguish isoforms and allelic expression.
Reads are classified as exonic, junction, or poly(A) end-reads; junction reads spanning splice sites are the basis for detecting and quantifying splicing diversity, connecting alternative-splicing analysis to short-read sequencing.
Key informatics challenges are highlighted: mapping reads across splice junctions, multi-mapping reads, sequencing errors/polymorphisms, and the coverage-versus-cost tradeoff — recurring themes for downstream splicing-methods.

Notable claims / data

Splice/exon-exon junctions can be identified by the GT-AG dinucleotide consensus flanking splice sites and confirmed by low intronic expression; for complex transcriptomes, mapping junction-spanning reads is harder owing to extensive alternative splicing and trans-splicing. A partial solution is mapping to a compiled junction library of known and predicted junctions.
In humans, 31,618 known splicing events were confirmed (11% of all known splicing events) plus 379 novel events (ref. 24); a separate human study found 94,241 junctions (4,096 novel) and showed exon skipping is the prevalent form of alternative splicing (ref. 41). Extensive alternative splicing was observed for 3,462 mouse genes, and 42 events joining exons from multiple mouse genes (trans-splicing-like) were detected.
Dynamic range: >9,000-fold estimated from 16M mapped reads in S. cerevisiae; five orders of magnitude for 40M mouse reads; microarrays span only ~100-300-fold. RNA fragmentation gives even gene-body coverage but depletes transcript ends, whereas oligo-dT-primed cDNA fragmentation is 3’-biased.
Coverage/cost: ~80% of yeast genes detected at 4M uniquely mapped reads (plateau thereafter); 30M 35-nt poly(A) reads observe >90% of genes in simple yeast transcriptomes; in S. pombe, 122M reads across six conditions detected >99% of annotated genes.
Caveat: a 2009 perspective on a then-nascent technology — short reads (30-400 bp), no strand-specific or paired-end as default, and isoform-level quantification was still aspirational; no benchmark of a specific splicing tool is provided.

Connections

Methodological ancestor to the RNA-seq-based splicing-quantification tools catalogued in this wiki such as rmats and reviewed in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the; the junction-read concept underlies later percent-spliced-in estimation.
Provides the data-generation substrate for studying splice-sites, alternative-splicing, and splicing-and-disease (it notes RNA-Seq’s promise for comparing diseased vs normal tissue).
Mentions early short-read aligners (ELAND, SOAP, MAQ, RMAP) as the mapping layer; complements the regulatory/biology reviews like lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative and rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing by supplying the measurement technology.

Open questions

How to computationally identify novel splicing events between distant sequences or across two different genes (trans-splicing) at scale — flagged here as a future challenge.
How much additional isoform and alternative-splicing complexity remains undetected at then-current depths, and how paired-end / longer / strand-specific reads would resolve it.
How to define transcriptome “coverage” rigorously given unknown true isoform number and highly variable transcription activity.

wiki/sources/wang-2012-intronic-splicing.title: wang-2012-intronic-splicing-enhancers-cognate-splicing
wiki/sources/wang-2012-intronic-splicing.content:
Intronic splicing enhancers, cognate splicing factors and context-dependent regulation rules
One-line: An unbiased functional screen of random decamers that defines >100 general intronic splicing enhancers (ISEs), shows they systematically overlap with exonic splicing silencers, and works out the cognate trans-factors (mainly hnRNP H/F) and two mechanistic models for how one element can activate or repress splicing depending on location.
Key takeaways

Built a cell-based fluorescence-activated screen (FAS) using a GFP splicing reporter with a retained intron: inserting an intronic-splicing-enhancer promotes intron removal and turns on GFP, allowing unbiased FACS recovery of active sequences from a random 10-mer library — a functional-screen counterpart to computational splicing-code approaches.
Recovered 109 unique ISE decamers that cluster into six core motif groups; all functioned across cell types (HEK-293T, HeLa) and in heterologous introns, i.e. they are general rather than context-specific ISEs.
Discovered a general rule: ISEs and exonic splicing silencers (ESSs) are the same sequences read by location — ISEs share base composition (high G/U, low C), positional distribution, and conservation with FAS-ESSs, and all ISE groups consistently inhibit splicing when placed inside exons. This unifies two classes of splicing-regulatory-elements.
Identified cognate splicing-factors by RNA-affinity purification + mass spec: five of six groups (A–E) are recognized by hnrnp-h (hnRNP H1), hnrnp-f and GRSF1 despite diverse motifs; group F is bound by DAZAP1, RBM4 and hnRNP D0.
The glycine/tyrosine-rich C-terminal domains of hnRNP H1 are sufficient to confer the context-dependent activity: tethered to an intron they enhance splicing, tethered to an exon they silence it — a modular activity opposite in sign to the RS domains of SR proteins.
Group F reveals a second, “factor-dependent” model: antagonistic factors compete for the same element. DAZAP1 activates while RBM4 represses, and the RBM4:DAZAP1 ratio acts as a sensitive splicing switch on both reporters and endogenous exons.

Notable claims / data

208 transfections gave ~1× coverage of the decamer space; 117 ISE decamers found (109 unique), with 7 recovered twice and 1 thrice — the screen self-converged. Recovered decamers were G-rich (40% G, 35% T, 18% A, 7% C).
ESS hexamers were 12× over-enriched (46 of 84 ISE-hex3 shared with 176 FAS-ESS hexamers); ISE and ESS sequence scores are strongly positively correlated across all 4,096 hexamers.
hnRNP H1/F overexpression synergistically promoted splicing of a group-B-ISE intron; RNAi knockdown of either reduced it — with no effect on a non-cognate (group F) control, proving specificity.
Even the last 85 amino acids of the hnRNP H1 Gly/Tyr-rich region were sufficient for the context-dependent activity when fused to a programmable PUF RNA-binding domain.
DAZAP1 raised group-F PSI from ~20% to ~68%; endogenous validation on CORO6, SF1, ANKS3 (intronic ISE) and ZBTB17, LAMP1, NOL8 (exonic ISE); higher RBM4 in A549 vs H157 lung cancer cells correlated with reduced inclusion of group-F-ISE exons.

Connections

A functional-screen pillar of the splicing-code / splicing-regulatory-elements program; complements motif screens for ESEs/ESSs (RESCUE-ESE, FAS-ESS) and the computational splicing code.
The location-flips-function principle (ISE in intron = enhancer, same motif in exon = silencer) is the same position-dependent logic mapped transcriptome-wide by iCLIP for hnRNP C in konig-2010-iclip-reveals-the-function-of and for Rbfox in lovci-2013-rbfox-proteins-regulate-alternative-mrna.
Establishes hnRNP H/F (hnrnp-h, hnrnp-f) and DAZAP1/RBM4 as programmable, modular splicing regulators — directly relevant to splicing-factors and to engineered/therapeutic splicing modulation (splicing-therapeutics).
The rationally-designed reporter approach anticipates the massively-parallel designed-library dissection of splicing in mikl-2019-dissecting-splicing-decisions-and-cell-to-cell.

Open questions

Because the screen ran in HEK-293T, tissue-specific ISEs (Nova, Fox binding sites) and low-expressed-factor motifs were missed — what does the full ISE repertoire look like across cell types?
How do promiscuous hnRNP H/F recognize such diverse motifs (direct low-specificity binding vs indirect recruitment through partner proteins)?
How do synergistic (hnRNP H/F) and antagonistic (DAZAP1/RBM4) interaction networks combine at native loci to set the observed splicing plasticity, and can this be modeled quantitatively?

wiki/sources/wang-2014-genetic-screens-i.title: wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells
wiki/sources/wang-2014-genetic-screens-i.content:
Genetic Screens in Human Cells Using the CRISPR-Cas9 System
One-line: Foundational paper establishing genome-scale pooled loss-of-function CRISPR-Cas9 screening in mammalian cells — a lentiviral 73,000-sgRNA library where each guide is a sequencing barcode, validated on positive- and negative-selection screens, plus the first rules and an SVM predictor for sgRNA cleavage efficacy.
Key takeaways

Introduces a pooled loss-of-function screening approach (pooled-crispr-screen): a genome-scale lentiviral sgRNA library is stably integrated so each sgRNA both makes a knockout and serves as a DNA barcode counted by massively parallel sequencing, enabling both positive and negative selection screens (Fig. 1A).
Single-copy integrated sgRNAs are sufficient for efficient biallelic cleavage via CRISPR-Cas9 — cleavage reached ~97% at the target locus and kept increasing over time because the sgRNA is stably expressed; repair produced mostly small deletions (<20 bp, 91%), yielding loss-of-function alleles.
The library had 73,151 sgRNAs targeting 7114 genes (10 sgRNAs/gene for 7031 genes; all possible sgRNAs for 83 ribosomal-protein genes) plus 100 non-targeting controls; >99% of guides were detectable after infection with only ~6-fold spread between 10th–90th percentile abundance.
Off-target activity was low in this stable-expression setting: of 13 candidate off-target sites for the AAVS1 guide, only the one with a perfect 8-bp “seed” match was cleaved (>2.5%); such sites (~2.2 per sgRNA on average) usually fall in noncoding DNA.
Establishes the first empirical rules of sgRNA efficacy and an SVM classifier to predict strong vs weak guides — used to design a whole-genome library of higher-efficacy guides (deposited in Addgene).

Notable claims / data

Positive selection — 6-thioguanine (KBM7): guides against the four DNA mismatch-repair genes (MSH2, MSH6, MLH1, PMS2) were dramatically enriched; the 20 most abundant sgRNAs all hit one of these four genes, together >30% of the final pool — evidence of a low off-target rate.
Positive selection — etoposide (KBM7 + pseudo-diploid HL60): both screens recovered identical hits scoring >100-fold above all other genes: the expected TOP2A (etoposide’s target) and, unexpectedly, CDK6 as a mediator of etoposide cytotoxicity; all 20 TOP2A/CDK6 guides ranked >90th percentile in both lines, and isogenic knockout lines confirmed resistance.
Negative selection — essentiality: deep sequencing across ~12 cell doublings showed strong Cas9-dependent depletion of ribosomal-protein-gene guides, concordant between HL60 and KBM7; non-essential exceptions were chrY genes (RPS4Y1/2) and lowly-expressed ribosome-like genes. Gene-set enrichment over 4722 sets flagged fundamental processes (DNA replication, transcription, proteasome, spliceosome) as depleted.
Specificity of essentiality: in a small BCR/ABL1 library, only guides targeting the exons forming the BCR-ABL fusion (which KBM7 survival depends on) were depleted — depletion tracks true gene function, not cutting per se.
sgRNA efficacy rules: high/low GC content, targeting the last coding exon, and targeting the transcribed strand all reduced efficacy (all P<0.05 but small effect). A parallel Cas9-binding profiling assay showed purines in the last 4 nt of the spacer favor Cas9 loading (pyrimidines disfavored), correlating with depletion (r=0.81) — so cleavage efficiency is largely set by affinity for Cas9. The SVM’s top two-thirds of predictions gave ~3-fold higher efficacy on held-out essential non-ribosomal genes.
Estimated hit rate >75% (46/60 guides) at known genes via z-score analysis of positive-selection screens, implying low false-negative rate.

Connections

Canonical foundation of the pooled-crispr-screen concept — the endogenous-genome, selection-based counterpart to episomal massively-parallel-reporter-assay assays.
Predates and enables the single-cell-readout extension single-cell-crispr-screen (dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits / perturb-seq, adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening) and tiling-deletion / precise-variant screens (gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements, sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel / crispey).
Motivated by the limitations of RNAi (partial knockdown, off-target) — argues CRISPR knocks out at the DNA level and can interrogate non-transcribed elements RNAi cannot reach.
Companion paper published back-to-back (Shalem et al., GeCKO library, doi:10.1126/science.1247005) in the same issue; both establish genome-scale CRISPR knockout screening.

Open questions

All screens here were proliferation/fitness-based; how well does the approach extend to non-selectable, marker- or FACS-based, or single-cell-transcriptome phenotypes? (Later answered by single-cell-crispr-screen methods.)
The sgRNA efficacy rules explain only a small fraction of variance — what additional sequence/chromatin features govern cutting, and how much does the SVM generalize across cell types and Cas9 variants?
Single-guide knockouts leave one intact allele possible; how are residual in-frame or hypomorphic alleles handled at scale in negative-selection screens?

wiki/sources/wang-2023-crispr-technology.title: wang-2023-crispr-technology-a-decade-of
wiki/sources/wang-2023-crispr-technology.content:
CRISPR technology: A decade of genome editing is only the beginning
One-line: A decade-in review from Doudna’s group surveying the state of CRISPR-mediated genome editing in human cells, animals, and plants — the successes (knockouts, screens, multiplexed plant editing) and the pressing challenges (accuracy/precision, precise insertions, delivery, cost) that define the next decade.
Key takeaways

CRISPR-Cas9 is the canonical RNA-guided genome-editing tool: the sgRNA directs Cas9 to a target where it creates a double-strand break (DSB), repaired by endogenous NHEJ, microhomology-mediated end joining, or the more precise HDR pathway. Swapping the guide RNA retargets the same protein anywhere PAM constraints allow.
The past decade’s “what works well” successes: routine gene knockouts (via NHEJ indels), rapid creation of knockout/transgenic animal models (cutting mouse generation time from ~1 year to as few as 4 weeks via one-cell embryo editing), CRISPR screens (KO, CRISPRi, CRISPRa; increasingly combined with single-cell multiomic readouts — see pooled-crispr-screen, single-cell-crispr-screen), and multiplexed editing in plants for domestication and trait engineering.
Two precision editing modalities reduce reliance on DSBs: base editing (nCas9/dCas9 fused to a deaminase; C>T, A>G and expanded conversions; no DSB, minimal indels) and prime editing (nCas9 fused to reverse transcriptase + pegRNA; can install substitutions, small insertions, and deletions in dividing and nondividing cells).
Four framing challenges for the next decade: (1) editing accuracy (on- vs off-target location) and precision (exact intended edit vs bystander/indel byproducts); (2) precise genetic sequence insertions; (3) delivery ex vivo and in vivo; (4) cost, regulation, and access.
CRISPR editors are already deployed clinically and agriculturally: a CRISPR therapy for sickle cell disease (FDA approval expected 2023), plus higher-nutrition CRISPR tomato and disease-resistant high-yield wheat as illustrative product examples.

Notable claims / data

Obtaining a complete human genome now feasible in <24 hours vs 5 years for the first sequence — context for why designing a CRISPR editor from genome data now takes days.
Base-editor imprecision data (from ref 130, chart by P. H. Yoon): of disease-related SNVs correctable by adenine base editors, ~4,997 of 10,320 unique edits achieved >50% precision overall, but in the subset containing bystanders only ~4,980 of 6,269 (i.e., precision drops sharply when >1 target base sits in the editing window; a separate study of 21 base editors found ~half of targetable pathogenic point mutations had bystanders in the window).
Prime editing shown ~30x more efficient than HDR for repairing a mutation in intestinal CF organoids, but prime editing efficiencies remain generally low for many applications; two organoid/mouse studies reported no detectable off-target indels.
Programmable large-insertion approaches emerging: CRISPR-associated transposons (CASTs, cargo up to ~10 kb but not yet shown to work in mammalian cells), engineered Cas9–reverse-transcriptase–serine-integrase fusions, and twin prime editing (corrected a large inversion in Hunter syndrome with up to ~9% efficiency).
Delivery framed as the major bottleneck: cargo delivered as plasmid DNA, mRNA+gRNA, or RNP; two in vivo successes highlighted — LNP delivery to liver for transthyretin amyloidosis and AAV delivery for Leber congenital amaurosis type 10.
Box 1 (cost/regulation/access): ex vivo bone-marrow-transplant-based cures can run up to ~$2,000,000 per patient; ADA-SCID gene therapy cited as a cautionary case of a therapy abandoned despite promising results due to manufacturing/regulatory burden.

Connections

Directly grounds the CRISPR-screen and perturbation lineage in the wiki: pooled-crispr-screen, single-cell-crispr-screen, and the perturb-seq entity (the review cites Procode, Perturb-ATAC, ECCITE-seq as multiomic screen readouts).
Extends genome-editing beyond cutting into base/prime editing precision modalities.
Outlook section foresees CRISPR intersecting with ai-in-genomics (machine learning for repair-outcome and off-target prediction), live-cell imaging, and cheaper sequencing — consistent with the wiki’s broader AI-in-biology thread.

Open questions

Can prime editing reach HDR-level flexibility with acceptably high efficiency and low indel byproducts for therapeutic use?
Will CASTs be made to function in mammalian cells, unlocking programmable large-cargo insertion?
What delivery modality (VLPs? engineered AAVs? LNPs with cell-type tropism?) will finally solve safe, efficient, cell-type-specific in vivo editing without random integration?

wiki/sources/wang-2024-alternative-splic.title: wang-2024-alternative-splicing-of-latrophilin-3-controls
wiki/sources/wang-2024-alternative-splic.content:
Alternative splicing of latrophilin-3 controls synapse formation
One-line: Cell-type-specific, activity-dependent alternative-splicing of the postsynaptic adhesion GPCR latrophilin-3 (lphn3) switches its intracellular signalling and scaffold-recruiting properties, controlling synapse formation through two convergent pathways — Gαs signalling and phase separation of postsynaptic scaffolds.
Key takeaways

lphn3 (Adgrl3), an adhesion GPCR that organizes hippocampal Schaffer-collateral synapses, undergoes extensive alternative-splicing at five sites; C-terminal splicing (exon 31 vs exon 32) determines its G-protein-coupling mode.
The exon-31 variant couples to Gαs (drives cAMP) and is the synaptogenic isoform; the exon-32 variant couples to Gα12/13 and is not synaptogenic. CRISPR-shifting the balance from Gαs to Gα12/13 impaired synaptic connectivity as severely as deleting Lphn3 entirely.
Only the Gαs-coupled (exon-31) variant recruits phase-transitioned postsynaptic scaffold condensates (biomolecular-condensates): its C-terminal PDZ-binding motif (PBM) binds shank and co-sediments with the GKAP–HOMER–PSD95–SHANK (GHPS) complex.
Presynaptic teneurin and flrt ligands cluster the LPHN3-coated condensates into higher-order assemblies, linking trans-synaptic adhesion to postsynaptic specialization.
Neuronal activity promotes the splice switch toward the synaptogenic exon-31 variant (E31 inclusion, E32 exclusion), tying synapse formation to neuronal-activation — an example of activity-dependent splicing-and-neurodevelopment.
Synapse formation therefore requires parallel activation of two mechanisms — Gαs/cAMP signalling and clustered phase separation of scaffolds — both encoded by a single alternatively spliced exon.

Notable claims / data

Five principal Lphn3 splice sites: E6/E9 (extracellular, teneurin-binding), E15 (GAIN domain), E24 (third intracellular loop, GPCR region), and E28-32 (three distinct C-terminal sequences). E31 is more abundant (60-80% of transcripts) than E32 (20-40%); inhibitory neurons carry more E31/E30b than excitatory neurons.
TRUPATH G-protein-coupling assays: the most abundant hippocampal variant (E24-E30b-E31-E32) couples preferentially to Gαs; replacing E31 with E32 switches coupling to Gα12/13. Only the E31 variant raised cAMP (quenched by PDE7b).
CRISPR E31-specific knockout switched E31→E32 mRNAs (via NMD of the mutant), ~60% decrease in E31 and ~100% increase in E32 transcripts, without loss of total LPHN3 protein; it reduced synchronous firing ~40% and monosynaptic CA3→CA1 inputs ~60% (rabies retrograde tracing) — matching full Lphn3 deletion.
The E31-encoded PBM (conserved in all latrophilins) is required for excitatory synapse formation; deleting it (ΔPBM) abolished co-sedimentation with the GHPS scaffold condensate. E31-containing (but not E32 or E31-ΔPBM) LPHN3 assembles onto and coats phase-separated scaffold droplets; FRAP shows LPHN3-E31 forms a fluid surface shell.
Teneurin-2 (dimeric) and FLRT3 synergistically cluster LPHN3-E31-coated condensates; clustering is lost with E32 or ΔPBM LPHN3.
Neuronal-activity paradigms (KCl, picrotoxin, kainate) induced Fos and shifted splicing toward E31 / away from E32; single-cell analysis of activated mPFC neurons confirmed higher E31 and lower E32 in high-immediate-early-gene neurons.

Connections

A flagship example of splicing-and-neurodevelopment where alternative-splicing of a synaptic adhesion molecule tunes circuit assembly; complements neuronal microexon logic in garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon and neurexin splicing in fernando-2025-phenotypic-complexities-of-rare-heterozygous.
Ties splicing directly to biomolecular-condensates / phase separation of postsynaptic densities (shared theme with CPEB4 condensates in garcia-cabau-2025-mis-splicing-of-a-neuronal-microexon).
Activity-dependent splicing connects to neuronal-activation and the developmental RBP regulation reviewed in baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Introduces teneurin/flrt trans-synaptic adhesion and shank-scaffold biology as splicing-controlled effectors.

Open questions

Which splicing factors read neuronal activity to drive the E31/E32 switch, and how is cell-type specificity (excitatory vs inhibitory) encoded?
Do the analogous C-terminal splice sites of latrophilins 1/2 (LPHN1/2) control G-protein coupling and synapse formation the same way?
Does the Gα12/13-coupled E32 variant have its own function (e.g. neuronal migration or axon guidance), as the authors speculate?

wiki/sources/wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-ef.title: wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-efficient-and-flexible
wiki/sources/wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-ef.content:
rMATS-turbo: an efficient and flexible computational tool for alternative splicing analysis of large-scale RNA-seq data
One-line: A step-by-step Nature Protocols guide to rmats-turbo, the fast, scalable re-implementation of rmats that keeps the original statistical framework and CLI but adds a decoupled prep/post workflow enabling alternative-splicing analysis on RNA-seq datasets with tens of thousands of samples.
Key takeaways

rMATS-turbo (rMATS v4.0.1+) is a re-engineering of rmats that maintains its statistical model and user interface while dramatically improving speed and data-storage efficiency, scaling to massive RNA-seq datasets. It was first released in 2017 (v4.0.1); open-source versions (v4.1.0+) on GitHub since 2020.
Core architectural novelty: a decoupled two-step workflow. The prep step processes each FASTQ/BAM file independently into a weighted splicing-graph file (.rmats); the post step integrates .rmats files across samples to discover and quantify events. Prep can run in parallel/distributed on a cluster; adding new samples only requires re-running post.
Uses a weighted splicing-graph data structure (exons = nodes, splice junctions = edges weighted by read counts) stored in the compact .rmats format.
Discovers and quantifies the five canonical alternative-splicing patterns — skipped exon (SE), alternative 5′ splice-sites (A5SS), alternative 3′ splice sites (A3SS), mutually exclusive exons (MXE), retained intron (RI) — and quantifies them with percent-spliced-in (PSI/Ψ), normalized by effective isoform lengths. Read counts come in junction-only (JC) and junction+exon-body (JCEC) flavors.
Two worked application scenarios: (1) a two-group differential-splicing comparison (PC3E vs GS689 prostate-cancer cell lines, EMT phenotypes); (2) large-scale quantification across ~1,000 samples (1,019 CCLE cancer cell lines) on a compute cluster.
The companion rmats2sashimiplot tool visualizes rMATS-turbo output as sashimi plots.

Notable claims / data

Procedure 1 (PC3E vs GS689, n=3 each, --task both, --novelSS on): ~5.5 h wall-clock, 11 GB peak RAM. Identified 690,089 AS events total via junction reads; 155,056 after filtering (≥10 reads, non-extreme PSI), SE most prevalent; 14,210 differential events after FDR ≤0.01 and |ΔPSI| ≥0.05. Example: USO1 exon 13 PSI 91% (PC3E) vs 5% (GS689), FDR ≤1.0×10⁻¹⁵; a 21-nt novel “micro-exon” in MAST3 (PSI 9% vs 41%, FDR 2.03×10⁻⁹) found only because --novelSS was enabled.
Procedure 2 (1,019 CCLE cell lines, decoupled prep/post, --statoff): ~3 d wall-clock, 24 GB peak RAM, on a CHOP HPC cluster (82 nodes × 24 cores × 128 GB, one core per job). Identified 1,428,168 AS events (1,078,334 SE); 52,797 high-confidence SE after filtering (≥20 mean reads, 5th-percentile PSI ≥0.05 and 95th ≤0.95); 162 SE events with PSI correlated (R²>0.4) with EMT score. Per-sample prep step typically <1 h.
New/expanded features vs original rMATS: single-group mode (omit --b2/--s2), --statoff to decouple statistics, --task stat to re-run only the test on extracted counts, --novelSS for unannotated splice sites/exons (with --mil/--mel), --fixed-event-set, --paired-stats invoking the PAIRADISE paired model, plus --variable-read-length and --allow-clipping.
Recommended significance criteria (two-group): mean read count ≥10 in both groups; filter PSI <0.05 or >0.95 in both; FDR ≤0.01; |ΔPSI| ≥0.05.
Positioning vs alternatives: AS tools fall into isoform-based (Cufflinks, kallisto), exon-based (DEXSeq), and event-based categories; event-based is most widely used. rMATS-turbo is reported to perform favorably or comparably to other tools (MISO, SpliceTrap, SUPPA/SUPPA2, MAJIQ, LeafCutter) in multiple benchmarks, with high citation count attributed to ease of use and scalability. Different tools tend to give concordant PSI estimates.
Caveat: organism-agnostic; designed for short reads only — handles only binary (basic) events, cannot resolve complex multi-choice events or long-range coupling between distant events. Authors note long-read RNA-seq may overcome these limits but needs new tooling. Y.X. and Z.X. receive licensing income for commercial use of rMATS-turbo.

Connections

Direct successor to shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (the original rmats statistical-model paper); this protocol is the first systematic description of the turbo engine, which the field had already broadly adopted in practice.
Defines the practical percent-spliced-in measurement workflow underpinning event-based splicing-methods; complements the prediction-side tools (spliceai) and the broader toolkit framing in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Both worked examples target EMT in cancer cell lines → relevant to splicing-and-disease; the protocol also notes downstream use for sQTL discovery from population-scale RNA-seq.

Open questions

How do the speed/storage gains of the prep/post decoupling quantify against contemporaries (SUPPA2, LeafCutter, MAJIQ) on identical large cohorts — the protocol cites favorable benchmarks but runs none itself?
What is lost by the binary-event restriction in genes with complex/coupled splicing, and how will the announced long-read successor tooling address it?
How robust is --novelSS micro-exon discovery (e.g., the 21-nt MAST3 exon) to alignment choices and false positives at population scale?

wiki/sources/wang-2025-a-deep-dive-into-.title: wang-2025-a-deep-dive-into-statistical
wiki/sources/wang-2025-a-deep-dive-into-.content:
A Deep Dive into Statistical Modeling of RNA Splicing QTLs Reveals Variants That Explain Neurodegenerative Disease
One-line: majiqtl — a three-part statistical framework (Barash lab, UPenn) that re-engineers sQTL discovery — comprehensive splicing representation + covariate-weighted multiple-testing + a beta-binomial effect-size model — to find more functionally relevant sQTLs in GTEx and explain neurodegenerative-disease GWAS signals, including an Alzheimer mechanism via MS4A3 exon skipping.
Key takeaways

The gap it targets: many GWAS hits are unexplained by eQTLs, and sQTLs — though a key contributor to disease — stay underrepresented because sQTL pipelines inherit eQTL machinery (linear regression + rank-inverse-normal transform) ill-suited to splicing. The paper argues three things must improve: the splicing representation, the statistical effect-size model, and the multiple-testing strategy.
(1) Comprehensive splicing phenotype from MAJIQ quantifications (majiq-v2): local splice variations capture de novo junctions, intron retention, and alternative starts/ends that event-based rMATS and LeafCutter miss, and that annotation-bound isoform tools (Salmon/Kallisto/RSEM) can’t handle.
(2) Beta-binomial composite testing model for sQTL effect sizes: models the discrete, heteroskedastic read counts behind Ψ (sparse junction reads, [0,1] domain) rather than treating Ψ as Gaussian — improving power and false-discovery control, especially where splicing associations are confounded by expression/coverage.
(3) Weighted multiple-hypothesis correction for sGene discovery: a weighted FWER (Westfall–Young) scheme that spends the testing budget using power-relevant covariates — SNP-to-splice-site proximity and junction coverage — instead of treating all SNP×junction tests equally.
Also ships VIOLA-QTL, an sQTL visualization tool.

Notable claims / data

Applied to GTEx, MAJIQTL finds significantly more functionally relevant sQTLs, enriched for functional annotations and co-localizing with neurodegenerative-disease GWAS variants, versus prior sQTL approaches.
Alzheimer mechanism: identifies and experimentally tests rs528823, an AD-implicated variant that disrupts the RBP-binding site of YBX3 in MS4A3 → skipping of exon 7, a plausible mechanism of action — a concrete win for “improved sQTL detection → new GWAS explanation.”
Positions against prior tools it improves on: GLiMMPS / DRIMSeq (count-based GLMs but ignore multiple junctions per gene), sQTLSeeker / THISTLE (multivariate but don’t model the limited count data), IHW (covariate weighting but assumes independent tests — invalid for correlated splicing/SNP structure).
Validation uses orthogonal evidence: splicing-prediction algorithms (splicing codes, cf. spliceai) and molecular experiments.

Connections

A method-side companion to the wiki’s population/sQTL cluster (park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative, zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls) and the brain e/sQTL atlases (walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and): it improves the statistical engine of sQTL mapping rather than producing a new tissue atlas.
Builds directly on MAJIQ (majiq-v2) for its splicing representation, and benchmarks against the broader splicing-methods toolkit (leafcutter, rmats). Uses splicing-code predictors (spliceai) as orthogonal validation.
Reinforces the topic thesis that isoform/splicing regulation mediates much disease heritability: like the brain atlases, it ties splicing-and-disease to GWAS via splicing, here landing a tested RBP-mediated Alzheimer mechanism.

Open questions

How much of the remaining eQTL-unexplained GWAS signal across diseases can the beta-binomial + weighted-testing approach recover beyond neurodegeneration?
Can the count-based model extend to long-read isoform quantifications, where unannotated transcripts are observed directly?
How many of the newly prioritized sQTLs validate molecularly, beyond the MS4A3/YBX3 exemplar?

wiki/sources/wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d.title: wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure
wiki/sources/wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d.content:
Predicting RNA 3D structure and conformers using a pre-trained secondary structure model and structure-aware attention
One-line: Introduces trrosettarna2, an end-to-end deep learning method for RNA 3D structure and conformer prediction that injects a pre-trained secondary-structure prior into a structure-aware attention model, a parameter-efficient approach to rna-structure-prediction.
Key takeaways

trrosettarna2 addresses RNA 3D-data scarcity by pre-training a secondary-structure module (trRNA2-SS) on the large bpRNA database (~12× more entries than RNA structures in the PDB) and feeding base-pairing priors into the 3D pipeline — a concrete rna-structure-prediction strategy.
The pipeline is four-stage: an SS prior module, an RNAformer two-track transformer (12 blocks), an SS-aware invariant-point-attention structure module (8 blocks), and PyRosetta relaxation/energy minimization; it offers both an end-to-end (E2E) and a PyRosetta-minimization variant.
The embedded SS module doubles as a standalone state-of-the-art secondary-structure predictor, trRNA2-SS, trained via transfer learning (bpRNA pre-train then PDB fine-tune).
A key novelty is “SS-weighted softmax”: base-pairing probabilities multiply the attention maps (with an annealing temperature schedule) so secondary structure directly biases 3D attention — analogous in spirit to other AI-for-biology methods like spliceai that bake domain priors into the network.
By varying the SS input, trrosettarna2 can sample distinct RNA conformers without experimental data, recapitulating RNase P RNA conformational heterogeneity.

Notable claims / data

On the TS28 benchmark (28 non-redundant, temporally split RNAs), final trRosettaRNA2 reaches average RMSD 8.66 Å — a 24% improvement over predecessor trRosettaRNA (11.35 Å) and better on 71.4% of targets; competitive with AlphaFold 3 (9.14 Å RMSD), with better RMSD/lDDT but slightly lower TM-score/INF.
Far fewer steric clashes than AlphaFold 3 (clashscore ~2 vs >10) and no geometric entanglements (verified via RNAspider).
Parameter/compute efficiency: ~30M parameters (1/2 of RoseTTAFoldNA, 1/4 of RhoFold+, 1/12 of AlphaFold 3); trained in ~33 days on a single A100/A800 GPU (~56 GPU-days) vs AlphaFold 3’s ~20 days on 256 A100 GPUs (5,120 GPU-days).
CASP16 blind test: Yang-Server was the top automated server group (1/16; 4th of 64 overall), surpassing AF3-server (9th) and dNAfold/DRfold2 (24th); on CASP15 it ranked second only to the top human group AIchemy_RNA2.
RNase P RNA conformers: the trRosettaRNA2 ensemble (11 SS inputs × 4 recycles) gives overall RMSF 12.5 Å and matches per-residue AFM dynamics (PCC 0.894), strongly outperforming AlphaFold 3 (RMSF ~4.7 Å, homogeneous, much lower PCC).
Caveats: cannot model full RNA–protein/ligand complexes (a limitation vs AlphaFold 3); crop size capped at 384 nt limited applicability to >400 nt CASP16 targets (AlphaFold 3 predictions were substituted for some large targets); struggles on synthetic RNAs; conformer “ground truth” rests on AFM-derived ensembles.

Connections

Sits within artificial-intelligence as an AI-for-structural-biology method; conceptually a sibling to genomics deep-learning models such as spliceai/splicing-code, all of which encode biological priors into neural architectures.
Extends the protein/biomolecular structure-prediction lineage (AlphaFold2 invariant point attention, AlphaFold 3, RoseTTAFoldNA, RhoFold+) into the RNA-specific, data-scarce regime.
The data-scarcity-driven design (leveraging abundant secondary-structure annotations to compensate for few 3D structures) is a concrete instance of the data-quality-and-bias theme in ai-in-genomics.

Open questions

Can the SS-prompting strategy be coupled with experimental inputs (cryo-EM, AFM) to push conformer accuracy and handle large/synthetic RNAs?
How well does diverse-SS-input conformer sampling generalize beyond RNase P to other dynamic RNAs, and is the AFM-derived ensemble a sufficient ground truth?
Could end-to-end complex (RNA–protein/ligand) modeling be added without sacrificing the parameter efficiency that distinguishes the method?

wiki/sources/wang-2026-regvelo-gene-regu.title: wang-2026-regvelo-gene-regulatory-informed-dynamics
wiki/sources/wang-2026-regvelo-gene-regu.content:
RegVelo: Gene-regulatory-informed dynamics of single cells
One-line: regvelo is a Bayesian deep-generative framework that couples RNA velocity (splicing kinetics) with gene regulatory networks, turning RNA-velocity inference into an actionable “virtual cell” for in silico perturbation and cell-fate prediction.
Key takeaways

Standard RNA velocity models cell dynamics under restrictive assumptions (gene independence, constant transcription rates) and ignores regulation; GRN-inference methods ignore dynamics. RegVelo bridges this gap by jointly inferring splicing kinetics AND a gene regulatory network in one model.
Transcription rate α is no longer constant but a regulated, cell-specific quantity: α_g = h([W·s(t) + b]_g), where W is a learned GRN weight matrix (positive=activation, negative=repression, zero=no edge) and b is a base-transcription bias. This yields a single coupled high-dimensional ODE solved with a parallel ODE solver, rather than the decoupled per-gene closed-form ODEs of prior methods.
Built on regvelo’s predecessor veloVI; uses stochastic variational inference, quantifying intrinsic and extrinsic velocity uncertainty. Prior GRNs come from single-cell multiome/ATAC-seq or public databases; sparsity is enforced via a sparse prior network or L1-regularizing the Jacobian of the dynamical system.
Integration with CellRank 2 enables regulon-wise in silico perturbation: masking a regulon and recomputing velocities yields fate-probability shifts. This is a form of single-cell-perturbation-modeling grounded in a learned dynamic GRN rather than a static knowledge-graphs-style interaction graph.
Applied to zebrafish cranial neural crest development (Smart-seq3 + multiome), RegVelo identified tfec as an early driver and elf1 as a regulator of pigment-cell fate; both validated in vivo by CRISPR-Cas9 knockout and single-cell Perturb-seq.

Notable claims / data

Velocity/latent-time benchmarks: outperforms scVelo (EM), veloVI, and TFvelo on simulated and cell-cycle data (1,146 U2OS-FUCCI cells); higher AUROC regardless of GRN corruption level; GRN-inference AUROC advantage of +6.6% over comparators.
Terminal-state recovery: on simulated data with 1/3/7/9 terminal states, significantly higher terminal-state-identification (TSI) score than scVelo and veloVI (p < 0.001) across all complexities; on the hindbrain dataset RegVelo was the only benchmarked method recovering all five terminal states (TSI = 0.95). Cell-cycle CBC = 0.864, velocity consistency 0.873.
Hematopoiesis (1,947 HSPCs): recovers the GATA1–SPI1(PU.1) toggle-switch motif and its erythroid/monocyte fate roles where prior velocity methods failed; Jacobian-L1 regularization improved stability.
Pancreatic endocrinogenesis (3,696 cells): recovers alpha/beta/delta/epsilon terminal states; driver ranking AUROC = 0.95; predicts E2f1, Neurod2, Rfx6 regulon effects.
In vivo validation (zebrafish): designed sgRNAs for tfec, mitfa, bhlhe40, tfeb, elf1, nr2f2, nr2f5; perturbation effects quantified by MELD relative-likelihood. mitfa KO → pigment depletion (MELD likelihood 0.3354, p = 0.001); nr2f2 KO → facial-mesenchyme depletion (0.47, p = 0.001).
Perturbation-prediction benchmark vs dynamo and CellOracle: on a joint panel of 11 single-TF + 4 multi-TF knockouts, RegVelo nearly doubled mean Spearman correlation with experimental MELD scores (single-TF 0.52 vs < 0.25; all knockouts 0.51 vs 0.32) and reached precision/recall ≈ 0.6 (~2× competitors).
elf1 finding: predicted (and CUT&RUN-confirmed, 1,967 binding sites) as a pro-pigment, anti-mesenchymal ETS factor — an exception to the pro-mesenchymal bias of most ETS factors; mitfa and sox10 significantly downregulated in elf1 KO.
Caveat: the regulatory model is restricted to simple linear interactions; does not yet use motif-derived TF activity or chromatin accessibility directly; bootstrap uncertainty estimation needs many model runs (costly); global latent time may miss gene-specific dynamics.

Connections

Sits in the artificial-intelligence / ai-in-genomics cluster as a generative deep-learning method for single-cell biology.
A single-cell-perturbation-modeling method, but distinct from latent-shift / graph-based predictors like txpert (which fuses static gene–gene knowledge-graphs and predicts the immediate perturbed transcriptome) and optimal-transport approaches like cmonge: RegVelo instead perturbs the underlying regulatory circuit and forward-simulates dynamics over latent time, capturing nonlinear long-term effects rather than first-order direction.
Uses prior GRNs as a knowledge-graphs-style inductive bias, but treats the graph as learnable (edge weights refined during training) rather than fixed.
Positioned by the authors as a step toward a physically grounded “virtual cell” / digital-twins-style simulator for cell-fate decisions.

Open questions

Can the linear regulatory model be extended with motif-derived TF activity, chromatin accessibility, protein/translation (ribosome profiling), or post-transcriptional RBP–RNA edges (CLIP/DART-seq) for richer regulation?
Is elf1 part of a shared regulatory circuit between normal neural-crest fate decisions and melanoma intermediate states?
Would a fully Bayesian (e.g. Bayesian neural network) uncertainty scheme replace the costly bootstrap and better calibrate GRN-edge confidence?
Does a single shared temporal coordinate (vs gene-specific latent times) improve fidelity without losing identifiability?

wiki/sources/wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atl.title: wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform
wiki/sources/wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atl.content:
Cross-ancestry atlas of gene, isoform, and splicing regulation in the developing human brain
One-line: A cross-ancestry xQTL atlas of 672 developing human neocortex samples (4–39 postconception weeks) that uniformly maps gene, isoform, and local-splicing QTLs (splicing-qtl), finds isoform/splicing regulation mediates more neuropsychiatric GWAS heritability than gene expression, and is published as the DevBrainHub resource (devbrainhub).
Key takeaways

Most neuropsychiatric GWAS variants are noncoding and enriched in regulatory elements active during brain development, but no unified, population-scale, ancestrally diverse fetal-brain regulatory atlas existed. This study fills that gap by integrating 5 cohorts into 672 distinct developing-neocortex donors and uniformly processing genotype + RNA-seq.
Three molecular readouts were mapped as cis-QTLs (collectively “xQTLs”): gene expression (eQTL), Salmon-imputed isoform expression (isoQTL), and leafcutter-quantified annotation-free local splicing / intron-excision ratios (sQTL). 15,752 genes harbored at least one cis-xQTL.
The reference is highly diverse — European (~45%), Latino/admixed American (~25%), African-American (~22%), East/Southeast Asian (~8%) — and cross-ancestry data improved statistical fine-mapping resolution (smaller credible sets), not merely via larger N but via distinct LD structure across ancestries.
Genetic regulation is developmentally dynamic: cis-heritability of gene expression and local splicing drops sharply from ~10 to ~18 PCW, coinciding with a shift from neural progenitors to maturing excitatory neurons (cellular heterogeneity) plus intrinsic neuronal-maturation effects.
Isoform-level regulation mediated the largest share of GWAS heritability across five psychiatric disorders, more than gene expression or local splicing — underscoring the value of isoform/splicing-qtl readouts for interpreting disease variants (splicing-and-disease).
Sibling to other brain sQTL resources here such as zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls and the microglia isoform atlas humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas; results are browsable via the devbrainhub portal.

Notable claims / data

672 distinct donors integrated; after strict QC, 654 samples with matched genotype + cortical RNA-seq spanning 4–39 PCW (Tri1 N=216, Tri2 N=433); imputed into the multiancestry TOPMed panel.
QTL counts (FDR < 0.05): 10,094 eGenes, 11,845 isoGenes (cis-isoQTL), 7,490 sGenes (cis-sQTL). Of 15,752 xQTL genes, 3,740 shared across all three; 2,312 isoform-only and 1,891 local-splicing-only — i.e. isoQTLs/sQTLs capture orthogonal signal beyond eQTLs (Storey’s pi1).
2,488 “fetal-specific” eGenes vs adult PsychENCODE (N=1,387) and GTEx (N=205); these are more loss-of-function intolerant (higher pLI) and enriched for spliceosomal pathways and ventricular radial glia markers. isoGenes and sGenes are also more LoF-intolerant than eGenes.
Effect sizes (allelic fold change) highly concordant developing-vs-adult (GTEx rho=0.93; PsychENCODE rho=0.89) and across ancestries (AMR–EUR and AFR–EUR rho=0.97). AFR, despite smallest N (135), gave the smallest credible sets; example gene MTFR1 CS shrank from EUR=49/AMR=57 to AFR=9, multiancestry=5, trans-ancestry+functional=3.
49 inversion-associated eQTLs (cis and trans) at four large recurrent inversions (17q21.31, 16p11.2, 7q11.22, 8p23.1); longer inversions more likely to affect expression.
Trimester split: ~2-fold more Tri1 than Tri2 eGenes (4,211 vs 2,220) and sGenes (5,312 vs 2,318); cis-h2SNP drops Tri1→Tri2 and developing→adult; linear decline 10–18 weeks confirmed by sliding-window analysis (N=150 batches). phNPC vs differentiated-neuron comparison supports an intrinsic-maturation contribution beyond cell-type proportion shifts.
MESC mediation across SCZ, ASD, BIP, ADHD, MDD: isoform regulation mediated 6.45 ± 1.92% of h2, vs gene expression 3.15 ± 1.25% and splicing 2.13 ± 1.74% — isoform > gene > splicing; Tri2 isoform regulation especially important (notably BIP). Overall mediation small but consistent, robust to excluding high-pLI and to protein-coding-only restriction.
Colocalization (eCAVIAR CLPP>0.01 / COLOC PP4>0.7) prioritized 293 of 485 psychiatric GWAS loci (~60%), with >2-fold more colocalizations than larger adult-brain panels (57 vs 20 eQTL colocalizations for SCZ+BIP). More loci colocalized via isoQTLs/sQTLs than eQTLs.
Convergence of common and rare variant signal at SCZ risk gene SP4: risk allele rs10276352 (G>A) is an sQTL increasing inclusion of a 181-bp cryptic, unannotated exon (chr7:21521120–21521300), introducing a frameshift/premature stop predicted to trigger nonsense-mediated decay and remove the DNA-binding zinc-finger domain — a mechanism invisible to eQTL mapping (no SP4 eQTL in fetal or adult brain). Other examples: FURIN isoQTL (SCZ/BIP), SCN2A splicing (BIP), GRM3 downregulation (SCZ), KMT2E isoform dysregulation (SCZ).
isoTWAS: 536 isoforms across 271 genes significant; fine-mapping gave 129 putatively causal isoforms (107 genes); 14 isoforms prioritized by both isoTWAS and isoQTL colocalization (e.g. SLC9C2, KMT2E, ABCB9). MRLocus added causal support for 7 of 14 multi-eQTL TWAS genes.
Networks: rWGCNA yielded 124 gene/isoform coexpression modules (isoform-level for >120,000 transcripts); isoform modules better captured cell-type marker enrichment (OR=2.37). 8,008 module-interaction QTLs (ieQTLs); 3,960 module-specific; ieQTLs added 22 SCZ colocalizations (13 not seen with bulk eQTLs, e.g. BRINP2 × module M93). CellWalker mapped 3,739 (21.7%) of eQTL genes to a specific cellular context. GWAS/rare-variant risk localized to maturing excitatory and interneuron modules, depleted in neural-progenitor modules.

Connections

A flagship human developing-brain splicing-qtl / isoQTL resource, squarely in splicing-and-disease; the SP4 cryptic-exon NMD finding is a concrete fetal-brain allele-specific-splicing-type mechanism linking a risk allele to altered intron-cluster usage.
Splicing quantified with annotation-free leafcutter intron-excision ratios; isoform expression imputed with Salmon (cf. li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna, bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification in the wiki’s quantification lineage).
data-quality-and-bias: uniform reprocessing across 5 heterogeneous cohorts was essential — sQTLs replicated within Walker (pi1=0.93) and HDBR (pi1=0.89) after uniform remapping, showing robustness to library-prep/sequencing-depth batch effects; heritability checks controlled for hidden covariates / cell-type composition.
Sibling brain/sQTL resources: zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (adult brain regional sQTLs) and humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas (microglia isoform/sQTL atlas), both of which likewise find splicing/isoform QTLs colocalizing with neuropsychiatric/neurodegenerative risk beyond eQTLs.
Complements developing-brain proteome work wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of and the disease-mechanism review scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease.

Open questions

Why does cis-heritability fall so steeply across 10–18 PCW — how much is cellular heterogeneity vs intrinsic neuronal-maturation chromatin/regulatory change, and is the same true for splicing specifically?
Can the SP4 cryptic-exon / NMD mechanism (and similar sQTL-driven poison-exon events) be experimentally validated and therapeutically targeted?
Single-cell or long-read resolution of isoform usage in fetal cortex would test whether bulk isoQTLs/sQTLs reflect specific cell types vs mixtures (the ieQTL/CellWalker approach is an approximation).
How transferable are these cross-ancestry fine-mapped causal variants to under-sampled populations (EA/SEA were only ~8%)?

wiki/sources/wen-2024-single-cell-multip.title: wen-2024-single-cell-multiplex-chromatin
wiki/sources/wen-2024-single-cell-multip.content:
Single-cell multiplex chromatin and RNA interactions in ageing human brain
One-line: Introduces MUSIC, a single-nucleus assay co-profiling multiplex chromatin interactions, gene expression and RNA–chromatin contacts, and applies it to aged human frontal cortex.
Key takeaways

music-assay concurrently maps multiplex DNA–DNA contacts, single-nucleus transcriptomes, and RNA–chromatin associations in one nucleus, extending single-cell chromatin-conformation-capture beyond pairwise contacts.
Multiplex clusters (≥3 DNA reads) recapitulate TAD and nested-TAD structure that pairwise contacts alone reflect poorly, arguing that topologically-associating-domains are predominantly built from multiway interactions rather than pairwise chromatin-loops.
In 14 aged cortical samples, nuclei with fewer short-range contacts (“LCS-eroded”) carried an older transcriptomic signature and higher Alzheimer’s pathology, linking loss of local genome-architecture to neurodegeneration and ageing.
Cell types where a cis-eQTL contacts its target promoter (via DNA–DNA contacts) tend to be the same cell types where that eQTL actually regulates the gene, tying cis-regulatory-elements to physical contact.
Female cortical cells show heterogeneous XIST–chromosome X association, with the active X exhibiting higher sub-10 Mb contact frequency than the inactive X.

Notable claims / data

H1 benchmark: 2,546 cells, avg 144,049 DNA + 11,384 RNA reads/cell; ensemble RNA correlates with bulk RNA-seq (rho=0.8) and iMARGI (rho=0.9); DNA contacts reproduce Micro-C compartments.
Among non-singleton DNA clusters, 27.74% were multiplex (≥3 reads); multiplex clusters enriched for long-range (submegabase–megabase) contacts.
MUSIC FC: 9,087 nuclei, 7 cortical cell types; excitatory neurons had the most frequent submegabase contacts and the highest proportion of XAL-positive cells.
LCS-erosion score (peak Pc(s) genomic distance) correlates with SCALE-model transcriptomic age far more than donor chronological age; small nuclear RNA genes (RNU4ATAC, RNU4-2, RNU5A-1, RNU12) track LCS preservation, echoing reduced spliceosome fidelity in senescence.
7/14 samples had high AD pathology (Braak ≥4); high-pathology samples showed elevated LCS-erosion across most cell types.
Caveat: RNA–DNA detection has false negatives; XIST/XAL heterogeneity persisted after filtering to >5,000 RNA reads/cell, arguing it is biological, not purely sensitivity-driven.

Connections

Complements bulk RNA–chromatin mapping (iMARGI) and Micro-C referenced here; extends chromatin-conformation-capture to single-cell multiplex + RNA layers.
Ties 3d-genome-and-disease to brain ageing/Alzheimer’s, connecting to neurodegeneration work in the wiki.
eQTL–promoter contact findings relate to cis-regulatory-elements and cell-type-specific gene regulation.

Open questions

Is LCS erosion a cause or consequence of transcriptomic ageing and AD pathology?
Does the active-vs-inactive X contact-frequency difference generalize beyond cortex, and what drives cell-type variation in XAL?
Can multiplex-contact resolution scale to disentangle causal 3D-genome changes across disease progression?

wiki/sources/wenkel-2026-txpert-using-mu.title: wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge
wiki/sources/wenkel-2026-txpert-using-mu.content:
TxPert: using multiple knowledge graphs for prediction of transcriptomic perturbation effects
One-line: txpert is a latent-transfer deep learning model that fuses multiple gene–gene knowledge-graphs (curated databases plus high-throughput perturbation screens) to predict transcriptomic responses to unseen single, double and cross-cell-line genetic perturbations — a single-cell-perturbation-modeling method.
Key takeaways

txpert couples two modules: a basal-state encoder (MLP best for unseen/double tasks) embedding batch-matched control profiles, and a GNN perturbation encoder that reads gene(product)–gene(product) interaction graphs; embeddings are combined by additive latent shift and decoded to the perturbed expression profile.
It targets three out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks in one framework: unseen single perturbations within a cell line, novel double perturbations from seen singles, and perturbations in unseen cell lines (zero-shot cross-context).
Combining complementary graphs is the central finding: database-derived graphs (STRING, GO) plus screen-derived proprietary Recursion “maps” (PxMap from phenomics/microscopy; TxMap from single-cell transcriptomics screens) yields best performance; incrementally adding all four graphs peaks performance.
It also delivers methodological best-practices: batch-matched (not global-mean) controls to avoid mistaking batch variance for perturbation effect, and retrieval metrics alongside Pearson Δ.

Notable claims / data

For unseen single perturbations, TxPert approaches split-half experimental reproducibility in 3 of 4 cell lines (K562, Jurkat, HepG2); it improves Pearson Δ for unseen singles by 8–25% over existing methods.
Best per-task architectures: Exphormer-MG graph transformer integrating STRING+GO+PxMap+TxMap for unseen singles; GAT-MLG for doubles. Adding the 4th graph source beats best 3-source (t-test P < 0.027).
