Cell lines from Replogle genome-scale Perturb-seq (K562, RPE1, HepG2, Jurkat; >2,000 perturbations each); doubles evaluated on the Norman dataset using GEARS predefined splits.
Baselines beaten: GEARS (falls below the nonlearned general/additive baseline), scLAMBDA (GPT-3.5 text embeddings), batch-only model, and a general additive+mean statistical baseline; cross-cell-line, TxPert beats general baseline in all 4 held-out lines.
Caveats: a documented failure mode is that the model does not predict the typical downregulation of the perturbation target’s own mRNA; cross-cell-line retrieval gap to split-half reproducibility is large; trained on largely cancer-derived immortalized lines, limiting generalization to human primary tissue. Robust until >60% of STRING edges are removed.

Connections

artificial-intelligence, ai-in-genomics, foundation-models (contextualizes a “reality check” on transcriptomics foundation models such as scGPT/scVI as basal encoders).
txpert (tool entity).
Sibling single-cell perturbation methods: CMonge and foundation-model interpretation SIGnature, sharing the single-cell-perturbation-modeling concept.
knowledge-graphs as inductive-bias priors for biology.

Open questions

Can few-shot/active-learning extensions beat the zero-shot cross-cell-line setting, and will giga-scale atlases improve cross-context generalization?
How to add metrics that explicitly score conditionality/specificity of effects in novel contexts, and fix the target-gene downregulation failure mode?
Will the proprietary PxMap/TxMap graphs (and the model) be broadly reproducible/reusable outside Recursion’s data?

wiki/sources/weyn-vanhentenryck-2018-pre.title: weyn-vanhentenryck-2018-precise-temporal-regulation-of-alternative
wiki/sources/weyn-vanhentenryck-2018-pre.content:
Precise temporal regulation of alternative splicing during neural development
One-line: A systematic, high-temporal-resolution map of developmentally regulated alternative-splicing in the mouse cortex that resolves “early-switch” vs “late-switch” exon modules, shows they are under combinatorial control by four neuronal RNA-binding-protein families (Nova, Rbfox, Mbnl, Ptbp), and unexpectedly finds that peripheral sensory neurons retain an “immature” splicing program because they lack Nova/Rbfox.
Key takeaways

AS is pervasively and dynamically regulated during neurodevelopment: across 9 mouse cortex time points (E14.5 → 21 months), >20,000 events (32% of brain-expressed genes) change significantly between at least two stages. A curated set of 2883 developmentally regulated cassette exons (1909 known, 974 novel) is the analysis core.
Developmentally regulated exons are far more constrained than cassette exons overall — more frame-preserving (68.7% vs 43.2%), more conserved in human brain (63.4% vs 29.2%), and more often under strong purifying selection (31% vs 3.9%) — implying functional importance.
WGCNA on these exons yields four temporal modules: M2 = early switches around birth, M1 = late switches between P4 and P15 (M1+M2 = 74% of events, monotonic); M3/M4 = complex non-monotonic. Early-switch genes are enriched for ion channels / transmembrane transport / synaptic transmission; late-switch genes for cytoskeleton / neuron projection / synapse formation.
The modular program is “pan-neuronal”: the same early/late switches recur in purified cortical pyramidal neurons, spinal motor neurons, and ESC-derived glutamatergic neurons; 92–94% of core M1/M2 monotonic exons switch in the same direction across cortex and specific subtypes.
Four splicing-factors families (ptbp1/Ptbp2, Nova1/2, Rbfox1–3, Mbnl1/2) combinatorially time the switches. Nova/Ptbp targets switch early (~P0), Mbnl targets switch late (~P7+), Rbfox in between. Mbnl, Rbfox and Nova promote the mature pattern (80–96% of targets); Ptbp suppresses it (80%).
A random-forest “developmental splicing code” predicts early/late switches with AUC 0.87–0.92; a model using only the four RBP families (“RBP4”) nearly matches the full model, arguing these four families are the dominant timers.
Striking finding: adult peripheral sensory neurons (olfactory sensory neurons, photoreceptors, DRG/trigeminal/jugular/nodose ganglia) retain an immature splicing pattern in early-switch (M2) exons — a “chimeric” state (immature early-switch, mature late-switch) — because they lack Nova and/or Rbfox while still expressing Mbnl and Ptbp.

Notable claims / data

Thresholds throughout: |ΔΨ| ≥ 0.2 and FDR ≤ 0.05 (percent-spliced-in / percent-spliced-in based).
A conserved 12-nt microexon in Kdm1a/LSD1 peaks in inclusion between P0–P7, consistent with its role in neurite outgrowth via a phosphorylation site.
RBP target networks defined by integrative modeling (depletion RNA-seq + CLIP protein–RNA maps) at ~95–98% specificity and 57–78% sensitivity. 36% of module exons (50% of core members) are regulated by ≥1 of the four RBPs vs 9% of all cassette exons (p < 2.2e-16); ~12% of module exons by >1 family; 11 exons regulated by all four (10 of them in M1).
Worked example Gabrg2 exon 9 (inclusion altered in schizophrenic brains): activated synergistically by Nova + Rbfox + Mbnl1/2 and repressed by Ptbp2; combined RBP overexpression in 293T cells drives inclusion in a reporter, abrogated by mutating a newly identified Mbnl site 4 nt downstream of the Rbfox site.
RBP-specific Splicescope (see below) amplifies maturation-stage shifts on RBP knockout: e.g. Mbnl1/2 dKO adult brain shifts stage 6→5 overall but 6→4 on Mbnl targets only; Nova depletion shifts stage 3→1 on Nova targets only (no shift on full model).
Sensory cells: 88–98% of differentially spliced M2 exons are more immature in sensory cells vs mature CNS neurons, but only 65–81% of M1 exons. OSNs lack Rbfox1/3 and Nova1/2 (confirmed by immunofluorescence: mature Calmegin+ OSNs lack Rbfox1/3, retain moderate Rbfox2); no global deviation across the 372 assayed RBPs.
Causal validation: lentiviral Nova1-GFP in rat embryonic DRG culture is sufficient to switch 5 Nova-target exons from the immature (DRG-like, Nova2-KO-like) to the mature (WT cortex) pattern.
Caveat: entirely mouse (with cross-species conservation to human brain); cortex is a tissue mixture, though authors argue switches are not driven by cell-composition changes; ESC-derived neurons plateau at predicted stage 4–5 (P4–P7), never fully mature in vitro.

Connections

A mouse, mechanism-focused counterpart to the human developmental-splicing siblings in this wiki: kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (developmentally regulated splicing, RBP control incl. Mbnl/Celf) and park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative (population splicing code, RBP binding sites); shares the “splicing code” framing with the deep-learning lineage around spliceai.
The four-RBP combinatorial logic instantiates the splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins control of alternative-splicing and the broader splicing-code concept; ptbp1 here acts (with Ptbp2) as the principal repressor of the mature program.
Disease relevance (plain-text concept splicing-and-neurodevelopment): Gabrg2 exon 9 ties the program to schizophrenia; Mbnl loss links to myotonic-dystrophy-type mis-splicing — relevant to scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease and baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator.
Introduces the splicescope tool/atlas (maturation-stage classifier from splicing profiles).

Open questions

What is the functional consequence of the “chimeric” immature-early/mature-late splicing state for peripheral sensory neuron physiology?
Why is there an asymmetry toward increased exon inclusion (peptide insertion) in mature neurons — does it shape the neuronal proteome (e.g. phosphosite availability)?
How do the additional early-switch regulators flagged here (Elavl3/4, nSR100/Srrm4, Celf) quantitatively contribute once more depletion/CLIP datasets exist?
Do the same temporal modules and RBP timing hold in human cortical development, where only conservation (not direct time-course) is shown here?

wiki/sources/wilkins-2020-baz1b-neural-c.title: wilkins-2020-baz1b-neural-crest-self-domestication
wiki/sources/wilkins-2020-baz1b-neural-c.content:
BAZ1B, Neural Crest Cells, and the Case for Human Self-Domestication
One-line: Short Trends in Genetics Spotlight by Adam Wilkins praising Zanella et al. (2019) for pinpointing BAZ1B as a dose-sensitive master regulator of neural-crest-driven facial patterning, tying it to the human self-domestication hypothesis.
Key takeaways

BAZ1B, a gene in the 7q11.23 Williams-Beuren syndrome region, acts as a dose-sensitive master regulator of genes (e.g., TFAP2A, ZEB2) required for building the bones of the face via neural crest cells — a story of neurodevelopment and craniofacial morphogenesis.
Anatomically modern humans carry fixed regulatory mutations near BAZ1B absent from archaic hominins (Neanderthals, Denisovans); these are almost certainly mild loss-of-function changes that slightly reduce expression and, hence, facial-bone robustness — a proposed correlate of human-brain-evolution and behavioral change.
The commentary frames the finding as support for the neural crest domestication syndrome (NCDS) mechanism and the claim that humans are a self-domesticated species.

Notable claims / data

The reduced facial bones of modern humans versus archaic hominins parallel the domestication syndrome seen across other mammals (reduced facial projection, tameness).
This is a Spotlight/commentary, not primary research — it reports no new data; all empirical results are attributed to the commented study, Zanella, M. et al. (2019) Sci. Adv. 5, eaaw7908, which performed dosage analysis of the 7q11.23 Williams region.
(Brevity note: the source is a very short opinion piece; only the closing portion was legible in the ingested OCR, so the framing above is reconstructed from that text plus its reference list.)

Connections

Complements developmental and evolutionary-genomics coverage in the wiki via human-brain-evolution and neurodevelopment; the neural-crest / self-domestication angle is otherwise novel to this collection.

Open questions

What are the mechanistic links between the molecular biology of BAZ1B/neural-crest genes and the neurobiological foundations of social behavior seen in Williams syndrome?
Can dose-sensitive regulatory changes in a single master regulator plausibly account for the coordinated suite of traits attributed to a domestication syndrome?

wiki/sources/willsey-2013-coexpression-n.title: willsey-2013-coexpression-networks-implicate-human-midfetal
wiki/sources/willsey-2013-coexpression-n.content:
Coexpression Networks Implicate Human Midfetal Deep Cortical Projection Neurons in the Pathogenesis of Autism
One-line: A landmark “bottom-up” spatiotemporal gene-coexpression-network analysis showing that high-confidence autism risk genes converge on glutamatergic layer 5/6 cortical projection neurons of human midfetal prefrontal/motor-somatosensory cortex.
Key takeaways

Starting from only nine high-confidence ASD (hcASD) “seed” genes (genes with two or more de novo loss-of-function mutations in unrelated probands), the authors built coexpression networks across human brain development to ask when, where, and in what cell type these biologically pleiotropic ASD genes converge — a question central to psychiatric-genetics and neurodevelopment.
Networks were built on the BrainSpan spatiotemporal transcriptome (psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource): 16 brain regions, 57 postmortem brains, 15 developmental periods (5.7 postconceptual weeks to 82 years).
An independent set of 122 probable ASD (pASD) genes (single de novo LoF mutation each) was significantly enriched in coexpression networks of prefrontal and primary motor-somatosensory cortex (PFC-MSC) during two overlapping midfetal windows (periods 3-5 and 4-6, ~10-24 postconceptual weeks).
Three independent layers of evidence (human laser-microdissected laminar data, mouse neocortical-layer RNA-seq, and cell-type marker genes) localized the signal to deep-layer (L5/L6) cortical glutamatergic projection neurons in the inner cortical plate (CPi).
Immunostaining/in situ hybridization confirmed robust expression of five hcASD genes (TBR1, POGZ, chd8, DYRK1A, SCN2A) in midfetal CPi projection neurons; TBR1 and POGZ colocalized with FOXP2 and SATB2 projection-neuron subtype markers.

Notable claims / data

Consolidated cohort of 1,043 ASD families (599 quartets, 444 trios), including 56 newly sequenced Simons Simplex Collection (simons-simplex-collection) quartets; 144 de novo LoF mutations identified in probands.
De novo LoF enrichment in probands vs unaffected siblings: OR 2.21 (95% CI 1.45-3.36). Three de novo LoF hits in one gene define an ASD gene (FDR q = 0.0002); ANK2 reported as a novel hcASD gene.
pASD enrichment survived a stringent permutation test (100,000 iterations weighting seed selection by gene size and GC content): PFC-MSC period 3-5 (p = 0.003) and period 4-6 (p = 0.05); the period 3-5 signal was the strongest and most robust.
pASD genes falling within the midfetal networks had significantly lower (more significant) TADA scores (tada-method) than permuted expectation (p = 6.0e-5 for period 3-5), i.e. stronger independent evidence of true ASD association.
Increased laminar connectivity was specific to CPi (inner cortical plate; corresponding to L5/6) for the period 3-5 network (corrected p = 2.7e-4); superficial-layer markers were not enriched.
TBR1 (most connected hcASD gene) was validated functionally: Tbr1 knockout P0 mouse cortex RNA-seq altered four network genes (NR4A2, SV2B down; FEZF2, NEFM up), all prior TBR1 ChIP-seq targets. Removing TBR1 did not change the convergence result.

Connections

Extends the human brain transcriptomic substrate of BrainSpan (psychencode-brainspan-developmental-resource) into a disease-convergence framework, complementary to broader gene-coexpression-network approaches.
Uses the TADA statistical framework (tada-method) to weight independent ASD association of network genes.
Anchors ASD (autism) risk in specific neuronal-cell-types and a defined developmental window, informing neurodevelopment and psychiatric-genetics models of convergence.
The authors note their accompanying-issue companion (Parikshak et al. 2013) reached partly overlapping conclusions (CPNs, midfetal, L5/6) but additionally implicated superficial L2/3.

Open questions

The nine-seed, specificity-over-sensitivity design likely reveals only one of several convergence points; preliminary signals in thalamus and cerebellum during postnatal development remain unresolved.
Well-established syndromic ASD genes (FMR1, TSC1, TSC2, PTEN) were absent from the highlighted networks — how do they fit a convergence model?
How do these midfetal deep-layer projection-neuron findings translate to functional/circuit-level ASD mechanisms and treatment targets?

wiki/sources/witten-2011-understanding-s.title: witten-2011-understanding-splicing-regulation-through-rna
wiki/sources/witten-2011-understanding-s.content:
Understanding splicing regulation through RNA splicing maps
One-line: A conceptual review arguing that integrating genome-wide protein–RNA interaction data (CLIP/iCLIP) with splicing profiles into “RNA splicing maps” reveals shared, position-dependent principles by which RNA-binding proteins regulate alternative splicing.
Key takeaways

Interactions between rna-binding-proteins (RBPs) and pre-mRNA are presented as the primary regulators of alternative-splicing decisions; the review focuses on protein–RNA interactions over other modes (RNA–RNA, riboswitches, ncRNAs).
An “RNA splicing map” integrates genome-wide RBP-binding positions with splicing profiles to determine the position-dependent regulatory effect of binding, building a composite cassette exon centered on the 5’ and 3’ splice-sites and branch point.
A single genome-wide dataset is insufficient: binding sites are often far from alternative exons, motifs are degenerate (e.g. Nova’s YCAY), and many high-affinity binding events are non-functional — so independent binding AND splicing datasets must be integrated.
Across diverse, non-homologous RBPs a shared positional logic emerges: binding near branch points, splice sites, or within exons tends to silence inclusion, whereas binding downstream of exons tends to enhance inclusion. This contributes to the broader notion of a positional splicing-code / splicing-regulatory-elements grammar.
SR proteins are the notable exception, enhancing inclusion when bound within the exon; relates to splicing-factors and splicing-regulatory-elements.

Notable claims / data

Estimated proportion of human genes producing alternative isoforms rose from 35% (1999) to 94% (2008) as genome-wide depth increased.
The Dscam transcript (dscam) in Drosophila contains exon clusters of 12, 48 and 33 mutually exclusive exons that could theoretically yield 38,016 isoforms, regulated by RNA–RNA secondary structure.
Three independently derived Nova RNA splicing maps (bioinformatic+microarray; HITS-CLIP+microarray; RNA-seq for the orthologue Pasilla) differ in detail but agree on the same primary enriched-binding positions; conservation between mouse Nova and Drosophila Pasilla shows position-dependent effects are evolutionarily conserved.
PTB (ptbp1) illustrates dataset dependence: a CLIP+RT-PCR map suggested PTB mostly silences inclusion when bound downstream, while an independent microarray-based map indicated it more often enhances inclusion when bound downstream — integration of independent data is required.
Silencing RBPs near the exon: Nova, hnrnpa1 (hnRNP A1), hnRNP C/L/H, Fox, PTB, Mbnl1; enhancing RBPs downstream: Nova, hnRNP L, Fox, PTB, Mbnl1, TIA. hnRNP C (binds RNA as a homo-tetramer, ~230–240 nt) and TIA show more restricted maps.
TIA proteins interact with U1 snRNP (via U1-C); their U1 association may restrict binding to downstream positions and lets them silence distal exons by acting on splicing kinetics (H→E→A complex transitions; commitment at A complex).
Mechanistic models discussed for transcription–splicing coupling: recruitment model (SR proteins bind Pol II CTD), first-come-first-served model (challenged by fibronectin exon 33 data showing the downstream intron splices first even under slow elongation), and the splicing kinetics model (temporal window for RBP assembly governs choice).
Caveat: the review is conceptual; RNA splicing maps had been derived for only a handful of individual RBPs and combinatorial multi-RBP maps remained future work.

Connections

Foundational mechanistic complement to wiki RBP entities hnrnpa1, ptbp1 and to dscam; underpins concept pages splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-code, splicing-factors, splice-sites.
Pairs with regulation-focused reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of as an earlier, CLIP-era account of position-dependent regulation.
The CLIP/iCLIP methodology it surveys is the experimental basis later mined by computational splicing-code efforts surveyed in capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
Touches cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin via the recruitment / kinetics / first-come-first-served coupling models.

Open questions

Can combinatorial RNA splicing maps (multiple RBPs on the same pre-mRNA, e.g. via high-resolution iCLIP) capture competitive/cooperative regulation across the genome?
What biochemical mechanism explains the shared “enhance when downstream” principle — U1 snRNP stabilization, TIA-mediated complexes, RNA-structure change, or intron-definition looping?
Is fast splicing kinetics of flanking introns a general feature of exons sensitive to distal RBP binding or transcriptional elongation?
How do SR-protein RNA splicing maps (binding both exons and introns) refine or break the basic positional rules?

wiki/sources/wolfe-2025-deep-transcripto.title: wolfe-2025-deep-transcriptomics-reveals-cell-specific-isoforms
wiki/sources/wolfe-2025-deep-transcripto.content:
Deep transcriptomics reveals cell-specific isoforms of pan-neuronal genes
One-line: Uses deep, replicated neuron-type-specific transcriptomes from the C. elegans CeNGEN consortium to map alternative splicing across neuron types at single-cell-type resolution, revealing widespread differential intron retention, pan-neuronal genes with cell-specific isoforms, and three splicing factors controlling splicing in individual neurons.
Key takeaways

Leverages CeNGEN deep bulk transcriptomes (FACS-sorted, replicated, rRNA-depleted) from 46 of 118 C. elegans neuron types to call high-confidence alternative splicing even for lowly expressed genes — overcoming single-cell RNA-seq’s low capture/sensitivity (splicing-methods).
Identified 15,515 alternative-splicing events in 5,779 genes across neuron types; strikingly, intron-retention accounts for >52% of all AS events — far more than cassette exons (7.2%) — and differential intron retention is >7-fold more prevalent than differential cassette-exon splicing.
Most retained introns introduce frameshifts and/or premature stop codons (66.6% frame-shifting; 32.8% frame-preserving + stop codon), implying widespread coding-consequence and regulatory (NMD/expression-tuning) roles rather than just noise.
Differential intron retention is robustly biological, not artifact: retained introns are covered by hundreds of reads, reproducible across biological replicates, absent of strain-specific de novo mutations, and confirmed in whole-animal polyA-selected RNA and by RT-PCR.
Gene expression and alternative splicing are regulated orthogonally: differential AS correlates poorly with differential gene expression across neuron types (adj. R² ≈ 0.008), but different AS types correlate with each other.
Developed a “uniqueness index” algorithm that aggregates statistically significant pairwise-differential AS to find splicing events (and gene-expression) uniquely regulated in one neuron type — revealing unannotated cell-specific isoforms of pan-neuronal genes (e.g. unc-31/CADPS, unc-104 kinesin, dgk-1, mec-2).
Genetic interrogation identified three distinct splicing factors that each control splicing in a single neuron, demonstrating in vivo regulatory logic; e.g. the RBP unc-75/CELF directs composite splicing choices.
Built a user-friendly platform for spatial transcriptomic visualization of these splicing patterns at single-neuron resolution.

Notable claims / data

AS event breakdown: 5′ splice site 26.8% (1,922 events), 3′ splice site 11.4% (1,503), cassette 7.2% (1,137), composite 1.9% (751), intron retention 52.0% (10,160), mutually exclusive 0.6% (42).
Example gradation: unc-31 exon 3 inclusion ranges continuously from 78% (AVL) to 1% (AVG) across neuron types; uniqueness index of 23 for AVL (max possible 45).
dgk-1 (diacylglycerol kinase theta): a broadly expressed gene with a small subset of neurons (excitatory motor neurons DA/VB) selecting an unannotated downstream 3′ splice site (two isoforms differing by 67 vs 31 amino acids), validated with an RFP/GFP splicing reporter in vivo.
pct-1 intron retained ~69% in CAN neuron but ~0% in most others; pqn-53 intron differentially retained across neuron types — both reproducible across replicates and seen in whole-animal RNA.
OLL sensory neuron is a strong outlier for upstream 3′ splice site selection (83% of its differential 3′ss events are upstream-biased), e.g. rskn-1 (S6 kinase) 75% upstream in OLL vs 0–5% elsewhere.
ASK sensory neuron uniquely expresses “serpentine” G-protein-coupled receptor genes (16/20 top uniquely expressed), consistent with its chemosensory role.
Caveats: findings are in C. elegans (invariant nervous system enabling deep isogenic sampling); generality and biological significance of extreme single-neuron outliers (e.g. OLL) remain unknown; some conclusions rest on the uniqueness-index statistic.

Connections

A single-cell-type-resolution map of alternative-splicing and especially intron-retention across neuron types — connects to splicing-and-neurodevelopment and functional single-cell genomics; contrasts with the low sensitivity of scRNA-seq splicing.
The prominence of regulated intron-retention with coding consequences echoes intron-detention/NMD-based regulation seen elsewhere (relates to gonzalez-iglesias-2024-intron-detention-tightly-regulates-the and detained-intron literature).
Identifies neuron-specific splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins (e.g. unc-75/CELF) as regulators — connects to splicing-regulatory-elements and cell-type-specific splicing codes.
The uniqueness-index concept and spatial-visualization platform are reusable tools (splicing-methods) applicable to other organisms and data types.

Open questions

Do the extreme single-neuron splicing outliers (e.g. OLL upstream-3′ss bias) have defined physiological functions, and are analogous patterns present in vertebrate neurons?
What is the functional/regulatory fate of the many frameshifting retained introns — NMD substrates, expression buffers, or protein-generating?
How many pan-neuronal genes harbor functionally important unannotated cell-specific isoforms genome-wide, and how are they missed by standard annotation?
Which trans-factors establish each cell-specific isoform, and how do combinations of RBPs encode the observed neuron-type splicing signatures?

wiki/sources/won-2016-chromosome-conform.title: won-2016-chromosome-conformation-elucidates-regulatory
wiki/sources/won-2016-chromosome-conform.content:
Chromosome conformation elucidates regulatory relationships in developing human brain
One-line: Hi-C maps of mid-gestation human cortex link noncoding enhancers and disease variants to distal target genes, reannotating human-lineage evolution and schizophrenia risk loci.
Key takeaways

Generates high-resolution 3D chromatin contact maps (chromatin-conformation-capture / Hi-C) from the cortical plate (CP, post-mitotic neurons) and germinal zone (GZ, progenitors) of mid-gestation human cortex, resolving genome-architecture during neurogenesis.
Physical chromatin-loops within topologically-associating-domains connect distal cis-regulatory-elements to promoters; interacting enhancer-promoter pairs show higher DHS/chromatin-accessibility correlation, eQTL contact frequency, and eRNA-mRNA coexpression than non-interacting pairs.
~40% of genes contacting human-gained enhancers do so brain-specifically (“Hi-C-evol” genes), enriched for axon guidance/synaptic function, intellectual-disability risk genes (e.g. ASPM), and purifying selection — supporting regulatory (not coding) change in human cortical evolution.
Assigns intergenic/intronic schizophrenia noncoding-variants to ~500 target genes not adjacent to or in LD with index SNPs, advancing psychiatric-genetics interpretation of GWAS loci.
Genome editing validates a distal credible SNP (rs1191551, 760 kb away) as a regulator of FOXG1, a telencephalic-development/interneuron-migration-relevant transcription factor.

Notable claims / data

Two cortical zones (CP, GZ) profiled by Hi-C; A/B compartment switching between GZ and CP parallels DHS and epigenetic-mark changes.
eQTL-gene pairs vs background: CP OR=3.26 (P=2.33x10^-110), GZ OR=3.12 (P=5.06x10^-103) for Hi-C interaction.
Integrated 2,104 human-gained corticogenesis enhancers; ARHGAP11B contacts a human-gained enhancer ~500 kb away in CP.
108 PGC schizophrenia loci integrated via CAVIAR/PGC credible SNPs; candidate risk genes enriched for post-synaptic density, acetylcholine receptors (CHRM2/4, CHRNA2/3/5, CHRNB4), neuronal differentiation, chromatin remodellers; recovers DRD2, SOX2, CACNA1C, GRIN2A.
rs1191551 risk allele (T) decreases luciferase activity (P=3.40x10^-49); CRISPR/Cas9 deletion of the SNP-flanking region reduces FOXG1 (not PRKD1) expression.
Caveat: regulatory SNPs frequently skip the nearest gene, so linear proximity mis-assigns targets.

Connections

Companion 3D-genome / brain-evolution work: che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome on 3d-genome-evolution.
Schizophrenia/autism functional-genomics context overlaps li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across, and parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna (shared Geschwind-lab lineage).
Massively-parallel and CRISPR enhancer-mapping follow-ups: lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric, deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory, groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at.
Contact-map dataset captured as entity won-developing-brain-hic-dataset.

Open questions

How do cell-type-resolved (vs bulk CP/GZ) contact maps refine enhancer-gene assignments in the developing cortex?
Do the ~500 Hi-C-nominated schizophrenia genes replicate as causal under perturbation, and how many are shared with autism/ID?
Can 3D-informed target assignment generalize as a template across tissue-specific complex-trait GWAS?

wiki/sources/wright-2022-alternative-spl.title: wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source
wiki/sources/wright-2022-alternative-spl.content:
Alternative splicing as a source of phenotypic diversity
One-line: A Perspective arguing that alternative-splicing is a dynamic, evolutionarily labile process that — despite mostly producing non-functional transcripts — can fuel adaptation, speciation and organismal complexity.
Key takeaways

alternative-splicing generates multiple transcripts (and protein isoforms) per gene, expanding coding capacity and enabling quantitative regulation of protein levels; ~90–95% of human genes undergo AS.
The relative importance of AS event types varies by lineage: exon skipping dominates in metazoans, intron retention in plants and fungi. AS prevalence correlates with organism complexity (splicing-and-evolution).
The mode of spliceosome assembly shapes regulation: intron definition tends to give intron retention; exon definition (more prevalent in animals) gives exon skipping/inclusion and yields viable alternative protein-coding isoforms.
Most AS events are probably noise/errors and non-conserved, but a functional subset diversifies the proteome and is tied to development; coupled AS–NMD via splicing-and-disease-relevant poison exons drives autoregulation of splicing-factors.
Novel splice patterns arise via cis-effects (exon duplication, transition, exonization, intronization at splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements) and trans-effects (mutations in splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins); cis effects appear more common in interspecies divergence.
Divergent splicing underpins documented adaptations and contributes to splicing-and-evolution and speciation, often by producing non-functional truncated proteins rather than new functions.

Notable claims / data

An estimated 90–95% of human genes undergo AS; 88% of GWAS heritability-associated variants are in non-coding regions.
Long-read RNA-seq identified 7,874 complete transcript isoforms of Dscam1, thousands essential for neural circuit development.
~75% of human exon-skipping events seen by RNA-seq were also detected by ribosome profiling; ~40% of RNA-level splicing changes were concordant with translation changes in human glia/glioma.
Of one-third of human tissue-specific AS isoforms detected proteomically, 95% were highly conserved across vertebrates. Mutations in consensus splice sites account for ~10% of human inherited disease.
FOXP1 exons 18/18b: AS switch controls DNA-binding and pluripotency (OCT4, NANOG) vs differentiation; conserved across 46 vertebrate species (splicing-code).
Turtle temperature-dependent sex determination: CLK1/4 phosphorylates SR proteins (active <26°C → male; inactive >31°C → female), e.g. intron retention in chromatin modifier Jarid2 (splicing-and-chromatin).
Drosophila sex-determination cascade: Sxl → tra → dsx/fru/msl-2; dsx sex-specific splicing conserved across insects (Bombyx mori, Bactrocera oleae, Tribolium castaneum).
Stickleback MSX2A: a single exon-1 mutation creates a poly-G/ISE that activates an alternative 5′ splice site → frameshift + PTC → truncated protein → shorter spines in freshwater fish.
Vampire bat (Desmodus rotundus) TRPV1: trigeminal-specific exon 14a inclusion (PTC) yields a short isoform activating ~30°C, enabling infrared prey detection while full-length heat sensing is retained in dorsal root ganglia (~40% short transcripts in TG).
Capsella rubella selfing: two SNPs make CYP724A1 introns splice efficiently → more brassinosteroid → smaller petals (outbreeding→selfing transition).
Cavefish (Astyanax mexicanus) per2 exon-21 skipping (PTC) → truncated Per2 → enhanced lipogenesis via Pparγ; convergent in a second cavefish and Phreatichthys andruzzi.
TBXT (hominoid tail loss, preprint): Alu insertion in intron 6 forms a stem-loop driving ~50% exon-6 skipping; mouse heterozygotes lose tails; linked to neural tube defects.

Connections

alternative-splicing, splicing, spliceosome — core machinery and the central subject.
splice-sites, splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-code — weak splice sites of alternative exons, enhancers/silencers, and RBP-interpreted codes (rna-binding-proteins, splicing-factors).
cotranscriptional-splicing — splicing is largely co-transcriptional; nascent-RNA data show long introns spliced before downstream exon transcription.
splicing-and-chromatin — chromatin marks/Pol II processivity affect AS; SR proteins regulate Jarid2 in turtle sex determination.
splicing-and-evolution, splicing-and-disease — divergent splicing, drift vs selection, and disease links (consensus-site mutations, poison exons, myelodysplasia).

Open questions

What fraction of AS events are genuinely functional vs noise, and how to distinguish them experimentally (CRISPR, long-read, single-cell)?
Relative contribution of cis vs trans regulatory mutations to AS evolution remains unclear.
Whether NMD acts as an evolutionary capacitor buffering AS variation needs experimental evidence.
How AS divergence relates causally to speciation and adaptation in most documented cases is still untested.
AS scaling with complexity rests on small species samples and needs broader validation across the tree of life.

wiki/sources/wrighton-2017-the-diagnosti.title: wrighton-2017-the-diagnostic-power-of-rna-seq
wiki/sources/wrighton-2017-the-diagnosti.content:
Genetic testing: The diagnostic power of RNA-seq
One-line: A short Research Highlight on how RNA-seq of diseased muscle tissue boosts molecular diagnosis of Mendelian disorders left unsolved by exome/genome sequencing (highlighting Cummings et al., Sci. Transl. Med. 2017).
Key takeaways

Whole-exome (WES) and whole-genome (WGS) sequencing identify rare Mendelian diseases in only up to ~50% of cases; functional/clinical interpretation of variants is limited. rna-seq of disease-relevant tissue adds a complementary layer.
Cummings et al. applied rna-seq (GTEx-protocol) to muscle from 63 patients with suspected monogenic muscle disorders, comparing against 184 GTEx skeletal-muscle controls to find patient-unique transcript-level changes.
The approach first validated itself on 13 already-diagnosed patients (recovering their known splice aberrations), then yielded a 35% diagnosis rate among the previously undiagnosed — a molecular diagnosis for 17 patients.
Pathogenic coding and non-coding variants produced a range of splicing-and-disease defects (exon skipping, exon extension, exonic/intronic splice gain) invisible at the DNA level.

Notable claims / data

NEB exon-extension event (nemaline myopathy) missed by WES; pathogenic TTN missense; RYR1 synonymous variant causing exonic splice gain; POMGNT1 variants disrupting splice-enhancer motifs.
Non-coding pathogenic variants in 8 patients, including an intronic DMD variant creating a pseudo-exon in 3 patients (missed by WGS/WES alone); plus three DMD structural variants confirmed by WGS.
New genetic subtype of severe collagen VI-related dystrophy: a COL6A1 intron-inclusion event adding 24 amino acids, also detected in 27 additional undiagnosed collagen-VI-like dystrophy patients.

Connections

Concrete clinical application of splicing-and-disease: aberrant splicing as the diagnostic readout for unsolved Mendelian cases.
Methodologically adjacent to brechtmann-2018-outrider-a-statistical-method, which formalizes statistical outlier detection in patient-vs-reference RNA-seq for rare-disease diagnosis (here done by manual comparison to GTEx controls).

Open questions

Tissue accessibility: muscle was the affected, samplable tissue here — how to extend to disorders whose relevant tissue cannot be biopsied?
How to systematize and statistically calibrate the detection of aberrant splicing/expression beyond cohort-specific manual comparison.

wiki/sources/wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method.title: wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify
wiki/sources/wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method.content:
SpliceTrap: a method to quantify alternative splicing under single cellular conditions
One-line: Introduces splicetrap, a Bayesian method that quantifies local exon-inclusion ratios from paired-end RNA-seq, treating each exon as an independent inference problem rather than reconstructing full-length transcripts.
Key takeaways

SpliceTrap estimates the inclusion level of every exon from paired-end RNA-seq using a Bayesian inference framework, in contrast to full-transcript tools that infer isoform expression.
It works under a single cellular condition without requiring a background/reference set of reads to call relative splicing changes — useful for building maps of AS activity within one sample.
It covers the major classes of alternative splicing (see alternative-splicing): cassette exons (CA), alternative 5’ splice sites (AD), alternative 3’ splice sites (AA), and intron retention (IR).
Relies on a precompiled human exon database, TXdb, built from RefSeq (hg18) and the EST-based AS database dbCASE, decomposed into 3-exon “trios” (and “duos” for AD/AA/IR) used as independent mappable units.
Outputs the inclusion ratio (analogous to percent-spliced-in) for each exon; the authors position it as a tool to study AS regulation and to help discover nearby cis-regulatory elements.

Notable claims / data

TXdb (hg18) totals 224,995 exon trios/duos spanning 182,560 human exons: 167,445 cassette-exon (CA) candidates (11,812 annotated CA, 155,633 constitutive/CS to be tested), plus 8,667 AA, 4,838 AD, and 1,170 IR candidates.
Trio/duo representation captures 74.14% of RefSeq single-exon AS events (64.2% when ESTs included) as a single trio/duo; +9.82% (RefSeq) describable by combining two TXdb entries; CA/AA/AD/IR together account for ~75% of known human AS events in RefSeq.
Simulation accuracy (Pearson correlation, 36 nt / 75 nt reads): SpliceTrap 0.87 / 0.83, MLE (uniform priors) 0.84 / 0.79, Cufflinks 0.83 / 0.78, RPKM 0.76 / 0.75, Scripture 0.72 / 0.61. Lowest mean absolute error too (0.11 / 0.13). 5555 simulations, 1000 repeats each.
The full Bayesian model (with FSM fragment-size prior and IRM inclusion-ratio prior, smoothed with beta distributions) beats the maximum-likelihood-only variant, evidencing the value of priors.
On >60M 36-nt paired-end HeLa reads: inclusion-ratio distribution is “U”-shaped (exons mostly fully included or fully skipped). Replicate reproducibility: SpliceTrap PCC 0.74–0.77 across thresholds while reporting 97,068 exons; Cufflinks reaches PCC 0.70 only at FPKM=10 with just 11,606 exons; Scripture is reproducible (0.83–0.92) but averages ratios ~0.5 (implausibly calling most exons alternative).
SpliceTrap inclusion ratios are independent of expression level (constant ~0.95), whereas Cufflinks ratios decline with expression — evidence SpliceTrap is insensitive to transcript abundance.
Practical: ~3 h per lane (20×2M 36-nt pairs) on a 2 GHz AMD CPU, <500 MB RAM, 10–30 GB disk; supports SGE qsub; available via CSH Galaxy and at rulai.cshl.edu/splicetrap.
Caveats: no ab initio exon prediction (limited to ~200k annotated exons in TXdb); limited power on complex multi-exon AS; assumes uniform read distribution within a trio/duo; slight 3’-end bias from dbCASE-unique exons (~6%).

Connections

Computes exon-level inclusion ratios, the same quantity later standardized as percent-spliced-in (PSI); conceptually a precursor in the lineage of splicing-methods for RNA-seq AS quantification.
Complements/precedes rmats (Shen 2014), which also quantifies exon inclusion and detects differential splicing from RNA-seq — see shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection; SpliceTrap differs by scoring single conditions without a background set.
Benchmarked against full-transcript tools Cufflinks and Scripture, which it treats as the state of the art for isoform-level quantification at the time.
Authored by the Krainer and M.Q. Zhang groups; relevant to the broader splicing-regulation toolkit surveyed in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.

Open questions

How does SpliceTrap’s trio/duo Bayesian inclusion ratio compare quantitatively to the modern PSI estimators (rMATS, MISO, SUPPA) on the same data?
Could TXdb be regenerated on a current human assembly with junction-mapper-derived novel exons to remove the hg18 / dbCASE 3’-bias limitations?
How much does the uniform-read-distribution assumption bias estimates for exons with strong coverage non-uniformity, and would a learned coverage model help?

wiki/sources/wu-2014-tissue-specific-rna.title: wu-2014-tissue-specific-rna-expression-marks
wiki/sources/wu-2014-tissue-specific-rna.content:
Tissue-Specific RNA Expression Marks Distant-Acting Developmental Enhancers
One-line: Deep total RNA-seq of embryonic mouse tissues shows that enhancer RNA (eRNA) expression is tissue-specific, tracks in vivo enhancer activity, and can predict developmental enhancers independent of epigenomic marks.
Key takeaways

Enhancer RNA (eRNA) transcription is a common feature of in vivo cis-regulatory-elements: ~80% of validated enhancers show tissue-specific RNA expression that correlates with tissue-specific activity.
Strand-specific total rna-seq of E11.5 mouse heart and limb de novo identifies thousands of tissue-specifically transcribed non-coding regions (TSTRs) bearing hallmarks of bona fide enhancers.
eRNA expression signatures predict tissue-specific enhancers independent of known epigenomic marks (p300, H3K27ac), offering a transcription-based route to enhancer discovery among developmental noncoding-variants targets.
In transgenic mouse reporter assays, over half of tested TSTRs functioned as reproducible enhancers in the predicted tissue.

Notable claims / data



200M reads/tissue; 53% (heart) / 60% (limb) mapped to annotated cDNA, 38% / 30% to introns and intergenic regions.


145 intergenic heart/limb enhancers examined; enhancers 9x more likely than random regions to have ≥10 reads within 1 kb of midpoint (p=5.5E-108); 92% showed ≥1 uniquely mapped read (p=2.3E-15).
79% of heart enhancers and 83% of limb enhancers had higher eRNA expression in the active tissue (p<1E-28, Fisher’s exact).
De novo: 3,422 (heart) and 3,775 (limb) marked intergenic regions at ≥10 reads; 834 heart-specific and 1,078 limb-specific TSTRs. 69%/73% overlap evolutionarily constrained elements vs ~28% for controls; 36%/46% marked by p300 and/or H3K27ac.
Transgenic validation: 8/19 (42%) newly tested TSTRs were positive enhancers; combined with retrospective set, 17/31 (55%) were in vivo enhancers, and eRNA tissue-specificity correctly predicted activity pattern in 15/17 (88%).
Data: GEO GSE58157; transgenic results in the VISTA Enhancer Browser (enhancer.lbl.gov). Mouse mm9; Bowtie2, MACS, USEQ, GREAT, HOMER used.

Connections

Provides an in vivo, transcription-based complement to reporter-assay approaches for cis-regulatory-elements, e.g. deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory and groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at.
Extends the total-RNA-seq / non-coding transcription framework surveyed in mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian and stark-2019-rna-sequencing-the-teenage-years.
Relevant to interpreting disease-associated noncoding-variants that fall in enhancers.

Open questions

What fraction of eRNA-marked-but-transgenic-negative TSTRs are inactive/poised enhancers versus other regulatory element classes?
Would exosome depletion plus deeper sequencing recover the many enhancers still missed at current depth?
Do eRNAs here play a functional role in target-gene activation, or are they byproducts of enhancer activity?

wiki/sources/wu-2026-single-cell-proteom.title: wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of
wiki/sources/wu-2026-single-cell-proteom.content:
Single-cell proteomic landscape of the developing human brain
One-line: Label-free single-cell mass spectrometry resolves quantitative proteomes (~800 proteins/cell) of individual cells from the prenatal human cortex, revealing pervasive transcriptome–proteome discordance that pinpoints the intermediate progenitor-to-neuron transition as an autism-vulnerable window.
Key takeaways

An optimized workflow couples label-free single-cell-proteomics (one MS run per cell, Orbitrap Eclipse Tribrid, DIA-NN quantification) with precise sample prep to profile very small primary human cells, including ~7–10 μm prenatal neurons (~5–50 pg protein) — far below inputs validated in prior single-cell MS.
Achieves deep coverage (~800 proteins/cell on average) across major developing-brain cell types, the largest label-free single-cell proteomics dataset of primary human cells to date.
Documents extensive, cell-type-specific transcriptome–proteome discordance, strongest in neurodevelopmental-disorder genes; proteins are markedly more cell-type-specific than their mRNAs.
Reconstructs a proteome-level RG→IPC→EN trajectory and identifies a co-expression module (module 4) activating at the intermediate-progenitor-to-neuron transition that is evolutionarily conserved and enriched for autism risk — flagging this transition as a genetically vulnerable phase.
Argues mRNA is an imperfect proxy for protein; direct single-cell proteomics is needed to localize disease mechanisms to specific cell types.

Notable claims / data

2,310 independent MS runs → 2,310 single-cell quantitative proteomes (post-QC); ~800 proteins/protein-groups per cell on average (excluding erythroid cells).
Samples: fresh prenatal human brain at gestational weeks (GW) 13, 15, 19; GW15/GW19 microdissected into germinal zone (GZ) and cortical plate (CP); GW13 whole frontal telencephalon. FACS into low-bind microwells, label-free LC–MS.
1,505 brain cells used for cell-type analysis; cell types: RG, oRG, IPC–EN, EN, CGE-INs, OPC, microglia, vascular; coverage comparable to typical scRNA-seq gene coverage (1,000–2,000 genes); contrasts with 4,000–6,000 proteins/cell in cultured/cancer lines.
Cell diameter ~7–10 μm (fetal cortical neuron somata); prior primary single-cell MS targeted size-advantaged cells (cardiomyocytes 100–150 μm, hepatocytes 20–50 μm).
Bulk benchmark: 28 bulk proteomes, avg 8,042 proteins/sample. Paired scRNA-seq: 31,639 cells; plus population-scale snRNA-seq reference (232,328 cells).
Discordance validated by RNAscope/immunostaining: TBR1, SCGN, TRIM33 (R=0.93 with VGLUT1), ZBTB18, CHD3 (EN-restricted protein despite broad mRNA), MAPT, MKI67 (protein half-life ~1–1.5 h vs mRNA ~5 h). Even in ENs (highest concordance), mRNA–protein Spearman ρ=0.36 (<0.4).
Two discordance groups: group A (low RNA/high protein, n=143, high translational efficiency) and group B (high RNA/low protein, n=159, dosage-sensitive, high pLI, enriched for SFARI confidence-1 ASD genes and de novo truncating mutations in ASD/NDD).
New method ORBIT (Observability-Regularized, Biochemistry-Integrated Transformation) adjusts DIA-NN intensities for detectability for within-condition cross-protein ranking. WGCNA along pseudotime → 6 protein modules; modules 4 and 5 evolutionarily conserved (low dN/dS) and ASD-associated.

Connections

functional-single-cell-genomics — this is the single-cell-omics atlas anchor for the new topic.
single-cell-proteomics — the primary methodology this paper advances (label-free single-cell mass spectrometry).
Pairs with single-cell/single-nucleus RNA-seq atlases of the developing human neocortex (Kriegstein/Li lab) as a protein-level complement.
Disease-genomics connections: SFARI autism genes, de novo mutation cohorts, NDD/ASD risk genes (CHD3, PHIP, NOVA2, module-4 genes).

Open questions

Cross-modal comparisons were pseudobulk (cell-type level), not within the same single cell — can true same-cell RNA+protein co-measurement confirm the discordance map?
Coverage (~800 proteins/cell) and soluble-protein bias mean membrane proteins and chromatin remodelers are under-recovered — how much biology is missed?
Findings limited to early/midgestation cortex and few donors/regions; do they generalize to later development and other brain regions?
Will throughput-oriented approaches (plexDIA, isobaric multiplexing) preserve the quantitative fidelity that one-cell-per-run achieves here?

wiki/sources/xiao-2009-quantifying-corre.title: xiao-2009-quantifying-correcting-winners-curse
wiki/sources/xiao-2009-quantifying-corre.content:
Quantifying and Correcting for the Winner’s Curse in Genetic Association Studies
One-line: An analytic treatment of how the winner’s curse upwardly biases genetic effect-size estimates in case-control association studies, plus an ascertainment-corrected maximum likelihood estimator to reduce that bias.
Key takeaways

Effect-size estimates for newly discovered associated variants (allele frequency difference d and odds ratio OR) are systematically overestimated because investigators condition on statistically significant results — the winner’s curse, an ascertainment bias analogous to overpaying for a winning auction bid.
The authors compute analytically (not just by simulation) the conditional expectation, standard error, and bias of the naïve estimators given significance, extending prior work to a direct, model-free correction.
They propose an ascertainment-corrected maximum likelihood estimator that maximizes a likelihood conditioned on X exceeding the significance threshold, using the Nelder-Mead simplex; it estimates d and OR directly rather than penetrance parameters.
At fixed significance level, proportional bias of the naïve estimator of d (and of ln OR) is essentially a function of study power alone, independent of sample size, allele frequency, or genetic model.
Relevant to missing-heritability and effect-size estimation in the genome-wide-association-study era, where underpowered studies make the curse most severe.

Notable claims / data

Example: N=1,000 cases/controls, p=0.1, p+d=0.1258 (OR=1.295), alpha=1e-6 (power=0.01): naïve estimate of d is 0.0524 vs true 0.0258 — a proportional bias of ~103%; naïve OR estimate is 1.699 vs true 1.295.
A follow-up study sized off the inflated estimate for 80% power would enroll only 310 cases/controls and achieve just ~30% actual power; sizing off the corrected estimate gives 1,350/1,350 (actual ~85%).
Correction cuts proportional bias of d from ~103% to ~7% and of OR from ~31% to ~2% in the low-power example; corrected estimator instead underestimates d by ~10-20% and OR by ~5-10% over power 0.001-0.95.
Correction reduces absolute bias and MSE unless power is high (>~60%); at high power the two estimators have similar bias but the corrected one has larger variance.
Standard errors of the corrected MLE are 2-6x those of the naïve MLE and ~1.5-2x those of an unascertained random sample of the same size — the ascertained sample provides ~50-60% of the information of a fresh sample.
Method assumes Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (reasonable for modest complex-trait effect sizes, not for a Mendelian major locus); requires no genetic model. Extended to two-stage designs, where results mirror the one-stage case because power drives proportional bias. Software available at csg.sph.umich.edu/boehnke/winner.

Connections

A statistical-genetics contribution to quantitative-genetics and effect-size estimation feeding into replication-study design.
Corrected effect sizes bear on missing-heritability debates, since inflated initial estimates distort perceived variance explained.
Most similar to the maximum likelihood approach of Zöllner and Pritchard (2007), but estimates d and OR directly instead of penetrance parameters.

Open questions

The corrected MLE’s residual downward bias motivates an empirical Bayes alternative using genome-wide information to set a prior; ad hoc multiplicative corrections (1.05-1.10x) are counterproductive at very low power.
Confidence interval coverage of the corrected interval ranged 82-100% vs 0-92% for the naïve — how to build well-calibrated intervals given the two-component (near-zero plus normal) mixture distribution of the corrected estimator.

wiki/sources/yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-.title: yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions
wiki/sources/yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-.content:
Explicit dynamic cross-strand interactions for DNA sequence language modelling
One-line: crossdna is a parameter-efficient DNA language model that explicitly and dynamically models cross-strand (forward/reverse-complement) interactions, achieving orientation-robust, competitive performance against far larger DNA foundation models.
Key takeaways

Existing dna-language-models either treat DNA as unidirectional text (single-strand: NTv2, HyenaDNA, GROVER, DNABERT-2) or enforce reverse-complement (RC) symmetry implicitly/statically via data augmentation or equivariant architectures (Caduceus, JanusDNA). CrossDNA instead moves symmetry enforcement to the representation layer with explicit, context-dependent interstrand information exchange.
Architecture: a dual-branch model (Students A and B, identical architecture, non-shared parameters) where forward and RC segments are alternately routed to branches in a rotating (“cross-view”) schedule. Backbone combines the Comba bilinear recurrent module (long-range memory) with sliding-window attention (SWA, window 512, FlashAttention-2). A lightweight TokenBridge module exchanges cross-strand context at token resolution; a temperature-controlled gated fusion merges strands; EMA teacher self-distillation stabilizes representations against rotation-induced semantic discontinuity.
Single-nucleotide tokenization; pretrained on human reference genome GRCh38 with a BERT-style masked-LM objective (15% masked). Variants at 8.1M, 71.6M, 519M parameters (plus a 408K compact variant for matched-scale Genomic Benchmark).
Central claim: explicit dual-strand modelling delivers near-orientation-invariant predictions (forward vs RC scores tightly aligned) without data augmentation or hard equivariance constraints, plus strong parameter efficiency.
Adaptable to classification, regression, prediction, and zero-shot embedding tasks; supports interpretability (in silico mutagenesis), non-coding disease-variant prioritization, and de novo enhancer discovery.

Notable claims / data

Genomic Benchmark (8 tasks, matched compact ~264K–470K params): CrossDNA (408K) best on 5/8 tasks, mean accuracy 88.2% (top overall). Same 408K variant vs much larger models: 88.2% vs GROVER 86.4% (87M), AlphaGenome 85.9% (450M), NTv2 84.4% (500M), Evo2 82.4% (7B) — i.e. 2–4 orders of magnitude fewer params.
NTv2 benchmark (18 datasets, forward+RC reported): CrossDNA (8.1M) best/second-best on 33/36 strand-specific evaluations. Orientation gaps stayed below 0.042 vs e.g. NTv2 dropping to 0.176 on RC Splice-all (0.797 orientation gap) — illustrating CrossDNA’s orientation robustness; splice-site F1 up to 0.987.
Chromatin profiles (DeepSEA, 919 features): +1.9% mean AUC over GROVER; exceeds task-specific DeepATT. DNALONGBENCH eQTL (450,000-bp window): 9 tissues, AUC mean 0.841, up to +26% over Enformer/HyenaDNA/Caduceus.
Dual- vs single-strand contexts: CrossDNA’s largest gain is in double-strand (heterochromatin) regions (+17.90% over single-strand baseline); +37.3% ΔMCC for splice-site prediction in antisense-transcribed regions.
Caveats: poor at individual-level expression prediction (eQTL effect sizes near zero), attributed to single-reference-genome pretraining; regression shrinkage at high-activity enhancers; histone-mark prediction not optimal; scaling 71.6M→519M shows attenuating, near-saturating gains.
Interpretability: ISM recovers TF motifs; prioritizes a CHD-associated variant (rs113716316) linking the AHDC1 locus to a cardiac enhancer; discovers 748 high-confidence K562 enhancer candidates enriched for haematopoietic motifs.

Connections

Core entity: crossdna.
A foundation-models instance specialized for genomics — sits within ai-in-genomics and the broader dna-language-models lineage; explicitly benchmarked against DNABERT-2, NTv2, HyenaDNA, GROVER, Caduceus, JanusDNA, AlphaGenome, Evo2, Enformer.
Sequence-to-regulatory-function modelling connects to the splicing-code / spliceai lineage: CrossDNA reports splice-site acceptor/donor prediction as a downstream task and shows large gains for splice sites in antisense regions, extending the sequence-model approach to regulatory grammar from a cross-strand perspective.
A generative-ai adjacency: evaluated on DREAM-generated synthetic enhancers, bridging generative sequence design and discriminative scoring.

Open questions

Does population-aware pretraining (multi-genome data, variant-frequency-weighted masking) close the gap on individual-level/eQTL effect-size prediction?
Would parameter-sharing between the two branches improve lagging tasks (histone marks) without sacrificing orientation invariance?
How much of the orientation robustness and parameter efficiency transfers to out-of-distribution species and complex multilabel regulatory tasks?

wiki/sources/yano-2025-crosssplice-a-pip.title: yano-2025-crosssplice-a-pipeline-for
wiki/sources/yano-2025-crosssplice-a-pip.content:
CrosSplice: A Pipeline for Identifying Rare Splice-Site Creating Variants from Cross-Tissue Transcriptome Data
One-line: crosssplice — a pipeline that combines SpliceAI prediction with statistical association testing across cross-tissue transcriptomes (GTEx) to detect rare, often deep-intronic splice-site-creating variants (SSCVs) as actionable antisense-oligonucleotide (ASO) targets in rare disease.
Key takeaways

SSCVs — variants that create a novel splice site → cryptic exon inclusion / aberrant splicing — are a major cause of rare/intractable disease and are especially amenable to ASO splice modulation (e.g. sepofarsen for the deep-intronic CEP290 c.2991+1655A>G). Many evade conventional detection.
CrosSplice integrates ML prediction (SpliceAI delta + allele-frequency filter) + statistical association and aggregates splicing signal across many tissues, recovering rare and tissue-specific SSCVs that single-tissue sQTL or prediction-only methods miss.
Applied to GTEx (8,656 transcriptomes, 54 tissues, 479 donors): 1,743 significant SSCVs, 65% deep-intronic; 185 in ClinVar (5 pathogenic/likely-pathogenic).
Discovered a novel deep-intronic SSCV in PLA2G6 (infantile neuroaxonal dystrophy) and experimentally confirmed ASO correction of the aberrant splicing — proof of therapeutic actionability.
Deliverable = an SSCV catalogue + pipeline framed as a platform for systematic ASO-target discovery.

Notable claims / data

Method logic: candidate variants filtered by AF and SpliceAI delta, then tested for association with novel-junction usage across tissues — the cross-tissue aggregation is the sensitivity gain over single-tissue sQTL.
65% of detected SSCVs are deep-intronic — the class most invisible to exome-centric pipelines and most ASO-tractable.
Validation anchored on a clinical example (PLA2G6) with in-vitro ASO rescue.

Connections

The disease/cross-tissue counterpart to the wiki’s splice-variant-effect tools: shares the SpliceAI-prediction core with spliceai and the aberrant-splicing-from-variants goal with absplice2, but adds cross-tissue statistical confirmation and an ASO-target framing.
Therapeutically aligned with poison-exon / cryptic-exon ASO work (poison-exons, neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing) and splicing-therapeutics; mechanistically about cryptic splice-site creation (splice-sites, splicing-and-disease).
Methodologically adjacent to non-SNP / rare-variant splicing discovery (splicestr for STRs) — together these extend rare-variant splicing analysis beyond common-SNP sQTLs.

Open questions

The pipeline is cross-tissue and disease-oriented, with no developmental axis — would SSCV detection and effect differ in developing tissues (e.g. fetal brain), where many splice programs are stage-specific?
How complete is GTEx-based SSCV discovery for ultra-rare / private variants seen in only one donor?
Can cross-tissue aggregation be reconciled with strongly tissue-specific SSCVs without diluting them?

wiki/sources/yao-2015-coexpression-netwo.title: yao-2015-coexpression-networks-identify-brain-region
wiki/sources/yao-2015-coexpression-netwo.content:
Coexpression networks identify brain region–specific enhancer RNAs in the human brain
One-line: Identifies a robust set of enhancer RNAs (eRNAs) transcribed from brain-expressed enhancers in the human brain, builds eRNA–gene coexpression networks across fetal and adult brain regions, and shows these enhancer regions are enriched for autism-associated genetic variants.
Key takeaways

Defines “brain-expressed enhancers” (BEEs) and a stringently filtered robust subset (rBEEs, N = 103) that express eRNAs in a replicable, brain-specific manner and carry enhancer chromatin marks (H3K4me1/H3K27ac).
Uses a signed weighted gene coexpression network (gene-coexpression-network) to link eRNAs to candidate cis target genes across 33 human brain samples, identifying 19 modules, 12 of which are brain region– or developmental stage–specific.
rBEEs are significantly enriched for autism-associated noncoding-variants (P = 7 × 10⁻⁴), unlike previously published enhancer sets, positioning eRNA-expressing cis-regulatory-elements as risk loci in neurodevelopmental disorders.
Provides a reusable human-brain eRNA resource (data + genome browser track), the first to assess eRNA coexpression networks in the human brain — see brain-expressed-enhancers-browser.

Notable claims / data

Starting from an atlas of >43,000 human enhancers (FANTOM5 CAGE), 908 BEEs expressed eRNA in the brain (≥0.5 TPM in ≥2 samples); 673 were intronic/intergenic, none strictly exonic.
eRNA expression at 314 BEE loci replicated in independent deep CAGE + poly(A) RNA-seq from frontal, temporal, occipital cortex and cerebellum (63% replication rate against matched brain regions).
131 BEEs showed brain-enriched eRNA expression (z > 3 vs nonbrain tissues); 103 (78%) overlapped H3K4me1/H3K27ac peaks → rBEEs.
Little overlap (only 10 rBEEs) with ROADMAP brain enhancer chromatin clusters, suggesting brain-specific eRNAs and brain-specific enhancer chromatin marks mark distinct genomic regions with distinct disease associations (ASD vs BPI/SCZ).
44% (46/103) of rBEEs expressed in brain but not in cultured neurons/astrocytes, indicating cellular context strongly shapes enhancer activity (vs 93% concordance for protein-coding genes).
rBEE at chr6:21587723–21589288 (~5 kb upstream of SOX4) is top-coexpressed with SOX4 and FABP7, and also with AUTS2 — genes implicated in neuronal differentiation and autism.
rBEEs significantly enriched within introns of SFARI ASD genes (P = 0.001) but not within schizophrenia/ADD gene sets or de novo variant genes alone.

Connections

Extends cis-regulatory-elements and noncoding-variants research into the human brain, complementing genome-wide-association-study evidence for noncoding risk in psychiatric-genetics.
Applies gene-coexpression-network methodology (WGCNA) to noncoding transcripts, linking autism genetics to enhancer regulation during neurodevelopment.

Open questions

What is the mechanistic role of eRNAs at these enhancers, and are the predicted cis targets validated functionally?
Coexpression only captures targets that vary across brain regions or the fetal–adult transition; enhancers acting in other physiological states (e.g., neuronal differentiation) would be missed.
The stringent 0.5 TPM threshold may exclude functional low-expression eRNAs.

wiki/sources/you-2024-splicetransformer-.title: you-2024-splicetransformer-predicts-tissue-specific-splicing-linked
wiki/sources/you-2024-splicetransformer-.content:
SpliceTransformer predicts tissue-specific splicing linked to human diseases
One-line: A transformer-based deep-learning model (SpTransformer) that predicts tissue-specific splice-site usage across 15 human tissues directly from pre-mRNA sequence, outperforming SpliceAI/Pangolin and revealing that tissue-specific splicing alterations match the tissue of clinical manifestation for many disease variants.
Key takeaways

SpTransformer is a splicing-code model built on multi-head attention (transformer) layers that predicts, for each position in a pre-mRNA input, the probability of being a splice donor, acceptor, or neither, simultaneously across 15 human tissues (splicing-methods, deep-learning).
Trained on GTEx tissue splicing data plus a mammalian organ transcriptome dataset (human, rhesus, mouse, rat); the extra mammalian/homology data measurably improved splice-site identification and tissue-specificity prediction (ablation-tested).
Outperforms prior methods on the general splice-prediction task (~84% top-k accuracy, 91% AU-PRC) and clearly beats SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice, HAL, MaxEntScan on tissue-wise usage prediction; predicted tissue-usage distributions match GTEx-measured usage in each tissue.
Learns interpretable regulatory context: in silico mutagenesis recovers the canonical GT/AG splicing motif in all tissues plus distal intronic elements, and MEME motif discovery finds tissue-restricted de novo motifs matching rna-binding-proteins (e.g. PTBP1, SRSF4, ELAVL, DDX19B, FMR1) — implicitly integrating expression and sequence context.
Genome-wide ClinVar analysis (ΔSplice score over 1,273,053 SNVs): splicing alterations account for ~60% of intronic and synonymous pathogenic mutations; higher ΔSplice correlates with higher pathogenicity (AU-PRC 0.98 for pathogenic vs benign).
Tissue-specific splicing alterations correlate with disease manifestation independent of gene expression: derived a tissue z-score to rank tissue-specificity and found top disease genes’ tissue-specific splice-altering SNVs align with affected tissues (e.g. heart-specific TTN, muscle-specific DMD).
Validated in three brain-disorder cohorts (>164,000 individuals: ASD, SCZ, BD): only brain-specific splice-altering SNVs were significantly enriched across all three disorders, highlighting brain-specific splicing as a shared mechanism.
Applied to whole-exome data from diabetic-nephropathy blood samples, SpTransformer predicted kidney-specific splicing alterations with 83% accuracy — demonstrating inference of disease-causing tissue-specific splicing from accessible tissue.

Notable claims / data

15 tissues modeled; SpTransformer processes long input sequences and accuracy improved with longer input context.
ClinVar ΔSplice: splice acceptor/donor variants had ΔSplice near 1; missense/nonsense minimal; bimodal ΔSplice for intronic and synonymous pathogenic variants (57–64% of intronic and 56–58% of synonymous likely-pathogenic variants predicted splice-altering).
Example validations (RT-PCR): FGB chr4:154569800 G>T (Congenital Afibrinogenemia) creates a cryptic site 24 nt upstream (ΔSplice 0.99); TP53 chr17:7675994 C>A (Li-Fraumeni) creates a cryptic site 200 nt upstream (ΔSplice 0.98).
Top tissue-enriched genes recovered known biology: TTN (>300 tissue-specific events, heart/muscle), DMD (brain-specific SNVs near exon 51 matching brain isoforms), NF1 (muscle/blood-vessel/adipose/skin, matching tumorigenesis tissues).
Blood and skin (most clinically accessible) showed lower average tissue-specific splicing, cautioning against using them as universal proxies.
Benign-SNV false-positive rates for brain-specific splice-altering calls: 7.84% (ASD), 0.37% (SCZ), 4.11% (BD).
Available as an online server (tools.shenlab-genomics.org/tools/SpTransformer).
Caveats: predictions are computational (subset RT-PCR validated); tissue z-score is an empirical normalization; ClinVar/GTEx serve as ground truth with their inherent annotation limits.

Connections

Advances the tissue-specific splicing-code beyond SpliceAI/Pangolin — relates to spliceai, variant-effect-prediction, and dna-language-models / foundation-models applied to genomics (transformer architecture).
Complements experimental splice-variant assays (e.g. ParSE-seq, oneill-2024-parse-seq-a-calibrated-multiplexed-assay) by providing genome-wide in silico prioritization of splice-disrupting intronic/synonymous variants for splicing-and-disease.
Links rna-binding-proteins motifs to tissue-specific regulation, connecting to splicing-regulatory-elements and splicing-factors; brain-specific findings connect to splicing-and-neurodevelopment and psychiatric-genetics.
The finding that ~60% of intronic/synonymous pathogenic variants act via splicing reinforces the under-appreciated role of noncoding/synonymous splice-altering variation (noncoding-variants).

Open questions

How accurately does SpTransformer generalize to tissues/cell types absent from GTEx, and to single-cell resolution splicing?
Can predicted tissue-specific ΔSplice scores be experimentally validated at scale (e.g. against ParSE-seq-style assays) beyond the RT-PCR examples?
How much of the tissue-specificity is genuinely sequence-encoded vs confounded by tissue-specific RBP expression the model only partially captures?
Could brain-specific splice-altering SNV burden become a usable risk metric for ASD/SCZ/BD given the modest but nonzero benign false-positive rates?

wiki/sources/yurko-2021-approach-to-gene.title: yurko-2021-approach-to-gene-based-testing
wiki/sources/yurko-2021-approach-to-gene.content:
An approach to gene-based testing accounting for dependence of tests among nearby genes
One-line: A gene-based genome-wide-association-study testing workflow that agglomerates nearby genes into LD loci to remove between-gene test-statistic correlation, then boosts power with metadata-guided FDR control (AdaPT + gradient boosted trees), demonstrated on autism, schizophrenia and educational attainment.
Key takeaways

Gene-based tests (VEGAS/MAGMA-style quadratic statistics) already handle LD within a gene, but LD among SNPs in different nearby genes induces correlation between gene-level statistics that most studies ignore, corrupting family-wise and FDR error guarantees.
The agglomerative-ld-loci-testing algorithm merges genes into “LD loci” until all remaining pairwise squared test-statistic correlations fall below a threshold r^2 (within a window w Mb), producing a set of weakly correlated testing units; merged-locus P-values are computed by Monte-Carlo simulation of null Gaussian z-vectors.
Power is further improved with adaptive P-value thresholding (AdaPT), which uses XGBoost gradient boosted trees over gene/locus metadata to maintain finite-sample FDR control while accounting for covariates — the entire workflow runs on summary statistics.
An interactive R Shiny + plotly “LD locus zoom” tool localizes association signal within genes/loci via kernel smoothing, aiding hypothesis generation about causal variation.
Largest power gains occur for studies of intermediate power: modest for well-powered SCZ/EA, absent for extremely weak studies, greatest for the weakly powered autism (ASD) GWAS.

Notable claims / data

Three GWAS analyzed: ASD (18,381 cases / 27,969 controls), SCZ (33,426 / 32,541), EA (~1.1M subjects); n = 5,238,256 SNPs (MAF > 0.05) aligned across all three via 1000 Genomes EUR (503 individuals) and LiftOver to GRCh38.
eSNPs for functional SNP-to-gene assignment from BrainVar dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (176 individuals) and GTEx V7 adult cortex; union = 510,583 unique cis-eQTL SNP-gene pairs, 370,749 eSNPs, 12,854 genes. GENCODE v21, initial G = 57,005 candidate genes.
At the recommended r^2 = 0.25, w = 6 Mb: 17,915 genes merge into 4,136 LD loci (Positional + eSNPs), reducing 41,301 units to 27,522 genes/loci; >75% of loci contain <=5 genes; the largest is a chromosome 11 olfactory-receptor locus of >60 genes.
AdaPT:XGBoost outperforms Benjamini–Hochberg and intercept-only AdaPT for all phenotypes; for Positional ASD, intercept-only AdaPT selects zero genes/loci without metadata. ASD: 483 genes (405 in 47 loci) for Positional + eSNPs.
Important predictors: number of SNPs per gene/locus and z-statistics from genetically correlated traits for all phenotypes; low gnomAD LOEUF (loss-of-function intolerance) and GTEx WGCNA module membership mattered more for SCZ/EA than ASD.
Case studies: the 17p11.2 Smith-Magenis / Potocki-Lupski deletion/duplication locus (RAI1), FOXP1 on chr3, and the 17q21.31 inversion locus (MAPT, KANSL1) show phenotype-specific signal localization.
GSEA (size-residualized) finds ASD genes enriched for synaptic and epigenetic gene sets; size-matched FUMA GO analysis finds no ASD enrichment, illustrating a gene-size confounding tradeoff.

Connections

Extends gene-based association within the broader genome-wide-association-study and psychiatric-genetics toolkit; complementary in spirit to transcriptome-wide-association-study approaches that also map SNP effects to genes.
Focuses on autism as the motivating weakly-powered phenotype, contrasted against schizophrenia and educational attainment.
Uses metadata (eQTL slopes, co-expression modules, LOEUF constraint) reminiscent of covariate-informed methods in quantitative-genetics.
Method entity: agglomerative-ld-loci-testing.

Open questions

The workflow is sensitive to LD reference-panel mismatch (shared with MAGMA/VEGAS); properly quantifying this sensitivity in gene-level testing is left for future work.
No error-rate guarantees are available at the localized (within-locus) SNP/gene level — the visualization tool generates hypotheses but cannot pinpoint causal variation.
How broadly the AdaPT-plus-metadata gains transfer to other omics problems, and how much per-problem adaptation is required, remains to be shown.

wiki/sources/zabala-letona-2026-polyamin.title: zabala-letona-2026-polyamine-dependent-metabolic-shielding-regulates-alternative
wiki/sources/zabala-letona-2026-polyamin.content:
Polyamine-dependent metabolic shielding regulates alternative splicing
One-line: Discovers that polyamines (spermidine/spermine/putrescine) are direct regulators of alternative splicing by binding acidic phosphorylatable motifs in U2 snRNP SF3 splicing factors and shielding them from kinases — a mechanism the authors call “metabolic shielding.”
Key takeaways

Inhibiting de novo polyamine biosynthesis (via inducible shRNA against AMDI/AMD1, or drugs SAM486A/DFMO) increases phosphorylation of spliceosome proteins and perturbs alternative-splicing in cells and tissues — coupling a metabolic pathway to pre-mRNA splicing.
The effect is mediated by cationic polyamines physically binding acidic (Glu/Asp-rich) phosphorylatable motifs in splicing-factors of the U2 snRNP SF3 subcomplex (notably sf3a3 and SF3B members), preventing upstream kinases (casein-kinase-1/CK2) from phosphorylating them — termed metabolic-shielding.
Polyamine-regulated splicing changes strongly resemble the splicing signatures produced by knocking down SF3A/SF3B complex members, and rank in the top quartile of >50 splicing-factor knockdowns for extent of splicing perturbation.
SF3 subcomplex members recognize the 3′ branch-point region of introns, tying polyamine levels to splice-sites selection and cassette-exon skipping; the pathway is frequently altered in cancer (prostate, neuroblastoma).
Metabolic shielding is required for embryonic stem cell self-renewal: polyamine-deprived ES cells lose Nanog-GFP (pluripotency) and shift splicing, and a polycationic analogue rescues this without restoring conventional polyamine functions.

Notable claims / data

Phosphoproteomics after AMD1 silencing: cluster-1 phosphopeptides (progressive phosphorylation increase) enriched for spliceosomal complex / nuclear speck proteins; splicing regulation was the top-enriched GO biological process.
Exogenous polyamine supplementation partially rescues both the phosphoproteome changes and the splicing changes; blocking ODC1 with DFMO potentiates them — dose-dependent.
NMR: spermidine binds the acidic SF3A3 350–380 region with Kd ≈ 0.7 mM; spermine binds ~3× tighter (Kd ≈ 0.22 mM) and 50–100× more strongly than a control SRRM1 acidic peptide. Binding affinity scales with number of ammonium groups (Put < Spd < Spm).
Polyamines dose-dependently inhibit CK1α phosphorylation of recombinant SF3A3 in vitro (Spd IC50 ≈ 6 mM; Spm IC50 ≈ 2.5 mM) with minimal effect on kinase autophosphorylation.
Sf3a3 knock-in mice mutating the phosphorylatable serines (S365/S367/S369 → neutral) show basal splicing changes and blunted response to polyamine-biosynthesis inhibition (96% of AMD1-inhibitor-induced events unaltered in mutant MEFs).
BENSpm (a polyamine analogue that inhibits polyamine synthesis but stays polycationic) still binds SF3A3 (Kd ≈ 0.05 mM), blocks CK1, and prevents the splicing changes caused by SAM486A+DFMO — separating “conventional” from “shielding” polyamine functions.
Hypoxia lowers polyamine-biosynthesis gene expression (ODC1, SRM, SMS) and alters splicing in an SF3-dependent manner, linking the tumour microenvironment to this axis.

Connections

Adds a metabolite-driven layer of regulation upstream of the spliceosome, complementary to kinase-based control of splicing-factors (SRPK/CLK phosphorylation of SR proteins) discussed in immune-activation splicing work like bodelon-2026-native-long-read-rna-sequencing.
SF3B is the target of splicing-modulator drugs (pladienolide B) and is recurrently mutated in cancer (see sf3b1); this paper shows a physiological metabolite that tunes the same complex, relevant to splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics.
The “shielding of acidic phosphomotifs by polycations” concept generalizes beyond splicing to other post-translational modifications in acidic motifs — a new metabolic-shielding concept page.

Open questions

Which specific splicing events (and downstream protein isoforms) mediate the biological consequences (cancer growth, stemness) versus being bystanders?
Can polycationic analogues like benspm be used therapeutically to normalize splicing in cancers with dysregulated polyamine metabolism?
How is the compartmentalized/vesicular pool of polyamines (the biologically active fraction for shielding) regulated relative to total polyamine content?
Does metabolic shielding extend to other spliceosome sub-complexes and to non-phosphorylation modifications on acidic motifs?

wiki/sources/zeeberg-2004-mistaken-ident.title: zeeberg-2004-mistaken-identifiers-gene-name-errors
wiki/sources/zeeberg-2004-mistaken-ident.content:
Mistaken Identifiers: Gene name errors can be introduced inadvertently when using Excel in bioinformatics
One-line: A short cautionary correspondence documenting how Excel’s default auto-formatting silently and irreversibly corrupts gene names and clone identifiers, a foundational example of data-quality-and-bias in bioinformatics.
Key takeaways

While beta-testing MatchMiner and GoMiner on microarray data, the authors found gene names bouncing back as “unknown” and traced it to Excel’s default auto-conversions.
