Two distinct failure modes: (1) date conversion, where symbols that look like dates are rewritten (e.g. DEC1, a tumor suppressor “Deleted in Esophageal Cancer 1”, becomes “1-DEC”); (2) floating-point conversion, where identifiers of form nnnnnnnEnn become scientific notation (e.g. RIKEN clone “2310009E13” becomes “2.31E+13”).
Both conversions are irreversible: once written back, the original gene name cannot be recovered.
The errors contaminate even carefully curated public resources — an instance is shown propagating into the LocusLink database via the human-mouse homology map.

Notable claims / data

Date conversion affects at least 30 gene names (enumerated in Figure 1).
Floating-point conversion affects more than 2,000 identifiers out of a total set of 60,770 RIKEN identifiers (~3%), and generalizes to any clone designation derived from plate coordinates.
Because 96-well plates have 8 rows and 12 columns, all row-E clones are ~12.5% of a plate (6.25% for 384-well plates); most libraries contain hundreds of plates.
Work-arounds all require sustained user vigilance: prepend a space or apostrophe upstream; use the Text Import Wizard “text” mode; pre-format recipient cells; or use Paste Special > As Text (needed for pasting from Word).
The authors provide a Unix C-shell script (companion “SymbolMutation” site) to scan data for date- and float-conversion errors, integrated into MatchMiner and GoMiner quality checks.

Connections

Concrete, widely-cited case study of data-quality-and-bias arising from tooling rather than biology, relevant to any high-throughput pipeline that maps clone identifiers or GenBank accessions into human-readable gene names.
Motivates automated identifier hygiene checks over impractical manual proofreading and curation in high-throughput genomics.

Open questions

How much undetected historical contamination remains in public and private databases, given errors propagate silently and irreversibly?
The authors call for date and floating-point conversions to be made non-default options; even so, a long lag before fixes and expunged errors reach all researchers.

wiki/sources/zemke-2023-conserved-and-di.title: zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory
wiki/sources/zemke-2023-conserved-and-di.content:
Conserved and divergent gene regulatory programs of the mammalian neocortex
One-line: Single-cell multiomics across four mammals’ primary motor cortex maps how divergence of cis-regulatory elements, driven largely by transposable elements, underlies neocortex evolution.
Key takeaways

Comparative single-cell multiomics (transcriptome + chromatin-accessibility + DNA methylome + 3D genome) of the primary motor cortex (M1) in human, macaque, marmoset and mouse, >200,000 cells across 21 shared cell types, to trace evolution of gene regulatory programs.
Divergence of transcription-factor expression tracks species-specific epigenome landscapes; conserved vs divergent cis-regulatory-elements are mirrored in evolution of the three-dimensional genome (topologically-associating-domains).
Transposable elements contribute ~80% of human-specific candidate cis-regulatory elements (cCREs), a recurring driver of species- and cell-type-specific regulation relevant to human-brain-evolution.
Sequence-based machine-learning predictors of cCREs show the genomic regulatory syntax is highly preserved from rodents to primates; epigenetic conservation plus sequence similarity improves interpretation of disease-associated variants.
Cell-type-resolved analysis is essential: neuronal-cell-types fractions are highly species-specific (oligodendrocyte expansion and excitatory-neuron reduction from mouse to human).

Notable claims / data

Profiled via 10x multiome: 40,937 human / 34,773 macaque / 34,310 marmoset / 47,404 mouse nuclei; via snm3C-seq: 8,198 / 5,737 / 4,999 / 5,349 nuclei.
Of 13,822 expressed one-to-one orthologues: 2,689 (~20%) mammal-conserved, 2,638 (~20%) primate-conserved, 3,511 (~25%) species-biased (human 1,376; mouse 1,367; marmoset 638; macaque 451).
384,412 human cCREs; 7,532 (~2%) human-specific, 204,921 (~53%) mammal level-0 sequence-conserved, of which 16,068 (7.8%) reach level-3 epi-conservation.
75% of human-specific cCREs and 16% of human-biased cCREs lie within TEs, versus <1% of mammal level-3 cCREs; LINE-1/LINE-2 most common; glia enriched for ERV1/ERVK LTRs.
C2H2 zinc-finger motifs (KLFs, ZBTB33, ZNF610, CTCF) mark the most epi-conserved distal cCREs; bHLH/bZIP/homeodomain (OLIG1/2, NEUROG1, POU2F1/3F1) mark the most epi-divergent.
1.36M human DMRs (methylpy); mCG conservation strongly correlates with chromatin-accessibility conservation (Spearman r=0.903). TE expression divergence vs epigenome divergence Pearson r=0.855.
3D genome: 12,641 mammal level-0 TAD boundaries; 67% of human-specific boundaries contain TEs vs 32% of mammal level-2; CTCF dosage linked to boundary insulation.

Connections

Complements human-brain-evolution and neurodevelopment work in the brain-development topic; provides the regulatory-element counterpart to expression-focused primate cortex studies.
The dna-methylation, chromatin-accessibility and 3D-genome (topologically-associating-domains, chromatin-loops) modalities connect to genome-architecture and cis-regulatory-grammar.
Sequence-based cCRE predictors and variant interpretation relate to noncoding-variants and massively-parallel-reporter-assay-style functional dissection.
Tracks are publicly browsable via the washu-comparative-epigenome-browser data hub.

Open questions

Do the low-epi-conservation homeodomain motifs in adult cortex show high epi-conservation during embryonic development?
What are the alternative (non-CTCF-motif) mechanisms producing human-biased TAD boundary insulation?
How well do the sequence-based cCRE predictors transfer to disease variant prioritization in cell types beyond M1?

wiki/sources/zeng-2017-neuronal-cell-typ.title: zeng-2017-neuronal-cell-type-classification-challenges
wiki/sources/zeng-2017-neuronal-cell-typ.content:
Neuronal cell-type classification: challenges, opportunities and the path forward
One-line: A conceptual and methodological review arguing that grouping neurons into types is the essential prerequisite for understanding brain circuits, importing taxonomic principles from species systematics and using the retina and cortex as case studies to chart a five-stage path toward a systematic, hierarchical cell-type census.
Key takeaways

Frames neuronal classification under brain-development-neurogenomics as facing two historical obstacles: a technical one (past methods were underpowered, biased and only semi-quantitative) now eased by high-throughput tools, and a conceptual one (how fine/firm type distinctions should be) that persists.
A “type” is defined pragmatically as a population of neurons whose properties are homogeneous within the population but differ from other neurons; the three main classification criteria are morphological, physiological and molecular, with connectivity a fourth, harder-to-assess criterion.
When criteria disagree, the authors give molecular criteria interim precedence — partly because markers are currently most useful and partly because some molecular features are enduring lifelong identity signatures, whereas physiology varies with temperature, mood, sensory input, etc.
Borrows from species taxonomy (specifically phenetic/typological systematics) five guiding principles: use multiple criteria (not one “essential” feature), make criteria rule-based and quantitative, build hierarchical (not flat) systems, treat groupings as hypotheses to be tested, and classify on discontinuous (not continuous) variation.
Proposes a unified hierarchy — class > subclass(es) > type > subtype — with groups defined within anatomical brain regions; types are the smallest discrete functional units and subtypes are often provisional (may later split into types or merge).
Lays out a five-stage, ~decade-long path forward, ending with a community-adopted unified nomenclature that avoids naming types after putative functions.

Notable claims / data

Current estimate of retinal neuronal types: 100–150; the retina has five neuronal classes across three layers and is simplified for classification by its laminar structure, hard-wired activity-independent development, complete circuit, and “mosaic” spacing (same-type neurons spaced more regularly than chance), which gives a type-grouping criterion independent of structure/physiology/molecules.
Retinal bipolar cells (BCs) are a model success: three high-throughput modalities — EM reconstruction (crowd-sourced), calcium imaging, and Drop-seq scRNA-seq (~20,000 cells) — converged on 14–15 types (1 rod BC + 14 cone BCs, split into 8 ON and 6 OFF). Convergence was near 1:1; nearly every cell assigned to a single type with scant “intermediate” forms; transcriptomic relationships paralleled morphology; classification revealed new types (a prior authoritative study found 11; 3 were missed for lack of markers and 1 — type 1B — was mistaken for an amacrine cell because it is actually unipolar).
Retinal ganglion cell (RGC) type estimates grew from ~12 (2004) to ~30 (2015) to >50 (newer physiology + ongoing EM); ~20 amacrine types characterized with evidence for many more (45 reported in one EM study).
Cortex is harder: ~180 human cortical areas (cytoarchitecture + inferred connectivity); two broad classes (glutamatergic excitatory, GABAergic inhibitory). Excitatory neurons fall into ~5–7 subclasses (L4, intratelencephalic/IT, pyramidal-tract/PT, cortico-thalamic/CT, L6b subplate); inhibitory into 4 marker-defined subclasses (PVALB+, SST+, VIP+, HTR3A+VIP-).
Smart-seq scRNA-seq of 1,679 adult mouse visual cortex cells (Tasic et al. 2016) yielded 49 transcriptomic types: 23 inhibitory, 19 excitatory, 7 non-neuronal — correlating with 4 inhibitory and 7 excitatory previously-known subclasses. Comprehensive cortical estimates put total cell types in the 100–200 range; unlike retina, cortex shows no mosaic/tiling, shows intermediate transcriptomic cells, and shows orthogonality between electrophysiologically- and morphologically-defined types.
scRNA-seq method survey (Table 1): plate-based methods (Smart-seq, STRT, CEL-seq, Fluidigm C1) give millions of reads/cell and (Smart-seq/MATQ-seq) preserve full-length transcripts for isoform detection; droplet methods (Drop-seq, inDrop, GemCode) give ~10K–50K reads/cell at huge cost savings; most methods use UMIs for in-silico PCR-bias correction. Single-nucleus RNA-seq enables profiling of frozen/autopsy and heavily myelinated tissue.
Multiplexed spatial methods noted: seqFISH, MERFISH (>140 genes low-error, >1,000 genes moderate-error), ExFISH, and in situ sequencing (FISSEQ, padlock/rolling-circle) — adding precise anatomical location/density that scRNA-seq of dissected tissue cannot.
Predicts a complete retinal classification of ~100–150 cell types is “within sight.”

Connections

The flagship topic source for the new brain-development-neurogenomics hub on neuronal cell types and the conceptual basis of cell-type taxonomy.
Methodologically anchors single-cell/spatial approaches central to the wiki’s rna-seq and single-cell threads; the discussion of Smart-seq/MATQ-seq preserving full-length transcripts to “identify alternative isoforms” links directly to alternative-splicing and long-read-rna-seq readouts of cell-type-resolved isoforms.
Complements the developing-human-brain QTL/transcriptome siblings (walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis, parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna) by supplying the cell-type framework those bulk/region studies aim to resolve at single-cell level.
Directly relevant to cell-type-resolved single-cell efforts catalogued elsewhere in the wiki (wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of, humphrey-2025-long-read-rna-sequencing-atlas, zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory): this review predates and motivates the atlas-scale taxonomies those works extend.
The cis-regulatory transgenic-line driver theme (Cre/GAL4 lines built from cis-regulatory elements to label types) connects to cis-regulatory-elements and cis-regulatory-grammar.

Open questions

Do molecular, morphological, physiological and connectional criteria genuinely co-vary into the same types, and how should discrepancies (e.g., cortical orthogonality between electrophysiology and morphology) be resolved beyond the proposed molecular-precedence rule?
How many true types constitute each cortical excitatory and inhibitory subclass, and how do transcriptomic types map onto projection-target-defined types?
Can a unified, region- and species-spanning nomenclature be agreed and widely adopted, and which neuronal types are evolutionarily conserved versus species-unique?
How will the staged plan extend classification from healthy adult animals into development, evolution, and disease (which vulnerable types are affected in specific disorders)?

wiki/sources/zhang-2019-deep-learning-au.title: zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis
wiki/sources/zhang-2019-deep-learning-au.content:
Deep-learning augmented RNA-seq analysis of transcript splicing
One-line: Introduces DARTS, a computational framework that couples a deep neural network trained on large-scale public RNA-seq data with a Bayesian hypothesis test, so that differential alternative splicing can be inferred reliably even from low-coverage RNA-seq.
Key takeaways

A central limitation of RNA-seq splicing analysis is its reliance on high sequencing depth; genes with low expression cannot be reliably quantified. DARTS addresses this by injecting deep-learning priors into the inference.
DARTS has two core parts: the DARTS DNN, which predicts differential splicing between two conditions from exon-specific cis sequence features plus trans RBP mRNA levels; and the DARTS BHT, a Bayesian hypothesis-testing model that combines that prediction (as a prior) with observed RNA-seq read counts (as the likelihood).
Two BHT modes: BHT(flat) uses an uninformative prior (RNA-seq data only, equivalent to a likelihood-ratio test) and is used during training to generate high-confidence labels; BHT(info) uses the DNN prediction as an informative prior for augmented analysis.
The DNN’s distinguishing design is that it models trans effects — it ingests RBP expression in both conditions, letting it reason about how altered RBP levels reshape splicing, unlike prior cis-only sample-specific exon-inclusion predictors.
Trained on ENCODE shRNA RBP-knockdown data and Roadmap Epigenomics tissue/cell-type data; combined training generalizes best across both leave-out sets.
DARTS uses rmats for splicing event detection and read counting, and operates on exon-inclusion levels (percent-spliced-in, psi). It builds a learned model of splicing regulation, contributing to the broader effort of a predictive splicing-code.

Notable claims / data

DARTS DNN (exon skipping) reached AUROC 0.97 in cross-validation and 0.86 in held-out testing; average AUROC 0.87 on 58 leave-out RBPs never seen in training.
DNN trained on large-scale ENCODE data beat baseline methods (per-dataset DNN, L2 logistic regression, random forest, and absolute predicted |psi_KD − psi_CTRL|) in 57 of 58 leave-out experiments; the per-dataset DNN was the worst, underscoring the value of large diverse training data.
Training set: 196 RBPs knocked down in both K562 and HepG2 (408 KD-vs-control comparisons); 58 RBPs in only one cell line held out. Feature set: 2,926 cis features (exon skipping) and 1,498 annotated RBPs (2,996 = 1,498×2 trans expression features per comparison).
BHT(info) beat BHT(flat) in all HepG2-vs-K562 pairwise comparisons; the gain was negatively correlated with RNA-seq depth (Spearman rho = −0.69, P < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶), i.e. biggest gains on low-coverage samples.
DNN extended beyond exon skipping to alternative 5’ splice sites, alternative 3’ splice sites, and retained introns (feature vectors of 2,973 / 2,971 / 1,748), with high leave-out accuracy.
EMT application: best AUROC 0.82 (day 6 vs day 0, combined ENCODE+Roadmap model) on an inducible H358 lung-cancer EMT time course. DARTS DNN “rescued” events were enriched for the ESRP1/2 splicing-factor consensus motif downstream of alternative exons. DNN scores between H358 and PC3E/GS689 EMT systems correlated at Spearman rho = 0.87 (P < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶), suggesting a captured core EMT splicing signature.
RASL-seq validation: DARTS DNN recovered 52 additional differential splicing events beyond the 77 called by RNA-seq alone; rescued events sat in genes with significantly lower expression (P = 0.001) and lower RNA-seq coverage (P = 2.1×10⁻⁷). BHT(info) outperformed BHT(flat), DNN alone, rMATS, and SUPPA2 on inconclusive events.
Architecture detail: skipped-exon DNN has 4 hidden layers (1,200/500/300/200 units, ReLU), Softmax output, ~7.92M parameters, dropout (0.6/0.5/0.3/0.1), batch-norm, RMSprop; Keras with Theano backend. Spike-and-slab prior with tau0 = 0.03, tau1 = 0.3; significance threshold p(|Δpsi| > 0.05) > 0.9.
Caveat: methods/labels are bootstrapped from BHT(flat) itself, so “ground truth” labels inherit that model’s assumptions; validation against independent RASL-seq partly mitigates this. ENCODE training data are modest depth (~32M read pairs/replicate, replicates pooled).

Connections

Uses and benchmarks against rmats (Shen et al. 2014, the shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection method) for event detection and as a comparison baseline, alongside MISO and SUPPA2.
Operates on percent-spliced-in (psi) quantification, the standard metric for exon inclusion used across the splicing-methods literature.
Conceptually a data-driven splicing-code model: it learns the cis + trans determinants of differential splicing, complementing sequence-only predictors such as spliceai but uniquely conditioning on RBP expression state.
Fits the methods/tools cluster of the splicing topic as a reusable software resource (darts).

Open questions

How well does the trans-RBP-expression prior transfer to primary human tissues and disease cohorts versus the cell-line / knockdown training regime?
Training labels are derived from BHT(flat) — how much do systematic errors in that labeler propagate into the learned prior?
Could modern sequence/foundation models replace the hand-compiled 2,926 cis features and improve generalization to unseen splicing contexts?
How does performance scale to long-read RNA-seq, where coverage limitations differ?

wiki/sources/zhang-2020-regional-variati.title: zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls
wiki/sources/zhang-2020-regional-variati.content:
Regional Variation of Splicing QTLs in Human Brain
One-line: A population-scale sQTL analysis across 13 human brain regions (GTEx, 1,209 samples) showing that splicing QTLs are either “regionally ubiquitous” or “regionally specific,” and that regional variation can be exploited to fine-map likely causal variants and their trans-regulators.
Key takeaways

sQTLs were mapped separately in each of 13 GTEx brain regions using exon inclusion levels (percent spliced in, PSI; see percent-spliced-in) quantified by rmats (rMATS-turbo) and WGS genotypes; between 387 and 849 significant sQTL events (p < 10⁻⁵) per region, with 133 significant across all 13 regions.
Two classes emerge: “regionally ubiquitous” sQTLs (significant in ≥10 regions; 148 total) are enriched for SNPs at the 5′/3′ essential splice sites, whereas “regionally specific” sQTLs (758 total; 86% significant in only 1–2 regions) are enriched for SNPs in exonic and proximal/distal intronic regions — pointing to distinct molecular mechanisms.
SNP position tracks significance and breadth: SNPs nearer the alternative exon (5′SS > 3′SS > exonic > proximal intronic > distal intronic) are more significant; sQTLs with SNPs at the splice-site dinucleotide are mostly pan-regional (53% significant in all 13 regions, 93% in ≥7).
An integrative strategy combining RBP binding prediction (DeepBind), region-specific RBP expression, and sQTL signal fine-maps causal cis variants and their trans regulators — illustrated for MAPT, CXXC5, and POU6F1.
The flagship example: SNP rs17651213 (G>A) creates a downstream RBFOX2 binding site that increases inclusion of MAPT (tau) exon 3, specifically in cerebellar tissues where RBFOX2 is highly expressed; the variant is in high LD with 11 PD and 1 AD GWAS variants — a concrete instance linking a brain region-specific sQTL to neurodegenerative disease (see splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

Inputs: GTEx v7 RNA-seq, ultimately 1,209 samples with matched RNA-seq + WGS genotype across 13 brain regions; 6,317,213 SNPs after MAF ≥ 0.05 and sex-chromosome filtering.
Splicing events retained after filtering: 10,665 skipped exons (SE), 1,443 alternative 5′SS (A5SS), 2,434 alternative 3′SS (A3SS). Total association tests: ~111.7M (SE), ~14.7M (A5SS), ~25.1M (A3SS).
Significance: two-tier cutoff — permutation FDR < 10% (corresponding to p ≈ 10⁻⁶ per region) and uncorrected p < 10⁻⁵. sQTL SNP = closest SNP with most significant association within a 200-kb window per side.
Brain region (not age or sex) is the dominant driver of variation in expression and splicing (t-SNE + linear mixed model, FDR < 5%); validated against known KHDRBS3/KHDRBS1 (T-STAR/Sam68) regulation of neurexin exon 4.
Disease annotation via LD (r² > 0.8) to NHGRI-EBI GWAS Catalog variants across 14 neurological disorders; neurodegenerative-disease sQTLs tend to be region-specific, neuropsychiatric ones more shared. No significant enrichment of brain sQTLs in brain disorders vs non-brain phenotypes (BMI, height) — consistent with sQTL pleiotropy / sharing across tissues.
Colocalization (coloc): 278 brain sQTL-associated GWASs, 27 with harmonized summary stats; 124 tests on 43 sQTL-GWAS pairs, 77 supporting a single shared causal variant (posterior ≥ 75%) — e.g., PGAP3 exon 4 / rs1565922 with bipolar disorder.
Additional examples: RTEL1 exon 23 / rs6062302 (regionally ubiquitous, glioma GWAS); SLC26A10 exon 12 / rs1871417 (cerebellum-specific, LD with immune-disease GWAS); CXXC5 exon 2 / rs6580200 (disrupts HNRNPK site; see hnrnpa1 family context); POU6F1 exon 4 / rs4077093 (disrupts a NOVA1 YCAY motif).
Caveats stated by authors: one sQTL SNP per event ignores multiple independent causal SNPs; bulk tissue mixes cell types and may dilute power for cell-type-specific sQTLs (RBFOX2 is neuron-enriched and cerebellum is neuron-dense, so the MAPT signal may be neuronal).

Connections

Method backbone is rmats (rMATS-turbo) for PSI quantification; conceptually this is an application/population-genetics study built on splicing-quantification tooling rather than a new tool — contrast with method papers like shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection.
Extends the “splicing variation in human populations” line of work (Park/Xing 2018) into a brain-region-resolved sQTL atlas; complements disease-focused splicing reviews such as marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative and the editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Mechanistic story sits squarely in splicing-and-disease, splicing-factors, and rna-binding-proteins (RBFOX2, NOVA1, HNRNPK as trans regulators); the splice-site vs regulatory-element distinction connects to splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements.
Uses a deep-learning RBP-binding predictor (DeepBind) for cis-variant prioritization, relevant to ai-in-genomics and splicing-code efforts to predict splicing impact of variants.

Open questions

Would purified-cell-type or single-cell population RNA-seq sharpen (or reassign) the MAPT/RBFOX2 cerebellar signal to specific neurons?
How many sQTL events are driven by multiple independent causal SNPs that the single-SNP-per-event design misses?
Why is a cerebellum-specific sQTL (SLC26A10) in strong LD with immune-disease GWAS variants — is the same sQTL active in immune cell types?
Can the integrative RBP-motif + region-specific-expression + sQTL strategy generalize beyond brain to fine-map tissue/cell-type-specific causal variants in other systems?

wiki/sources/zhang-2023-a-new-method-mul.title: zhang-2023-a-new-method-multiancestry
wiki/sources/zhang-2023-a-new-method-mul.content:
A new method for multiancestry polygenic prediction improves performance across diverse populations
One-line: Introduces ct-sleb, a fast model-free method for building multiancestry polygenic risk scores that markedly improves prediction in non-European (especially African-ancestry) populations.
Key takeaways

ct-sleb combines a 2D extension of clumping-and-thresholding, an empirical Bayes step for cross-population effect-size estimation, and a superlearning model to build ancestry-specific PRSs from multiancestry genome-wide-association-study summary statistics.
Borrows information from large EUR GWASs to boost PRS performance in under-powered non-EUR target populations, addressing a major equity gap in polygenic prediction.
Benchmarked against nine alternatives, including the Bayesian PRS-CSx; no single method is uniformly best, motivating running multiple methods per ancestry.
Simulation studies quantify how sample size, SNP density, and heritability architecture govern multiancestry risk-prediction accuracy, connecting to quantitative-genetics of complex traits.

Notable claims / data

Evaluated in ~5.1 million individuals of diverse ancestry (1.18M from four non-EUR populations) across 13 complex traits using 23andMe, GLGC, All of Us, and UK Biobank data; simulations used ~19 million common variants across five ancestry groups.
CT-SLEB is ~25x faster than PRS-CSx in two-ancestry analyses (4.35 vs 109.11 min, chr22 AFR) and ~91x faster in five-ancestry settings (4.62 vs 420.96 min).
In GLGC AFR data, CT-SLEB improved adjusted R2 for log(triglycerides) and LDL-cholesterol by 140% and 66.4% respectively vs PRS-CSx; conversely PRS-CSx was superior in EAS (e.g. LDL R2 ratio 52.9%).
Averaged over 13 traits, CT-SLEB/PRS-CSx performance ratio was 144% (AFR), 88.5% (EAS), 103% (Latino), 92.1% (SAS) using EUR+target data.
A substantial performance gap persists in non-EUR populations even with best methods: height PRS relative R2 ~0.48 (AFR), 0.76 (EAS), 0.86 (Latino), 0.80 (SAS) vs EUR; AFR sample-size requirements depend heavily on per-SNP heritability.
Denser SNP panels (HM3 -> HM3+MEGA -> ~19M 1KG) substantially improved PRS performance, most when target sample size was large and causal SNPs fewer.

Connections

Builds multiancestry PRSs on top of genome-wide-association-study summary statistics, the core biometrical-genetics data source.
Relies on heritability estimates (LD-score regression) to bound maximum achievable prediction accuracy, linking PRS gains to quantitative-genetics of complex traits.
Method entity page: ct-sleb.

Open questions

How large must AFR-ancestry GWASs grow to close the prediction gap given trait-dependent per-SNP heritability?
Can model-free (CT-SLEB) and Bayesian (PRS-CSx) approaches be principledly ensembled rather than run separately per ancestry?
How well does the approach extend beyond the five broad ancestry groups to admixed and finer-scale populations?

wiki/sources/zhang-2025-polygenic-and-de.title: zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of
wiki/sources/zhang-2025-polygenic-and-de.content:
Polygenic and developmental profiles of autism differ by age at diagnosis
One-line: Using four birth cohorts plus two large genotyped autism cohorts (iPSYCH, SPARK), this study shows that earlier- versus later-diagnosed autism follow distinct developmental trajectories and are driven by two only-modestly-correlated autism polygenic factors — arguing that “autism” diagnosed at different ages reflects partly different aetiologies (the “developmental model”) rather than a single polygenic trait differing only in severity (the “unitary model”).
Key takeaways

Two latent socioemotional/behavioural trajectories underlie autism diagnosis timing: an “early childhood emergent” trajectory (difficulties early, stable/attenuating) linked to earlier diagnosis, and a “late childhood emergent” trajectory (few early difficulties, rising in adolescence) linked to later diagnosis — replicated across three birth cohorts via growth mixture models of the Strengths and Difficulties Questionnaire (SDQ).
Age at autism diagnosis is itself heritable: common SNPs explain ~11% of variance across iPSYCH and SPARK, comparable to or larger than any single clinical/sociodemographic factor (which together explain <15%). This is part of human autism / psychiatric-genetics and links to the brain-development-neurogenomics hub.
The SNP-heritability of diagnosis age does NOT attenuate after controlling for parental sociodemographics, clinical features, or co-occurring developmental delays — contradicting the unitary model and supporting the developmental model.
Autism’s polygenic architecture decomposes (via GenomicSEM over 13 autism GWASs) into two correlated factors: Factor 1 “earlier-diagnosed autism” and Factor 2 “later-diagnosed autism”, genetically correlated only modestly (rg = 0.38, s.e. = 0.06–0.07).
The two factors differ sharply in psychiatric comorbidity genetics: the earlier-diagnosed factor has low (rg ~0.1–0.2) correlations with ADHD and mental-health traits, whereas the later-diagnosed factor has moderate-to-high (rg ~0.5–0.7) correlations with ADHD, depression, PTSD, childhood maltreatment and self-harm.

Notable claims / data

SNP-based h2 of age at autism diagnosis ~11% in three samples: iPSYCH (LDSC, n = 18,965), SPARK Discovery and Replication (GCTA-GREML), and their meta-analysis (n = 25,344). Robust to covariate adjustment (developmental milestones, regression, IQ, SCQ/RBS-R, SES, deprivation).
No association of diagnosis age with rare de novo or inherited PTV/missense variants in constrained genes (n = 6,206 SPARK trios) — possibly low power or diagnostic overshadowing by co-occurring intellectual disability/global developmental delay.
Genetic correlation between age at diagnosis and 13 autism GWASs becomes systematically more positive as the GWAS’s median diagnosis age rises — the opposite of the unitary-model prediction (which expected negative correlations decaying toward zero).
iPSYCH vs SPARK age-at-diagnosis GWASs are only moderately correlated (rg = 0.51, s.e. = 0.19, P = 7.56 × 10⁻³), attributed to different recruitment and median diagnosis ages (iPSYCH median 10 yr; SPARK median 4 yr).
Genetic correlations among the 13 autism GWASs span rg = 0.02 (s.e. = 0.13) to 1.00; correlation tracks similarity in median diagnosis age. Hierarchical clustering yields a childhood-diagnosed cluster and an adolescent/later-diagnosed cluster.
Correlated two-factor GenomicSEM model fit best (AIC 38.64; CFI 0.99; SRMR 0.08). Factor 1 loaded by PGC-2017 and SPARK-before6 (median age ~3); Factor 2 loaded by iPSYCH-after10, FinnGen, SPARK-after10; iPSYCH-before9 cross-loaded. Sensitivity analyses with different GWAS subsets reproduced two correlated factors (rg = 0.37–0.52).
Decomposition via GenomicSEM showed later-diagnosed autism is NOT simply the additive sum of earlier-diagnosed autism plus six mental-health conditions (schizophrenia, ADHD, anorexia, depression, bipolar, PTSD).
Caveats stated by authors: h2 only ~11% (many unmeasured factors affect diagnosis timing); trajectory models rely solely on the SDQ, which does not capture core autistic features; “earlier”/“later” are relative (a gradient, no consensus age threshold); birth-cohort diagnoses are carer/self-reported, not standardized clinical assessments; genetic analyses limited to European-inferred ancestry.

Connections

Strong thematic complement to courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development (early brain overgrowth / early-childhood-emergent autism phenotype) under brain-development-neurogenomics: both concern developmental heterogeneity in autism, here framed genetically by diagnosis timing.
Sits alongside other developing-brain transcriptomic/genetics siblings in the wiki (e.g. walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and, parikshak-2016-genome-wide-changes-in-lncrna) that map autism-relevant genetics onto brain development, but this paper works at the polygenic/GWAS level rather than tissue expression.
Reframes prior discordant autism-vs-ADHD genetic-correlation findings (limited rg in PGC-2017 vs high rg in later GWASs) as an artefact of differing average diagnosis age across cohorts.

Open questions

Do these two polygenic factors and their developmental trajectories replicate in non-European ancestries and in cohorts with standardized clinical (rather than community/self-reported) diagnoses?
A large fraction of the FinnGen autism signal is unexplained by either factor — are there additional (≥3) developmental polygenic dimensions of autism?
What unmeasured mediators carry the genetic effect on diagnosis age, given measured developmental/sociodemographic factors do not mediate it?
Can sibling/longitudinal designs that track autistic-feature emergence over time causally separate aetiological developmental pathways from diagnostic/environmental delay?

wiki/sources/zhang-2026-uncovering-spati.title: zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics
wiki/sources/zhang-2026-uncovering-spati.content:
Uncovering spatially resolved functional genomics with CRISPR screen sequencing
One-line: Introduces spac-seq, a sequencing-based spatial CRISPR screen platform that captures pooled sgRNA libraries alongside single-cell-resolution whole transcriptomes in situ, plus tardis-tool, a statistical toolkit for spatial perturbation analysis — together enabling transcriptome-wide functional genomics with spatial phenotypes in vivo.
Key takeaways

spac-seq (spatial CRISPR screen sequencing) couples a pooled sgRNA library (delivered via a retroviral SPACseq plasmid) with single-cell-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms (10x Visium HD, Stereo-seq), capturing both sgRNA and whole transcriptome in situ via NGS — addressing the gap that prior spatial CRISPR screens were imaging-based and not transcriptome-wide.
The plasmid embeds the sgRNA within the GFP mRNA (transcribed by RNA Pol II) so it is captured like any transcript, plus a U6/Pol III cassette; this avoids a separate barcode library and its recombination risk. It also supports direct probe capture of the sgRNA spacer+scaffold (Visium HD).
tardis-tool (target prioritization toolkit for perturbation data in spatial omics) handles sgRNA-transcriptome spatial alignment, library preprocessing, statistical modeling of global effects and local perturbation enrichments, and spatial pathway analysis.
Applied in mouse syngeneic tumor models to three biology questions: (1) Icam1 loss in tumor cells promotes lung-metastasis colonization and immune exclusion; (2) Cd44 in CD8+ T cells regulates spatial localization via Spp1 on macrophages; (3) a transcription factor-chemokine receptor axis couples cell state to chemotaxis.
A pooled perturbation/spatial-omics method conceptually adjacent to single-cell-perturbation-modeling but readout-based and in vivo rather than predictive; relevant to ai-in-genomics for the statistical/spatial-niche modeling in TARDIS.

Notable claims / data

sgRNA detection accuracy: mRNA-embedded vs genomic DNA sequencing near-perfect in a CD8+ T cell mini-pool (Pearson ρ = 0.9970), beating CROP-seq (ρ = 0.8913); held at scale with a 9,040-sgRNA library (ρ = 0.9414) and direct capture (ρ = 0.9596).
SPACseq plasmid showed 9.08-fold higher mRNA-embedded sgRNA expression than CROP-seq, and higher transduction efficiency in MC38 and primary CD8+ T cells; Cd8a KO efficiency 75.19% (vs CROP-seq 85.2%, attributed to CROP-seq’s dual-sgRNA cassette).
In vivo 1,520-sgRNA MC38 subQ screen: recovered 1,393 (91.64%) sgRNAs by direct capture and 1,161 (76.38%) by mRNA embedding per section; 1,068/1,520 (70.26%) shared across both methods and mice. Top depleted hits enriched for MHC presentation (H2-T23, B2m, H2-K1, Ifngr2, H2-Q7); validated B4galt1.
Single-cell resolution: StarDist segmentation assigned 95.54% of sgRNA counts to single cells; 44.58% of cells had a single unambiguous sgRNA (1.51 sgRNA UMIs/cell). Serial-section reproducibility: pairwise Spearman > 0.92; 85.87% of perturbations spatially consistent (FDR < 0.05), median SSIM 0.9809 at perturbation level.
Metastasis screen: lung metastases harvested 14 days post-IV injection; TARDIS identified 138 and 48 of 221 clonal perturbations enriched in immune-infiltrated vs immune-excluded niches. sgIcam1 was both highly expanded and immune-excluded; sgIcam1 region was T cell-poor / macrophage-rich, with M2 polarization and elevated Spp1/Cd163 vs Cxcl9/Cxcl10. mIF validation: significantly larger tumor area (p<0.001) and reduced CD8+ T cell infiltration on Icam1 loss.
T cell screen: two-step design — bulk in vivo infiltration screen (2,135 genes / 9,040 sgRNAs, 99.62% recovered, MAGeCK; positive controls Zc3h12a/Regnase-1, Gata3, Klrd1, Ddit3, Nmb, Piezo1) then a spatiotemporal mini-pool (top 33 hits, tumors at days 4/7/10), identifying Cd44 as a regulator of T cell spatial localization interacting with macrophage Spp1.
Caveats: demonstrated in mouse syngeneic tumor models; sgRNA-transcript capture is subject to spatial-transcriptomics dropout (only ~0.8–1 UMI per 8–10 μm bin); spatial sgRNA vs tissue-NGS correlations are modest (ρ ≈ 0.28–0.47).

Connections

functional-single-cell-genomics — extends pooled CRISPR screens / Perturb-seq / CROP-seq from dissociated single cells to spatially resolved in vivo phenotypes; benchmarked directly against the CROP-seq sgRNA-embedding system.
single-cell-perturbation-modeling — a complementary, experimental (rather than predictive/generative) route to linking perturbations to cell-state phenotypes; sibling in spirit to in-silico methods like cmonge and txpert but measures spatial phenotypes directly.
ai-in-genomics — TARDIS’s statistical niche/program modeling and StarDist segmentation are the computational backbone enabling target prioritization from spatial readouts.

Open questions

Can SPAC-seq be ported from mouse syngeneic models to human tissue / patient-derived systems despite spatial-transcriptomics dropout?
How well do the modest spatial-vs-bulk sgRNA correlations (ρ ≈ 0.28–0.47) constrain quantitative effect-size estimates per niche?
Could predictive perturbation models (e.g. single-cell-perturbation-modeling) be trained on SPAC-seq spatial readouts to forecast niche-level effects?

wiki/sources/zheng-2024-massively-parall.title: zheng-2024-massively-parallel-in-vivo-perturb-seq
wiki/sources/zheng-2024-massively-parall.content:
Massively parallel in vivo Perturb-seq reveals cell-type-specific transcriptional networks in cortical development
One-line: An AAV-based, fast-onset in vivo Perturb-seq platform that labels >6% of brain cells and profiles >30,000 cells per experiment, revealing cell-type-specific transcription-factor effects during mouse corticogenesis.
Key takeaways

Extends perturb-seq from in vitro lentiviral screens to a scalable in vivo platform by pairing a fast-acting AAV vector (aav-sch9) with a hyperactive piggyBac (hypPB) transposon for sustained transgene/gRNA expression.
A barcoded screen of 86 phylogenetically diverse AAV serotypes identified AAV-SCH9, which reaches robust expression in developing brain within 48 h (vs. weeks for conventional AAVs), enabling perturbation of dynamic neurodevelopment contexts.
Proof-of-principle in utero pooled-crispr-screen of neurodevelopmental-disorder TFs (Foxg1, Nr2f1, Tbr1, Tcf4) with a single-cell-crispr-screen readout resolved cell-type-specific effects across cortical neuronal-cell-types.
Foxg1 loss-of-function acts pleiotropically but predominantly on Layer 6 corticothalamic (L6-CT) neurons, de-repressing other TF networks and driving a hybrid cell state — an effect absent in other cells of the same layer/lineage.
Relevant to autism (autism) and developmental-delay risk-gene biology: the platform aims to map the vulnerable cell types and molecular networks through which risk variants act.

Notable claims / data

AAV-SCH9 top hit: 2.3% of initial library rising to 27.4% of enriched population 48 h post-transduction in vivo (11.5-fold increase); by contrast AAV2/AAV9/PHP.B/PHP.eB/DJ showed minimal 48 h expression.
Labeling efficiency: >6% of cerebral cells (vs. <0.1% state-of-the-art lentivirus); AAV9-PHP.eB + hypPB labeled 6.5–7.0% of cortical nuclei (4.0-fold higher than without hypPB); embryonic cortex hypPB gave 2.2-fold more GFP+ cells; 4–15-fold more GFP+ cells than lentivirus across regions.
Scale: >30,000 cells analyzable in one experiment; screen yielded 50,075 cells post-QC across 5 replicates, 16,067 high-confidence single-gRNA cells (avg 849 gRNA UMIs, 14,647 endogenous UMIs/cell), narrowed to 11,471 high-quality cells across 14 cell types; 1,609–2,527 perturbed cells/gene, 3,316 controls.
Editing: Foxg1-gRNA1 produced distinct indels in 61% of cells; minimal indels in controls. Foxg1 KO increased L6-CT subcluster-3 (FDR<0.028, 1.8–24.4-fold) and gave 9.9-fold reduction of L6-IT with 2.0-fold increase of upper-layer neurons.
WGS: 46 hypPB integration events, mostly intergenic (41%) at expected TTAA sites — largely random, mitigated by sampling many cells/perturbation. Caveat: gliosis markers (IBA1, GFAP) elevated with systemic AAV9-PHP.eB-hypPB, flagging toxicity.

Connections

Directly builds on perturb-seq (dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits) and the direct-capture / GBC lineage in adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening.
Situated in CRISPR functional-genomics reviews kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for and wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells; uses cas9-based genome-editing.
In vivo neuronal editing context parallels park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing; cortical cell-type framing connects to velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal and kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell.

Open questions

Can more gRNAs per vector and coupling with CRISPRi/CRISPRa/CRISPRoff or cDNA overexpression improve editing efficiency and expand modularity?
How to correct for viral cell-type tropism bias that limits power in less-transduced cell types?
Can the platform integrate multimodal (single-nucleus epigenetic, spatial in situ) readouts to capture non-transcriptomic and morphological phenotypes and scale to hundreds of risk genes?

wiki/sources/zhou-2018-prioritization-an.title: zhou-2018-prioritization-and-functional-assessment
wiki/sources/zhou-2018-prioritization-an.content:
Prioritization and functional assessment of noncoding variants associated with complex diseases
One-line: Introduces pafa, a supervised classifier that prioritizes noncoding disease risk variants by adding population-differentiation features (FST) and cleaner training labels, outperforming seven existing tools.
Key takeaways

PAFA is a sparse L1-regularized logistic-regression model (LIBLINEAR) built to be noncoding-sensitive, combining conservation metrics, ENCODE genomic annotations, and population-level measures for noncoding-variants prioritization.
Its central novelty is introducing the fixation index FST and a dispersion score as features; population-level metrics alone reached AUC 0.840 in cross-validation, better than any other single feature group.
It recalibrates training labels: rather than labeling all common 1000 Genomes variants as benign (as GWAVA/DIVAN do), it selects low-FST variants ranked as inversely associated with disease cSNPs, plus simulated rare benign variants, as controls.
Adds a gene-centric annotation layer and an online platform integrating 23 curated resources for variant functional assessment.

Notable claims / data

Training set: 41,847 functional and 41,847 control variants from ClinVar (30,277 pathogenic, 13,010 benign), GWASdb (11,570 element-overlapping cSNPs at p <= 1e-8), and 1000 Genomes; 7131 pre-selected features.
On a five-way benchmark, PAFA reached AUC 0.821 on pathogenic vs benign coding variants (comparable to CADD/Eigen), 0.796 for recurrent noncoding variants, and 0.701 for cSNPs — the best across tools; several competitors dropped below AUC 0.5 on noncoding tasks.
Against CADD, FATHMM-MKL, DANN, GWAVA, DIVAN, Eigen, and LINSIGHT on 36 GRASP disease sets, PAFA achieved the best noncoding performance (AUC 0.738–0.858, median 0.799).
On 560-breast-cancer driver variants vs adjacent 1000 Genomes controls, PAFA reached AUC 0.947; removing population-level metrics dropped it to 0.714, and removing pathogenic rare training variants collapsed it to 0.052.
Population analysis of 35 GWASdb cSNP sets showed most disease-associated variants have high genetic differentiation (FST > 0.25), with disease-specific population skews (e.g., testicular cancer SNPs enriched in Europeans).
Caveat: population-level metrics only help variants with >=1% frequency (needed for allele-frequency estimates); on rare simulated noncoding controls, population features alone gave AUC ~0.48, so genomic annotations carried performance there.

Connections

A mutation-effect-prediction / noncoding-variants method comparable to CADD-style scores; overlaps in spirit with the wiki’s cadd-score entity as a competing prioritization tool.
Population-differentiation framing connects to population-genetics and gene-regulation themes; relies on ENCODE functional-genomics annotations (histone marks, TFBS, open chromatin).
Demonstrated on autism spectrum disorder de novo mutations, linking to autism via pathway-enrichment of coding and noncoding affected genes.

Open questions

How well does FST-based prioritization transfer to rare variants and non-European or admixed populations, given the >=1% frequency requirement?
Does labeling low-FST 1000 Genomes variants as controls risk discarding genuinely functional common variants, and how sensitive are results to that heuristic?
Can the approach generalize beyond the GRCh37 annotation landscape as GRCh38 functional annotations mature?

wiki/sources/zhou-2023-genetics-of-human.title: zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain
wiki/sources/zhou-2023-genetics-of-human.content:
Genetics of human brain development
One-line: A Nature Reviews Genetics review arguing that human brain development is governed by precise spatiotemporal genetic control whose human-specific features are now testable directly in human cells — by integrating cross-species comparative genomics, neurodevelopmental/neuropsychiatric disease genetics, and human pluripotent stem cell (hPS cell) 2D cultures and 3D brain organoids with genome editing to establish genotype–phenotype causality.
Key takeaways

Frames the genetics of human brain development through two complementary lenses — evolutionary specialization (human-enriched features) and brain disorders — and positions hPS cell-derived 2D cultures and 3D brain organoids, combined with genome editing, as the platform that converts the correlative evidence from GWAS/omics into causative genotype–phenotype links in the human system. This is the developmental-biology backbone of the brain-development-neurogenomics hub.
Lays out the conserved, stepwise cellular program of cortical development: neurulation → neuroepithelial cells (NECs) → radial glial cells (RGCs) producing excitatory/inhibitory neurons then glia, via intermediate progenitor cells (IPCs) in the SVZ, inside-out radial migration of excitatory neurons and tangential migration of interneurons, neuritogenesis/synaptogenesis, gliogenesis, and postnatal synaptic pruning and myelination.
Human-specific features are concentrated in neurogenesis and neuronal development, located in both protein-coding genes and non-coding regulatory regions such as human-accelerated regions (HARs); the dominant human signature is neoteny (protracted timing) — e.g., human RGC-mediated neurogenesis spans >3 months vs ~1 week in mouse and 1–2 months in macaque.
Outer radial glial cells (oRGCs) of the outer SVZ — a hallmark of gyrencephalic/primate cortex, virtually absent in rodents — are a recurring target of human-specific genes that drive cortical expansion and folding (e.g., ARHGAP11B, TBC1D3, TKTL1, TMEM14B, CROCCP2).
Disease genetics supplies the other axis: monogenic malformations (microcephaly, lissencephaly, macrocephaly, heterotopia) and neurodevelopmental/neuropsychiatric disorders (ASD, schizophrenia, epilepsy, intellectual disability, Rett, fragile X) map causal variants onto specific developmental steps, and many human-relevant phenotypes (e.g., ASPM, CDK5RAP2, DCX, FLNA, IER3IP1) are robust in hPS cell models but mild or absent in mouse — a central argument for human cell-based modeling.

Notable claims / data

Neoteny / timing: human RGC neurogenesis >3 months vs ~1 week (mouse) and 1–2 months (macaque); the primate subplate is ~5× thicker than other mammals’. These extended windows are argued to enable greater size/complexity/plasticity but also increased vulnerability.
Evolutionary neurogenesis genes (Table 1, modeled in animal and/or hPS cell systems): ZEB2 (delays NEC→RGC transition, faster cell cycle, greater cortical expansion vs apes; haploinsufficiency → Mowat–Wilson syndrome); HAR-regulated FZD8 (WNT receptor) and PPP1R17 (cell-cycle regulator) lengthen RGC cycle; NOTCH2NL (human-specific duplication enhancing Notch — deletion/duplication → microcephaly/macrocephaly at 1q21.1); ARHGAP11B, TBC1D3, TKTL1, TMEM14B, CROCCP2 expand oRGCs; ZNF558 (human-specific mitophagy regulator of SPATA18); DUF1220/NBPF dosage; PDGFD–PDGFRB signaling absent in mice.
Evolutionary neuron/circuit genes: FOXP2 (speech/vocalization, dendrite length, synaptic plasticity); SRGAP2C (human-specific duplication → neuron neoteny, increased spine density/connectivity); OSTN/osteocrin (re-purposed to brain, MEF2-dependent activity-induced dendritic growth in primates); CBLN2 (SOX5-regulated), PLXNA1 (FEZF2-regulated), EPHA7 (human-specific chromatin loop, elevated in subplate neurons); GATA3 upregulated in human maturing neurons to contribute to neoteny.
Cautions on evolutionary inference: TKTL1 and FOXP2 “human-specific” variant claims are contested — variants thought absent from modern humans appear when more ancestrally diverse genomes are examined; most studies are European-ancestry-biased; ectopic human-protein expression in other species can yield nonspecific artefacts; single physical-trait variants may not explain complex, likely polygenic human behaviors. Calls for resources like the more-inclusive human pangenome reference.
Disease → developmental step (Table 1, Fig. 5): microcephaly via centrosome/mitosis genes (ASPM, CDK5RAP2, CENPJ, WDR62), DNA-damage (MCPH1), ER secretion (IER3IP1); macrocephaly via PTEN/mTOR; tuberous sclerosis via TSC2; lissencephaly via LIS1, YWHAE, TUBA1A, DCX (X-linked, sex-biased severity); periventricular heterotopia via DCHS1/FAT4, FLNA; ASD risk epigenetic regulators ARID1B/CHD8/SUV420H1 (excess interneurons, premature deep-layer differentiation); “synaptopathies” — CACNA1C (Timothy), DGCR8 (DiGeorge/22q11.2), FMR1 (fragile X), MECP2 (Rett), SHANK3 (Phelan–McDermid), UBE3A (Angelman), DISC1/FGFR1 (schizophrenia); PLP1 (Pelizaeus–Merzbacher, myelination).
Environment: Zika virus-induced congenital microcephaly modeled in organoids/mice via TLR3, ANKLE2, adherens-junction and centrosome genes; severity depends on host genetic background; retinoic acid (RXRG/RARB/CYP26B1) gradients pattern prefrontal cortex and cortical–thalamic connectivity.
hPS cell limitations stated: iPS-line variability and need for line-specific protocols; organoids lack microglia/endothelial cells (mesodermal/haematopoietic lineages) and mature cytoarchitecture/circuitry; assembloids and multi-region patterning are emerging; high-throughput, reproducible organoid production is needed for genome-wide CRISPR/prime-editing and MPRA-scale (massively parallel reporter assay) variant screens; xenografting raises ethical considerations.
Genetic-architecture themes: genetic multiplicity (pleiotropy + polygenicity) revealed by single-cell multi-omics and GWAS for ASD/schizophrenia; cell-type-/stage-specific variant effects (e.g., SCN2A, CACNA1C, SHANK3, SYNGAP1 having distinct roles in immature vs mature neurons); CRISPR editing of schizophrenia risk variants (FURIN, SNAP91, TSNARE1, CLCN3) in isogenic iPS lines shows synergistic synaptic effects; DISC1 presented as a worked multidisciplinary pipeline (patient pedigree → isogenic iPS 2D/3D → druggable target → humanized-mouse rescue).

Connections

Anchors the brain-development-neurogenomics hub on the developmental-biology and evolutionary side, complementing the splicing/transcriptomic developing-brain sources.
Strong sibling to li-2018-integrative-functional-genomic-analysis (PsychENCODE/BrainSpan): both emphasize a developmentally regulated, spatiotemporal program and the convergence of neuropsychiatric GWAS risk onto specific developmental cell types/windows. Li 2018 supplies the multimodal molecular atlas and “late fetal transition” framing; Zhou reviews the functional/causal genetics and human-cell modeling that such atlases motivate. Both invoke shared risk genes (e.g., MEF2C, SOX5, TCF4) and the GWAS→developmental-context logic.
Complements walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and (prenatal cortical eQTL/sQTL → TWAS): Walker maps the correlative genetic-regulatory layer (including splicing) that carries prenatal neuropsychiatric risk, whereas Zhou argues such candidate variants must then be tested in hPS cell models to establish causality. Shared disease focus (schizophrenia, ASD) and shared emphasis on stage-specific (prenatal) regulation; Zhou’s FGFR1 and DISC1 schizophrenia threads connect to Walker’s SCZ TWAS gene nominations.
The review repeatedly invokes massively parallel reporter assays (links to massively-parallel-reporter-assay) and non-coding regulatory/HAR variation interacting with neurodevelopmental genes — touching cis-regulatory-elements and 3D-genome rewiring relevant to chromatin-loops / genome-architecture (EPHA7 human-specific chromatin loop; HAR–gene interactions via altered 3D folding).
Disease-gene catalog overlaps the neuro wing of splicing-and-disease / splicing-and-neurodevelopment at the gene level (e.g., FMR1, MECP2, CACNA1C, SHANK3), though Zhou’s mechanisms are largely transcriptional/cytoskeletal/signaling rather than splice-variant-based.

Open questions

How many more human-specific regulatory mechanisms remain undiscovered — the review stresses only a small number of genes have been functionally validated, and that we have “only scratched the surface”?
Can organoid/assembloid platforms be matured enough to model later developmental stages, distinct cortical layers, functional columns, gyrification, and long-range circuitry — the steps where reliable hPS cell models are still lacking?
Will MPRA- and genome-wide CRISPR/prime-editing screens on high-throughput, reproducible organoids let the field functionally test “every GWAS risk variant” to establish causal genotype→phenotype links at scale?
How should ancestral diversity and patient-specific genetic backgrounds (currently European-biased) be incorporated so that human-specific and disease-risk claims (and polygenic scores) generalize across populations?
Which contested human-specific variants (TKTL1, FOXP2) survive scrutiny as more ancestrally diverse genomes (e.g., pangenome) are sequenced?

wiki/sources/zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-.title: zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex
wiki/sources/zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-.content:
Digital twins of ex vivo human lungs enable accurate and personalized evaluation of therapeutic efficacy
One-line: Builds hybrid physics-informed + machine-learning digital-twins of individual ex vivo human lungs from the largest clinical ex vivo lung perfusion (EVLP) dataset, forecasting >75 multimodal parameters and serving as per-organ untreated controls to evaluate drug efficacy and safety.
Key takeaways

Uses EVLP (a closed circuit perfusing an isolated human lung at 37 °C on an ICU-grade ventilator) to generate multiscale data — physiology, biochemistry, radiography, transcriptomics, metabolomics, proteomics — then trains organ-level digital-twins (DTs) that simulate future lung function without altering circuit settings.
Hybrid architecture: physics-based lung-mechanics equations combined with data-driven models (XGBoost for hourly/imaging data, GRU for per-breath time series). One bespoke model per parameter — an instance of multimodal-learning.
Two complementary DT modes run simultaneously: a “static” twin (baseline-only, long-range forecast) and a “dynamic” twin (continuously recalibrated with real-time data, short-range forecast). Dynamic generally matches or beats static for physiology/biochemistry.
Headline application: the DT acts as a personalized, paired untreated counterfactual control for the same lung, enabling individual treatment-effect inference. Demonstrated on alteplase (tPA) for pulmonary embolism — the DT detected efficacy that a conventional control cohort missed.
A reusable resource (code, trained models, web app, demo data) rather than a single hypothesis test — the topic’s translational/biomedical multimodal “digital twin” entry, built on ex vivo organ perfusion, slightly outside pure genomics.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 1,000 consecutive Toronto General Hospital EVLP cases (2008–2024); 49 excluded → 951 isolated human lungs for DT training (donors disease-free, mean age 46 ± 16 y, 64% male). Independent test cohort of 45 clinical EVLP cases (2024–2025). No imputation — complete-record cases only.
Accurately models >75 parameters across 6+ modalities. ~200,000 individual breaths semi-supervised-labeled; ventilator data at 100 Hz.
Performance (dynamic DT MAPE): physiology 2–11%; biochemistry 0.46–7.2% (pH MAPE 0.46%); metabolites lactate 6.9% / glucose 4.9%; transcriptomic Hallmark pathways 0.68–3% MAPE and 79–100% accuracy for direction of gene-set enrichment change (hypoxia pathway 100%); proteins (IL-6/8/10/1β) median MAPE ~22–37%. DT absolute error consistently below the %CV of an empirical 5-lung control group; no systematic bias.
Modeling scale: 216 XGBoost models (grid search) + 96 GRU configurations (20-fold CV × 5 seeds).
Therapeutic test: 16 EVLP cases received 20-mg alteplase (left out of training; 14 analyzed; n=6 suitable, n=8 unsuitable). DT-control paired analysis showed significantly lower PAP 2 h post-treatment (P=0.031, Wilcoxon) and an edema trend (P=0.063) in suitable lungs; conventional cohort analysis found nothing (PAP P=0.36/0.78). Estimated ~18 cases (3× sample size) needed to reach the same conclusion conventionally. Unsuitable lungs showed no efficacy.
Deliverables: code at github.com/Sage-Lab-ai/DT_Lung; models + demo data at huggingface.co/SageLabUHN; web app at dt-lung.streamlit.app. See entity dt-lung.

Connections

Hub: functional-single-cell-genomics — the topic’s translational/biomedical multimodal “digital twin” entry; a human-tissue platform that includes a transcriptomics (microarray Hallmark gene-set) modality but is broader than pure genomics.
New concepts: digital-twins, multimodal-learning (joint/hybrid modeling across imaging, omics, and time-series physiology via physics-informed + data-driven ML); enabling platform = ex vivo organ perfusion (EVLP).
Incorporates ML (XGBoost/GRU/ResNet50) but does not invoke foundation models; connects loosely to ai-in-genomics via the transcriptomic-forecasting component.

Open questions

Generalizability beyond a single center and disease-free donor lungs; external validation still required. How well does the framework transfer to other ex vivo organs (liver, heart, kidney)?
Transcriptomics limited to <100 cases and microarray Hallmark gene sets — would single-cell/full RNA-seq sharpen the genomic modality?
Forecasts cover only the first 3 h; can longer EVLP enable evaluation of slower therapeutics?
DT models the target organ only (no whole-body biodistribution/off-target safety); data are not publicly shareable (re-identification concerns), limiting reproducibility to request-based access.

wiki/sources/zhu-2026-integrative-analys.title: zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory
wiki/sources/zhu-2026-integrative-analys.content:
Integrative analyses elucidate transcriptional regulatory functions of risk alleles for metabolic liver disease
One-line: A functional-genomics dissection of MASLD GWAS risk alleles that pairs single-nucleus chromatin accessibility of human liver with a context-dependent MPRA and orthogonal target-gene mapping (eQTL, Hi-C, single-cell CRISPRi) to convert noncoding variants into mechanistic, cell-type-specific regulatory hypotheses.
Key takeaways

Profiled chromatin accessibility (snATAC–seq) in 69,595 nuclei from 12 human livers (6 normal, 6 MASLD), resolving 15 cell clusters; MASLD GWAS risk variants are enriched in cell-type-specific cis-regulatory-elements bound by lineage-determining TFs, broadly across hepatocytes, stellate cells and Kupffer cells.
Single-cell-resolution GWAS enrichment (SCAVENGE, an algorithm the group previously developed) showed liver-fat-trait variants enriched specifically in hepatocytes, while MASLD variants spread across cell types; risk enrichment increased along the hepatic stellate cell activation pseudotime trajectory (S-MASLD subcluster highest).
A massively-parallel-reporter-assay of both alleles of 5,369 LD-tagged noncoding variants, run in HepG2 (hepatocyte) and LX-2 (stellate) lines ± metabolic stimuli (palmitic/oleic acid, TGFβ), identified hundreds of differential activity variants (DAVs) acting in a cell-type- and stimulus-dependent manner.
Target genes assigned by integrating liver eQTLs, chromatin looping/co-accessibility, proximity and single-cell CRISPRi/CROP-seq; key DAVs at SLC22A3, APOA5, ANGPTL3 and LPL were functionally validated to modulate gene expression, lipid accumulation and stellate-cell activation.
This is firmly an expression/regulatory (cis-regulatory + functional single-cell) study, NOT splicing-centric — no splicing/sQTL/isoform analysis appears.

Notable claims / data

snATAC: 69,595 high-quality nuclei, 15 clusters; MASLD livers showed higher cholangiocyte/stellate and lower hepatocyte/endothelial proportions. Stratified LDSC + SCAVENGE localize MASLD heritability to hepatocyte and stellate peaks.
MPRA library: median 714 barcodes/allele, 40 positive controls; 2,430 active regulatory elements in HepG2; 365 DAVs (FDR < 0.01) in baseline HepG2 across 21 chromosomes; 8/10 randomly picked DAVs validated by individual luciferase assays.
Cell-type/stimulus specificity: 464 DAVs in LX-2, 338 in PAOA-treated HepG2, 614 in TGFβ-treated LX-2; >half of DAVs were active exclusively in one cell line; ~1/3 of PAOA DAVs were stimulus-dependent. DAVs preferentially disrupt HNF4G, SREBF1/2, ETS, STAT/TEAD/TCF21, PPARE/RUNX1 motifs.
TF-binding disruption score correlated with MPRA effect size (ρ = 0.45, P = 1.3 × 10⁻¹¹).
Target genes: 794–1,009 genes assigned per DAV set; 995 DAVs have ≥1 target; 338 DAVs overlap liver eQTLs, with 64% concordant in effect direction. CROP-seq (67,700 cells) yielded 25 gene–sgRNA pairs with reduced target expression.
Functional examples (genome editing in HepG2/LX-2/spheroids): rs1748197 (ANGPTL3 promoter, MYC motif) and rs10889356 (ANGPTL3 enhancer, GABPA motif) deletions increased lipid accumulation; rs10790167 (APOA5, PPAR/RXR motifs) deletion lowered APOA5 and raised lipid droplets; rs1441754 (LPL, RUNX motif, stellate-specific) deletion raised LPL and fibrosis genes; rs474513 controls SLC22A3/LPA (opposite directions) via a RUNX motif (G allele 997× stronger).
DAVs improved prediction of MASLD risk (claimed in abstract and risk-prediction section).
Caveats: small donor cohort (n = 12); MPRA performed in transformed cell lines (HepG2/LX-2/Huh7/293T), an episomal context that may not capture native chromatin; target-gene assignment is correlative for most loci, with editing validation only for selected variants.

Connections

Sibling to groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at under functional-single-cell-genomics — both use massively-parallel-reporter-assay to assign function to cis-regulatory-elements; this paper adds disease GWAS variants, single-cell chromatin context and CRISPRi validation.
Uses chromatin looping/Hi-C for enhancer–promoter target assignment, touching chromatin-loops and chromatin-conformation-capture.
Complements eQTL-based variant interpretation; shares the human disease-genetics framing with sources like zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (different molecular layer: expression vs splicing).

Open questions

Do DAV effects replicate in primary hepatocytes/stellate cells and patient-derived organoids beyond cell lines?
How much of the >100-locus MASLD GWAS heritability is captured once all cell-type/stimulus contexts are screened?
Can the DAV-augmented polygenic risk score generalize across ancestries beyond the Million Veteran Program cohort used here?

wiki/sources/zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-mis.title: zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability
wiki/sources/zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-mis.content:
The mystery of missing heritability: Genetic interactions create phantom heritability
One-line: A theory paper arguing that much of the missing-heritability is a statistical artifact — standard estimates of total heritability assume no epistasis and are biased upward, creating “phantom heritability” — and that the limiting pathway (LP) model is a biologically plausible mechanism producing exactly this bias.
Key takeaways

The proportion of heritability “explained” is the ratio π_explained = h²_known / h²_all, where the numerator (variance from known variants, estimated bottom-up from genome-wide-association-study effect sizes) is reliable but the denominator (total heritability h²_all) is inferred top-down from population/family data as an “apparent heritability” h²_pop.
The central thesis: a substantial part of missing-heritability may be an artifact of overestimating the denominator, not missing variants in the numerator. Standard top-down estimators implicitly assume a strictly additive architecture with no genetic interactions; when epistasis is present they are biased upward. The gap π_phantom = 1 − (h²_all / h²_pop) is termed phantom heritability.
This directly critiques the family/variance-components-model estimators (e.g. the ACE twin model: h²_pop(ACE) = 2(r_MZ − r_DZ)) used throughout quantitative-genetics. They show h²_pop(ACE) = h²_all + W with W ≥ 0, where W collects all higher-order additive×additive interaction variances; the field has had “no way to estimate W from population data” and so simply assumes W = 0.
They introduce the limiting pathway (LP) model: a phenotype set by the rate-limiting (minimum) value among k additive pathways, each individually following the standard additive model. For k = 1 the LP model reduces to the strictly additive model; for k > 1 it generates substantial phantom heritability. This is motivated by real biology — rate-limiting steps in metabolic/biochemical pathways, stoichiometric complexes, transcription requirements.
Crucially, additive and LP models fit the same observable population data (sib correlations r_MZ/r_DZ, relative risks λ_R, phenotypic distributions) equally well, so genetic architecture cannot be inferred from standard family data — an “inference by default” trap.
They propose Theorem 1: a top-down estimator of h²_all that is consistent for any genetic architecture, based on regressing phenotypic similarity against the degree of genome shared identical-by-descent (IBD) among nearly-unrelated individuals in isolated populations (h²_slope(κ₀) = (1 − κ₀)ρ′(κ₀)).

Notable claims / data

Crohn’s disease (additive assumption): 71 GWAS risk loci give h²_known ≈ 10.8% of phenotypic variance, i.e. π_explained ≈ 21.5% of the heritability (assuming h²_all = h²_pop = 50%).
Crohn’s disease (LP(3) model): the same data are consistent with an LP(3) architecture giving phantom heritability π_phantom = 62.8%. Genetic interactions would then account for ~80% [= 62.8 / (1 − 0.215)] of the currently missing heritability; the known loci would actually explain 57.5% [= 21.5 / (1 − 0.628)] of the true h²_all = 18.6%.
LP(3) disease example (Δ = LP_Δ(3, 50%, 1%), c_R = 0):* a geneticist assuming additivity would estimate apparent h²_pop = 49.0% and conclude variants explain h²_known = 21.2% (43.2% of apparent heritability) — leaving 56.8% as phantom heritability.
Schizophrenia: an additive model and an LP model both fit Risch’s relative-risk data; the additive model has no phantom heritability whereas the LP model has phantom heritability of 46%.
A worked LP(4, 50%, c_R) quantitative example with P* = LP(4), c_sib = 50%, c_R = 0: apparent heritability h²_pop = 54.0% (from r_MZ = 62.4%, r_DZ = 35.4%), variants appear to explain ~25.4/54.0 ≈ 47% — but ~53% is phantom and can never be explained by additional variants.
A mathematical theorem implies π_phantom → 100% as the number of pathways k grows.
Detection is hard: for the Crohn’s LP(3) example, detecting the main effects of two frequency-20% variants needs ~4,900 samples (GWAS-scale), but detecting their pairwise interaction needs ~450,000 — sample size for n loci scales as n², but for interactions as n⁴. So failure to detect epistasis does not rule it out.
Validation on simulated isolated-population data (1,000 individuals, mean IBD sharing κ₀ = 3.5%, like Qatar): the IBD-slope estimator recovered h²_all = 25.4 ± 8.2%, very close to the true 25.4% and clearly distinct from the apparent h²_pop = 54%.

Connections

This is a direct methodological critique of biometrical-genetics and the variance-components-model / ACE-style heritability estimation used by rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and and mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models: those frameworks partition variance under additivity assumptions, and Zuk et al. show the resulting h² is an apparent (upper-bound) heritability when epistasis is present.
It reframes the problem posed by manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of, the landmark review that defined missing heritability as undiscovered variants in the numerator; Zuk et al. argue a large share lives in an inflated denominator instead.
Connects to the liability-threshold-model: their disease-trait LP model (LP_Δ) applies a liability threshold τ to the rate-limiting pathway value, extending the standard additive liability model used for binary disease traits.
Relevant to pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and and any work estimating genetic vs environmental variance components, since the bias they describe applies whenever additivity is assumed across loci.
Distinguishes broad-sense H² (= V_G/Var(P), full genetic contribution, the relevant target for prediction) from narrow-sense h² (additive contribution), noting that lower h² does not mean traits are “less genetic” — only that additive effects are a smaller fraction of H².

Open questions

Can genetic interactions actually be detected and quantified in human data, given that interaction effects scale inversely with the square of effect size and require sample sizes (~500,000+) far beyond current GWAS?
How much real missing heritability is phantom vs genuinely undiscovered variants? The authors stress many causal variants surely remain and discovery should continue — but the split is currently unidentifiable from standard data.
Will isolated-population IBD-sharing designs (Iceland, Finland, Hutterites, Amish, Qatar) provide the unbiased h²_all estimates the theory requires, in practice and at scale?
The LP model is offered as an existence proof, not a claim that real traits follow it; what are the true rate-limiting / multi-pathway architectures underlying specific complex traits?
Do more general multi-pathway and gene–environment (G×E) interaction models inflate heritability even further than the G×G LP model considered here?

wiki/syntheses/alternative-splicing-func.title: alternative-splicing-functionality-debate
wiki/syntheses/alternative-splicing-func.content:
Alternative Splicing: Functional or Noise? (Contradiction resolution)
Resolves the flag raised in overview and alternative-splicing:
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative argues most alternative-splicing
is functional, while wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source argues
most events are probably noise (with a conserved functional subset). Grounded
strictly in the ingested sources.
Verdict
Not a genuine factual contradiction — largely a difference in (1) the definition
of “functional”, (2) the denominator being counted, and (3) which evidence is
weighted. Both reviews actually agree on the central empirical points; they
disagree on how to classify the large tail of low-abundance, non-conserved
isoforms, which neither can yet settle with available data.
Where they actually AGREE

Prevalence: ~90–95% of human genes undergo AS
(wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source,
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative).
A conserved, functional core exists and drives development and physiology —
both accept this. Wright: of proteomically-detected tissue-specific isoforms,
95% are highly conserved across vertebrates; Marasco: highly conserved
neural microexons (SRRM4). Both treat conservation as evidence of function.
The same ribosome-profiling number: ~75% of (medium/high-abundance)
exon-skipping mRNAs are ribosome-engaged — this figure appears in both reviews
(marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source).
A low-abundance error tail exists: deep sequencing detects rare isoforms
that may be spliceosome errors (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative) —
Marasco doesn’t deny this; he denies it’s the majority of meaningful events.

Why the headline conclusions differ
1. Different definition of “functional”

Marasco ≈ reflected at the protein level / has a physiological role. He
emphasizes that mass-spec undercounts real isoforms (“absence of evidence is
not evidence of absence”) and that single-gene physiology (Dscam, CLK1 thermo-
sensing, PKM/Warburg, TRPV1, hTERT) shows pervasive function.
Wright ≈ encodes a preserved/selected protein function. Crucially, Wright
notes many adaptive AS events work by producing non-functional truncated
proteins (PTC-bearing) — stickleback MSX2A, cavefish per2, bat TRPV1.
So in Wright’s framing a transcript can be “non-functional” (at the protein
level) yet phenotypically and evolutionarily consequential. This dissolves
much of the apparent conflict: “non-functional protein” ≠ “no effect”.

2. Different denominator

Wright counts all RNA-seq-detected events, including the large pool of
low-abundance, non-conserved, lineage-specific variants → “most are noise.”
Marasco focuses on medium/high-abundance events and conserved switches →
“most [that matter] are functional.” They are describing different slices of the
same distribution.

3. Different evidence weighting

Marasco leans on ribosome profiling + the insensitivity of proteomics.
Wright leans on cross-species conservation as the functionality filter, and
treats AS as an evolutionarily labile, mostly-drift process from which
selection occasionally recruits useful variants.

Synthesised position
A defensible reconciliation consistent with all three sources:

~90–95% of human genes undergo AS, but isoform output is a steep
abundance/conservation distribution.
The head (abundant, conserved, often tissue-specific) is largely
functional — and both reviews agree here.
The long tail (rare, non-conserved) is dominated by stochastic
spliceosome noise/error — Wright’s emphasis, compatible with Lee’s error caveat.
“Functional” must be split into senses: translated stable protein /
conserved-selected / quantitative regulator via AS–NMD
poison exons (even untranslated isoforms regulate protein level — both accept).
Disagreement shrinks once the sense is fixed.

Residual genuine uncertainty
The real open question — what fraction of the tail is functional — is
unresolved because current tools can’t settle it. Both reviews propose the same
fixes: long-read sequencing, single-cell, and CRISPR perturbation to test
isoforms directly (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source). Until then this is a
difference of emphasis over an unmeasured tail, not a contradiction of fact.
Sources

marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — “mostly functional”; proteomics
undercounts; ribosome profiling; physiological case studies; long-read proposal
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — “mostly noise + conserved
subset”; conservation as filter; adaptation via non-functional truncated proteins
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — low-abundance isoforms may
be spliceosome errors (the error-tail caveat)

wiki/syntheses/cotranscriptional-vs-post.title: cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing
wiki/syntheses/cotranscriptional-vs-post.content:
Co-transcriptional vs Post-transcriptional Splicing
A comparison of the two timing modes of intron removal. Grounded in the locally
ingested sources. See the concept pages cotranscriptional-splicing and
post-transcriptional-splicing.
The core distinction
Both modes use the same spliceosome and the same assembly steps
(E → A → B → Bact/B* → C); what differs is when splicing happens relative to
transcription and 3′-end processing
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of,
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).

Co-transcriptional — intron removed during synthesis, while the nascent
pre-mRNA is still tethered to elongating RNA Pol II.
Post-transcriptional — removal completes after transcription and
polyadenylation, typically while the polyadenylated transcript is still
chromatin-associated.

Side-by-side


















































AxisCo-transcriptionalPost-transcriptionalTimingDuring transcription, on nascent RNAAfter transcription + polyadenylationLocationElongating Pol II / chromatinChromatin-associated (poly(A)+ RNA), nuclear specklesExtent~70% of mammalian introns excised on chromatin (much co-transcriptional)~27–45% of introns (up to ~40% retained after termination); >70% of genes have ≥1SpliceosomeIdentical assembly stepsIdentical assembly steps (only timing differs)Primary control knobPol II elongation rate (kinetic coupling) + chromatincis features (GC, length, ss strength, RNA structure) + trans factors + modificationsMechanistic model”Window of opportunity”: slow Pol II favors inclusion of class I / skipping of class II exonsDelay buys time for splice-site choice; splicing completion gates transcript releaseFunctional consequenceSets alternative-splicing outcomes via kineticsGates nuclear export & translation; regulatory reservoir (detained introns)Key sourcesmarasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternativechoquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of
What drives each

Co-transcriptional → kinetic coupling. Splice-site choice depends on
cis-splicing-regulatory-elements, trans splicing-factors, and Pol II
elongation rate (~3–4 kb/min). A slow polymerase widens the window for an
upstream splice site to be recognized before a competitor
appears — slow elongation promotes class I exon inclusion and class II exon
skipping. Elongation is itself tuned by chromatin
(histone marks, nucleosomes) and the Pol II CTD
(marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).
Post-transcriptional → delayed, gated removal. Timing is set by cis
features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary
structure) and trans factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins, U2
