snRNA, SNIP1), modulated by CLK/SRPK phosphorylation, PRMT methylation and m6A.
Detained introns are a specialized held-until-signaled class (neurons,
spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation, heat shock)
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).

How they relate (not a strict binary)
The two are endpoints of a timing continuum, not mutually exclusive
categories — a single transcript’s introns can be removed at different times
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of). Notably, introns flanking
alternative exons are preferentially post-transcriptional (67% vs 30% for
constitutive introns in mESCs), plausibly buying time for correct splice-site
choice — so the post-transcriptional delay is itself a regulatory device feeding
alternative-splicing (choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Supporting data (post-transcriptional)

Median chromatin half-life of mRNA ~50 min in K562 (some genes >170 min),
often exceeding transcription time itself.
A β-globin reporter intron was excised ~10× faster and more often (87%) after
release from the transcription site; for 6 of 10 endogenous human introns
post-transcriptional excision was more probable than co-transcriptional.
Most retained introns are eventually spliced productively; nuclear decay is the
minority fate (~4% of genes, “PUND” transcripts).
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)

Open questions

What triggers the eventual post-transcriptional reaction, and does the delay
occur before or after spliceosome engagement?
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)
Do exons regulated by transcription rate (co-transcriptional kinetics) overlap
with those regulated by delayed removal of flanking introns?
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)

Sources

choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — primary: extent, kinetics,
detained introns, release gating, determinants of post-transcriptional splicing
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — kinetic coupling model;
class I/II exons; elongation–chromatin link
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — identical assembly
steps; Pol II ~3–4 kb/min; slow-polymerase effects

wiki/syntheses/crispr-screens-and-genome.title: CRISPR Screens & Genome Editing
wiki/syntheses/crispr-screens-and-genome.content:
CRISPR Screens & Genome Editing
A cross-cutting read of the wiki’s CRISPR cluster — the entry point for CRISPR
in this wiki. Note there is no standalone “CRISPR” topic: these methods live inside
functional-single-cell-genomics as its endogenous-perturbation family. Thesis:
CRISPR is the tool that converts a regulatory correlation into a causation — by
editing the endogenous genome at scale and reading the consequence, whether that
readout is cell fitness, a bulk selection, or a whole single-cell transcriptome.
The core methods pages: pooled-crispr-screen, single-cell-crispr-screen,
genome-editing; the platforms: perturb-seq, scandel, crispey.
1. One idea, three readouts
A pooled-crispr-screen delivers a library of guide RNAs (or guide pairs)
across a cell population so each cell carries one perturbation; the change in each
guide’s abundance from before to after a selection/readout scores that
perturbation’s effect. The three readouts define the family
(pooled-crispr-screen):

Fitness / survival — count guides under selection (e.g. drug or metabolic
selection). Used by the tiling-deletion and precise-editing screens below.
Bulk — e.g. drug resistance in a population.
Single-cell transcriptome — each cell’s full scRNA-seq profile is the
phenotype (single-cell-crispr-screen).

It is the endogenous-genome counterpart to the episomal reporter assay
MPRA: MPRA tests sequences on a plasmid,
CRISPR screens act on the sequence in its native chromosomal context
(pooled-crispr-screen, genome-editing).
2. Transcriptome-readout single-cell screens (Perturb-seq)
dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits founded
perturb-seq — a pooled CRISPR screen with a droplet scRNA-seq readout, so
one experiment yields, per perturbation, the affected genes, programs, cell states,
and even genetic interactions (single-cell-crispr-screen).

Guide barcode (GBC) trick. A standard sgRNA is not polyadenylated and is poorly
captured by 3′ scRNA-seq, so an expressed, poly-A GBC reports the guide identity
inside the transcriptome (perturb-seq).
MIMOSCA model. A regularized (elastic-net) linear model deconvolves overlapping
perturbation effects, folding in technical/biological covariates and interaction
terms — an early instance of single-cell-perturbation-modeling and
model-interpretability.
MOI tuning sets the screen for single-gene effects (low MOI) or epistasis
(higher MOI, multiple guides/cell).
adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening developed the same GBC
multiplexing in parallel (in the CRISPRi setting) and used it to dissect the
three-arm mammalian unfolded protein response.

3. Tiling-deletion scans (ScanDel)
gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements uses paired gRNAs
(scandel) to program thousands of overlapping kilobase-scale deletions that
densely tile a locus, testing which cis-regulatory-elements are required in
place — much larger edits than the 1–10 bp indels of single-guide screens
(genome-editing).

Demonstrated at HPRT1 (HAP1 cells): 4,342 deletions tiling 206 kb, selected
with 6-thioguanine; median 27-fold coverage per base.
Key negative result: no single distal regulatory element was required; an
apparent exon-proximal signal was an artifact of rare exon-extending deletions,
exposed only by long-read (PacBio) genotyping
(gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements). A caution that
redundancy and careful controls (self-pairs) are essential in CRE screens.

4. Precise single-base editing at scale (CRISPEY)
sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel installs thousands of
exact natural variants by genome-editing and reads each variant’s relative
fitness — moving from “delete a region” to “swap a single base”
(crispey).

CRISPEY couples a bacterial retron to the sgRNA so each cell makes its own
tethered single-stranded HDR donor (msDNA) at the cut site — up to ~100% editing
efficiency, single-base resolution (crispey).
Screened 16,006 yeast variants by pooled growth competition; noncoding /
cis-regulatory variants dominate the fitness landscape
(sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel,
noncoding-variants) — echoing the theme that most functional variation is
regulatory.

5. CRISPR as validation, not just discovery
CRISPR also serves as the causal check on hypotheses generated by other methods:

In KCl-stimulated iPSC neurons, CRISPR knockout of the ASD-risk TFs RORB,
MEF2C, and TCF4 validated the predicted dysregulation of their ASD target genes
— turning a GRN-inferred network into tested causation
(liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron, autism,
neuronal-activation).
A human-neural MPRA of eight psychiatric
disorders (lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric) is the
reporter-based sibling of these endogenous screens — the two together bracket a
variant’s function from episomal and native-context angles.

Spatial & in-situ extensions
The idea keeps generalizing within functional-single-cell-genomics: spatial
pooled CRISPR screening reads perturbations in situ
(zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics, spac-seq),
and tethered base editing maps regulatory DNA–protein interactions at single-cell
resolution (chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory, d-and-d-seq).
Open questions

Which CREs matter, and when? Tiling deletions can return “no single element
required” (gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements) — how much
regulatory logic is redundant/distributed vs pinpointable?
From yeast fitness to human disease. CRISPEY’s fitness readout is powerful in
yeast; how far do single-base precise-editing screens scale into human cells and
disease-relevant phenotypes (sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel)?
Screen ↔ model loop. Perturb-seq data trains
perturbation-response models; can those models
predict well enough to prioritize the next screen rather than run it exhaustively?

Sources

dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits — founding perturb-seq: pooled CRISPR + scRNA-seq + MIMOSCA
adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening — GBC-multiplexed single-cell CRISPRi screen (UPR)
gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements — scandel paired-gRNA tiling deletions at HPRT1
sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel — crispey retron single-base precise editing of 16,006 yeast variants
lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric — reporter-assay sibling (psychiatric MPRA)
liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron — CRISPR-KO validation of ASD-TF network in activated neurons

wiki/syntheses/poison-exons-regulation-a.title: poison-exons-regulation-and-therapy
wiki/syntheses/poison-exons-regulation-a.content:
Poison Exons: From Regulatory Knob to Drug Target
A cross-cutting read of the wiki’s poison-exons cluster — the genome-wide
catalog (pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in), the therapeutic review
(neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing), and the disease/mechanism sources
that touch AS–NMD. Grounded strictly in the ingested sources. Thesis: a poison
exon is the same object viewed three ways — a dosage knob cells use to autoregulate,
a failure mode that causes neurological disease, and a handle that lets RNA drugs
tune otherwise-undruggable genes.
1. One mechanism, by definition
A poison exon (PE) is an alternatively spliced exon
whose inclusion inserts a premature termination codon (PTC) >50 nt upstream of
the final exon–exon junction, so the host transcript is destroyed by
nonsense-mediated decay (NMD) — “AS–NMD”. The output is not a new protein but
less of the existing one: PE inclusion is a post-transcriptional
quantitative regulator, not a proteome-diversifier
(neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing, pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in).
This is the sense in which even an untranslated isoform is “functional” — the point
on which the functionality debate turns.
2. How common, and how trustworthy the count?
The two primary sources agree PEs are pervasive but flag the same measurement
caveat:

Prevalence. ~15% of human mRNAs are NMD targets; ~5% of exons (in ~13% of
genes) show AS–NMD-attributable differential expression; many genes route >20% of
their transcripts through PEs (neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing).
A systematic annotation finds 9,814 putative human PEs (17.8% of annotated AS
events), and humans have the highest NMD-isoform enrichment of 957 eukaryotic
transcriptomes (39.6% of genes give ≥1 NMD isoform)
(pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in).
Caveat (shared). Those human-high numbers may partly reflect deeper
annotation, and short-read PSI measures only overall exon inclusion, not which
isoform actually carries the PTC. Pigini explicitly cautions on annotation depth;
Neil notes many PEs only surface under NMD perturbation. Both name
long-read-rna-seq as the way to resolve true transcript-context PE usage.
But not just noise. PEs show stronger splice sites and greater cross-species
conservation than stochastic cryptic events, and a CRISPR screen put up to ~50% of
PEs as fitness-relevant (neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing) — i.e. PEs
sit in the conserved head, not the noisy tail, of the AS distribution.

3. Endogenous role: autoregulation and a brain/developmental bias

Splicing factors autoregulate through PEs. PE-containing genes are enriched
for the spliceosome and mRNA-surveillance machinery; SR/SF3 factors include their
own PEs to homeostatically cap their levels
(pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in) — the AS–NMD autoregulation theme from
baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator and
kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated.
The brain is the hotspot. PE splicing is developmentally and regionally
patterned: 83 PEs are brain-unique, 973 differ in brain vs other tissue, and 1,284
shift across brain development (pre- vs post-birth); brain RBPs RBFOX1/NOVA1/UPF3B
drive brain-specific PEs, and NOVA1 PE inclusion falls as neurons mature
(pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in). Neil notes ~33% of the ~3,000
RBP-coordinated neuronal cassette exons are predicted to trigger NMD. This puts
PEs squarely inside splicing-and-neurodevelopment.

4. Failure mode: a disease mechanism (esp. neurological)

Catalog → pathogenic variants. Integrating ClinVar with spliceai flags
1,625 pathogenic variants predicted to disrupt 743 PEs, enriched for
seizures/epilepsy, intellectual disability, and neurodegeneration; CRISPR
prime-editing validated several (e.g. deep-intronic NDUFAF6 c.420+784C>T → ↑PE
inclusion → ↓protein → mitochondrial deficit in iPSC neurons)
(pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in).
Cis and trans. PE misregulation arises from cis splice-site/SRE
mutations or trans splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins dysfunction:
TDP-43 mislocalization (ALS) de-represses cryptic/poison exons such as a UNC13A
PE (neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing); the same TDP-43-loss → cryptic/
poison-exon axis appears in Huntington’s disease alongside altered m6A
(nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease); and sf3b1
gain-of-function reshapes the NMD-targeted landscape in myeloid cancer. This
extends the disease-mechanism framing of
scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease to PTC/NMD-coupled events specifically.

5. The handle: PEs as a drug target
The therapeutic logic is symmetric — modulate the knob in either direction
(neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing, see splicing-therapeutics):

Skip a pathogenic PE → restore expression (for haploinsufficiency): ASOs
STK-001/zorevunersen (SCN1A, Dravet) and STK-002 (OPA1, ADOA).
Induce a PE → degrade a disease driver (RNA analog of targeted protein
degradation): U1-snRNP small-molecule “glues” — branaplam, PTC518, SKY-0515 (HTT,
Huntington’s), REM-422/RGT-61159 (MYB, leukemias).
Precedent is already approved. Two smn2 splice modulators — nusinersen (ASO)
and risdiplam (small molecule) — validate both modalities for SMA, and ~6
PE-targeting drugs are in trials. Pigini frames its NMD-sensitive PEs (e.g. the
SCN1A “20N” PE / NDUFAF6) as exactly this kind of correctable defect.

Open questions (shared across the cluster)

Which PEs matter? Of ~10⁴ (or, per ML estimates, possibly >10⁶) candidate PEs,
how many are bona fide regulatory switches vs low-abundance annotation artifacts,
and how do we predict NMD penetrance up front to pick tractable targets?
(pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in, neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing)
Measurement. Short reads can’t say which isoform carries the PTC — long-read-rna-seq
is the needed fix.
NMD escape. Some PE transcripts evade NMD and translate truncated peptides, so
PE inclusion is not always clean loss-of-function (neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing).
Therapeutic selectivity. Which SREs are selectively druggable, and can
paralog-selective targeting exploit faster-evolving intronic elements?

Sources

pigini-2026-poison-exon-splicing-in — genome-wide human PE catalog (9,814 PEs),
brain/developmental patterning, 1,625 pathogenic variants, prime-editing validation,
evonmd pipeline
neil-2025-poison-exons-tuning-rna-splicing — review: AS–NMD mechanism, disease
(cis/trans), and the druggable-handle landscape (ASOs, U1-glue small molecules)
scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease — disease mis-splicing incl. NMD-inducing cryptic exons
baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator · kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated — AS–NMD autoregulation in development
nguyen-2025-aberrant-splicing-in-huntingtons-disease — TDP-43-loss cryptic/poison exons in HD

wiki/topics/3d-genome.title: 3d-genome
wiki/topics/3d-genome.content:
3D Genome
Topic hub for the three-dimensional organization of the genome — how
chromatin folds in the nucleus (loops → TADs → compartments), how that
architecture is measured, and how it evolves. This is the wiki’s third topic
(alongside splicing and artificial-intelligence); it will grow as more
sources are ingested.
Why it matters
The nuclear genome is folded into precise 3D structures that compact DNA while
controlling access to genetic information — tuning enhancer–promoter proximity
and therefore gene regulation. Treating this genome-architecture as an
evolvable trait reveals how form and function co-vary across scales
(che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome).
Sources (7)













































SourceYearAngleche-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome2026Micro-to-macro framework for 3D-genome evolution: cancer, between species, across kingdoms (Trends in Genetics)won-2016-chromosome-conformation-elucidates-regulatory2016Hi-C of mid-gestation human cortex links enhancers/GWAS variants to distal genes (Nature) · humanfudenberg-2020-predicting-3d-genome-folding2020Akita — CNN predicts locus 3D folding (Hi-C/Micro-C) from ~1 Mb of sequence (Nat Methods) · resourceschwessinger-2020-deepc-predicting-3d-genome-folding2020DeepC — transfer-learning net predicts folding + variant impact from megabase sequence (Nat Methods) · resourcepampari-2024-chrombpnet-bias-factorized-base-resolution2024ChromBPNet — base-resolution chromatin-accessibility model learns TF grammar + footprints (bioRxiv) · resourcewen-2024-single-cell-multiplex-chromatin2024MUSIC — single-nucleus multiplex chromatin + RNA interactions in ageing human brain (Nature) · human · resourcemao-2024-structurally-divergent-and-recurrently-mutated2024Long-read atlas of structurally divergent / recurrently mutated primate genome regions (Cell) · resource
Concept map

The hierarchy — genome-architecture · chromatin-compartments · topologically-associating-domains · chromatin-loops
How we measure it — chromatin-conformation-capture
How it changes — 3d-genome-evolution · cis-regulatory-elements · 3d-genome-and-disease

Current synthesis (7 sources)

Sequence encodes folding. Deep models predict 3D folding — and the impact of
noncoding variants on it — from DNA sequence alone
(fudenberg-2020-predicting-3d-genome-folding, schwessinger-2020-deepc-predicting-3d-genome-folding),
and base-resolution accessibility models learn the underlying TF grammar
(pampari-2024-chrombpnet-bias-factorized-base-resolution).
Architecture wires regulation and disease. Hi-C in developing human cortex maps
enhancers and psychiatric-GWAS variants to distal target genes
(won-2016-chromosome-conformation-elucidates-regulatory); single-nucleus multiplex
chromatin/RNA contact maps extend this into the ageing brain
(wen-2024-single-cell-multiplex-chromatin).
Architecture is an evolvable trait across scales. A micro-to-macro
framework links the diversity of genome folding to the evolution of form and
function (3d-genome-evolution).
Cancer = fastest 3D evolution. Frequent mutation + plastic epigenetic
marks rapidly rewire loops, TADs and enhancer–promoter contacts; a transient
“structural bottleneck” can funnel cells toward higher fitness
(3d-genome-and-disease).
Close species diverge through sequence, not structure. Architectural
divergence between related species is locus-restricted and driven mainly by
cis-regulatory-elements within conserved folding frameworks — only the
information that re-forms structures is heritable, not the structures
themselves.
Convergence across kingdoms. Similar higher-order architectures recur via
distinct molecular machinery (e.g. insect insulators, ctenophore CTEP1/2,
bacterial NAPs) — selection acts on function, not components. Plants cluster
genes linearly and lack CTCF / canonical compartments.

Open questions

How does architectural complexity relate causally to organismal complexity
(shown mainly within animals)? (che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome)
What are the distinct mechanisms behind convergent higher-order features?


Cross-topic note: chromatin and Pol II elongation also shape splicing
(see splicing-and-chromatin) — genome architecture touches gene regulation
broadly.


Claims grounded in the locally ingested source only.

wiki/topics/artificial-intelligence.title: artificial-intelligence
wiki/topics/artificial-intelligence.content:
Artificial Intelligence
Topic hub for AI/ML in biology and genomics — both the methods (foundation
models and deep learning for sequences, structures, single cells, and
perturbations) and the safety, dual-use, and governance questions they raise.
The wiki’s second topic (alongside splicing and
functional-single-cell-genomics).
Why it matters
Since the transformer (2017), foundation-models for biological sequences (DNA,
RNA, protein) and single cells have become central tools in genomics. They now span
structure prediction, regulatory-grammar modelling, mutation-effect prediction, and
perturbation response — while the same generative capability that designs beneficial
molecules can lower the barrier to harmful ones, keeping innovation vs safety in
tension (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Sources (23)

A regulatory-genomics / single-cell modeling wave was added 2026-07-04; the
experimental resources these models learn from live under gene-regulation,
3d-genome, and functional-single-cell-genomics.




































































































































SourceYearAnglehuman?resource?theodoris-2023-transfer-learning-enables-predictions2023geneformer — transformer pretrained on ~30M single cells; transfer learning for network biology✗✓cui-2024-scgpt-toward-building-a-foundation2024scgpt — generative foundation model for single-cell multi-omics (annotation, perturbation, integration)✗✓roohani-2024-predicting-transcriptional-outcomes-of-novel2024gears — knowledge-graph GNN predicts single/multi-gene perturbation outcomes✗✓klie-2023-predictive-analyses-of-regulatory-sequences2023eugene — FAIR PyTorch toolkit for deep learning on regulatory sequences✗✓karollus-2023-current-sequence-based-models2023benchmark: sequence-to-expression models capture promoters but under-weight distal enhancers✗✗sokolova-2024-deep-learning-sequence-models2024review of sequence-based deep-learning models of transcriptional regulation and 3D genome✗✗seplyarskiy-2023-a-mutation-rate-model2023roulette-model — basepair-resolution germline mutation-rate model; reveals Pol III mutagenesis✗✓hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation2026Safety-centric view of generative AI in genomics — dual-use, bias, governance (Trends in Genetics)✗✗wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure2026trrosettarna2 — rna-structure-prediction via a pre-trained SS prior + structure-aware attention (Nat Mach Intell)✗✓han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across2026uniair — multimodal mutation-effect-prediction across immune-recognition (Nat Mach Intell)✗✓driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable2026cmonge — conditional optimal-transport for single-cell-perturbation-modeling (Nat Mach Intell)✗✓yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions2026crossdna — cross-strand DNA language model (dna-language-models) (Nat Mach Intell)✗✓wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge2026txpert — knowledge-graphs for transcriptomic perturbation prediction (Nat Biotechnol)✗✓gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting2026signature-tool (SIGnature) — model-interpretability of single-cell-foundation-models (Nat Biotechnol)✗✓wang-2026-regvelo-gene-regulatory-informed-dynamics2026regvelo — RNA velocity coupled to a learnable gene regulatory network for in-silico perturbation & cell-fate prediction (Cell)✗✓marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding2026flare — deep-learning chromatin-accessibility models (ChromBPNet) + evolutionary constraint tie common-vs-rare noncoding-variants to fetal-neuron purifying selection & disease (Nat Genet)✓✓rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl2026ingene-module — co-expression-network models add trans-eQTL signal to cis transcriptome-wide-association-study, surfacing 641 novel schizophrenia genes across six human brain regions (Nat Genet)✓✓
Concept map

Sequence & structure models — foundation-models · generative-ai · ai-in-genomics · dna-language-models · protein-language-models · rna-structure-prediction
Single cells & perturbation — single-cell-foundation-models · single-cell-perturbation-modeling · optimal-transport · knowledge-graphs · model-interpretability
Tasks & techniques — mutation-effect-prediction · immune-recognition · multimodal-learning
Regulatory genomics — noncoding-variants · chromatin-accessibility · cis-regulatory-grammar · cis-regulatory-elements
Statistical genetics / expression imputation — transcriptome-wide-association-study · gene-coexpression-network · genome-wide-association-study
Risks & response — dual-use-risk · biosecurity · data-quality-and-bias · ai-safety · ai-governance

Key entities

Models / tools — trrosettarna2 · uniair · cmonge · crossdna · txpert · signature-tool · regvelo · flare · ingene-module · spliceai

Current synthesis (across the 17 sources)

Foundation models are now general-purpose biological tools. The same paradigm
spans DNA language models (crossdna), protein language models (uniair),
RNA structure (trrosettarna2), and single-cell embeddings (the FMs
signature-tool interprets) — a clear arc from the splicing-code/spliceai
origins into every biomolecular modality.
Inject domain priors, win efficiency. The strongest methods bake biology into
the architecture rather than scaling blindly: secondary-structure priors
(trrosettarna2), explicit cross-strand symmetry (crossdna, 408K params
beating 7B Evo2), gene knowledge-graphs (txpert), and optimal-transport
structure (cmonge). Several beat models orders of magnitude larger.
Generalization to the unseen is the shared frontier. Unseen drugs/doses
(cmonge), unseen perturbations and cell lines (txpert), unseen mutations
(uniair), and unseen RNA conformers (trrosettarna2) — all foreground OOD
generalization, and all hit limits tied to data-quality-and-bias.
Interpretation and measurement check the models. signature-tool turns FM
embeddings into testable gene-importance hypotheses (model-interpretability);
and across the wiki, direct measurement (massively-parallel-reporter-assay,
ΔPSI) still beats prediction where models are weak.
From sequence models to population genetics. Deep-learning
chromatin-accessibility models scale to billions of noncoding-variant
effects, and folding in evolutionary constraint (flare) ties them to
heritability and selection — common variants more cell-type-specific, ultra-rare
variants larger/broader with strongest purifying selection in fetal neurons
(marderstein-2026-decoding-common-and-rare-noncoding). This bridges the AI methods
to functional-single-cell-genomics (the scATAC atlases they train on) and
biometrical-genetics (the missing-heritability debate).
Expression imputation goes beyond cis. TWAS
has classically modelled only local genetic effects; co-expression-network models
(ingene-module) add distal trans signal (gene-coexpression-network) to expand
the imputable transcriptome and surface 641 new schizophrenia genes
(rossi-2026-coexpression-based-models-improve-eqtl) — connecting these methods to the
human-brain disease genetics of psychiatric-genetics and the functional
massively-parallel-reporter-assay validation in functional-single-cell-genomics.
Safety stays the backdrop. Capability gains (immunogen/antibody design in
uniair, sequence generation) keep dual-use-risk live; no single safeguard
suffices, motivating layered ai-safety + ai-governance
(hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).

Open questions

How to govern foundation-model development that largely does not yet meet “high-risk” standards? (ai-governance)
How to make safeguards robust to novel sequences, not just known threats? (ai-safety)
Can sequence models predict individual-level and activating effects, where they currently fail? (yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions, groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at)
Will single-cell perturbation models generalize across cell types and beat simple baselines? (driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable, wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge)


Cross-topic notes: the splicing-code / spliceai connection links this topic
to splicing; and functional-single-cell-genomics supplies the atlases,
variant maps, and platforms these models learn from and are tested against.


Claims grounded in the locally ingested sources only.

2026-07 batch additions — models, methods & computational science
lu-2021-wider-field-of-view (News & Views on the enformer sequence-to-expression model); rahimikollu-2024-slide-significant-latent-factor-interaction (the slide latent-factor method); nadig-2024-transcriptome-wide-characterization-genetic-perturbations (the trade perturbation-effect framework); vong-2024-grounded-language-acquisition-through (grounded language acquisition from one child’s video, cvcl); goh-2026-rethinking-bioinformatics-expertise-era (rethinking bioinformatics expertise in the AI era); bond-2012-61-million-person-experiment-social-influence (a 61-million-person social-influence experiment — computational social science).

wiki/topics/biometrical-genetics.title: biometrical-genetics
wiki/topics/biometrical-genetics.content:
Biometrical Genetics & Variance-Components Modeling
Topic hub for the statistical methodology of estimating how much of a trait’s
variation is genetic — decomposing phenotypic variance into genetic and
environmental components (heritability) from twin-and-family-study-design
data. This is the methods/inference counterpart to the biology-heavy topics in this
wiki: where they ask which genes and cells matter, this one asks how do we
partition the variance at all, and with what software.
Why it matters
The topic has two intertwined strands:

How to estimate heritability (the methodology, 3 papers). Classic biometrical
genetics (quantitative-genetics) was historically done in specialized
structural-equation-model software (e.g. mx-software). These sources
advance a unifying argument: the same models can be cast as a
variance-components-model and fitted with standard (generalized) linear
mixed-model software — making heritability estimation more accessible, more
flexible (covariates, missing data, varying family sizes), and applicable to both
continuous and binary traits.
What the estimate means (the missing-heritability debate, 2 papers). Once
family studies put trait heritability high but a
genome-wide-association-study explains little of it, the field confronts
missing-heritability — and disagrees on whether the gap is undiscovered
variants (numerator) or an inflated estimate from ignoring epistasis
(denominator).

Sources (13)






















































SourceYearAnglehuman?resource?pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and2004(methodology) Binary-trait genetic/environmental components over extended families via generalized-linear-mixed-model + liability-threshold-model + hierarchical-likelihood; Swedish pre-eclampsia (Stat Med)✓✗mcardle-2005-mixed-effects-variance-components-models2005(methodology) Proves PAM (structural-equation-model) ↔ VCM equivalence; VCM (not PAM) fits in sas-proc-mixed; MEMA vs SEMA software (Behav Genet)✗✗rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and2008(methodology) Fits twin/family ACE/ADE variance-components-models in standard mixed-model software (gllamm) for continuous and categorical traits, replacing SEM tools (Biometrics)✗✗manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of2009(debate) Landmark review naming missing-heritability: common-variant genome-wide-association-study explains little of twin/family heritability; catalogues fixes (Nature)✓✗zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability2012(debate) “Phantom heritability”: additive-assuming estimators inflate total h²; epistasis (limiting-pathway model) can explain much of the gap (PNAS)✓✗zhang-2023-a-new-method-multiancestry2023(application) ct-sleb — fast model-free multiancestry polygenic risk scores that improve prediction in non-European populations (genome-wide-association-study) (Nat Genet)✓✓
Concept map

Goal — heritability · quantitative-genetics
The model — variance-components-model · structural-equation-model (PAM, the equivalent form)
The data — twin-and-family-study-design
The estimation engine — linear-mixed-model (continuous) · generalized-linear-mixed-model (binary) · liability-threshold-model · hierarchical-likelihood
The missing-heritability debate — missing-heritability · genome-wide-association-study · epistasis

Key entities

Standard mixed-model software (the new route) — gllamm (Stata) · sas-proc-mixed (SAS)
Specialized SEM software (the incumbent) — mx-software

Current synthesis (across the 5 sources)

One thesis, three angles. The three methodology papers take biometric genetics off dedicated
SEM software and onto mainstream mixed models. McArdle (2005) supplies the
theoretical license — the path-analysis (PAM) and variance-components (VCM)
formulations are algebraically equivalent, but only the VCM maps onto a
linear-mixed-model. Rabe-Hesketh (2008) operationalizes it for both
continuous and dichotomous traits in gllamm. Pawitan (2004) pushes the
hardest case — binary traits over large pedigrees — using a
generalized-linear-mixed-model.
Continuous → binary is the difficulty axis. Gaussian traits fit a plain LMM;
binary traits need a GLMM (and a liability-threshold-model reading), where
the likelihood loses its closed form and estimation gets harder
(hierarchical-likelihood is one answer).
Flexibility is the selling point. The mixed-model route handles covariates,
missing data, multiple relatives per family, and varying family sizes more
naturally than fixed-structure twin SEM — extending analysis from twin pairs to
arbitrary twin-and-family-study-design pedigrees.
The estimate is also contested, not just computed. The two newer papers turn
the lens on the heritability number itself. manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of
documents that family heritability dwarfs what a genome-wide-association-study
explains (missing-heritability) and treats the family estimate as the target;
zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability argues that family estimate may be
inflated because standard variance-components-model estimators ignore
epistasis (“phantom heritability”). So the very quantity the methodology papers
estimate so carefully sits on a contested additivity assumption.

Connections to other topics

This family/pedigree heritability is the methodological cousin of the
SNP/GWAS heritability partitioned across cell types in
functional-single-cell-genomics (e.g. sc-linker via S-LDSC) — same target
quantity (genetic share of variance), different data (pedigrees vs genotyped
unrelateds). See heritability for the distinction.

Open questions

How well do the standard-software variance-component estimates for binary
traits agree with purpose-built SEM/threshold estimates across realistic
pedigree sizes? (rabe-hesketh-2008-biometrical-modeling-of-twin-and,
pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and)
The sources predate modern genomic-relatedness (GREML/LDSC) heritability — how
does the pedigree variance-components tradition relate to today’s molecular
heritability estimators?
Can the maternal-vs-foetal effect separation in
pawitan-2004-estimation-of-genetic-and be reproduced in the standard
mixed-model parameterizations of the other two papers?
If epistasis inflates pedigree heritability (zuk-2012-the-mystery-of-missing-heritability),
how much of the missing-heritability gap in manolio-2009-finding-the-missing-heritability-of
survives once the additivity assumption is dropped — i.e. how much is “phantom”
vs genuinely undiscovered?


Grounded strictly in the locally ingested sources. See each source page for citations.

2026-07 batch additions — multiple testing, association statistics & data hygiene
Multiple-testing / FDR. The Storey q-value / positive-FDR trilogy: storey-2002-direct-approach-to-false-discovery, storey-2003-positive-false-discovery-rate, and storey-2003-statistical-significance-genomewide-studies.
Association-study statistics. xiao-2009-quantifying-correcting-winners-curse (winner’s-curse correction); yurko-2021-approach-to-gene-based-testing (dependence-aware gene-based testing, agglomerative-ld-loci-testing); povysil-2019-rare-variant-collapsing-analyses-complex-traits (rare-variant collapsing analyses).
Data hygiene. zeeberg-2004-mistaken-identifiers-gene-name-errors (Excel gene-name corruption).

wiki/topics/brain-development-neurogenom.title: brain-development-neurogenomics
wiki/topics/brain-development-neurogenom.content:
Brain Development & Neuro-genomics
Topic hub for how the human brain is built and how its genetics goes wrong —
neurodevelopment (neurogenesis → cell types → circuits), human-brain-evolution,
and psychiatric-genetics. This is the biological + disease context for the
wiki’s brain-RNA work: the developing-brain sQTL/isoform atlases
(under splicing) and the single-cell/MPRA methods (under
functional-single-cell-genomics) live next door; this topic supplies the cells,
stages, and disorders they are mapping.
Why it matters
Most neuropsychiatric risk is polygenic and acts early in development
(grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across). Reading that risk
requires knowing the developmental program (neurodevelopment), the cell-type
“parts list” (neuronal-cell-types), and human-specific biology
(human-brain-evolution) — and an experimental model to test causality
(brain-organoids).
Sources (70)
Brain development biology

stiles-2010-the-basics-of-brain (2010) — multi-level overview of human brain development (review)
tau-2010-normal-development-of-brain-circuits (2010) — the “normal” neural-circuit development timeline (review)
duan-2008-development-of-neural-stem (2008) — adult neurogenesis in SGZ/SVZ niches (review)
bertrand-2002-proneural-genes-and-the (2002) — proneural bHLH factors specify neural cell types (review)
zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain (2023) — genetics of human brain development; HARs, organoids (NRG review)
kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular (2021) — brain-organoids + single-cell view of human cortical development (review)
di-lullo-2017-the-use-of-brain (2017) — brain organoids for development & disease (NRN review)
lindhout-2024-a-molecular-and-cellular-perspective (2024) — human-brain-evolution as slowed developmental tempo (Nature Perspective)

Neuronal cell types

von-bartheld-2016-search-for-true-numbers (2016) — true neuron/glia counts in the human brain (~86B neurons, glia-neuron ratio <1:1); debunks the 100B/10:1 myth (J Comp Neurol review) · human
masland-2004-neuronal-cell-types (2004) — the neuronal “parts list” (Current Biology primer)
zeng-2017-neuronal-cell-type-classification-challenges (2017) — molecular-precedence cell-type classification (NRN review)
kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell (2020) — connectivity splits one transcriptomic cluster into types (Neuron)

Neuropsychiatric disease genetics

courchesne-2007-mapping-early-brain-development (2007) — early brain overgrowth in autism (Neuron review) · human
gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across (2022) — pan-cortical ASD transcriptome/isoform dysregulation (Nature) · human
zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of (2025) — autism polygenic factors differ by age at diagnosis (Nature) · human
grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across (2025) — cross-disorder GWAS of 14 psychiatric disorders (Nature) · human
dear-2024-cortical-gene-expression-architecture-links (2024) — cortical expression architecture ↔ imaging, ASD/SCZ (Nat Neurosci) · human
schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism (2026) — snRNA+snATAC across 11 monogenic ASD mouse models (3 stages, both sexes); convergence on a transient radial-glial delay + early-postnatal synaptic/ion-channel downregulation, sex effects larger in females (Nature)
eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation (2026) — snmct-seq single-cell DNA-methylome+transcriptome of ASD vs control prefrontal cortex (49 donors); modest cell-type-specific ASD-DMRs in regulatory elements, uncorrelated with expression, dwarfed by profound aging effects (Cell Genomics) · human
liang-2026-single-cell-multiomics-of-neuron (2026) — snRNA+ATAC of KCl-activated iPSC Glut/GABA neurons (100 donors); stimulation-specific eQTL/caQTL/ASoC + cTWAS unmask context-specific NPD genetics; caQTLs explain ~25% SCZ h²g vs ~10% eQTL; cholesterol/CPT1C implicated (Science) · human
nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8 (2026) — time/cell-type-controlled chd8 mouse alleles pin ASD-like behavior to midfetal (~E14.5) ventral/ganglionic-eminence progenitors of interneurons & oligodendrocytes; ventral CHD8 restoration rescues behavior (Nat Commun)
sun-2024-strategies-for-dissecting-the-complexity (2024) — review of the experimental toolkit (model organisms, iPSCs, single-cell CRISPR, MPRAs) for dissecting NDD genetic complexity (Trends Genet)

CRISPR in the brain

CRISPR applied to brain biology/disease; the method context is the crispr topic.


meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact (2023) — pooled CRISPR screen in human forebrain assembloids maps 425 NDD genes onto migration (LNPK/ER) (Nature) · human
amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes (2026) — genome-wide CRISPR-KO screen of neuronal differentiation implicates plasmalogen enzyme PEDS1 as a recessive NDD gene (Nat Neurosci) · human · resource
tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related (2025) — CRISPRa of endogenous SCN2A rescues haploinsufficiency neurophysiology in mice + human neurons (Nature)
park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing (2019) — non-viral Cas9-RNP nanocomplex edits Bace1 in vivo, rescuing two Alzheimer’s models (Nat Neurosci) · human

ASD & NDD genetic architecture (2026-07-04 backlog)
Rare + common variation, and how it maps to biology — the genetics half of the topic.

sanders-2015-insights-into-autism-spectrum-disorder (2015) — combined CNV+exome of the Simons Simplex Collection nominates 71 ASD risk loci (tada-method) (Neuron) · human
fu-2022-rare-coding-variation-provides (2022) — joint TADA over 63,237 people finds 72 ASD (373 DD) genes mapped to fetal cortical cell types (Nat Genet) · human
dong-2014-de-novo-insertions-and-deletions (2014) — de novo frameshift indels, predominantly paternal, raise ASD risk (Cell Reports) · human
guo-2019-genome-sequencing-identifies-multiple-deleterious (2019) — multiple-hit (oligogenic) variant carriers have more severe cognitive impairment (Genet Med) · human
takumi-2018-cnv-biology-in-neurodevelopmental-disorders (2018) — review of CNV biology in NDD (15q11-13, 16p11.2) (Curr Opin Neurobiol)
antaki-2022-phenotypic-spectrum-of-autism (2022) — rare and polygenic risk inversely correlated, greater in females; ASD as a spectrum of architectures (Nat Genet) · human
cirnigliaro-2023-contributions-of-rare-inherited (2023) — rare inherited + overtransmitted polygenic risk combine additively in multiplex families (PNAS) · human
ruzzo-2019-inherited-and-de-novo-genetic (2019) — WGS of 493 multiplex families; rare inherited risk + a shared de-novo/inherited network (ihart-cohort) (Cell) · human
shin-2023-rare-variation-in-noncoding-regions (2023) — rare recessive variants in evolutionarily-flagged noncoding regions (HARs, enhancers) contribute to ASD (bioRxiv) · human
rolland-2023-phenotypic-effects-of-genetic-variants (2023) — rare LoF in autism genes shifts cognition/SES even in undiagnosed carriers, across 220k+ exomes (Nat Med) · human
perochon-2023-early-detection-of-autism (2023) — tablet app (sensetoknow) quantifies autism-related behaviors for primary-care screening (Nat Med) · human
de-la-torre-ubieta-2016-advancing-the-understanding-of-autism (2016) — landmark review integrating ASD genetic architecture with neuropathology and models (Nat Med)
jeste-2014-disentangling-the-heterogeneity-of-autism (2014) — genetic findings parse ASD phenotypic heterogeneity into treatment-relevant subgroups (Nat Rev Neurol)
lord-2020-autism-spectrum-disorder (2020) — authoritative ASD Primer (epidemiology, genetics, neurobiology, diagnosis, management) (Nat Rev Dis Primers)
searles-quick-2021-leveraging-large-genomic-datasets (2021) — review of big-data human genomics mapping ASD architecture to pathobiology (Neuropsychopharmacology)
sestan-2018-lost-in-translation-traversing (2018) — Perspective: convergence of rare large-effect risk genes in the developing brain, and paths to therapy (Neuron)
bicks-2024-functional-neurogenomics-in-autism-spectrum (2024) — a decade of functional genomics: ASD risk converges on early cortical neurogenesis (Curr Opin Neurobiol)

Brain biology, glia & evolution (2026-07-04 backlog)

gallo-2014-glial-development-crossroads-regeneration (2014) — developmental astrocyte/oligodendrocyte programs are reused in CNS injury repair (Neuron review)
ryan-2009-the-origin-and-evolution (2009) — the synapse’s molecular machinery predates neurons and metazoans (a “protosynapse”) (Nat Rev Neurosci)
wilkins-2020-baz1b-neural-crest-self-domestication (2020) — Spotlight: BAZ1B as a dose-sensitive master regulator of neural-crest facial patterning, tied to human self-domestication (Trends Genet)

Aging & neurodegeneration (2026-07-04 backlog)

handsaker-2025-long-somatic-dna-repeat-expansion (2025) — HTT CAG repeat is silent until it somatically expands past ~150, then rapidly kills striatal projection neurons (Cell) · human
sziraki-2023-a-global-view-of-aging (2023) — whole-brain single-cell atlas (easysci) of aging/Alzheimer cell-population dynamics in mouse and human (Nat Genet) · human


Tags: source pages carry human_research / resource flags (most disease-genetics sources are human; the biology pieces are reviews → resource:✗).

Concept map

Development — neurodevelopment · neurogenesis · radial-glia · brain-organoids
Cell types — neuronal-cell-types
Evolution — human-brain-evolution
Disease genetics — psychiatric-genetics · autism
Regulatory layers — dna-methylation · chromatin-accessibility

Current synthesis (across the 26 sources)

Build, then break. The topic runs from how the brain is built —
neurogenesis (proneural specification, adult niches), migration/lamination,
and the diversification into neuronal-cell-types — to how its genetics goes
wrong. A repeated lesson: transcriptomic identity alone under-specifies cells
(connectivity matters, kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell), which
is exactly the resolution bulk genomics lacks.
Human-specific and slow. Human brain distinctiveness is framed as expanded
progenitors + prolonged (“neotenic”) development + HAR/duplication innovations,
best dissected in brain-organoids
(zhou-2023-genetics-of-human-brain, lindhout-2024-a-molecular-and-cellular-perspective,
kelley-2021-human-brain-organogenesis-toward-cellular).
Disease is developmental, polygenic, and convergent. Cross-disorder risk is
shared across 14 conditions, enriched in early development and specific cells
(grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across); ASD shows broad
cortical transcriptomic convergence
(gandal-2022-broad-transcriptomic-dysregulation-occurs-across) and
developmental heterogeneity (zhang-2025-polygenic-and-developmental-profiles-of);
normative developmental expression axes link to ASD/SCZ across imaging + genetics
(dear-2024-cortical-gene-expression-architecture-links).
Convergence runs deeper than transcription — and earlier than birth. Across
11 genetically diverse mouse models, ASD mutations converge on a transient
radial-glial developmental delay (largest signal in
chromatin-accessibility, early-postnatal synaptic/ion-channel downregulation,
larger female effects) that fades with maturation
(schwarz-2026-cortical-development-dynamics-across-autism); meanwhile the human
ASD cortical methylome (dna-methylation) shows only modest, cell-type-specific
changes that are uncorrelated with expression and dwarfed by aging
(eyring-2026-single-cell-profiling-of-dna-methylation) — pushing the field toward
cell-type- and stage-resolved regulatory readouts beyond bulk transcriptomics.
Causal mapping: when and which cell type. Beyond correlating risk with
development, conditional-genetics papers now pin ASD mechanisms to a specific
window and cell population — chd8 haploinsufficiency acts in midfetal
(~E14.5) ventral/ganglionic-eminence progenitors, and restoring CHD8 there
rescues behavior (nitahara-2026-defective-ventral-neurogenesis-midfetal-chd8);
timed reactivation logic parallels SCN2A (tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related,
scn2a-conditional-ki-mouse) and the assembloid interneuron screen
(meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact) — CRISPR-based tools
(topic crispr) are what make these causal tests possible.
The bridge to RNA work. This topic is the why behind the brain-RNA atlases:
it explains the cells and stages that developing-brain splicing-qtl/isoform
studies (walker-2019-genetic-control-of-expression-and,
wen-2024-cross-ancestry-atlas-of-gene-isoform) and single-cell methods
(functional-single-cell-genomics) are mapping — and why cell-type and
developmental resolution are the field’s open frontiers.

Open questions

Which developmental window and cell type does each disorder’s risk act in, causally? (grotzinger-2025-mapping-the-genetic-landscape-across, psychiatric-genetics)
Can organoids faithfully model the human-specific and late features where disease risk concentrates? (brain-organoids, human-brain-evolution)
How to annotate cell types when transcriptome ≠ identity (connectivity, projection)? (kim-2020-extraction-of-distinct-neuronal-cell, neuronal-cell-types)


Cross-topic: the brain RNA/isoform genetics (splicing) and single-cell/MPRA
resources (functional-single-cell-genomics) of developing human cortex are the
molecular instruments mapping the biology framed here.

2026-07 batch additions — autism genetics, brain development & evolution
Autism genetic architecture. willsey-2013-coexpression-networks-implicate-human-midfetal (midfetal deep-cortical-neuron convergence); parikshak-2013-integrative-functional-genomic-analyses-implicate (integrative functional-genomic ASD networks); turner-2017-genomic-patterns-de-novo-mutation (de novo mutation in simplex autism); krupp-2017-exonic-mosaic-mutations-contribute-risk (postzygotic mosaic mutations); liu-2018-statistical-framework-mapping-risk (the tada-a framework for DNM risk-gene mapping); du-2020-nonrandom-occurrence-multiple-de-novo (oligogenic multiple-DNM model); fischbach-2010-simons-simplex-collection-resource (the simons-simplex-collection); and CHD8 regulatory biology (chd8) in cotney-2015-autism-associated-chromatin-modifier-chd8-regulates, sugathan-2014-chd8-regulates-neurodevelopmental-pathways-associated and derafshi-2022-autism-risk-factor-chd8.
Brain development, cell types & evolution. sloan-2014-mechanisms-astrocyte-development (astrocyte development); chen-2023-robust-differences-cortical-cell-type (cortical cell-type proportions in aging); kang-2011-spatio-temporal-transcriptome-human-brain (the human-brain-transcriptome-atlas); suresh-2023-comparative-single-cell-transcriptomic-analysis-primate (human-specific regulatory evolution across primate brains); atkinson-2018-no-evidence-recent-selection (no recent selection at FOXP2, population-genetics); jovasevic-2024-formation-memory-assemblies-through-dna-sensing (TLR9 DNA-sensing in memory).
Clinical, epidemiology & burden. mccarty-2020-early-detection-diagnosis-autism; piven-2017-conceptual-framework-early-brain-behavior; cortese-2022-association-between-autism-spectrum; tromans-2020-prevalence-of-diabetes-in-autistic; chen-2021-association-maternal-diabetes-neurodevelopmental-disorders; cakir-2020-lifetime-social-cost-autism.

wiki/topics/cancer-immunology.title: Cancer Immunology
wiki/topics/cancer-immunology.content:
Cancer Immunology
Topic hub for anti-tumour immunity and immunotherapy, currently centred on
natural-killer-cells — the innate cytotoxic lymphocytes that kill transformed
cells without prior sensitization and that are emerging as an immunotherapy platform
distinct from T cells.
Why it matters
NK-cell biology reframes tumour immunology around innate effectors. The field
describes a “cancer–NK cell immunity cycle” mirroring the T-cell cancer-immunity
cycle (see huntington-2020-cancer-natural-killer-cell), and asks how tumours
suppress NK surveillance and how to reverse it. Therapeutic angles include
immune-checkpoint molecules expressed on NK cells (see
cao-2020-immune-checkpoint-molecules-natural-killer), and the broader toolkit of
cytokines, engager antibodies, and adoptive/CAR-NK transfer (e.g. trike) reviewed
in maskalenko-2022-harnessing-natural-killer-cells. NK cells act within the wider
family of innate lymphoid cells in cancer (see chiossone-2018-natural-killer-cells-other).
Sources (4)

huntington-2020-cancer-natural-killer-cell — the cancer–NK cell immunity cycle (NRC review).
cao-2020-immune-checkpoint-molecules-natural-killer — NK immune-checkpoint molecules as immunotherapy targets.
maskalenko-2022-harnessing-natural-killer-cells — the NK-cell immunotherapy toolkit (trike, CAR-NK).
chiossone-2018-natural-killer-cells-other — NK cells and other innate lymphoid cells in cancer.

Open directions

Integration with the genomics-heavy topics is thin; a future bridge would be
tumour single-cell/spatial immunophenotyping.

wiki/topics/crispr.title: crispr
wiki/topics/crispr.content:
CRISPR & Genome Editing
Topic hub for CRISPR-Cas as biology, tool, and medicine — from its origin as a
prokaryotic adaptive immune system to its repurposing into the dominant platform for
genome-editing, functional-genomic screening, and
gene therapy. This topic was promoted out of functional-single-cell-genomics once
the CRISPR cluster grew large enough to stand on its own; the deep-dive synthesis is
crispr-screens-and-genome-editing.
Why it matters
CRISPR is the through-line that connects most experimental threads in this wiki: it
turns a regulatory correlation into causation (screens and editing), reads
perturbations at single-cell resolution (single-cell-crispr-screen), and is now
entering the clinic. The sources here span the full arc — mechanism → tool →
screen → therapy → safety — and repeatedly intersect the brain-disease genetics of
brain-development-neurogenomics.
Sources (19)
























































































































































SourceYearAnglehuman?resource?hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance2016CRISPR-Cas as the prokaryotic adaptive immune system; class 1/2, six types; anti-CRISPR (cas9, cas3, cpf1, dcas9)✗✓mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic2016Evolutionary origins + mechanistic variation of CRISPR-Cas; class 2 effectors from mobile elements✗✓barrangou-2016-applications-of-crispr-technologies-in2016Review: CRISPR-Cas9 as a democratized toolkit across research, therapy, agriculture (addgene)✗✗wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells2014Foundational genome-scale pooled CRISPR-Cas9 loss-of-function screening in human cells (pooled-crispr-screen)✗✓dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits2016Founding perturb-seq: pooled CRISPR + scRNA-seq + MIMOSCA (single-cell-crispr-screen)✗✓adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening2016GBC-multiplexed single-cell CRISPRi screen of the unfolded protein response✗✓gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements2017scandel paired-gRNA tiling-deletion scan of HPRT1 (non-homologous-end-joining)✗✓sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel2018crispey retron single-base precise editing of 16,006 variants (homology-directed-repair)✗✓kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for2020Review: CRISPR + crispri-crispra screens in iPSC neurons for neurological disease (crisprbrain)✗✗pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of2024Review: nucleases, base/prime/epigenome/RNA editors; HDR vs NHEJ; therapeutic applications✗✓wang-2023-crispr-technology-a-decade-of2023Doudna-group decade review: successes + next-decade challenges (precision, delivery, cost)✗✗nambiar-2022-crispr-based-genome-editing-through2022Mechanistic review: every edit is a DNA lesion whose outcome DNA repair decides (non-homologous-end-joining, homology-directed-repair, base-editing, prime-editing); steering repair-pathway choice as the master lever✗✗villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome2024Toolbox review: nucleases → base-editing → prime-editing → large insertions (CASTs/PASTE/TwinPE) → epigenome-editing (crispri-crispra, CRISPRoff) → RNA editors (Cas13, REPAIR/RESCUE) → delivery✗✗stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of2025SCGE Perspective: benefit-risk framework + catalog of off-target nomination/verification methods (guide-seq, circle-seq, casgevy)✗✓kernohan-2024-expanding-diagnostic-toolbox-for-rare2024Rare-disease diagnostics review: exome/genome + long-read sequencing, optical genome mapping, functional multi-omics (dna-methylation episignatures, RNA-seq, MAVEs); the diagnosis half of the diagnosis→therapy arc✓✗pao-2025-dawn-of-gene-editing2025Book chapter (popular science): the story of sickle cell disease → fetal-haemoglobin/BCL11A → casgevy, the first approved CRISPR medicine✓✗meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact2023CRISPR screen in human forebrain forebrain-assembloid → interneuron-migration (LNPK/ER)✓✗amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes2026Genome-wide CRISPR-KO screen of neuronal differentiation; implicates peds1 (microcephaly)✓✓tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related2025crispri-crispra (CRISPRa) of endogenous SCN2A rescues haploinsufficiency in mice + human neurons (scn2a-crispra)✗✗park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing2019Non-viral Cas9-RNP nanocomplex edits Bace1 in vivo, rescuing Alzheimer’s models (cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex)✓✗
Concept map

Mechanism / repair — genome-editing · homology-directed-repair · non-homologous-end-joining
Precision editing modalities — base-editing · prime-editing · epigenome-editing
Screening — pooled-crispr-screen · single-cell-crispr-screen
Transcriptional control — crispri-crispra
Sibling assay — massively-parallel-reporter-assay (episomal, non-CRISPR)

Key entities

Cas effectors — cas9 · dcas9 · cpf1 · cas3
Screen / editing platforms — perturb-seq · scandel · crispey · eccite-seq · crisprbrain · castle · neuro-diff-screen · forebrain-assembloid
Off-target detection & annotation — guide-seq · circle-seq · change-seq · digenome-seq · one-seq · site-seq · discover-seq · induce-seq · cast-seq · crisprme · cas-offinder · crispor · deepcrispr · elevation-crispr · cosmic-database · ensembl-vep · cadd-score · crisprot-database
Therapy / models / resources — casgevy · scn2a-crispra · scn2a-conditional-ki-mouse · cas9-r7l10-nanocomplex · 5xfad-mouse-model · peds1 · addgene

Current synthesis

Deep dive: crispr-screens-and-genome-editing — screens → tiling deletions →
precise editing → validation, in one page.


Mechanism → tool. CRISPR-Cas is the only known prokaryotic adaptive immune
system (hille-2016-crispr-cas-biology-mechanisms-and-relevance), whose class 2
effectors arose repeatedly from mobile genetic elements
(mohanraju-2016-diverse-evolutionary-roots-and-mechanistic) and were repurposed
into a democratized editing toolkit
(barrangou-2016-applications-of-crispr-technologies-in,
wang-2023-crispr-technology-a-decade-of,
pacesa-2024-past-present-and-future-of).
Beyond the double-strand break. The modern toolbox moves past cut-and-repair:
villiger-2024-crispr-technologies-for-genome-epigenome maps nucleases →
base-editing → prime-editing → large-insertion (CAST/PASTE/TwinPE) →
epigenome-editing → RNA editors, while
nambiar-2022-crispr-based-genome-editing-through argues the unifying principle is
DNA repair — every editor makes a lesion, and which repair pathway resolves it
(homology-directed-repair vs non-homologous-end-joining, mismatch/base-excision
repair) decides the outcome, so steering repair-pathway choice is the master lever for
precision.
Diagnosis → therapy. Genome medicine begins with a molecular diagnosis:
kernohan-2024-expanding-diagnostic-toolbox-for-rare reviews the rare-disease
diagnostic arsenal (exome/genome + long-read sequencing, optical genome mapping,
functional multi-omics incl. dna-methylation episignatures) — the upstream half of
the diagnosis→edit pipeline that the therapy sources below complete.
Tool → screen. Genome-scale pooled screening
(wang-2014-genetic-screens-in-human-cells) matured into transcriptome-readout
single-cell screens (perturb-seq), tiling-deletion scans (scandel), and
precise-variant screens (crispey) — the pooled-crispr-screen family.
Screen → brain biology. CRISPR screens in iPSC/organoid models map disease genes
onto neurodevelopment: interneuron migration
(meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact) and neuronal differentiation
(amelan-2026-crispr-knockout-screens-reveal-genes);
kampmann-2020-crispr-based-functional-genomics-for frames this for neurological
disease.
Screen → therapy → safety. Dosage therapy by CRISPRa
(tamura-2025-crispr-activation-for-scn2a-related) and in vivo editing
(park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing) move toward the clinic, where measuring
and bounding off-target risk becomes the central question
(stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of).

Open questions

Can precise in-place editing (homology-directed-repair, base/prime editing) reach
the efficiency and delivery needed for post-mitotic neurons in vivo?
(park-2019-in-vivo-neuronal-gene-editing, wang-2023-crispr-technology-a-decade-of)
How “off-target-perfect” must a CRISPR therapy be — and which detection assay is the
right one per context? (stewart-2025-measurement-and-clinical-interpretation-of)
Do organoid/assembloid CRISPR screens
(meng-2023-assembloid-crispr-screens-reveal-impact) recapitulate in vivo human
neurodevelopment well enough to trust their hits?


Grounded strictly in the locally ingested sources. See each source page for citations.

2026-07 batch additions — in vivo base & epigenome editing
an-2024-vivo-base-editing-extends-lifespan (in vivo PRNP base editing in prion disease); neumann-2024-brainwide-silencing-prion-protein (the charm-epigenetic-editor silencing PRNP); cappelluti-2024-durable-efficient-gene-silencing (hit-and-run epigenome editing, evoetr).

wiki/topics/functional-single-cell-genom.title: functional-single-cell-genomics
wiki/topics/functional-single-cell-genom.content:
Functional & Single-cell Genomics
Topic hub for experimental and resource-generating approaches that measure
biological function at single-cell, single-variant, or whole-organ resolution —
the wet-lab/measurement counterpart to the computational methods under
artificial-intelligence. Where that topic is about models, this one is about
maps, atlases, and platforms that those models learn from and are validated
against.
Why it matters
Genome sequence alone does not reveal function. This topic collects high-throughput
ways to measure it directly: protein abundance in single cells
(single-cell-proteomics), the expression effect of thousands of regulatory
variants (massively-parallel-reporter-assay), and the multimodal behavior of an
intact human organ (digital-twins). A recurring lesson across them is that
direct measurement still beats prediction in regimes where models are weak
(e.g. activating cis-regulatory variants, mRNA→protein inference).
Sources (22)

The CRISPR-screen / editing cluster (Wang 2014, Perturb-seq, ScanDel, CRISPEY, …) was
promoted to its own crispr topic. A single-cell perturbation / developmental
functional-genomics wave was added 2026-07-04.


















































































































































SourceYearAnglehuman?resource?zheng-2024-massively-parallel-in-vivo-perturb-seq2024AAV-based fast-onset in vivo Perturb-seq of mouse corticogenesis (perturb-seq, single-cell-crispr-screen)✗✗chardon-2024-multiplex-single-cell-crispra-screening2024multiplexed CRISPRa + scRNA-seq maps cell-type-specific regulatory elements (single-cell-crispr-screen)✗✗huang-2023-single-cell-whole-embryo-phenotyping2023whole-embryo sci-RNA-seq platform phenotyping 22 mouse developmental-disorder mutants✗✓saunders-2023-embryo-scale-reverse-genetics2023zscape — individual-embryo scRNA-seq of 23 F0 CRISPR perturbations across zebrafish development✗✓mitra-2024-single-cell-multi-ome-regression-models2024scarlink — regression links scATAC enhancers to genes; chromatin-potential analysis✗✓gupta-2023-dynamic-regulatory-elements-in-single-cell2023dynamic snATAC peaks in RA synovium enriched for autoimmune heritability (S-LDSC)✓✗gillani-2025-rare-germline-structural-variants2025germline structural variants (incl. TAD-boundary disruptions) raise pediatric solid-tumor risk✓✗lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric2024massively-parallel-reporter-assay of 8-disorder cross-disorder GWAS variants in human neural progenitors; pleiotropy linked to broad neuronal regulation & PPI connectivity✓✓wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of2026single-cell-proteomics atlas of the developing human brain; transcriptome–proteome discordance & autism window (Nat Biotechnol, Resource)✓✓groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at2026massively-parallel-reporter-assay of >30k cis-regulatory mutations in sorghum (plant); cis-regulatory-grammar for crop edits (Nat Biotechnol)✗✓zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex2026digital-twins of ex vivo human lungs (EVLP); personalized therapy evaluation (Nat Biotechnol)✓✓zhang-2026-uncovering-spatially-resolved-functional-genomics2026spac-seq: spatial CRISPR screen sequencing (+tardis-tool); in-situ pooled perturbation + scWhole-transcriptome (Cell)✗✓chi-2026-single-cell-mapping-of-regulatory2026d-and-d-seq: single-cell mapping of regulatory DNA–protein (TF) interactions via tethered base editing (Cell)✓✓zhu-2026-integrative-analyses-elucidate-transcriptional-regulatory2026MASLD risk-allele regulatory function: snATAC + MPRA (massively-parallel-reporter-assay) + CRISPRi → differential-activity variants (Nat Genet)✓✗velmeshev-2023-single-cell-analysis-of-prenatal2023snRNA+snATAC atlas of prenatal→adult human cortex (>700k nuclei); ASD risk in deep-layer ExN (human-cortical-development-cell-browser) (Science)✓✓kim-2024-leveraging-single-cell-atac-seq-and2024scATAC+scRNA → disease-critical fetal/adult brain cell types via S-LDSC (Nat Commun)✓✗loupe-2024-multiomic-profiling-of-transcription-factor2024braintf: multiomic ChIP-seq of >100 TFs in human brain → neuropsychiatric risk (Nat Neurosci)✓✓deng-2024-massively-parallel-characterization-of-regulatory2024psychencode2-developing-cortex-lentimpra: lentiMPRA of 102k regulatory elements in developing cortex + seq-to-activity model (Science)✓✓jagadeesh-2022-identifying-disease-critical-cell-types2022sc-linker: links GWAS heritability to scRNA cell types/programs via S-LDSC (Nat Genet)✓✗
Concept map

Single-cell omics — single-cell-proteomics
Single-cell & spatial methods — spac-seq · d-and-d-seq · tardis-tool
Perturbation screens — now the crispr topic (pooled-crispr-screen · single-cell-crispr-screen · genome-editing); single-cell-perturbation-modeling (modeling) stays here
Functional genomics — massively-parallel-reporter-assay · cis-regulatory-grammar · cis-regulatory-elements
Translational / platforms — digital-twins · multimodal-learning

Key entities

Platforms / tools — dt-lung · spac-seq · tardis-tool · d-and-d-seq
CRISPR screen / editing methods — moved to the crispr topic (perturb-seq · scandel · crispey)
Brain single-cell / regulatory resources — human-cortical-development-cell-browser · braintf · psychencode2-developing-cortex-lentimpra · sc-linker

Current synthesis

CRISPR moved out: the CRISPR-screen / editing cluster is now its own crispr
topic (deep dive: crispr-screens-and-genome-editing).


Endogenous-genome screens complement episomal assays. Beyond reporter-based
massively-parallel-reporter-assay, the topic now spans endogenous perturbation:
programmed kilobase deletions (scandel/gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements),
single-base precise edits (crispey/sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel),
and transcriptome-readout single-cell screens
(perturb-seq/dixit-2016-perturb-seq-dissecting-molecular-circuits,
adamson-2016-multiplexed-single-cell-crispr-screening) — the
pooled-crispr-screen family that turns regulatory correlation into causation.
A recurring negative result: at HPRT1, no single distal element is required
(gasperini-2017-crispr-cas9-scanning-regulatory-elements); and in yeast, noncoding/
cis-regulatory variants dominate the fitness landscape
(sharon-2018-functional-genetic-variants-massively-parallel).
Functional assays close the loop on disease variants. A human-neural
massively-parallel-reporter-assay of eight psychiatric disorders
(lee-2024-massively-parallel-reporter-assay-psychiatric) ties pleiotropic risk
variants to broad excitatory-neuronal regulation — the experimental counterpart to the
computational TWAS in artificial-intelligence.
Measurement beats prediction where models are weakest. Direct MPRA measurement
out-predicts a fine-tuned genomic language model for activating regulatory edits
(groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at); single-cell proteomics
exposes mRNA–protein discordance that transcriptomics cannot
(wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of).
Resources first. All three papers are reusable resources (an atlas, a
variant-effect map, a digital-twin platform) more than single hypothesis tests —
the resource: true tag holds across the topic.
Human vs non-human split. Two are centrally human (developing brain, ex vivo
lungs); one is a crop plant (sorghum) — captured by the human_research tag.
Bridges to AI. Each is also a substrate for the artificial-intelligence
topic: atlases train single-cell-foundation-models, variant maps test
sequence models, and digital twins are themselves multimodal-learning.

Open questions

Can same-cell multi-omics confirm the pseudobulk transcriptome–proteome discordance map? (wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of)
Do MPRA-nominated activating edits translate to whole plants in the field? (groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at)
Does the digital-twin framework generalize beyond one center and to other organs? (zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex)


Grounded strictly in the locally ingested sources. See each source page for citations.

2026-07 batch additions — spatial, combinatorial & interaction methods
singhal-2024-banksy-unifies-cell-typing (spatial cell typing / domain segmentation, banksy); li-2024-combinatorial-genetic-strategy-exploring (combinatorial genetic screening in cancer organoids); armingol-2024-diversification-of-methods-for-studying (methods for inferring cell–cell interactions).

wiki/topics/gene-regulation.title: Gene Regulation & cis-Regulatory Genomics
wiki/topics/gene-regulation.content:
Gene Regulation & cis-Regulatory Genomics
Topic hub for how the noncoding genome controls gene expression — the
enhancers, promoters, the [[cis-regulatory-grammar|sequence
grammar]] that transcription factors read, the enhancer–promoter logic that connects
them, and the reference maps and models that let us interpret regulatory variation.
This is the regulatory-biology counterpart to the wiki’s sequence-model work (topic
artificial-intelligence), its 3d-genome architecture, and the disease genetics
that regulatory variants feed (brain-development-neurogenomics, splicing).
Why it matters
Most trait- and disease-associated variation is noncoding and regulatory
(noncoding-variants) — it changes when, where, and how much a gene is expressed
rather than the protein. Reading it requires (a) a catalogue of the regulatory elements
(cis-regulatory-elements), (b) the grammar of how sequence encodes activity
(cis-regulatory-grammar), and (c) the wiring between elements and their target genes
(chromatin-loops). This topic assembles the experimental and reference foundations;
the predictive models that learn this grammar live under artificial-intelligence.
Sources (27)
Enhancer discovery & developmental enhancer biology

visel-2009-chip-seq-accurately-predicts-tissue (2009) — in vivo p300 ChIP-seq maps distant-acting enhancers and predicts tissue-specific activity (Nature)
wu-2014-tissue-specific-rna-expression-marks (2014) — enhancer RNA (eRNA) expression is tissue-specific and marks in vivo developmental enhancers (Cell Reports)
dickel-2018-ultraconserved-enhancers-are-required (2018) — CRISPR deletion shows ultraconserved forebrain enhancers near Arx are required for normal development (Cell)
rajderkar-2023-cell-type-and-tissue-specific (2023) — catalogue of ~14,000 human craniofacial enhancers for interpreting facial-trait/birth-defect variants (Nat Genet) · resource
catta-preta-2023-combinatorial-transcription-factor-binding-encodes (2023) — combinatorial TF binding + 3D contacts encode cis-regulatory wiring of forebrain GABAergic neurogenesis (Nat Commun)
nord-2020-neurobiological-functions-of-transcriptional (2020) — review connecting genome-scale CNS enhancer maps to enhancer mechanism and brain-disorder risk (Nat Neurosci)

Enhancer–promoter logic

bergman-2022-compatibility-rules-of-human-enhancer (2022) — ExP STARR-seq of 1,000×1,000 E–P pairs: broad compatibility, activities combine multiplicatively (Nature)
pollex-2024-enhancer-promoter-interactions-become-more (2024) — E–P regulation shifts from permissive (specification) to instructive (differentiation) in Drosophila (Nat Genet)
chen-2024-increased-enhancer-promoter-interactions-during (2024) — in vivo capture Hi-C: E–P proximity rises upon developmental enhancer activation across mouse tissues (Nat Genet)
doughty-2024-single-molecule-chromatin-configurations-link (2024) — single-molecule footprinting decomposes activation into separable TF-binding and potency terms (bioRxiv)

Regulatory variation & reference resources

kilpinen-2013-coordinated-effects-of-sequence-variation (2013) — allele-specific profiling: variants coordinately perturb TF binding, chromatin, and transcription, TFs as drivers (Science) · human
moore-2020-expanded-encyclopaedias-of-dna-elements (2020) — ENCODE3 registry of ~927k human / ~340k mouse candidate cis-regulatory elements (SCREEN) (Nature) · resource

Comparative & epitranscriptomic regulation

zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory (2023) — cross-species single-cell multiomics: transposable-element-driven cis-regulatory divergence shapes neocortex evolution (Science)
sun-2023-crosstalk-between-rna-m6a (2023) — m6A reader YTHDC2 → TET1 keeps LTR7/HERV-H demethylated, restraining neural differentiation (Nat Genet) · human
li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-m6a-promotes-local (2023) — super-enhancer-RNA m6A opens local chromatin to drive oncogene transcription in pancreatic cancer (Nat Genet) · human

Concept map

Elements — cis-regulatory-elements · massively-parallel-reporter-assay
Grammar — cis-regulatory-grammar · chromatin-accessibility
Wiring — chromatin-loops · topologically-associating-domains · genome-architecture
Variation — noncoding-variants · dna-methylation

Current synthesis

From cataloguing to grammar to wiring. The topic runs from finding enhancers
(p300 ChIP-seq, eRNA, ENCODE cCREs) to reading their sequence grammar
(cis-regulatory-grammar) to connecting elements to genes — where the emerging
picture is broad enhancer–promoter compatibility (bergman-2022-compatibility-rules-of-human-enhancer)
that becomes more instructive and physically closer as cells differentiate
(pollex-2024-enhancer-promoter-interactions-become-more, chen-2024-increased-enhancer-promoter-interactions-during).
Regulatory variation is coordinated and mostly noncoding. Sequence variants
perturb TF binding, chromatin, and transcription in a haplotype-coordinated way with
TFs as primary drivers (kilpinen-2013-coordinated-effects-of-sequence-variation);
interpreting such noncoding-variants is the throughline into disease genetics.
Regulation evolves and is layered. cis-regulatory divergence (often
transposable-element-driven) underlies neocortex evolution
(zemke-2023-conserved-and-divergent-gene-regulatory), and an epitranscriptomic
(m6A) layer tunes enhancer/TE activity and chromatin accessibility
(sun-2023-crosstalk-between-rna-m6a, li-2023-super-enhancer-rna-m6a-promotes-local).

Open questions

What is the true “grammar” mapping enhancer sequence → activity → target gene, and can models learn it? (cross-topic: artificial-intelligence sequence models)
How instructive vs permissive are E–P contacts in mammals, causally? (chromatin-loops)
Which noncoding regulatory variants are causal for disease, and through which cell type and stage? (noncoding-variants, brain-development-neurogenomics)


Cross-topic: predictive sequence-to-function models (artificial-intelligence),
3D folding (3d-genome), and regulatory disease variants
(brain-development-neurogenomics, splicing) all build on the elements and
grammar catalogued here.

2026-07 batch additions — enhancers, promoters & noncoding regulation
Enhancers & cis-regulatory activity. osterwalder-2018-enhancer-redundancy-provides-phenotypic-robustness (enhancer redundancy/robustness); giorgio-2015-large-genomic-deletion-enhancer-adoption (enhancer adoption in ADLD); capauto-2024-characterization-enhancer-activity-early (MPRA in forebrain organoids); preissl-2023-characterizing-cis-regulatory-elements-single-cell (single-cell epigenomics of CREs); meuleman-2020-index-biological-spectrum-human (the dhs-index); nott-2019-brain-cell-type-specific-enhancer (brain cell-type enhancer–promoter interactomes); yao-2015-coexpression-networks-identify-brain-region (brain-region eRNAs, brain-expressed-enhancers-browser).
Promoters, transcription & noncoding variants. dudnyk-2023-sequence-basis-transcription-initiation (the puffin transcription-initiation model); nepal-2023-alternative-promoters-cpg-depleted (alternative promoters in liver cancer); radrizzani-2023-selection-on-synonymous-sites (selection on synonymous sites); zhou-2018-prioritization-and-functional-assessment (noncoding-variant prioritization, pafa); castro-2023-challenges-screening-noncoding-variants (challenges screening de novo noncoding variants).

wiki/topics/genome-sequencing-and-refere.title: Genome Sequencing & Reference Resources
wiki/topics/genome-sequencing-and-refere.content:
Genome Sequencing & Reference Resources
Topic hub for the infrastructure of genomics: reference genomes, sequencing and
assembly technology, the data formats and methods that make genomes usable, and the
translation of all this into the clinic. Where the biology topics consume genomic
data, this topic is about how that data is produced, represented, and standardized.
Strands

Reference genomes & genome-assembly. From the public draft human genome
(see ihgsc-2001-initial-sequencing-analysis-human) to the first gapless
telomere-to-telomere assembly (t2t-chm13, see
nurk-2022-complete-sequence-of-a-human), the completion of hard regions such
as centromeres (see logsdon-2024-variation-and-evolution-of-complete;
chm1-centromere-assemblies), and the shift to diversity-representing
pangenomes (hprc-pangenome, see liao-2023-draft-human-pangenome-reference).
Sequencing methods & analysis. Variant calling and benchmarking in the
